> **Design Insight:** The CVT is engineered to produce a $V_m$ in the range **5–25 kV** because this range is a *practical sweet spot*: low enough for a compact intermediate transformer, high enough to keep divider impedance manageable. The compensating reactor *exactly cancels* the capacitive divider impedance at 50 Hz, ensuring *flat frequency response* and *low burden sensitivity*.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Numerical Protection Scheme Using CTs and CVTs

This diagram maps the *complete signal path* from the high-voltage power line, through the CT/CVT, the cable/burden, the analog interface, and finally into the numerical relay's A/D converter and protection algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HVBus[HV Power Line 400 kV Bus]
    PCT[CT Primary: N1 turns in series with line]
    SCT[CT Secondary: N2 turns loaded by burden]
    Burden[Burden: Relay Z plus lead resistance]
    SecTerm[CT Secondary Terminals S1 S2]
    CTequiv[CT Equivalent Circuit Referred to Primary: Magnetizing Branch Xe parallel Rc, Referred Secondary Impedance Z2 prime, Referred Burden Zb prime]
    MHO[CT Magnetizing Current Ie source]
    Pcap1[Capacitor C1 HV Divider Capacitor]
    Pcap2[Capacitor C2 LV Divider Capacitor]
    Pdiv[CVD Thevenin Source Vth and Zth capacitive]
    Pind[Compensating Reactor L tuned to 50 Hz]
    Pxfmr[Step Down Intermediate Transformer ratio k]
    Pdamper[Ferro Resonance Suppression Gap and Resistor]
    SecVT[VT Secondary 110 over root 3 V output]
    AnaInt[Analog Interface: Anti Aliasing Filter and Surge Protection]
    ADC[Numerical Relay A to D Converter]
    Alg[Protection Algorithm Running in DSP]

    HVBus --> PCT
    PCT --> SCT
    SCT --> Burden
    SCT --> SecTerm
    SecTerm --> CTequiv
    MHO --- CTequiv
    Burden --> CTequiv
    HVBus --> Pcap1
    Pcap1 --> Pcap2
    Pcap2 --> Pdiv
    Pdiv --> Pind
    Pind --> Pxfmr
    Pdamper --- Pxfmr
    Pxfmr --> SecVT
    SecTerm --> AnaInt
    SecVT --> AnaInt
    AnaInt --> ADC
    ADC --> Alg
```

The phasor relationships in a CT are mapped below as a *block-diagram*, which a student can mentally visualize when drawing the actual phasor figure in the exam.


## 4.4 Phasor Diagram of CT Errors (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RefAxis[Reference Phasor E2 horizontal]
    SecCurr[Phasor I2 lagging E2 by alpha]
    MagCurr[Phasor Ie leading E2 by 90 minus phi]
    SinProj[Component of Ie along E2: Ie sin alpha plus phi: causes ratio error]
    CosProj[Component of Ie perpendicular to E2: Ie cos alpha plus phi: causes phase error]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams in an exam:** The student should reproduce a *phasor diagram* showing $E_2$ as the reference, $I_2$ lagging $E_2$ by $\alpha$, $I_e$ leading $E_2$ by $(90° - \phi)$, and *projecting* $I_e$ along and perpendicular to $E_2$ to obtain the ratio and phase errors. Most KTU valuation scripts give full credit for the *correct construction* of the phasor diagram even if the final numerical answer has a minor error.

> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - **Part A:** 3 marks each, *short answer*, no choice. (2 questions × 3 = 6 marks)
> - **Part B:** 14 marks each, *internal choice* (Q-A or Q-B). (1 question × 14 = 14 marks)
> - All questions are *CO-mapped* and *Bloom-level tagged*.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] 
**Define Knee-Point Voltage ($V_{knee}$) of a Current Transformer. Why is it a critical parameter for *protection* CTs?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Knee-Point Voltage** of a CT is the *secondary voltage* at which a **10 % increase** in secondary voltage produces a **50 % increase** in exciting (magnetizing) current. Equivalently, it is the voltage at which the magnetizing reactance drops sharply due to the onset of core saturation.
> 
> **Mathematical form (0.5 mark):** 
> $$V_{knee} = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, A_c \, B_{sat}$$
> where $A_c$ is the core cross-section, $B_{sat}$ is the saturation flux density.
> 
> **Significance (1 mark):** For *relaying* CTs, the $V_{knee}$ must be high enough that the CT does *not saturate* during the largest internal fault current. If $V_{knee}$ is too low, the CT enters saturation, the secondary current is *severely distorted*, and the relay may *misoperate* (failure to trip or false trip on through-faults).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Capacitive Voltage Transformer (CVT). Mention any two advantages of CVT over an electromagnetic VT at EHV levels.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Block diagram (1.5 marks):** [Student should draw: HV line → $C_1$ → tap → $C_2$ → ground; tap → compensating reactor $L$ → step-down transformer → secondary $110/\sqrt{3}$ V; with ferro-resonance damper across one winding.]
> 
> **Working (1 mark):** The line voltage $V_p$ is divided by the capacitor divider ($C_1, C_2$) to produce a *low intermediate voltage* $V_m = V_p C_1/(C_1+C_2)$. The compensating reactor $L$ cancels the capacitive Thevenin impedance at 50 Hz. The step-down transformer delivers the standard 110 V (phase) / $110/\sqrt{3}$ V to the relay/meter burden.
> 
> **Advantages over EMVT (0.5 mark):**
> 1. **Economical** for EHV ($> 66$ kV): capacitors are far cheaper than thousands of turns of HV-insulated wire.
> 2. **Acts as a Coupling Capacitor** for Power-Line Carrier (PLC) communication when a line trap is connected at the tap.
> 3. **Better transient response** with modern active dampers.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. The student chooses **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

#### (a) Derive the expressions for ratio error and phase error of a Current Transformer from its equivalent circuit. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the Equivalent Circuit (2 marks):**
The student should draw the T-equivalent circuit of a CT referred to the primary:
- Primary winding with $N_1$ turns carrying current $I_1$.
- Magnetizing branch: $R_c \parallel jX_m$ carrying $I_e$.
- Referred secondary: $Z_2' = (R_2 + jX_2)/a^2$ in series with the referred burden $Z_B' = (R_B + jX_B)/a^2$.

**Step 2 — Apply MMF Balance (1 mark):**
$$N_1 \vec{I_1} = N_2 \vec{I_2} + N_1 \vec{I_e}$$
$$\vec{I_1} = a \vec{I_2} + \vec{I_e}$$

**Step 3 — Phasor Diagram Construction (1 mark):**
Draw the phasor diagram with $E_2$ (or $E_2'$, the induced EMF) as the reference. Mark:
- $\vec{I_2}$ lagging $E_2$ by $\alpha$ (the loop angle).
- $\vec{I_e}$ leading $E_2$ by $(90° - \phi)$ where $\phi$ is the core-loss angle.

**Step 4 — Decompose $I_e$ (1 mark):**
- In-phase component: $I_e \sin(\alpha + \phi)$ — responsible for ratio error.
- Quadrature component: $I_e \cos(\alpha + \phi)$ — responsible for phase error.

**Step 5 — Final Formulas (2 marks):**
$$\boxed{\epsilon_I = -\frac{I_e N_1}{I_2 N_2} \sin(\alpha + \phi) \times 100\ \%}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_e = \frac{I_e N_1}{I_2 N_2} \cos(\alpha + \phi) \text{ radians}}$$

> **Conclusion:** The ratio error arises from the *in-phase component* of magnetizing current, while the phase error arises from the *quadrature component*. Both errors are *inversely proportional* to the secondary current — meaning errors are *largest at small primary currents* and *decrease* as primary current increases.

#### (b) A 1000/5 A, 50 Hz CT has a bar primary (1 turn) and 200 secondary turns. The secondary winding resistance is $0.5\ \Omega$, the burden is purely resistive at $1.5\ \Omega$, and the magnetizing branch has $R_c = 1000\ \Omega$, $X_m = 500\ \Omega$ referred to the secondary. With a primary current of 1000 A, calculate the actual secondary current, the ratio error, and the phase error. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities (1 mark):**
- $N_1 = 1$, $N_2 = 200$, $I_1 = 1000$ A.
- $R_2 = 0.5\ \Omega$, $R_B = 1.5\ \Omega$ (burden), $X_2 = 0$ and $X_B = 0$ (purely resistive).
- Referred core: $R_c = 1000\ \Omega$, $X_m = 500\ \Omega$ (already referred to secondary).

**Step 2 — Compute Loop Impedance (1 mark):**
$$Z_s = R_2 + R_B = 0.5 + 1.5 = 2.0\ \Omega$$
$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0/2.0) = 0°$$

**Step 3 — Compute Core Loss Angle (0.5 mark):**
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{R_c}{X_m}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1000}{500}\right) = \tan^{-1}(2) = 63.43°$$

(Note: this is unusually large — a real CT core has a much smaller $\phi$, but we proceed with the given values.)

**Step 4 — Compute Induced EMF (1 mark):**
Ideal secondary current: $I_{2,\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{1000}{200} = 5.0$ A.
$$E_2 \approx I_{2,\text{ideal}} \times Z_s = 5.0 \times 2.0 = 10\ \text{V (ideal)}$$

For the *first iteration*, we estimate the *secondary voltage* across the magnetizing branch as approximately $E_2 \approx 10$ V.

**Step 5 — Compute Magnetizing Current (1 mark):**
$$I_e = \dfrac{E_2}{Z_e} = \dfrac{E_2}{\sqrt{R_c^2 + X_m^2}} \quad \text{(magnitude of exciting current)}$$
$$Z_e = \sqrt{1000^2 + 500^2} = \sqrt{1{,}250{,}000} = 1118.03\ \Omega$$
$$I_e = \dfrac{10}{1118.03} = 8.944\ \text{mA} = 0.008944\ \text{A}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Actual Secondary Current (1 mark):**
Using $\alpha + \phi = 0° + 63.43° = 63.43°$:
$$I_2 = \dfrac{N_1 I_1 - I_e \sin(\alpha + \phi)}{N_2} = \dfrac{1 \times 1000 - 0.008944 \times \sin(63.43°)}{200}$$

$$\sin(63.43°) = 0.8944$$
$$I_2 = \dfrac{1000 - 0.008944 \times 0.8944}{200} = \dfrac{1000 - 0.008}{200} = \dfrac{999.992}{200} = 4.99996\ \text{A}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Ratio and Phase Errors (1.5 marks):**
$$\epsilon_I = \dfrac{K_n I_2 - I_1}{I_1} \times 100\ \% = \dfrac{200 \times 4.99996 - 1000}{1000} \times 100\ \%$$
$$= \dfrac{999.992 - 1000}{1000} \times 100\ \% = -0.0008\ \%$$

$$\theta_e = \dfrac{I_e N_1}{I_2 N_2} \cos(\alpha + \phi) = \dfrac{0.008944 \times 1}{4.99996 \times 200} \cos(63.43°)$$
$$= \dfrac{0.008944}{999.992} \times 0.4472 = 8.944 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.4472 = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad}$$

Converting to minutes:
$$\theta_e = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{180 \times 60}{\pi} = 0.0137\ \text{minutes}$$

**Final Answer:** $I_2 = 4.99996$ A, $\epsilon_I = -0.0008\ \%$, $\theta_e \approx 0.014$ minutes.

> The CT is *measuring-class accurate* (ratio error < 0.1%, phase error < 5').

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

#### (a) Explain the construction and working of a Capacitive Voltage Transformer (CVT) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the compensating reactor and the ferro-resonance suppression circuit. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The student must draw:
- HV bus → $C_1$ (HV capacitor stack) → tap point → $C_2$ (LV capacitor stack) → ground.
- Tap point → compensating reactor $L$ → primary of intermediate transformer $T$.
- Secondary of $T$ → burden (relays, meters) → $110/\sqrt{3}$ V output.
- Ferro-resonance damper (spark gap + resistor, *or* active electronic damper) connected across a winding of $T$.

**Step 2 — Construction Details (1.5 marks):**
- $C_1, C_2$: typically *paper-oil* or *SF₆*-insulated capacitor units stacked in a porcelain housing.
- Compensating reactor: *iron-core inductor* with taps, oil-immersed.
- Intermediate transformer: *5-limbed* core type (to provide a *zero-sequence flux path* for the damper circuit).
- Damper: passive RC snubber *or* active IGBT-based damper.

**Step 3 — Working Principle (1.5 marks):**
- $V_m = V_p \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_1+C_2}$ — capacitive divider scales the voltage.
- $L$ resonates with $C_{eq}$ at 50 Hz: $\omega_0^2 L C_{eq} = 1$, *nulling* the divider's source impedance.
- Transformer $T$ steps $V_m$ to $110/\sqrt{3}$ V secondary.
- Damper prevents *ferro-resonance* (subharmonic oscillation at $f_0/3 \approx 16.7$ Hz) when the CVT is switched or experiences a disturbance.

**Step 4 — Compensating Reactor Function (1 mark):**
Without $L$, the *capacitive* Thevenin impedance of the divider would cause:
- *Burden-dependent* error (more burden → larger voltage drop → more error).
- *Frequency-dependent* error (off-nominal frequency → wrong division ratio).
The reactor *cancels* $Z_{th}$, making the CVT *accurate* across the relay's frequency bandwidth (typically 50 Hz ± 5 Hz).

**Step 5 — Ferro-Resonance (1 mark):**
The non-linear magnetizing inductance of $T$ plus $C_{eq}$ can form a *subharmonic oscillator*. Once excited, the CVT output contains a large $f_0/3$ component, leading to *relay misoperation*. The damper (a saturable reactor + resistor) *damps* this oscillation in a few cycles.

#### (b) A 220 kV CVT uses capacitors $C_1 = 0.008\ \mu F$ and $C_2 = 0.08\ \mu F$. The intermediate transformer has a turns ratio of 200:1. Calculate: (i) the intermediate voltage $V_m$, (ii) the Thevenin equivalent capacitance and source impedance at 50 Hz, (iii) the value of the compensating reactor, and (iv) the secondary voltage delivered to the relay. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data (0.5 mark):**
$V_p = 220{,}000$ V, $C_1 = 0.008\ \mu F = 8 \times 10^{-9}$ F, $C_2 = 0.08\ \mu F = 8 \times 10^{-8}$ F, $N_{ratio} = 200{:}1$, $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Intermediate Voltage $V_m$ (1 mark):**
$$V_m = V_p \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_1+C_2} = 220{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.008}{0.008+0.08} = 220{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.008}{0.088}$$
$$V_m = 220{,}000 \times 0.0909 = 20{,}000\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Thevenin Capacitance (1.5 marks):**
$$C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1+C_2} = \dfrac{0.008 \times 0.08}{0.088} = \dfrac{0.00064}{0.088} = 0.007273\ \mu F = 7.273\ \text{nF}$$

Thevenin impedance (capacitive):
$$Z_{th} = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{j \cdot 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 7.273 \times 10^{-9}}$$
$$= \dfrac{1}{j \cdot 2.285 \times 10^{-6}} = -j \cdot 437{,}640\ \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compensating Reactor (2 marks):**
For resonance at 50 Hz:
$$L = \dfrac{1}{\omega^2 C_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi \cdot 50)^2 \cdot 7.273 \times 10^{-9}}$$
$$L = \dfrac{1}{98{,}696 \cdot 7.273 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{1}{7.177 \times 10^{-4}} = 1393.3\ \text{H}$$

**Step 5 — Secondary Voltage (2 marks):**
$$V_{sec} = \dfrac{V_m}{N_{ratio}} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{200} = 100\ \text{V (RMS, line-to-ground)}$$

> **Design Verification:** This is a *standard phase-to-ground* secondary voltage. The *phase-to-phase* secondary would be $100 \sqrt{3} = 173.2$ V, which is close to the standard 110 V class used by some relays. The 200:1 ratio is the *physical turns ratio* of the intermediate transformer; the *overall CVT ratio* is $220{,}000/100 = 2200{:}1$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 1 CT/VT/CVT):**
> 
> 1. **Open CT Secondary = Lethal:** A student who writes "the CT secondary can be open-circuited safely if there is no load" will lose **full marks** on any safety-related question. Always write: *"The CT secondary must NEVER be open-circuited while the primary is energized — this causes HV arcing and core saturation."*
> 
> 2. **Sign Convention of Ratio Error:** Many students forget the *negative sign* in the ratio error formula. The standard convention is that the CT *under-reads* (negative error) for normal operation. Writing the *magnitude* without sign will lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 
> 3. **Confusing CT vs VT Operation:** CT operates *near short-circuit* (low burden impedance), VT operates *near open-circuit* (high burden impedance). Confusing the two in a conceptual question will lose 2–3 marks.
> 
> 4. **CVT Ferro-Resonance:** A common mistake is to omit the *damper* in the CVT block diagram. Examiners specifically look for it because it is a *mandatory* component in EHV substations.
> 
> 5. **Polarity Markings:** Always mark $H_1, H_2$ (primary) and $X_1, X_2$ (secondary) on CT diagrams. For VTs, mark $A, a$ (primary) and $B, b$ (secondary) using the *old ANSI convention* or $1.1, 1.2$ / $2.1, 2.2$ using the *new convention*. Forgetting polarity will lose 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Phase Error Units:** Phase error in CTs is typically expressed in **minutes of arc**, *not* in radians or degrees. Mixing units in the answer will be penalized.
> 
> 7. **Compensating Reactor Derivation:** Many students simply write "$L$ is tuned to 50 Hz" without showing $L = 1/(\omega^2 C_{eq})$. Always show the *derivation* — it is worth at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (CTs / VTs / CVTs):**
> 
> **1. Current Transformer (CT) — Quick Facts**
> - Primary in *series* with line; secondary feeds a *low-impedance burden*.
> - Standard secondary ratings: **1 A** or **5 A**.
> - Polarity: $H_1$ and $X_1$ are dotted (in-phase MMF).
> - Errors depend on *magnetizing current* $I_e$: ratio error $\propto I_e \sin(\alpha+\phi)$; phase error $\propto I_e \cos(\alpha+\phi)$.
> - **NEVER** open-circuit the secondary of an energized CT.
> - Knee-point voltage $V_{knee} = 4.44 f N_2 A_c B_{sat}$ determines *relaying performance*.
> - Accuracy classes: **Metering** (0.2, 0.5, 1.0) vs **Protection** (5P, 10P, TPS, TPX, TPY, TPZ).
> 
> **2. Voltage Transformer (Electromagnetic VT/PT)**
> - Primary in *parallel* with line; secondary feeds a *high-impedance burden*.
> - Standard secondary: **110 V** (line-to-line) or **$110/\sqrt{3}$ V** (phase-to-ground).
> - Modelled exactly like a small power transformer.
> - Economic limit: ≈ 66 kV (above this, use CVT).
> 
> **3. Capacitive Voltage Transformer (CVT)**
> - Three functional blocks: Capacitor divider ($C_1, C_2$) + Compensating reactor ($L$) + Step-down transformer ($T$).
> - Voltage division: $V_m = V_p \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_1+C_2}$.
> - Thevenin: $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1+C_2}$, $Z_{th} = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{eq}}$.
> - Reactor condition: $\omega_0^2 L C_{eq} = 1$ at 50 Hz.
> - **Ferro-resonance damper** is *mandatory* for EHV CVTs.
> - Bonus: CVT tap doubles as a **PLC coupling point** (line-carrier communication).
> 
> **4. Key Numerical Benchmarks to Memorize**
> - $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$, $110/\sqrt{3} \approx 63.51$ V, $2\pi \cdot 50 = 314.16$ rad/s.
> - 400 kV / $\sqrt{3} \approx 230.94$ kV (phase voltage).
> 
> **5. Common Exam "Must-Draw" Components**
> - CT equivalent circuit with magnetizing branch.
> - CVT block diagram (capacitor divider + reactor + transformer + damper).
> - CT phasor diagram with $E_2$, $I_2$, $I_e$, $\alpha$, and $\phi$.
> 
> **6. Safety & Polarity Mantra (always write in exam)**
> - "CT secondary must always be shorted (or connected to a low-impedance burden)."
> - "Polarity: current entering $H_1$ produces in-phase current leaving $X_1$."
> - "For CVT, the damper is mandatory to prevent ferro-resonance damage."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Operation of Magneto Optic CT/PT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle of Operation of Magneto Optic CT/PT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Magneto-Optic Current Transformer (MOCT)

A **Magneto-Optic Current Transformer (MOCT)** is a non-conventional, optically-coupled instrument transformer that senses alternating current in a high-voltage conductor by exploiting the **Faraday Effect (Magneto-Optic Effect)** — the rotation of the plane of polarization of a linearly polarized light beam as it propagates through an optically active medium placed in a magnetic field generated by the primary current.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Formal KTU Definition:**  
> An *optical current transducer* in which the sensing element is a magneto-optic material (e.g., a fused-silica glass ring, SF₆ gas, or a single-mode optical fiber) wound or arranged around the primary current conductor. The rotation of the polarization plane is proportional to the instantaneous current, and is converted to an electrical signal by an analyzer–photodetector pair.

## 1.2 Magneto-Optic Potential Transformer (MOPT)

A **Magneto-Optic Potential Transformer (MOPT)** (more correctly termed an *Electro-Optic Voltage Transformer*) uses the **Pockels Effect** — the field-induced birefringence in certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (e.g., Lithium Niobate – LiNbO₃, Bismuth Germanium Oxide – BGO, or Bismuth Silicon Oxide – BSO) — to convert an applied high voltage into a measurable change in the polarization state of light.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why the prefix "Magneto-Optic"?**  
> In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, both transducers fall under the umbrella of *Optical Transducers*. CT refers to the *current* sensing version (Faraday) and PT to the *voltage* sensing version (Pockels). They are *passive on the high-voltage side* — no iron core, no copper windings, no oil, no SF₆ insulation in the conventional sense — making them intrinsically safe, immune to electromagnetic interference, and bandwidth-limited only by the speed of light.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Spinning Compass in a Whirlpool"

> [!TIP]  
> **Analogy for MOCT (Faraday Effect):**  
> Imagine a thin beam of light as a spinning coin tossed through a giant invisible whirlpool created by the current. The stronger the current, the tighter and faster the whirlpool — and the more the coin's flat face twists as it passes through. Measure the twist angle, and you measure the current.

> [!TIP]  
> **Analogy for MOPT (Pockels Effect):**  
> Now imagine passing that same spinning coin through a stretched rubber sheet. The higher the voltage, the more the sheet is stretched, and the coin's spin axis gets squeezed or stretched — its shape changes from a perfect circle to an ellipse. The degree of "squish" tells you the voltage.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need Optical CTs/PTs?

Conventional iron-core CTs saturate, have limited bandwidth, contain oil/SF₆, and pose catastrophic-failure risks at EHV/UHV levels (400 kV, 765 kV, 1200 kV). Optical transducers offer:

- **Wide bandwidth:** DC to several hundred kHz (vs. ~3 kHz for iron-core).
- **No saturation:** Linear response over a huge dynamic range.
- **Galvanic isolation:** Complete dielectric isolation between HV and LV sides.
- **Digital-friendly output:** Easily interfaced with IEC 61850 process bus.
- **Eco-friendly:** No oil, no SF₆ greenhouse gas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Faraday Effect — Physical Foundation

When a linearly polarized light beam propagates through a transparent optical medium in the presence of a magnetic field **B** oriented *parallel* to the direction of propagation, the plane of polarization rotates by an angle **θ_F** (the *Faraday rotation*).

### 2.1.1 Key Operational Logic

1. **Source of Light:** A stable, narrow-bandwidth LED (typically 850 nm) is used because the Verdet constant is wavelength-dependent and LEDs are temperature-stable.
2. **Polarization:** Light is first linearly polarized by a high-extinction-ratio polarizer (Glan–Taylor prism).
3. **Magnetic Field Generation:** The primary current **I_p** flowing in the HV busbar creates a circumferential magnetic field **H** around the conductor (right-hand rule).
4. **Magneto-Optic Interaction:** The polarized light is routed through a sensing element (an optical fiber wound **N** times around the conductor, or a block of flint glass or SF₆).
5. **Rotation:** The plane of polarization rotates by an angle θ_F proportional to the line integral of **H** along the optical path.
6. **Detection:** An analyzer (a second polarizer at 45°) and a photodetector convert the intensity modulation back into an electrical signal.
7. **Processing:** Signal processing electronics extract **θ_F** and hence the primary current.

### 2.1.2 The Core Faraday Equation

The Faraday rotation for a light path traversing a length **L** through a medium of Verdet constant **V** in a magnetic field **H** is:

$$
\theta_F = V \cdot \int_0^{L} \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}
$$

For a *closed* optical loop encircling a current **N** times, applying **Ampère's Circuital Law**:

$$
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = N \cdot I_p
$$

Therefore, the total Faraday rotation reduces to the elegant, position-independent form:

$$
\boxed{\theta_F = V \cdot N \cdot I_p}
$$

This is the single most important KTU-result-bearing equation for MOCT design.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Verdet Constant V:**  
> - For **SF₆ gas**: $V \approx 0.0156 \text{ rad/(T·m)}$ at 633 nm  
> - For **fused silica (SiO₂)**: $V \approx 4.1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad/A}$ (in air)  
> - For **flint glass (dense)**: $V \approx 32 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad/A}$  
>  
> V is temperature-dependent, so compensation circuits are mandatory in commercial designs.

## 2.2 The Pockels Effect — Physical Foundation

When a transparent, non-centrosymmetric crystal (e.g., LiNbO₃) is subjected to an external electric field **E**, the ordinary and extraordinary refractive indices shift by different amounts, inducing a **field-proportional birefringence**. Linearly polarized light entering the crystal emerges as **elliptically polarized**, with the degree of ellipticity depending on the applied voltage.

### 2.2.1 Key Operational Logic

1. **High voltage application:** The phase-to-ground voltage is divided down by a capacitive/inductive divider and applied across the crystal's electrodes.
2. **Electric field inside crystal:** $E = V/d$, where **d** is the inter-electrode distance.
3. **Field-induced birefringence:** $\Delta n = \tfrac{1}{2} n_0^{3} \cdot r \cdot E$ (for transverse configuration), where **r** is the linear electro-optic coefficient.
4. **Phase retardation:** Light polarized at 45° to the principal axes splits into two components travelling at different speeds; the resulting phase difference **Γ** is proportional to the applied voltage.
5. **Optical read-out:** An analyzer converts this phase shift into an intensity modulation at the photodetector.
6. **Decoding:** Signal processing extracts **Γ** and hence the primary voltage.

### 2.2.2 The Core Pockels Equation (Transverse Configuration)

For a crystal of length **L** along the light path, inter-electrode spacing **d**, and applied voltage **V**:

$$
\Gamma = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta n \cdot L = \frac{\pi n_0^{3} r L}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{V}{d}
$$

Defining the **half-wave voltage** $V_{\pi}$ as the voltage required to produce $\Gamma = \pi$:

$$
\boxed{V_{\pi} = \frac{\lambda \, d}{n_0^{3} \, r \, L}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why BGO (Bismuth Germanium Oxide)?**  
> BGO has no natural birefringence and is transparent across a wide spectral range, making it the industrial standard for MOPT at EHV levels. Its electro-optic coefficient $r_{41} \approx 0.95 \text{ pm/V}$ is smaller than LiNbO₃, but the absence of natural birefringence is a more decisive advantage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Governing Equation | Typical Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|--------------------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Faraday rotation | $\theta_F = V \cdot N \cdot I_p$ | rad (output), A (input) | **N = number of optical fiber turns** around conductor |
| 2 | General Faraday | $\theta_F = V \cdot \int_0^L H \, dl$ | rad | Path-dependent, use when sensing H directly |
| 3 | Ampère's Law link | $\oint H \, dl = N I_p$ | A (turns) | **Position-independent** if loop is closed |
| 4 | Verdet constant (typical) | $V = \theta_F \, / \, (N \cdot I_p)$ | rad/A | Material property, $\lambda$-dependent |
| 5 | Pockels retardation (transverse) | $\Gamma = \pi n_0^{3} r L V \, / \, (\lambda d)$ | rad | Field along $\perp$ to light propagation |
| 6 | Half-wave voltage | $V_{\pi} = \lambda d \, / \, (n_0^{3} r L)$ | V | Crystal design parameter |
| 7 | Output intensity (MOCT) | $I_{out} = I_0 \cos^{2}(\theta_F + \phi_0)$ | W/m² | $\phi_0$ is bias angle (often 45°) |
| 8 | Sensitivity (MOPT) | $d\Gamma \, / \, dV = \pi n_0^{3} r L \, / \, (\lambda d)$ | rad/V | Higher for thin, long crystals with large r |
| 9 | Sensitivity (MOCT) | $d\theta_F \, / \, dI_p = V \cdot N$ | rad/A | Increase N to boost sensitivity |
| 10 | Sense check | $\theta_F \ll 1 \text{ rad} \Rightarrow$ linear regime | — | Otherwise $\sin\theta \neq \theta$ distortion |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]  
> **Industrial Applications of MOCT/MOPT in Power Systems:**  
> - **EHV substations (400 kV – 1200 kV):** For metering, protection (87, 51), and power-quality monitoring.  
> - **HVDC converter stations:** Optical CTs are the *only* way to measure DC currents (Faraday works on DC; iron-core CTs cannot).  
> - **GIS (Gas-Insulated Switchgear):** Optical sensors are intrinsically compatible with SF₆ environments.  
> - **Smart-grid process bus:** Direct digital output per IEC 61850-9-2 (Sampled Values).  
> - **UHV fault recorders:** Bandwidth > 100 kHz captures lightning strikes and high-frequency transients.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday Rotation from Maxwell's Equations (Conceptual Outline)

The rigorous derivation proceeds from the constitutive relation in a magnetized medium. We present the key physical steps:

### Step 1: Light as a superposition of circular polarizations
A linearly polarized wave propagating along **z** can be written as the sum of a left-circularly polarized (LCP) and a right-circularly polarized (RCP) component.

$$
\vec{E}(z,t) = \vec{E}_R(z,t) + \vec{E}_L(z,t)
$$

The two components experience *different* refractive indices $n_R$ and $n_L$ in a magnetized medium (Faraday discovered this experimentally in 1845).

### Step 2: Define the magneto-induced birefringence
The difference in indices is proportional to the applied magnetic flux density **B**:

$$
n_L - n_R = \Delta n = \frac{e^{2}}{2 m_e \varepsilon_0 c} \cdot \frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{\lambda^{2}} \cdot B
$$

where $e$ is the electron charge, $m_e$ the electron mass, $\lambda_0$ the natural resonance wavelength of the medium, $\lambda$ the optical wavelength, and $c$ the speed of light. This proportionality is the **physical origin of the Verdet constant**.

### Step 3: Compute the phase accumulation over a path of length L
After traversing length **L**, the two circular components accumulate a relative phase:

$$
\Delta \phi = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} (n_L - n_R) L = \frac{2 \pi \, \Delta n \, L}{\lambda}
$$

### Step 4: Relate the phase difference to polarization-plane rotation
The plane of polarization of the resultant linearly polarized wave rotates by *half* the relative phase difference between LCP and RCP components:

$$
\theta_F = \frac{\Delta \phi}{2} = \frac{\pi \, \Delta n \, L}{\lambda}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute the linear relation $\Delta n \propto B$ and define the Verdet constant
Collecting all material and wavelength dependencies into **V**:

$$
\theta_F = V \cdot B \cdot L
$$

Since $B = \mu_0 H$ in a non-magnetic optical medium, and for a closed loop $\oint H \, dl = N I_p$:

$$
\boxed{\theta_F = V \cdot \mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I_p}
$$

(In optical-engineering practice, **V** is often redefined to absorb the $\mu_0$ factor, yielding the compact form $\theta_F = V N I_p$.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pockels Effect Phase Retardation

### Step 1: Index ellipsoid in the presence of an electric field
The optical indicatrix of a crystal like LiNbO₃ is described by a $3 \times 3$ symmetric tensor $[\eta_{ij}]$ whose coefficients change linearly with applied field. For a transverse configuration (field $\perp$ to light propagation):

$$
\eta_{xx} - \eta_{yy} = \frac{2}{n_0^{3}} \cdot r \cdot E_z
$$

where $r$ is the relevant electro-optic tensor element and $E_z = V/d$.

### Step 2: Birefringence calculation
The change in refractive index for the two orthogonal polarization eigenmodes is:

$$
\Delta\!\left(\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right) = \frac{2}{n_0^{3}} \cdot r \cdot E
$$

Inverting (using the differential relation between index and $1/n^{2}$):

$$
\Delta n \approx \frac{n_0^{3}}{2} \cdot r \cdot E
$$

### Step 3: Phase retardation over crystal length L

$$
\Gamma = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta n \cdot L
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $E = V/d$ and simplify

$$
\Gamma = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{n_0^{3} r L}{2} \cdot \frac{V}{d} = \frac{\pi \, n_0^{3} r L \, V}{\lambda \, d}
$$

### Step 5: Define the half-wave voltage by setting $\Gamma = \pi$

$$
\pi = \frac{\pi \, n_0^{3} r L \, V_{\pi}}{\lambda \, d}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\pi} = \frac{\lambda \, d}{n_0^{3} \, r \, L}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Photodetector Output for MOCT (Malus's Law with Bias)

### Step 1: Apply Malus's Law
If the analyzer is oriented at a bias angle $\phi_0$ from the polarizer, and the Faraday rotation adds $\theta_F$ to the angle, the output intensity is:

$$
I_{out} = I_0 \, \cos^{2}(\phi_0 + \theta_F)
$$

### Step 2: Expand using the identity $\cos^{2}x = (1 + \cos 2x)/2$

$$
I_{out} = \frac{I_0}{2} \left[ 1 + \cos\!\big(2\phi_0 + 2\theta_F\big) \right]
$$

### Step 3: Choose the quarter-wave bias $\phi_0 = \pi/4$ (i.e., 45°)
This makes the signal swing symmetric about the linear region:

$$
I_{out} = \frac{I_0}{2} \left[ 1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2} + 2\theta_F\right) \right] = \frac{I_0}{2} \left[ 1 - \sin(2\theta_F) \right]
$$

### Step 4: For small signals $\theta_F \ll 1$, use $\sin(2\theta_F) \approx 2\theta_F$

$$
I_{out} \approx \frac{I_0}{2} \left[ 1 - 2\theta_F \right] = \frac{I_0}{2} - I_0 \theta_F
$$

### Step 5: AC-couple to remove the DC term, leaving

$$
i_{signal} \propto \theta_F \propto I_p
$$

Thus the photodetector output is **directly proportional to the primary current** — the ultimate goal of the MOCT.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A fused-silica MOCT uses $N = 10$ turns of optical fiber around a busbar carrying $I_p = 2000 \text{ A}$ (peak). The Verdet constant of fused silica at the operating wavelength is $V = 4.1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad/A}$. Find the peak Faraday rotation.

**Solution:**

Using the governing equation:

$$
\theta_F = V \cdot N \cdot I_p
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\theta_F = (4.1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10 \times 2000
$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:

$$
\theta_F = 4.1 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{4} = 8.2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_F \approx 0.082 \text{ rad} \approx 4.7^{\circ}}
$$

Since $0.082 \ll 1$, the small-signal approximation is valid and the photodetector output is linear in $I_p$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MOCT — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HV Busbar Carrying Primary Current I_p] --> B[Magnetic Field H Around Conductor]
    B --> C[Optical Fiber Wound N Turns Around Conductor]
    D[LED Light Source 850 nm] --> E[Linear Polarizer Glan Taylor]
    E --> F[Multimode Coupler to Sensing Fiber]
    F --> C
    C --> G[Return Coupler]
    G --> H[Analyzer Polarizer at 45 deg Bias]
    H --> I[Photodetector PIN Diode]
    I --> J[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    J --> K[Analog to Digital Converter]
    K --> L[Digital Signal Processing Unit DSP]
    L --> M[Output Current Proportional to I_p]

    style A fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#cde,stroke:#333
    style M fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
```

**Description of signal flow:** The LED emits unpolarized light, which the linear polarizer converts to a single linearly polarized state. This is routed via an optical coupler to the sensing fiber wound around the HV busbar. The Faraday effect rotates the polarization plane by $\theta_F = V N I_p$. The light returns via a second coupler, passes through the analyzer, and falls on the photodetector, where Malus's law converts the rotation into a proportional electrical current.

## 4.2 MOPT — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HV Phase Conductor] --> B[Capacitive Voltage Divider]
    B --> C[Reduced Voltage V Applied Across Crystal]
    C --> D[Electro Optic Crystal BGO or LiNbO3]
    E[LED 850 nm Stable Source] --> F[Linear Polarizer at 45 deg to Crystal Axes]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Quarter Wave Plate Compensator]
    G --> H[Analyzer Polarizer Crossed at 90 deg]
    H --> I[Photodetector APD or PIN]
    I --> J[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    J --> K[Analog to Digital Converter]
    K --> L[DSP Unit and Temperature Compensation]
    L --> M[Output Voltage Proportional to V_phase]

    style A fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#cde,stroke:#333
    style M fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
```

**Description of signal flow:** The HV phase voltage is capacitively divided to a safe level and applied across the electro-optic crystal. Linearly polarized light at 45° to the principal axes enters the crystal; the Pockels effect introduces a phase retardation $\Gamma \propto V$ between the two polarization eigenmodes. The quarter-wave plate compensates for natural birefringence drift; the analyzer converts phase modulation to intensity modulation; the photodetector delivers an analog signal proportional to the phase voltage.

## 4.3 Combined MOCT + MOPT — Integrated Substation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HV [High Voltage Side 400 kV or Higher]
        HV1[Phase R Conductor] 
        HV2[Phase Y Conductor]
        HV3[Phase B Conductor]
    end

    HV1 --> MOCT1[MOCT Fiber Loop around R]
    HV2 --> MOCT2[MOCT Fiber Loop around Y]
    HV3 --> MOCT3[MOCT Fiber Loop around B]

    HV1 --> MOPT1[MOPT Crystal Bank R]
    HV2 --> MOPT2[MOPT Crystal Bank Y]
    HV3 --> MOPT3[MOPT Crystal Bank B]

    MOCT1 --> OCU1[Optical Combining Unit]
    MOCT2 --> OCU1
    MOCT3 --> OCU1
    MOPT1 --> OCU1
    MOPT2 --> OCU1
    MOPT3 --> OCU1

    OCU1 --> OFC[Optical Fiber Cable to Control Room]
    OFC --> PCD[Photodetector and Signal Conditioning Rack]
    PCD --> MER[Merging Unit IEC 61850 9 2]
    MER --> IED1[Protection IED 87 Differential]
    MER --> IED2[Metering IED]
    MER --> IED3[Phasor Measurement Unit PMU]

    style HV fill:#fee,stroke:#900
    style PCD fill:#efe,stroke:#060
    style IED1 fill:#eef,stroke:#006
    style IED2 fill:#eef,stroke:#006
    style IED3 fill:#eef,stroke:#006
```

**Engineering Note:** This integrated architecture shows how MOCTs and MOPTs feed a single Merging Unit, which then publishes *Sampled Values* (SV) over the process bus to all downstream IEDs. This is the canonical digital-substation architecture for KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 / IEC 61850 aligned designs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the Faraday Effect. How is it used in a Magneto-Optic Current Transformer?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Faraday Effect** is the rotation of the plane of polarization of a linearly polarized light wave as it propagates through a transparent optical medium in the presence of a magnetic field parallel to the direction of propagation. The rotation angle is given by:

$$
\theta_F = V \cdot \int_0^L \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = V \cdot N \cdot I_p
$$

In an **MOCT**, an optical fiber (or gas-filled sensing cell) is wound **N** times around the HV current-carrying conductor. The current $I_p$ produces a magnetic field, which rotates the polarization of light traveling in the fiber. An analyzer and photodetector convert this rotation into an electrical signal proportional to $I_p$. (3 marks)

---

### Question A.2 — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**State the Pockels Effect and write the expression for half-wave voltage of an electro-optic crystal.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Pockels Effect** is the linear electro-optic effect in which an applied electric field induces birefringence in a non-centrosymmetric crystal, causing a phase retardation between two orthogonal polarization components of light passing through it.

The phase retardation for transverse configuration is:

$$
\Gamma = \frac{\pi n_0^{3} r L V}{\lambda d}
$$

The **half-wave voltage** $V_{\pi}$ (the voltage required for $\Gamma = \pi$) is:

$$
V_{\pi} = \frac{\lambda d}{n_0^{3} r L}
$$

In an MOPT, this relationship is used to measure high voltage by converting voltage into optical phase shift. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question B-A — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Magneto-Optic Current Transformer. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Block diagram** (refer to SECTION 4.1 of these notes for the complete architecture).

**Working Principle — Step by Step:**

1. **Light source:** A temperature-stabilized LED (typically 850 nm) emits a stable, low-coherence optical beam. LEDs are preferred over laser diodes because they are insensitive to back-reflections and have wider spectral tolerance.

2. **Polarization:** The light is passed through a high-extinction-ratio Glan–Taylor linear polarizer, which forces the output to be 100% linearly polarized along a single axis.

3. **Coupling to sensing fiber:** A multimode fiber coupler routes the polarized light to a sensing fiber (or sensing gas cell) wound **N** turns around the HV busbar.

4. **Faraday rotation:** The current $I_p$ in the busbar produces a circumferential magnetic field $H = I_p / (2\pi r)$. By Ampère's circuital law applied to a closed loop:

$$
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = N I_p
$$

The Faraday rotation is then:

$$
\theta_F = V \cdot N \cdot I_p
$$

where **V** is the Verdet constant of the sensing medium.

5. **Return path and analyzer:** The light is coupled back, passes through an analyzer polarizer set at a 45° bias angle, and falls on a PIN photodiode.

6. **Photodetection (Malus's law):**

$$
I_{out} = I_0 \cos^{2}(\pi/4 + \theta_F) = \tfrac{I_0}{2} \left[ 1 - \sin(2\theta_F) \right]
$$

For small $\theta_F$, $i_{signal} \propto \theta_F \propto I_p$.

7. **Signal processing:** Transimpedance amplifier, ADC, DSP, and digital output (typically IEC 61850-9-2 SV stream).

**Advantages:** Wide bandwidth, no saturation, DC-capable, intrinsically safe, digital-friendly. (7 marks)

#### (b) Derive the expression for Faraday rotation and explain why the optical loop must be a *closed* path. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

The plane of polarization rotates by an angle proportional to the line integral of the magnetic field along the optical path:

$$
\theta_F = V \int_0^L \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}
$$

For a uniform field over a path length **L**:

$$
\theta_F = V \cdot H \cdot L
$$

If the optical path is wound **N** times around a straight conductor, each turn contributes an equal amount:

$$
\theta_F = V \cdot H \cdot N \cdot (2\pi r) = V \cdot N \cdot I_p
$$

(using $H \cdot 2\pi r = I_p$ for a single turn).

**Why the loop must be closed:**

The Verdet constant relation uses the *line integral* of **H**. If the loop is **open**, the result depends on the position and shape of the fiber — making calibration impossible. By Ampère's law:

$$
\oint_{closed} \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = N I_p
$$

For a *closed* path encircling the conductor **N** times, the integral is independent of the exact geometry (radius, shape, twists) and depends *only* on $N I_p$. This is what makes the MOCT measurement *position-independent* and robust to mechanical vibrations, sagging, or thermal expansion. (7 marks)

---

### Question B-B — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

#### (a) Describe the principle of operation of a Magneto-Optic Potential Transformer. Derive the expression for phase retardation in a Pockels crystal. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of operation:**

A Magneto-Optic (or more accurately, Electro-Optic) Potential Transformer uses the **Pockels Effect** in a non-centrosymmetric crystal (BGO, LiNbO₃). The high voltage to be measured is capacitively divided and applied across the crystal, producing an internal electric field $E = V/d$. This field induces birefringence in the crystal, so a linearly polarized light beam passing through the crystal emerges with a *phase difference* between its two orthogonal polarization components that is linearly proportional to the applied voltage.

**Derivation:**

Consider a transverse modulator: light propagates along **y**, electric field **E** is along **z**, crystal length along **y** is **L**, and the inter-electrode spacing is **d**.

The applied field is $E = V/d$.

The field-induced change in the index ellipsoid gives a birefringence:

$$
\Delta n = \tfrac{1}{2} n_0^{3} r E
$$

The two orthogonal polarization components travel with different speeds and accumulate a phase difference:

$$
\Gamma = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta n L = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{n_0^{3} r L}{2} \cdot \frac{V}{d}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Gamma = \frac{\pi n_0^{3} r L V}{\lambda d}}
$$

The half-wave voltage is obtained by setting $\Gamma = \pi$:

$$
V_{\pi} = \frac{\lambda d}{n_0^{3} r L}
$$

(7 marks)

#### (b) Compare MOCT and MOPT on the basis of: (i) physical effect used, (ii) sensing material, (iii) measurement quantity, (iv) typical application, (v) main limitation. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | MOCT | MOPT |
|---------|-----------|------|------|
| (i) | Physical effect | **Faraday Effect** (Magneto-Optic) | **Pockels Effect** (Electro-Optic) |
| (ii) | Sensing material | Fused silica, SF₆, flint glass, optical fiber | BGO, LiNbO₃, BSO single crystals |
| (iii) | Measurement quantity | Primary current $I_p$ | Primary phase voltage $V_{phase}$ |
| (iv) | Typical application | EHV/HVDC current measurement, bus-bar monitoring, 87 differential | EHV voltage measurement, metering, capacitor-bank switching |
| (v) | Main limitation | Low Verdet constant → small $\theta_F$ → need high-N, sensitive detectors; temperature drift of V | Natural birefringence, temperature-dependent $r$, high drive voltage for low-sensitivity crystals |

(7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in MOCT/MOPT Questions:**  
> 1. **Confusing Faraday with Kerr effect:** The Kerr effect is *quadratic* in E and is *not* used in MOCTs. Writing "Kerr effect" instead of "Faraday effect" → lose 1–2 marks.  
> 2. **Forgetting the "closed-loop" requirement:** Many students write $\theta_F = V H L$ without explaining that the *closed* path makes the result geometry-independent. Examiners explicitly look for this.  
> 3. **Mixing up Verdet and electro-optic coefficients:** Verdet **V** is for magnetic field; electro-optic **r** is for electric field. Don't substitute one into the other's formula.  
> 4. **Omitting the bias angle:** In the photodetection derivation, the 45° analyzer bias (quarter-wave bias) is essential for linear operation. Skipping it is a 1-mark loss.  
> 5. **Wrong units:** Verdet constant in rad/A, not rad/T (unless explicitly stated as B-field).  
> 6. **Sign convention:** Faraday rotation direction is *non-reciprocal* — flipping the current direction flips the rotation, but flipping the light direction does *not* undo the rotation. This is the *physical basis* of optical isolation. Examiners love this trick question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MOCT = Faraday Effect** (magnetic field rotates polarization): $\theta_F = V N I_p$.
- **MOPT = Pockels Effect** (electric field induces birefringence): $\Gamma = \pi n_0^{3} r L V / (\lambda d)$.
- The **closed optical loop** is non-negotiable for position-independent MOCT measurement.
- **Verdet constant V** is the magneto-optic equivalent of susceptibility; units rad/A.
- **Half-wave voltage $V_{\pi}$** characterizes a Pockels crystal: lower $V_{\pi}$ = more sensitive device.
- Use a **quarter-wave (45°) bias** in the analyzer to linearize the photodetector response.
- Optical CTs measure **DC** — a unique advantage in HVDC; iron-core CTs cannot.
- Optical PTs are limited by **temperature-dependent natural birefringence** — compensation is critical.
- **BGO** is the industrial-standard MOPT crystal due to zero natural birefringence.
- Optical transducers have **bandwidth from DC to hundreds of kHz** — ideal for power-quality and protection.
- They interface directly to **IEC 61850-9-2 Sampled Values** for digital substations.
- **Reciprocity theorem exception:** Faraday rotation is non-reciprocal — a cornerstone of optical isolators.
- KTU 2024 emphasizes **digital substation architecture** — know the merging unit, process bus, and SV stream.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect on Relaying Philosophy. Relays: Over Current Relays - Time-Current Characteristics of Over Current Relays: Definite Time Over Current Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect on Relaying Philosophy. Relays: Over Current Relays - Time-Current Characteristics of Over Current Relays: Definite Time Over Current Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Effect of Faults on Relaying Philosophy

**Relaying Philosophy** refers to the systematic engineering strategy employed to detect, classify, and isolate abnormal conditions (faults) in a power system while maintaining selectivity, speed, sensitivity, and reliability. When a fault occurs in a power system, several electrical parameters deviate from their nominal values, forcing the relaying philosophy to react decisively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Relaying Philosophy Postulate (KTU 2024 Module-1 Highlight):**
> A protective relay must satisfy four cardinal properties — **Speed, Selectivity, Sensitivity, and Reliability (the 4-S Rule)**. The presence of a fault disturbs the pre-fault equilibrium and dictates a coordinated trip-command sequence from primary to backup protection zones.

### Quantified Effect of Faults on System Quantities

| System Quantity | Pre-Fault Condition | During Fault | Effect on Relay Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| Line Current $I$ | Rated / Full Load | 5× to 20× rated | Over-current element picks up |
| Bus Voltage $V$ | 1.0 pu | 0.0 – 0.5 pu | Under-voltage element assists |
| Phase Angle | Balanced ±120° | Unbalanced | Negative / Zero sequence detect |
| Frequency $f$ | 50 Hz (India) | May deviate | df/dt element activates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The Highway Toll-Booth):**
> Imagine a multi-lane highway where vehicles normally cruise at a steady speed (rated current). When an accident occurs downstream (a fault), traffic piles up rapidly — *this pile-up is the fault current*. The toll-booth operator (the relay) is trained to detect unusual density of vehicles (current magnitude) and automatically close the gate (trip the breaker) to prevent further chaos upstream. Just as a good operator distinguishes between a momentary slow-down and a real pile-up, a well-designed relay distinguishes between inrush transients and genuine faults using time-current logic.

## 1.2 Over-Current Relays (OCR) — Formal Definition

An **Over-Current Relay (OCR)** is a protective device that initiates a trip signal whenever the magnitude of the operating current exceeds a predetermined threshold (the **Pickup Value**, $I_{pickup}$) for a specified duration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:**
> "An over-current relay is a protective relay whose operating quantity is a function of the magnitude of the current in the protected circuit, and which operates when this current exceeds a preset value."

**Classification of Over-Current Relays by Time-Current Behaviour:**

1. **Definite Time Over-Current Relay (DTOC)**
2. **Inverse Definite Minimum Time (IDMT) Relay**
3. **Very Inverse Relay**
4. **Extremely Inverse Relay**
5. **Inverse Relay**

## 1.3 Definite Time Over-Current Relay (DTOC) — Formal Definition

A **Definite Time Over-Current Relay** is one whose **operating time is constant and independent of the magnitude of the fault current**, provided the fault current is above the pickup threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Syllabus Highlight Box:**
> The defining equation of a DTOC relay is:
>
> $$t_{op} = T \quad \text{(constant)} \quad \forall \; I_{fault} \geq I_{pickup}$$
>
> where $t_{op}$ is the operating time and $T$ is the **Time Setting** of the relay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The Fire Alarm with a Fixed Delay):**
> Picture a smoke detector in a hotel corridor. Once smoke crosses the alarm's sensitivity threshold (pickup), the alarm does NOT ring immediately — it waits a *fixed* 30 seconds (the definite time) before sounding, to avoid nuisance from cooking fumes. Similarly, a DTOC relay waits for a *fixed* time delay $T$ after the current exceeds pickup, regardless of how big the over-current is. A tiny overload and a massive short-circuit both wait the *same* time before tripping.

### Operating Philosophy of DTOC

- **Pickup Condition:** $I_{fault} \geq I_{pickup}$
- **Time Logic:** When pickup is satisfied, a **dashpot timer / digital counter** starts.
- **Trip Command:** After a pre-set time $T$ elapses, the trip coil is energised.
- **Reset:** When current falls below pickup, the timer resets automatically.

### Constructional Elements of a DTOC Relay

| Element | Function |
|---|---|
| **Current Sensing Coil** | Measures line current via CT secondary |
| **Pickup Element** | Compares $I$ with $I_{pickup}$ threshold |
| **Timing Element** | Dashpot, clockwork, or digital counter producing delay $T$ |
| **Trip Output Contact** | Closes CB trip circuit after delay $T$ |
| **Indicating Flag** | Mechanically shows relay has operated |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

The DTOC relay operates through a deterministic, sequential logic chain. The decision flow is:

1. **Current Sensing Stage:** The current transformer (CT) steps down the primary current $I_p$ to a secondary current:
   $$I_s = \frac{I_p}{N_{CT}}$$
   where $N_{CT}$ is the CT ratio.

2. **Pickup Comparison Stage:** The relay coil energises when the secondary current exceeds the pickup value:
   $$I_s \geq I_{pickup} \equiv \frac{I_{pickup,primary}}{N_{CT}}$$

3. **Actuation of Timer:** Once pickup is achieved, the **definite-time timer** is latched into action. The relay **ignores** any further increase in current magnitude.

4. **Time-Delay Generation:** The timer produces a fixed time delay $T$, irrespective of fault severity. The instantaneous value of the operating quantity is therefore decoupled from the time coordinate.

5. **Trip Output:** At the instant $t = T$, the trip contacts close, sending a trip pulse to the circuit-breaker trip coil.

6. **Breaker Operation:** The circuit-breaker opens, isolating the faulted section.

7. **Reset:** When the system is restored (or fault cleared), the relay resets to its quiescent state.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why is the time constant even when the fault is huge?"** — *Engineering rationale:* This deliberate decoupling is chosen to provide **simple, predictable co-ordination** with downstream fuses and other relays. Because the time is fixed, the discrimination margin with the next upstream relay is the simple algebraic difference $T_{backup} - T_{primary}$, which is easy to set in the field.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations govern DTOC relay design and co-ordination. All are required for KTU 2024 ESE problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{op} = T = \text{const}$ | Operating time of DTOC relay | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $I_{pickup} = \text{PSM} \times I_{base}$ | Pickup current setting | Amperes (A) |
| 3 | $\text{PSM} = \dfrac{I_{fault}}{I_{pickup}}$ | Plug Setting Multiplier | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T = \text{TMS} \times t_{0.1}$ | Total operating time relation (general) | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $\Delta t = t_{backup} - t_{primary}$ | Discrimination time interval | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $t_{CB} = 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \, s$ | Circuit-breaker interrupting time | seconds (s) |
| 7 | $t_{o} = 0.1 \text{ to } 0.3 \, s$ | Relay overshoot time | seconds (s) |
| 8 | $\text{CT ratio} = \dfrac{I_{primary}}{I_{secondary}}$ | Turns ratio of current transformer | A/A |
| 9 | $V_{relay} = I_s \cdot Z_{coil}$ | Voltage developed across relay coil | Volts (V) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note (Exam Safety):** The DTOC relay time formula $t_{op} = T$ is **independent of PSM**. Many students wrongly apply IDMT-type empirical equations (e.g., $t = \frac{0.14 \cdot \text{TMS}}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}$) to DTOC relays — this is a **valuation trap** and will cost marks.

## 2.3 Time-Current Characteristic (TCC) Curve of DTOC

The **Time-Current Characteristic** is a plot of operating time $t_{op}$ on the Y-axis versus multiples of pickup current (PSM) on the X-axis.

For a DTOC relay, the TCC is a **horizontal straight line** at $t_{op} = T$ for all $\text{PSM} \geq 1$. At $\text{PSM} < 1$ the relay does not operate at all.

**Discrimination Logic with Fuses:**

A DTOC relay is typically coordinated with a downstream fuse using a vertical-stacked TCC. The relay time setting $T$ is chosen so that for the **maximum fuse blow time**, the relay curve lies strictly above the fuse curve, providing **time-grading discrimination** of approximately $\Delta t \geq 0.4 \, s$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of DTOC Relays

- **Backup Protection:** Used as the second line of defence behind a primary distance / differential relay.
- **Busbar Protection:** Coordinated with incoming/outgoing feeder relays.
- **Distribution Feeders (11 kV / 33 kV radial lines):** Cost-effective grading.
- **Motor and Transformer Back-up:** Where inverse-time characteristic is unsuitable.
- **Industrial Switchgear:** LV and MV panels where the time-current grading must be explicit and human-readable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Context (Digital Relays — KTU 2024):** In numerical/digital relays, the DTOC function is implemented in software as a counter incremented at a fixed clock rate once the comparator flag is set. The relay's numerical setting is typically called **Time Delay Setting (TDS)** instead of TMS, but the philosophy is mathematically identical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Operating Time Logic of DTOC Relay

### Step 1: Express the Pickup Threshold in Primary Quantities

The relay pickup in the secondary winding is fixed by the relay's design as $I_{pickup,sec}$. Referred to the primary side of the CT:

$$I_{pickup,pri} = N_{CT} \cdot I_{pickup,sec}$$

**Textual Logic:** Since the CT steps the primary current *down* by the ratio $N_{CT}$, a larger primary current is needed to induce the same pickup current in the secondary. The pickup threshold is therefore scaled by the CT turns ratio.

### Step 2: Define the Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)

The actual fault current expressed as a multiple of the pickup is the **Plug Setting Multiplier**:

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{fault,pri}}{I_{pickup,pri}}$$

**Textual Logic:** A fault that produces exactly the pickup current corresponds to PSM = 1. A fault 10× larger gives PSM = 10. The PSM is a *dimensionless magnification factor* of the fault relative to pickup.

### Step 3: Apply the DTOC Defining Equation

The defining behaviour of the DTOC relay is:

$$t_{op} = T \quad \text{for} \quad \text{PSM} \geq 1$$

**Textual Logic:** This is *not* a derived relation — it is the *definition* of the DTOC class. It states that the operating time is **independent** of PSM once the pickup is exceeded. Therefore, the time-derivative:

$$\frac{dt_{op}}{d(\text{PSM})} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \text{PSM} \geq 1$$

**Textual Logic:** The horizontal slope on the TCC is the *mathematical signature* of DTOC. Compare this with IDMT, where the slope is negative (time decreases as fault current grows).

### Step 4: Co-ordination Time Margin (Discrimination)

For two DTOC relays R1 (primary) and R2 (backup), the time grading is:

$$T_2 = T_1 + \Delta t$$

where the discrimination margin $\Delta t$ is the sum of four time-lags:

$$\Delta t = t_{CB} + t_o + t_{margin} + t_{relay\,reset}$$

Plugging in standard KTU-design values:

$$\Delta t = (0.05) + (0.1) + (0.1) + (0.05) = 0.30 \, s$$

Hence, the time-step between successive relays is typically chosen as:

$$\Delta t \approx 0.4 \, s \quad \text{(with engineering safety factor)}$$

**Textual Logic:** Each successive upstream DTOC relay waits an additional 0.3 to 0.4 s longer than the relay immediately downstream. This guarantees a *time-graded cascade* in which only the relay closest to the fault trips first.

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 11 kV, 50 Hz radial feeder has a DTOC relay at the sending end. The CT ratio is 200/5 A. The relay is set to pickup at 125% of the feeder rated current of 200 A. The maximum fault current is 4000 A. The relay is required to coordinate with a downstream relay set at 0.3 s. Calculate: (a) Pickup current in primary and secondary. (b) PSM for the maximum fault. (c) Time setting $T$ for co-ordination with $\Delta t = 0.4 \, s$.

#### Part (a): Pickup Current

Primary pickup current:

$$I_{pickup,pri} = 1.25 \times 200 = 250 \, \text{A}$$

Secondary pickup current:

$$I_{pickup,sec} = \frac{250}{200/5} = \frac{250}{40} = 6.25 \, \text{A}$$

#### Part (b): Plug Setting Multiplier

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{fault,pri}}{I_{pickup,pri}} = \frac{4000}{250} = 16$$

#### Part (c): Time Setting for Co-ordination

$$T_2 = T_1 + \Delta t = 0.3 + 0.4 = 0.7 \, \text{s}$$

**Result:** The sending-end DTOC relay must be set at $T = 0.7 \, s$ to grade 0.4 s above the downstream relay.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DTOC vs IDMT

| Attribute | DTOC | IDMT |
|---|---|---|
| **Time-Current Curve** | Horizontal line | Inverse hyperbola |
| **Operating Time at PSM = 1** | $T$ (longest) | $\infty$ (theoretical) |
| **Operating Time at PSM = 10** | $T$ (same) | $\sim 0.3 \cdot \text{TMS}$ |
| **Sensitivity to PSM** | Zero | Strong (negative slope) |
| **Discrimination with Fuses** | Excellent | Poor at high currents |
| **Co-ordination Complexity** | Very Simple | Moderate |
| **CT Saturation Effect** | Tolerant | Sensitive |
| **Field of Application** | Industrial / LV backup | Transmission lines / feeders |
| **KTU Numeric Setting** | Time Delay Setting (TDS) | Time Multiplier Setting (TMS) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a horizontal line?** A horizontal line in a TCC means the relay has *no* "urgency bias" — it does not speed up for larger faults. This is mathematically expressed as $dt/dI = 0$, which is the dual of an IDMT relay whose $dt/dI < 0$. The horizontal signature is the **diagnostic feature** examiners use to identify DTOC problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a DTOC Relay (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the internal signal-flow of a digital Definite Time Over-Current Relay, since the physical construction involves a moving-iron plunger that is cumbersome to draw.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Primary Line Current] --> B[Current Transformer CT]
    B --> C[Secondary Current Is]
    C --> D[Rectifier Bridge]
    D --> E[Pickup Comparator]
    E --> F{Pickup Condition}
    F -- No --> G[Reset State]
    F -- Yes --> H[Set Latch Flag]
    H --> I[Digital Counter Starts]
    I --> J[Time Setting T Loaded]
    J --> K{Counter = T}
    K -- No --> I
    K -- Yes --> L[Trip Coil Energised]
    L --> M[Circuit Breaker Trips]
    M --> N[Fault Isolated]
    N --> O[Auto Reset]
    O --> G
```

**Reading the Flow:**
- The CT samples the primary current.
- The rectifier converts AC to proportional DC.
- The comparator checks the **pickup condition** $I_s \geq I_{pickup}$.
- On satisfaction, a **latch flag** is set and the **digital counter** starts.
- The counter increments at a clock rate and after exactly $T$ seconds, the trip coil is energised.
- The CB opens, isolating the fault.

## 4.2 Time-Current Characteristic Curve of DTOC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TCC[Time-Current Characteristic]
        direction LR
        X[PSM Axis] --> P1[PSM = 1]
        P1 --> P2[PSM = 5]
        P2 --> P3[PSM = 10]
        P3 --> P4[PSM = 20]
        Y[Time Axis] --> T1[T = 0.5 s]
        T1 --> T2[T = 0.7 s]
    end
    DTH[DTOC Horizontal Line at T] -.-> P1
    DTH -.-> P2
    DTH -.-> P3
    DTH -.-> P4
    IDMTC[IDMT Inverse Curve] --- P1
    IDMTC --- P2
    IDMTC --- P3
    IDMTC --- P4
```

**Reading the Flow:**
- The **DTOC curve** is a **horizontal straight line** at $t = T$ across all PSM values $\geq 1$.
- The **IDMT curve** descends steeply as PSM grows, asymptotically approaching a minimum definite time.
- The horizontal nature of the DTOC curve is the **mathematical signature** distinguishing it from inverse-time relays.

## 4.3 Co-ordination Cascade of DTOC Relays

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Source[Source Bus] --> R1[Relay R1: T1 = 0.3 s]
    R1 --> F1[Feeder 1]
    R1 --> R2[Relay R2: T2 = 0.7 s]
    R2 --> F2[Feeder 2]
    R2 --> R3[Relay R3: T3 = 1.1 s]
    R3 --> F3[Feeder 3]
    F1 -.-> FLT1[Fault on F1: trips R1 only]
    F2 -.-> FLT2[Fault on F2: R2 trips after R1 fails]
    F3 -.-> FLT3[Fault on F3: R3 trips after R2 fails]
```

**Reading the Flow:**
- Time-graded cascade: each upstream relay waits 0.4 s longer.
- This guarantees **selectivity**: only the relay nearest to the fault operates first.
- If the primary relay fails, the backup relay takes over after the discrimination delay.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define a Definite Time Over-Current (DTOC) relay. Why is its time-current characteristic a horizontal line?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A DTOC relay is one in which the operating time is **constant and independent of the magnitude of fault current** once the current exceeds the pickup value. Its time-current characteristic is a **horizontal straight line** because the operating time $t_{op} = T$ does not vary with the plug setting multiplier (PSM), i.e., $dt_{op}/d(\text{PSM}) = 0$. This constant time is achieved by a dashpot / digital counter that begins timing the instant the pickup element operates and triggers the trip coil after a fixed delay $T$ regardless of how large the fault current is. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Mention any three applications of a Definite Time Over-Current relay in a power system."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Backup protection** of transformers, generators, and transmission lines where the primary relay is a differential or distance relay. **(1 mark)**
2. **Busbar protection** in HV substations as the second zone of defence. **(1 mark)**
3. **LV/MV distribution feeders** where the system is radial and time-graded co-ordination with downstream fuses is required. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**"Explain in detail the operating principle of a Definite Time Over-Current (DTOC) relay with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss its time-current characteristic and derive the co-ordination time interval between two DTOC relays in cascade. A 11 kV radial feeder is protected by a DTOC relay with CT ratio 400/5 A, pickup at 150% of rated current 250 A, and time setting 0.6 s. Calculate: (a) the PSM for a fault of 5000 A; (b) the time setting of the upstream backup relay if the discrimination margin is 0.4 s." [14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — Operating Principle, Block Diagram & Time-Current Characteristic [7 Marks]

**Principle:** A DTOC relay senses the line current through a CT, compares it with a preset pickup value, and produces a **fixed time delay $T$** between the instant of pickup and the closing of the trip contact. The magnitude of the fault current beyond pickup has no influence on the time delay. **(2 marks)**

**Block Diagram (as in Section 4.1 above):**
The functional flow is: Primary Current → CT → Rectifier → Pickup Comparator → Latch → Digital Counter → Trip Coil → Circuit Breaker. **(2 marks)**

**Time-Current Characteristic:** The TCC is a horizontal straight line at $t_{op} = T$ for all PSM $\geq 1$, mathematically expressed as $dt_{op}/d(\text{PSM}) = 0$. The curve has *zero slope*, which is the diagnostic feature. **(1 mark)**

**Co-ordination Time Interval Derivation:** For two relays R1 (primary) and R2 (backup) in cascade:

$$\Delta t = t_{CB} + t_o + t_{margin} + t_{relay\,reset}$$

Substituting standard KTU-design values:

$$\Delta t = 0.05 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.30 \, s$$

With an engineering safety margin, the discrimination interval is chosen as $\Delta t \approx 0.4 \, s$. **(2 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- System voltage = 11 kV, 50 Hz
- CT ratio = 400/5 A
- Rated current $I_{rated} = 250$ A
- Pickup = 150% of rated current
- Time setting of primary relay $T_1 = 0.6$ s
- Fault current = 5000 A
- Discrimination margin $\Delta t = 0.4$ s

**Step 1: Primary Pickup Current**

$$I_{pickup,pri} = 1.50 \times 250 = 375 \, \text{A} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2: Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)**

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{fault}}{I_{pickup,pri}} = \frac{5000}{375} = 13.33 \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Time Setting of Backup Relay**

$$T_2 = T_1 + \Delta t = 0.6 + 0.4 = 1.0 \, \text{s} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4: Verification of Operating Time Independence**

Since the relay is DTOC, the operating time remains $T_2 = 1.0$ s even at PSM = 13.33. This confirms the defining property of DTOC. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5: Secondary Reference Current**

$$I_{pickup,sec} = \frac{375}{400/5} = \frac{375}{80} = 4.6875 \, \text{A} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Final Answer:** PSM = 13.33, Backup relay time setting $T_2 = 1.0$ s.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]

**"Compare the Time-Current Characteristics of Definite Time, Inverse, Very Inverse, and IDMT over-current relays. With the help of a sketch, explain why DTOC relays are preferred for backup protection and for co-ordination with fuses. A DTOC relay has a pickup of 5 A in a 100/5 A CT circuit. The relay must operate for all currents above 600 A in primary. (a) Find the plug setting multiplier when the fault current is 5000 A. (b) If the time setting is 0.5 s, what is the operating time of the relay? (c) If the discrimination margin with the downstream relay is 0.4 s, find the time setting of the next upstream relay." [14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — Comparison of Time-Current Characteristics [7 Marks]

| Relay Type | Equation Form | Behaviour at High Current |
|---|---|---|
| **Definite Time** | $t_{op} = T$ | Constant time |
| **Inverse** | $t = \dfrac{K \cdot \text{TMS}}{\text{PSM}^n}$ with $n \approx 0.02$ | Slow reduction |
| **Very Inverse** | $n \approx 1.0$ | Faster reduction |
| **IDMT** | $t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot \text{TMS}}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}$ | Asymptotic to definite minimum time |

**(3 marks for the table and equation forms)**

**Explanation:**
- A **Definite Time** curve is a horizontal line, hence independent of current magnitude. **(1 mark)**
- An **Inverse** curve falls gradually; useful where fault current magnitude varies widely. **(1 mark)**
- A **Very Inverse** curve falls more steeply — suited for lines with high source impedance. **(1 mark)**
- An **IDMT** curve approaches a definite minimum time asymptotically. **(1 mark)**

**Sketch (Mermaid Description):**
A four-curve plot on a TCC with PSM on X-axis and time on Y-axis. The DTOC line lies flat at the top, the IDMT curve dips down asymptotically, and the inverse / very inverse curves fall between them. **(0 marks — text explanation suffices; the diagram in Section 4.2 is the visual reference.)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- CT ratio = 100/5 A
- Pickup current (secondary) = 5 A
- Primary pickup current = 600 A (from the statement "operates for all currents above 600 A primary")
- Time setting = 0.5 s
- Fault current = 5000 A
- Discrimination margin = 0.4 s

**Step 1: Verify the Pickup Current**

The CT secondary pickup is 5 A. Referred to primary:

$$I_{pickup,pri} = 5 \times \frac{100}{5} = 100 \, \text{A}$$

But the question states the relay must operate for all currents above 600 A primary. This is a slight wording variation; in standard KTU problems, we take the given 600 A as the effective primary pickup. The CT ratio is 100/5 = 20, so the secondary pickup would be:

$$I_{pickup,sec} = \frac{600}{20} = 30 \, \text{A}$$

**(Note: The problem statement treats the secondary pickup as 5 A and the primary pickup as 600 A. The 100/5 CT gives a ratio of 20, so the *implied* secondary pickup from 600 A is 30 A. We accept the given 600 A primary as the pickup threshold for the calculation.)** **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)**

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{fault,pri}}{I_{pickup,pri}} = \frac{5000}{600} = 8.33 \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Operating Time of DTOC Relay**

$$t_{op} = T = 0.5 \, \text{s}$$

Since the relay is DTOC, the operating time is **independent of PSM**, hence it remains $0.5$ s even at PSM = 8.33. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4: Time Setting of Upstream Relay**

$$T_{upstream} = T_{downstream} + \Delta t = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9 \, \text{s} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Final Answer:** PSM = 8.33, Operating time = 0.5 s, Upstream relay time setting = 0.9 s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The most common mistake** in DTOC problems is to apply IDMT-style empirical equations ($t = 0.14 \cdot \text{TMS} / (\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1)$). **This is wrong** — DTOC relays do not have such a dependence. The operating time is **always** $T$ for $\text{PSM} \geq 1$. Examiners *intentionally* include PSM in the data to test whether students incorrectly insert it into an IDMT formula. **(Loss: 2–3 marks)**
> 2. **Discrimination margin trap:** Students often forget to add the **circuit-breaker interrupting time** (typically 0.05 s) and the **relay overshoot time** (0.1 s) into the discrimination margin. The KTU-recommended $\Delta t$ is $\geq 0.4$ s, not $0.3$ s. **(Loss: 1–2 marks)**
> 3. **Pickup vs PSM confusion:** Pickup is a *threshold*; PSM is a *multiplier*. Examiners frequently test whether the student correctly computes $I_{pickup,pri}$ first and only then divides the fault current by it. **(Loss: 1 mark)**
> 4. **CT ratio misapplication:** Always convert primary to secondary (or vice-versa) using the ratio BEFORE writing the PSM. The PSM is fundamentally a *primary-side* dimensionless quantity. **(Loss: 1 mark)**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module-1 DTOC:**

- **DTOC** = Definite Time Over-Current Relay. **Operating time is constant** regardless of fault current magnitude (provided $I_{fault} \geq I_{pickup}$).
- **Defining equation:** $t_{op} = T$ (constant).
- **Time-Current Characteristic (TCC):** **Horizontal straight line** for all PSM $\geq 1$ — this is the *diagnostic feature*.
- **Pickup Current** $I_{pickup}$: The threshold current above which the relay *starts* timing.
- **Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM):** $\text{PSM} = I_{fault} / I_{pickup}$ — dimensionless.
- **Time Setting ($T$ or TDS):** The fixed delay between pickup and trip, adjustable in steps (e.g., 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 … 1.0 s).
- **Discrimination margin $\Delta t \approx 0.4$ s** between successive DTOC relays in cascade.
- **Components of $\Delta t$:** Circuit-breaker time (0.05 s) + Relay overshoot (0.1 s) + Safety margin (0.1 s) + Reset time (0.05 s) = ~0.3 s; with field tolerance, **0.4 s** is the design standard.
- **Effect on Relaying Philosophy:** Faults cause **5× to 20×** rated current; the relay philosophy shifts from "monitor rated operation" to "detect abnormal magnitude and isolate with time-grade."
- **Key Applications:** Backup protection, busbar protection, radial distribution feeders, industrial LV/MV switchgear.
- **Modern digital implementation:** DTOC = digital counter incremented at a fixed clock rate once pickup flag is set. Setting is called **TDS (Time Delay Setting)**.
- **Comparison with IDMT:** DTOC has *zero slope* on the TCC, IDMT has a *negative slope*. DTOC gives better co-ordination with fuses because the horizontal curve clearly lies above the fuse's melting curve.
- **KTU Board-Favourite Numerical Setup:** Given CT ratio, primary current, pickup percentage, fault current → Find PSM. Then time setting is independent of PSM. Add discrimination margin for upstream relay time setting.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** NEVER apply the IDMT equation $t = 0.14 \cdot \text{TMS}/(\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1)$ to a DTOC problem. **This is the #1 valuation killer in KTU exams.**
- **CT Saturation Effect:** DTOC relays are *more tolerant* of CT saturation than IDMT relays because their timing is independent of secondary current magnitude above pickup.
- **Effect of Faults on Relaying Philosophy — Summary:** Faults produce over-current, under-voltage, and unbalance. The relaying philosophy must detect *all three* using the 4-S rule (Speed, Selectivity, Sensitivity, Reliability). DTOC is the simplest, most deterministic element of this philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Definite Minimum Time - Directional Over Current Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inverse Definite Minimum Time - Directional Over Current Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Inverse Definite Minimum Time (IDMT) Relay

### Formal Definition
An **Inverse Definite Minimum Time (IDMT) relay** is a non-directional, time-overcurrent protective relay whose operating time is a composite function of the magnitude of fault current: it is *inverse* (decreasing) at low multiples of pickup current, transitions to a *definite minimum time* (constant) plateau at high multiples of pickup current, and exhibits a *minimum* operating time floor that is independent of further current increase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** IDMT relays form the workhorse of radial distribution and sub-transmission protection, particularly in single-end-fed systems where grading with upstream/downstream devices is critical.

The IDMT characteristic is defined by the **standard inverse equation** as per **IEC 60255-151** and **IEEE C37.112-1996**:

$$t = \text{TMS} \times \left[ \dfrac{K}{\left(\dfrac{I}{I_s}\right)^n - 1} \right] + C$$

where $t$ is the operating time, TMS is the Time Multiplier Setting, $I/I_s$ is the plug setting multiplier (PSM), and $K, n$ are characteristic constants.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **roadside speed-camera with adaptive grace time**. The faster a car (current) goes, the quicker the camera triggers (relay operates) — *inverse* behavior.
