However, beyond a certain extreme speed (e.g., 200 km/h), the camera still cannot trigger faster than its mechanical shutter limit — *definite minimum* time. The camera also has a fixed warm-up (a minimum deliberate delay to avoid nuisance triggering from harmless transients) — *definite* component.

Similarly, the IDMT relay:
- At a fault current **just above pickup** → very **slow** trip (coordination friendly).
- At a **moderate** fault → **fast** trip (inverse part).
- At a **very high** fault (10–20× pickup) → trips at a **fixed minimum time** (definite part), preventing false discrimination collapse.

> [!NOTE]
> The **constant $C$** in modern numerical IDMT relays is virtually zero, but in older electromechanical induction-disc relays, $C \approx 0.14$ s represented the auxiliary definite-time floor.

---

## 1.2 Directional Overcurrent Relay (DOCR)

### Formal Definition
A **Directional Overcurrent Relay (DOCR)** is a protective relay that combines the time-overcurrent (IDMT) measuring function with a **directional discriminating element**. It operates only when the fault current magnitude exceeds a preset pickup **AND** the direction of power/energy flow is within a pre-defined angular sector (typically $\pm 60°$ to $\pm 90°$ around the maximum-torque angle, MTA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DOCR is needed:** In **loop systems**, **parallel feeders**, or **grid-connected networks**, fault current can flow in *both* directions through a relay location. A non-directional overcurrent relay cannot determine *which side* of the bus is faulted. The directional element injects a *polarizing* voltage reference to discriminate direction.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a **one-way toll gate on a flyover**:
- The **toll gate** = the overcurrent unit (trips on high current magnitude).
- The **directional sign** (one-way arrow) = the directional unit (allows the gate to open only for cars coming from the permitted direction).
- Cars approaching from the wrong side (reverse power flow) — even at high speed — get **no gate response**. This prevents the relay from tripping for an *upstream* fault when it should stay idle for a *downstream* fault.

### Standard Polarization Quantities
| Polarizing Quantity | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase-to-neutral voltage** | $V_{an}, V_{bn}, V_{cn}$ | Polarizes the corresponding phase unit |
| **Phase-to-phase voltage** | $V_{ab}, V_{bc}, V_{ca}$ | Provides memory against close-in 3-phase faults (where $V \to 0$) |
| **Positive-sequence voltage** | $V_1$ | Robust against all unsymmetrical faults |
| **Negative-sequence voltage** | $V_2$ | Phase-A directional element for ground faults |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory action** — a key feature in numerical DOCRs — stores the pre-fault voltage phasor for 3–5 cycles so that directionality is preserved even when the bolted 3-phase fault collapses the local voltage to zero.

---

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **PSM (Plug Setting Multiplier):** $\text{PSM} = I_{fault} / I_{pickup}$, dimensionless.
- **TMS (Time Multiplier Setting):** typically **0.05 to 1.10** in steps of 0.05.
- **Characteristic angles (IEC/BS 142):** **Standard Inverse 0.14 / 0.02**, **Very Inverse 13.5 / 1.0**, **Extremely Inverse 80 / 2.0**, **Long-Time Inverse 120 / 13.33** ($K$ / $n$).
- **MTA (Maximum Torque Angle):** typically **30°**, **45°**, or **60°** depending on the polarization connection.
- **Operating time range:** **0.01 s to 10 s** in modern numerical relays.
- **Burden:** electromechanical DOCR burden is **< 5 VA**; numerical < 0.5 VA.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IDMT — Underlying Operating Principle

The IDMT characteristic is synthesized from **two physical mechanisms** stacked in series:

1. **Induction-disc (electromechanical) / Square-law algorithm (numerical) → Inverse part**
2. **Auxiliary electromagnet / definite-time timer → Definite part**

### Inverse Region Operational Logic
- As fault current $I$ rises above pickup $I_s$, the disc-driving torque $\tau \propto I^2 - I_s^2$ increases quadratically.
- Disc acceleration $\alpha = \tau/J$, and operating time $t \propto 1/\alpha$.
- Therefore $t \propto 1/[(I/I_s)^n - 1]$, producing the **inverse curve**.

### Definite Minimum Time (DMT) Logic
- At very high currents, the disc would otherwise operate almost instantly — creating grading nightmares.
- A **second electromagnet (or digital timer latch)** holds the trip output for a fixed minimum delay (typically 0.1–0.3 s).
- This yields a **flat horizontal asymptote** in the time-current curve.

### Standard IDMT Curves (IEC 60255-151)
| Curve Type | Equation Form | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Inverse (SI)** | $t = 0.14 \cdot \text{TMS} / (\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1)$ | General distribution, transformer backup |
| **Very Inverse (VI)** | $t = 13.5 \cdot \text{TMS} / (\text{PSM}^1 - 1)$ | Cable feeders, earth-fault, inrush-rich environments |
| **Extremely Inverse (EI)** | $t = 80 \cdot \text{TMS} / (\text{PSM}^2 - 1)$ | Fuse-relay coordination, generator protection, motor starting |
| **Long-Time Inverse (LTI)** | $t = 120 \cdot \text{TMS} / (\text{PSM}^{13.33} - 1)$ | Long feeders, sub-transmission lines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** For **discrimination** in radial feeders, the **standard inverse** curve gives the best balance. For **fuse-relay backup** in a fuse-saving scheme, the **very inverse** is the workhorse.

---

## 2.2 Directional Element — Theory of Operation

The directional unit is essentially a **phase-comparator** that measures the angle between the operating current $\bar{I}_o$ and a **polarizing voltage** $\bar{V}_p$.

### Phase Comparator (Rectifier/Amp Comparator Type)
The relay operates when:

$$\cos(\theta - \phi) \geq 0$$

where:
- $\theta$ is the angle between $\bar{I}_o$ and $\bar{V}_p$.
- $\phi$ is the maximum-torque angle (MTA), preset in the relay.

The relay **restrains** when the angle is outside the operating half-plane ($\pm 90°$ around MTA).

### Connection Diagrams — The Three Classical Schemes
For an **A-phase unit** with operating current $I_a$ and polarizing voltage $V_p$:

| Connection | Polarizing Voltage $V_p$ | Phase Displacement | Typical MTA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0° (Homopolar) connection** | $V_{an}$ | $0°$ between $I_a$ and $V_{an}$ at unity pf | 30° |
| **90° Connection (Quadrature)** | $V_{bc}$ | $I_a$ lags $V_{bc}$ by 90° at unity pf | 45° |
| **30° Connection** | $-V_{an} + V_{bc}$ | $30°$ phase shift synthesized | 60° |

> [!NOTE]
> The **90° (quadrature) connection** is the **most widely used** for phase-fault directional elements because it gives the relay its best sensitivity near the **MTA = 45°**, which corresponds to the typical arc-fault power-factor angle of 60°–85° lagging.

### R-X Diagram of Directional Characteristic
The directional boundary is a **straight line in the R-X plane** passing through the origin, with its perpendicular distance to the origin determined by the polarising voltage. The **operating region** lies on one side of this line.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities are in **SI units** unless otherwise stated. Use $\vert$ or $\text{abs}$ inside tables (NEVER the literal pipe symbol to avoid breaking markdown).

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t = \text{TMS} \cdot K \big/ [(\text{PSM})^n - 1]$ | General IDMT operating time | $\text{PSM} > 1.0$, else $t \to \infty$ |
| 2 | $\text{PSM} = I_{fault} / I_{pickup}$ | Plug-setting multiplier | $\text{PSM} \geq 1.0$ to operate |
| 3 | $I_{pickup} = \text{CT ratio} \times \text{Plug setting (PU)}$ | Current pickup | Plug setting in **A** |
| 4 | $t_{disc} = \sqrt{t_{total}^2 - t_{aux}^2}$ | Discriminating time (when auxiliary def. time exists) | Electromechanical only |
| 5 | $t_{op} = t_{directional} + t_{OC} + t_{CB}$ | Total fault-clearing time | $t_{CB} \approx 2\text{–}5$ cycles |
| 6 | $\Delta t = t_{upstream} - t_{downstream}$ | Grading margin | **0.3 s – 0.5 s** typical |
| 7 | $\cos(\theta - \phi) \geq 0$ | Directional phase-comparator condition | $\theta \in [\phi - 90°, \phi + 90°]$ |
| 8 | $V_{p,min} \geq 0.05 \times V_{rated}$ | Polarizing voltage sensitivity floor | Below → directional block |
| 9 | $t_{operate} = t_{IDMT} + t_{directional, pickup} + t_{OC pickup}$ | Numerical DOCR total operating time | $t_{dir, pickup} \approx 20\text{–}30$ ms |
| 10 | $K_{rel} = I_{pickup, F} / I_{load, max}$ | Reliability factor for load security | $K_{rel} \geq 1.5$ for feeders |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distribution feeders** — primary protection via IDMT OC relays; coordination along radial lines.
- **Sub-transmission loop networks** — DOCR discriminates the faulted section in a ring-main or parallel-feeder topology.
- **Transformer backup** — extremely inverse IDMT on HV side backs up downstream fuses/breakers.
- **Generator protection** — IDMT earth-fault element with definite-minimum-time floor ensures sub-100 ms clearance for stator ground faults.
- **Railway traction** — DOCR with very inverse curve discriminates between train starting current and traction feeder faults.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern **numerical/multi-function IEDs** (e.g., ABB REF615, Siemens 7SA86, SEL-451), IDMT and directional logic are implemented in **software phasor algorithms** (typically half-cycle or full-cycle DFT) — making the electromechanical curve a *mathematical template* rather than a physical device.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Time-Current Characteristic

### Starting Point — Newton's Law for the Induction Disc

For an electromechanical induction-disc IDMT element, the torque on the disc is:

$$\tau(t) = K_1 \cdot I^2 - K_2 \cdot I_s^2$$

where $I$ is the RMS fault current, $I_s$ is the pickup current, and $K_1, K_2$ are torque constants of the operating and restraining electromagnets respectively.

**Step 1 — Net driving torque at $I = I_s$**

At pickup, the net torque is zero (no movement):

$$\tau_{net} = K_1 I^2 - K_2 I_s^2 = 0 \quad \text{at } I = I_s$$

This yields the relation $K_1 = K_2$, so we can write a single constant $K$:

$$\tau_{net} = K \left( I^2 - I_s^2 \right)$$

**Step 2 — Disc equation of motion**

Treating the disc as a rigid body with moment of inertia $J$ and viscous damping $D$:

$$J \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \tau_{net}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state simplification**

The disc reaches a steady angular velocity $\omega = d\theta/dt$ when acceleration vanishes. In steady operating region:

$$D \cdot \omega = K \left( I^2 - I_s^2 \right)$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{K}{D} \left( I^2 - I_s^2 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Time to traverse the disc angle $\theta_{trip}$**

The total angle the disc must rotate to trip is $\theta_{trip}$. Therefore operating time:

$$t = \dfrac{\theta_{trip}}{\omega} = \dfrac{\theta_{trip} \cdot D}{K \left( I^2 - I_s^2 \right)}$$

**Step 5 — Normalization to PSM**

Define $A = I / I_s$ (the **PSM**). Substitute $I = A \cdot I_s$:

$$t = \dfrac{\theta_{trip} \cdot D}{K \cdot I_s^2 \left( A^2 - 1 \right)}$$

Letting $\text{TMS} = \theta_{trip} \cdot D / (K \cdot I_s^2)$ (a settable constant via the time-multiplier dial):

$$\boxed{\,t = \dfrac{\text{TMS}}{A^2 - 1} = \dfrac{\text{TMS}}{(\text{PSM})^2 - 1}\,}$$

This is the **inverse-square characteristic**. For the general $n^{th}$-order inverse, the disc is geometrically tapered to yield:

$$t = \dfrac{\text{TMS}}{(\text{PSM})^n - 1}$$

which is the classical IDMT template.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **TMS** is the *only* adjustable parameter that linearly scales the entire curve vertically (on a log-log plot). It is the principal coordination tool between upstream and downstream relays.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark flavor):**

> A 11 kV, 3-phase, 50 Hz radial feeder is protected by an IDMT relay with **CT ratio 400/5**, **plug setting 125%**, and **TMS = 0.4**. The relay follows the **standard inverse curve** with $K = 0.14$, $n = 0.02$. A 3-phase fault on the feeder draws fault current $I_f = 5000$ A. Compute the operating time of the relay.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute primary pickup current**

$$I_{pickup, primary} = 1.25 \times \text{CT primary current} = 1.25 \times 400 = 500 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Compute secondary pickup current**

$$I_{pickup, secondary} = 500 \times \dfrac{5}{400} = 6.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute secondary fault current**

$$I_{fault, secondary} = 5000 \times \dfrac{5}{400} = 62.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Compute PSM**

$$\text{PSM} = \dfrac{I_{fault, secondary}}{I_{pickup, secondary}} = \dfrac{62.5}{6.25} = 10.0$$

**Step 5 — Apply Standard Inverse IDMT equation**

$$t = \text{TMS} \times \dfrac{K}{(\text{PSM})^n - 1}$$

$$t = 0.4 \times \dfrac{0.14}{(10.0)^{0.02} - 1}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate $10^{0.02}$**

$$10^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \ln 10} = e^{0.02 \times 2.302585} = e^{0.046052} = 1.04713$$

**Step 7 — Compute denominator**

$$1.04713 - 1 = 0.04713$$

**Step 8 — Compute final operating time**

$$t = 0.4 \times \dfrac{0.14}{0.04713} = 0.4 \times 2.970 = \boxed{1.188 \text{ s}}$$

---

## 3.3 Grading Margin (Coordination Time Interval) — Derivation

For two series IDMT relays, the upstream (R1) must operate later than the downstream (R2) by at least the **grading margin $\Delta t$**:

$$\Delta t = t_{R1,\ min} - t_{R2,\ max} = t_{CB} + t_{Overshoot} + t_{Safety}$$

Substituting typical KTU/KSEB values:
- Circuit-breaker interrupting time: $t_{CB} \approx 0.05$ s (3 cycles)
- Relay overshoot (for electromechanical): $t_{Overshoot} \approx 0.05$ s
- Safety margin: $t_{Safety} \approx 0.20$ s

$$\Delta t_{typical} = 0.05 + 0.05 + 0.20 = 0.30 \text{ s}$$

For **numerical relays**, the overshoot is essentially zero:

$$\Delta t_{numerical} = 0.05 + 0.00 + 0.15 = 0.20 \text{ s}$$

---

## 3.4 Directional Relay — Vector Analysis

For a 90° connection with $I_a$ and $V_{bc}$:

At unity power factor, $I_a$ lags $V_{bc}$ by **90°**. The MTA is set such that maximum sensitivity occurs at typical fault-power-factor angle of $\cos^{-1}(0.3) \approx 72.5°$ lagging.

The relay operates when:

$$\theta_{I_a - V_{bc}} \in [\phi - 90°, \phi + 90°]$$

For $\phi = 45°$ (MTA):

$$\theta \in [-45°, +135°]$$

Equivalently, the relay **restrains** for reverse power flow where $\theta$ falls outside this sector.

---

## 3.5 Summary — Analytical Matrix of IDMT + DOCR

| Design Choice | IDMT Behavior | DOCR Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Pickup setting** | Single threshold; current-only | Two-fold: magnitude + angle check |
| **Operating time** | Inverse + DMT floor | Same as IDMT *if* direction is forward; else **no trip** |
| **Memory action** | Not required | **Required** for 3-phase close-in faults |
| **Polarization source** | N/A | Voltage (phase, phase-phase, or sequence) |
| **Typical curve** | Standard / Very / Extremely / Long | Inherits IDMT curve; adds directional gate |
| **Use case** | Radial feeders, transformers | Loop systems, parallel feeders, grid interconnect |
| **Coordination** | Time-grading (TMS) | Time-grading *and* directional zone logic |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Numerical IDMT-DOCR IED

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CTa[CT Secondary Ia] --> AAF[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    CTb[CT Secondary Ib] --> AAF
    CTc[CT Secondary Ic] --> AAF
    PTa[VT Secondary Van] --> AAF
    PTb[VT Secondary Vbn] --> AAF
    PTc[VT Secondary Vcn] --> AAF

    AAF --> ADC[16-bit ADC Sampler]

    ADC --> DFT[Full-Cycle DFT Phasor Estimator]

    DFT --> OCmag[Overcurrent Magnitude Comparator]
    DFT --> DIRcmp[Directional Phase Comparator]

    OCmag --> IFTHEN{PSM Greater Than Pickup}
    IFTHEN -->|Yes| IDMTcurv[IDMT Curve Engine SI / VI / EI / LTI]
    IFTHEN -->|No| BLK1[Restrain Trip]

    DIRcmp --> DIRCOS{Cos theta minus phi Greater or Equal Zero}
    DIRCOS -->|Yes Forward| IDMTcurv
    DIRCOS -->|No Reverse| BLK1

    IDMTcurv --> DMTdef{Definite Minimum Time Latch Active}
    DMTdef -->|Yes| HOLD[Hold for DMT floor]
    DMTdef -->|No| TRIP[Close Trip Contact]

    HOLD --> TRIP
    BLK1 --> RST[Reset and Restart Timer]

    TRIP --> CB[Send Trip to Circuit Breaker]
    RST --> CB
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level architecture abstracts the *physical* schematics of electromechanical relays into a **numerical IED** function flow, which is the modern KTU-relevant representation. Each block is a DSP/algorithm step.

---

## 4.2 IDMT Time-Current Characteristic — Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Xaxis[Current Axis - Log Scale]
        A1[1.0 PU Pickup] --> A2[2.0 PU] --> A3[5.0 PU] --> A4[10.0 PU] --> A5[20.0 PU]
    end

    subgraph Yaxis[Operating Time Axis - Log Scale]
        T1[10.0 s] --> T2[3.0 s] --> T3[1.0 s] --> T4[0.3 s] --> T5[0.1 s]
    end

    A1 -.-> T1
    A2 -.-> T2
    A3 -.-> T3
    A4 -.-> T4
    A5 -.-> T5

    style A1 fill:#fff7c2
    style A5 fill:#c2e0ff
    style T1 fill:#ffd6c2
    style T5 fill:#c2ffd6
```

> [!NOTE]
> The "mappings" between A and T nodes represent the **inverse-definite-minimum-time** curve trajectory: at low PSM, time is long; at high PSM, time plateaus at the DMT floor (right side).

---

## 4.3 Directional Relay Operating Region — R-X Diagram Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RXplane[R-X Diag plane]
        ORIG[Origin 0,0]
        FWD[Forward Operating Region]
        REV[Reverse Restraining Region]
        BOUND[Directional Boundary Line Through Origin]
    end

    ORIG --- BOUND
    BOUND --> FWD
    BOUND --> REV

    FWD -->|Fwd power flow angle within phi minus 90 to phi plus 90| TRIP[Relay TRIP Authorized]
    REV -->|Reverse power flow angle outside sector| BLOCK[Relay BLOCK]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **boundary line** passes through the origin and is perpendicular to the **MTA** phasor. The forward operating half-plane spans $\pm 90°$ on either side of the MTA.

---

## 4.4 Coordination Hierarchy — Radial Feeder with IDMT Grading

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Source 11 kV] --> R1[Relay R1 at Substation - 800 A Pickup - TMS 0.3]
    R1 --> BUS1[Bus 1]
    BUS1 --> R2[Relay R2 - 400 A Pickup - TMS 0.2]
    BUS1 --> R3[Relay R3 - 400 A Pickup - TMS 0.2]
    R2 --> LD1[Load A]
    R3 --> LD2[Load B]

    R1 -.->|Backup GRD 0.30 s margin| R2
    R1 -.->|Backup GRD 0.30 s margin| R3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** The grading margin $\Delta t$ between R1 and R2/R3 must be **0.3 s–0.5 s** for electromechanical and **0.2 s–0.3 s** for numerical relays. The *higher* TMS of R1 reflects upstream coordination.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Q: Define Inverse Definite Minimum Time (IDMT) characteristic. Why is the definite minimum time portion essential in the characteristic?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The IDMT characteristic is a relay operating-time-vs-current curve in which operating time is **inversely proportional** to the magnitude of fault current (above pickup) and asymptotically approaches a **constant minimum time** at high fault currents. The **definite minimum time (DMT)** portion is essential to (i) **prevent miscoordination** at very high currents where inverse curves become near-instantaneous and would defeat downstream discrimination, (ii) provide a **safety floor** to allow downstream protection to operate first, and (iii) prevent unnecessary mechanical/thermal stress on the relay contacts and trip coil. The DMT region ensures selectivity is preserved across the full fault-current spectrum.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**Q: What is a Directional Overcurrent Relay? State the need for directional discrimination in overcurrent protection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Directional Overcurrent Relay (DOCR)** is a protective relay that operates when the fault current exceeds a preset magnitude **AND** flows in a predefined direction (forward or reverse) as determined by a **polarizing voltage** reference. The need for directional discrimination arises in **loop networks, parallel feeders, and grid-tie lines** where fault current can flow from both directions through a relay location. A non-directional overcurrent relay would trip incorrectly for an *upstream* fault, causing unnecessary outage. The directional element uses a **phase comparator** between operating current and a polarizing voltage to permit tripping only for forward faults, ensuring correct **discrimination and selectivity** in non-radial topologies.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the operating time equation of an IDMT relay starting from the induction-disc equation of motion. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 22 kV, 3-phase, 50 Hz radial feeder is protected by a numerical IDMT relay with **CT ratio 600/5**, **plug setting 100%**, and **TMS = 0.5**. The relay follows the **standard inverse** characteristic ($K = 0.14$, $n = 0.02$). A 3-phase bolted fault on the far end draws **3500 A** primary. Compute:
- (i) Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM) **(2 marks)**
- (ii) Operating time of the relay **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** The induction disc experiences a net torque proportional to the difference of squares of operating current $I$ and pickup current $I_s$:

$$\tau_{net} = K \left( I^2 - I_s^2 \right)$$

**Step 2:** Disc equation of motion (neglecting inertia in steady-state, viscous damping dominant):

$$D \cdot \omega = K \left( I^2 - I_s^2 \right)$$

**Step 3:** Steady-state angular velocity:

$$\omega = \dfrac{K}{D} \left( I^2 - I_s^2 \right)$$

**Step 4:** Time to traverse trip angle $\theta_{trip}$:

$$t = \dfrac{\theta_{trip}}{\omega} = \dfrac{\theta_{trip} \cdot D}{K(I^2 - I_s^2)}$$

**Step 5:** Normalize by $I_s^2$ and define PSM = $A = I/I_s$:

$$t = \dfrac{\theta_{trip} \cdot D}{K I_s^2 (A^2 - 1)}$$

**Step 6:** Define TMS = $\theta_{trip} \cdot D / (K I_s^2)$:

$$\boxed{\,t = \dfrac{\text{TMS}}{(\text{PSM})^2 - 1}\,}$$

Generalized for $n^{th}$-order inverse:

$$t = \dfrac{\text{TMS}}{(\text{PSM})^n - 1}$$

**Assumptions:** Disc operates in steady-state (inertia neglected), viscous damping dominant, no saturation of magnetic circuit, single-frequency fault current.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) PSM (2 marks):**

- Primary pickup: $I_{pickup} = 1.0 \times 600 = 600$ A (since plug setting 100%)
- Secondary pickup: $I_{pickup, sec} = 600 \times 5/600 = 5$ A
- Secondary fault current: $I_{f, sec} = 3500 \times 5/600 = 29.167$ A
- $\text{PSM} = I_{f, sec} / I_{pickup, sec} = 29.167 / 5 = \mathbf{5.833}$

**(ii) Operating time (3 marks):**

$$t = 0.5 \times \dfrac{0.14}{(5.833)^{0.02} - 1}$$

Compute $(5.833)^{0.02}$:

$$5.833^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \ln 5.833} = e^{0.02 \times 1.7633} = e^{0.03527} = 1.0359$$

$$t = 0.5 \times \dfrac{0.14}{1.0359 - 1} = 0.5 \times \dfrac{0.14}{0.0359} = 0.5 \times 3.899 = \mathbf{1.95 \text{ s}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a numerical Directional Overcurrent Relay (DOCR). Explain the role of the **directional phase comparator** and **memory action**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two relays R1 (upstream) and R2 (downstream) protect a series 11 kV feeder using standard inverse IDMT characteristics. The fault current at the far end is **2500 A**; CT ratios are 400/5 (R1) and 200/5 (R2). Pickup settings are 125% (R1) and 100% (R2). Determine the **minimum TMS for R1** such that the grading margin is **0.3 s** and the operating time of R2 is **0.5 s** at the far-end fault. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Numerical DOCR Architecture (7 marks):**

A numerical DOCR comprises the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):

1. **Input transducers:** CTs/VTs step down the primary quantities to standardized 1 A/110 V secondary levels.
2. **Anti-aliasing filters:** Low-pass filters (cut-off ~1/3 of sampling rate) prevent aliasing errors in ADC.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC):** 16-bit, ≥32 samples per cycle, simultaneous sampling on all channels.
4. **Phasor estimation (DFT/FFT):** Full-cycle Discrete Fourier Transform extracts fundamental-frequency magnitude and phase of currents and voltages.
5. **Overcurrent magnitude comparator:** Computes $\text{PSM} = \vert I \vert / I_{pickup}$ and compares with pickup threshold (typically 1.0).
6. **Directional phase comparator:** Computes $\cos(\theta - \phi)$ where $\theta = \angle(\bar{I}_o, \bar{V}_p)$ and $\phi$ is MTA. Output is logic "1" if forward, "0" if reverse. The two conditions (magnitude AND direction) are ANDed.
7. **IDMT curve engine:** Applies the time equation $t = \text{TMS} \cdot K/[(\text{PSM})^n - 1]$ and starts an internal timer.
8. **Definite minimum time latch:** Holds trip output for the DMT floor.
9. **Trip logic & output contact:** Energizes the trip coil of the circuit breaker.

**Role of the directional phase comparator:** The phase comparator distinguishes forward faults from reverse faults by comparing the angle between the operating current and a **polarizing voltage**. The output is a binary "direction" signal that **gates** the trip command. Without it, an overcurrent element alone would trip for any fault of sufficient magnitude, regardless of location.

**Role of memory action:** During a **close-in 3-phase bolted fault**, the local voltage collapses to nearly zero. The polarizing signal $V_p$ vanishes, making the directional comparator **indeterminate** and risking a loss-of-directionality failure mode. **Memory action** stores the pre-fault voltage phasor (typically 3–5 cycles) in a circular buffer, allowing the comparator to continue operating reliably until the fault is cleared. It is an essential feature for **security** of numerical DOCRs.

---

**(b) Grading Coordination Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute pickup currents**

- R1 primary pickup: $1.25 \times 400 = 500$ A; secondary $= 500 \times 5/400 = 6.25$ A
- R2 primary pickup: $1.0 \times 200 = 200$ A; secondary $= 200 \times 5/200 = 5$ A

**Step 2 — Compute secondary fault current at far end**

$$I_{f, sec} = 2500 \times \dfrac{5}{400} = 31.25 \text{ A (seen by R1)}$$

$$I_{f, sec,R2} = 2500 \times \dfrac{5}{200} = 62.5 \text{ A (seen by R2)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute PSMs**

$$\text{PSM}_{R1} = 31.25 / 6.25 = 5.0$$

$$\text{PSM}_{R2} = 62.5 / 5 = 12.5$$

**Step 4 — Apply IDMT equation for R2 with $t_{R2} = 0.5$ s**

$$0.5 = \text{TMS}_{R2} \times \dfrac{0.14}{(12.5)^{0.02} - 1}$$

Compute $(12.5)^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \times 2.5257} = e^{0.05051} = 1.05181$

$$0.5 = \text{TMS}_{R2} \times \dfrac{0.14}{0.05181} = \text{TMS}_{R2} \times 2.702$$

$$\text{TMS}_{R2} = 0.5 / 2.702 = 0.185$$

**Step 5 — Required operating time of R1**

$$t_{R1, required} = t_{R2} + \Delta t = 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for TMS of R1**

$$0.8 = \text{TMS}_{R1} \times \dfrac{0.14}{(5.0)^{0.02} - 1}$$

Compute $(5.0)^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \times 1.6094} = e^{0.03219} = 1.03272$

$$0.8 = \text{TMS}_{R1} \times \dfrac{0.14}{0.03272} = \text{TMS}_{R1} \times 4.279$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{TMS}_{R1} = 0.8 / 4.279 = 0.187\,}$$

(For practical relay settings, this would be rounded up to the next available TMS tap, typically **0.20**.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — IDMT & DOCR Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $5/400$ or $5/200$ CT-ratio conversion:** A frequent error is computing PSM using *primary* fault current divided by *primary* pickup. PSM **must** be computed on the **secondary side** for both numerator and denominator — otherwise the ratio is dimensionally inconsistent. *Expected mark loss: 1–2 marks.*
>
> 2. **Confusing PSM with pickup:** Students often write $\text{PSM} = 1.25$ because the plug setting is 125%. Wrong — PSM = (Fault current) / (Pickup current), not the plug setting itself. The plug setting is a relay dial setting, not PSM. *Expected mark loss: 1 mark.*
>
> 3. **Using $n = 0.02$ in $A^n$ vs $A^2$:** Standard inverse uses $n = 0.02$ (a *very* shallow exponent, making the curve nearly flat). Students who write $A^2$ obtain a **steep inverse** that does NOT match the standard curve. *Expected mark loss: 1 mark.*
>
> 4. **Skipping the grading-margin step:** In coordination problems, students often forget that upstream operating time = downstream time + $\Delta t$. *Expected mark loss: 2 marks.*
>
> 5. **Forgetting memory action in DOCR:** If a question on DOCR asks "what happens during a 3-phase close-in fault?", writing only about polarizing voltage collapse without mentioning **memory action** loses full credit on the security/dependability sub-part. *Expected mark loss: 1–2 marks.*
>
> 6. **Using absolute value symbol `|x|` inside markdown tables:** This breaks the markdown rendering in KTU answer scripts. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ in LaTeX contexts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IDMT & DOCR**

- ⭐ **IDMT** = **Inverse** at low PSM + **Definite Minimum Time** plateau at high PSM.
- ⭐ **General equation:** $t = \text{TMS} \cdot K / [(\text{PSM})^n - 1]$.
- ⭐ **PSM** = fault current (secondary) ÷ pickup current (secondary) — **always on the secondary side**.
- ⭐ **Standard inverse:** $K = 0.14$, $n = 0.02$.
- ⭐ **Very inverse:** $K = 13.5$, $n = 1.0$.
- ⭐ **Extremely inverse:** $K = 80$, $n = 2.0$.
- ⭐ **Grading margin** = **0.3 s** (electromechanical) / **0.2 s** (numerical).
- ⭐ **DOCR** = Overcurrent + Directional phase comparator.
- ⭐ **Polarization source:** phase-neutral, phase-phase, or sequence voltage.
- ⭐ **90° quadrature connection** is most common; $V_p = V_{bc}$ for A-phase unit.
- ⭐ **Memory action** is essential for 3-phase close-in faults (local voltage collapse).
- ⭐ **MTA** typically 30°/45°/60°; operating sector is $\pm 90°$ around MTA.
- ⭐ **TMS is linearly scalable** — the primary coordination knob.
- ⭐ **Plug setting** sets pickup; **TMS** sets operating time scale.
- ⭐ Numerical DOCRs implement IDMT curves as **software templates**, not physical mechanisms.
- ⭐ Always **re-check PSM ≥ 1.0** — below pickup, relay must not operate.
- ⭐ For numerical exam answers, retain **3–4 significant figures** in $t$.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — IDMT & Directional Overcurrent Relays**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Current Setting and Time Setting - Numerical Problems - Differential Relays: Operating and Restraining Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Current Setting in Overcurrent Relays

**Current Setting (Pickup Setting)** is the minimum value of fault current above which an overcurrent relay is designed to operate. It represents the threshold current at which the relay coil just begins to develop sufficient torque to close its contacts. The current setting is normally expressed as a percentage of the nominal secondary current of the current transformer (CT) supplying the relay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *Current setting is the value of current (in Amperes) at which the relay is adjusted to operate. It is also called the Pickup Current (Ipickup). The relay will not operate for currents below this value and will operate for currents equal to or above this value.*

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of current setting as the **"wake-up threshold"** of a security alarm. Imagine a fire alarm that is set to ring only when the temperature crosses 60 °C. Similarly, the overcurrent relay is "asleep" for normal load currents and "wakes up" the moment the current exceeds the pickup value. Just as a sensitive alarm (low threshold) is useful for fire safety but may trigger falsely for a sunny day, a low current setting protects quickly but can cause nuisance tripping; a high setting avoids nuisance trips but may fail to protect against actual faults.

**Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM):**
The ratio of the actual secondary fault current to the relay pickup (plug setting) current. It is dimensionless and indicates how many times the fault current exceeds the pickup value.

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{\text{fault, secondary}}}{I_{\text{pickup}}} = \frac{I_{\text{fault, primary}} / \text{CT Ratio}}{I_{\text{plug}}}$$

## 1.2 Time Setting in Overcurrent Relays

**Time Setting Multiplier (TSM)** is the dial/knob setting on an electromechanical inverse-time relay that adjusts the operating time for a given multiple of pickup current. TSM typically ranges from **0.1 to 1.0** in steps of **0.05** for digital/numerical relays and discrete steps of 0.1 for electromechanical relays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The operating time of an IDMT (Inverse Definite Minimum Time) relay is given by the product of the time derived from the standard characteristic curve and the TSM. A higher TSM means slower operation; a lower TSM means faster operation.

### Conceptual Analogy
TSM is like the **"snooze duration"** on a morning alarm clock. If the fault is only slightly above pickup (small PSM), the relay waits longer before tripping (snooze for big trouble). For a severe fault (large PSM), the relay acts almost instantly — it cannot "snooze" for a fatal crisis. The TSM simply stretches or compresses this snooze duration uniformly.

## 1.3 Differential Relays — Operating and Restraining Quantities

A **Differential Relay** compares the currents entering and leaving a protected zone. Under normal conditions or external (through) faults, the current entering equals the current leaving (Kirchhoff's Current Law), producing near-zero **operating current**. For internal faults, the difference is large, and the relay trips.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *The Operating Current (Iop) is the phasor difference of the currents entering the protected zone, and the Restraining Current (Ires) is the average of the magnitudes of these currents. The relay operates only when Iop exceeds a preset percentage of Ires.*

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **water tank with an inlet pipe and an outlet pipe fitted with flow meters**. If the meters show the same reading, there is no leakage — the system is healthy. If they differ, water is leaking (an internal fault). A simple alarm (differential relay) would ring when the difference is large, but it would false-trigger due to small meter errors during heavy flow. To avoid this, we use a **biased (percentage differential) relay**: it allows a small percentage of imbalance — the bigger the flow, the larger the allowed imbalance, mimicking a "traffic policeman" who gives more leeway during rush hour (through-faults).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Overcurrent Relay: Theoretical Background

An IDMT overcurrent relay has the following operating philosophy:

- **Inverse portion:** Operating time decreases as PSM increases.
- **Definite Minimum Time (DMT) portion:** Below a high PSM threshold, the operating time saturates to a fixed minimum value (this prevents mechanical/contact wear).

### 2.1.1 Standard IDMT Characteristics (IEC 60255-151)

The operating time of a numerical/electromechanical IDMT relay is given by:

$$t = \frac{A \times \text{TSM}}{(\text{PSM})^n - 1} + B \times \text{TSM}$$

For pure inverse forms (often used in KTU problems), the simpler form is applied:

| Characteristic Type | Formula (t in seconds) | Constant A | Exponent n |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Inverse (SI) | $t = \dfrac{0.14 \times \text{TSM}}{(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1}$ | 0.14 | 0.02 |
| Very Inverse (VI) | $t = \dfrac{13.5 \times \text{TSM}}{(\text{PSM}) - 1}$ | 13.5 | 1.0 |
| Extremely Inverse (EI) | $t = \dfrac{80 \times \text{TSM}}{(\text{PSM})^2 - 1}$ | 80 | 2.0 |
| Long-Time Inverse (LI) | $t = \dfrac{120 \times \text{TSM}}{(\text{PSM}) - 1}$ | 120 | 1.0 |
| Definite Minimum Time (DMT) | $t = \text{constant}$ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For most numerical problems in KTU 2024 Scheme, **Standard Inverse** or **Very Inverse** characteristics are used. Always confirm which curve is given in the problem statement.

### 2.1.2 Current Setting Procedure

The standard procedure to determine relay current setting is:

1. Identify the **full-load current (FLC)** of the protected equipment.
2. Choose a **CT ratio** such that CT secondary current ≥ FLC.
3. Select a **Plug Setting (PS)** ≥ **125% to 150%** of FLC (secondary).
4. Compute **PSM = (Fault current / CT ratio) / PS**.
5. From the chosen curve and selected TSM, read or compute the **operating time**.

## 2.2 Differential Relay: Theoretical Background

### 2.2.1 Operating and Restraining Current Definitions

For a two-winding protected element (e.g., a transformer or generator):

$$I_{\text{op}} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$$

$$I_{\text{res}} = \frac{\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert}{2}$$

Where:
- $I_1$ = secondary current of CT-1 (entering the zone)
- $I_2$ = secondary current of CT-2 (leaving the zone, referred through the CT ratio)

### 2.2.2 Percentage Biased (Dual-Slope) Characteristic

A modern numerical differential relay uses a **dual-slope bias characteristic** to provide:

- **High sensitivity** for low currents (internal faults at low load).
- **Stability** for high through-fault currents (avoiding CT saturation-induced tripping).

The operating boundary is defined by:

$$I_{\text{op}} \ge I_{\text{op,min}} \quad \text{when} \quad I_{\text{res}} \le I_{\text{res,threshold}}$$

$$I_{\text{op}} \ge K_1 \times I_{\text{res}} + C_1 \quad \text{when} \quad I_{\text{res,threshold}} < I_{\text{res}} \le I_{\text{res,break}}$$

$$I_{\text{op}} \ge K_2 \times (I_{\text{res}} - I_{\text{res,break}}) + C_2 \quad \text{when} \quad I_{\text{res}} > I_{\text{res,break}}$$

Typical values (used in KTU textbook problems):
- $I_{\text{op,min}} = 0.2$ to $0.5$ pu
- $K_1 = 20\%$ to $30\%$ (lower slope)
- $K_2 = 50\%$ to $80\%$ (higher slope)
- $I_{\text{res,break}} = 1.0$ to $2.0$ pu

### 2.2.3 Through-Fault vs. Internal Fault Analysis

| Condition | $I_1$ | $I_2$ | $I_{\text{op}}$ | $I_{\text{res}}$ | Relay Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Normal Load | $I_L$ | $I_L$ | $\approx 0$ | $I_L$ | No trip |
| External (Through) Fault | $I_F$ | $I_F$ | Small (only CT error) | Large ($I_F$) | No trip (restrained) |
| Internal Fault | $I_F$ | $\approx 0$ | $I_F$ | $I_F/2$ | Trip |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CT Secondary Current | $I_s = I_p / \text{CTR}$ | A | $I_p$ = primary current |
| 2 | Plug Setting Multiplier | $\text{PSM} = I_s / I_{\text{pickup}}$ | dimensionless | Must be ≥ 1 for operation |
| 3 | Operating Time (SI) | $t = 0.14 \times \text{TSM} / (\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1)$ | seconds | IEC 60255-151 |
| 4 | Operating Time (VI) | $t = 13.5 \times \text{TSM} / (\text{PSM} - 1)$ | seconds | IEC 60255-151 |
| 5 | Operating Time (EI) | $t = 80 \times \text{TSM} / (\text{PSM}^2 - 1)$ | seconds | IEC 60255-151 |
| 6 | Differential Operating Current | $I_{\text{op}} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$ | A or pu | Phasor difference |
| 7 | Differential Restraining Current | $I_{\text{res}} = (\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert) / 2$ | A or pu | Average of magnitudes |
| 8 | Bias (Operating Slope) | $K = I_{\text{op}} / I_{\text{res}}$ | % | Compare with characteristic slope |
| 9 | Through-Fault Stability | $I_{\text{op}} < K \times I_{\text{res}}$ | — | Inequality must hold |
| 10 | CT Polarity | $I_1$ and $I_2$ in phase | — | Opposite polarity gives internal-fault-like behavior |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Overcurrent relays** form the backbone of radial distribution system protection and act as backup protection for distance and differential relays.
- **Differential relays** are the primary protection for transformers, generators, busbars, and large motors — they offer **unit protection**, meaning they detect faults *only inside* the protected zone and remain completely stable for external disturbances.
- **Bias characteristics** in modern numerical relays (e.g., ABB REF615, Siemens 7UT75, GE T60) implement the dual-slope theory to handle CT saturation during heavy external faults.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Problem 1: Operating Time of IDMT Overcurrent Relay

**Problem Statement:**
A 11 kV, 50 Hz, 3-phase transmission line is protected by an IDMT overcurrent relay. The CT ratio is 400/5 A, the relay plug setting is **125%**, and the time setting multiplier is **0.3**. A three-phase fault occurs on the line producing a fault current of **5000 A** (primary). The relay follows the **Standard Inverse** characteristic. Calculate the **operating time of the relay**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the CT secondary current at fault**

$$I_{s,\text{fault}} = \frac{I_{p,\text{fault}}}{\text{CT Ratio}} = \frac{5000}{400/5} = \frac{5000}{80} = 62.5 \text{ A}$$

*Explanation:* Dividing the primary current by the CT turns ratio gives the secondary current that flows into the relay coil.

**Step 2: Calculate the relay pickup (plug setting) current**

The CT secondary nominal current is:

$$I_{s,\text{nominal}} = 5 \text{ A (CT secondary rating)}$$

The plug setting of 125% means:

$$I_{\text{pickup}} = 1.25 \times 5 = 6.25 \text{ A}$$

*Explanation:* The "plug" is a calibrated tap on the relay that sets the threshold. 125% means the relay starts when secondary current exceeds 6.25 A.

**Step 3: Calculate the Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)**

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{s,\text{fault}}}{I_{\text{pickup}}} = \frac{62.5}{6.25} = 10$$

*Explanation:* The fault current is exactly 10 times the pickup current. PSM must be ≥ 1 for operation.

**Step 4: Apply the Standard Inverse formula**

$$t = \frac{0.14 \times \text{TSM}}{(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1}$$

Substituting the values:

$$t = \frac{0.14 \times 0.3}{(10)^{0.02} - 1}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate $10^{0.02}$**

$$10^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \times \ln 10} = e^{0.02 \times 2.3026} = e^{0.04605} = 1.0471$$

**Step 6: Compute the denominator**

$$1.0471 - 1 = 0.0471$$

**Step 7: Final calculation**

$$t = \frac{0.042}{0.0471} = 0.892 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 0.89 \text{ s}}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 2: Determining the Time Setting for Coordination

**Problem Statement:**
For the same system above, the operating time of the relay should be **0.6 seconds** to coordinate with the downstream relay. Calculate the required **TSM** for the same Standard Inverse characteristic.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Re-arrange the SI formula to solve for TSM**

$$t = \frac{0.14 \times \text{TSM}}{(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1} \implies \text{TSM} = \frac{t \times \left[(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1\right]}{0.14}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values** ($t = 0.6$ s, PSM = 10)

$$\text{TSM} = \frac{0.6 \times 0.0471}{0.14} = \frac{0.02826}{0.14} = 0.2019$$

$$\boxed{\text{TSM} \approx 0.2}$$

*Explanation:* The relay can be set to TSM = 0.2 to achieve a 0.6-second operating time at the given fault level.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 3: Differential Relay — Through Fault Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A 10 MVA, 11 kV / 0.4 kV delta-star transformer is protected by a percentage differential relay. The CT ratios are:
- HV side: 600/5 A
- LV side: 1500/5 A

A **through-fault (external)** produces secondary currents of $I_1 = 4.5$ A on the HV side and $I_2 = 4.5$ A on the LV side, both entering the protected zone. The relay has a **bias slope of 20%** and a **minimum operating current of 0.2 A**.

**Determine whether the relay will operate or restrain.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the operating current**

$$I_{\text{op}} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert = \vert 4.5 - 4.5 \vert = 0 \text{ A}$$

*Explanation:* For an ideal external fault with identical CTs, the operating current is exactly zero. In practice, a small value (e.g., 0.1 A) appears due to CT ratio mismatch and magnetizing current.

**Step 2: Calculate the restraining current**

$$I_{\text{res}} = \frac{\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert}{2} = \frac{4.5 + 4.5}{2} = 4.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Compute the relay's required operating threshold at this restraining level**

For a 20% bias:

$$I_{\text{op,required}} = 0.2 \times I_{\text{res}} = 0.2 \times 4.5 = 0.9 \text{ A}$$

But the relay also enforces a minimum operating current of 0.2 A (already exceeded by 0.9 A here, so 0.9 A dominates).

**Step 4: Compare the actual operating current with the required operating current**

$$I_{\text{op,actual}} = 0 \text{ A} < I_{\text{op,required}} = 0.9 \text{ A}$$

*Conclusion:* The relay **restrains (does not trip)**. This demonstrates the stability of differential protection for through-faults.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 4: Differential Relay — Internal Fault Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
For the same transformer and CTs, an **internal fault** produces $I_1 = 4.8$ A entering from HV side and $I_2 = 0$ A leaving from LV side (fault current flows to ground inside the transformer). Determine whether the relay operates.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Operating current**

$$I_{\text{op}} = \vert 4.8 - 0 \vert = 4.8 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Restraining current**

$$I_{\text{res}} = \frac{4.8 + 0}{2} = 2.4 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Required operating threshold**

$$I_{\text{op,required}} = 0.2 \times 2.4 = 0.48 \text{ A}$$

(The 0.2 A minimum operating current is also satisfied since 0.48 A > 0.2 A.)

**Step 4: Decision**

$$I_{\text{op,actual}} = 4.8 \text{ A} \gg I_{\text{op,required}} = 0.48 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{\text{The relay OPERATES (trips the circuit breaker).}}$$

*Explanation:* The operating point $(I_{\text{res}}, I_{\text{op}}) = (2.4, 4.8)$ lies well above the 20% bias line, ensuring reliable tripping for internal faults.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 5: CT Saturation Effect on Differential Relay

**Problem Statement:**
A 50 MVA, 110 kV / 11 kV transformer is protected by a biased differential relay. For an external 3-phase fault, the **ideal** current on each side is 4.2 A secondary. However, due to CT saturation on the HV side, the actual measured current is $I_1 = 3.5$ A. The bias characteristic has:
- Minimum operating current: 0.3 A
- Lower slope $K_1 = 25\%$ (for $I_{\text{res}} < 2.0$ A)
- Higher slope $K_2 = 60\%$ (for $I_{\text{res}} \geq 2.0$ A)

**Check whether the relay restrains or falsely trips.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Operating current due to CT mismatch**

$$I_{\text{op}} = \vert 4.2 - 3.5 \vert = 0.7 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Restraining current**

$$I_{\text{res}} = \frac{4.2 + 3.5}{2} = 3.85 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Determine the slope region**

Since $I_{\text{res}} = 3.85 \text{ A} > 2.0 \text{ A}$, we are in the **higher-slope region** with $K_2 = 60\%$.

**Step 4: Calculate the required operating current at this restraining level**

$$I_{\text{op,required}} = K_2 \times I_{\text{res}} = 0.6 \times 3.85 = 2.31 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5: Compare**

$$I_{\text{op,actual}} = 0.7 \text{ A} < I_{\text{op,required}} = 2.31 \text{ A}$$

*Conclusion:* The relay **correctly restrains**. The high bias slope (60%) at high currents absorbs the CT saturation error and prevents false tripping.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Problem 6: Selecting Plug Setting for Overcurrent Relay

**Problem Statement:**
A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz motor rated 100 kW has a full-load current of 180 A. The CT ratio is 200/5 A. Choose a suitable **plug setting** such that the relay does not operate under normal conditions but operates for a fault of **1200 A** primary. Compute the PSM for the fault.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Full-load secondary current**

$$I_{s,\text{FLC}} = \frac{180}{200/5} = \frac{180}{40} = 4.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Choose plug setting**

Standard practice is to set pickup ≥ 125% of FLC:

$$I_{\text{pickup}} \geq 1.25 \times 4.5 = 5.625 \text{ A}$$

Nearest available plug settings: 50%, 75%, 100%, 125%, 150%, 175%, 200% of 5 A.

- 125% of 5 A = 6.25 A
- 150% of 5 A = 7.5 A

We choose **150%** for additional safety margin.

**Step 3: Fault secondary current**

$$I_{s,\text{fault}} = \frac{1200}{40} = 30 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: PSM**

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{30}{7.5} = 4$$

$$\boxed{\text{Plug Setting} = 150\%, \quad \text{PSM} = 4}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Problem Type | Key Inputs | Primary Calculation | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Time | $I_F$, CT, PS, TSM, Curve | $t = A \cdot \text{TSM}/(\text{PSM}^n - 1)$ | $t > 0$, finite value |
| TSM Determination | $t$, PSM, Curve | $\text{TSM} = t \cdot (\text{PSM}^n - 1)/A$ | $0.1 \leq \text{TSM} \leq 1.0$ |
| Through-Fault Stability | $I_1 \approx I_2$ | $I_{\text{op}} < K \cdot I_{\text{res}}$ | Inequality holds |
| Internal Fault Trip | $I_2 \approx 0$ | $I_{\text{op}} \gg K \cdot I_{\text{res}}$ | Inequality reversed |
| CT Saturation | $I_1 \neq I_2$ (external) | Use high-slope region | $I_{\text{op}} < K_2 \cdot I_{\text{res}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: IDMT Overcurrent Relay Operational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Primary Current from Power System] --> B[Current Transformer CT]
    B --> C[Secondary Current 5A or 1A]
    C --> D[Relay Input Sensing Circuit]
    D --> E[Plug Setting Selector PS]
    E --> F[Compare with Pickup Threshold]
    F --> G{Is I_secondary >= I_pickup?}
    G -- No --> H[No Operation Relay Restrains]
    G -- Yes --> I[Calculate PSM = I_s / I_pickup]
    I --> J[Apply IDMT Curve from Memory]
    J --> K[Multiply by TSM 0.1 to 1.0]
    K --> L[Generate Trip Signal after t seconds]
    L --> M[CB Trip Coil Energized]
    M --> N[Circuit Breaker Opens]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style E fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
    style J fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C
    style N fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Percentage Biased Differential Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[HV Side CT] -->|I1| SUM1
    P2[LV Side CT] -->|I2| SUM1
    SUM1[Phasor Summation Node] --> OP[Operating Quantity Iop = |I1-I2|]
    SUM1 --> RES[Restraining Quantity Ires = |I1|+|I2|/2]

    OP --> CMP{Comparator}
    RES --> BIAS[Bias Characteristic Generator]
    BIAS -->|Threshold| CMP

    CMP -->|Iop >= Threshold| TRIP[Trip Signal Output]
    CMP -->|Iop less Threshold| REST[Restrain Signal]

    style P1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0277BD
    style P2 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0277BD
    style OP fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style RES fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style BIAS fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457
    style TRIP fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
    style REST fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

## 4.3 Operating-Restraining Characteristic of Biased Differential Relay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AX[X-Axis: Ires in pu] --> ORIGIN[Origin 0,0]
    ORIGIN --> SEGA[Ires less than 0.5 pu: Iop_min = 0.2 pu constant]
    SEGA --> SEGB[Ires from 0.5 to 2.0 pu: Lower Slope K1 = 25 percent]
    SEGB --> SEGC[Ires greater than 2.0 pu: Higher Slope K2 = 60 percent]
    SEGC --> ENDX[Stable Region: Relay Restrains]
    ORIGIN --> OPREG[Operating Region above curve: Relay Trips]

    style ORIGIN fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593
    style SEGA fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style SEGB fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#EF6C00
    style SEGC fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
    style ENDX fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style OPREG fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C
```

## 4.4 Comparative Flow: Through-Fault vs. Internal-Fault Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Fault Detected] --> Q1{Is Fault Internal or External?}
    Q1 -- External Through Fault --> A1[I1 and I2 are large and equal]
    A1 --> A2[Iop is small, near zero]
    A2 --> A3[Ires is large]
    A3 --> A4{Check: Iop greater than K times Ires?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Relay Restrains Correctly]
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[FALSE TRIP due to CT saturation]

    Q1 -- Internal Fault --> B1[I1 is large, I2 is near zero]
    B1 --> B2[Iop is large, equals I1]
    B2 --> B3[Ires is I1/2]
    B3 --> B4{Check: Iop greater than K times Ires?}
    B4 -- Yes --> B5[Relay Trips Correctly]
    B4 -- No --> B6[FAILURE TO TRIP Missed Fault]

    style START fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD
    style A5 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style A6 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
    style B5 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style B6 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
```

## 4.5 Standard IDMT Characteristic Curves (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PSM increasing rightward] --> B[Operating Time t decreasing upward inverse]
    B --> C{Which Curve Type?}
    C -- SI --> D[Standard Inverse: t = 0.14 TSM / PSM^0.02 - 1]
    C -- VI --> E[Very Inverse: t = 13.5 TSM / PSM - 1]
    C -- EI --> F[Extremely Inverse: t = 80 TSM / PSM^2 - 1]
    C -- DMT --> G[Definite Minimum Time: t = constant]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0277BD

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM) and Time Setting Multiplier (TSM). What is the role of each in IDMT relay coordination?

**Model Answer:**

**Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM):** PSM is the ratio of the secondary fault current flowing through the relay coil to the pickup (plug) current setting of the relay.

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{\text{secondary fault}}}{I_{\text{pickup}}}$$

**Time Setting Multiplier (TSM):** TSM is a dial/knob setting on the IDMT relay (range 0.1 to 1.0) that proportionally scales the operating time obtained from the standard inverse-time curve.

**Role in Coordination:** PSM determines *how many times* the fault current exceeds pickup, fixing a point on the time-PSM curve. TSM stretches this time uniformly to coordinate with adjacent upstream/downstream relays — ensuring the relay nearest the fault operates first (selectivity).

---

### Question 2: What is a percentage biased differential relay? Why is the bias characteristic dual-slope in modern numerical relays?

**Model Answer:**

A **percentage biased differential relay** operates when the operating current $I_{\text{op}} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$ exceeds a fixed *percentage* (the bias) of the restraining current $I_{\text{res}} = (\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert)/2$.

**Reason for Dual-Slope:**

1. **Low current region (small $I_{\text{res}}$):** A low bias slope (20-30%) provides high sensitivity to detect internal faults even at low load.
2. **High current region (large $I_{\text{res}}$):** A high bias slope (50-80%) accommodates the larger errors due to CT saturation during heavy through-faults, preventing false trips.

This combination gives both **sensitivity at low currents** and **stability at high currents**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: 14 Marks

**(a)** A 3-phase, 11 kV transmission line has a full-load current of 200 A and is protected by a relay with CT ratio 300/5 A, plug setting of 125%, and TSM of 0.4. The line is subjected to a 3-phase fault of 4000 A primary. Using the **Standard Inverse** characteristic of IEC 60255-151, calculate:
- (i) The PSM
- (ii) The operating time of the relay **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 25 MVA, 33 kV / 11 kV delta-star power transformer is protected by a percentage differential relay with 20% bias and minimum operating current of 0.3 A. The CT ratios are 500/5 A (HV) and 1500/5 A (LV). For an external 3-phase fault, both CTs ideally carry 4.0 A secondary, but the HV CT saturates and delivers only 3.2 A. Determine:
- (i) The operating and restraining currents
- (ii) Whether the relay trips or restrains
- (iii) The bias percentage actually seen by the relay **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Operating Time Calculation

**Step 1: CT secondary full-load current**

$$I_{s,\text{FLC}} = \frac{200}{300/5} = \frac{200}{60} = 3.333 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Pickup (Plug) current setting**

$$I_{\text{pickup}} = 1.25 \times 5 = 6.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Fault secondary current**

$$I_{s,\text{fault}} = \frac{4000}{60} = 66.67 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: PSM**

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{66.67}{6.25} = 10.67$$

**Step 5: Compute $\text{PSM}^{0.02}$**

$$10.67^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \times \ln(10.67)} = e^{0.02 \times 2.3678} = e^{0.04736} = 1.0485$$

**Step 6: Apply Standard Inverse formula**

$$t = \frac{0.14 \times 0.4}{1.0485 - 1} = \frac{0.056}{0.0485} = 1.155 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 1.16 \text{ s}, \quad \text{PSM} = 10.67}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Differential Relay Stability

**Step 1: Operating current**

$$I_{\text{op}} = \vert 3.2 - 4.0 \vert = 0.8 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Restraining current**

$$I_{\text{res}} = \frac{3.2 + 4.0}{2} = 3.6 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Compare with 20% bias threshold**

$$I_{\text{op,required}} = 0.20 \times 3.6 = 0.72 \text{ A}$$

Since $0.8 \text{ A} > 0.72 \text{ A}$, the relay operating point is **just above** the bias line. However, we also need to compare with the minimum operating current of 0.3 A — which is already exceeded.

**Step 4: Decision**

$$I_{\text{op,actual}} = 0.8 \text{ A} > I_{\text{op,required}} = 0.72 \text{ A}$$

With a 20% bias, the relay **will falsely trip**! This is precisely why modern numerical relays use a **dual-slope characteristic** — the high-current slope (50-80%) would have prevented this trip.

**Step 5: Actual bias percentage seen**

$$\text{Bias}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{I_{\text{op}}}{I_{\text{res}}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.8}{3.6} \times 100\% = 22.22\%$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{op}} = 0.8 \text{ A}, \quad I_{\text{res}} = 3.6 \text{ A}, \quad \text{False Trip Occurs, Actual Bias} = 22.22\%}$$

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain with neat characteristic curves the operating and restraining currents of a percentage biased differential relay. Show the dual-slope characteristic and explain why it is used in modern numerical relays. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An IDMT overcurrent relay has CT ratio 400/5 A, plug setting 100%, and TSM 0.5. A fault of 6000 A primary occurs on the protected line. For the **Very Inverse** characteristic, compute the operating time. If the desired operating time is 0.4 s for the same fault, find the new TSM. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Explanation of Biased Differential Relay

**Operating Current:** $I_{\text{op}} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$
**Restraining Current:** $I_{\text{res}} = (\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert)/2$

**Characteristic Curve (Schematic in Section 4.3):**
- X-axis: $I_{\text{res}}$ (in pu)
- Y-axis: $I_{\text{op}}$ (in pu)
- Region 1 ($I_{\text{res}} < 0.5$ pu): Constant minimum operating current.
- Region 2 ($0.5 \leq I_{\text{res}} \leq 2.0$ pu): Low slope (20-30%) — high sensitivity.
- Region 3 ($I_{\text{res}} > 2.0$ pu): High slope (60-80%) — high stability.

**Why Dual Slope:**
- For small through-faults, magnetizing current and CT errors are small → low slope is sufficient.
- For heavy through-faults, CT saturation produces large spurious differences → high slope absorbs these errors and prevents false trips.
- Internal faults always produce $I_{\text{op}} \gg K \cdot I_{\text{res}}$ for both slopes, so sensitivity is preserved.

---

#### Part (b) — Very Inverse IDMT Calculation

**Step 1: CT secondary current at fault**

$$I_{s,\text{fault}} = \frac{6000}{400/5} = \frac{6000}{80} = 75 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Pickup current at 100% plug setting**

$$I_{\text{pickup}} = 1.0 \times 5 = 5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: PSM**

$$\text{PSM} = \frac{75}{5} = 15$$

**Step 4: Apply Very Inverse formula**

$$t = \frac{13.5 \times 0.5}{15 - 1} = \frac{6.75}{14} = 0.482 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Find new TSM for t = 0.4 s**

$$0.4 = \frac{13.5 \times \text{TSM}}{14} \implies \text{TSM} = \frac{0.4 \times 14}{13.5} = \frac{5.6}{13.5} = 0.415$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{old}} = 0.482 \text{ s}, \quad \text{TSM}_{\text{new}} = 0.415}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students often forget to convert primary fault current to secondary current using the CT ratio. This single mistake causes cascading errors in PSM, time, and final answer. **Always show CT secondary current first.**
> 2. **Curve Misidentification:** KTU problems specify "Standard Inverse," "Very Inverse," or "Extremely Inverse" — using the wrong constants (0.14, 13.5, 80) is a **3-mark penalty** in ESE.
> 3. **TSM Range Violation:** TSM must be between 0.1 and 1.0. If a calculated TSM falls outside, the problem is ill-posed — check your arithmetic.
> 4. **Differential Relay Polarity:** Students commonly assume $I_1$ and $I_2$ have the same polarity for internal faults. The convention is: **for internal fault, $I_2$ becomes near-zero**, not the negative of $I_1$. Drawing the polarity dots incorrectly leads to wrong $I_{\text{op}}$ values.
> 5. **Dual-Slope Oversight:** In biased differential relay problems, students often use a single 20% slope for all currents. KTU questions at the 14-mark level frequently test the **transition point** between slopes — losing 3-4 marks for ignoring it.
> 6. **PSM must be ≥ 1:** If PSM < 1, the relay does not operate. The formula $t = A \cdot \text{TSM}/(\text{PSM}^n - 1)$ gives a negative or infinite value — students should explicitly state "relay does not operate" rather than blindly applying the formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Key Takeaways — Quick Revision Checklist

- [x] **PSM Definition:** PSM = Secondary fault current ÷ Pickup current (must be ≥ 1)
- [x] **TSM Range:** 0.1 ≤ TSM ≤ 1.0 (typically 0.1 increments)
- [x] **CT Ratio Usage:** Always convert primary to secondary before computing PSM
- [x] **Standard Inverse (SI):** $t = 0.14 \cdot \text{TSM} / (\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1)$ — used for general overcurrent
- [x] **Very Inverse (VI):** $t = 13.5 \cdot \text{TSM} / (\text{PSM} - 1)$ — better discrimination between faults and inrush
- [x] **Extremely Inverse (EI):** $t = 80 \cdot \text{TSM} / (\text{PSM}^2 - 1)$ — used for transformer inrush, fuses
- [x] **Plug Setting Selection:** Always ≥ 125% of full-load secondary current to avoid nuisance trips
- [x] **Differential Operating Current:** $I_{\text{op}} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$
- [x] **Differential Restraining Current:** $I_{\text{res}} = (\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert)/2$
- [x] **Through-Fault Stability:** $I_{\text{op,actual}} < K \cdot I_{\text{res}}$ → Restrain
- [x] **Internal Fault Trip:** $I_{\text{op,actual}} > K \cdot I_{\text{res}}$ → Trip
- [x] **Dual-Slope Bias:** Low slope for low currents, high slope for high currents (CT saturation handling)
- [x] **Minimum Operating Current:** $I_{\text{op,min}}$ sets the floor for the bias curve
- [x] **Bias Percentage:** $K = (I_{\text{op}} / I_{\text{res}}) \times 100\%$
- [x] **CT Polarity:** Mark dots on CTs; for internal fault, $I_2$ reduces to zero, not inverts
- [x] **CT Saturation Effect:** Causes $I_1 \neq I_2$ during external faults — high bias slope prevents false trip
- [x] **KTU Board Expectation:** Always show step-by-step calculation; do not write only the final answer
- [x] **Unit Consistency:** Amperes for currents, seconds for time, pu for normalized quantities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, examiners allocate **2 marks for the diagram** (differential relay schematic or characteristic curve), **2 marks for formulas used**, and the remaining **10 marks for numerical steps**. Always include a labeled diagram — it acts as a "free" 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Differential Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Differential Relay** is a unit protection scheme that operates based on the vector difference between two or more similar electrical quantities (currents or voltages) entering and leaving a protected zone. Under normal (healthy) operating conditions, the phasor difference of these quantities is theoretically zero; any deviation beyond a predetermined threshold indicates an internal fault within the protected zone, causing the relay to issue a trip command.

Mathematically, the operating criterion of an ideal differential relay is:

$$I_{diff} = \left| \vec{I}_1 - \vec{I}_2 \right| > I_{pickup}$$

where $\vec{I}_1$ and $\vec{I}_2$ are the phasor currents at the two ends of the protected zone, and $I_{pickup}$ is the minimum operating current setting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Differential protection is classified as the **primary form of unit protection** in KTU Module 1. It is *unit protection* because its zone of coverage is **precisely defined** and **independent of adjacent system operating conditions or fault levels**. The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on classifying the various types and their characteristic curves.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipe Balance" Intuition

Imagine a water pipeline with a flow meter installed at both the **inlet** and the **outlet** of a protected section (say, a filter).

- **Healthy condition:** Water enters at 100 L/min from the left and exits at 100 L/min on the right. The *balance* is perfect.
- **Leak inside the protected section (the "fault"):** Water enters at 100 L/min but only 80 L/min exits on the right. The 20 L/min "difference" is the leakage current — a fault.
- **Differential Relay:** This is the *electronic accountant* that subtracts the two readings. If the difference is greater than some small tolerance (e.g., 5 L/min for sensor noise), it triggers an alarm or shutoff valve (the trip coil).

In an electrical power system, the two ends of the protected apparatus (e.g., a transformer winding, a generator armature, a transmission line) are equipped with **Current Transformers (CTs)**. The CT secondary currents are compared in a relay. Any mismatch (other than the small no-load excitation current of a transformer) signals trouble.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Unit" Protection?**
> Because the relay only "sees" the protected zone between the two CTs. A fault *outside* this zone (an "external fault" or "through fault") will not produce a difference, and the relay will *correctly restrain* from operating.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Differential Protection

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **CT Secondary Rated Current** | **1 A or 5 A** | Industry-standard CT secondary |
| **Burden of CT** | ≤ 10 VA (typical) | Loading limit for accuracy |
| **Operating Time** | **15 – 30 ms** | For high-speed differential relays |
| **Restraint Ratio (Bias)** | 15% – 60% | Slope of biased characteristic |
| **Through-Fault Current** | Up to **20 × In** | Capability of CT to remain linear |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Note:** The term "**pickup**" refers to the threshold current above which the relay operates. Modern numerical/digital relays (per KTU Module 2 & 3 of PEEET631) use **adaptive pickup** based on real-time system conditions.

## 1.4 The Universal Need for Types — Why One Relay Doesn't Fit All

A simple, unbiased (overcurrent) differential element will fail in practice for these reasons:

1. **CT Ratio Mismatches** — The two CTs rarely have exactly matched ratios.
2. **CT Magnetization Errors** — During heavy external faults, one CT may saturate differently from the other.
3. **Tap-Changer Variation (Transformers)** — The turns ratio changes with tap position.
4. **Magnetizing Inrush (Transformers)** — Inrush current appears only on one side, falsely producing a difference.
5. **Over-Fluxing (5th harmonic)** — CT saturation produces even harmonics.

Hence, multiple **types** of differential relays have been engineered, each addressing one or more of these issues. The classification of **types** of differential relays is the topic of this lesson.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Differential Relays

Differential relays are classified along several orthogonal axes:

### Axis A — By the Quantities Compared
- **Current Differential Relays** (most common)
- **Voltage Differential Relays** (used for busbars with high impedance grounding)

### Axis B — By the Operating Principle / Characteristic
1. **Plain (Unbiased) Differential Relays**
2. **Biased (Percentage) Differential Relays**
3. **Dual-Slope Biased Differential Relays**
4. **Harmonic-Restraint Differential Relays**
5. **High-Impedance Differential Relays**
6. **Variable-Percentage / Multi-Restraint Relays**

### Axis C — By the Protected Apparatus
- **Transformer Differential Relays**
- **Generator Differential Relays**
- **Busbar Differential Relays**
- **Line (Pilot) Differential Relays**
- **Motor Differential Relays**

## 2.2 Detailed Operational Analysis of Each Type

### 2.2.1 Plain (Unbiased) Differential Relay

**Construction:** A simple overcurrent element fed by the *spill current* (the difference of the two CT secondary currents).

**Characteristic Curve:** A single horizontal line at $I_{diff} = I_{pickup}$. Operates for any spill current above pickup, regardless of through-current.

**Problem:** Highly sensitive to CT saturation during external faults. If one CT saturates while the other doesn't, a large false spill current flows, causing **false tripping**.

**Modern Relevance:** Obsolete in pure form. Found only as a *building block* in numerical relays.

### 2.2.2 Biased (Percentage) Differential Relay

**Construction:** Two coils on a single electromagnet:
- **Operating coil** (operated by spill/difference current $I_{diff}$)
- **Restraint coil** (operated by average/through current $I_{rest}$)

**Characteristic Curve:** A straight line through the origin with slope equal to the **bias setting** $K_b$:

$$I_{op} = K_b \cdot I_{rest}$$

The relay operates when the spill current exceeds the operating threshold *plus* the bias-derived value:

$$I_{diff} > I_{pickup} + K_b \cdot I_{rest}$$

**Why It Works:** During external (through) faults, both $I_{diff}$ and $I_{rest}$ are large, but the relay demands a *proportionally larger* spill current. CT saturation produces a small spill, but the high restraint prevents false operation.

### 2.2.3 Dual-Slope (Biased with Two Slopes) Differential Relay

**Characteristic Curve:** Two straight-line segments.

- **Initial slope** $K_1$ (typically 10% – 30%) for low through-currents (normal load).
- **Steeper slope** $K_2$ (typically 50% – 80%) for high through-currents (heavy external faults, beyond the CT linear limit).

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$I_{op} = \begin{cases} K_1 \cdot I_{rest}, & 0 \le I_{rest} \le I_{break} \\ K_2 \cdot I_{rest}, & I_{rest} > I_{break} \end{cases}$$

where $I_{break}$ is the "break-point" or "knee" current (e.g., 2.0 pu).

**Real-World Utility:** This is the **industry standard** for transformer and generator differential protection, found in nearly all modern numerical relays (e.g., SEL 387, GE T60, ABB RET615).

### 2.2.4 Harmonic-Restraint Differential Relay

**Motivation:** When a transformer is energized, the magnetizing inrush current can be 6–10 × full-load current. This inrush appears *only on the energized side*, producing a large false differential current. If treated as a fault, the relay will trip on every energization.

**Engineering Insight:** The inrush current is rich in **2nd harmonic** (typically 15%–70% of fundamental), while genuine internal faults contain mostly fundamental and small 2nd harmonic.

**Operating Logic:**

$$\text{Trip if} \quad \frac{I_{diff,1}}{I_{diff,total}} > K_{th} \quad \text{AND} \quad \frac{I_{2nd}}{I_{1st}} < K_{2nd}$$

Typical values: $K_{th} = 0.15$–$0.30$, $K_{2nd} = 0.15$–$0.20$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Refinement:** Some relays also use **5th-harmonic restraint** to detect over-excitation conditions, which can also produce false differential currents.

### 2.2.5 High-Impedance Differential Relay

**Operating Principle:** A high-value resistor (e.g., 2000 Ω) is inserted in the differential circuit. Under healthy conditions or external faults, the CT secondary currents circulate, producing only a small voltage across the high-impedance resistor — insufficient to operate the sensitive voltage-sensing element.

During an **internal fault**, one CT supplies current into the high-impedance branch; the voltage rises to several hundred volts, reliably operating the relay.

**Voltage Across Stabilizing Resistor:**

$$V_{relay} = I_{f,secondary} \cdot R_{stab}$$

**Stabilizing Resistor Selection (Per Auxiliary CT Method):**

$$R_{stab} = \frac{V_{s}}{I_{f,primary}/\text{CT ratio}}$$

where $V_s$ is the relay voltage setting.

**Applications:** Busbar protection, restricted-earth-fault protection, generator stator winding protection.

**Advantage:** Immune to CT saturation; extreme stability for external faults.

### 2.2.6 Variable-Percentage (Dual-Bias / Multi-Restraint) Differential Relay

**Concept:** Combines the dual-slope characteristic with harmonic restraint and CT-saturation detection in a single characteristic. Some relays also add a **cross-blocking** feature: if one phase detects inrush, all three phases are blocked, ensuring single-phase energization of a three-phase transformer doesn't cause false tripping on adjacent phases.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{diff} = \vert \vec{I}_1 - \vec{I}_2 \vert$ | Spill / operating current | A or pu |
| 2 | $I_{rest} = \frac{\vert \vec{I}_1 \vert + \vert \vec{I}_2 \vert}{2}$ | Average / through current | A or pu |
| 3 | $I_{op} = K_b \cdot I_{rest}$ | Biased operating current | A |
| 4 | $I_{op} = K_1 I_{rest} + K_2(I_{rest} - I_{break})$ | Dual-slope operating current | A |
| 5 | $\% \text{Bias} = \frac{I_{diff}}{I_{rest}} \times 100$ | Bias percentage (slope) | % |
| 6 | $I_{2nd}/I_{1st} < 0.15$ to $0.20$ | 2nd-harmonic restraint ratio | pu |
| 7 | $V_{relay} = I_f \cdot R_{stab}$ | High-impedance relay voltage | V |
| 8 | $R_{stab} = \frac{V_s}{I_{f,sec}}$ | Stabilizing resistor design | Ω |
| 9 | $I_{pkp} = 0.10$ to $0.50 \cdot I_n$ | Typical pickup setting | A |
| 10 | $\text{CT knee-point} > 2 \cdot I_{f,\max}$ | CT linear operation limit | V, A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU Numerical Problems:** The relationship $I_{diff}$ vs. $I_{rest}$ is plotted on a **bias characteristic plane**. The operating region is *above* the characteristic line. Master drawing this plot — it appears in nearly every KTU question on biased differential relays.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transformer differential protection** uses dual-slope + harmonic restraint.
- **Generator differential** uses dual-slope only (no inrush issue).
- **Busbar differential** uses high-impedance or low-impedance biased schemes.
- **Transmission line differential** (87L) is used in EHV cables and short lines, communicating via **pilot wires, fiber optics, or microwave**.
- **Restricted Earth Fault (REF)** is a special differential for grounded-wye transformer windings.
