# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Operating Criterion of an Ideal Differential Element

**Statement:** An ideal differential relay operates when the phasor difference of the two CT secondary currents exceeds a threshold.

**Step 1 — Define the protected zone currents.**
Let $I_1$ and $I_2$ be the phasor currents entering and leaving the protected zone.

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) to the protected zone.**
For any conductor, KCL states that the algebraic sum of currents leaving a node is zero:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k = 0$$

Applied to a two-terminal zone:

$$I_1 + I_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_1 = -I_2$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that CTs invert polarity for consistency.**
With matched CT ratios, the CT secondary currents $\vec{I}_1^{''}$ and $\vec{I}_2^{''}$ flow *into* the relay. They are equal in magnitude and opposite in polarity if the protected zone is healthy. Hence the spill current is:

$$I_{diff} = \vert \vec{I}_1^{''} - \vec{I}_2^{''} \vert = 0$$

**Step 4 — Define the operating criterion.**

$$I_{diff} > I_{pickup} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TRIP}$$

This is the **fundamental** differential principle.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Slope of a Biased Differential Characteristic

**Statement:** Show that the bias percentage is the slope of the $I_{diff}$ vs. $I_{rest}$ characteristic.

**Step 1 — Define the two quantities.**

$$I_{diff} = \vert \vec{I}_1 - \vec{I}_2 \vert$$

$$I_{rest} = \frac{\vert \vec{I}_1 \vert + \vec{I}_2 \vert}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Define the operating boundary.**
The relay operates when:

$$I_{diff} = I_{pickup} + K_b \cdot I_{rest}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the slope.**
This is a linear equation of the form $y = mx + c$, with $y = I_{diff}$, $x = I_{rest}$, $c = I_{pickup}$, and $m = K_b$.

**Step 4 — Therefore, the bias percentage is:**

$$\% \text{Bias} = \frac{I_{diff} - I_{pickup}}{I_{rest}} \times 100 = K_b \times 100$$

For $K_b = 0.30$, the bias is **30%**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Dual-Slope Operating Characteristic

**Statement:** Derive the equation of a dual-slope characteristic with break-point $I_{bp}$.

**Step 1 — Define piecewise regions.**

$$I_{op} = \begin{cases} I_{p0} + K_1 \cdot I_{rest}, & 0 \le I_{rest} \le I_{bp} \\ I_{p0} + K_1 \cdot I_{bp} + K_2 (I_{rest} - I_{bp}), & I_{rest} > I_{bp} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Ensure continuity at $I_{rest} = I_{bp}$.**
At the break-point, both expressions must yield the same value:

$$I_{p0} + K_1 \cdot I_{bp} = I_{p0} + K_1 \cdot I_{bp} + K_2(I_{bp} - I_{bp})$$

$$I_{p0} + K_1 \cdot I_{bp} = I_{p0} + K_1 \cdot I_{bp} \quad \checkmark$$

The characteristic is **continuous** at the break-point.

**Step 3 — Realistic numeric example.**
Take $I_{p0} = 0.20$ pu, $K_1 = 0.20$, $K_2 = 0.80$, $I_{bp} = 2.0$ pu.

For $I_{rest} = 1.0$ pu:

$$I_{op} = 0.20 + 0.20 \times 1.0 = 0.40 \text{ pu}$$

For $I_{rest} = 5.0$ pu:

$$I_{op} = 0.20 + 0.20 \times 2.0 + 0.80 \times (5.0 - 2.0) = 0.20 + 0.40 + 2.40 = 3.00 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
At low load, the relay tolerates 20% mismatch (CT error margin). At high fault current, it tolerates 80% mismatch (CT saturation margin). The relay trips only if the *spill current* exceeds the operating value at the corresponding through-current.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: High-Impedance Relay Voltage Equation

**Statement:** Derive the voltage developed across a stabilizing resistor in a high-impedance busbar differential scheme.

**Step 1 — Consider an internal fault with primary current $I_f$.**
The CT on the faulted feeder delivers secondary current $I_f^{''}$ into the high-impedance branch. Other CTs (on healthy feeders) are effectively open-circuited because their currents circulate *locally* through the fault path.

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's law.**

$$V_{stab} = I_f^{''} \cdot R_{stab}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute CT ratio relationship.**

$$V_{stab} = \frac{I_f}{N_{CT}} \cdot R_{stab}$$

**Step 4 — Set $V_{stab} \ge V_{pickup}$.**
The relay operates when:

$$\frac{I_f}{N_{CT}} \cdot R_{stab} \ge V_{pickup} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{stab} \le \frac{V_{pickup} \cdot N_{CT}}{I_{f,min}}$$

where $I_{f,min}$ is the minimum internal fault current to be detected.

**Step 5 — Add a margin for CT saturation.**

$$V_{knee-point,CT} \ge 2 \cdot V_{stab,max}$$

This ensures the CT remains linear (does not saturate) during the maximum external fault.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Types at a Glance

| Relay Type | Operating Quantity | Stability for External Faults | Inrush Handling | CT Saturation Tolerance | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plain Differential | $I_{diff}$ | Poor | None | Very poor | Educational, simple |
| Biased (% Differential) | $I_{diff}$ vs. $I_{rest}$ | Good | Poor | Good | Generators, motors |
| Dual-Slope Biased | Two-region $I_{diff}$ | Excellent | Poor | Excellent | Transformers, generators |
| Harmonic Restraint | Fundamental vs. 2nd harm. | Excellent | **Excellent** | Excellent | Transformers |
| High Impedance | Voltage across $R_{stab}$ | **Outstanding** | N/A | **Outstanding** | Busbars, REF |
| Variable Percentage | Multi-criterion | Outstanding | Excellent | Excellent | All major apparatus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Numerical relays now combine **dual-slope + harmonic restraint + CT-saturation detection + 5th-harmonic over-fluxing detection + REF** into a single logic function (ANSI/IEEE Code **87**). The KTU examiner expects students to know which *combination* is appropriate for which apparatus.

## 3.6 Numerical Example — Biased Differential Setting Verification

**Problem:** A 5 MVA, 11 kV / 0.4 kV delta-star transformer is protected by a biased differential relay with bias = 25% and pickup = 0.2 pu (on 5 A CT secondary). Determine whether the relay will restrain or trip for the following operating conditions:

- **Case 1:** Healthy operation; $I_1^{''} = 4.8$ A, $I_2^{''} = 5.0$ A.
- **Case 2:** External (LV-side) fault of 1500 A primary; $I_1^{''} = 4.5$ A, $I_2^{''} = 4.0$ A (CT saturation).
- **Case 3:** Internal (LV-winding) fault of 800 A primary; $I_1^{''} = 3.0$ A, $I_2^{''} = 0.5$ A.

**Solution:**

**Case 1 — Healthy:**
$I_{diff} = \vert 4.8 - 5.0 \vert = 0.2$ A
$I_{rest} = (4.8 + 5.0)/2 = 4.9$ A
$I_{op,required} = 0.2 \text{ pu} \times 5 \text{ A} + 0.25 \times 4.9 = 1.0 + 1.225 = 2.225$ A
Since $I_{diff} = 0.2$ A < $I_{op,required} = 2.225$ A → **RESTRAIN** ✓

**Case 2 — External Fault with CT Saturation:**
$I_{diff} = \vert 4.5 - 4.0 \vert = 0.5$ A
$I_{rest} = (4.5 + 4.0)/2 = 4.25$ A
$I_{op,required} = 1.0 + 0.25 \times 4.25 = 1.0 + 1.0625 = 2.0625$ A
Since $I_{diff} = 0.5$ A < $I_{op,required} = 2.0625$ A → **RESTRAIN** ✓
(Bias characteristic saved the relay from false tripping despite CT saturation.)

**Case 3 — Internal Fault:**
$I_{diff} = \vert 3.0 - 0.5 \vert = 2.5$ A
$I_{rest} = (3.0 + 0.5)/2 = 1.75$ A
$I_{op,required} = 1.0 + 0.25 \times 1.75 = 1.0 + 0.4375 = 1.4375$ A
Since $I_{diff} = 2.5$ A > $I_{op,required} = 1.4375$ A → **TRIP** ✓

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture of the differential relay family, showing how each type builds on the operating-quantity extraction stage and adds progressively more sophisticated restraint mechanisms.

```mermaid
    B1 --> C6[Variable Percentage Differential]

    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1

    D2 --> D3[Second Harmonic Detector]

```

## 4.2 Operational State Diagram — Differential Relay Decision Flow

This diagram shows how a digital differential relay (in a numerical IED) makes a trip decision. The horizontal plane represents the $I_{diff}$ vs. $I_{rest}$ characteristic.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MeasureCurrents
    MeasureCurrents --> ComputeIdiff: Calculate spill current
    ComputeIdiff --> ComputeIrest: Calculate through current
    ComputeIrest --> CheckOperatingRegion: Compare with dual slope
    CheckOperatingRegion --> Restrain: Operating region not entered
    CheckOperatingRegion --> CheckHarmonics: Operating region entered
    CheckHarmonics --> Restrain: Inrush detected second harmonic high
    CheckHarmonics --> CheckOverflux: Harmonics normal
    CheckOverflux --> Restrain: Fifth harmonic high overflux
    CheckOverflux --> Trip: All conditions satisfied
    Restrain --> [*]
    Trip --> [*]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a percentage differential relay. Why is it preferred over a plain differential relay for protecting power transformers?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
A percentage (biased) differential relay uses *two* coils — an *operating coil* carrying the spill/difference current $I_{diff}$ and a *restraint coil* carrying the through/restraint current $I_{rest}$. The relay operates only when $I_{diff}$ exceeds a fixed pickup *plus* a fixed *percentage* of the through current. **(1 mark)**
It is preferred over the plain differential relay because, during heavy external (through) faults, CT saturation may produce a large false spill current. A plain differential relay would false-trip. The biased relay's high restraint at high through-currents *prevents* such false operation while retaining sensitivity to internal faults. **(2 marks)**

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by harmonic restraint in transformer differential protection? Which harmonic is most significant and why?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
Harmonic restraint is a logic technique in which the relay *blocks* its trip command when the differential current contains a significant proportion of a particular harmonic, indicating a *non-fault* condition. **(1 mark)**
The **2nd harmonic** is most significant because the magnetizing inrush current of a transformer is rich in 2nd-harmonic content (typically 15%–70% of fundamental). Inrush appears as a false differential current and could cause false tripping. **(2 marks)**
The 5th harmonic is also restrained to detect *over-fluxing* (over-excitation) conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain with characteristic curves the operating principle of a **dual-slope biased percentage differential relay**. Clearly label the operating, restraining, and break-point regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 5 MVA, 11 kV / 0.433 kV, Dyn11 transformer is protected by a biased differential relay with $I_{pickup} = 0.30$ pu and a bias of 30%. The CT ratios on the HV and LV sides are 100/5 A and 1500/5 A respectively. Determine whether the relay will trip for the following conditions (currents are secondary CT currents):
- (i) Internal fault: $I_{HV} = 4.5$ A, $I_{LV} = 3.0$ A
- (ii) External fault with CT saturation: $I_{HV} = 4.0$ A, $I_{LV} = 3.7$ A **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A dual-slope biased differential relay has a *piecewise-linear* characteristic in the $I_{rest}$ vs. $I_{diff}$ plane. **(1 mark)**

The characteristic consists of two straight-line segments joined at a "break-point" $I_{bp}$ (also called the "knee" point), typically set at 2.0 pu. **(1 mark)**

- For $0 \le I_{rest} \le I_{bp}$: a *gentle* slope $K_1$ (typically 20%–30%) accommodates normal-load CT errors and tap-changer variations. **(1 mark)**
- For $I_{rest} > I_{bp}$: a *steeper* slope $K_2$ (typically 50%–80%) provides extra security against heavy external faults where CT saturation is severe. **(1 mark)**

The operating equation is:
$$I_{op} = I_{pickup} + K_1 I_{rest}, \quad 0 \le I_{rest} \le I_{bp}$$
$$I_{op} = I_{pickup} + K_1 I_{bp} + K_2 (I_{rest} - I_{bp}), \quad I_{rest} > I_{bp}$$

**(1 mark)** for the equations, **(1 mark)** for sketching the curve with all regions labeled (operate, restrain, break-point, pickup intercept), and **(1 mark)** for explaining the *real-world necessity* of two slopes — CT saturation behavior changes with current magnitude.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Case (i) — Internal fault:**

$$I_{diff} = \vert 4.5 - 3.0 \vert = 1.5 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{rest} = \frac{4.5 + 3.0}{2} = 3.75 \text{ A}$$

Required operate current (assuming 30% bias, single slope for simplicity):

$$I_{op} = 0.30 \text{ pu} \times 5 \text{ A} + 0.30 \times 3.75 = 1.5 + 1.125 = 2.625 \text{ A}$$

Since $I_{diff} = 1.5$ A < $I_{op} = 2.625$ A, the relay **RESTRAINS** in this calculation.

*Note:* Real relays use 2nd-harmonic detection and dual-slope features; for a textbook KTU problem, the *direction* of result is what matters. The above calculation indicates the boundary; in practice the fault is *just below* the trip threshold. **(3.5 marks)**

**Case (ii) — External fault with CT saturation:**

$$I_{diff} = \vert 4.0 - 3.7 \vert = 0.3 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{rest} = \frac{4.0 + 3.7}{2} = 3.85 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{op} = 1.5 + 0.30 \times 3.85 = 1.5 + 1.155 = 2.655 \text{ A}$$

Since $I_{diff} = 0.3$ A < $I_{op} = 2.655$ A → **RESTRAIN** ✓ (correct security against external fault) **(3.5 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **high-impedance differential scheme** used for busbar protection. Derive the expression for the stabilizing resistor and state why a Metrosil (non-linear resistor) is connected across the relay. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **harmonic-restraint** technique used in transformer differential relays. Why is 2nd-harmonic restraint necessary even after using a biased characteristic? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The high-impedance differential scheme uses a *high-value* stabilizing resistor (typically 1000–3000 Ω) in series with a sensitive voltage-sensing relay connected across the parallel combination of all CT secondaries. **(1 mark)**

**Operating principle:**
- For an *external* fault, all CT secondary currents circulate *locally* through the differential circuit; only a small voltage (millivolts) develops across $R_{stab}$, insufficient to operate the relay. **(1 mark)**
- For an *internal* fault, the CT on the faulted feeder *pushes* current through $R_{stab}$ (since other CTs cannot return the current), developing a *large* voltage (tens to hundreds of volts) that operates the relay. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation of $R_{stab}$:**

Setting the relay voltage equal to the pickup voltage:

$$V_{relay} = \frac{I_{f,primary}}{N_{CT}} \cdot R_{stab} = V_{pickup}$$

$$\boxed{R_{stab} = \frac{V_{pickup} \cdot N_{CT}}{I_{f,min,primary}}}$$

**(1 mark)** for the derivation, **(1 mark)** for choosing CT knee-point voltage $V_{knee} \ge 2 \cdot I_{f,max,sec} \cdot R_{stab}$.

**Role of Metrosil (non-linear resistor):**
A Metrosil is connected *in parallel* with the relay coil to *limit* the maximum voltage that can develop across the relay during a heavy internal fault. **(1 mark)**
This protects the relay from insulation breakdown and limits the secondary EMF that the CTs must support, preventing CT saturation from disabling the scheme. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Block diagram description (verbal):**
The CT secondary currents are summed to form $I_{diff}$. This spill current is passed through an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** and a **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** algorithm (in numerical relays) or a **tuned LC filter** (in static relays). The FFT extracts the **fundamental ($I_1$)** and **2nd-harmonic ($I_2$)** components. A comparator computes the ratio $I_2 / I_1$. If this ratio exceeds a *threshold* (typically 15%–20%), the relay is *blocked* (restrained); otherwise, the relay is *allowed* to evaluate the trip condition from the fundamental alone. **(2 marks)**

**Why 2nd-harmonic restraint is necessary even with bias:**
The biased characteristic *does* provide security against *external* faults, but it *cannot* distinguish between an *internal fault* and *magnetizing inrush*. During inrush, the inrush current is *one-sided* (flows only into the energized winding) and is *not* a "through" current; therefore, the biased characteristic would *trip*, because $I_{diff}$ is large while $I_{rest}$ is small (restraint is small). **(2 marks)**

The biased characteristic helps for external faults; the harmonic restraint helps for inrush. They are *complementary* and *both* are required for robust transformer protection. **(1 mark)**

Additional 2 marks for: (a) identifying that 5th harmonic is also used for overfluxing restraint (1 mark) and (b) explaining that "cross-blocking" is used so that inrush detected on one phase blocks all three phases (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $I_{diff}$ and $I_{rest}$:** Students often swap these in calculations. *Remember:*
>    - $I_{diff} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$ → always the *smaller* quantity
>    - $I_{rest} = (\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert)/2$ → always the *larger* quantity
>
> 2. **Drawing the wrong characteristic shape:** For *biased* relays, students draw a horizontal line. For *plain* relays, students draw a sloped line. **Plain = horizontal; Biased = sloped.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting the pickup intercept:** The sloped line does *not* pass through the origin; it has a y-intercept of $I_{pickup}$.
>
> 4. **Not labeling units (pu vs A):** The KTU examiner *always* deducts marks for missing unit annotations.
>
> 5. **Omitting the "why" behind inrush handling:** Simply stating "2nd harmonic is used" is worth 1 mark. Adding *why* (inrush contains 2nd harmonic, internal faults do not) is worth the full 2–3 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing up high-impedance vs low-impedance biased:** High-impedance = voltage sensing across a stabilizing resistor; Low-impedance = current-operated biased relay. These are *not* the same scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Types of Differential Relays"**
>
> ☐ **Unit Protection Definition:** Differential protection operates only on faults *within* the defined zone; it is *independent* of system conditions.
>
> ☐ **Core Quantities:** $I_{diff} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$ (operating quantity); $I_{rest} = (\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert)/2$ (through quantity).
>
> ☐ **Plain Differential:** Operates on $I_{diff}$ alone. *Sensitive but unstable* for external faults due to CT saturation.
>
> ☐ **Biased (Percentage) Differential:** Operating boundary $I_{diff} = I_{pickup} + K_b \cdot I_{rest}$. Slope = bias percentage.
>
> ☐ **Dual-Slope:** Two slopes — gentle at low through-current, steep at high through-current. *Industry standard.*
>
> ☐ **Harmonic Restraint:** 2nd-harmonic blocks trip for inrush; 5th-harmonic blocks trip for overfluxing.
>
> ☐ **High-Impedance:** Voltage-operated; uses $R_{stab}$ and Metrosil. Used for busbars and REF.
>
> ☐ **Variable Percentage:** Combines bias + harmonic restraint + CT saturation detection + cross-blocking.
>
> ☐ **Key CT Rule:** CT knee-point voltage $V_{knee} \ge 2 \cdot I_{f,max,sec} \cdot R_{stab}$ for linear operation.
>
> ☐ **Standard Settings:** $I_{pickup} = 0.10$–$0.50$ pu; Bias $K_b = 0.20$–$0.30$ (low region), $0.50$–$0.80$ (high region).
>
> ☐ **Applications Mapping:** Generators → dual-slope; Transformers → variable percentage; Busbars → high-impedance; Lines → pilot differential.
>
> ☐ **ANSI/IEEE Code 87** is the universal relay function number for differential protection. Sub-codes: 87T (transformer), 87G (generator), 87B (busbar), 87L (line), 87M (motor), 87N (neutral/restricted earth fault).
>
> ☐ **Operating Time:** 15–30 ms for high-speed numerical differential relays — among the *fastest* protection functions.
>
> ☐ **Numerical Relays:** Modern IEDs implement all these types as *software logic* using sampled CT currents, FFT, and adaptive algorithms — the underlying principles remain identical to the electromechanical and static predecessors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance Relays: Impedance Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distance Relays: Impedance Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Impedance Relay** is a non-directional distance protection relay whose operation is governed by the measured **apparent impedance** seen at the relay terminals, defined as the ratio of the fundamental RMS voltage phasor $\vec{V}$ to the fundamental RMS current phasor $\vec{I}$ at the relay location.

$$Z_{apparent} = \frac{\vec{V}}{\vec{I}}$$

The relay issues a trip command whenever the magnitude of this measured apparent impedance falls **below a pre-set threshold** known as the **reach setting** ($Z_{r}$). The locus of equal operating threshold in the complex impedance plane ($R\text{–}X$ plane) is a **circle centered at the origin**, making it inherently a non-directional relay.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**  
> *"An impedance relay is a voltage-restrained or voltage-controlled overcurrent relay whose operation is a function of the ratio of voltage to current. Since impedance is the ratio of voltage to current, the relay essentially responds to a measure of impedance."*  
> — Standard Power System Protection Textbook Definition

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a substation bus holding a **voltmeter** and an **ammeter** simultaneously. As a fault occurs somewhere down the transmission line:

- The **voltmeter** reading drops (because the fault "shorts out" the voltage at that point).
- The **ammeter** reading rises (because the fault current is large).

If you divide Voltage by Current ($\frac{V}{I}$), you get a number that is *smaller* when the fault is *closer* (because $V$ collapses drastically while $I$ surges). Conversely, the ratio is *larger* when the fault is *farther* away (because $V$ hasn't dropped as much).

The impedance relay **continuously computes this $\frac{V}{I}$ ratio** and checks: *"Is the apparent impedance smaller than my preset threshold?"* If yes → **TRIP**, because the fault must be within the protected zone.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-world intuition:** The relay has no idea where the fault is geographically. It just measures an *electrical "distance proxy"* — the impedance. Since transmission line impedance is proportional to physical line length ($Z = z \cdot l$, where $z$ is impedance per km and $l$ is line length in km), a smaller measured $Z$ means the fault is electrically closer.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Reach Setting ($Z_{r}$)**: The pre-programmed impedance threshold, typically expressed as a **percentage of the protected line's positive-sequence impedance** (e.g., 80%, 90%, 120% of line impedance). Standard KTU practice: **Zone-1 = 80–90%**, **Zone-2 = 120–150%**, **Zone-3 = 250%**.
- **Operating Time**: For **electromechanical** impedance relays ≈ **20–40 ms**; for **static/microprocessor-based** ≈ **10–25 ms**; for **modern numerical** ≈ **5–15 ms**.
- **Reach Accuracy**: ±5% to ±10% of the set value (typical KTU-lab standard).
- **Characteristic Angle**: Impedance relay circle is centered at origin — angle doesn't affect the threshold, but is critical for **co-ordination with other distance relays** (mho, reactance).

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment

| CO Tag | Course Outcome | Coverage in this Topic |
|--------|----------------|------------------------|
| **CO1** | Understand the operating principles of distance relays | ✅ Impedance relay principle, $V/I$ ratio |
| **CO2** | Analyze relay characteristics in R-X plane | ✅ Circle characteristic centered at origin |
| **CO3** | Apply reach setting and coordination principles | ✅ Zone definitions, coverage analysis |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Measurement**
The relay's **Voltage Transformer (VT/PT)** steps down the line voltage to a measurable level (e.g., 110 V secondary for a 110 kV line). The **Current Transformer (CT)** steps down line current (e.g., 1000 A → 5 A secondary).

**Step 2: Phasor Extraction**
In a numerical relay, **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** or **Cosine filter** extracts the fundamental 50 Hz/60 Hz phasors:
$$\vec{V} = V_{R} + jV_{I}, \quad \vec{I} = I_{R} + jI_{I}$$

**Step 3: Complex Impedance Computation**
$$Z_{apparent} = \frac{\vec{V}}{\vec{I}} = \frac{(V_{R} + jV_{I})(I_{R} - jI_{I})}{|\vec{I}|^{2}}$$

Rationalizing:
$$Z_{apparent} = \frac{V_{R}I_{R} + V_{I}I_{I}}{I_{R}^{2} + I_{I}^{2}} + j\frac{V_{I}I_{R} - V_{R}I_{I}}{I_{R}^{2} + I_{I}^{2}}$$

So the **resistance** $R$ and **reactance** $X$ components are:
$$R = \frac{V_{R}I_{R} + V_{I}I_{I}}{I_{R}^{2} + I_{I}^{2}}, \quad X = \frac{V_{I}I_{R} - V_{R}I_{I}}{I_{R}^{2} + I_{I}^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Decision Criterion**
$$\boxed{\vert Z_{apparent} \vert \leq Z_{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TRIP}}$$

### 2.2 The Why and How of Each Step

| Step | Why? | How? |
|------|------|------|
| VT/CT scaling | Line voltages (kV) and currents (kA) are too high for relay electronics | Step down to standard 110 V / 5 A / 1 A |
| Phasor extraction | Relay must respond to **fundamental frequency** and reject harmonics/DC offset | DFT algorithm, anti-aliasing filters |
| Complex division | Voltage and current are AC phasors, must be divided as complex numbers | Algebraic rationalization (multiply by conjugate) |
| Trip decision | Compare magnitude of $Z$ with threshold | Squared-magnitude comparison: $V^{2} \leq (Z_{r}I)^{2}$ |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The following table is the SINGLE most important reference for KTU exams on this topic. Memorize every cell.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Operating Region | Unit | Typical Use |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|------------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Apparent Impedance | $Z_{apparent} = \dfrac{\vec{V}}{\vec{I}}$ | — | $\Omega$ (primary) | Fundamental relay quantity |
| 2 | Operating Criterion | $\vert Z_{apparent} \vert \leq Z_{r}$ | Inside the circle | $\Omega$ | Trip decision |
| 3 | Circle Equation (R-X plane) | $R^{2} + X^{2} = Z_{r}^{2}$ | Boundary | $\Omega$ | Characteristic |
| 4 | Impedance in terms of V,I | $R^{2} + X^{2} \leq \dfrac{V^{2}}{I^{2}}$ | Operating region | $\Omega$ | Squared form (avoid sqrt) |
| 5 | Reach in pu | $Z_{r,pu} = \dfrac{Z_{r}}{Z_{base}}$ | — | pu | Normalized study |
| 6 | Line Impedance | $Z_{line} = z_{1} \cdot l$ | — | $\Omega$ | Conversion from line length |
| 7 | Voltage at Relay | $V = I \cdot Z_{fault}$ | — | V | Fault analysis |
| 8 | Reach % Setting | $\% Z = \dfrac{Z_{r}}{Z_{line}} \times 100$ | — | % | Zone coordination |
| 9 | Trip voltage for given current | $V_{trip} = Z_{r} \cdot I$ | — | V | Implementation |
| 10 | Operating Quantity (in numerical) | $R = \dfrac{V_{R}I_{R} + V_{I}I_{I}}{I_{R}^{2}+I_{I}^{2}}$ | — | $\Omega$ | Real part |
| 11 | Operating Quantity (in numerical) | $X = \dfrac{V_{I}I_{R} - V_{R}I_{I}}{I_{R}^{2}+I_{I}^{2}}$ | — | $\Omega$ | Imag part |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Backbone protection** of 11 kV, 33 kV, 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV transmission lines.
- **Sub-transmission** lines where simple overcurrent relays are inadequate due to varying fault levels.
- **First-zone (Zone-1) instantaneous protection** — typically set at 80% of line length.
- **Out-of-step detection** in generators (using impedance trajectory monitoring).
- **Loss-of-excitation (L-O-E) protection** of synchronous generators (uses an impedance relay with offset circle).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Insight:** The impedance relay is the *father* of all distance relays. Mho relay, Reactance relay, Quadrilateral relay — all are *modifications* of the basic impedance relay circle to add directionality, arc resistance immunity, or other features. KTU examiners love asking comparative questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Impedance Relay Circle Characteristic

**Given:** A relay measures apparent impedance $Z = \dfrac{\vec{V}}{\vec{I}} = R + jX$.

**Operating Principle:** The relay trips when $\vert Z \vert \leq Z_{r}$.

**Step 1:** Start from the operating criterion:

$$\vert Z \vert \leq Z_{r}$$

**Step 2:** Express the magnitude of complex impedance:

$$\sqrt{R^{2} + X^{2}} \leq Z_{r}$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides (to eliminate the square root — this is the **decision logic** in numerical relays because squaring is computationally cheaper than square-rooting):

$$R^{2} + X^{2} \leq Z_{r}^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize this as the **standard equation of a circle** in the $R\text{–}X$ plane:

$$\text{Circle:} \quad (R - 0)^{2} + (X - 0)^{2} = Z_{r}^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the geometric parameters:
- **Center:** $(0, 0)$ — i.e., the origin of the R-X plane
- **Radius:** $Z_{r}$ — the reach setting

**Step 6:** Therefore, the operating region is the **interior of a circle of radius $Z_{r}$ centered at the origin**. The relay trips for any $(R, X)$ lying inside or on this circle.

$$\boxed{Z_{r}^{2} - R^{2} - X^{2} \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TRIP}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Operating Boundary in the V–I Plane

An alternative (and **exam-favorite**) representation is the $V/I$ operating characteristic.

**Step 1:** The relay operating equation:

$$V \leq I \cdot Z_{r}$$

**Step 2:** Square both sides:

$$V^{2} \leq I^{2} Z_{r}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** This is a **straight line** in the $V\text{–}I$ plane, with slope equal to $Z_{r}$, passing through the origin.

**Step 4:** Interpretation:
- For a **given** fault current $I$, the maximum permissible voltage is $V_{max} = I \cdot Z_{r}$.
- If the measured voltage drops **below** this line → trip.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Apparent Impedance for a Single-Line-to-Ground Fault

> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "For a SLG fault, why is the apparent impedance seen by the relay *different* from the actual line impedance?"

Consider a single-line-to-ground fault on phase A at a distance $m \cdot Z_{L}$ from the sending end, where $0 \leq m \leq 1$.

**Step 1:** Sequence network connection: Positive, Negative, and Zero sequence networks are connected **in series** (this is a hallmark of SLG faults).

**Step 2:** Total impedance from source to fault:

$$Z_{total} = Z_{1S} + m \cdot Z_{1L} + Z_{2S} + m \cdot Z_{2L} + Z_{0S} + m \cdot Z_{0L} + 3R_{f}$$

For a **homogeneous line** (same positive/negative sequence impedances, ignoring source):

$$Z_{total} = 3 \cdot m \cdot Z_{1L} + 3R_{f}$$

**Step 3:** Fault current in phase A:

$$I_{a} = \frac{3 \cdot E_{a}}{Z_{total}}$$

**Step 4:** Voltage at relay terminal (phase A):

$$V_{a} = I_{a} \cdot \left[ (1-m) \cdot Z_{1L} + (1-m) \cdot Z_{2L} + (1-m) \cdot Z_{0L} \right] \cdot \text{(sequence factor)}$$

After simplification, the apparent impedance seen by the relay for a **homogeneous line** is:

$$\boxed{Z_{apparent} = m \cdot Z_{1L} + \frac{(1-m) \cdot (Z_{0L} - Z_{1L})}{2} + R_{f} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{(1-m)(Z_{0L} - Z_{1L})}{2 Z_{1L}}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** **Key KTU insight:** The apparent impedance is **NOT simply** $m \cdot Z_{1L}$. It includes an extra term due to **zero-sequence current compensation** (factor $k_0 = \dfrac{Z_{0L} - Z_{1L}}{3 Z_{1L}}$). This is why numerical relays apply a compensation factor:

$$V_{relay} = V_{a} - (k_0) \cdot 3I_{0} \cdot Z_{1L}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Voltage-Restrained vs Voltage-Controlled Impedance Relay

In **electromechanical / static** implementations, the impedance relay is realized using a **single actuating quantity** that compares voltage and current. Two classic variants exist — KTU expects students to know the distinction:

| Feature | Voltage-Restrained Impedance Relay | Voltage-Controlled Impedance Relay |
|---------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Construction** | Single induction cup / comparator with two coils: a **current coil** (operating) and a **voltage coil** (restraining) | Two separate units: a **voltage relay** (control) in series with an **impedance relay** (operating) |
| **Operating equation** | $T_{operating} - T_{restraining} \geq 0 \Rightarrow V^{2} \leq K I^{2}$ | Voltage unit picks up first; then impedance unit operates |
| **Pickup current** | Increases with fault current (restraining torque grows with $I^{2}$) | Relatively constant pickup at high currents |
| **Arc resistance immunity** | Better — restraint grows with $I^{2}$ | Better — controlled by voltage threshold |
| **KTU exam preference** | Less commonly asked | More commonly asked |

---

### 3.5 Reach Setting — Mathematical Analysis

Given a transmission line of positive-sequence impedance $Z_{L} = 30 \angle 75^{\circ} \, \Omega$ (primary), and we want Zone-1 protection at 80%:

**Step 1:** Compute Zone-1 reach:

$$Z_{r,\text{Zone-1}} = 0.80 \times 30 = 24 \, \Omega \text{ (primary)}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to secondary (using CT ratio $n_{CT} = 400:5 = 80$ and VT ratio $n_{VT} = 110000:110 = 1000$):

$$Z_{r,\text{secondary}} = Z_{r,\text{primary}} \times \frac{n_{CT}}{n_{VT}} = 24 \times \frac{80}{1000} = 1.92 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3:** The relay setting dial (or software parameter) is set to $1.92 \, \Omega$ secondary.

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Tip:** The CT/VT transformation is one of the most common mistakes. Always remember: $Z_{secondary} = Z_{primary} \times \dfrac{CT \text{ ratio}}{VT \text{ ratio}}$. Many students get this backwards.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Impedance Relay Operating Characteristic on R-X Plane

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional operating characteristic** of an impedance relay plotted on the resistance–reactance plane.

```mermaid
graph TD
    origin[ORIGIN 0,0]:::center
    Rdir[R AXIS POSITIVE]:::axisR
    Xdir[X AXIS POSITIVE]:::axisX
    Reach[REACH Zr ON R AXIS]:::boundary
    Reach2[REACH Zr ON X AXIS]:::boundary
    LoadLine[LOAD IMPEDANCE ZL]:::load
    FaultPoint[FAULT POINT Zf SMALLER THAN Zr]:::fault
    InsideRegion[OPERATING REGION INTERIOR OF CIRCLE]:::region
    OutsideRegion[RESTRAINT REGION OUTSIDE CIRCLE]:::region

    origin --> Rdir
    origin --> Xdir
    origin --> Reach
    origin --> Reach2
    origin --> Reach2b[REACH Zr NEGATIVE X]:::boundary
    origin --> Reach3[REACH Zr NEGATIVE R]:::boundary
    LoadLine -.-> LoadLabel[LOAD IMPEDANCE TYPICALLY 4 TO 10 TIMES Zr]:::note
    FaultPoint -.-> FaultLabel[FAULT IMPEDANCE WITHIN Zr CAUSES TRIP]:::note
    InsideRegion -.-> TripNote[RELAY OPERATES AND ISSUES TRIP COMMAND]:::note
    OutsideRegion -.-> RestNote[RELAY REMAINS INERT NO TRIP COMMAND]:::note

    classDef center fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef axisR fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef axisX fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef boundary fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef load fill:#bf9000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef fault fill:#ff0000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef region fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> **Figure 4.1 Interpretation:**
> - **ORIGIN (0,0)** = Relay location.
> - **REACH $Z_r$** = Pre-set threshold (radius of operating circle).
> - **LOAD IMPEDANCE** = Normal load condition; this should lie **OUTSIDE** the circle to avoid load encroachment.
> - **FAULT POINT** = Should lie **INSIDE** the circle for the relay to operate.
> - **OPERATING REGION** = Interior of the circle → TRIP.
> - **RESTRAINT REGION** = Exterior of the circle → NO TRIP.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Numerical Impedance Relay

```mermaid
graph TD
    PT[PRIMARY VT 110 kV to 110 V]:::input
    CT[PRIMARY CT 1000 A to 5 A]:::input
    AA1[ANTI ALIASING FILTER]:::process
    AA2[ANTI ALIASING FILTER]:::process
    ADC1[ADC SAMPLING AT 32 SAMPLES PER CYCLE]:::process
    ADC2[ADC SAMPLING AT 32 SAMPLES PER CYCLE]:::process
    DFT[DFT OR COSINE FILTER EXTRACTS 50 Hz PHASOR]:::process
    COMP[COMPLEX DIVIDER V DIVIDED BY I COMPUTES R AND X]:::process
    DEC[DECISION LOGIC CHECK R SQUARED PLUS X SQUARED LESS THAN Zr SQUARED]:::logic
    TIMER[INTEGRATING TIMER TO PREVENT TRANSIENT TRIP]:::logic
    TRIP[TRIP OUTPUT TO CIRCUIT BREAKER]:::output
    DISP[LOCAL DISPLAY AND SCADA COMMUNICATION]:::output

    PT --> AA1 --> ADC1 --> DFT
    CT --> AA2 --> ADC2 --> DFT
    DFT --> COMP --> DEC --> TIMER --> TRIP
    DEC --> DISP

    classDef input fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef process fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef logic fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#c00000,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef output fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:1.5px
```

> **Figure 4.2 Interpretation:** The relay takes analog VT and CT signals, filters and samples them, extracts the 50 Hz phasor, computes the apparent impedance components $R$ and $X$, compares $\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ with the threshold $Z_r$, and issues a trip if the condition is sustained for a minimum duration.

---

### 4.3 Operating Characteristic in V–I Plane

```mermaid
graph TD
    ORG[V I PLANE ORIGIN]:::center
    VAX[V AXIS]:::axisV
    IAX[I AXIS]:::axisI
    LINE[OPERATING LINE V EQUALS Zr TIMES I]:::boundary
    ZONEOP[OPERATING ZONE BELOW THE LINE]:::region
    ZONERE[RESTRAINT ZONE ABOVE THE LINE]:::region
    LOADPT[LOAD POINT V HIGH I MODERATE]:::load
    FAULTPT[FAULT POINT V LOW I HIGH]:::fault

    ORG --> VAX
    ORG --> IAX
    ORG --> LINE
    LOADPT -.-> LOADNOTE[LOAD LIES IN RESTRAINT ZONE NO TRIP]:::note
    FAULTPT -.-> FAULTNOTE[FAULT LIES IN OPERATING ZONE TRIP]:::note
    ZONEOP -.-> OPTNOTE[RELAY OPERATES]:::note
    ZONERE -.-> RENOTE[RELAY REMAINS INERT]:::note

    classDef center fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef axisV fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef axisI fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef boundary fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef load fill:#bf9000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef fault fill:#ff0000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef region fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> **Figure 4.3 Interpretation:** The V–I characteristic is a **straight line of slope $Z_r$** through the origin. The relay operates when the operating point falls **below** the line (i.e., $V$ is too low for the prevailing $I$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> *Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A questions test **Remember/Understand** level. Answers should be 3–5 sentences with a defining equation where applicable.*

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define an impedance relay. Write its operating equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An impedance relay is a non-directional distance relay whose operation depends on the ratio of voltage to current at the relay location. The measured apparent impedance is $Z_{apparent} = \dfrac{\vec{V}}{\vec{I}}$. The relay issues a trip command when the magnitude of this apparent impedance becomes less than or equal to a pre-set value called the **reach setting** $Z_r$. The characteristic in the R-X plane is a **circle centered at the origin** with radius $Z_r$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
**Why is the impedance relay considered a non-directional relay? Mention one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The impedance relay characteristic is a circle centered at the **origin** of the R-X plane. This means the relay will operate for any fault — whether in the **forward direction** (positive R, positive X) or in the **reverse direction** (negative R, positive X) — provided the apparent impedance magnitude is within the reach $Z_r$. Since the relay cannot distinguish the direction of the fault, it is inherently **non-directional**.

**Limitation:** The relay may operate for **reverse-direction faults** (back-up protection required), and it is also susceptible to **arc resistance**, which can push the apparent impedance out of the operating circle, causing the relay to **under-reach**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> *Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each. Module Internal Choice means the student can answer either **Q(A)** or **Q(B)** entirely.*

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**  
**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the operating equation of an impedance relay and explain its **circular characteristic** in the R-X plane with a neat sketch. Discuss how the reach setting is implemented using a **voltage-restrained** induction cup type construction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 110 kV, 50 Hz transmission line has a positive-sequence impedance of $Z_L = 30 \angle 75^{\circ} \, \Omega$ per phase. The CT ratio is 400:5 and the VT ratio is 110000:110. If Zone-1 is set at 80% of the line, determine:  
(i) The primary reach in ohms.  
(ii) The secondary reach in ohms.  
(iii) The trip voltage if a fault current of 3000 A flows.

---

**Model Answer Q(A)(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition and Operating Equation:**

The impedance relay operates on the principle that the apparent impedance at the relay bus is the ratio of the relay voltage to the relay current:

$$Z_{apparent} = \frac{\vec{V}}{\vec{I}}$$

The relay trips when:

$$\vert Z_{apparent} \vert \leq Z_{r}$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic in R-X plane:**

Substituting $Z = R + jX$:

$$\sqrt{R^{2} + X^{2}} \leq Z_{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R^{2} + X^{2} \leq Z_{r}^{2}$$

This is the equation of a **circle centered at the origin (0,0)** with **radius $Z_r$** in the R-X plane.

**Step 3 — Sketch Description:**

The circle passes through points $(Z_r, 0)$, $(0, Z_r)$, $(-Z_r, 0)$, and $(0, -Z_r)$. The interior of the circle is the operating region (trip). The exterior is the restraint region (no trip).

**Step 4 — Voltage-Restrained Implementation:**

In a **voltage-restrained impedance relay**, an induction-cup type comparator is used. Two torques act on the cup:
- **Operating torque** $T_{op} \propto I^{2}$ (proportional to current squared, from current coil)
- **Restraining torque** $T_{rest} \propto V^{2}$ (proportional to voltage squared, from voltage coil)

The relay trips when:

$$T_{op} \geq T_{rest} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_{1}I^{2} \geq K_{2}V^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V^{2} \leq \frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}} I^{2}$$

Defining $Z_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}}$, we get $V \leq Z_r \cdot I$, which is precisely the impedance relay characteristic.

The reach $Z_r$ is adjusted by varying the **pickup current** (via CT tap) and the **pickup voltage** (via VT tap / resistor).

---

**Model Answer Q(A)(b):**

**Given:**
- Line voltage: 110 kV
- $Z_L = 30 \angle 75^{\circ} \, \Omega$
- CT ratio: $n_{CT} = \dfrac{400}{5} = 80$
- VT ratio: $n_{VT} = \dfrac{110000}{110} = 1000$
- Zone-1 setting: 80%

**Step 1 — Primary reach:**

$$Z_{r,\text{primary}} = 0.80 \times 30 \, \Omega = 24 \, \Omega \text{ (primary)}$$

**Step 2 — Secondary reach:**

$$Z_{r,\text{secondary}} = Z_{r,\text{primary}} \times \frac{n_{CT}}{n_{VT}} = 24 \times \frac{80}{1000} = 1.92 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Trip voltage for $I = 3000 \, A$:**

$$V_{trip} = I \times Z_{r,\text{primary}} = 3000 \times 24 = 72000 \, V = 72 \, kV$$

Or alternatively, in terms of secondary current:

$$I_{secondary} = \frac{3000}{80} = 37.5 \, A$$
$$V_{trip,\text{secondary}} = I_{secondary} \times Z_{r,\text{secondary}} = 37.5 \times 1.92 = 72 \, V$$

**Verification:** Primary side: $V_{trip} = 72 \times \frac{110000}{110} = 72 \times 1000 = 72000 \, V = 72 \, kV$ ✓

---

### 📘 QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Q(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**  
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat diagrams the difference between **voltage-controlled** and **voltage-restrained** impedance relays. Derive the operating equation for each and comment on their relative merits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 220 kV, 50 Hz, 100 km transmission line has positive-sequence impedance $z_1 = 0.4 \angle 80^{\circ} \, \Omega$/km. The line is protected by an impedance relay set at 90% reach. A three-phase fault occurs at 60 km from the sending end with an arc resistance of $R_{arc} = 5 \, \Omega$. Determine:  
(i) Whether the relay will operate.  
(ii) The effect of arc resistance on the apparent impedance.

---

**Model Answer Q(B)(a):**

**Voltage-Restrained Impedance Relay:**

In this relay, a **single induction-cup comparator** has two torques:
- **Operating torque** proportional to $I^{2}$ (from current-driven coil)
- **Restraining torque** proportional to $V^{2}$ (from voltage-driven coil)

The relay trips when operating torque exceeds restraining torque:

$$K_{1}I^{2} \geq K_{2}V^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{V}{I}\right)^{2} \leq \frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}} = Z_{r}^{2}$$

**Voltage-Controlled Impedance Relay:**

This relay is a **cascade combination** of two units:
1. A **voltage relay** (instantaneous, low pickup) that energizes the second unit when voltage drops below a threshold $V_{s}$ (say 70% of nominal).
2. An **impedance relay** that operates only when the voltage unit has picked up AND the apparent impedance is within reach.

**Operating Logic:** $V < V_{s}$ **AND** $V/I \leq Z_{r}$ → Trip.

**Merits Comparison:**

| Aspect | Voltage-Restrained | Voltage-Controlled |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Construction | Single unit, simpler | Two units, complex |
| Pickup current | Increases with fault current (good arc immunity) | Approximately constant at high $I$ |
| DC offset sensitivity | Sensitive — needs filter | Less sensitive (dual unit) |
| Speed | Slightly slower | Slightly faster |
| Use in modern numerical relays | Replaced by software logic | Replaced by software logic |

---

**Model Answer Q(B)(b):**

**Given:**
- Line: 220 kV, 50 Hz, 100 km
- $z_1 = 0.4 \angle 80^{\circ} \, \Omega$/km
- Reach: 90% (i.e., $Z_r = 0.9 \times Z_L$)
- Fault location: 60 km from sending end ($m = 0.6$)
- Arc resistance: $R_{arc} = 5 \, \Omega$

**Step 1 — Total line impedance:**

$$Z_L = z_1 \times l = 0.4 \angle 80^{\circ} \times 100 = 40 \angle 80^{\circ} \, \Omega$$

Converting to rectangular: $Z_L = 40(\cos 80^{\circ} + j\sin 80^{\circ}) = 6.946 + j39.392 \, \Omega$

**Step 2 — Reach setting:**

$$Z_r = 0.9 \times 40 = 36 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Apparent impedance WITHOUT arc resistance (pure 3-phase fault):**

For a three-phase fault, the apparent impedance is simply the line impedance from relay to fault:

$$Z_{f,no\,arc} = m \cdot Z_L = 0.6 \times 40 = 24 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Apparent impedance WITH arc resistance:**

Arc resistance appears as a **resistive addition** to the line impedance (since arc voltage is in phase with fault current for a 3-phase fault, the arc resistance is purely real):

$$Z_{f,with\,arc} = m \cdot Z_L + R_{arc} = (0.6 \times 6.946 + 5) + j(0.6 \times 39.392)$$

$$Z_{f,with\,arc} = (4.168 + 5) + j(23.635) = 9.168 + j23.635 \, \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_{f,with\,arc} \vert = \sqrt{9.168^{2} + 23.635^{2}} = \sqrt{84.06 + 558.6} = \sqrt{642.7} = 25.35 \, \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$\vert Z_{f,with\,arc} \vert = 25.35 \, \Omega \quad \text{vs} \quad Z_r = 36 \, \Omega$$

Since $25.35 \, \Omega < 36 \, \Omega$, the **apparent impedance lies inside the operating circle**, so the **relay WILL OPERATE**. ✓

**Step 6 — Effect of arc resistance:**

Arc resistance **adds a resistive component** to the apparent impedance. In the R-X plane, the fault point moves **horizontally to the right** by $R_{arc} = 5 \, \Omega$. For an impedance relay (centered at origin), this is generally acceptable because the magnitude still falls within reach. However, for **reactance relays** and **mho relays**, arc resistance causes **under-reach** (the apparent impedance moves outside the operating region). The impedance relay is **less susceptible** to arc resistance than other distance relays in terms of pure-magnitude comparison, but it is more susceptible to **load encroachment**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common places where students lose marks on Impedance Relay questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the impedance is COMPLEX** ($R + jX$), not just magnitude. Always compute both $R$ and $X$ components when fault involves arc resistance or non-homogeneous lines.
> 2. **CT/VT ratio direction error**: $Z_{secondary} = Z_{primary} \times \dfrac{n_{CT}}{n_{VT}}$ — many students invert it and lose 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing voltage-restrained with voltage-controlled**: These are TWO DIFFERENT relays. The voltage-restrained has a single comparator with two torques; the voltage-controlled has two cascaded units.
> 4. **Missing the zero-sequence compensation** for SLG faults: Apparent impedance ≠ actual impedance from source to fault. Always mention $k_0$ compensation for ground-distance protection.
> 5. **Directionality issue**: A bare impedance relay is **non-directional** — failing to mention this earns zero marks on "compare with mho relay" type questions.
> 6. **Sloppy sketches**: KTU examiners strictly award 1–2 marks for the R-X diagram. A poorly labeled circle without axes, center, and radius marking = 0 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-day revision before KTU exam):**

- ✅ **Core definition**: Impedance relay measures $Z = V/I$ and trips when $|Z| \leq Z_r$.
- ✅ **Characteristic**: Circle centered at **ORIGIN**, radius $Z_r$ — in the R-X plane.
- ✅ **NON-DIRECTIONAL** — the most distinctive feature compared to mho relay.
- ✅ **Operating equation**: $R^2 + X^2 \leq Z_r^2$ (or equivalently $V^2 \leq (Z_r I)^2$).
- ✅ **Reach** typically set as **% of protected line**: Zone-1 = 80–90%, Zone-2 = 120–150%, Zone-3 = 250%.
- ✅ **Secondary reach calculation**: $Z_{sec} = Z_{prim} \times \dfrac{CT_{ratio}}{VT_{ratio}}$.
- ✅ **Two implementations**: **Voltage-Restrained** (single unit, $K_1 I^2 \geq K_2 V^2$) and **Voltage-Controlled** (cascade of voltage unit + impedance unit).
- ✅ **Advantages**: Simple principle, no phase-angle transformers needed, suitable for **OUT-OF-STEP detection** and **LOSS-OF-EXCITATION** protection of generators.
- ✅ **Disadvantages**: **Non-directional** (operates for reverse faults), **load encroachment** risk (heavy load can enter the circle), **arc resistance under-reach** is less severe than mho/reactance but still exists.
- ✅ **SLG fault**: Apparent impedance ≠ actual impedance; requires **zero-sequence compensation** ($k_0$ factor) in numerical relays.
- ✅ **Comparison points for KTU**:
   - *vs. Reactance relay*: Impedance responds to $Z$, reactance responds to $X$ only — reactance is more sensitive to arc resistance.
   - *vs. Mho relay*: Mho is directional (passes through origin and tilts to provide direction), impedance is non-directional.
- ✅ **Time delays**: Zone-1 = **instantaneous** (≤ 30 ms), Zone-2 = **0.3–0.5 s**, Zone-3 = **0.6–1.0 s** (typical KTU values).
- ✅ **Characteristic angle** of the line: $\theta = \arctan(X/R)$ — usually 60°–85° for HV transmission lines.
- ✅ **Numerical implementation**: DFT-based phasor extraction → complex division → compare $R^2 + X^2$ with $Z_r^2$ → integrate (count cycles) → trip.
- ✅ **Reach accuracy**: ±5% typical for numerical relays; ±10% for static/electromechanical.
- ✅ **Memory voltage** is NOT needed in impedance relays (unlike mho) because there is no directional comparison.

> **Final Note:** The impedance relay is the *conceptual foundation* of all distance protection. Master its circle characteristic, its non-directional nature, and its V/I operating equation. Every KTU question on this topic boils down to these three things plus a numerical problem on reach setting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactance Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reactance Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactance Relay:** A reactance relay is a *single-quantity measuring relay* whose operation depends solely on the **reactance component (X)** of the impedance presented to its terminals. It is a non-directional distance relay that operates when the measured reactance **falls below a pre-set threshold value (X_setting)**, irrespective of the corresponding resistance value.

Mathematically, the relay tripping criterion is expressed as:

$$X_{\text{measured}} \leq X_{\text{setting}}$$

where $X_{\text{measured}} = \text{Imag}(Z_{\text{relay}}) = \text{Imag}\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{relay}}}{I_{\text{relay}}}\right)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Spring Analogy:**
> Imagine you are pushing a heavy trolley along a rough track. The track has two kinds of opposition to motion:
> 1. **Friction (R — Resistance):** Roughness of the surface. This depends on the materials in contact.
> 2. **Inertia of the load (X — Reactance):** The natural "springiness" or energy-storage tendency due to inductance in the system. This depends on the geometry and magnetic fields.
>
> A **reactance relay** is like a safety guard that only watches the **springiness (X)**, completely ignoring the friction (R). The moment the springiness drops below a safe limit, the guard trips the system — even if friction is enormous. This is exactly why reactance relays are preferred where **arc resistance (R) is large and unpredictable**, such as in **ground (earth) faults**, where the high-resistance arc is the dominant R-component that we want to ignore.

### 1.3 Physical Meaning of the Operating Boundary

The reactance relay characteristic on the **R–X impedance plane** is a **horizontal straight line** drawn at $X = X_{\text{setting}}$ in the lower half-plane (operating region is **below** this line, since fault impedance is inductive and lies in the +X region).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary for KTU:**
> - **Operating Quantity (Op):** Reactance X of the protected zone
> - **Restraining Quantity:** Resistance R (intentionally ignored)
> - **Reach (X_setting):** Pre-set reactance threshold, usually expressed in **ohms (Ω)** or **per unit (p.u.)**
> - **Pickup:** The minimum X at which the relay begins to operate
> - **Non-directional:** It does not distinguish between forward and reverse faults (no directional element)

### 1.4 Why Reactance Relays Are Used

- **Arc resistance independence:** A phase-to-ground fault arc can have resistance from **1 Ω to several hundred ohms**. An impedance (mho) relay would be misled by this R and under-reach; the reactance relay is immune.
- **Selective ground fault protection:** Especially suited for **single line-to-ground (SLG) faults** on transmission lines.
- **Backup for distance relays:** Often used in conjunction with mho relays for full-scheme protection.

### 1.5 Standard Reach Settings (KTU 2024 Convention)

| Line Voltage Class | Typical Primary Reach |
|---|---|
| 11 kV / 22 kV | 80 – 90 % of protected line reactance |
| 66 kV / 110 kV | 80 – 90 % of line reactance |
| 220 kV / 400 kV | 80 – 90 % of line reactance, with arc compensation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reactance relay does NOT measure true reactance directly. Instead, it implements a **comparison of two phasor quantities** derived from voltage and current using analog phase-shifting networks (in static relays) or numerical computations (in digital/numerical relays).

**Step 1 — Voltage and Current Sensing**
The relay senses the fundamental-frequency phasors of the protected line:
- $V_{\text{relay}}$ (voltage across the relay location)
- $I_{\text{relay}}$ (current flowing into the protected section)

**Step 2 — Generation of Operating and Restraining Quantities**
For a **single-input reactance relay** (the classic induction-cup type):
- **Operating quantity:** $V_{\text{op}} = V_{\text{relay}} \cdot \sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the phase angle between $V$ and $I$
- **Restraining quantity:** $V_{\text{res}} = I_{\text{relay}} \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$ (a voltage derived from current and the reactance setting)

> [!NOTE]
> For the relay to operate: $V_{\text{op}} > V_{\text{res}}$ ⟹ $V_{\text{relay}} \sin\theta > I_{\text{relay}} X_{\text{setting}}$

**Step 3 — Substituting Z = V / I**

$$\dfrac{V_{\text{relay}}}{I_{\text{relay}}} \sin\theta > X_{\text{setting}}$$

$$Z_{\text{relay}} \sin\theta > X_{\text{setting}}$$

Since for an inductive fault $Z = R + jX$ and $\theta = \tan^{-1}(X/R)$:

$$Z \cdot \sin\left(\tan^{-1}\dfrac{X}{R}\right) = Z \cdot \dfrac{X}{\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}} = X$$

Therefore the operating condition reduces to:

$$\boxed{\,X_{\text{measured}} > X_{\text{setting}}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign convention:** In the KTU convention, the operating region is **BELOW** the line $X = X_{\text{setting}}$, because we are using an inverse-time induction cup construction where the *operating torque* is proportional to $+X$ and *restraining* is from the current coil. Always re-derive the inequality sign based on the relay construction given in the question.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_{\text{measured}} = \text{Im}\left(\dfrac{V}{I}\right)$ | Reactance seen by relay | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $X_{\text{relay setting}} = k \cdot X_{\text{line}}$ | Reach setting ($k = 0.8$ to $0.9$) | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $X_{\text{measured}} \leq X_{\text{setting}}$ | Trip criterion | — |
| 4 | $Z = R + jX$ | Impedance in rectangular form | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $Y = G - jB$ | Admittance form (used in some derivations) | S |
| 6 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(X/R)$ | Impedance angle | rad or deg |
| 7 | $V_{\text{op}} = V \sin\theta$ | Operating voltage in classic construction | V |
| 8 | $V_{\text{res}} = I \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$ | Restraining voltage in classic construction | V |
| 9 | $\tau_{\text{op}} = K_1 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \sin\theta - K_2 \cdot I^2 \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$ | Net operating torque | N·m |
| 10 | $R_{\text{arc, max}} = X_{\text{setting}} \cdot \tan\theta$ | Maximum arc resistance tolerated | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | $X_{\text{relay, secondary}} = X_{\text{primary}} \cdot \dfrac{\text{CT ratio}}{\text{PT ratio}}$ | CT/PT scaling for secondary setting | $\Omega$ |
| 12 | $\text{Reach accuracy} = \dfrac{X_{\text{actual}} - X_{\text{setting}}}{X_{\text{setting}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Error percentage | % |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU problems, you are expected to work in **per-unit (p.u.)** when base values are given. Convert primary $\Omega$ to p.u. using $X_{\text{p.u.}} = X_{\text{actual}} / X_{\text{base}}$, where $X_{\text{base}} = (kV_{\text{base}})^2 / MVA_{\text{base}}$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sub-transmission and distribution lines (11 kV – 66 kV):** These lines have high arc resistance during single line-to-ground faults, making reactance relays ideal.
- **Ground-fault neutral compensation schemes:** Used in **resonant grounded (Peterson coil) systems** to detect ground faults via neutral displacement voltage and zero-sequence current.
- **Traveling-wave and digital relays:** Modern numerical relays compute reactance using the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) of sampled voltages and currents.
- **Series-compensated lines:** Reactance relays retain their independence from arc resistance even when the line has series capacitors.

### 2.4 Construction — Induction-Cup (Electromechanical) Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A reactance relay uses an **induction cup** with two sets of coils:
> 1. **Voltage coil (operating coil):** Energized from $V_{\text{relay}}$ through a phase-shifting network. Produces a flux proportional to $V \sin\theta$.
> 2. **Current coil (restraining coil):** Energized from $I_{\text{relay}}$ with a reactance-type burden. Produces a flux proportional to $I \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$.
>
> The rotor torque is the algebraic sum of the two torques. When operating torque exceeds restraining torque, the cup rotates, closing the trip contacts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Operating Torque to Reach Boundary on the R–X Plane

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **KTU-favourite** and frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-question in the End Semester Examination (ESE). Walk through every step without skipping.

**Given:**
The net electromagnetic torque on the relay rotor is:

$$\tau_{\text{net}} = K_1 \cdot V_{\text{relay}} \cdot I_{\text{relay}} \cdot \sin\theta - K_2 \cdot I_{\text{relay}}^2 \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$$

where $\theta = \angle(V, I)$ and $K_1, K_2$ are relay constants.

**Step 1 — Relay operates when net torque ≥ 0 (positive operating torque):**

$$K_1 \cdot V_{\text{relay}} \cdot I_{\text{relay}} \cdot \sin\theta - K_2 \cdot I_{\text{relay}}^2 \cdot X_{\text{setting}} \geq 0$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the trip condition:**

$$K_1 \cdot V_{\text{relay}} \cdot I_{\text{relay}} \cdot \sin\theta \geq K_2 \cdot I_{\text{relay}}^2 \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $I_{\text{relay}}^2$ (assuming $I_{\text{relay}} \neq 0$):**

$$K_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_{\text{relay}}}{I_{\text{relay}}} \cdot \sin\theta \geq K_2 \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Z_{\text{relay}} = V_{\text{relay}} / I_{\text{relay}}$:**

$$K_1 \cdot Z_{\text{relay}} \cdot \sin\theta \geq K_2 \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 5 — Express $Z_{\text{relay}}$ in polar form and use $\theta = \tan^{-1}(X/R)$:**

$$Z_{\text{relay}} = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}, \quad \sin\theta = \dfrac{X}{\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the inequality:**

$$K_1 \cdot \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} \cdot \dfrac{X}{\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}} \geq K_2 \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$$

$$K_1 \cdot X \geq K_2 \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 7 — Define the relay constant ratio $K = K_2 / K_1$ and solve for X:**

$$X \geq K \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 8 — Choose the relay construction so that $K = 1$ (a calibrated relay):**

$$\boxed{\,X \geq X_{\text{setting}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The operating region of a reactance relay on the R–X plane is **the entire half-plane above** the horizontal line $X = X_{\text{setting}}$ (in the conventional +X direction). Equivalently, when the inequality is written as $X_{\text{measured}} \leq X_{\text{setting}}$, the relay trips for any point **on or below** the line.

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Arc Resistance for Correct Operation

**Given:**
- Line-to-ground fault at distance $d$ from the relay
- Line reactance per km: $x$ (Ω/km)
- Arc resistance: $R_{\text{arc}}$
- Reach setting: $X_{\text{setting}} = 0.8 \cdot d_{\text{total}} \cdot x$

**Step 1 — Apparent impedance seen by the relay during fault:**

$$Z_{\text{fault}} = R_{\text{arc}} + j \cdot d \cdot x$$

**Step 2 — Reactance component of the apparent impedance:**

$$X_{\text{measured}} = d \cdot x$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The arc resistance appears ONLY in the real (R) part. It does NOT affect the X-component. This is the **defining property** of the reactance relay.

**Step 3 — For the relay to operate, the measured X must exceed the setting:**

$$d \cdot x \geq 0.8 \cdot d_{\text{total}} \cdot x$$

$$d \geq 0.8 \cdot d_{\text{total}}$$

**Conclusion:** The relay operates correctly for any fault beyond **80 % of the line length**, regardless of arc resistance. This is the **key advantage** over impedance and mho relays.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Reactance Relay | Impedance (Mho) Relay | Mho Relay |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Measured Quantity** | X only | R and X (modulus $\vert Z \vert$) | Admittance Y |
| **R–X Characteristic** | Horizontal line | Circle through origin | Circle through origin, expanding with $\theta$ |
| **Directional** | No (Non-directional) | No | Yes |
| **Effect of Arc Resistance** | None (immune) | Causes under-reach | Causes under-reach |
| **Effect of Power Swings** | High susceptibility | Moderate | Lowest |
| **Best Suited For** | Phase-to-ground faults | Phase-to-phase faults | Long EHV lines |
| **Construction** | Single-input (X) | Single-input ($\vert Z \vert$) | Two-input (mho) |
| **Reach Setting** | 80 – 90 % of X | 80 – 90 % of $\vert Z \vert$ | 80 – 90 % of $\vert Z \vert$ |

### 3.4 Derivation: Secondary Reach from CT/PT Ratios

**Given:**
- Primary line reactance: $X_{\text{primary}}$
- CT ratio: $n_{\text{CT}} = I_{\text{primary}} / I_{\text{secondary}}$
- PT ratio: $n_{\text{PT}} = V_{\text{primary}} / V_{\text{secondary}}$

**Step 1 — Voltage transformation:**

$$V_{\text{sec}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{primary}}}{n_{\text{PT}}}$$

**Step 2 — Current transformation:**

$$I_{\text{sec}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{primary}}}{n_{\text{CT}}}$$

**Step 3 — Secondary impedance:**

$$Z_{\text{sec}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{sec}}}{I_{\text{sec}}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{primary}} / n_{\text{PT}}}{I_{\text{primary}} / n_{\text{CT}}} = Z_{\text{primary}} \cdot \dfrac{n_{\text{CT}}}{n_{\text{PT}}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to the reactance setting:**

$$\boxed{\,X_{\text{setting, sec}} = X_{\text{setting, primary}} \cdot \dfrac{n_{\text{CT}}}{n_{\text{PT}}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example (typical KTU problem):**
> Line reactance = 50 Ω (primary), CT = 400/5, PT = 110 kV/110 V
> $n_{\text{CT}} = 80$, $n_{\text{PT}} = 1000$
> $X_{\text{setting, sec}} = 50 \cdot (80 / 1000) = 4 \, \Omega$ (secondary)

### 3.5 Per-Unit Conversion for Setting

**Step 1 — Choose base values:**
- $V_{\text{base}} = 110 \text{ kV}$ (line-to-line)
- $S_{\text{base}} = 100 \text{ MVA}$

**Step 2 — Compute base impedance:**

$$Z_{\text{base}} = \dfrac{(V_{\text{base}})^2}{S_{\text{base}}} = \dfrac{(110)^2}{100} = 121 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Convert primary reactance to p.u.:**

$$X_{\text{p.u.}} = \dfrac{50}{121} \approx 0.413 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 4 — Set reach at 80 % of line reactance:**

$$X_{\text{setting, p.u.}} = 0.8 \times 0.413 = 0.331 \text{ p.u.}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 R–X Plane Operating Characteristic of a Reactance Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reactance Relay R-X Plane"] --> B["Upper Half: X greater than Xset - NON-OPERATING region"]
    A --> C["Lower Half: X less than Xset - OPERATING / TRIP region"]
    A --> D["Horizontal line X = Xset represents the boundary"]
    A --> E["Impedance vectors fan from origin into plane"]
    A --> F["Vector length = fault impedance magnitude"]
    A --> G["Vector angle from R axis = impedance phase angle"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The R–X plane is divided into two regions by the horizontal line $X = X_{\text{setting}}$:
> - **Region above the line** ($X > X_{\text{setting}}$): No trip. Impedance is too large.
> - **Region on or below the line** ($X \leq X_{\text{setting}}$): Trip. The fault is within reach.
> - The line **extends infinitely to the left and right** — this is what makes the reactance relay non-directional.

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Static / Digital Reactance Relay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1["Line Voltage VT Secondary"] --> filt1["Anti-Aliasing Filter and Analog Isolation"]
    input2["Line Current CT Secondary"] --> filt2["Anti-Aliasing Filter and Analog Isolation"]
    filt1 --> adc1["ADC Channel 1 Sampler"]
    filt2 --> adc2["ADC Channel 2 Sampler"]
    adc1 --> dft1["DFT Computation - Fundamental Component of V"]
    adc2 --> dft2["DFT Computation - Fundamental Component of I"]
    dft1 --> comp["Complex Division Block Z equal to V divided by I"]
    dft2 --> comp
    comp --> imag["Imaginary Part Extractor X = Im of Z"]
    imag --> compare["Comparator: X measured against X setting"]
    compare --> tripout["Trip Output to Circuit Breaker"]
    setting["Operator Setting via HMI"] --> compare
    logic["Trip Logic and Annunciation"] --> tripout
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - VT = Voltage Transformer, CT = Current Transformer
> - ADC = Analog-to-Digital Converter
> - DFT = Discrete Fourier Transform (extracts the 50/60 Hz fundamental from the sampled waveform)
> - The "Imaginary Part Extractor" is the **digital equivalent** of the analog phase-shifting network used in electromechanical relays.
> - This is the **numerical relay** architecture used in modern digital protection.

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Electromechanical (Induction-Cup) Reactance Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    v1["Relay Terminal Voltage Vrelay"] --> psn["Phase Shifting Network 90 degree compensator"]
    psn --> vcoil["Voltage Operative Coil"]
    ct1["Relay Terminal Current Irelay"] --> xburden["Reactance Burden Xset"]
    xburden --> icoil["Current Restraining Coil"]
    vcoil --> cup["Induction Cup Rotor"]
    icoil --> cup
    cup --> contact["Trip Contacts NO normally open"]
    contact --> breaker["Circuit Breaker Trip Coil"]
    spring["Control Spring"] --> cup
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **phase-shifting network** is the heart of the analog reactance relay. It produces a voltage proportional to $V \sin\theta$ from the measured $V$ and $I$, where $\theta$ is the angle between them. The **reactance burden** is a calibrated inductor that produces a voltage proportional to $I \cdot X_{\text{set}}$. The two voltages are applied to opposing coils, and the relay trips when the operating coil torque exceeds the restraining coil torque.

### 4.4 Operating Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Voltage and Current samples available"] --> read["Read V and I complex phasors"]
    read --> compz["Compute Z equal to V divided by I"]
    compz --> extract["Extract X = Imaginary part of Z"]
    extract --> compare{"Is X less than or equal to Xset?"}
    compare -- yes --> trip["Issue TRIP command to breaker"]
    compare -- no --> nore["Hold NO-TRIP and continue monitoring"]
    nore --> read
    trip --> log["Log event in fault recorder and annunciate"]
    log --> reset["Await operator reset or auto-reclose"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a reactance relay and state its operating principle.**
*(3 Marks — CO1, K2: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
A reactance relay is a single-input distance relay whose operation depends on the **reactance component (X)** of the impedance presented to its terminals. It uses a phase-shifting network that produces a torque proportional to $V \sin\theta$ (operating) and another torque proportional to $I^2 \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$ (restraining). The relay operates when $X_{\text{measured}} \leq X_{\text{setting}}$. On the R–X plane, its characteristic is a **horizontal straight line at $X = X_{\text{setting}}$**. It is widely used for **single line-to-ground fault protection** because it is immune to arc resistance.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. Why is a reactance relay preferred over an impedance relay for ground fault protection?**
*(3 Marks — CO2, K3: Apply)*

**Model Answer:**
A ground fault produces a high-resistance arc (often tens to hundreds of ohms) that adds a large **R-component** to the fault impedance. An impedance (mho) relay responds to the **modulus $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$**, so the arc resistance pushes $\vert Z \vert$ beyond the relay setting, causing **under-reach** or failure to trip. A reactance relay responds **only to the imaginary part X**, so the arc resistance (which lies entirely on the R-axis) has **no effect** on the measured reactance. Hence the reactance relay trips correctly for ground faults even with substantial arc resistance.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**A(a)** With the help of a torque equation, derive the operating characteristic of a single-input reactance relay on the R–X impedance plane. Show clearly the operating and restraining regions.
*(7 Marks — CO2, K3: Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the operating torque equation:**

$$\tau_{\text{net}} = K_1 V I \sin\theta - K_2 I^2 X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 2 — Set $\tau_{\text{net}} = 0$ for the boundary condition:**

$$K_1 V I \sin\theta = K_2 I^2 X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $I$ and rearrange:**

$$K_1 \dfrac{V}{I} \sin\theta = K_2 X_{\text{setting}}$$

$$K_1 Z \sin\theta = K_2 X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 4 — Express $Z \sin\theta$ in terms of R and X:**

$$Z \sin\theta = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} \cdot \dfrac{X}{\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}} = X$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$K_1 X = K_2 X_{\text{setting}}$$

$$X = \dfrac{K_2}{K_1} X_{\text{setting}} = K \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 6 — Choose relay construction so that $K = 1$:**

$$X = X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 7 — Identify the operating region (where $\tau_{\text{net}} > 0$):**

$$X \leq X_{\text{setting}}$$

**Step 8 — Sketch the R–X plane:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["R-X Plane with reactance relay characteristic"] --> B["Origin O at left"]
    A --> C["Horizontal R axis to the right"]
    A --> D["Vertical X axis upward"]
    A --> E["Horizontal boundary line at X = Xset, extending both directions"]
    A --> F["Region below the line is OPERATING region"]
    A --> G["Region above the line is NON-OPERATING region"]
    A --> H["Fault impedance vectors from origin: tip below line means trip"]
```

**Step 9 — Conclude:**
The operating characteristic is a **horizontal line at $X = X_{\text{setting}}$** parallel to the R-axis. The relay operates for all fault impedances whose tips lie **on or below** this line (i.e., smaller X). The relay is **non-directional** because the line extends infinitely in both +R and –R directions.

---

**A(b)** A 110 kV, 50 Hz, three-phase transmission line is 80 km long. The line reactance is 0.4 Ω/km. The relay is set to protect 80 % of the line. CT ratio is 400/5 and PT ratio is 110 kV/110 V. Determine:
*(i)* the primary reach in ohms,
*(ii)* the secondary reach in ohms,
*(iii)* the per-unit reach on a 100 MVA, 110 kV base.
*(7 Marks — CO3, K3: Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total primary line reactance:**

$$X_{\text{line, primary}} = 0.4 \, \Omega/\text{km} \times 80 \text{ km} = 32 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Primary reach (80 % of line reactance):**

$$X_{\text{reach, primary}} = 0.8 \times 32 = 25.6 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — CT and PT ratios:**

$$n_{\text{CT}} = \dfrac{400}{5} = 80, \quad n_{\text{PT}} = \dfrac{110{,}000}{110} = 1000$$

**Step 4 — Secondary reach:**

$$X_{\text{reach, secondary}} = X_{\text{reach, primary}} \cdot \dfrac{n_{\text{CT}}}{n_{\text{PT}}} = 25.6 \cdot \dfrac{80}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{\,X_{\text{reach, secondary}} = 2.048 \, \Omega\,}$$

**Step 5 — Base impedance on 100 MVA, 110 kV base:**

$$Z_{\text{base}} = \dfrac{(110)^2}{100} = 121 \, \Omega$$

**Step 6 — Per-unit reach:**

$$X_{\text{reach, p.u.}} = \dfrac{25.6}{121}$$

$$\boxed{\,X_{\text{reach, p.u.}} = 0.2116 \text{ p.u.}\,}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**B(a)** Explain with neat phasor diagrams the construction and working of an induction-cup type reactance relay. Compare it with the induction-cup type impedance relay.
*(7 Marks — CO2, K2: Understand)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
The induction-cup reactance relay consists of:
1. **A voltage-driven operating coil** energized from the VT secondary through a **phase-shifting network** (typically a resistor–inductor combination that introduces a 90° phase shift).
2. **A current-driven restraining coil** energized from the CT secondary through a **reactance-type burden** (a calibrated inductor with reactance equal to $X_{\text{setting}}$).
3. **A rotor (induction cup)** placed in the air gap of a laminated magnetic core. The rotor carries the trip contacts.

**Working:**
- The voltage coil produces a flux $\phi_V \propto V \sin\theta$ in the operating air gap.
- The current coil produces a flux $\phi_I \propto I \cdot X_{\text{setting}}$ in the restraining air gap.
- These two alternating fluxes induce eddy currents in the cup, and the **interaction produces a net torque** proportional to $\phi_V \phi_I \sin(\text{angle between them})$.
- The net torque is: $\tau = K_1 V I \sin\theta - K_2 I^2 X_{\text{setting}}$.
- When $\tau \geq 0$, the cup rotates, closing the trip contacts.

**Comparison with Impedance Relay:**

| Feature | Reactance Relay | Impedance Relay |
|---|---|---|
| Operating quantity | X (reactance) | $\vert Z \vert$ (modulus) |
| R–X characteristic | Horizontal line | Circle centered at origin |
| Burden on current coil | Inductive ($jX$) | Resistive ($R$) |
| Directional | No | No |
| Effect of arc R | None | Under-reaches |
| Best application | Ground faults | Phase-to-phase faults |

---

**B(b)** A 66 kV, three-phase line has positive-sequence reactance 0.3 Ω/km. A reactance relay is set at 85 % of the line. The line length is 60 km. The relay uses a CT of 300/5 A and a PT of 66 kV/110 V. If a single line-to-ground fault occurs at 50 km from the relay with an arc resistance of 20 Ω, determine whether the relay will operate. Assume the fault current is 1500 A (secondary).
*(7 Marks — CO3, K4: Analyse)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total line reactance:**

$$X_{\text{line}} = 0.3 \times 60 = 18 \, \Omega \text{ (primary)}$$

**Step 2 — Relay reach setting (primary):**

$$X_{\text{setting, primary}} = 0.85 \times 18 = 15.3 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Convert reach to secondary:**

$$n_{\text{CT}} = 300/5 = 60, \quad n_{\text{PT}} = 66{,}000/110 = 600$$

$$X_{\text{setting, sec}} = 15.3 \times \dfrac{60}{600} = 1.53 \, \Omega \text{ (secondary)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute fault reactance at fault location (primary):**

$$X_{\text{fault, primary}} = 0.3 \times 50 = 15 \, \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Convert fault reactance to secondary:**

$$X_{\text{fault, sec}} = 15 \times \dfrac{60}{600} = 1.5 \, \Omega \text{ (secondary)}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the relay's operating criterion:**

The reactance relay trips when $X_{\text{measured}} \leq X_{\text{setting}}$:

$$X_{\text{measured, sec}} = 1.5 \, \Omega \leq 1.53 \, \Omega = X_{\text{setting, sec}}$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\text{Yes, the relay WILL operate.}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** The arc resistance of 20 Ω does not enter the comparison because the reactance relay ignores R. The fault reactance (1.5 Ω secondary) is within the reach (1.53 Ω secondary), so the relay trips correctly.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout
