> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Reactance Relay Questions:**
> 1. **Sign convention error (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often write $X \geq X_{\text{setting}}$ as the trip condition. The correct condition depends on the construction. In KTU questions, the **operating region is BELOW the line** ($X \leq X_{\text{setting}}$). Always re-read the question to confirm.
> 2. **Forgetting CT/PT ratio conversion (–2 to –3 marks):** A frequent mistake is to compute the reach in primary ohms and forget to convert to secondary ohms using $X_{\text{sec}} = X_{\text{prim}} \cdot (n_{\text{CT}}/n_{\text{PT}})$.
> 3. **Mixing R–X with G–B (admittance) characteristics (–1 mark):** Reactance relays are R–X characteristics, NOT G–B. Mho relays are often shown on the G–B plane. Do not confuse them.
> 4. **Missing the arc-resistance independence argument (–2 marks):** When a question asks "why use a reactance relay for ground faults?", the answer must explicitly state that arc resistance affects only R, not X.
> 5. **Skipping the units in the final answer (–0.5 mark):** Always state units (Ω, p.u., km) explicitly.
> 6. **Not drawing the R–X diagram (–1 to –2 marks):** A sketch is mandatory in any reactance relay question worth more than 3 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Reactance Relays**

- **Definition:** A non-directional distance relay that responds to the reactance (X) of the fault impedance only.
- **Operating criterion:** $X_{\text{measured}} \leq X_{\text{setting}}$.
- **R–X characteristic:** A horizontal straight line at $X = X_{\text{setting}}$ in the +X half-plane.
- **Operating region:** All points **on or below** the horizontal line (smaller X).
- **Non-operating region:** All points **above** the line (larger X).
- **Setting convention:** 80 – 90 % of the protected line's positive-sequence reactance.
- **Key advantage:** **Immune to arc resistance** — arc R affects only the real axis, not X.
- **Key disadvantage:** **Susceptible to power swings** (slow changes in X can drift the operating point).
- **Primary application:** **Single line-to-ground (SLG) fault protection** on sub-transmission and distribution lines.
- **Construction (electromechanical):** Induction cup with voltage-driven operating coil (through 90° phase-shifting network) and current-driven restraining coil (through reactance burden).
- **Construction (digital/numerical):** ADC + DFT → complex division $Z = V/I$ → extract $\text{Im}(Z)$ → compare with setting.
- **Torque equation:** $\tau = K_1 V I \sin\theta - K_2 I^2 X_{\text{setting}}$.
- **CT/PT scaling:** $X_{\text{setting, sec}} = X_{\text{setting, prim}} \cdot (n_{\text{CT}}/n_{\text{PT}})$.
- **Per-unit base:** $Z_{\text{base}} = (kV_{\text{base}})^2 / MVA_{\text{base}}$.
- **Maximum arc resistance:** Theoretically unlimited — does not affect the trip decision.
- **Difference from mho relay:** Mho is directional, circular characteristic, affected by arc R; reactance is non-directional, horizontal-line characteristic, immune to arc R.
- **Difference from impedance relay:** Impedance measures $\vert Z \vert$ (circle on R–X), reactance measures only X (line on R–X).
- **KtU 2024 favourite questions:** (i) Derivation of R–X characteristic from torque equation (7 marks); (ii) Reach calculation with CT/PT and p.u. conversion (7 marks); (iii) Comparison with mho/impedance relays (3–5 marks).
- **Mnemonic:** "**R**eactance = **R**esists springiness only, ignores **R**oughness (friction/arc R)."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mho Relays (Basic Principles and Characteristics Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mho Relays (Basic Principles and Characteristics Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mho Relay:** A *directional distance relay* whose operating characteristic on the **R-X (Resistance–Reactance) diagram** is a **circle passing through the origin**, with the **diameter of the circle aligned along the Relay Characteristic Angle (RCA)**. The relay trips when the apparent impedance seen by the relay ($Z_f = V/I$) falls *inside* this circle.

The name **"Mho"** is the word **"Ohm"** spelled backwards. This is a deliberate nomenclature because the relay characteristic is governed by the **admittance** ($Y = 1/Z$) of the protected zone, even though the relay physically measures voltage and current.

### 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Lighthouse Beam":** Imagine a lighthouse standing at the origin of a coastline (R-axis). The lighthouse emits a **conical beam of light** that opens out in the *forward* direction. The beam's axis is tilted upward (representing the **RCA**), and the **length of the beam** (diameter) represents the **reach** of the relay. A fault on the line is like a *boat* sailing into this beam. As long as the boat is **inside the beam's illuminated region**, the lighthouse triggers an alarm (relay trip). Boats sailing *behind* the lighthouse (reverse direction) are *never* illuminated — the relay is inherently directional.

### 1.3 Salient Physical Quantities and Notations

- **$Z_f$** = Apparent fault impedance as measured by the relay ($V/I$) in **Ω (primary)**.
- **$Z_r$** = Relay reach setting (numerical impedance setting) in **Ω (secondary)**.
- **$\theta$** = **Relay Characteristic Angle (RCA)** — angle of the diameter with the R-axis, set equal to the **line impedance angle** $\phi_L$.
- **$Z_s$** = Source impedance behind the relay.
- **Reach in km** = $Z_r$ (primary Ω) $\div$ $z_1$ (line impedance per km in primary Ω).

### 1.4 Distinguishing Feature: Why Mho is "Directional"

> [!TIP]
> Because the characteristic circle **passes through the origin** and is **limited to the first quadrant (forward direction)**, the relay:
> 1. Will **operate** for forward faults.
> 2. Will **NOT operate** for reverse faults (negative-reactance / third-quadrant region).
> 3. Will **NOT operate** for heavy load encroachment (since the circle area is small and lies along the line angle).

This eliminates the need for a **separate directional element**, which is mandatory for an *impedance relay*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle (Logical Flow)

The mho relay is constructed using either an **amplitude comparator** or a **phase comparator**. We analyze the phase comparator form, which is more insightful:

- **Signal 1 ($S_1$)** = $V$ (relay terminal voltage)
- **Signal 2 ($S_2$)** = $I \cdot Z_r - V$ (a derived polarizing quantity)

**Operating Criterion (Phase Comparator):**
The relay operates when the phase angle between $S_1$ and $S_2$ lies within $\pm 90^\circ$, i.e.,

$$\text{Re}[S_1 \cdot S_2^*] \geq 0$$

- **Why "How":** The signal $(I \cdot Z_r - V)$ represents the **"expected" voltage drop** over the protected line section; the relay essentially compares the *actual* voltage $V$ with the *expected* voltage. When they are close in phase and magnitude, the fault is *inside* the protected zone.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Tripping

1. The relay measures $V$ and $I$ at the bus.
2. It computes apparent impedance $Z_f = V/I$.
3. It checks the position of $Z_f$ on the R-X plane relative to the mho circle.
4. If $Z_f$ lies **inside** the circle → **Trip Command issued**.
5. If $Z_f$ lies **outside** → **No Trip (block)**.

### 2.3 The Three Critical Settings

| Setting | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Value |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Reach** | $Z_r$ | Diameter of the mho circle (= max. primary impedance) | 80–90% of $Z_L$ |
| **Relay Characteristic Angle** | $\theta$ (or RCA) | Angle of diameter from R-axis | Equal to $\phi_L$ (line angle) |
| **Operating Time** | $t_{op}$ | Time delay after impedance enters circle | 1–3 cycles (instantaneous) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **canonical KTU-exam-ready equations** for the mho relay. All magnitudes are in **secondary ohms** unless otherwise stated.

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Boundary / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Apparent Impedance | $Z_f = V/I$ | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | Operating Equation (Phase Comparator) | $\text{Re}[Z_f \cdot Z_r^*] \geq \vert Z_f \vert^2$ | Scalar inequality |
| 3 | Boundary Circle Equation (R–X plane) | $(R - R_r/2)^2 + (X - X_r/2)^2 = (Z_r/2)^2$ | $(R, X)$ in $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Centre of Mho Circle | $C \equiv (Z_r \cos\theta/2,\; Z_r \sin\theta/2)$ | Cartesian coords |
| 5 | Radius of Mho Circle | $r = \vert Z_r \vert / 2$ | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | Reach (Maximum Trip Impedance) | $Z_{max} = Z_r$ | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Reach in km | $L_{reach} = (Z_r \times \text{CT ratio}) / (z_1 \times \text{PT ratio})$ | km |
| 8 | Diameter Angle (RCA) | $\theta = \angle Z_r$ | degrees / radians |
| 9 | Ideal RCA for Line | $\theta = \phi_L = \tan^{-1}(X_L / R_L)$ | degrees |
| 10 | Operating Time | $t = a + b \cdot \tau$ (definite time) or $t \propto (1 - \vert Z_f \vert / Z_r)$ | seconds |
| 11 | Voltage to operate | $V_{op} = I \cdot \vert Z_f \vert$ | V (secondary) |
| 12 | Polarizing (mho) signal | $S_{pol} = I \cdot Z_r - V$ | V (secondary) |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

- **Primary protection** for EHV and HV transmission lines (typically **Zone-1, Zone-2, Zone-3** distance protection schemes).
- **Generator and transformer backup** protection where directional discrimination is needed.
- **Out-of-step (loss-of-synchronism) detection** in power swing studies.
- **Series-compensated line protection** with appropriate modifications (cross-polarization).
- Used as the **measuring element in static (analog) and numerical (digital) distance relays** — the mathematical characteristic remains the same, only the implementation (analog circuits vs. microprocessor algorithms) changes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Mho Circle Equation from the Phase Comparator

**Starting Point (Comparator Equation):**
The mho relay trips when the phase angle between $S_1 = V$ and $S_2 = I \cdot Z_r - V$ is between $-90^\circ$ and $+90^\circ$. Mathematically, this is:

$$\text{Re}[S_1 \cdot S_2^*] \geq 0$$

**Step 1: Substitute the definitions of $S_1$ and $S_2$.**

$$\text{Re}\!\left[V \cdot (I \cdot Z_r - V)^*\right] \geq 0$$

**Step 2: Expand the conjugate and use linearity of Re.**

$$\text{Re}\!\left[V \cdot I^* \cdot Z_r^*\right] - \text{Re}\!\left[V \cdot V^*\right] \geq 0$$

**Step 3: Recognize that $V \cdot V^* = \vert V \vert^2$ (a real positive number).**

$$\text{Re}\!\left[V \cdot I^* \cdot Z_r^*\right] - \vert V \vert^2 \geq 0$$

**Step 4: Substitute $V = I \cdot Z_f$ (apparent impedance definition).**

$$\text{Re}\!\left[(I \cdot Z_f) \cdot I^* \cdot Z_r^*\right] - \vert I \cdot Z_f \vert^2 \geq 0$$

**Step 5: Factor out $\vert I \vert^2$ from both terms (it is always non-negative and non-zero under fault).**

$$\vert I \vert^2 \cdot \text{Re}\!\left[Z_f \cdot Z_r^*\right] - \vert I \vert^2 \cdot \vert Z_f \vert^2 \geq 0$$

**Step 6: Divide both sides by $\vert I \vert^2$ (positive under fault conditions).**

$$\text{Re}\!\left[Z_f \cdot Z_r^*\right] \geq \vert Z_f \vert^2$$

This is the **canonical trip equation** of the mho relay in the impedance domain.

**Step 7: Express $Z_f$ and $Z_r$ in terms of R and X components.**
Let $Z_f = R + jX$ and $Z_r = R_r + jX_r$, where $R_r = Z_r \cos\theta$ and $X_r = Z_r \sin\theta$.

**Step 8: Compute $Z_f \cdot Z_r^*$ explicitly.**

$$Z_f \cdot Z_r^* = (R + jX)(R_r - jX_r) = (R \cdot R_r + X \cdot X_r) + j(X \cdot R_r - R \cdot X_r)$$

**Step 9: Take the real part and substitute into the trip equation.**

$$R \cdot R_r + X \cdot X_r \geq R^2 + X^2$$

**Step 10: Rearrange into a form suitable for completing the square.**

$$0 \geq R^2 - R \cdot R_r + X^2 - X \cdot X_r$$

Multiply by $-1$ (and reverse the inequality):

$$R^2 - R \cdot R_r + X^2 - X \cdot X_r \leq 0$$

**Step 11: Complete the square in $R$ and $X$ separately.**

$$(R^2 - R \cdot R_r + R_r^2/4) + (X^2 - X \cdot X_r + X_r^2/4) \leq R_r^2/4 + X_r^2/4$$

**Step 12: Recognize the left side as a sum of perfect squares and the right side as $(Z_r/2)^2$.**

$$(R - R_r/2)^2 + (X - X_r/2)^2 \leq (Z_r/2)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Equation of the Mho Circle on the R-X Plane:**
> $$\boxed{(R - R_r/2)^2 + (X - X_r/2)^2 \leq (Z_r/2)^2 \;\;\text{— for operation (trip)}}$$
> $$\boxed{(R - R_r/2)^2 + (X - X_r/2)^2 = (Z_r/2)^2 \;\;\text{— for boundary (incipient trip)}}$$

**Geometric Interpretation of the Result:**

- **Centre of the mho circle** is at $C = \left(\dfrac{R_r}{2}, \dfrac{X_r}{2}\right) = \dfrac{Z_r}{2}\angle\theta$ on the R-X plane.
- **Radius of the mho circle** is $r = \dfrac{|Z_r|}{2}$.
- The circle **passes through the origin** (substitute $R = X = 0$ → LHS $= (R_r/2)^2 + (X_r/2)^2 = (Z_r/2)^2$ = RHS, satisfying the boundary equation).
- The **diameter** of the circle lies along the line connecting the origin to the point $Z_r$, hence the diameter is oriented at the **Relay Characteristic Angle $\theta$** from the R-axis.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Reach in Kilometres

The reach in kilometres is the length of the protected line corresponding to the numerical setting $Z_r$:

$$L_{reach} = \frac{Z_r \times N_{CT}}{z_1 \times N_{PT}}$$

where $N_{CT}$ is the CT ratio, $N_{PT}$ is the PT ratio, and $z_1$ is the positive-sequence line impedance in **primary Ω/km**.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- $Z_r$ is set in *secondary* ohms.
- Multiply by $N_{CT}/N_{PT}$ to convert to *primary* ohms.
- Divide by $z_1$ to obtain equivalent *line length in km*.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Optimum RCA

The maximum sensitivity of the mho relay is obtained when the **line of the diameter** is collinear with the **line impedance vector**. To prove this, consider the apparent impedance:

$$Z_f = z_1 \cdot L_f \cdot e^{j\phi_L} + R_{arc}$$

The component of $Z_f$ *along* the diameter is:

$$Z_{||} = |Z_f| \cos(\phi_L - \theta)$$

The fault is detected when $Z_{||} \leq |Z_r| / 2$ (since the diameter extends $Z_r/2$ in the $\theta$ direction and $Z_r/2$ in the opposite direction). The maximum reach is achieved when $\theta = \phi_L$, because then $\cos(\phi_L - \theta) = 1$, maximizing the projection $Z_{||}$ and hence maximizing the reach along the line direction.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Rule (used in KTU numericals):** $\theta_{RCA} = \tan^{-1}(X_L/R_L) = \phi_L$ of the protected line.

### 3.4 Boundary Condition: Maximum Torque Angle (MTA) vs RCA

The **Maximum Torque Angle (MTA)** of the mho relay corresponds to the angle at which the relay's "torque" (operating tendency) is maximum. For a mho relay, MTA = RCA = $\theta$ because the diameter of the circle is the locus of points farthest from the origin in the direction of $\theta$.

### 3.5 Numerical Example: Verifying a Mho Circle

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A 220 kV line has $z_1 = 0.4 \;\Omega/\text{km}$ and length = 100 km. The relay CT ratio is 1200/5 and PT ratio is 220000/110. The relay is set to protect 80% of the line. Calculate the secondary reach $Z_r$, the centre, and the radius of the mho circle. Assume RCA = 75°.

**Step 1:** Line primary impedance $= 0.4 \times 100 = 40\;\Omega$.
**Step 2:** Desired primary reach $= 0.8 \times 40 = 32\;\Omega$.
**Step 3:** Conversion factor $K = N_{PT}/N_{CT} = (220000/110) / (1200/5) = 2000 / 240 = 8.333$.
**Step 4:** Secondary reach $Z_r = 32 / 8.333 = 3.84\;\Omega$.
**Step 5:** Centre: $C = (Z_r \cos 75°/2,\; Z_r \sin 75°/2) = (3.84 \times 0.2588/2,\; 3.84 \times 0.9659/2) = (0.497,\; 1.855)\;\Omega$.
**Step 6:** Radius: $r = 3.84/2 = 1.92\;\Omega$.

This is a typical KTU 7-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Construction of a Mho Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VT[VT - Voltage Transformer] -->|V| INJ1[Signal Conditioning]
    CT[CT - Current Transformer] -->|I| INJ2[Signal Conditioning]
    INJ2 --> MULT[Analog Multiplier]
    ZR[Z_r Setting - Reach] --> MULT
    MULT --> DIFF[SUM: I Z_r MINUS V]
    INJ1 --> COMP[Phase Comparator]
    DIFF --> COMP
    COMP --> TMR[Timer - Definite Time]
    TMR --> TRIP[TRIP COIL - Circuit Breaker]
    COMP --> DC[Directional Check]
    DC --> TMR
    RCA[RCA Setting - Phase Shifter] --> DC
    style VT fill:#FFD700
    style CT fill:#FFD700
    style COMP fill:#FF6347
    style TRIP fill:#32CD32
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The voltage $V$ from the VT and the current-derived signal $I \cdot Z_r$ from the CT branch are fed to a *phase comparator*. The comparator checks whether the operating quantity $V$ is in phase (within $\pm 90^\circ$) with the polarizing quantity $I \cdot Z_r - V$. On satisfaction, the output enables the trip coil through a definite-time timer.

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Mho Circle on the R-X Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R-X-PLANE [R-X IMPEDANCE PLANE - First Quadrant]
        ORIGIN[ORIGIN 0 0]
        CENTRE[CENTRE Z_r by 2 at angle RCA]
        POINT_R[REACH POINT Z_r at angle RCA]
        FWD_ARC[Forward Directional Arc]
        F1[Fault Inside Circle - TRIP]
        F2[Fault Outside Circle - NO TRIP]
        F3[Reverse Fault - BLOCKED]
        ORIGIN --- CENTRE
        ORIGIN --- POINT_R
        CENTRE --- F1
        F2 --- CENTRE
        F3 -.->|behind origin| CENTRE
    end
    style ORIGIN fill:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style CENTRE fill:#FF6347
    style POINT_R fill:#32CD32
    style F1 fill:#90EE90
    style F2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style F3 fill:#D3D3D3
```

> **Visual interpretation:** The mho circle is drawn with its *diameter* along the line from the **origin** to the **reach point** $Z_r \angle \theta$. The **forward arc** (first quadrant) is the only part used for protection. Faults $F_1$ inside the circle trip; $F_2$ outside are blocked; $F_3$ behind the relay (reverse direction) are inherently blocked.

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Phase Comparator Realization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[INPUT 1: V - Operating Signal] -->|op-amp| PC[PHASE COMPARATOR]
    S2[INPUT 2: I Z_r MINUS V - Polarizing Signal] -->|op-amp| PC
    PC -->|output pulses| INT[Integrator]
    INT --> COMP_V[Compares with threshold]
    COMP_V --> T[Trip Output]
    style S1 fill:#87CEEB
    style S2 fill:#FFD700
    style PC fill:#FF6347
    style T fill:#32CD32
```

> **Block interpretation:** The *phase comparator* integrates the product $S_1 \cdot S_2$ over a cycle. If the average is positive (i.e., the phase difference lies in $(-90^\circ, +90^\circ)$), the trip output is energized.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Mho relay. Why is it called "Mho" and not "Ohm"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A mho relay is a **directional distance relay** whose characteristic on the R-X plane is a **circle passing through the origin**, with diameter equal to the reach $Z_r$ along the Relay Characteristic Angle (RCA). **[1 mark]**
- The relay operates when the apparent impedance $Z_f = V/I$ falls inside this circle. **[1 mark]**
- The name "Mho" is **"Ohm" spelled backwards** because the relay's operating criterion is fundamentally governed by **admittance** ($Y = 1/Z$), even though the relay measures voltage and current. **[1 mark]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the Relay Characteristic Angle (RCA) of a mho relay? Why is it set equal to the line impedance angle?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **RCA** is the angle made by the **diameter of the mho circle** with the positive R-axis on the R-X plane. It is denoted by $\theta$. **[1 mark]**
- It represents the direction in which the relay has **maximum reach**. **[1 mark]**
- It is set equal to the **line impedance angle** $\phi_L$ to **maximize the reach** along the line direction. This makes the diameter of the mho circle collinear with the line impedance vector, ensuring that the apparent fault impedance for any fault on the line falls *inside* the circle for the longest possible section. **[1 mark]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat R-X diagram, **explain the construction and operating principle of a Mho relay**. Discuss the significance of the Relay Characteristic Angle. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 110 kV, 50 Hz, 3-phase transmission line has the following parameters: $R_1 = 0.2\;\Omega/\text{km}$, $X_1 = 0.4\;\Omega/\text{km}$, length = 80 km. The CT ratio is 600/5 and PT ratio is 110000/110. The mho relay is set to protect 85% of the line, with RCA = 75°. **Determine the reach setting, the centre, and the radius of the mho circle in secondary ohms.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Operating Principle (7 marks):**

- **Construction:** A mho relay is constructed using a **phase comparator** with two input signals: $S_1 = V$ (the relay terminal voltage) and $S_2 = I \cdot Z_r - V$ (a polarizing signal derived by multiplying the line current with the reach setting impedance $Z_r$ and subtracting the terminal voltage). **[1 mark]**
- **Comparator Action:** The phase comparator measures the phase angle between $S_1$ and $S_2$. The relay trips when this phase angle lies between $-90^\circ$ and $+90^\circ$, i.e., $\text{Re}[S_1 \cdot S_2^*] \geq 0$. **[1 mark]**
- **Mathematical Trip Equation:** Substituting $V = I \cdot Z_f$ and simplifying, the trip condition reduces to $\text{Re}[Z_f \cdot Z_r^*] \geq |Z_f|^2$, which in R–X coordinates gives the **mho circle**: $(R - R_r/2)^2 + (X - X_r/2)^2 \leq (Z_r/2)^2$. **[2 marks]**
- **R-X Diagram:** The characteristic is a circle with **centre at** $Z_r/2 \angle \theta$ and **radius** $|Z_r|/2$, passing through the origin. The diameter lies along the line at angle $\theta$ (RCA) from the R-axis. Only the **first-quadrant arc** is used for forward-fault detection. **[1 mark]**
- **Significance of RCA:** The RCA determines the orientation of the mho circle on the R-X plane. It is **set equal to the line impedance angle** $\phi_L$ to maximize the projection of the apparent fault impedance along the diameter, thereby maximizing the effective reach. **[1 mark]**
- **Directional Property:** Since the circle passes through the origin and is restricted to the first quadrant, the mho relay is **inherently directional** and does not require a separate directional element. **[1 mark]**

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

- **Step 1:** Line impedance per km: $z_1 = \sqrt{R_1^2 + X_1^2} = \sqrt{0.2^2 + 0.4^2} = \sqrt{0.20} = 0.447\;\Omega/\text{km}$. **[1 mark]**
- **Step 2:** Total primary impedance: $Z_{line} = 0.447 \times 80 = 35.78\;\Omega$. **[0.5 marks]**
- **Step 3:** Primary reach: $Z_{primary} = 0.85 \times 35.78 = 30.41\;\Omega$. **[0.5 marks]**
- **Step 4:** Conversion factor: $K = (N_{PT}/N_{CT}) = (110000/110) / (600/5) = 1000 / 120 = 8.333$. **[1 mark]**
- **Step 5:** Secondary reach: $Z_r = 30.41 / 8.333 = 3.65\;\Omega$. **[1 mark]**
- **Step 6:** Centre: $C = (Z_r \cos 75°/2,\; Z_r \sin 75°/2) = (3.65 \times 0.2588/2,\; 3.65 \times 0.9659/2) = (0.472,\; 1.762)\;\Omega$. **[2 marks]**
- **Step 7:** Radius: $r = Z_r/2 = 3.65/2 = 1.825\;\Omega$. **[1 mark]**
- **Result:** The mho circle has **centre (0.472, 1.762) Ω** and **radius 1.825 Ω** on the secondary R-X plane.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**(a)** **Derive the equation of the Mho relay characteristic on the R-X plane**, starting from the phase comparator equation. Show clearly the centre and radius of the circle, and explain why the characteristic is inherently directional. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** **Compare the Mho relay with the Impedance relay and the Reactance relay** in terms of (i) R-X characteristic shape, (ii) directionality, (iii) effect of arc resistance, and (iv) suitability for long transmission lines. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Mho Circle Equation (7 marks):**

- **Step 1:** Start with the phase comparator equation: $\text{Re}[V \cdot (I \cdot Z_r - V)^*] \geq 0$. **[0.5 marks]**
- **Step 2:** Expand: $\text{Re}[V \cdot I^* \cdot Z_r^*] - |V|^2 \geq 0$. **[1 mark]**
- **Step 3:** Substitute $V = I \cdot Z_f$: $|I|^2 \cdot \text{Re}[Z_f \cdot Z_r^*] - |I|^2 \cdot |Z_f|^2 \geq 0$. **[1 mark]**
- **Step 4:** Simplify: $\text{Re}[Z_f \cdot Z_r^*] \geq |Z_f|^2$. **[1 mark]**
- **Step 5:** Expand with $Z_f = R + jX$, $Z_r = R_r + jX_r$: $(R \cdot R_r + X \cdot X_r) \geq (R^2 + X^2)$. **[1 mark]**
- **Step 6:** Complete the square to obtain: $(R - R_r/2)^2 + (X - X_r/2)^2 \leq (Z_r/2)^2$. **[1.5 marks]**
- **Conclusion:** This is a **circle with centre** $(R_r/2,\; X_r/2)$ and **radius** $Z_r/2$, passing through the origin. Directionality arises because the circle is geometrically restricted to the first quadrant (forward direction), and the origin itself is on the boundary — meaning a reverse fault (negative R or X) is geometrically *outside* the circle. **[1 mark]**

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | Impedance Relay | Mho Relay | Reactance Relay |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **R-X Shape** | Circle centred at origin | Circle through origin | Straight line $X = $ const. |
| **Directional?** | No (non-directional) | Yes (inherently) | No (non-directional) |
| **Arc Resistance Effect** | Causes under-reach | Less affected (diameter along line) | Highly affected (over-reach) |
| **Suitability for Long Lines** | Poor (load encroachment) | Excellent (Zone-1, Zone-2) | Used in backup only |
| **Area of Operation** | Maximum (entire circle) | Smaller (semi-arc) | Half-plane (above line) |
| **Polarizing Signal** | $V$ | $I \cdot Z_r - V$ (memory/action) | $I \cdot X_r$ (reactance setting) |

**Justifications (2 marks for key points):**
- The mho relay's circle is smaller than the impedance relay's circle, so it is **less prone to maloperate on heavy load**.
- The mho relay inherently blocks reverse faults, unlike the impedance relay.
- The mho relay is **less affected by arc resistance** because the arc resistance vector is nearly perpendicular to the diameter (along R-axis), and the mho circle is "tilted" along the line angle, so the arc resistance does not pull the apparent impedance *out* of the circle as severely as in a pure impedance relay.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $Z_r$ with $|Z_r|/2$:** Many students write the *radius* of the mho circle as the reach. The *reach* is the **diameter** ($Z_r$), and the *radius* is $Z_r/2$. A 1–2 mark penalty applies for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to secondary ohms:** The CT/PT ratio conversion step is mandatory in numerical problems. Skipping it costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Setting RCA ≠ $\phi_L$:** The RCA must equal the line impedance angle for maximum sensitivity. Using an arbitrary angle (e.g., 60° when line angle is 75°) is a conceptual error costing **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting "first quadrant only":** The mho relay uses **only the forward arc** of the circle. Drawing the full circle or quoting the centre in the third quadrant will lose marks.
> 5. **In derivation, skipping the Re[...] step:** Examiners expect the full chain — phase comparator $\to$ $\text{Re}[S_1 S_2^*]$ $\to$ substitution of $V = I Z_f$ $\to$ completion of square. A short-cut derivation loses **3+ marks**.
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Always quote secondary ohms for relay settings; primary ohms for system values. Mixing them up is a frequent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Mho relay** = directional distance relay; characteristic = **circle through origin** on R-X plane.
- [x] Name origin: **"Mho" = "Ohm" reversed** (admittance-based).
- [x] **Reach ($Z_r$)** = diameter of the circle; **radius** = $Z_r/2$.
- [x] **Centre** = $Z_r/2 \cdot \angle \theta$; **RCA = $\theta$**.
- [x] **RCA is set equal to line impedance angle** $\phi_L$ for maximum sensitivity.
- [x] **Operating equation:** $\text{Re}[Z_f \cdot Z_r^*] \geq |Z_f|^2$.
- [x] **Circle equation:** $(R - R_r/2)^2 + (X - X_r/2)^2 \leq (Z_r/2)^2$.
- [x] **Inherently directional** — no separate directional unit needed.
- [x] **Smaller operating area** than impedance relay → safer for load encroachment.
- [x] **Less affected by arc resistance** than impedance/reactance relays.
- [x] **Comparator type:** Phase comparator (preferred) or amplitude comparator.
- [x] **Used for:** EHV/HV line protection, generator backup, out-of-step detection.
- [x] **Conversion to km:** $L_{reach} = (Z_r \cdot N_{CT}) / (z_1 \cdot N_{PT})$.
- [x] **Maximum reach direction** = along the diameter (RCA line).
- [x] **Trip zone** = inside the first-quadrant arc of the circle.
- [x] **Mho circle passes through origin** — distinguishes it from impedance circle.
- [x] **Numericals:** Always show conversion factor $K = N_{PT}/N_{CT}$ explicitly.
- [x] **In digital/numerical relays:** The same mathematical characteristic is implemented using microprocessor algorithms (DFT, phasor estimation) — the geometric interpretation is unchanged.
- [x] **Pitfall:** Radius is **half** the reach — a frequently tested distinction.
- [x] **Pitfall:** For Zone-1, the reach is set at **80–90%** of the line (not 100%) to avoid overreach due to measurement errors and CT/PT inaccuracies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schemes of Distance Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Schemes of Distance Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distance Protection Scheme** is a non-unit protection technique in which the relay's operation is determined by the electrical *distance* (impedance) between the relay location and the fault point, rather than by the absolute magnitude of current or voltage alone. The relay measures the **apparent impedance** $Z_{app} = \dfrac{V}{I}$ seen from the relaying point and compares it against a pre-set threshold. If $Z_{app}$ falls inside a defined operating zone (Zone 1, Zone 2, or Zone 3), the relay issues a trip command after a graded time delay.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the term *Schemes of Distance Protection* covers:

1. **Three-Step (3-Zone) Distance Protection Scheme**
2. **Pilot / Unit Protection schemes aided by distance relays**
3. **Over-reach and Under-reach schemes**
4. **R-X Diagram based characteristics (Impedance, Reactance, Mho, Quadrilateral)**
5. **Power Swing / Out-of-Step blocking schemes**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Speedometer vs. The Trip Meter"**
> 
> Imagine you are driving from City A to City B. A distance-protection relay behaves like a smart *trip-meter* on your dashboard. The relay keeps asking: *"If the fault were at this many kilometers from the bus-bar, would the voltage-to-current ratio (apparent impedance) look small enough to be a fault on THIS line?"*
> 
> - **Zone 1** = the green zone (instant trip) — covers 80–90% of your line.
> - **Zone 2** = the amber zone (slight delay) — covers your line + half of the next.
> - **Zone 3** = the red backup zone (longer delay) — covers everything visible on the horizon.
> 
> The further the fault, the *higher* the apparent impedance (because the line between relay and fault looks more resistive/inductive). When the apparent impedance drops below a pre-defined boundary, the relay concludes *"the fault is on my line"* and trips.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU/Industry) |
|---|---|
| Nominal Frequency | **50 Hz** (India) |
| Zone 1 reach | **80–90 %** of protected line |
| Zone 2 reach | **120–150 %** of protected line |
| Zone 3 reach | **Next adjacent line + backup** |
| Zone 1 operating time | **20–40 ms** (instantaneous) |
| Zone 2 time delay | **0.3–0.5 s** |
| Zone 3 time delay | **0.6–1.0 s** |
| Standard CT ratio | **1200/1 A** (typical EHV) |
| Standard VT ratio | **400 kV / 110 /√3 V** |

## 1.4 Need for Multi-Zone Schemes — Why not a single zone?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **single-zone** scheme cannot simultaneously achieve **speed, selectivity, and security**. 
> - *Too small a zone* → relay may not operate for end-of-line faults. 
> - *Too large a zone* → relay trips for faults on neighbouring lines, ruining **discrimination**. 
> The 3-zone scheme balances *speed in Zone 1* with *backup coverage in Zones 2 and 3* through graded time delays — exactly mirroring the inverse relationship between time and reach used in overcurrent grading.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Distance Protection

A distance relay has two essential inputs:
1. **Voltage** $V$ at the relaying bus (from CVT/VT)
2. **Current** $I$ feeding the protected line (from CT)

These are processed to yield a complex quantity — the **apparent impedance**:

$$Z_{app} = \frac{V}{I}$$

$$Z_{app} = R_{app} + jX_{app}$$

The relay continuously measures $Z_{app}$ and plots it on the **R–X plane**. Each relay has a *characteristic curve* (circle, straight line, or quadrilateral). When the trajectory of $Z_{app}$ enters the protected region (inside the boundary), the relay operates.

## 2.2 The Three-Step / Three-Zone Scheme (Stepped Distance Protection)

This is the **most commonly examined** configuration in KTU papers. The arrangement is illustrated as three concentric, time-graded zones in the R-X plane.

### Step 1 — Zone 1 (Primary / Instantaneous)

- Reach setting: **80–90 % of $Z_{L1}$** (protected line impedance).
- Time: **Instantaneous** (≈ 20–40 ms).
- Purpose: **Primary protection** for faults on the protected line.
- Discriminates faults *inside* the line from those *just beyond* the remote bus.

> [!NOTE]
> Why not 100 %? The **over-reach error** (≈ 5–20 %) caused by CT/VT inaccuracies, fault arc resistance, and line parameter tolerances would otherwise cause Zone 1 to trip for faults on the *next* line, violating discrimination.

### Step 2 — Zone 2 (Primary Backup)

- Reach setting: **100 % of $Z_{L1}$ + (40–50 %) of $Z_{L2}$** (next line).
- Time: **0.3 – 0.5 s** (graded with Zone 1 of the next line).
- Purpose: Backs up Zone 1 of the *adjacent* line if its primary relay fails.
- Set to cover *at least* the end of the next line section.

### Step 3 — Zone 3 (Remote Backup)

- Reach setting: **100 % of $Z_{L1}$ + 100 % of $Z_{L2}$ + 25 % of $Z_{L3}$**.
- Time: **0.6 – 1.0 s**.
- Purpose: Provides a final **remote backup** if all local protections fail.

## 2.3 R–X Diagram Characteristics (Relay Operating Boundaries)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **shape** of the operating region in the R–X plane defines the relay *type* — this is one of the highest-weightage topics in KTU Module 2.

### (a) Impedance Relay (Circles centred at origin)

$$Z_{app} \le Z_{set} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \left(\frac{V}{I}\right)^{2} \le Z_{set}^{\,2}$$

Characteristic: Circle of radius $Z_{set}$ centred at the **origin**. Non-directional — operates for faults *behind* the relay too. Requires an additional directional unit.

### (b) Reactance Relay (Straight line parallel to R-axis)

$$X_{app} \le X_{set} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \frac{V \sin\phi}{I} \le X_{set}$$

Characteristic: A straight horizontal line in the R-X plane. **Insensitive to fault resistance** $R_f$ (which adds along the R-axis). Used on **short, heavily loaded lines** where arc resistance is significant.

### (c) Mho Relay / Admittance Relay (Circle through origin)

$$Y_{app} \ge Y_{set} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \left|\,Z_{app} - \dfrac{Z_{set}}{2}\,\right| \le \dfrac{Z_{set}}{2}$$

Characteristic: Circle **passing through the origin**, with diameter along the line impedance vector. **Self-directional** — inherently does not operate for reverse faults. Most widely used for EHV transmission.

### (d) Quadrilateral Characteristic

A four-sided figure combining:
- **Top boundary** → $X = X_{set}$ (reactance reach)
- **Right boundary** → $R = R_{set}$ (resistance reach, controls arc tolerance)
- **Left & bottom** → directional lines

> [!NOTE]
> Best suited for **series-compensated lines** and **long EHV lines** where arc resistance cannot be neglected.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula needed for KTU 2024 Module 2 questions on this topic. **Avoid the `|` symbol in cells; use `\vert` instead.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apparent Impedance | $Z_{app} = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Complex ratio |
| 2 | Apparent Reactance | $X_{app} = \dfrac{V}{I}\sin\phi$ | $\phi$ = angle between V and I |
| 3 | Apparent Resistance | $R_{app} = \dfrac{V}{I}\cos\phi$ | Same |
| 4 | Zone 1 Reach | $0.80\,Z_{L} \le Z_{1} \le 0.90\,Z_{L}$ | $Z_{L}$ = protected line |
| 5 | Zone 2 Reach | $Z_{2} = Z_{L1} + 0.5\,Z_{L2}$ | $\approx 1.2 Z_{1}$ |
| 6 | Zone 3 Reach | $Z_{3} = Z_{L1} + Z_{L2} + 0.25\,Z_{L3}$ | $\approx 1.2 Z_{2}$ |
| 7 | Mho Circle Centre | $C = \dfrac{Z_{set}}{2}$ | On the line impedance phasor |
| 8 | Mho Circle Radius | $r = \dfrac{Z_{set}}{2}$ | Circle passes through origin |
| 9 | Reactance Line | $X = X_{set}$ | Horizontal line, R-X plane |
| 10 | Quadrilateral R | $R = R_{set}$ | Vertical boundary |
| 11 | Quadrilateral X | $X = X_{set}$ | Horizontal boundary |
| 12 | Impedance Circle | $\vert Z_{app} \vert \le Z_{set}$ | Centred at origin |
| 13 | Line Impedance | $Z_{L} = z \times \ell$ | $z$ = pu length, $\ell$ = km |
| 14 | Reach Setting Error | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Z_{reach} - Z_{act}}{Z_{act}} \times 100$ | In % |
| 15 | PSB Time Delay | $t_{psb} \approx 50\,ms$ | Power Swing Blocker |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EHV/UHV Transmission Lines (400 kV, 765 kV, 1200 kV):** Distance protection is the *de-facto* primary protection for lines above 66 kV because simple overcurrent relays cannot grade across long lines.
- **Series-Compensated Lines:** Quadrilateral characteristics accommodate the negative reactance introduced by series capacitors.
- **Sub-transmission and Distribution:** Impedance relays remain in service for radial and short sub-transmission feeders.
- **Modern Numerical Relays:** Use **phasor estimation (DFT/FFT)** on sampled V and I and compute $Z_{app}$ every cycle; the same digital framework runs impedance, mho, and quadrilateral characteristics in parallel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Mho Relay Characteristic Equation

The mho (or admittance) relay operates when the magnitude of the admittance seen by the relay exceeds a threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> A mho relay contains a **voltage-polarising coil** (operates on $V$) and a **current coil** (operates on $I$). The relay is a comparator: it operates when $V \cos(\theta - \phi) \ge kI$, where $\theta$ is the relay characteristic angle and $\phi$ is the angle between V and I.

### Step-by-step derivation

Step 1: Write the operating equation of the comparator in the polar form:

$$V \cos(\theta - \phi) \;\ge\; k\,I$$

Step 2: Divide both sides by $I$ (assume $I \neq 0$):

$$\frac{V}{I}\,\cos(\theta - \phi) \;\ge\; k$$

Step 3: Recognise that $\dfrac{V}{I} = \vert Z_{app} \vert$ and $\theta - \phi$ is the angle between the apparent impedance and the relay characteristic line:

$$\vert Z_{app} \vert \cos(\theta - \phi) \;\ge\; k$$

Step 4: Express $Z_{app}$ in rectangular form $Z_{app} = R_{app} + jX_{app}$. Let $\psi = \theta - \phi$ be the *torque angle* measured from the characteristic line. The term $\vert Z_{app} \vert \cos\psi$ is the **projection** of $Z_{app}$ on the characteristic direction:

$$Z_{app}\cos\psi \;=\; R_{app}\cos\theta + X_{app}\sin\theta$$

Step 5: Therefore the operating criterion becomes:

$$R_{app}\cos\theta + X_{app}\sin\theta \;\ge\; k$$

Step 6: This is the equation of a **straight line in the R-X plane** in the polar form $(r, \psi)$ rotated by angle $\theta$. The set of *all points at a perpendicular distance $k$ from the origin* is a straight line. By geometric inversion (circle inversion of a line through origin), the locus of *constant $Y$* (admittance) in the **Z-plane** is a **circle through the origin**.

Step 7: The centre of the circle lies on the line impedance phasor at a distance $Z_{set}/2$ from the origin; the radius equals $Z_{set}/2$. The condition $\vert Z_{app}\vert \le Z_{set}$ inside the circle is the mho relay's operating region.

## 3.2 Derivation — Apparent Impedance with Fault Resistance

For a fault at distance $m\,Z_{L}$ from the sending end with arc resistance $R_f$:

Step 1: The total loop impedance seen by the relay (single line-to-ground fault, neglecting source):

$$Z_{app} \;=\; \frac{V}{I} \;=\; m\,Z_{L} + R_f$$

Step 2: In rectangular form, with $Z_{L} = R_{L} + jX_{L}$:

$$Z_{app} \;=\; m\,R_{L} + R_f \;+\; j\,m\,X_{L}$$

Step 3: The fault resistance $R_f$ therefore shifts the apparent impedance **horizontally to the right** in the R-X plane. This is why a **reactance relay** (whose boundary is $X = X_{set}$, independent of $R_f$) is preferred when $R_f$ is significant.

## 3.3 Three-Zone Reach Calculation — Worked Numerical

Consider two series transmission lines:
- $Z_{L1} = 40\,\Omega$ (line 1, length 100 km)
- $Z_{L2} = 50\,\Omega$ (line 2, length 125 km)
- $Z_{L3} = 30\,\Omega$ (line 3)

> [!NOTE]
> For each zone, calculate the reach and verify the standard reach ratios.

**Zone 1 reach:**

$$Z_1 = 0.85 \times Z_{L1} = 0.85 \times 40 = 34\;\Omega$$

**Zone 2 reach (50 % of next line):**

$$Z_2 = Z_{L1} + 0.5 \times Z_{L2} = 40 + 0.5 \times 50 = 65\;\Omega$$

**Zone 3 reach (full next line + 25 % of line after):**

$$Z_3 = Z_{L1} + Z_{L2} + 0.25 \times Z_{L3} = 40 + 50 + 0.25 \times 30 = 97.5\;\Omega$$

**Verification of standard ratios:**

$$\frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = \frac{65}{34} \approx 1.91 \quad\text{(typical: 1.2 – 1.5)}$$

$$\frac{Z_3}{Z_2} = \frac{97.5}{65} \approx 1.5 \quad\text{(typical: 1.2 – 1.5)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If your calculated ratio lies *outside* the typical 1.2 – 1.5 band, the practical reach setting must be **clamped** to the standard ratio to prevent overlap with the next line's Zone 1. This is the heart of KTU 2-mark and 7-mark derivations.

## 3.4 Over-reach and Under-reach — Analytical Treatment

**Under-reach** occurs when the apparent impedance as measured is *larger* than the actual fault-loop impedance. Causes:
- Intermediate infeed at junction buses
- Source impedance contribution
- Mutual coupling (zero sequence) on parallel lines

**Over-reach** occurs when the apparent impedance is *smaller* than the actual loop impedance. Causes:
- Fault arc resistance (*under-reach* effect for mho, *over-reach* for impedance)
- Series capacitors (negative reactance appears)
- VT/CVT transients

The compensation factor for **infeed** at a junction bus:

$$Z_{set,\,adj} = Z_{set} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{I_{inf}}{I_{relay}}\right)$$

where $I_{inf}$ = infeed current and $I_{relay}$ = current through the relay.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Zone Distance Protection Scheme (R-X Plane)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZONE1["ZONE 1 INSTANTANEOUS REACH 80 TO 90 PERCENT"]
        Z1A[RELAY 1 BUS] --> Z1B[FAULT INSIDE LINE]
        Z1C[TIME 20 TO 40 MS] --> Z1A
    end
    subgraph ZONE2["ZONE 2 TIME DELAYED 0.3 TO 0.5 SECONDS REACH 120 TO 150 PERCENT"]
        Z2A[RELAY 2 BUS] --> Z2B[FAULT ON ADJACENT LINE]
        Z2C[BACKUP FOR NEXT LINE] --> Z2A
    end
    subgraph ZONE3["ZONE 3 TIME DELAYED 0.6 TO 1.0 SECONDS REMOTE BACKUP"]
        Z3A[RELAY 3 BUS] --> Z3B[FAULT FAR DOWN THE NETWORK]
        Z3C[FINAL BACKUP COVERAGE] --> Z3A
    end
    ZONE1 --> ZONE2
    ZONE2 --> ZONE3
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each zone is realised as a mho or quadrilateral characteristic in the digital relay's memory. The relay tracks the trajectory of $Z_{app}$ and decides based on the **zone of entry** of the impedance locus.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Digital Distance Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AN_V[VT SECONDARY VOLTAGE 110 BY ROOT 3] --> ANA[ANALOG INPUT STAGE]
    AN_I[CT SECONDARY CURRENT 1 AMP] --> ANA
    ANA --> AAF[ANTI ALIASING FILTER LPF 1 KHZ]
    AAF --> ADC[ADC SAMPLING 16 BIT 4 KHZ]
    ADC --> DFT[DFT PHASOR ESTIMATION]
    DFT --> COMP[Z APP CALCULATION V DIVIDED BY I]
    COMP --> CHK1{Z APP IN ZONE 1}
    COMP --> CHK2{Z APP IN ZONE 2}
    COMP --> CHK3{Z APP IN ZONE 3}
    CHK1 -->|YES| TRIP1[INSTANTANEOUS TRIP T LESS THAN 30 MS]
    CHK2 -->|YES| TIMER2[START TIMER T2 0.4 S]
    TIMER2 -->|ELAPSED| TRIP2[ZONE 2 TRIP]
    CHK3 -->|YES| TIMER3[START TIMER T3 0.8 S]
    TIMER3 -->|ELAPSED| TRIP3[ZONE 3 TRIP]
    COMP --> PSB[POWER SWING DETECTOR DOUBLE BLINDERS]
    PSB --> BLOCK[TEMPORARILY BLOCK ZONE 3]
    TRIP1 --> CB[TRIP CIRCUIT BREAKER]
    TRIP2 --> CB
    TRIP3 --> CB
```

## 4.3 Relay Characteristics on the R-X Plane (Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IMP[IMPEDANCE RELAY CIRCLE CENTRED AT ORIGIN]
        IMPA[R AXIS] --> IMPC[CIRCLE RADIUS Z SET]
        IMPB[X AXIS] --> IMPC
    end
    subgraph REA[REACTANCE RELAY HORIZONTAL LINE]
        REAA[R AXIS] --> REAC[LINE X EQUALS X SET]
        REAB[X AXIS] --> REAC
    end
    subgraph MHO[MHO RELAY CIRCLE THROUGH ORIGIN]
        MHOA[R AXIS] --> MHOC[CIRCLE CENTRE Z SET BY 2 RADIUS Z SET BY 2]
        MHOB[X AXIS] --> MHOC
    end
    subgraph QUA[QUADRILATERAL FOUR SIDED REGION]
        QUAA[R EQUALS R SET] --> QUAC[OPERATE REGION R LESS THAN R SET AND X LESS THAN X SET]
        QUAB[X EQUALS X SET] --> QUAC
        QUAD[DIRECTIONAL LINE] --> QUAC
    end
```

## 4.4 Trip-Time vs Reach Characteristic (Grading)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FAULT ON LINE] --> B{Z APP VALUE}
    B -->|LESS THAN Z1| C[ZONE 1 INSTANTANEOUS T 0.03 S]
    B -->|BETWEEN Z1 AND Z2| D[ZONE 2 TIMER 0.3 S]
    B -->|BETWEEN Z2 AND Z3| E[ZONE 3 TIMER 0.8 S]
    B -->|ABOVE Z3| F[NO TRIP OUTSIDE PROTECTED ZONE]
    C --> G[CIRCUIT BREAKER TRIP COMMAND]
    D --> G
    E --> G
```

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

### Question 1: Define Distance Protection. List the three operating zones of a stepped distance protection scheme with their typical reach and time settings.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Distance protection is a non-unit scheme in which the relay's operation depends on the impedance $Z = V/I$ measured at the relaying point. A fault close to the relay results in a small apparent impedance; a distant fault results in a large apparent impedance.

The three zones are:

| Zone | Reach | Time Delay |
|---|---|---|
| Zone 1 (Primary) | 80–90 % of protected line | Instantaneous (≈ 30 ms) |
| Zone 2 (Primary backup) | 120–150 % of protected line (covers next line partially) | 0.3 – 0.5 s |
| Zone 3 (Remote backup) | Covers next full line + 25 % of line beyond | 0.6 – 1.0 s |

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Question 2: Differentiate between Impedance, Reactance, and Mho relays using their R–X characteristics.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Impedance Relay | Reactance Relay | Mho Relay |
|---|---|---|---|
| Characteristic | Circle centred at origin | Straight line $X = X_{set}$ | Circle through origin |
| Directional | No (non-directional) | No | Yes (self-directional) |
| Sensitive to $R_f$ | Yes | No | Yes (moderately) |
| Application | Sub-transmission | Short heavily-loaded lines | EHV transmission |
| Comparator quantity | $\vert V \vert \ge k \vert I \vert$ | $V\sin\phi \ge kI$ | $V\cos(\theta - \phi) \ge kI$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Model Question Paper]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **three-step distance protection scheme**. Discuss the reach and time coordination of each zone, and state why Zone 1 is set to 80–90 % rather than 100 %.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 220 kV double-circuit line is protected by a numerical distance relay. The line impedances are $Z_{L1} = 30\,\Omega$ (protected line, 80 km), $Z_{L2} = 40\,\Omega$ (next line, 110 km), and $Z_{L3} = 25\,\Omega$ (next-next line, 70 km). Determine the **Zone 1, Zone 2, and Zone 3 reach settings** and the **corresponding time delays** using standard KTU practice.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

A three-step distance scheme is implemented by configuring three concentric, graded zones in the R–X plane.

1. **Zone 1** — Primary, instantaneous. Reach = 80–90 % of $Z_{L1}$. Coverage stops short of the remote bus to avoid over-reaching faults on the next line.

2. **Zone 2** — Primary backup, time-delayed (0.3–0.5 s). Reach covers 100 % of $Z_{L1}$ and 40–50 % of $Z_{L2}$. Acts as backup for Zone 1 of the next line.

3. **Zone 3** — Remote backup (0.6–1.0 s). Reach covers $Z_{L1} + Z_{L2} + 0.25\,Z_{L3}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not 100 % in Zone 1?**
> - CT/VT errors introduce ≈ 5 % uncertainty.
> - Fault arc resistance adds to $R_{app}$.
> - Relay overshoot during CVT transients.
> - Mutual coupling on parallel circuits.
> A 10–20 % safety margin (i.e., 80–90 %) prevents mis-tripping for faults just beyond the remote bus.

#### Model Solution to (b)

Step 1: Zone 1 reach (80–90 % of $Z_{L1}$):

$$Z_1 = 0.85 \times Z_{L1} = 0.85 \times 30 = 25.5\;\Omega$$

Step 2: Zone 2 reach (100 % of $Z_{L1}$ + 50 % of $Z_{L2}$):

$$Z_2 = Z_{L1} + 0.5 \times Z_{L2} = 30 + 0.5 \times 40 = 50\;\Omega$$

Step 3: Zone 3 reach (100 % of $Z_{L1}$ + 100 % of $Z_{L2}$ + 25 % of $Z_{L3}$):

$$Z_3 = Z_{L1} + Z_{L2} + 0.25 \times Z_{L3} = 30 + 40 + 0.25 \times 25 = 76.25\;\Omega$$

Step 4: Time delays per standard practice:

- $t_1 = 0.03\,s$ (instantaneous, ≈ 30 ms)
- $t_2 = 0.4\,s$
- $t_3 = 0.8\,s$

Step 5: Verify reach ratios:

$$\frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = \frac{50}{25.5} \approx 1.96 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Higher than 1.5; clamp to 1.5}\;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Z_2 = 1.5 \times 25.5 = 38.25\;\Omega$$

$$\frac{Z_3}{Z_2} = \frac{76.25}{50} \approx 1.53 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Acceptable}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often quote the *theoretical* Zone 2 reach as 1.5 × $Z_{L1}$ without checking the **clamping** rule. In real relays (and KTU 14-mark answers), the setting is the **smaller** of (a) the calculated reach and (b) 1.5 × $Z_{L1}$. Failure to apply the clamp is the **single most common 2-mark loss** in this question.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with R–X diagrams the operating principles of **(i)** Impedance relay, **(ii)** Mho relay, and **(iii)** Quadrilateral relay. State one application of each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 400 kV line has a positive sequence impedance of $0.04 + j0.4\;\Omega/\text{km}$. The line is 250 km long. Compute the **Zone 1, Zone 2, and Zone 3 reach settings in ohms and in percent** if the relay is at the sending end. The next line has impedance $0.04 + j0.35\;\Omega/\text{km}$ × 180 km.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**(i) Impedance Relay**

$$\vert Z_{app}\vert \le Z_{set}$$

Circle of radius $Z_{set}$ centred at the origin. **Non-directional** → an extra directional relay is required. Application: short sub-transmission feeders where fault current magnitude matters more than direction.

**(ii) Mho (Admittance) Relay**

$$\left|\,Z_{app} - \dfrac{Z_{set}}{2}\,\right| \le \dfrac{Z_{set}}{2}$$

Circle **through the origin** with diameter along the line impedance. **Self-directional** — operates only for forward faults. Application: most common for 220 kV / 400 kV / 765 kV EHV lines.

**(iii) Quadrilateral Relay**

- Reactance reach: $X \le X_{set}$ (top boundary)
- Resistance reach: $R \le R_{set}$ (right boundary)
- Two directional lines (left and bottom)
Application: **long EHV lines, series-compensated lines**, where arc resistance $R_f$ must be tolerated.

#### Model Solution to (b)

Step 1: Per-km line impedance $Z_{L1}$:

$$Z_{L1} = (0.04 + j0.4) \times 250 = 10 + j100\;\Omega$$

Step 2: Impedance magnitude of protected line:

$$\vert Z_{L1}\vert = \sqrt{10^{2} + 100^{2}} = \sqrt{10100} \approx 100.5\;\Omega$$

Step 3: Next-line impedance magnitude:

$$Z_{L2} = 0.04 + j0.35 \times 180 = 7.2 + j63\;\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \vert Z_{L2}\vert \approx 63.4\;\Omega$$

Step 4: Zone reaches:

- $Z_1 = 0.85 \times 100.5 \approx 85.4\;\Omega \;\;(\approx 85\%)$
- $Z_2 = 100.5 + 0.5 \times 63.4 = 132.2\;\Omega \;\;(\approx 132\%)$
- $Z_3 = 100.5 + 63.4 + 0.25 \times Z_{L3} \;\;(\text{assume } Z_{L3} = 60\;\Omega) = 178.9\;\Omega$

Step 5: Time settings per standard practice: $t_1 = 0.03\,s$, $t_2 = 0.4\,s$, $t_3 = 0.8\,s$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students sometimes use **per-unit** reach without converting back to ohms, or they confuse the **line impedance per km** with the **total line impedance**. Always show: $Z_{total} = z_{per\,km} \times \ell_{km}$. This is a 2-mark deduction zone in valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Schemes of Distance Protection"**

- [x] Distance protection is a **non-unit scheme** based on measured impedance $Z_{app} = V/I$.
- [x] **Zone 1:** 80–90 % of protected line, instantaneous, **primary** protection.
- [x] **Zone 2:** ≈ 120–150 % reach, 0.3–0.5 s delay, **primary backup**.
- [x] **Zone 3:** covers adjacent line + 25 % beyond, 0.6–1.0 s, **remote backup**.
- [x] Zone 1 is *not* 100 % because of **CT/VT errors, arc resistance, and CVT transients**.
- [x] **Impedance relay:** circle at origin, non-directional.
- [x] **Reactance relay:** straight line, immune to $R_f$.
- [x] **Mho relay:** circle through origin, self-directional, most popular for EHV.
- [x] **Quadrilateral:** top + right + directional boundaries, used on long/series-compensated lines.
- [x] **Over-reach:** apparent impedance smaller than actual; under-reach: larger than actual.
- [x] **Infeed correction** at a T-junction bus: $Z_{adj} = Z_{set} \times (1 + I_{inf}/I_{relay})$.
- [x] **Power Swing Blocking (PSB):** outer blinders in the R-X plane, ≈ 50 ms delay, prevents Zone 3 mis-trip during stable swings.
- [x] **Time grading** follows the cardinal rule: *smaller reach ⇒ smaller time* (inverse time–reach relationship).
- [x] Numerical relays compute $Z_{app}$ via **DFT on sampled V and I**, then check against all three zones in parallel every cycle.
- [x] Standard KTU reach ratios: $Z_2/Z_1 \le 1.5$ and $Z_3/Z_2 \le 1.5$ (with **clamping** when exceeded).
- [x] **Mho circle** centre = $Z_{set}/2$, radius = $Z_{set}/2$, located on the line-impedance phasor.
- [x] **Mho comparator** equation: $V\cos(\theta - \phi) \ge kI$, the **cosine comparator**.
- [x] For 14-mark KTU questions, always show: (i) given data, (ii) reach computation, (iii) clamping, (iv) time grading, and (v) final setting table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Line Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential Line Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Differential Line Protection** is a unit protection scheme in which currents entering and leaving a protected transmission line section are compared using current transformers (CTs) at both ends. Under normal operating conditions and for external faults, the sum of currents (taking directional sign convention into account) flowing into the protected zone equals zero. For internal faults, this balance is disturbed, and the difference (spill current) operates the relay.

Mathematically, the operating criterion is expressed as:

$$I_{op} = \vert \vec{I_1} + \vec{I_2} \vert > I_{pickup}$$

$$I_{restraint} = \vert \vec{I_1} \vert + \vert \vec{I_2} \vert$$

where $\vec{I_1}$ and $\vec{I_2}$ are the phasor currents measured at the two terminals of the protected line section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Differential protection offers **absolute discrimination**, meaning it operates only for faults *within* the protected zone without requiring time grading with adjacent protection. This is the gold standard of selectivity in modern power system protection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highway toll booth system** on a controlled-access motorway:
- Vehicles enter through **Booth A** (Terminal 1) and exit through **Booth B** (Terminal 2).
- During normal traffic, the number of vehicles entering equals the number leaving. The **net count = 0**.
- If a **crash occurs on the highway between the booths** (an *internal fault*), some vehicles are "lost" in the accident. The exit count will be less than the entry count. The net count becomes non-zero, and an **alarm is raised instantly** to block further traffic (trip the circuit breaker).
- If a **crash occurs outside the controlled segment** (an *external fault*), the count remains balanced because no vehicles are lost within the protected zone — even though traffic flow is disrupted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Differential Line Protection:**
> - **Standard Bias Coefficient ($K_1$):** Typically **30%** for transmission line applications (to handle high through-fault currents).
> - **Standard Bias Coefficient ($K_2$):** Typically **60%–80%** to prevent CT saturation misoperation during extreme external faults.
> - **Operating Current ($I_{op}$):** Usually set between **0.3 to 0.5 pu** of the rated line current.
> - **Communication Channel Latency:** Must be less than **5 ms** for proper operation of numerical differential schemes.

### 1.3 Why Differential Protection for Lines?

Unlike transformers, generators, and busbars where Merz-Price protection works well due to short physical distances between CTs, **transmission lines pose unique challenges**:

1. **Long physical length** (from a few km to hundreds of km) between CT locations.
2. **Significant charging current** ($I_c$) due to the line's shunt capacitance.
3. **CT performance mismatch** at two remote substations.
4. **Communication link requirements** for exchanging current samples between line ends.

These challenges gave rise to sophisticated schemes such as **Percentage Differential Relays**, **Biased Differential Relays**, and modern **Numerical/Phasor-Based Differential Line Protection**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Merz-Price Differential Protection — Operating Principle

The classical **Merz-Price circulating current scheme** is the basis of all differential protection. Consider a transmission line with CTs at both ends:

- For an **external (through) fault**: Current enters at end 1, leaves at end 2. Both CTs carry the same current, so the secondary currents circulate in the pilot loop, and the differential relay sees essentially zero spill current.

$$I_{diff} = I_1^{sec} - I_2^{sec} \approx 0 \quad \text{(external fault)}$$

- For an **internal fault**: Current contributions flow *into* the protected zone from both ends. The CT secondary currents are no longer equal in the circulating loop — they add up and flow through the relay operating coil.

$$I_{diff} = I_1^{sec} + I_2^{sec} > I_{pickup} \quad \text{(internal fault)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of Simple (Unbiased) Merz-Price Scheme for Lines:**
> If one CT saturates unequally during a heavy external fault, a large **false spill current** appears in the relay coil, causing **maloperation**. This is the primary reason percentage/biased differential relays were developed.

### 2.2 Percentage (Biased) Differential Relay

To overcome CT saturation issues, a **restraint (bias) winding** is added in addition to the operating winding. The relay characteristic becomes:

$$I_{op} \geq K \cdot I_{restraint} + I_{min}$$

where $K$ is the bias slope (percentage bias).

### 2.3 The Biased Characteristic Curve

The relay operating boundary is divided into **two regions**:

| Region | Slope | Purpose |
|--------|-------|---------|
| **Region 1 (Low current)** | $K_1$ — typically **30%** | Sensitive to internal faults at low current levels |
| **Region 2 (High current)** | $K_2$ — typically **60%–80%** | Stable during CT saturation caused by heavy through-faults |

The operating condition becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{op} &\geq K_1 \cdot I_{restraint} \quad \text{for } I_{restraint} < I_{th} \\
I_{op} &\geq K_2 \cdot I_{restraint} \quad \text{for } I_{restraint} \geq I_{th}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $I_{th}$ is the **bias threshold current** (typically 1.0–1.5 pu).

### 2.4 Charging Current Compensation

For long EHV/UHV transmission lines, the **distributed capacitance** of the line produces a charging current $I_c$ that flows into the line at each end even under normal conditions. This charging current appears as a false differential current.

Two compensation methods are used:

**Method 1: Phasor Subtraction**

$$I_{diff}^{comp} = (I_1 - I_{c1}) + (I_2 - I_{c2})$$

**Method 2: Distributed Parameter Model (Beta Compensation)**

The current at any point $x$ along a lossless line is:

$$I(x) = I_1 \cos(\beta x) - j \frac{V_1}{Z_c} \sin(\beta x)$$

where $\beta = \omega \sqrt{LC}$ is the phase constant and $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$ is the surge impedance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Differential line protection with proper charging current compensation is used on **765 kV and 1200 kV EHV lines** in India (e.g., Powergrid's Champa-Kurukshetra HVDC lines and Raigarh-Pugalur UHVAC lines), where distributed capacitance effects are dominant.

### 2.5 Alpha Plane (Complex Plane) Analysis

A powerful modern visualization tool represents the complex ratio of currents at the two line ends:

$$\vec{I_R} = \frac{\vec{I_1}}{\vec{I_2}} = \frac{\vert I_1 \vert}{\vert I_2 \vert} \angle (\phi)$$

In the **alpha plane**, the operating characteristics appear as:

| Condition | Alpha Plane Location |
|-----------|---------------------|
| **No fault / Healthy** | $\alpha = 1 \angle 0°$ (point at 1,0) |
| **External fault** | $\alpha = 1 \angle 180°$ (point at -1,0) |
| **Internal fault (source dominant at end 1)** | $\alpha$ near $(1, 0)$ with small angle shift |
| **Internal fault (source dominant at end 2)** | $\alpha$ near $(1, 0)$ with angle near $0°$ |

> [!TIP]
> The **alpha plane** is a favorite KTU 2024 examiner question topic because it elegantly unifies all the operating principles of numerical differential line protection in a single complex-plane graph.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $I_{diff} = \vec{I_1} + \vec{I_2}$ | Differential (spill) current | A (primary) |
| 2 | $I_{bias} = \frac{\vert \vec{I_1} \vert + \vert \vec{I_2} \vert}{2}$ | Average biasing current | A (primary) |
| 3 | $I_{op} \geq K_1 \cdot I_{bias}$ | Operating criterion (low current) | A |
| 4 | $I_{op} \geq K_2 \cdot I_{bias} + C$ | Operating criterion (high current with offset $C$) | A |
| 5 | $I_c = \omega C V$ | Total line charging current | A |
| 6 | $I(x) = I_1 \cos(\beta x) - j \frac{V_1}{Z_c} \sin(\beta x)$ | Distributed line current profile | A |
| 7 | $V(x) = V_1 \cos(\beta x) - j I_1 Z_c \sin(\beta x)$ | Distributed line voltage profile | V |
| 8 | $Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$ | Surge (characteristic) impedance | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | $\beta = \omega \sqrt{LC}$ | Phase constant | rad/km |
| 10 | $\alpha = \frac{\vec{I_1}}{\vec{I_2}}$ | Alpha-plane current ratio | complex |
| 11 | $I_{th} = 1.0$–$1.5 \text{ pu}$ | Bias threshold (typical) | pu |
| 12 | $K_1 = 0.3$, $K_2 = 0.6$–$0.8$ | Typical bias slopes | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipeline Pilot Channel Latency:** Differential line protection requires **synchronized current sampling** at both ends. The synchronization error should be less than **$\pm 1$ ms**. Communication media used include **fiber-optic pilot cables**, **PLCC**, and **IEC 61850-9-2 SV (Sampled Values) over Ethernet**.

### 2.7 Classification of Differential Line Protection Schemes

```
Differential Line Protection
├── 1. Pilot Wire Differential (Classical)
│       ├── Merz-Price Circulating Current
│       └── Opposed Voltage (Beveridge) Scheme
├── 2. Biased Differential (Percentage)
│       ├── Single Bias Slope
│       └── Dual Bias Slope (K1-K2)
├── 3. Charging Current Compensated
│       ├── Phasor Subtraction
│       └── Distributed Parameter (Beta)
└── 4. Numerical / Digital Differential
        ├── Alpha Plane Method
        ├── Travelling Wave Differential
        └── Phasor-based with GPS Sync
```

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Operating Current for a Simple Merz-Price Scheme

Consider a transmission line section with CTs at both ends. Let:
- $I_1$ = primary current entering at end 1
- $I_2$ = primary current leaving at end 2
- $n$ = CT turns ratio
- $I_1^{sec} = I_1 / n$, $I_2^{sec} = I_2 / n$

**Step 1:** Define the secondary circulating current loop.

In the pilot loop, the two CT secondaries are connected in opposition. Under a healthy (no-fault) condition, the currents circulate without entering the relay operating coil.

**Step 2:** Apply KCL at the relay node.

The current through the relay operating coil is:

$$I_{relay} = I_1^{sec} - I_2^{sec} = \frac{I_1 - I_2}{n}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute external fault condition.

For an external fault, $I_1 = I_2 = I_f$ (current flows through):

$$I_{relay} = \frac{I_f - I_f}{n} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Substitute internal fault condition.

For an internal fault at point F, current $I_{fa}$ flows in from end 1 and $I_{fb}$ flows in from end 2 (both directions into the fault):

$$I_{relay} = \frac{I_{fa} + I_{fb}}{n} = \frac{I_{f,total}}{n}$$

This is a **large value** that operates the relay.

### 3.2 Derivation of Charging Current Compensation Using Distributed Model

For a long transmission line, the distributed line model is governed by the telegrapher's equations. The exact current at any distance $x$ from end 1 is:

$$I(x) = I_1 \cos(\beta x) - j \frac{V_1}{Z_c} \sin(\beta x)$$

**Step 1:** Evaluate the current at the remote end ($x = L$, where $L$ is the line length):

$$I(L) = I_1 \cos(\beta L) - j \frac{V_1}{Z_c} \sin(\beta L)$$

**Step 2:** This must equal $-I_2$ (current leaving the line at end 2 is the negative of current flowing *into* the line from end 2's source):

$$-I_2 = I_1 \cos(\beta L) - j \frac{V_1}{Z_c} \sin(\beta L)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $V_1$ in terms of $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$V_1 = \frac{j Z_c (I_1 \cos(\beta L) + I_2)}{\sin(\beta L)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the current at the *midpoint* of the line ($x = L/2$):

$$I(L/2) = I_1 \cos(\beta L/2) - j \frac{V_1}{Z_c} \sin(\beta L/2)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $V_1$ from Step 3:

$$I(L/2) = I_1 \cos(\beta L/2) - j \frac{\sin(\beta L/2)}{Z_c} \cdot \frac{j Z_c (I_1 \cos(\beta L) + I_2)}{\sin(\beta L)}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify using $j \cdot j = -1$:

$$I(L/2) = I_1 \cos(\beta L/2) + \frac{\sin(\beta L/2)}{\sin(\beta L)} (I_1 \cos(\beta L) + I_2)$$

**Step 7:** Group terms with $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$I(L/2) = I_1 \left[ \cos(\beta L/2) + \frac{\cos(\beta L) \sin(\beta L/2)}{\sin(\beta L)} \right] + I_2 \frac{\sin(\beta L/2)}{\sin(\beta L)}$$

**Step 8:** Apply the trigonometric identity:

$$\frac{\cos(\beta L) \sin(\beta L/2) + \sin(\beta L/2) \cos(0)}{\sin(\beta L)} = \frac{\sin(\beta L/2) \cdot 2 \cos(\beta L/2)}{\sin(\beta L)}$$

Using $\sin(2\theta) = 2 \sin\theta \cos\theta$ and $\sin(2\theta) = 2 \sin\theta \cos\theta$:

$$I(L/2) = I_1 \cdot \frac{2 \sin(\beta L/2) \cos(\beta L/2)}{\sin(\beta L)} + I_2 \frac{\sin(\beta L/2)}{\sin(\beta L)}$$

**Step 9:** Substitute $\sin(\beta L) = 2 \sin(\beta L/2) \cos(\beta L/2)$:

$$I(L/2) = I_1 + I_2 \cdot \frac{1}{2 \cos(\beta L/2)}$$

**Step 10:** Rearrange to obtain the **mid-point current**:

$$\boxed{I_{mid} = I_1 + \frac{I_2}{2 \cos(\beta L/2)}}$$

This result is critical: the current at any point on the line is a **weighted linear combination** of the terminal currents. The **beta compensation factor** $\frac{1}{2 \cos(\beta L/2)}$ corrects the apparent differential current by accounting for distributed capacitance.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A 200 km, 400 kV transmission line has the following parameters:
- Positive sequence impedance: $Z_1 = 0.03 + j0.32$ $\Omega$/km
- Positive sequence admittance: $Y_1 = j3.5 \times 10^{-6}$ S/km
- A single-line-to-ground fault occurs at the midpoint, fed by sources at both ends:
  - $I_1 = 3000 \angle -30°$ A
  - $I_2 = 2000 \angle -150°$ A
- Pickup current: $I_{pickup} = 500$ A
- Bias slope: $K_1 = 0.3$, $K_2 = 0.6$

**Find:** Does the biased differential relay operate?

**Step 1:** Compute the differential current.

$$I_{diff} = \vert \vec{I_1} + \vec{I_2} \vert$$

Convert to rectangular form:
$$I_1 = 3000(\cos(-30°) + j \sin(-30°)) = 2598.08 - j1500 \text{ A}$$
$$I_2 = 2000(\cos(-150°) + j \sin(-150°)) = -1732.05 - j1000 \text{ A}$$

Sum:
$$I_1 + I_2 = 866.03 - j2500 \text{ A}$$

Magnitude:
$$I_{diff} = \sqrt{866.03^2 + 2500^2} = \sqrt{750{,}008 + 6{,}250{,}000} = \sqrt{7{,}000{,}008}$$
$$I_{diff} \approx 2645.75 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the bias (restraint) current.

$$I_{bias} = \frac{\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert}{2} = \frac{3000 + 2000}{2} = 2500 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Check operating criterion with bias $K_1 = 0.3$ (assuming $I_{bias} < I_{th}$).

$$I_{op,min} = K_1 \cdot I_{bias} = 0.3 \times 2500 = 750 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4:** Compare $I_{diff}$ with $I_{op,min}$.

$$I_{diff} = 2645.75 \text{ A} \gg I_{op,min} = 750 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5:** Conclude.

$$\boxed{\text{Since } I_{diff} > I_{op,min}, \text{ the relay WILL OPERATE (Trip command issued).}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Differential Schemes

| Scheme | Sensitivity | CT Saturation Stability | Charging Current Handling | Communication Required | Cost |
|--------|-------------|------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------|------|
| **Simple Merz-Price** | Very High | **Poor** | Poor | Yes | Low |
| **Single-Bias % Diff** | High | Good | Poor | Yes | Moderate |
| **Dual-Bias % Diff** | High | **Very Good** | Moderate | Yes | Moderate |
| **Phasor Subtraction (Charging Comp)** | High | Good | **Good** | Yes | High |
| **Distributed Parameter (Beta) Comp** | **Very High** | Very Good | **Excellent** | Yes | High |
| **Alpha-Plane Numerical** | Very High | Excellent | Excellent | Yes (GPS sync) | High |
| **Travelling Wave Diff** | **Ultra High** | Excellent | **Ultra Fast** | Yes (sub-ms) | Very High |

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP["Alpha Plane Complex Diagram"]
```

### 4.3 CT Saturation Detection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Receive CT Secondary Current I sec"] --> B["Sample at 80 Samples per Cycle"]
    B --> C{"Differential Current Exceeds Bias Characteristic?"}
    C -- No --> D["No Trip Action"]
    C -- Yes --> E["Run CT Saturation Detection Algorithm"]
    E --> F{"Second Derivative of I sec Exceeds Threshold AND Duration less than 2 ms?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["FLAG CT Saturation Detected"]
    F -- No --> H["Genuine Internal Fault"]
    G --> I["Inhibit Trip for 2 Cycles"]
    H --> J["Issue Trip Command"]
    I --> K["Reassess After 2 Cycles"]
    K --> C
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

### 4.5 Sequence of Operations During Internal vs External Fault

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant END1 as Line End 1
    participant COMM as Pilot Communication
    participant END2 as Line End 2
    END1->>COMM: Transmit phasor I1 with timestamp
    END2->>COMM: Transmit phasor I2 with timestamp
    COMM->>END1: Receive I2
    COMM->>END2: Receive I1
    Note over END1,END2: Calculate Idiff and Ibias locally
    alt Internal Fault
        END1->>END1: Idiff exceeds bias curve
        END2->>END2: Idiff exceeds bias curve
        END1->>END1: TRIP Breaker End 1
        END2->>END2: TRIP Breaker End 2
    else External Fault
        END1->>END1: Idiff within restraint
        END2->>END2: Idiff within restraint
        END1->>END1: NO TRIP
        END2->>END2: NO TRIP
    end
```
