## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Explain the principle of Merz-Price differential protection as applied to a transmission line. Why is it preferred for EHV lines?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Merz-Price protection uses CTs at both ends of the protected line. The secondaries are interconnected such that under normal conditions, currents circulate in the pilot loop and the relay sees zero spill current. For an internal fault, currents from both ends add up at the relay, producing a large differential current that trips the breakers.

For EHV lines, it provides **unit protection** — absolute discrimination without time delay, which is essential for system stability on 220 kV/400 kV/765 kV networks. (3 marks: 1 mark principle + 1 mark circuit description + 1 mark EHV advantage)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Define "bias" in a percentage differential relay. What is the typical value of bias slope for transmission line applications?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bias is a stabilizing technique where a **restraint winding** in the relay is energized by the *through-current*, making the relay less sensitive at higher fault levels. This prevents maloperation due to **CT saturation** during external faults.

Typical bias slope:
- $K_1 = 0.3$ (30%) for low-current region.
- $K_2 = 0.6$ to $0.8$ (60–80%) for high-current region. (3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark function + 1 mark typical values)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **dual-slope percentage biased differential protection scheme** for a transmission line. Derive the operating equation and discuss how the bias slopes $K_1$ and $K_2$ are selected. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 km, 220 kV line has a positive sequence series impedance of $0.1 + j0.4$ $\Omega$/km and a shunt admittance of $j2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ S/km. For a three-phase fault at 60% of the line length from end 1, the following currents are measured:
- $I_1 = 4000 \angle -60°$ A
- $I_2 = 1500 \angle -120°$ A

The relay has $I_{pickup} = 400$ A, $K_1 = 0.3$, $K_2 = 0.6$, and $I_{th} = 1.2$ pu (base = 2000 A). Determine if the relay operates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dual-slope biased differential scheme (7 marks):**

Schematic: A single-line diagram showing CTs at both ends, biased relay with operating coil, two restraint coils, and pilot link. (2 marks for diagram)

Operating equations:
$$I_{op} = \vert \vec{I_1} + \vec{I_2} \vert$$
$$I_{bias} = \frac{\vert \vec{I_1} \vert + \vert \vec{I_2} \vert}{2}$$

For $I_{bias} < I_{th}$:
$$I_{op} \geq K_1 \cdot I_{bias}$$

For $I_{bias} \geq I_{th}$:
$$I_{op} \geq K_2 \cdot I_{bias} + C \quad \text{(2 marks for equations)}$$

**Selection of $K_1$ and $K_2$ (3 marks):**
- $K_1$ is set low (0.3) to provide high sensitivity for low-current internal faults.
- $K_2$ is set high (0.6–0.8) to provide security against CT saturation during heavy external faults.
- The crossover threshold $I_{th}$ is typically 1.0–1.5 pu of rated current.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert currents to rectangular form (1 mark):**

$$I_1 = 4000(\cos(-60°) + j\sin(-60°)) = 2000 - j3464.1 \text{ A}$$
$$I_2 = 1500(\cos(-120°) + j\sin(-120°)) = -750 - j1299.0 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $I_{diff}$ (1 mark):**

$$I_{diff} = I_1 + I_2 = (2000 - 750) + j(-3464.1 - 1299.0)$$
$$I_{diff} = 1250 - j4763.1 \text{ A}$$

$$|I_{diff}| = \sqrt{1250^2 + 4763.1^2} = \sqrt{1{,}562{,}500 + 22{,}687{,}121} = \sqrt{24{,}249{,}621}$$
$$|I_{diff}| \approx 4924.4 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $I_{bias}$ (1 mark):**

$$I_{bias} = \frac{|I_1| + |I_2|}{2} = \frac{4000 + 1500}{2} = 2750 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with $I_{th}$ (1 mark):**

Base current = 2000 A, so $I_{th} = 1.2 \times 2000 = 2400$ A.
Since $I_{bias} = 2750 \text{ A} > 2400 \text{ A}$, we use the **$K_2$ slope**.

**Step 5 — Compute required operating current (1 mark):**

$$I_{op,min} = K_2 \cdot I_{bias} = 0.6 \times 2750 = 1650 \text{ A}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion (2 marks):**

$$I_{diff} = 4924.4 \text{ A} \gg I_{op,min} = 1650 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{\text{The relay WILL OPERATE — Trip command is issued.}}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **alpha-plane method** of differential line protection. Sketch the alpha-plane characteristic and identify the regions corresponding to healthy, external fault, and internal fault conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a block diagram the **numerical differential protection of a transmission line with charging current compensation using the distributed parameter model**. Derive the expression for the compensation factor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Alpha-plane method (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The alpha-plane method defines a complex ratio:

$$\vec{\alpha} = \frac{\vec{I_1}}{\vec{I_2}}$$

where $\vec{I_1}$ and $\vec{I_2}$ are the local and remote phasor currents. Each complex $\alpha$ value is plotted on a 2-D complex plane.

**Characteristic regions (3 marks):**
- **Healthy condition:** $\alpha = 1 \angle 0°$ → point at $(1, 0)$.
- **External fault:** $I_1 = -I_2$ → $\alpha = -1 \angle 0°$ → point at $(-1, 0)$.
- **Internal fault:** Depending on source impedances, $\alpha$ lies near $(1, 0)$ but rotated; if source at end 1 dominates, $\alpha$ is near $(1, 0)$ small angle.

**Operating logic (2 marks):** The relay operates when $\alpha$ enters a defined "operating region" enclosing $(1, 0)$ and excluding $(-1, 0)$. The shape (rectangle, lens, or circle) defines selectivity and security.

**Sketch (0 marks — verbal description):** A complex plane showing point $(1, 0)$ circled by an operating region, and point $(-1, 0)$ inside a restraint region.

**(b) Distributed parameter compensation (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):** CTs → ADC → Phasor extraction → Distributed parameter model → Current profile computation → Corrected differential current → Operating criterion.

**Derivation of compensation factor (4 marks):**

The current at any point $x$ along a lossless line of length $L$:

$$I(x) = I_1 \cos(\beta x) - j \frac{V_1}{Z_c} \sin(\beta x)$$

where $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ and $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$.

Eliminating $V_1$ using the boundary condition $I(L) = -I_2$:

$$V_1 = \frac{j Z_c}{\sin(\beta L)} (I_1 \cos(\beta L) + I_2)$$

Substituting back:

$$I(x) = I_1 \left[ \cos(\beta x) + \frac{\cos(\beta L) \sin(\beta x)}{\sin(\beta L)} \right] + I_2 \cdot \frac{\sin(\beta x)}{\sin(\beta L)}$$

Using $\sin(A+B)$ identity and $\sin(\beta L) = 2\sin(\beta L/2)\cos(\beta L/2)$:

$$I(x) = I_1 \cdot \frac{\sin(\beta(L - x) + \beta x)}{\sin(\beta L)} + I_2 \cdot \frac{\sin(\beta x)}{\sin(\beta L)}$$

The **compensation factor** at any point $x$ is therefore:

$$\boxed{k(x) = \frac{\sin(\beta x)}{\sin(\beta L)}}$$

For compensation to the line midpoint, $k(L/2) = \frac{\sin(\beta L/2)}{\sin(\beta L)} = \frac{1}{2\cos(\beta L/2)}$.

The relay uses the corrected differential current:

$$I_{diff}^{comp} = I_1 + I_2 \cdot \frac{1}{2\cos(\beta L/2)}$$

This cancels the **steady-state charging current** that would otherwise appear as a false spill current.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Many students forget that $I_2$ enters the line with **opposite sign** to $I_1$. Use $I_{diff} = I_1 + I_2$ only with the correct directional sign convention; otherwise, you get the false impression that $I_{diff} = 0$ for *internal* faults.
> 2. **Forgetting $\sqrt{3}$ in line currents:** Use **phase currents**, not line currents, in the differential equation.
> 3. **Mixing up bias slopes:** Always check whether $I_{bias} < I_{th}$ or $I_{bias} \geq I_{th}$ before choosing $K_1$ or $K_2$.
> 4. **Forgetting charging current compensation:** On long EHV lines (>100 km), neglecting $I_c$ gives a **false trip** — lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Rectangular vs. polar conversions:** When computing $|I_1 + I_2|$, convert phasors to **rectangular form first**, then add, then take magnitude. Many students incorrectly add magnitudes.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers:** Always include units (A, pu, $\Omega$) — KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 7. **Sketching mistakes in alpha plane:** Forgetting to label the healthy point $(1, 0)$ and external fault point $(-1, 0)$ — instant 2-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Differential protection is a unit protection** — operates only for faults inside the protected zone.
- **Merz-Price scheme** is the foundation; it uses CTs at both line ends in a circulating-current configuration.
- **Unbiased Merz-Price** is **unstable** for transmission lines due to **CT saturation** during external faults.
- **Percentage (biased) differential relays** introduce a **restraint winding** energized by through-current.
- **Dual-slope characteristic** uses $K_1 \approx 0.3$ for low current and $K_2 \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ for high current.
- **Charging current** of long EHV lines must be compensated; use **phasor subtraction** or **distributed parameter (beta)** model.
- **Beta compensation factor** at line midpoint: $k = \frac{1}{2\cos(\beta L/2)}$.
- **Alpha-plane method** plots the complex ratio $\alpha = I_1 / I_2$; healthy → $(1, 0)$, external fault → $(-1, 0)$.
- **Communication channel** (fiber-optic, PLCC, IEC 61850 SV) is **essential** for exchanging current samples.
- **GPS time synchronization** (±1 μs or better) is mandatory for numerical differential schemes.
- **CT saturation detection** algorithms (e.g., second-derivative, pattern recognition) are required to prevent **external-fault maloperation**.
- **Travelling-wave-based differential protection** offers **sub-cycle (≤1 ms)** operating time — emerging future technology.
- **KTU 2024 most-likely 14-mark question type:** Numerical on biased characteristic with internal/external fault discrimination, plus a 7-mark derivation of compensation factor or alpha-plane characteristic.
- **Key trap:** An external fault with unequal CT saturation produces a *false differential current* — the **bias** is what saves the relay.
- **Standard test values to memorize:** $K_1 = 0.3$, $K_2 = 0.6$–$0.8$, $I_{th} = 1.0$–$1.5$ pu, $I_{pickup} = 0.3$–$0.5$ pu.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Comparison Line Protection. Protection of Bus-Bar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phase Comparison Line Protection. Protection of Bus-Bar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — PHASE COMPARISON LINE PROTECTION & BUS-BAR PROTECTION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Phase Comparison Line Protection — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Comparison Protection** is a *unit-type* (absolute selectivity) protection technique used for transmission lines, EHV cables, and transformer feeders. It operates on the principle of **comparing the phase angle between the currents entering and leaving the protected zone** at the two remote terminals, conveyed through a communication (pilot) channel. The relay issues a trip command when the phase displacement between the local-end current $I_L$ and the remote-end current $I_R$ falls within a predetermined *operating window* (typically $0° \pm 60°$).

The scheme is fundamentally a **pilot protection** technique in which the discriminator quantity is the **phase relationship** of the two end-currents, not their magnitude. It belongs to the family of *teleprotection schemes* and is widely classified as either **Block-Comparison (BC)** or **Phase-Comparison (PC)** depending on the granularity of the comparison.

### 1.2 Bus-Bar Protection — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bus-Bar Protection** refers to the coordinated relay schemes employed to detect and isolate internal short-circuit faults occurring on the bus-bar itself, which is the common node interconnecting multiple incoming and outgoing circuits. The primary scheme is **Current-Differential Protection**, realised in two dominant forms: **(a) High-Impedance (Circulating-Current) Differential Protection** and **(b) Biased (Low-Impedance) Differential Protection**, supplemented by a *check feature* to prevent maloperation due to CT saturation during external faults.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase Comparison Analogy — "The Two Lifeguards"**
> Imagine a river with two lifeguards stationed at the upstream and downstream ends of a swimming zone. Each guard shines a torch along the river in the direction of the water flow. During *normal swimming*, water flows **in one direction** — so the upstream torch points downstream (let's say "12 o'clock") and the downstream torch points upstream ("6 o'clock"). The torches are **opposite** (180° apart).
> When a swimmer (fault) gets into trouble *inside* the zone, water rushes **into** the trouble spot **from both ends**. Both guards now shine their torches **towards the swimmer** — both pointing inward — so the torches become **aligned** (in phase, 0° apart). The moment the guards' torch beams align, they trigger the alarm. That alignment is the trip signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bus-Bar Analogy — "The Junction Check"**
> Picture a railway junction where many trains converge. A "supervisor" continuously adds up the **tokens (current)** arriving from every incoming track and subtracts the tokens leaving on every outgoing track. Under normal operation, tokens in = tokens out (Kirchhoff's law holds). If a train derails *at the junction* (a fault), tokens vanish from the system into the ground — so **tokens in ≠ tokens out**, and the supervisor sounds the alarm. That imbalance is what a differential relay detects on a bus-bar.

### 1.4 Physical & Standard Metrics

- Operating phase window: **$\pm 60°$** (typical), **$\pm 40°$** (stability preferred)
- Through-current stability: 180° ± 60° (blocking band)
- Communication channel delay allowance: **< 5 ms** for EHV lines
- Bias slope of biased differential: **30%–60%**
- Stabilising resistor in high-impedance scheme: **few hundred ohms to 2 k$\Omega$**
- CT knee-point voltage: at least **2 × (relay setting + lead burden)**

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of Phase Comparison

The currents sampled at the local end ($S$) and remote end ($R$) of a protected line are:

$$I_S(t) = \hat{I}_S \sin(\omega t + \phi_S)$$
$$I_R(t) = \hat{I}_R \sin(\omega t + \phi_R)$$

The relay forms the *angular displacement*:

$$\Delta \phi = \phi_S - \phi_R$$

The decision logic is:

- **Internal fault** — current flows **into** the fault from both ends. Both $I_S$ and $I_R$ point *toward* the fault, so their phasors are essentially **in phase**: $\Delta \phi \approx 0°$.
- **External fault / healthy / load** — current enters one end and exits the other. The two phasors are in **anti-phase**: $\Delta \phi \approx 180°$.

The relay trips when $\vert \Delta \phi \vert < 60°$ (operating band) and blocks when $120° < \vert \Delta \phi \vert < 240°$ (blocking band).

### 2.2 Comparison of the Two Discriminator Variants

| Feature | Block Comparison (BC) | Phase Comparison (PC) |
|---|---|---|
| Discriminator | Time-overlap of current pulses | Continuous phase angle |
| Sensitivity | Lower (coarse) | Higher (precise) |
| CT saturation effect | Tolerable | Must be guarded |
| Channel bandwidth | Narrow (telegraphy ok) | Wide (analogue) |
| Typical application | Sub-transmission / HV | EHV / series-compensated lines |

### 2.3 Bus-Bar Protection — Operating Principle

For a bus with $n$ circuits, define the **operating current** (spill / differential current) and **restraining current**:

$$I_{op} = \left\vert \sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k \right\vert$$
$$I_{res} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert I_k \vert$$

For the **Biased Differential** scheme, the trip condition is:

$$I_{op} \ge I_{s} + K \cdot I_{res}$$

where $I_{s}$ is the minimum pickup (typically 0.2–0.3 pu) and $K$ is the bias slope (0.3 to 0.6). For the **High-Impedance** scheme, the relay voltage $V_r$ must exceed the stabilising voltage:

$$V_{r} = I_{f} \cdot (R_{s} + R_{l})$$

The stabilising resistor $R_{s}$ is sized so that for the maximum external-fault CT spill current $I_{f}$, the voltage remains below the relay voltage setting $V_{s}$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all equations, discriminators, and design constraints a student must memorise for KTU University Examination — ESE Module 2.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Condition | Units / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phase difference (internal fault) | $\Delta\phi \in [-60°, +60°]$ | degrees | Trip criterion |
| 2 | Phase difference (external / load) | $\Delta\phi \in [120°, 240°]$ | degrees | Block criterion |
| 3 | Operating current (bus) | $I_{op} = \vert \sum I_k \vert$ | A | Differential spill |
| 4 | Restraining current (bus) | $I_{res} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum \vert I_k \vert$ | A | Bias quantity |
| 5 | Biased trip condition | $I_{op} \ge I_{s} + K \cdot I_{res}$ | A | Trip characteristic |
| 6 | Bias slope | $K = 0.3$ to $0.6$ | per unit | Stability |
| 7 | Stabilising voltage (Hi-Z) | $V_{s} = I_{f}(R_{s} + R_{l})$ | V | External fault stability |
| 8 | Stabilising resistor | $R_{s} = V_{s}/I_{f} - R_{l}$ | $\Omega$ | Design equation |
| 9 | CT kneepoint requirement | $V_{k} \ge 2 V_{s}$ | V | CT specification |
| 10 | Channel delay budget | $t_{ch} < 5$ ms | ms | Teleprotection |
| 11 | Through current stability | $\Delta\phi \approx 180°$ | degrees | Healthy state |
| 12 | Operating band | $\pm 60°$ | degrees | Default relay setting |
| 13 | Blocking band | $120°$–$240°$ | degrees | Default blocking |
| 14 | Backup zone (distance) | Zone 2 of line relays | — | Local back-up |
| 15 | Check feature current | $I_{chk} \ge 0.1 I_{rated}$ | A | Discriminator arm |

> All values shown with no vertical pipes inside cells to prevent markdown table breakage (use $\vert$ instead of \vert inside text columns).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Phase comparison** is the de-facto protection for **series-compensated lines**, **HVDC links**, and **long EHV/UHV overhead lines** where distance relays struggle with non-linear impedance trajectories.
- **Bus-bar protection** is mandatory in any **400 kV / 765 kV** substation as bus faults, although rare, are catastrophic — the entire substation can be lost within 80 ms if not cleared.
- Modern numerical relays (e.g., **GE T60, ABB REL670, Siemens SIPROTEC 7SA86**) implement both schemes as firmware modules, with IEC 61850 **GSE / Sampled-Values (SV)** communication replacing copper pilots.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Operating Current of a Biased Differential Bus-Bar Relay

**Step 1 — Define the circuit.** Consider a bus-bar with three feeders (A, B, C). Define current convention: currents entering the protected zone are positive.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL to the protected zone (Kirchhoff's Current Law).** For a healthy bus or an external fault, the algebraic sum of currents entering equals those leaving, hence:

$$\sum_{k} I_k = I_A + I_B + I_C = 0$$

So the operating current $I_{op}$ becomes zero in the ideal case.

**Step 3 — Compute the spill under CT mismatch.** Because CTs have ratio and phase errors, a small spill $I_{sp}$ appears even for through-faults. With CT ratio error $\varepsilon$:

$$I_{op} = \varepsilon \cdot I_{through}$$

**Step 4 — Define the restraining quantity.** To prevent maloperation on heavy through-faults, form:

$$I_{res} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left( \vert I_A \vert + \vert I_B \vert + \vert I_C \vert \right)$$

This grows linearly with through-fault magnitude, providing a *self-balancing* restraint.

**Step 5 — Form the biased characteristic.** The relay operates only when the operating quantity exceeds a fixed pickup $I_{s}$ plus a slope-proportional restraint:

$$I_{op} \ge I_{s} + K \cdot I_{res}$$

$$\varepsilon \cdot I_{through} \ge I_{s} + K \cdot I_{through}$$

Rearranging for the maximum through-fault current $I_{f,\max}$ the relay will *not* operate on:

$$I_{f,\max} \le \frac{I_{s}}{\varepsilon - K}$$

This is the design constraint: the bias slope $K$ must exceed the worst-case CT error $\varepsilon$, otherwise the relay will trip on through-faults.

### 3.2 Derivation: Stabilising Resistor for High-Impedance Bus-Bar Protection

**Step 1 — Equivalent circuit.** For an external fault on feeder A, CT-A saturates. The remaining CTs deliver secondary currents that flow through the lead resistance $R_{l}$ and the stabilising resistor $R_{s}$ placed across the relay coil.

**Step 2 — Worst-case spill current.** Let $I_{f}$ be the maximum secondary through-fault current. If one CT fully saturates, the entire fault current $I_{f}$ is forced through $R_{s}$.

**Step 3 — Voltage across the relay.** By Ohm's law:

$$V_{r} = I_{f} \cdot (R_{s} + R_{l})$$

**Step 4 — Stability requirement.** The relay must NOT operate, so $V_{r}$ must be less than the relay voltage setting $V_{s}$:

$$I_{f} \cdot (R_{s} + R_{l}) \le V_{s}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_{s} \le \dfrac{V_{s}}{I_{f}} - R_{l}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity check.** For an internal fault producing minimum internal current $I_{int}$, the relay must operate:

$$I_{int} \cdot R_{s} \ge V_{s}$$

This gives the lower bound for $R_{s}$, and the two inequalities are reconciled during design.

### 3.3 Derivation: Phase-Comparison Phase-Angle Decision Boundary

**Step 1 — Phasor setup.** Let $I_S = I_S \angle 0°$ and $I_R = I_R \angle \phi$. The relay measures $\phi$ modulo $360°$.

**Step 2 — Internal fault criterion.** Both currents flow *into* the fault, so they are nearly in phase:

$$\phi_{int} \approx 0° \quad \text{(mod 360°)}$$

**Step 3 — External fault / load criterion.** Current enters one end and exits the other — the relay sees the currents as if they are referenced to opposite polarities, producing near anti-phase:

$$\phi_{ext} \approx 180° \quad \text{(mod 360°)}$$

**Step 4 — Trip / block window.** Define a half-window $\theta_{w}$. Trip if:

$$\vert \phi \vert < \theta_{w} \quad \lor \quad \vert \phi - 360° \vert < \theta_{w}$$

Block if:

$$\vert \phi - 180° \vert < \theta_{w}$$

A typical choice is $\theta_{w} = 60°$, giving the **operating band of $\pm 60°$ around 0°** and the **blocking band of $\pm 60°$ around 180°**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Application Cases

| Case | Local-end current direction | Remote-end current direction | Net $\Delta\phi$ | Relay action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Healthy load | Into line | Out of line | $\approx 180°$ | Block |
| External fault near $S$ | Out of $S$ | Into $R$ | $\approx 180°$ | Block |
| External fault near $R$ | Into $S$ | Out of $R$ | $\approx 0°$ (rare) | Block (secure via timer) |
| Internal fault | Into fault | Into fault | $\approx 0°$ | **Trip** |
| CT saturation (internal) | Distorted | Into fault | Erratic | Trip (with anti-sat) |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Phase-Comparison Line Protection — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    busL[Source Bus L] --> ctS1[CT at End S]
    ctS1 --> relayS[Local Relay S]
    relayS --> breakerS[CB at End S]
    breakerS --> lineA[Protected Line]
    lineA --> breakerR[CB at End R]
    breakerR --> relayR[Remote Relay R]
    relayR --> ctR1[CT at End R]
    ctR1 --> busR[Load Bus R]
    relayS -- PLC / Fiber Pilot Channel --> relayR
    relayS -- Trip Coil --> breakerS
    relayR -- Trip Coil --> breakerR
    style relayS fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style relayR fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style lineA fill:#7fbfff,color:#000
    style ctS1 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style ctR1 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style breakerS fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style breakerR fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Phase-Comparison Decision Logic — Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Is and Ir at both ends] --> B[Transmit via pilot channel]
    B --> C[Compute phase angle delta phi]
    C --> D{Is delta phi within +/- 60 deg}
    D -- Yes --> E[Internal fault suspected]
    E --> F{Check feature OK and overcurrent}
    F -- Yes --> G[TRIP both breakers]
    F -- No --> H[Alarm / restrain]
    D -- No --> I[External or healthy]
    I --> J[BLOCK trip signal]
    style D fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style G fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style J fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Biased Differential Bus-Bar Protection — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fa[Feeder A] --> cta[CT A]
    fb[Feeder B] --> ctb[CT B]
    fc[Feeder C] --> ctc[CT C]
    fd[Feeder D] --> ctd[CT D]
    cta --> sum[ Summation Node ]
    ctb --> sum
    ctc --> sum
    ctd --> sum
    sum --> bias[ Biased Differential Element ]
    bias --> trip[ Trip Bus ]
    cta -.-> bias
    ctb -.-> bias
    ctc -.-> bias
    ctd -.-> bias
    chk[Check Zone CT] --> trip
    trip --> b1[CB A]
    trip --> b2[CB B]
    trip --> b3[CB C]
    trip --> b4[CB D]
    style bias fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style sum fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style trip fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style chk fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 High-Impedance Bus-Bar Protection — Stabilising Resistor Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1[CT of Feeder 1] --> r1[Lead Rl1]
    c2[CT of Feeder 2] --> r2[Lead Rl2]
    c3[CT of Feeder 3] --> r3[Lead Rl3]
    c4[CT of Feeder 4] --> r4[Lead Rl4]
    r1 --> node1[Common Neutral]
    r2 --> node1
    r3 --> node1
    r4 --> node1
    node1 --> rs[Stabilising Resistor Rs]
    rs --> relay[Voltage-Sensitive Relay]
    node1 --- meter[Over-Voltage Element]
    style rs fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style relay fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style meter fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the operating principle of a phase comparison relay? Mention the typical operating and blocking phase windows.*

**Model Answer:**
A phase comparison relay compares the *phase angle* between the currents at the two ends of a protected line, transmitted via a pilot channel. The relay operates (trips) when the phase displacement is small — typically within $\pm 60°$ of $0°$ — indicating an internal fault. It blocks (restrains) when the displacement is around $180°$ (within $\pm 60°$ of $180°$), indicating healthy load or external fault. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between high-impedance and biased (low-impedance) differential bus-bar protection schemes.*

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | High-Impedance | Biased (Low-Z) |
|---|---|---|
| Stabilisation | Stabilising resistor $R_s$ | Bias winding on the relay |
| CT requirement | Identical ratio, low knee-point | Identical ratio, low leakage |
| Spill handling | Voltage-clamped | Slope-controlled |
| Setting | Voltage setting $V_s$ | Current setting $I_s$ + slope $K$ |
| Sensitivity | Lower | Higher |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### ❓ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat block schematic, explain the working of a **phase comparison protection scheme** for an EHV transmission line. Discuss the operating and blocking phase-angle windows. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 kV, 100 km line is protected by phase comparison. The CTs at both ends have ratio error 2%. The communication channel introduces a delay of 3 ms. Justify whether this scheme is suitable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**(a) Working of phase comparison scheme (7 marks):**

1. **CTs at both ends** ($S$ and $R$) step down the line currents $I_S$ and $I_R$ to relaying levels. *(1 mark)*
2. The local relay **samples** $I_S$ and converts it into a square-wave (block pulses) using a zero-crossing detector. *(1 mark)*
3. The remote-end waveform is transmitted to the local end via **pilot channel** (PLC / fibre / microwave). *(1 mark)*
4. The local relay performs an **AND-gate comparison** (or an exclusive-OR based phase-angle measurement). *(1 mark)*
5. The relay measures the **overlap time** of the two square pulses, which is proportional to $\cos(\Delta\phi)$. *(1 mark)*
6. Decision logic:
   - $\Delta\phi \in \pm 60°$ (around 0°): **TRIP**
   - $\Delta\phi \in \pm 60°$ (around 180°): **BLOCK** *(2 marks)*

**Schematic (reproduced in the answer sheet):** Show the block diagram from §4.1.

---

**(b) Suitability analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — CT error analysis.** The ratio error of 2% introduces a *magnitude* mismatch, not a phase error. For pure phase-comparison (not amplitude-comparison), this magnitude error is *irrelevant* as long as the CTs are not saturated. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Channel delay.** At 50 Hz, the period is $T = 20$ ms. A 3 ms delay corresponds to a phase shift of:

$$\Delta\phi_{ch} = \frac{3 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} \times 360° = 54°$$

**Step 3 — Effect on operating window.** The operating window is $\pm 60°$. A channel-induced shift of 54° consumes most of the margin. The available margin becomes only $6°$, which is *insufficient* for security during transients, CT saturation, and line-charging currents. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Compensation.** A **dedicated phase-shift equaliser** must be inserted in the receiving end, calibrated to subtract 54°. With compensation, the scheme becomes suitable. *(1 mark)*

**Conclusion:** Without compensation, the scheme is **not recommended** for this line; with phase-shift compensation, it is fully suitable. **(2 marks for verdict + engineering recommendation)**

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Describe the **biased (low-impedance) differential protection** of a bus-bar. Derive the operating equation and explain the role of the bias winding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-feeder bus-bar has through-fault current 20 kA. CTs have ratio error 4% and lead resistance 0.5 $\Omega$ each. The relay voltage setting is 100 V. Design the stabilising resistor for a high-impedance scheme and verify the CT knee-point voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**(a) Biased differential protection (7 marks):**

1. **Topology:** All CT secondaries are paralleled, with their secondaries connected in opposition. The *operating* coil senses the algebraic sum, while the *restraining* coils sense $\tfrac{1}{2}\sum \vert I_k \vert$. *(2 marks)*
2. **Operating current:**

$$I_{op} = \left\vert I_A + I_B + I_C + I_D \right\vert$$

3. **Restraining current:**

$$I_{res} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\vert I_A \vert + \vert I_B \vert + \vert I_C \vert + \vert I_D \vert \right)$$

4. **Trip condition:**

$$I_{op} \ge I_{s} + K \cdot I_{res}$$

5. **Role of bias winding:** During heavy through-faults, $I_{res}$ grows large, demanding a higher $I_{op}$ to trip. This **prevents maloperation** due to CT saturation or ratio errors. The characteristic is a *straight line* with slope $K$ on the $I_{op}$–$I_{res}$ plane, intersecting the $I_{op}$-axis at $I_s$. *(3 marks)*
6. **Selection of K:** $K$ must exceed the worst-case CT ratio error (typically 5–10%) to guarantee stability; $K = 0.4$ to $0.6$ is common. *(1 mark)*

**Characteristic diagram:** The student should draw the biased characteristic with operating region above the line and restraining region below.

---

**(b) Design of stabilising resistor (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Through-fault current $I_f = 20$ kA (primary)
- Assume CT ratio = 2000/5 = 400
- Lead resistance $R_l = 0.5\ \Omega$ per feeder (assume all 4 equal)
- Relay voltage setting $V_s = 100$ V
- CT ratio error $\varepsilon = 4\%$

**Step 1 — Secondary through-fault current:**

$$I_{f,sec} = \frac{20{,}000}{400} = 50\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case spill (one CT fully saturated):**

$$I_{spill} = 50\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Stabilising resistor formula:**

$$R_{s} = \frac{V_{s}}{I_{spill}} - R_{l} = \frac{100}{50} - 0.5 = 2 - 0.5 = 1.5\ \Omega$$

*(2 marks for formula, 1 mark for arithmetic)*

**Step 4 — Voltage check during internal fault.** For a minimum internal fault of, say, 10% of rated:

$$I_{int,sec} = 0.1 \times 50 = 5\ \text{A}$$

$$V_{int} = I_{int,sec} \times R_{s} = 5 \times 1.5 = 7.5\ \text{V}$$

Since $7.5\ \text{V} < 100\ \text{V}$, the relay will **not** operate on this internal fault. *The design must be revised with a lower $R_s$* or a non-linear (Metrosil) resistor in parallel. *(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Re-design with sensitivity constraint.** To make the relay operate on 5 A internal current with $V_s = 100$ V:

$$R_{s,\,min} = \frac{V_{s}}{I_{int,sec}} = \frac{100}{5} = 20\ \Omega$$

The two bounds $R_s \in [1.5, 20]\ \Omega$ allow us to pick $R_s = 10\ \Omega$ as a compromise, possibly with a Metrosil (non-linear resistor) for over-voltage protection. *(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — CT knee-point voltage check.** The CT knee-point voltage $V_k$ must be at least twice the maximum voltage that may appear across the CT secondary (i.e., $V_s$):

$$V_{k} \ge 2 \times 100 = 200\ \text{V}$$

So the CTs must be specified with $V_k \ge 200$ V. *(1 mark)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation**
> 1. **Confusing "Phase Comparison" with "Differential Protection"** — phase comparison uses *phase angle* as the discriminator; differential uses *magnitude sum*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the operating/blocking phase windows** explicitly on the phase axis — a must-do for 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Mixing up the operating band ($\pm 60°$ around 0°) and the blocking band ($\pm 60°$ around 180°)**. A common slip is writing "operating band $\pm 60°$ around 180°" — full mark loss on the discriminator.
> 4. **Not justifying the choice of bias slope $K$** in bus-bar protection. The answer must state $K > \varepsilon$ (worst CT error).
> 5. **Skipping the CT knee-point voltage check** in high-impedance design — it is mandatory and frequently asked.
> 6. **Forgetting the channel delay compensation** in phase-comparison suitability analysis.
> 7. **Omitting units** in numerical problems (Volts, Amperes, Ohms). KTU examiners strictly deduct ½ mark for each missing unit.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2**

- ✅ **Phase comparison** = unit protection using **phase-angle comparison** of currents at the two line ends.
- ✅ Communication channels: **pilot wires, PLC, microwave, fibre-optic, IEC 61850 GSE**.
- ✅ **Operating band:** $\Delta\phi \in \pm 60°$ (around 0°) → **TRIP**.
- ✅ **Blocking band:** $\Delta\phi \in \pm 60°$ (around 180°) → **BLOCK**.
- ✅ Internal fault → both end currents flow **into** fault → in-phase.
- ✅ External fault / load → one current in, one out → anti-phase.
- ✅ **Bus-bar protection** = differential (high-Z or biased).
- ✅ **High-impedance:** uses stabilising resistor $R_s = V_s/I_f - R_l$.
- ✅ **Biased (low-Z):** uses bias slope $K$ where $K > \varepsilon$ (CT error).
- ✅ **Operating current** $I_{op} = \vert \sum I_k \vert$.
- ✅ **Restraining current** $I_{res} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum \vert I_k \vert$.
- ✅ **Trip rule:** $I_{op} \ge I_s + K \cdot I_{res}$.
- ✅ **CT knee-point voltage** must satisfy $V_k \ge 2 V_s$.
- ✅ **Check feature (check zone)** is mandatory in modern numerical bus-bar schemes.
- ✅ **Backup:** local back-up = Zone 2 of line distance relays; remote back-up = Zone 3.
- ✅ For **series-compensated** and **HVDC** lines, phase comparison is preferred over distance.
- ✅ Always compensate for **channel delay** to maintain the operating window margin.
- ✅ Always specify **units** in numerical answers (KTU strict rule).
- ✅ Draw **schematic + characteristic curve** for every 7-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformer and Generator and Motor: Types of Faults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transformer and Generator and Motor: Types of Faults

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Equipment Faults in Power Systems

In any power system, the three most expensive and critical rotating/static assets are the **transformer**, the **synchronous generator (alternator)**, and the **induction/synchronous motor**. Each of these devices, by virtue of its construction, insulation, and operating stress, is susceptible to a unique family of internal and external faults. **Digital (numerical) protection** relies on correctly identifying *which type of fault* is present so that the appropriate relay algorithm (differential, overcurrent, distance, etc.) can be applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Equipment faults in transformers, generators, and motors are abnormal conditions—electrical, mechanical, or thermal—that deviate the equipment from its normal operating state, potentially leading to insulation breakdown, winding damage, fire, or catastrophic failure. Their classification forms the basis for selecting the correct numerical relay algorithm."

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Patient Triage" Concept

Imagine a hospital emergency ward. Before any treatment begins, doctors **triage** patients into categories — cardiac, trauma, respiratory, infectious. Each category needs a *specific* treatment protocol. Similarly, a digital relay must first identify the **type of fault** (electrical, mechanical, thermal) and its **location** (internal vs. external) before issuing a trip command.

- A **transformer** is like the *heart* of the system — circulating energy between voltage levels. Any fault is life-threatening.
- A **generator** is the *engine* — converting mechanical to electrical energy. Mechanical imbalances manifest as electrical signatures.
- A **motor** is the *muscle* — converting electrical back to mechanical energy. Mechanical load issues (locked rotor, overload) appear electrically.

| Equipment | "Organ System" | Most Common "Disease" |
|---|---|---|
| Transformer | Heart (circulatory) | Insulation deterioration → winding short |
| Generator | Brain + Lungs (generation) | Stator inter-turn, loss of excitation |
| Motor | Muscle (actuator) | Stator winding short, bearing failure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why classify faults?** Because a single numerical relay cannot optimally protect against *all* fault types. Classification allows relay engineers to **coordinate** differential, overcurrent, and distance elements with proper pickup values and time delays.

---

## 1.3 Standard Classification Framework

Faults across all three equipment types can be broadly classified into:

1. **Electrical Faults** — Insulation failure leading to short circuits or open circuits.
2. **Mechanical Faults** — Rotor, shaft, bearing, or core deformation.
3. **Thermal Faults** — Overheating due to overload, cooling failure, or hot spots.
4. **Abnormal Operating Conditions (AOC)** — Not strictly faults, but require protection (e.g., over-excitation, loss of synchronism).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often confuse *faults* with *abnormal operating conditions*. Remember — a **fault** is a *physical* insulation/connection failure; an **AOC** is a *parameter* deviation (voltage, frequency, excitation) that *may* lead to a fault if not cleared.

---

## 1.4 Transformer Faults — Overview

A power transformer is a static device, so its faults are predominantly **electrical** and **thermal** in nature. Mechanical faults (e.g., winding displacement due to short-circuit forces) are usually *consequences* of electrical faults.

| S.No. | Fault Type | Frequency of Occurrence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phase-to-Ground (Winding-to-Earth) | **~70%** (most common) |
| 2 | Phase-to-Phase (between windings) | ~15% |
| 3 | Turn-to-Turn (inter-turn) | ~10% (most damaging if undetected) |
| 4 | Three-Phase (symmetrical) | ~5% (rarest but most severe) |
| 5 | Inter-winding (HV–LV) | Special case |

---

## 1.5 Generator Faults — Overview

Generators have both **electrical** and **mechanical** fault modes due to the rotating field. They are also subject to system-side disturbances.

| S.No. | Fault Category | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stator (Armature) Faults | Phase-to-ground, phase-to-phase, inter-turn |
| 2 | Rotor (Field) Faults | Field winding short, earth fault, open circuit |
| 3 | Abnormal Operating Conditions | Loss of excitation, loss of synchronism, over/under voltage, over/under frequency, overcurrent |
| 4 | Mechanical Faults | Bearing failure, vibration, overspeed |

---

## 1.6 Motor Faults — Overview

Motors (typically induction motors in industry) experience a mix of stator, rotor, and bearing-related issues. Induction motor stator faults are similar to generator stator faults but occur under **starting** and **varying load** conditions.

| S.No. | Fault Category | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stator Faults | Phase-to-ground, phase-to-phase, inter-turn |
| 2 | Rotor (Cage) Faults | Broken rotor bar, end-ring fracture, rotor eccentricity |
| 3 | Bearing Faults | Inner/outer race defect, ball defect, lubrication failure |
| 4 | Abnormal Operating Conditions | Locked rotor, overload, single-phasing, under-voltage |
| 5 | Shaft / Mechanical | Bent shaft, air-gap eccentricity |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** Remember the **"S-R-B-A"** mnemonic for rotating machines — **S**tator, **R**otor, **B**earing, **A**bnormal conditions.

---

## 1.7 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU

- It appears in **Module 2** which carries significant weightage in Part B questions.
- Questions typically ask students to *"List and explain the types of faults in a transformer/generator/motor"* (14-mark full question).
- Numerical relay **algorithm selection** (discussed in later modules) is *entirely* based on this classification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Transformer Faults

### 2.1.1 Electrical Faults (Internal)

**(a) Phase-to-Ground Fault (Winding-to-Earth)**
- Most common transformer fault (~70% of all faults).
- Occurs when insulation between a phase winding and the grounded tank/core fails.
- Causes: Lightning surge, insulation aging, oil contamination, moisture.
- Detection: **Restricted Earth Fault (REF)** relay, **Differential** relay.
- Severity: **High** — generates high fault currents (limited by neutral impedance and system impedance).

**(b) Phase-to-Phase Fault**
- Insulation failure between two phase windings of the **same** voltage level.
- Causes: Severe overvoltage, contamination, foreign object intrusion.
- Detection: **Differential protection (Biased or High-Impedance)**.
- Severity: **Very High** — fault current limited only by leakage impedance.

**(c) Turn-to-Turn Fault (Inter-Turn Short)**
- Insulation failure between **adjacent turns** of the same winding.
- Most insidious fault — small fault current, hard to detect.
- Causes: Insulation aging, thermal cycling, mechanical stress from short circuits.
- Detection: **Buchholz relay** (for oil-immersed), **Differential relay** (only if >5% of turns shorted), **Dissolved Gas Analysis (DGA)** offline.
- Severity: **Catastrophic if undetected** — escalates to phase-to-ground within minutes.

**(d) Three-Phase Fault (Symmetrical)**
- All three phases shorted together (with or without ground).
- Causes: Severe insulation failure, catastrophic damage.
- Detection: **Differential, Overcurrent, Distance** relays.
- Severity: **Extreme** — highest short-circuit MVA, biggest mechanical stress on windings.

**(e) Inter-Winding Fault (HV-to-LV)**
- Short between primary and secondary windings.
- Causes: Insulation breakdown between HV and LV sections.
- Detection: **Differential relay with through-fault restraint**.
- Severity: **Extreme** — full system voltage appears across the fault.

### 2.1.2 Incipient / Developing Faults

These are **early-stage** faults that develop over time. Numerical relays use **trend monitoring**.

| Fault | Detection Method |
|---|---|
| Partial Discharge (PD) | UHF sensors, acoustic sensors |
| Overheating | Temperature sensors, optical fibers |
| Moisture in oil | DGA, dielectric breakdown voltage test |
| Tap-changer failure | Buchholz relay, oil flow indicator |

### 2.1.3 External Faults (Through-Faults)

Faults **outside** the transformer zone (on connected lines/cables). The transformer must **withstand** the mechanical and thermal stress of carrying the through-fault current for the time the upstream relay takes to clear it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Modern numerical transformer relays use **through-fault monitoring** algorithms to record the I²t accumulation and predict end-of-life insulation degradation.

### 2.1.4 Mechanical Faults

- **Winding displacement** (radial/axial) due to repeated through-fault currents.
- **Core faults** — lamination shorts, core bolt insulation failure → localized heating.
- **Tank damage** — internal arc causes pressure rise → tank rupture.
- **Buchholz relay** detects gas accumulation from arcing.

### 2.1.5 Thermal Faults

- **Overloading** beyond nameplate rating.
- **Cooling system failure** (pump/fan stoppage).
- **Hot spots** in winding due to poor oil circulation.
- Detection: **Winding Temperature Indicator (WTI)**, **Oil Temperature Indicator (OTI)**, numerical thermal overload model.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Generator Faults

### 2.2.1 Stator (Armature) Faults

**(a) Phase-to-Ground Fault (Stator Winding Earth Fault)**
- Most common stator fault (~60–70% of all generator faults).
- Causes: Insulation deterioration, water leak in water-cooled windings, end-winding contamination.
- Detection: **Earth-fault relay (95% stator earth-fault protection using 3rd harmonic)**, **Neutral overvoltage relay (59GN)**.
- Severity: **High** — fault current limited by grounding impedance (typically high-impedance grounding).

**(b) Phase-to-Phase Fault (Stator Winding Short)**
- Short between two phases of the stator winding.
- Detection: **Generator Differential Relay (87G)**.
- Severity: **Very High** — high fault current.

**(c) Inter-Turn Fault (Stator Winding Turn Short)**
- Short between adjacent turns of the **same** phase.
- Hardest to detect — small differential current.
- Detection: **Split-phase differential protection** (used for generators with two parallel windings per phase).
- Severity: **Catastrophic** — can quickly escalate to ground fault.

### 2.2.2 Rotor (Field) Faults

**(a) Rotor Earth Fault (Field Winding Earth Fault)**
- Insulation failure between field winding and rotor body.
- Detection: **DC voltage injection** (since rotor is normally isolated from earth), or **AC injection method**.
- Severity: **Moderate** — but **two simultaneous earth faults cause severe unbalance**.

**(b) Rotor Winding Short (Inter-Turn)**
- Short between turns of the field winding.
- Detection: **Air-gap search coils** (sense rotating flux asymmetry).
- Severity: **Causes vibration, heating, loss of excitation**.

**(c) Field Winding Open Circuit**
- Broken rotor winding → loss of excitation.
- Detection: **Loss of excitation relay (40)**.

### 2.2.3 Abnormal Operating Conditions (AOC) of Generators

This is a **major KTU high-yield area** for generator protection.

| S.No. | AOC | Relay Code | Symptom |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loss of Excitation (LOE) | 40 | Reactive power flows *into* generator |
| 2 | Loss of Synchronism (Out-of-Step) | 78 | Pole-slip, oscillating power |
| 3 | Overcurrent (Stator) | 51 | Sustained overcurrent |
| 4 | Overvoltage (Stator) | 59 | Terminal voltage > 110% |
| 5 | Undervoltage (Stator) | 27 | Terminal voltage < 90% |
| 6 | Overfrequency | 81O | Frequency > nominal |
| 7 | Underfrequency | 81U | Frequency < nominal |
| 8 | Over-excitation (V/Hz) | 24 | V/Hz ratio exceeds limit |
| 9 | Reverse Power | 32 | Power flows *into* prime mover |
| 10 | Inadvertent Energization | 50/27 | Breaker closes on a non-energized machine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "list AOCs of a generator," always mention **at least 6–8** of the above. Simply stating "overcurrent and overvoltage" will earn only partial credit.

### 2.2.4 Mechanical Faults of Generators

- **Rotor earth fault** (covered above).
- **Bearing failure** → vibration → unbalance.
- **Turbine blade failure** → overspeed.
- **Loss of prime mover** → motoring (detected by reverse power relay 32).
- **Coolant leak** (in hydrogen/water-cooled machines).

---

## 2.3 Detailed Classification of Motor Faults

### 2.3.1 Stator Faults (Similar to Generator)

- Phase-to-ground, phase-to-phase, inter-turn.
- Detection: **Differential (87M)** for large motors, **instantaneous overcurrent (50)** for smaller motors.

### 2.3.2 Rotor Faults (Induction Motor Specific)

**(a) Broken Rotor Bar**
- Most common rotor fault in squirrel-cage induction motors.
- Causes: Thermal stress, mechanical fatigue, starting stress, manufacturing defects.
- Detection: **Motor Current Signature Analysis (MCSA)** — sidebands at $f_s \pm 2sf$ in stator current spectrum.
- Severity: **Progressive** — leads to vibration, reduced torque, eventual failure.

**(b) End-Ring Fault**
- Fracture or cracking of rotor end-ring.
- Detection: Same as broken bar (MCSA).

**(c) Rotor Eccentricity**
- Air-gap asymmetry due to bearing wear or shaft bowing.
- Detection: **Vibration analysis**, MCSA (sidebands at $f_s \pm f_r$).
- Severity: **Mechanical** — can cause stator-to-rotor rub.

### 2.3.3 Bearing Faults

- Most common cause of **motor failure in industry (~50% of all motor failures)**.
- Types: Inner race defect, outer race defect, ball/roller defect, cage defect.
- Detection: **Vibration analysis (envelope detection of high-frequency resonance)**, **Motor Current Signature Analysis**, **temperature sensors**.

### 2.3.4 Abnormal Operating Conditions of Motors

| S.No. | AOC | Detection |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Locked Rotor | High current, no rotation (50/51) |
| 2 | Single-Phasing | Negative sequence current (46) |
| 3 | Overload | Thermal overload (49) |
| 4 | Phase Reversal | Phase sequence relay (47) |
| 5 | Under-voltage | Under-voltage relay (27) |
| 6 | Stall Condition | Speed switch + overcurrent |
| 7 | Unbalanced Voltage | Negative sequence overcurrent (46) |
| 8 | Ground Fault | Zero-sequence overcurrent (51N) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-phasing** is one of the most common motor failures. It occurs when one of the three-phase fuses blows or a conductor breaks. A three-phase motor running on single phase will continue to rotate (with reduced torque) and overheat rapidly.

### 2.3.5 Mechanical Faults of Motors

- **Shaft misalignment**, **bent shaft**, **coupling failure**.
- **Air-gap eccentricity**.
- **Foundation looseness**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symmetrical fault current | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V_{prefault}}{Z_{eq}}$ | A | Three-phase fault calculation |
| 2 | Single line-to-ground fault | $I_a^{(1)} = \dfrac{3 \cdot V_{prefault}}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0 + 3Z_f}$ | A | L-G fault |
| 3 | Double line-to-ground fault | $I_a^{(1,1)} = \dfrac{3 \cdot V_{prefault}}{Z_1 + Z_2 \vert\vert (Z_0+3Z_f)}$ | A | L-L-G fault |
| 4 | Negative sequence current (unbalance) | $I_2 = \dfrac{1}{3}(I_a + a^2 I_b + a I_c)$ | A | Single-phasing, voltage unbalance |
| 5 | Broken rotor bar sideband | $f_{sb} = f_s(1 \pm 2s)$ | Hz | MCSA for rotor fault |
| 6 | Rotor slot frequency | $f_{rs} = f_s \cdot \dfrac{R}{p}(1-s)$ | Hz | Rotor slot harmonics |
| 7 | Eccentricity frequency | $f_{ecc} = f_s \pm f_r$ | Hz | Air-gap eccentricity |
| 8 | V/Hz ratio | $\dfrac{V}{Hz} = \dfrac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}$ | pu | Over-excitation limit |
| 9 | Overcurrent multiple | $I/I_{rated}$ | pu | Locked rotor, overload |
| 10 | Differential current | $I_d = \vert \vec{I_1} + \vec{I_2} \vert$ | A | Internal fault detection |
| 11 | Through (bias) current | $I_b = \dfrac{\vert \vec{I_1} \vert + \vert \vec{I_2} \vert}{2}$ | A | Restraint in biased differential |
| 12 | Buchholz gas volume | $V_{gas}$ (mL) | mL | Alarm > 250 mL, Trip > 600 mL |
| 13 | % impedance | $Z\% = \dfrac{Z_{pu} \cdot S_{base}}{V_{base}^2} \cdot 100$ | % | Through-fault current calculation |
| 14 | Thermal limit (overload) | $I^2 t = K$ (adiabatic) | A²s | Fuse, relay heating |
| 15 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}$ | pu | Induction motor rotor analysis |
| 16 | Synchronous speed | $n_s = \dfrac{120 f}{p}$ | rpm | Motor/generator speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always use **pu (per-unit)** values for fault calculations in multi-voltage-level systems. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in written work, **never** the pipe character `|` inside Markdown tables — it breaks the table syntax.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transformer fault data** informs utility insurance premiums and **transformer health indexing (THI)**.
- **Generator fault records** are required by grid codes (e.g., IEEE C37.101, C37.102) for forensic analysis after major disturbances.
- **Motor fault prediction** is a USD 2+ billion market globally — Industry 4.0 factories use **AI-based predictive maintenance** built on the fault signatures described above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Symmetrical Component Analysis of Generator Stator Faults

Symmetrical components are essential for analyzing **unsymmetrical faults** like single line-to-ground in generators. Let us derive the relationship between phase currents and sequence currents.

### Step 1: Define Symmetrical Components

The three sequence components — **positive (1)**, **negative (2)**, and **zero (0)** — are related to phase quantities by the **Fortescue transformation matrix**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} I_a \\ I_b \\ I_c \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & a^2 & a \\ 1 & a & a^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_0 \\ I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where the complex operator is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a = e^{j120°} = -\dfrac{1}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \quad a^2 = e^{j240°} = -\dfrac{1}{2} - j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \quad a^3 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This matrix converts three unbalanced phase quantities into three balanced (symmetrical) sets.

---

### Step 2: Inverse Transformation

The sequence components can be recovered from phase quantities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_0 &= \dfrac{1}{3}(I_a + I_b + I_c) \\
I_1 &= \dfrac{1}{3}(I_a + a I_b + a^2 I_c) \\
I_2 &= \dfrac{1}{3}(I_a + a^2 I_b + a I_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Divide the Fortescue matrix by 3 to obtain its inverse (since $1 + a + a^2 = 0$).

---

### Step 3: Derive Sequence Network for a Single Line-to-Ground (L-G) Fault

Consider Phase A-to-ground fault with fault impedance $Z_f$.

**Boundary conditions:**
- $I_b = 0$
- $I_c = 0$  
- $V_a = I_a \cdot Z_f$

**Step 3a: Compute sequence currents using $I_b = I_c = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_0 &= \dfrac{1}{3}(I_a + 0 + 0) = \dfrac{I_a}{3} \\
I_1 &= \dfrac{1}{3}(I_a + 0 + 0) = \dfrac{I_a}{3} \\
I_2 &= \dfrac{1}{3}(I_a + 0 + 0) = \dfrac{I_a}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** All three sequence currents are equal under L-G fault on Phase A. This is a **signature property** of L-G faults.

**Result:** $I_0 = I_1 = I_2 = \dfrac{I_a}{3}$

---

**Step 3b: Compute sequence voltages:**

The sequence networks are connected in **series** for an L-G fault (since $I_0 = I_1 = I_2$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= E_a - I_1 Z_1 \\
V_2 &= 0 - I_2 Z_2 = -I_2 Z_2 \\
V_0 &= 0 - I_0 Z_0 = -I_0 Z_0
\end{aligned}
$$

where $E_a$ is the pre-fault internal EMF, and $Z_1$, $Z_2$, $Z_0$ are positive, negative, and zero sequence impedances of the generator.

---

**Step 3c: Apply voltage boundary condition $V_a = I_a Z_f$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a &= V_0 + V_1 + V_2 \\
I_a Z_f &= -I_1 Z_0 + (E_a - I_1 Z_1) - I_1 Z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $I_a = 3I_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 I_1 Z_f &= E_a - I_1(Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0) \\
I_1 (Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0 + 3 Z_f) &= E_a \\
I_1 &= \dfrac{E_a}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0 + 3 Z_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3d: Fault current in Phase A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a^{(1)} = 3 I_1 = \dfrac{3 E_a}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0 + 3 Z_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the **standard L-G fault current formula**. For a generator with high-impedance grounding, $Z_0$ is very large, which **limits** the L-G fault current — a deliberate design choice to prevent iron-core damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** For a high-impedance grounded generator, if $Z_0 \to \infty$, then $I_a^{(1)} \to 0$. This is why **100% stator earth-fault protection** is needed (using 3rd harmonic voltage detection at the neutral) — the standard overcurrent relay cannot detect faults near the neutral point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Broken Rotor Bar Sideband Frequency

For a squirrel-cage induction motor, when a rotor bar breaks, the resulting **asymmetry** rotates at slip frequency and modulates the stator current.

### Step 1: Slip Frequency

$$
\begin{aligned}
s = \dfrac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}, \quad f_r = s \cdot f_s
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f_s$ is the supply frequency, $f_r$ is the slip frequency, and $s$ is the slip.

---

### Step 2: Asymmetry Rotation

A broken rotor bar causes a localized asymmetry that rotates at rotor speed $n_r$ in space. From the stator's frame, the asymmetry appears at frequency $f_s - f_r = f_s(1-s)$.

---

### Step 3: Modulation Effect

This rotating asymmetry **modulates** the stator MMF, producing two sidebands around the supply frequency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{sb} = f_s \pm 2sf_s = f_s(1 \pm 2s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor of 2 comes from the fact that the broken bar is a *local* defect (one per revolution), and the stator observes it twice per electrical cycle (forward and backward modulation).

---

### Step 4: Numerical Example

For a 4-pole, 50 Hz motor running at 1440 rpm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_s &= \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm} \\
s &= \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = 0.04 \\
f_{sb} &= 50(1 \pm 2 \times 0.04) = 50(1 \pm 0.08) \\
f_{sb,\text{lower}} &= 50 \times 0.92 = 46 \text{ Hz} \\
f_{sb,\text{upper}} &= 50 \times 1.08 = 54 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note:** In a healthy motor, the sidebands at $f_s(1 \pm 2s)$ are absent or below −60 dB. A broken bar causes them to rise to −35 to −45 dB. MCSA-based relays use **FFT** on the stator current to detect these sidebands.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Buchholz Relay Operation in Transformers

The Buchholz relay is a **gas-actuated** protection device mounted in the pipe between the transformer tank and conservator.

### Step 1: Physical Principle

When an incipient fault (e.g., turn-to-turn short) occurs inside the transformer, the resulting **arcing** decomposes the insulating oil into gases (mainly $H_2$, $CH_4$, $C_2H_2$). These gases rise and collect in the Buchholz relay chamber.

---

### Step 2: Alarm Operation (Slow-Gas Accumulation)

- Small, sustained gas accumulation → upper float drops → **alarm** contact closes.
- Typical alarm threshold: **250 mL** of accumulated gas.

---

### Step 3: Trip Operation (Sudden Oil Surge)

- Severe internal fault → sudden pressure wave → oil surges from tank to conservator → lower float tilts → **trip** contact closes.
- Typical trip time: **< 0.1 second** for fast oil surge.

---

### Step 4: Mathematical Model of Gas Generation Rate

Assuming a constant-energy incipient fault, the gas generation rate can be modeled as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dV_{gas}}{dt} = k \cdot I_{fault}^2 \cdot R_{arc}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k$ is a constant depending on oil composition, $I_{fault}$ is the fault current, and $R_{arc}$ is the arc resistance.

**Conversion logic:** The gas generation rate is proportional to the **arc energy** (Joule heating in the fault region), which is $I^2 R$ per unit time. This explains why severe faults generate gas rapidly (trip), while incipient faults generate gas slowly (alarm).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often think Buchholz detects *all* transformer faults. In reality, Buchholz detects **only** faults that produce gas or oil surge — i.e., **internal** faults below oil level. **Bushing faults** and **cable box faults** are NOT detected.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Fault → Detection Method

| Equipment | Fault Type | Primary Protection | Backup Protection |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transformer | Phase-to-Ground | REF (Restricted Earth Fault) | Differential + Overcurrent |
| Transformer | Phase-to-Phase | Biased Differential (87) | Overcurrent (50/51) |
| Transformer | Turn-to-Turn | Buchholz + DGA + 3rd Harmonic | Differential (if >5% turns) |
| Transformer | Three-Phase | Differential (87) | Overcurrent + Distance |
| Transformer | Tank Fault | Buchholz + Pressure Relief | — |
| Transformer | Overheating | WTI / OTI | — |
| Transformer | Oil Level | Oil Level Indicator (33) | — |
| Generator | Stator Phase-Ground | 3rd Harmonic + Neutral OV (59GN) | Differential (87G) |
| Generator | Stator Phase-Phase | Differential (87G) | Overcurrent (51) |
| Generator | Stator Inter-Turn | Split-Phase Differential | — |
| Generator | Rotor Earth | DC Injection | AC Injection |
| Generator | Loss of Excitation | Impedance Relay (40) | — |
| Generator | Loss of Sync | Out-of-Step (78) | — |
| Generator | Overcurrent | Time OC (51) | — |
| Generator | Over/Under Voltage | 59 / 27 | — |
| Generator | Over/Under Frequency | 81O / 81U | UFLS (load shedding) |
| Generator | Reverse Power | 32 | — |
| Motor | Stator Phase-Ground | Zero-Sequence OC (51N) | Differential (87M) |
| Motor | Phase-to-Phase | Differential (87M) | Instantaneous OC (50) |
| Motor | Locked Rotor | 50 + Speed Switch | Thermal (49) |
| Motor | Single-Phasing | Negative-Sequence (46) | Thermal (49) |
| Motor | Overload | Thermal (49) | — |
| Motor | Broken Rotor Bar | MCSA / Vibration | — |
| Motor | Bearing Fault | Vibration Sensor | Temperature |

