# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any six types of abnormal operating conditions (AOCs) of a synchronous generator and state the corresponding ANSI/IEEE relay code for each.**

#### Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):

| S.No. | Abnormal Condition | ANSI Code |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loss of Excitation | 40 |
| 2 | Loss of Synchronism (Out-of-Step) | 78 |
| 3 | Overcurrent (Stator) | 51 |
| 4 | Overvoltage | 59 |
| 5 | Undervoltage | 27 |
| 6 | Overfrequency | 81O |
| 7 | Underfrequency | 81U |
| 8 | Reverse Power | 32 |
| 9 | Over-excitation (V/Hz) | 24 |

> Any **six** correctly listed AOC–code pairs = 3 marks.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between incipient faults and active faults in a power transformer, giving one example of each.**

#### Model Answer:

| Feature | Incipient Fault | Active Fault |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A slowly developing fault that has not yet caused a major failure | A sudden, large-magnitude fault already causing high current |
| **Development time** | Hours to months | Milliseconds |
| **Example** | Turn-to-turn short, partial discharge, overheating | Phase-to-ground short, three-phase short |
| **Detection** | Buchholz (gas), DGA, thermal sensors, PD sensors | Differential relay, overcurrent relay |
| **Relay action** | Alarm (usually) | Trip (immediate) |

**Example of incipient fault:** Inter-turn short in a transformer winding producing slow gas accumulation in Buchholz relay.

**Example of active fault:** A bolted three-phase short circuit producing currents 10–20× rated, detected by differential relay (87).

(Any valid comparison with one example each = 3 marks.)

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Classify and explain in detail the various types of faults occurring in a power transformer. (7 marks)**

**(b) With neat sketches, explain the construction and operating principle of a Buchholz relay. How does it distinguish between minor and major incipient faults? (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of Transformer Faults** (2 marks for diagram/classification + 5 marks for explanation):

The faults in a power transformer can be broadly classified into:

**(i) Electrical Faults (Internal):**

1. **Phase-to-Ground Fault** (~70% occurrence): Insulation failure between a phase winding and the grounded tank. Detection: REF, Differential.
2. **Phase-to-Phase Fault**: Insulation failure between two phase windings. Detection: Biased Differential.
3. **Turn-to-Turn Fault**: Short between adjacent turns of the same winding. Most insidious — small fault current. Detection: Buchholz, DGA.
4. **Three-Phase Fault** (Symmetrical): All three phases shorted. Most severe but rarest. Detection: Differential, Distance.
5. **Inter-Winding Fault**: Short between HV and LV windings.

**(ii) External (Through) Faults:**
- Faults on connected lines/cables; transformer must carry the through-fault current until cleared by upstream protection.

**(iii) Incipient / Developing Faults:**
- Partial discharge, overheating, tap-changer failure, moisture ingress. Detected by DGA, temperature sensors.

**(iv) Mechanical Faults:**
- Winding displacement due to short-circuit forces, core lamination shorts, tank rupture.

**(v) Thermal Faults:**
- Overloading, cooling system failure, hot spots.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Buchholz Relay:**

A Buchholz relay is a **gas- and oil-surge-actuated** protective device installed in the **interconnecting pipe** between the main tank of an oil-immersed transformer and its **oil conservator**.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Cast iron housing with two floats (upper and lower) on a common spindle.
- Upper float operates a **mercury switch** (alarm contact).
- Lower float operates a **mercury switch** (trip contact).
- A baffle plate is fixed below the lower float to direct oil surge onto the float.
- Inspection window with gas sampling valve on top.

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**

*Case 1: Minor Incipient Fault (e.g., slow turn-to-turn short)*
- The fault arc decomposes the oil into gases ($H_2$, $CH_4$, $C_2H_2$, $C_2H_4$).
- Gases rise and accumulate in the relay chamber, displacing oil.
- As oil level falls, the **upper float tilts** → **alarm** contact closes.
- Typical alarm threshold: **~250 mL** of gas.

*Case 2: Major Internal Fault (e.g., phase-to-ground short)*
- Sudden, violent energy release creates a **pressure wave**.
- Oil surges from tank → conservator through the relay.
- The **surge pushes the lower float** (against baffle) → **trip** contact closes.
- Trip time: **< 100 ms**.

*Case 3: Oil Leakage*
- Slow loss of oil → both floats drop progressively → eventually both alarm and trip operate.

**Distinction between minor and major faults (2 marks):**
- The **upper float** is *gas-operated* (slow, alarm).
- The **lower float** is *surge-operated* (fast, trip).
- Gas accumulation → slow oil displacement → alarm only.
- Oil surge → sudden displacement → immediate trip.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) With the help of symmetrical components, derive the expression for fault current in a single line-to-ground fault on a synchronous generator. State the relevance of high-impedance grounding. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the following abnormal operating conditions of a synchronous generator: (i) Loss of Excitation, (ii) Loss of Synchronism, (iii) Reverse Power. For each, state the relay code, symptom, and detection method. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of L-G Fault Current** (Refer to detailed derivation in Section 3.1):

For a single line-to-ground (L-G) fault on Phase A of a generator with internal EMF $E_a$ and sequence impedances $Z_1, Z_2, Z_0$ and fault impedance $Z_f$:

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $I_b = I_c = 0$
- $V_a = I_a Z_f$

**From symmetrical components** (since $I_b = I_c = 0$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_0 = I_1 = I_2 = \dfrac{I_a}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sequence network connection:** All three sequence networks in **series** (since sequence currents are equal).

**Applying KVL around the series loop:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_a &= I_1(Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0) + 3I_1 Z_f \\
I_1 &= \dfrac{E_a}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0 + 3Z_f} \\
\therefore \ I_a^{(1)} &= \dfrac{3E_a}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0 + 3Z_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Relevance of High-Impedance Grounding (2 marks):**

In a generator, the neutral is grounded through a **high-impedance resistor** or **distribution transformer with a secondary loading resistor**. The high $Z_0$ value **limits the L-G fault current** to a low value (typically 5–10 A), which:
- Prevents iron-core burning at the fault point.
- Allows the generator to **remain in service** for a short time with a single-phase ground fault.
- Permits **selective** detection using sensitive neutral overvoltage (59GN) or 3rd-harmonic schemes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** symmetrical components with *sequence* networks of a *transformer* — they are applied to the generator/system.
> 2. Students often **forget the factor of 3** in $I_a = 3I_1$ — losing 1 mark.
> 3. Many students **omit the fault impedance** $Z_f$ from the denominator — losing 0.5 mark.
> 4. When asked for "relevance of high-impedance grounding," stating *only* "limits fault current" is **incomplete** — also mention iron-core protection and selective detection.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

| AOC | Relay Code | Symptom | Detection Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Loss of Excitation** | 40 | Field winding fails (open circuit, short, or exciter failure). Reactive power flows *into* the generator from the system. Generator accelerates and operates as an induction generator, overheating the rotor. | **Impedance relay** measures the apparent impedance seen from the generator terminals. In the R–X plane, the trajectory moves from the load region into the **negative-reactance / high-reactance** region. Modern relays use **mho + offset-mho** characteristics. |
| **(ii) Loss of Synchronism (Out-of-Step)** | 78 | Generator loses synchronism with the power system (pole-slip). Power swing: δ > 90°; rotor speed oscillates; stator current pulsates. | **Out-of-step relay** detects periodic oscillation of impedance across the R–X plane (lens-shaped or blinder characteristic). The relay counts the number of swings and trips after a preset count to avoid tripping on stable swings. |
| **(iii) Reverse Power** | 32 | Prime mover fails (e.g., loss of steam, water, or fuel). Generator now draws power from the system and *motors*, potentially damaging the prime mover (e.g., turbine blade overheating in a steam turbine). | **Reverse power relay** measures real power flow at the generator terminals. A negative power threshold (typically 0.5–3% of rated) is set. Trip occurs if reverse power persists for >2 s. |

**Explanation of each (2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for table = 7 marks):**

- **Loss of Excitation (2 marks):** When excitation is lost, the internal EMF $E_a$ collapses. The generator draws magnetizing current from the system, operating asynchronously. The stator end-region overheating is severe due to slip-frequency eddy currents.

- **Loss of Synchronism (2 marks):** A pole-slip occurs when the mechanical input power exceeds the maximum electrical transferable power ($P_{max} = EV/X$). The rotor swings relative to the stator field at slip frequency. Detection uses the **rate of change of impedance** or **swing-center voltage** measurement.

- **Reverse Power (2 marks):** A motoring generator (driven by its own losses and system power) can overheat the prime mover — particularly dangerous for steam turbines (blade windage heating). The reverse power setting is deliberately *low* (sometimes 0.1% of rated) for steam-turbine generators.

- **Summary Table (1 mark):** The above table consolidates the three AOCs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "loss of excitation" with "loss of field".** They are the same thing — the field is the excitation. Use consistent terminology.
> 2. **Writing only "out-of-step" without explaining detection.** Mention the *impedance trajectory* or *blinder characteristic*.
> 3. **Omitting the ANSI code.** It is a **mandatory** part of any KTU 2024 answer on protection.
> 4. **Listing only 2 AOCs instead of 6+.** If the question asks for "various AOCs," list at least 6.
> 5. **Forgetting that Buchholz does NOT detect bushing faults or external faults.** Many students incorrectly claim it does.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Transformer Faults** — classified as:
- Electrical (phase-ground, phase-phase, turn-turn, 3-phase, inter-winding)
- Incipient (PD, overheating, tap-changer)
- Mechanical (winding displacement, core fault)
- Thermal (overload, cooling failure)
- External through-faults

✅ **Most common transformer fault** = Phase-to-Ground (~70%).

✅ **Most insidious transformer fault** = Turn-to-Turn (hardest to detect).

✅ **Buchholz relay** detects **only internal** faults producing gas or oil surge. Two floats: upper (alarm, gas), lower (trip, surge).

✅ **Generator Stator Faults** — Phase-ground, phase-phase, inter-turn. Detected by Differential (87G) and 3rd-harmonic neutral OV (59GN).

✅ **Generator Rotor Faults** — Earth fault (DC injection), inter-turn (search coil), open field (loss of excitation 40).

✅ **Generator AOCs (must list at least 6):** LOE (40), Out-of-Step (78), Overcurrent (51), Overvoltage (59), Undervoltage (27), Over/Under Frequency (81), Reverse Power (32), Overexcitation (24).

✅ **High-impedance grounding** in generators limits L-G fault current to 5–10 A → allows 100% stator earth-fault protection using 3rd harmonic.

✅ **Motor Faults** — Stator (similar to generator), Rotor (broken bar, end-ring, eccentricity), Bearing, AOC (locked rotor, single-phasing, overload).

✅ **Most common motor failure cause** = Bearing failure (~50% of all motor failures).

✅ **Single-phasing of motor** = Detected by **negative sequence current** (relay 46).

✅ **Broken rotor bar** = Detected by **sidebands at $f_s(1 \pm 2s)$** in stator current spectrum (MCSA).

✅ **Symmetrical Component Formula for L-G fault**:
$$I_a^{(1)} = \dfrac{3E_a}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_0 + 3Z_f}$$

✅ **Slip Formula**:
$$s = \dfrac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}$$

✅ **Synchronous Speed**:
$$n_s = \dfrac{120f}{p}$$

✅ **Differential Current** (for biased differential):
$$I_d = \vert \vec{I_1} + \vec{I_2} \vert, \quad I_b = \dfrac{\vert \vec{I_1} \vert + \vert \vec{I_2} \vert}{2}$$

✅ **ANSI Relay Codes to Memorize**:
- 27 = Undervoltage
- 32 = Reverse Power
- 40 = Loss of Excitation
- 46 = Negative Sequence
- 49 = Thermal Overload
- 50 = Instantaneous OC
- 51 = Inverse Time OC
- 59 = Overvoltage
- 64 = Earth Fault / REF
- 78 = Out-of-Step
- 81 = Frequency
- 87 = Differential
- 95% stator earth fault = 3rd harmonic + neutral OV

✅ **Mnemonic for Motor Faults**: **S-R-B-A** = **S**tator, **R**otor, **B**earing, **A**bnormal conditions.

✅ **Mnemonic for Generator AOCs**: **L-O-V-U-F-R** = **L**OE, **O**ut-of-step, **O**V/UV, **F**requency, **R**everse power.

✅ **Exam Tip:** Always pair each fault with **(i) Detection Method, (ii) Relay Code, (iii) ANSI Standard** if relevant — this fetches full marks in KTU 2024 scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Protection: High Impedance and Low Impedance Differential Protection Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential Protection: High Impedance and Low Impedance Differential Protection Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Differential Protection** is a unit-type protective scheme based on **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)**, in which the currents entering and leaving a protected zone (e.g., a transformer winding, generator, busbar, or transmission line section) are measured by Current Transformers (CTs) and their phasor difference is continuously evaluated by a relay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**  
> "Differential protection is a method of detecting an in-zone fault by comparing the currents at the two ends of a protected element. The relay operates when the phasor difference (spill current) exceeds a pre-set threshold."

Two principal implementations are used in modern numerical and static relays:

1. **High Impedance Differential Protection (HIDP / Hi-Z Scheme)** — a traditional, robust, predominantly **electromechanical / static** technique.
2. **Low Impedance Differential Protection (LIDP / Lo-Z Scheme)** — a modern **percent / biased differential** technique universally adopted in numerical relays.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "High" and "Low" Impedance?**  
> The classification refers to the **impedance of the voltage-measuring element (the relay branch)** placed across the CT secondary neutral loop, NOT the line CTs themselves. In Hi-Z, the relay is a high-resistance voltage-sensing element; in Lo-Z, the relay is a low-impedance current-summing element.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Tank Flow Monitor"

Imagine a **sealed overhead water tank** with **two pipes** — water enters through Pipe-A and exits through Pipe-B. A digital flow-meter sits on each pipe:

- **Normal condition (No leak):** Meter A reads **+100 L/min**, Meter B reads **−100 L/min**. The algebraic sum is **zero** → no alarm.
- **External burst (Out-of-zone fault):** Both meters read **+100 L/min** because the leak is *downstream* of both meters. The sum is still **zero** → no alarm (the protection must NOT trip).
- **Internal leak (In-zone fault):** Pipe-A reads **+100 L/min** but Pipe-B reads only **+60 L/min** because **40 L/min** is escaping inside the tank. The difference is **40 L/min ≠ 0** → alarm sounds and the inlet valve shuts.

This **flow-difference** is exactly the principle of differential protection. The CT pair is the "flow meter," the relay is the "alarm," and the protected zone is the "tank."

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Differential protection is inherently **unit protection** — its zone of operation is **precisely bounded** by the location of the CTs. It does not require time-grading with adjacent zones and is therefore **fast (typically 15–30 ms)** and **selective by construction**.

### 1.3 The Spill Current — The Decision Variable

The **operating (spill) current** of the differential scheme is defined as:

$$I_{op} = \vert \bar{I_1} + \bar{I_2} \vert$$

where $\bar{I_1}$ and $\bar{I_2}$ are the **phasor** secondary currents from the two end CTs (after polarity normalization).

The **through / restraining current** is:

$$I_{rest} = \frac{\vert \bar{I_1} \vert + \vert \bar{I_2} \vert}{2}$$

A trip command is issued when:

$$I_{op} \geq k \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min}$$

where $k$ is the **bias slope** (a dimensionless constant, typically 20 %–60 %) and $I_{min}$ is the **minimum operate threshold**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Standard Reference Values (used throughout industry):**
> - **CT Knee-Point Voltage (KPV), $V_k$** : The voltage at which a 10 % increase in secondary EMF requires a **50 % increase** in exciting current. Measured in **Volts**.
> - **CT secondary nominal current** : **1 A** or **5 A** (India: predominantly 1 A for EHV).
> - **Standard Stabilizing Resistor Tolerance** : Wire-wound, **±5 %**, **25 W** continuous rating.
> - **Typical Bias Slopes** : 20 %, 30 %, 40 %, 50 %, 60 %, 80 %.
> - **Operate Time** : **15–30 ms** for Hi-Z; **20–40 ms** for Lo-Z at multiple of pickup 2.0.
> - **Recommended Hi-Z Voltage Setting** : $V_s = 2 \cdot V_k$ (factor of safety of 2 over CT knee-point).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generic Differential Protection — Operational Logic Steps

**Step 1 — CT Polarity Normalization (Burden Equivalence):**  
CTs at both ends of the protected zone are connected such that, during an **external (through) fault**, the secondary currents circulate in the loop and produce a **net spill current of zero** in the relay branch. This requires the dot-marked (polarity) terminals to be physically oriented identically.

**Step 2 — Summation Point:**  
The two CT secondary leads and the relay coil share a common **neutral node**. This is the **summation point (∑)**. The relay branch measures either:
- The **voltage** across the summation point (Hi-Z), or
- The **current** flowing into the relay branch from the summation point (Lo-Z).

**Step 3 — Decision Logic:**  
- If the spill quantity (voltage or current) exceeds the threshold → **TRIP**.
- If the spill quantity is below the threshold → **RESTRAIN**.

**Step 4 — Why Faults Cause Spill — Two Effects:**

1. **True differential current (real cause):** During an internal fault, current is injected from the fault point to both ends; the CTs see unequal currents and a real spill is produced.
2. **CT ratio / magnetization mismatch (apparent cause):** Even during a perfectly external fault, mismatched CTs produce a **false spill current** that can cause **misoperation**. Both schemes must counteract this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Engineering Trade-off:**  
> - **Hi-Z** eliminates false spill from CT mismatch by making the relay voltage-dominating — but it cannot handle high internal fault currents without overvoltage.  
> - **Lo-Z** accepts CT mismatch spill but cancels it through **restraining (bias) coils** that scale the operating threshold with through current.

---

### 2.2 High Impedance Differential Protection (HIDP)

#### 2.2.1 Circuit Description

In HIDP, the relay is connected across the **neutral point** of the CT secondary circuit through a **stabilizing resistor (R_stab)** in series with the relay voltage-sensing element. The relay is, therefore, a **high-impedance voltage-measuring relay** (typically 1000–5000 Ω at setting voltage).

**Key Construction Rules:**

- All CTs of the protected zone must have **identically rated transformation ratios** and very **low secondary leakage reactance**.
- CT secondary leads must be of **equal length** to keep burdens balanced.
- A **metrosil (non-linear resistor, ZnO varistor)** is connected across the relay to **clamp the peak voltage** during an internal fault and protect the relay insulation.

#### 2.2.2 Operating Principle — External (Through) Fault

During an external fault, the heavily saturated CT behaves like a low-impedance source. The current circulates between the two CTs, with negligible current flowing into the high-impedance relay. The voltage developed across the relay is small — the relay **does not operate**.

#### 2.2.3 Operating Principle — Internal Fault

During an internal fault, the fault current flows through **both** CTs in the same direction (both feed the fault). The CT secondaries cannot source this current through the high-impedance relay; instead, the voltage across the relay rises rapidly (proportional to the source voltage of the CTs divided by their internal impedance plus leads). When this voltage exceeds the **set voltage $V_s$**, the relay operates.

#### 2.2.4 Stabilizing Resistor Calculation (the core KTU formula)

The **stabilizing resistor** ensures that, during the worst external fault, the voltage developed across the relay circuit is less than the set voltage. Derivation is given in Section 3.

---

### 2.3 Low Impedance Differential Protection (LIDP) — Percentage / Biased Differential

#### 2.3.1 Circuit Description

In LIDP, the relay is a **current-operated (low-impedance) device** inserted in series with the neutral of the paralleled CTs. The relay internally has two coils (in classical electro-mechanical design) or two algorithms (in numerical design):

- **Operating Coil (Op):** Carries the spill current $I_{op} = \bar{I_1} + \bar{I_2}$.
- **Restraining Coil (Res):** Carries the through current $I_{rest} = (\bar{I_1} - \bar{I_2})/2$ or $\vert \bar{I_1} \vert + \vert \bar{I_2} \vert$ (depending on design).

In **numerical relays**, both quantities are computed in software from the sampled CT secondaries, but the bias characteristic remains identical.

#### 2.3.2 Bias Characteristic

The relay characteristic is a piecewise linear function in the $I_{op}$–$I_{rest}$ plane:

| Region | Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Below break-point 1** | $I_{op} \geq I_{min}$ | Unrestrained (sensitive) zone for low current |
| **Between break-points** | $I_{op} \geq S_1 \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min}$ | Single-slope bias (typically 20–40 %) |
| **Above break-point 2** | $I_{op} \geq S_2 \cdot I_{rest} + C_2$ | High-slope bias (typically 60–80 %) for heavy through-fault CT saturation |

Where:
- $S_1$ = **First bias slope** (e.g., 0.30)
- $S_2$ = **Second bias slope** (e.g., 0.80)
- $I_{min}$ = **Minimum operate current** (e.g., 0.30 A)
- Break-point 1 (BP1) and Break-point 2 (BP2) define the transition between slopes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Dual-Slope Characteristic?**  
> At high through-fault currents, **CTs saturate asymmetrically** — one CT may saturate while the other does not. This produces a **large false spill current** that scales roughly with the through current. A single low slope would either misoperate (slope too low) or desensitize internal faults (slope too high). The dual-slope characteristic provides **restraint where needed** (high currents) and **sensitivity where needed** (low currents).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE Module 2.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Units / Range | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{op} = \vert \bar{I_1} + \bar{I_2} \vert$ | Phasor sum of CT secondary currents | A (secondary) | Both schemes |
| 2 | $I_{rest} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert \bar{I_1} - \bar{I_2} \vert$ | Through / restraining current | A (secondary) | Lo-Z bias |
| 3 | $I_{op} \geq S \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min}$ | Bias characteristic (single slope) | Dimensionless S | Lo-Z |
| 4 | $V_s = I_f \cdot (R_{CT} + 2R_L + R_{lead})$ | Set voltage under worst external fault | V | Hi-Z design |
| 5 | $R_{stab} = \dfrac{V_s}{I_s} - (R_{CT} + 2R_L)$ | Stabilizing resistor value | Ω | Hi-Z design |
| 6 | $V_s = 2 \cdot V_k$ | Recommended safety factor (2 × CT KPV) | V | Hi-Z setting |
| 7 | $V_{relay,max} = K \cdot \dfrac{I_f}{N} \cdot (R_{CT} + 2R_L + R_{stab})$ | Peak voltage on relay (internal fault) | V (peak) | Hi-Z metrosil sizing |
| 8 | $E_{CT} = V_k \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{I_e}{I_s}\right)$ | Effective CT EMF under saturation | V | Hi-Z stability check |
| 9 | $K_{sens} = \dfrac{I_{op,min,internal}}{I_{pickup}}$ | Sensitivity coefficient (must be $\geq 2$) | Dimensionless | Both |
| 10 | $I_{unrestrained} = k \cdot I_{rest}$ | High-set unrestrained element | A | Lo-Z backup |
| 11 | $I_{f,min} = \dfrac{I_{pickup} \cdot N}{1}$ | Primary operating current sensitivity | A (primary) | Lo-Z |
| 12 | $V_{k,required} \geq \dfrac{V_s}{0.5}$ | Required CT KPV check | V | Hi-Z |

> [!WARNING]
> **Symbol convention alert:** $I_s$ in formula 5 is the **CT secondary current corresponding to the maximum external (through) fault**, NOT the relay setting current. Misreading this symbol is a **very common KTU exam mistake**.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Preferred Scheme | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **EHV Busbars (400 kV, 220 kV)** | Hi-Z (still used in many utilities) | Absolute stability, immunity to inrush |
| **Power Transformers** | Lo-Z (numerical) | Handles inrush, CT mismatch, over-excitation |
| **Generators (Stator)** | Lo-Z (biased) | High sensitivity needed for turn-to-turn faults |
| **Short Transmission Lines / Cables** | Lo-Z (current differential) | Telemetry-aided, fast |
| **Generator-Transformer Unit** | Lo-Z (single relay for both) | Common practice in digital schemes |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Operating (Spill) Current for an Internal Fault

Consider a single-phase protected element with CTs at both ends. Let the CT ratio be $N = I_{primary} / I_{secondary}$.

**Given:**
- Primary internal fault current: $I_{f,p}$ (A, primary)
- CT ratio: $N$
- CT secondary current from end-1: $\bar{I_1}$
- CT secondary current from end-2: $\bar{I_2}$

**Step 1:** For a **bolted internal fault** within the protected zone, both CTs feed the fault point. By KCL at the fault node:

$$\bar{I_1} + \bar{I_2} = \bar{I_f}$$

where $\bar{I_f}$ is the total fault current referred to the secondary side.

**Step 2:** If we assume the fault is fed symmetrically from both ends (worst case for sensitivity analysis = unsymmetrical feed), one end may source all the current:

$$\bar{I_1} = \dfrac{\bar{I_f}}{N}, \quad \bar{I_2} = 0$$

**Step 3:** The operating (spill) current in the relay branch is:

$$I_{op} = \vert \bar{I_1} + \bar{I_2} \vert = \dfrac{\bar{I_f}}{N}$$

**Step 4:** Sensitivity check — the relay must operate for the **minimum internal fault current** $I_{f,min}$ :

$$I_{op,min} = \dfrac{I_{f,min}}{N} \geq I_{pickup}$$

**Conversion logic:** The relay measures the *algebraic* sum of secondary currents. For an internal fault, this sum equals the *total* secondary fault current, which is the primary fault current divided by the CT turns ratio $N$. This is the foundation on which both Hi-Z and Lo-Z schemes operate.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage Developed Across the Relay During an External Fault (Hi-Z)

This is the **most important derivation for KTU ESE** — it yields the stabilizing resistor formula.

**Given Circuit (Hi-Z during external fault):**
- Maximum external (through) fault current in primary: $I_{f,ext,p}$
- CT ratio: $N$
- CT secondary winding resistance: $R_{CT}$
- Lead resistance from CT to relay (one-way): $R_L$
- Stabilizing resistor: $R_{stab}$
- Relay coil resistance: $R_{relay}$ (very high, ignored in current calculation)
- CT secondary current during external fault: $I_s = I_{f,ext,p} / N$

**Circuit Topology:**  
During an external fault, the two CTs form a series loop with their lead resistances. The high-impedance relay branch (containing $R_{stab}$ + $R_{relay}$) is connected in parallel between the two CT polarity points (i.e., across the summation point).

**Step 1 — Identify the loop current:**  
Since the relay branch has impedance $\gg R_{CT} + 2R_L$, the loop current $I_s$ circulates almost entirely through the CT secondary windings and the leads, with **negligible current diverted into the relay branch**. We therefore have:

$$I_s \approx \dfrac{I_{f,ext,p}}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage across the relay branch (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop):**  
The voltage that appears across the relay branch equals the voltage drop in the **non-relay** path:

$$V_{relay} = I_s \cdot (R_{CT} + 2 R_L)$$

The factor of 2 on $R_L$ accounts for the **go and return** path of the lead (current must travel *out* through one lead and *return* through the other).

**Step 3 — Apply the stability condition:**  
The relay must **NOT operate** for this external fault. The voltage across the relay must therefore be **less than the set voltage** $V_s$:

$$V_{relay} \leq V_s$$

$$I_s \cdot (R_{CT} + 2R_L) \leq V_s$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the stabilizing resistor to enforce the condition:**  
We add $R_{stab}$ in series with the relay coil. Now the relay branch is no longer a perfect voltmeter; it carries a small current. The loop is no longer purely the CT-lead loop. Let us recompute the voltage across the **relay coil alone** ($V_{coil}$):

The total loop impedance is: $R_{total} = R_{CT} + 2R_L + R_{stab} + R_{relay}$  
The loop current is still: $I_s$ (the high-impedance relay branch still diverts negligible current from the CTs themselves because of how the loop is configured — current circulates in the CT secondary loop; the relay branch is across it).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subtle but exam-critical point:** The CT secondary current is set by the primary current and the CT ratio. The relay branch is **shunt** across the two CTs. The **voltage across the relay branch** is the EMF induced in the CT minus the drop across its own secondary resistance. With the stabilizing resistor in place, the **voltage across the relay coil** is:

$$V_{coil} = I_s \cdot R_{stab}$$

(assuming $R_{relay} \gg R_{stab}$ and the CT EMF drives current through the loop).

**Step 5 — Stability condition with $R_{stab}$:**

$$V_{coil} \leq V_s$$

$$I_s \cdot R_{stab} \leq V_s$$

$$R_{stab} \leq \dfrac{V_s}{I_s}$$

**Step 6 — Practical design (worst-case):**  
For the relay to be **just at the verge of operation** (margin = 0), we set:

$$\boxed{R_{stab} = \dfrac{V_s}{I_s} - (R_{CT} + 2R_L)}$$

The term $(R_{CT} + 2R_L)$ is subtracted because part of the relay voltage setting is "used up" in driving the current through the CT winding resistance and the leads. The **net voltage** appearing across $R_{stab}$ must equal $V_s$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error in KTU valuation:**  
> Writing $R_{stab} = V_s / I_s$ **without** subtracting $(R_{CT} + 2R_L)$. This gives a value that is **too high** and the relay becomes over-stabilized — internal faults are **not cleared**. Marks are typically lost for the missing term and for not explaining its origin (Step 6).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Setting Voltage from CT Knee-Point Voltage (Hi-Z)

**Step 1:** The CT knee-point voltage $V_k$ is the maximum voltage the CT can deliver while maintaining its ratio (i.e., without saturation).

**Step 2:** During a worst-case external fault with one CT fully saturated, the unsaturated CT must drive the entire secondary EMF through the saturated CT and the relay circuit. To prevent the relay from misoperating, the relay set voltage must be **less than the EMF of the healthy CT**.

**Step 3:** Applying a safety factor of **2** (a standard KTU/IS convention for transformer and busbar protection):

$$\boxed{V_s = \dfrac{V_k}{2}}$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor 2 (i.e., operate at **half** the KPV) is a **conservative engineering margin** that accommodates:
- Manufacturing variation in $V_k$ (typical tolerance ±10 %).
- CT ageing and magnetizing current drift.
- DC offset in the fault current (which increases transient saturation).

Some references use $V_s = V_k / 1.5$ or $V_s = V_k$ directly, but **KTU examinations expect the factor of 2** unless otherwise specified.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bias Slope and Sensitivity (Lo-Z)

**Given:**
- Internal fault current (minimum): $I_{f,min,p}$ (primary)
- Through-fault current (maximum): $I_{thru,p}$ (primary)
- Bias slope: $S$
- Minimum operate current: $I_{min}$

**Step 1 — Convert to secondary using CT ratio $N$:**

$$I_{f,min,s} = \dfrac{I_{f,min,p}}{N}, \quad I_{thru,s} = \dfrac{I_{thru,p}}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Operating quantity at the internal fault:**

$$I_{op,internal} = I_{f,min,s}$$

**Step 3 — Restraining quantity at the internal fault (small, since fault is internal):**  
For a pure internal fault fed from one end only, the through component is small:

$$I_{rest,internal} \approx 0 \quad \text{(single-end-fed case)}$$

**Step 4 — Required operating threshold:**

$$I_{op,internal} \geq S \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min} \Rightarrow I_{f,min,s} \geq I_{min}$$

Hence the **sensitivity constraint** is:

$$I_{f,min,s} \geq 1.5 \cdot I_{min} \quad \text{(to provide 50 % margin)}$$

**Step 5 — Stability constraint during the maximum external (through) fault:**  
During a through fault, the relay sees a **false spill current** $I_{spill,false}$ due to CT mismatch. The relay must not operate, so:

$$I_{spill,false} \leq S \cdot I_{thru,s} + I_{min}$$

Solving for the **minimum bias slope** required for stability:

$$\boxed{S_{min} = \dfrac{I_{spill,false} - I_{min}}{I_{thru,s}}}$$

**Conversion logic:** A higher bias slope means **more restraint** and **higher security** against false trips, but **lower sensitivity** to internal faults. The choice of $S$ is a **trade-off** governed by CT quality, lead resistance, and the desired sensitivity coefficient.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Hi-Z vs Lo-Z

| Parameter | High Impedance (Hi-Z) | Low Impedance (Lo-Z) |
|---|---|---|
| **Relay type** | Voltage sensing (kΩ range) | Current sensing (mΩ range) |
| **CT ratio requirement** | Identical ratios mandatory | Identical ratios preferred, not mandatory |
| **Stability element** | Stabilizing resistor + metrosil | Bias (restraining) coil / algorithm |
| **CT knee-point requirement** | $V_k \geq 2 \cdot V_s$ | Less stringent |
| **Lead resistance effect** | Critical (appears directly in formula) | Compensated by bias |
| **Sensitivity to internal fault** | Moderate (limited by $V_s$ setting) | High (tunable slope) |
| **DC offset / inrush** | Inherently stable | Requires 2nd-harmonic restraint |
| **CT saturation handling** | Excellent (Hi-Z damps saturation) | Good with dual slope |
| **Speed** | Very fast (15–25 ms) | Fast (20–40 ms) |
| **Modern usage** | Declining (static relays) | Dominant (numerical relays) |
| **Typical application** | Busbars, short-zone transformer | Generators, large transformers, lines |
| **Cost & complexity** | Simple, low cost | Numerically intensive |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — High Impedance Differential Protection Scheme

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Protected Zone e.g. Busbar] -->|Primary current I1| B[CT1 End A]
    A -->|Primary current I2| C[CT2 End B]
    B -->|Secondary i1| D[Summation Neutral Point N]
    C -->|Secondary i2| D
    D -->|Lead R_L| E[Stabilizing Resistor R_stab]
    E --> F[Hi-Z Voltage Relay V_s]
    D -->|Parallel clamp| G[Metrosil / MOV varistor]
    F -->|Trip coil| H[Trip Output to Breaker]
    F -->|Operating quantity| I[Voltage V_coil across relay]
    I --> J{Compare with V_s}
    J -->|V_coil greater than V_s| K[TRIP COMMAND]
    J -->|V_coil less than V_s| L[RESTRAIN]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram left-to-right:** Primary current enters and exits the protected zone. CTs step down to secondary levels. The two secondaries meet at the **neutral point N**. From N, the circuit goes through a stabilizing resistor, into the high-impedance relay coil. A metrosil (varistor) is connected in parallel to clamp overvoltages during heavy internal faults. The relay measures the voltage across its coil and compares it to the set voltage $V_s$.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Low Impedance Differential Protection Scheme

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Protected Zone e.g. Transformer] -->|Primary I1| B[CT1 End A]
    A -->|Primary I2| C[CT2 End B]
    B -->|i1| D[Analog Summation Node]
    C -->|i2| D
    D -->|i1 plus i2| E[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    E --> F[16-bit ADC Sampler]
    F --> G[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    G -->|Phasor extraction| H[Compute I_op and I_rest]
    H --> I{Bias Characteristic Lookup}
    I -->|Below characteristic| J[RESTRAIN]
    I -->|Above characteristic| K[TRIP COMMAND]
    G -->|2nd harmonic check| L[Inrush Detector]
    L -->|Inrush detected| M[BLOCK TRIP]
    K --> N[Breaker Trip Coil]
    M --> N
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** The numerical Lo-Z relay is essentially an **embedded phasor calculator + bias-curve lookup table + harmonic restraint block**. The two CT secondaries are summed in hardware (or software), sampled, and decomposed into magnitude and phase. The DSP compares the resulting operating point to the piecewise-linear bias characteristic stored in firmware.

---

### 4.3 Bias Characteristic — Operating Region Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Origin 0,0] -->|I_rest axis| B[BP1]
    B -->|I_rest axis| C[BP2]
    C -->|I_rest axis| D[Heavy Through Fault]
    E[I_op axis] --> A
    E --> F[Region A Unrestrained]
    F --> G[Region B Slope S1]
    G --> H[Region C Slope S2]
    F --> B
    G --> C
    H --> D
    I[Region A I_op greater than I_min only] --> F
    J[Region B I_op greater than S1 times I_rest plus I_min] --> G
    K[Region C I_op greater than S2 times I_rest plus C2] --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpreting the diagram:** The vertical axis is the operating (spill) current $I_{op}$; the horizontal axis is the restraining (through) current $I_{rest}$. The piecewise-linear curve separates the **restrain** region (below the curve, no trip) from the **trip** region (above the curve, trip command issued). Three regions exist: (A) low-current sensitive zone with no bias, (B) single-slope bias zone, (C) high-slope bias zone for heavy through-fault CT saturation.

---

### 4.4 Equivalent Circuit — High Impedance Scheme (During External Fault)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CT1 Secondary EMF E1] -->|R_CT1| B[Node X]
    B -->|R_L lead 1| C[Common Neutral N]
    C -->|R_L lead 2| D[Node Y]
    D -->|R_CT2| E[CT2 Secondary EMF E2]
    C -->|Branch path| F[R_stab in series with R_relay coil]
    F --> G[Voltage measured V_coil]
    A -.->|Forces I_s| E
    E -.->|Forces I_s| A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational reading:** During an external fault, the two CT secondaries are connected in a **series-aiding loop** (because of polarity convention). The current $I_s$ circulates between them. The relay branch at the neutral is a high-impedance shunt. The voltage $V_{coil}$ across the relay branch is small because $R_{stab}$ plus $R_{relay}$ is large — the relay remains stable.

---

### 4.5 Phasor Diagram — Operating and Restraining Quantities

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[External Fault] --> B[I1 and I2 equal magnitude opposite phase]
    B --> C[I_op is approximately 0]
    B --> D[I_rest is large]
    D --> E[Relay restrains]
    F[Internal Fault] --> G[I1 and I2 same phase]
    G --> H[I_op is large]
    G --> I[I_rest is small]
    H --> J[Relay operates]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Phasor intuition:** During an *external* fault, the two CT secondary currents are **180° out of phase** (because the primary currents enter from one end and leave from the other). Their phasor sum is small — the relay restrains. During an *internal* fault, both CTs feed current **into** the fault, so their phasor sum is large and in phase — the relay operates. This is the elegant mathematical foundation of the entire scheme.
