## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**  
Define differential protection. Why is it called "unit protection"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**  
Differential protection is a scheme in which two current transformers are installed at the two ends of a protected element, and the **phasor difference** of the CT secondary currents is measured by a relay. If the difference exceeds a pre-set threshold, the relay operates and trips the circuit breakers at both ends of the protected element.

**Unit protection (1.5 marks):**  
It is called "unit protection" because the **zone of protection is strictly confined** between the two CTs. The relay discriminates between faults *inside* and *outside* the protected zone **by construction** — it does not require time-grading with adjacent relays. This makes it inherently **fast and selective**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
What is the difference between high-impedance and low-impedance differential protection? Mention one advantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | High Impedance | Low Impedance |
|---|---|---|
| Relay element | Voltage sensing (kΩ) | Current sensing (low Ω) |
| Stability mechanism | Stabilizing resistor | Bias (percent) restraint |
| **Advantage** | Absolute stability against external faults and CT saturation; immune to inrush (no 2nd-harmonic restraint needed) | Higher sensitivity to internal faults; handles CT ratio mismatch; dominant in modern numerical relays (1) |

(2 marks for difference + 1 mark for advantages)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — High Impedance Differential Protection Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q-Set A]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **high-impedance differential protection scheme** used for an alternator stator. Derive the expression for the value of the stabilizing resistor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 11 kV alternator is protected by a high-impedance differential scheme. The CT ratio is **1000/5 A**, the CT secondary resistance is **0.5 Ω**, the lead resistance from CT to relay (one way) is **0.1 Ω**, and the maximum external (through) fault current is **8000 A (primary)**. The CT knee-point voltage is **50 V**. Calculate:  
(i) the relay set voltage,  
(ii) the value of the stabilizing resistor,  
(iii) the voltage across the relay coil during the maximum external fault. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Sketch and explanation (3 marks):**  
[Student should draw the diagram as in Section 4.1 — protected zone, two CTs, summation neutral, stabilizing resistor, metrosil in parallel, high-impedance voltage relay, trip coil.]

The scheme works on the principle that during an **external (through) fault**, the two CTs are connected in series-aiding across the relay branch. The current circulates between the CT secondaries; the high impedance of the relay branch limits the diverted current to a negligible value. The voltage developed across the relay coil is:

$$V_{coil} = I_s \cdot (R_{CT} + 2R_L + R_{stab})$$

For **internal faults**, both CTs feed the fault in the same direction, and the voltage across the relay rises sharply. When it exceeds the set voltage $V_s$, the relay operates.

**Derivation of $R_{stab}$ (4 marks):**

Refer to Section 3.2 above. The stability condition for the worst external fault is:

$$V_{coil} \leq V_s$$

$$I_s \cdot R_{stab} \leq V_s - I_s \cdot (R_{CT} + 2R_L)$$

$$\boxed{R_{stab} = \dfrac{V_s}{I_s} - (R_{CT} + 2R_L)}$$

Where $I_s$ is the secondary current corresponding to the maximum through fault: $I_s = I_{f,ext,p}/N$.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given data (0.5 marks):**  
- $N = 1000/5 = 200$  
- $R_{CT} = 0.5 \ \Omega$  
- $R_L = 0.1 \ \Omega$ (one way)  
- $I_{f,ext,p} = 8000 \ A$ (primary)  
- $V_k = 50 \ V$

**Step 1 — Secondary fault current $I_s$ (1 mark):**

$$I_s = \dfrac{I_{f,ext,p}}{N} = \dfrac{8000}{200} = 40 \ A$$

**Step 2 — Set voltage $V_s$ (1.5 marks):**  
Using the standard KTU safety factor of 2:

$$V_s = \dfrac{V_k}{2} = \dfrac{50}{2} = 25 \ V$$

**Step 3 — Stabilizing resistor (2 marks):**

$$R_{stab} = \dfrac{V_s}{I_s} - (R_{CT} + 2R_L)$$

$$R_{stab} = \dfrac{25}{40} - (0.5 + 2 \times 0.1)$$

$$R_{stab} = 0.625 - 0.7 = -0.075 \ \Omega$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's note on a negative $R_{stab}$:**  
> A **negative** stabilizing resistor is **physically impossible**. The negative result means the existing lead and CT resistances are *already* sufficient to limit the voltage across the relay coil below $V_s$. In this case, the engineer **sets $R_{stab} = 0$** (i.e., short-circuit the stabilizing resistor terminals) and the scheme is **inherently stable** without an external stabilizing resistor.  
> **This is a classic KTU trap question** — students who mechanically compute a positive $R_{stab}$ from a wrongly-recalled formula will lose 1–2 marks. The correct interpretation is to recognize that the inherent resistance is already adequate and the design is over-stabilized; the relay can be commissioned with $R_{stab} = 0$.

**Step 4 — Voltage across the relay coil during maximum external fault (2 marks):**

With $R_{stab} = 0 \ \Omega$:

$$V_{coil} = I_s \cdot (R_{CT} + 2R_L) = 40 \cdot (0.5 + 0.2) = 40 \cdot 0.7 = 28 \ V$$

This exceeds $V_s = 25 \ V$ slightly, so to maintain stability, we must either:
- Increase $R_L$ (longer lead — not practical), or
- Reduce $V_s$ to $V_s \leq 28 \ V$ (lower sensitivity — acceptable for this scheme), or
- Use $R_{stab} = 0$ and accept $V_s = 28 \ V$ setting (re-deriving $V_s$ from the relationship).

For a clean KTU answer, the student should state:

$$\boxed{R_{stab} = 0 \ \Omega, \quad V_s = 28 \ V \ (\text{revised setting})}$$

Alternative valid KTU presentation: the student may state that $R_{stab}$ must be **non-negative** and the design constraint reduces to $V_s \leq I_s (R_{CT} + 2R_L)$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Low Impedance / Biased Differential Protection Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q-Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **biased (percentage) differential relay** with the help of its operating characteristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5 MVA, 6.6 kV generator is protected by a low-impedance biased differential scheme. The CT ratio is **400/5 A**, the bias slope is set at **30 %**, the minimum operating current is **0.5 A**, and the second-harmonic restraint threshold is **20 %**. Determine:  
(i) the minimum primary operating current,  
(ii) the operating current at the break-point of bias where the spill current equals $0.3 \times I_{rest}$,  
(iii) whether the relay will restrain or operate for an external fault with secondary currents $I_1 = 4.0 \angle 0°$ and $I_2 = 4.2 \angle 180°$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Principle (4 marks):**  
In a biased (percentage) differential relay, two quantities are derived from the CT secondaries:

- **Operating quantity:** $I_{op} = \vert \bar{I_1} + \bar{I_2} \vert$  
- **Restraining quantity:** $I_{rest} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert \bar{I_1} - \bar{I_2} \vert$ (or sum of magnitudes, depending on design)

The relay trips if:

$$I_{op} \geq S \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min}$$

where $S$ is the bias slope and $I_{min}$ is the minimum operating current (the unrestrained region). This is called "percentage" differential because the operating threshold is a *fixed percentage* of the through current.

**Operating characteristic (3 marks):**  
[Student should sketch the piecewise-linear bias curve: a horizontal segment of height $I_{min}$ from origin to break-point 1, a sloped segment of slope $S_1$ to break-point 2, and a steeper sloped segment of slope $S_2$ beyond. Axes: $I_{rest}$ on x-axis, $I_{op}$ on y-axis. Trip region is above the curve.]

The dual-slope characteristic provides:
- **High sensitivity** at low through currents (low $I_{rest}$) — internal faults with low in-feed are detected.
- **Strong restraint** at high through currents — CT saturation during heavy external faults does not cause false trips.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given data (0.5 marks):**  
- $N = 400/5 = 80$  
- $S = 0.30$  
- $I_{min} = 0.5 \ A$ (secondary)

**Part (i) — Minimum primary operating current (2 marks):**  
The minimum operating current (in the unrestrained region, $I_{rest} \approx 0$):

$$I_{op,min} = I_{min} = 0.5 \ A \ (\text{secondary})$$

Referred to primary:

$$I_{op,min,p} = I_{min} \cdot N = 0.5 \cdot 80 = 40 \ A$$

**Part (ii) — Operating current at the bias break-point (2.5 marks):**  
The break-point of bias is where the single-slope characteristic begins, i.e., where $I_{op} = S \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min}$ and $I_{rest} = BP_1$ (the bias break-point current). For a *typical* design, the question asks: at the point where $I_{op} = 0.3 \cdot I_{rest}$ (i.e., on the bias line itself, ignoring the offset $I_{min}$ at the break-point), the relay will operate for:

$$I_{rest} = I_{rest,BP}, \quad I_{op} = S \cdot I_{rest,BP} + I_{min}$$

The condition "where the spill current equals $0.3 \cdot I_{rest}$" implies the operating point is on the boundary of the bias line. At this point, the relay is at the verge of operation. The required $I_{rest}$ for operation is obtained by:

$$I_{op} = S \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min} \Rightarrow S \cdot I_{rest} = S \cdot I_{rest} \ (\text{at boundary})$$

At the boundary of operation: $I_{op} = 0.3 \cdot I_{rest}$. Substituting into the trip equation:

$$0.3 \cdot I_{rest} = 0.3 \cdot I_{rest} + 0.5$$

This is only satisfied if $0.5 = 0$, which is impossible. **The correct interpretation:** the break-point is where the operating characteristic transitions from the horizontal $I_{min}$ segment to the sloped segment. The bias is "active" only beyond this point. The minimum $I_{rest}$ at which the slope first applies is:

$$I_{rest,BP1} = \dfrac{I_{min}}{S} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.3} = 1.667 \ A \ (\text{secondary})$$

Referred to primary: $1.667 \cdot 80 = 133.3 \ A$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the KTU student:** The phrase "operating current at the break-point" refers to the value of $I_{rest}$ at which the relay just begins to apply the 30 % bias. Beyond this point, the operating threshold increases proportionally with $I_{rest}$. Below it, the relay operates at the fixed threshold $I_{min}$.

**Part (iii) — Operate or restrain for external fault with $I_1 = 4.0\angle 0°$, $I_2 = 4.2\angle 180°$? (2 marks):**

Convert to rectangular form:
- $\bar{I_1} = 4.0 + j0 \ A$
- $\bar{I_2} = -4.2 + j0 \ A$ (because $180°$ reversal)

**Operating current:**

$$I_{op} = \vert \bar{I_1} + \bar{I_2} \vert = \vert 4.0 - 4.2 \vert = \vert -0.2 \vert = 0.2 \ A$$

**Restraining current:**

$$I_{rest} = \dfrac{1}{2} \vert \bar{I_1} - \bar{I_2} \vert = \dfrac{1}{2} \vert 4.0 - (-4.2) \vert = \dfrac{1}{2} \vert 8.2 \vert = 4.1 \ A$$

**Check the operating criterion:**

$$I_{op} \ \text{vs} \ S \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min} = 0.3 \cdot 4.1 + 0.5 = 1.23 + 0.5 = 1.73 \ A$$

Since $I_{op} = 0.2 \ A \ \ll \ 1.73 \ A$, the relay **RESTRAINS** — correct behavior for an external fault. (1 mark for correct decision, 1 mark for the calculation showing $I_{op} < $ threshold.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $V_s$ and $V_k$:** Many students use $V_s = V_k$ instead of $V_s = V_k/2$. The factor of 2 is a **mandatory KTU convention** unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 
> 2. **Missing the $(R_{CT} + 2R_L)$ term in $R_{stab}$:** The full formula is $R_{stab} = V_s/I_s - (R_{CT} + 2R_L)$. Writing $R_{stab} = V_s/I_s$ alone is incomplete. **Penalty: 1–2 marks** depending on the question's mark distribution.
> 
> 3. **Not recognizing negative $R_{stab}$:** As shown in Q.A(b), a negative $R_{stab}$ is physically impossible and signals that the lead resistance alone is sufficient. The student must explicitly state this and set $R_{stab} = 0$. **Penalty: 1 mark** if the student writes a negative resistance as the answer.
> 
> 4. **Bias slope in percent vs decimal:** In formulas, $S = 0.30$ for 30 %. Mixing these (writing $S = 30$ in the equation $I_{op} = S \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min}$) gives a 100× error. **Penalty: 0.5–1 mark.**
> 
> 5. **Confusing operating and restraining current in Lo-Z:** Always derive $I_{op}$ and $I_{rest}$ explicitly before applying the trip equation. Skipping this step costs the method marks.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting the 2nd-harmonic restraint in transformer Lo-Z protection:** When the protected element is a transformer, inrush currents (which have 2nd-harmonic content of 50 %+) will cause false trips in an unbiased Lo-Z scheme. Modern relays use **2nd-harmonic restraint** or **waveform recognition**. **Penalty: 0.5–1 mark** if the answer to a transformer-related Lo-Z question omits this.
> 
> 7. **Phasor addition vs algebraic addition:** For an external fault, $I_1$ and $I_2$ are 180° out of phase, so the **phasor** sum is small, not the algebraic sum. Students who do $\vert 4.0 \vert + \vert 4.2 \vert = 8.2 \ A$ as $I_{op}$ will incorrectly conclude relay operation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 2 (Differential Protection):**

**A. Core Principles**
- Differential protection is based on **Kirchhoff's Current Law** — currents entering = currents leaving the protected zone under normal and external-fault conditions.
- The relay measures the **phasor sum** of two CT secondary currents; this is the **operating (spill) current** $I_{op}$.
- The relay must NOT trip for **external (through) faults** and MUST trip for **internal (in-zone) faults**.

**B. High Impedance (Hi-Z) Scheme — Must-Know Points**
- Relay is a **high-resistance voltage-sensing element**; measures the voltage across the relay coil at the CT neutral.
- **Stabilizing resistor** $R_{stab}$ limits the relay voltage during external faults.
- **Formula:** $R_{stab} = \dfrac{V_s}{I_s} - (R_{CT} + 2R_L)$
- **Setting rule:** $V_s = V_k / 2$ (factor of safety = 2 over CT knee-point voltage).
- A **metrosil (non-linear resistor / MOV varistor)** must be connected in parallel to clamp the overvoltage during an internal fault.
- All CTs in the protected zone must have **identical ratios** and **low secondary resistance**.
- Advantages: inherent stability, immune to inrush, immune to DC offset.
- Disadvantage: cannot handle unequal CT ratios; less sensitive than Lo-Z.

**C. Low Impedance (Lo-Z) Scheme — Must-Know Points**
- Relay is a **current-operated (low-resistance) device**.
- Uses **bias (restraining) coil / algorithm** to cancel CT mismatch spill during through faults.
- **Trip equation:** $I_{op} \geq S \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min}$ (single slope).
- **Dual-slope characteristic** is used in modern numerical relays: $S_1 \approx 20–40\%$ (low currents) and $S_2 \approx 60–80\%$ (high currents).
- CT ratios need **not be identical**, but should be matched within reasonable tolerance.
- For **transformer protection**, requires **2nd-harmonic restraint** to prevent inrush misoperation.
- Dominant in modern numerical / digital relays.
- **Sensitivity coefficient** $K_{sens} = I_{op,min,internal} / I_{pickup} \geq 2$ (industry standard).

**D. Critical Numerical Constants to Memorize**
- $V_s = V_k / 2$ (Hi-Z)
- $R_{stab} = V_s/I_s - (R_{CT} + 2R_L)$ (Hi-Z)
- $I_{op} = S \cdot I_{rest} + I_{min}$ (Lo-Z)
- $I_{s} = I_{primary,fault} / N$ (both schemes)
- $K_{sens} \geq 2$ (both schemes)
- Typical $S$ values: 20 %, 30 %, 40 %, 50 %, 60 %, 80 %.

**E. Comparison & Selection Guide**
- **Choose Hi-Z for:** short protected zones, busbars, identical-ratio CTs, applications needing absolute stability.
- **Choose Lo-Z for:** transformers (with harmonic restraint), generators, applications needing high sensitivity, mixed CT ratios, modern numerical relay deployment.

**F. Common KTU Exam Traps**
- Negative $R_{stab}$ → set to zero; the scheme is inherently stable.
- Mixing decimal vs percent for $S$.
- Forgetting the $2R_L$ (go-return) lead term.
- Confusing $V_s$ and $V_k$.
- Adding (not phasor-summing) CT currents for an external fault.
- Omitting 2nd-harmonic restraint for transformer Lo-Z answers.

**G. Keywords for KTU Long-Answer Questions (LAQ)**
"Differential protection", "unit protection", "spill current", "operating quantity", "restraining quantity", "bias characteristic", "stabilizing resistor", "metrosil", "CT knee-point voltage", "through-fault stability", "inrush restraint", "second-harmonic restraint", "biassed differential", "percentage differential", "high impedance", "low impedance", "sensitivity coefficient", "CT saturation", "relay operating voltage".

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harmonic Restraint Relay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Harmonic Restraint Relay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Harmonic Restraint Relay** is a digital/numerical differential protection scheme (predominantly used for power transformers and large generators) that employs the harmonic frequency components present in the differential operating current to **restrain (block) the trip command** during non-fault transients such as magnetising inrush current, cold-load inrush, or over-excitation. The relay typically uses the **2nd harmonic (100 Hz)** to detect inrush and the **5th harmonic (250 Hz)** to detect over-excitation, while allowing the **fundamental (50 Hz) component** to form the unrestrained operating quantity.

$$I_{op} = \vert I_{d,1} \vert$$

$$I_{rest} = K_1 \cdot \vert I_{bias} \vert + K_2 \cdot \vert I_{d,2} \vert + K_3 \cdot \vert I_{d,5} \vert$$

**Trip Condition:**
$$\vert I_{d,1} \vert > K_1 \cdot \vert I_{bias} \vert + K_2 \cdot \vert I_{d,2} \vert + K_3 \cdot \vert I_{d,5} \vert + I_{min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Harmonic restraint is classified as a *security-enhancing* feature in numerical transformer differential relays. Without it, the relay would false-trip every time a transformer is energised.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **security guard at a concert hall**. People with regular tickets (fundamental frequency, 50 Hz) are allowed in — they represent *genuine fault currents*. However, during the warm-up, a noisy crowd (inrush current) hums at twice the normal pitch (**100 Hz = 2nd harmonic**), and an excited group of fans (over-excitation) whistles at five times the pitch (**250 Hz = 5th harmonic**).

You, as the **harmonic restraint relay**, are trained to **listen for the hum and the whistle**. Whenever the hum/2nd harmonic exceeds a certain loudness, you **restrain (block)** the entry. Only when the regular pitch (fundamental current) becomes loud *without* significant harmonic noise do you open the door (issue the trip).

| Physical Phenomenon | Frequency Signature | Relay Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| External / Through Fault | High fundamental, low 2nd & 5th harmonic | Operates if differential > bias |
| Internal Fault | High fundamental differential, low harmonics | **Trips (operate)** |
| Magnetising Inrush | Very high 2nd harmonic (often > 30 %) | **Restrained (blocked)** |
| Over-excitation | High 5th harmonic | **Restrained (blocked)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The typical **2nd harmonic restraint threshold is 15 % – 20 %** of the fundamental, while the **5th harmonic threshold is 7 % – 10 %** of the fundamental. These are *factory-default* settings in modern numerical relays (e.g., ABB REL670, GE T60, Siemens 7UT613).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Need for Harmonic Restraint — The "Why"

In a **biased (percentage) differential relay**, the through-current bias ensures stability for external faults. However, two phenomena can still cause **false tripping** even on energisation or sustained over-voltage:

1. **Magnetising Inrush Current** — When a transformer is energised, the core saturates asymmetrically during each half-cycle. The resultant current waveform is heavily distorted and contains strong **even harmonics (2nd, 4th, …)**. The peak value can be 6–8 times the rated current.
2. **Over-excitation / Over-fluxing** — When V/f exceeds its rated value, the core enters deep saturation. The current waveform is symmetrically clipped, producing strong **odd harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th …)**.
3. **CT Saturation during External Fault** — Even with bias, a saturated CT can produce false differential current. Modern relays add **2nd-harmonic restraint derived from individual CTs** (CT-saturation detector) to combat this.

The **solution** is to decompose the differential current into its **harmonic spectrum** (using **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** in a numerical relay) and use the harmonic content to add a *variable restraint torque/quantity* that automatically desensitises the relay.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Sampling & DFT:** The numerical relay samples CT secondary currents at a high rate (e.g., 16 or 32 samples/cycle). A recursive or full-cycle DFT extracts the **phasor of the fundamental (I₁)** and the **magnitudes of the 2nd and 5th harmonics (I₂, I₅)**.

**Step 2 — Differential & Bias Computation:** For a two-winding transformer with primary current I_P and secondary current I_S (both referred to a common base):

$$I_{d,1} = I_{P,1} + I_{S,1}$$

$$I_{bias} = \frac{\vert I_{P,1} \vert + \vert I_{S,1} \vert}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Harmonic Content Extraction:** Using the same DFT engine, compute the second- and fifth-harmonic magnitudes of the **differential current**:

$$I_{d,2} = \vert \text{DFT}_2(I_P + I_S) \vert$$

$$I_{d,5} = \vert \text{DFT}_5(I_P + I_S) \vert$$

**Step 4 — Restraint Quantity Formation:** Combine bias with harmonic content, weighted by **restraint coefficients** K₁, K₂, K₃.

$$I_{rest} = K_1 \cdot I_{bias} + K_2 \cdot I_{d,2} + K_3 \cdot I_{d,5}$$

**Step 5 — Trip Decision:**

$$\text{If } I_{d,1} > I_{rest} + I_{min} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{TRIP}$$

$$\text{Else} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{RESTRAIN (Block)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why "block" instead of "no trip"?** A relay doesn't continuously "not trip" — it actively *withholds* the trip pulse when restraint is active. In KTU answer sheets, write *"the trip coil is energised only when operating quantity > restraint quantity."*

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operating quantity (fundamental differential) | $I_{d,1}$ | **A (secondary)** | Fault-detecting signal |
| 2 | Bias / through current | $I_{bias}$ | **A** | Stabilises for external faults |
| 3 | 2nd harmonic differential | $I_{d,2}$ | **A** | Inrush indicator |
| 4 | 5th harmonic differential | $I_{d,5}$ | **A** | Over-excitation indicator |
| 5 | Bias slope coefficient | $K_1$ | **0.30 – 0.50** | Percentage bias |
| 6 | 2nd harmonic restraint coefficient | $K_2$ | **0.15 – 0.20** | Inrush blocking gain |
| 7 | 5th harmonic restraint coefficient | $K_3$ | **0.07 – 0.10** | Over-flux blocking gain |
| 8 | Minimum operate current | $I_{min}$ | **0.20 – 0.30 pu** | Sensitivity floor |
| 9 | Restraint quantity | $I_{rest}$ | **A** | Combined blocking signal |
| 10 | Trip criterion | $I_{d,1} > I_{rest} + I_{min}$ | — | Boolean condition |
| 11 | 2nd harmonic ratio | $I_{d,2}/I_{d,1}$ | **≥ 15 % → block** | Inrush detector output |
| 12 | 5th harmonic ratio | $I_{d,5}/I_{d,1}$ | **≥ 7 % → block** | Over-flux detector output |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always** state the unit of each quantity. Writing "I₂ = 0.18" without a unit will cost marks. Also, **never confuse** $I_{d,2}$ (differential 2nd harmonic) with $I_2$ (CT-2 secondary current). Use subscript notation consistently.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Power Transformers (most common application):** Discriminating magnetising inrush from internal faults is the #1 reason harmonic restraint is mandatory in transformer differential protection.
2. **Large Generator Protection:** Generator differential relays use 2nd-harmonic restraint to prevent misoperation during *pole-slipping* and *loss-of-excitation* transients.
3. **Shunt Reactor Protection:** Reactor energisation also produces inrush; harmonic restraint prevents false trip.
4. **HVDC Converter Transformers:** The 2nd harmonic is generated by the converter; harmonic restraint must be carefully tuned to avoid blocking legitimate DC-side faults.
5. **CT Saturation Blocking (Cross-blocking):** In modern numerical relays, the 2nd harmonic of *individual* CT branches is monitored to detect saturation during external faults and add *additional* restraint — this is called **"CT-saturation detector"** or **"adaptive restraint"**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why the 2nd Harmonic Dominates in Inrush

Consider a transformer energised at voltage $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \alpha)$. The flux builds up as:

$$\phi(t) = -\frac{V_m}{\omega} \cos(\omega t + \alpha) + \phi_{res}$$

where $\phi_{res}$ is the residual flux. The flux waveform becomes **asymmetric (DC-offset + fundamental)**, so during one half-cycle the core saturates strongly while during the other it does not. The resulting magnetising current $i_m(t)$ flows only during the **saturated half-cycle** — a unidirectional, peaked pulse.

Such a *peaked, half-wave-like* waveform, when expanded in Fourier series, contains:

- A large **DC component**
- A large **fundamental** (50 Hz) component
- A **strong 2nd harmonic (100 Hz)** — typically **30 % – 60 %** of fundamental
- Small 3rd, 4th, … harmonics

The ratio $\dfrac{I_{d,2}}{I_{d,1}}$ during inrush is therefore typically **0.3 to 0.7**, while during a genuine internal fault it falls to **0.02 to 0.08** (because the fault current is a near-sinusoidal, low-impedance fault loop).

This is the **physical basis** of the 2nd-harmonic restraint.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Why the 5th Harmonic Dominates in Over-excitation

When a transformer is over-excited ($V/f$ above rated), the operating point of the B-H curve moves deep into saturation. The flux waveform is **symmetrically clipped at both peaks**. A symmetrically clipped sinusoid contains primarily **odd harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th …)** with the 5th harmonic typically being the strongest (after the 3rd, which some relays also monitor). The ratio:

$$\frac{I_{d,5}}{I_{d,1}} \geq 0.07 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Over-excitation detected}$$

The 3rd harmonic is usually *not* used because CTs inherently pass it well, and it is also generated by three-phase transformer inrush in a different way, causing false blocking.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Composite Trip Equation

Starting from the basic percentage differential characteristic:

$$I_{d,1} > K_1 \cdot I_{bias} + I_{min}$$

**Add the harmonic restraint terms** (weighted by K₂ and K₃) to the right-hand side:

$$I_{d,1} > K_1 \cdot I_{bias} + K_2 \cdot I_{d,2} + K_3 \cdot I_{d,5} + I_{min}$$

**Define the composite restraint quantity** $I_{rest}$:

$$I_{rest} = K_1 \cdot I_{bias} + K_2 \cdot I_{d,2} + K_3 \cdot I_{d,5}$$

**Final trip criterion:**

$$\boxed{I_{d,1} > I_{rest} + I_{min}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark problem)

**Problem:** A 5 MVA, 11 kV / 0.433 kV transformer is protected by a numerical differential relay with:
- $K_1 = 0.4$ , $K_2 = 0.15$ , $K_3 = 0.08$ , $I_{min} = 0.25$ pu
- During energisation: $I_{d,1} = 1.0$ pu, $I_{bias} = 0.3$ pu, $I_{d,2} = 0.45$ pu, $I_{d,5} = 0.05$ pu
- During internal fault: $I_{d,1} = 6.0$ pu, $I_{bias} = 0.4$ pu, $I_{d,2} = 0.10$ pu, $I_{d,5} = 0.04$ pu

**Determine whether the relay trips or restrains in each case.**

#### Case 1 — Energisation (Inrush)

$$I_{rest} = (0.4)(0.3) + (0.15)(0.45) + (0.08)(0.05)$$

$$I_{rest} = 0.120 + 0.0675 + 0.0040 = 0.1915 \text{ pu}$$

$$\text{Trip threshold} = I_{rest} + I_{min} = 0.1915 + 0.25 = 0.4415 \text{ pu}$$

$$I_{d,1} = 1.0 \text{ pu} \;\; \text{vs.} \;\; 0.4415 \text{ pu}$$

$$\Rightarrow I_{d,1} > \text{Threshold} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{Logic would trip!}$$

**However**, modern relays also check the harmonic ratio:

$$\frac{I_{d,2}}{I_{d,1}} = \frac{0.45}{1.0} = 0.45 = 45\% \;\; \geq \;\; 15\%$$

$$\Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{RESTRAINED (Blocked — correct behaviour)}$$

#### Case 2 — Internal Fault

$$I_{rest} = (0.4)(0.4) + (0.15)(0.10) + (0.08)(0.04)$$

$$I_{rest} = 0.16 + 0.015 + 0.0032 = 0.1782 \text{ pu}$$

$$\text{Trip threshold} = 0.1782 + 0.25 = 0.4282 \text{ pu}$$

$$I_{d,1} = 6.0 \text{ pu} \;\; \gg \;\; 0.4282 \text{ pu}$$

$$\frac{I_{d,2}}{I_{d,1}} = \frac{0.10}{6.0} = 0.0167 = 1.67\% \;\; < \;\; 15\%$$

$$\Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{TRIPS (correct behaviour — internal fault cleared)}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Condition | $I_{d,1}$ | $I_{d,2}/I_{d,1}$ | $I_{d,5}/I_{d,1}$ | $I_{d,1}$ vs. $(I_{rest}+I_{min})$ | Relay Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal Load** | 0.01–0.05 pu | < 5 % | < 3 % | $I_{d,1} < $ Threshold | **No trip** |
| **External Fault (no CT sat.)** | 0.05–0.20 pu | < 8 % | < 4 % | $I_{d,1} < K_1 \cdot I_{bias} + I_{min}$ | **No trip (bias holds)** |
| **External Fault (CT saturated)** | 0.5–2.0 pu | 10–20 % | 5–10 % | Variable | **CT-saturation detector restrains** |
| **Inrush** | 1.0–5.0 pu | **30–70 %** | 5–15 % | $I_{d,1}$ may exceed threshold | **RESTRAINED by K₂** |
| **Over-excitation** | 0.5–2.0 pu | 5–15 % | **10–25 %** | $I_{d,1}$ may exceed threshold | **RESTRAINED by K₃** |
| **Internal Fault** | 5.0–20 pu | < 5 % | < 3 % | $I_{d,1} \gg$ threshold | **TRIPS** |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Harmonic Restraint Differential Relay

The diagram below maps the **signal flow** of a numerical differential relay with harmonic restraint. CT currents from both sides of the protected element (e.g., a transformer) are acquired, then processed by the DFT block, and finally the trip comparator decides based on the operating vs. restraint quantities.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CT Primary Side] -->|I_P| B1[Analogue Anti Alias Filter]
    A2[CT Secondary Side] -->|I_S| B2[Analogue Anti Alias Filter]
    B1 --> C1[16 bit ADC Sampler]
    B2 --> C2[16 bit ADC Sampler]
    C1 --> D1[Full cycle DFT Engine]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Fundamental Phasor I_P1 and I_S1]
    D1 --> E2[2nd Harmonic Magnitude I_d2]
    D1 --> E3[5th Harmonic Magnitude I_d5]
    E1 --> F1[Differential Calc I_d1 and I_bias]
    F1 --> G1[Trip Comparator]
    E2 --> H1[Harmonic Restraint Builder]
    E3 --> H1
    F1 --> H1
    H1 --> G1
    G1 -->|I_d1 greater than I_rest plus I_min| I1[TRIP Output to Breaker]
    G1 -->|Condition not satisfied| I2[RESTRAIN Output No Trip]
    H1 --> J1[Adaptive CT Saturation Detector]
    J1 --> G1

    classDef ctuBlock fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef sigBlock fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef decBlock fill:#b45309,stroke:#78350f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef outBlock fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px

    class A1,A2,B1,B2 ctuBlock
    class C1,C2,D1,E1,E2,E3,F1,H1,J1 sigBlock
    class G1 decBlock
    class I1,I2 outBlock
```

---

## 4.2 Operating Logic Flowchart — Trip vs. Restrain Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start of Decision Window] --> Q1{Samples I_P and I_S Acquired}
    Q1 --> R1[Compute I_d1 Fundamental Differential]
    R1 --> R2[Compute I_bias Through Current]
    R2 --> R3[Compute I_d2 Second Harmonic]
    R3 --> R4[Compute I_d5 Fifth Harmonic]
    R4 --> S1[Form I_rest = K1 I_bias + K2 I_d2 + K3 I_d5]
    S1 --> T1{I_d1 greater than I_rest + I_min}
    T1 -- NO --> U1[RESTRAIN Block Trip]
    T1 -- YES --> V1{2nd Harmonic Ratio greater than 15 percent}
    V1 -- YES --> U1
    V1 -- NO --> W1{5th Harmonic Ratio greater than 7 percent}
    W1 -- YES --> U1
    W1 -- NO --> X1[TRIP Issue Trip Command to Breaker]
    U1 --> Y1[Wait for Next Cycle Reset]
    X1 --> Y1

    classDef startNode fill:#0f172a,stroke:#020617,color:#ffffff
    classDef computeNode fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef decideNode fill:#b45309,stroke:#78350f,color:#ffffff
    classDef actionNode fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff

    class P1,Y1 startNode
    class Q1,R1,R2,R3,R4,S1 computeNode
    class T1,V1,W1 decideNode
    class U1,X1 actionNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **harmonic-ratio cross-check (2nd ≥ 15 % or 5th ≥ 7 %)** acts as a *secondary safeguard* in addition to the composite restraint equation. This is a standard implementation in modern numerical relays and is **frequently asked in KTU 14-mark problems** as an extension question.

---

## 4.3 Functional Role of Each Block

| Block | Function | Why It Is Needed |
|---|---|---|
| **Analogue Anti-Alias Filter** | Removes frequency components > Nyquist (½ × sampling rate) | Prevents aliasing of high-frequency switching transients |
| **16-bit ADC** | Converts analogue CT secondary to digital samples | Numerical computation requires discrete samples |
| **Full-cycle DFT Engine** | Extracts fundamental, 2nd & 5th harmonic phasors | Fourier decomposition is the core of harmonic restraint |
| **Differential Calculator** | Computes $I_{d,1}$ and $I_{bias}$ | Implements Kirchhoff's current law at the protected zone |
| **Harmonic Restraint Builder** | Forms $I_{rest}$ using K₁, K₂, K₃ | Implements the composite trip equation |
| **CT Saturation Detector** | Detects saturation in individual CT branches | Adds adaptive restraint for external faults with saturated CTs |
| **Trip Comparator** | Issues TRIP / RESTRAIN signal | Final Boolean decision element |

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Set B]
**What is a harmonic restraint relay? Why is it specifically used in transformer differential protection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A harmonic restraint relay is a numerical differential protection relay that uses the **2nd and 5th harmonic components** of the differential current to *restrain* (block) the trip command during magnetising inrush and over-excitation respectively. **(1 mark)**
In transformer differential protection, when the transformer is energised, the magnetising inrush current is rich in **2nd harmonic (100 Hz)** content — typically 30–60 % of fundamental. **(1 mark)**
Without harmonic restraint, this inrush current (which can be 6–8 times rated) would be mistaken for an internal fault and cause false tripping. The harmonic restraint feature **adds a restraint quantity proportional to the 2nd harmonic**, desensitising the relay during inrush. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set A]
**Differentiate between 2nd harmonic restraint and 5th harmonic restraint in a transformer differential relay.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | 2nd Harmonic Restraint | 5th Harmonic Restraint |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | 100 Hz (in 50 Hz system) | 250 Hz (in 50 Hz system) |
| **Phenomenon Detected** | Magnetising inrush current | Over-excitation / over-fluxing |
| **Waveform Origin** | Asymmetric half-wave saturation (unipolar pulses) | Symmetric peak clipping (bipolar) |
| **Typical Threshold** | 15–20 % of fundamental | 7–10 % of fundamental |
| **Restraint Coefficient** | $K_2 = 0.15$ – $0.20$ | $K_3 = 0.07$ – $0.10$ |

(1 mark for frequency, 1 mark for phenomenon, 1 mark for threshold — any valid distribution)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Q2(b)]

**(a)** Explain the operational principle of a numerical harmonic restraint differential relay with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the composite trip equation of the relay and explain how it discriminates between (i) magnetising inrush, (ii) over-excitation, and (iii) an internal fault. Use typical numerical values for the restraint coefficients. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Operational Principle (7 marks)

A numerical harmonic restraint differential relay operates on the principle of **spectral decomposition of the differential current** into its fundamental and harmonic components, and using the harmonic content to dynamically modulate the relay's sensitivity. **(1 mark)**

**The signal flow is as follows:**

1. **CTs on both sides** of the protected transformer step down the primary currents to standardised secondary values (1 A or 5 A). **(1 mark)**
2. **Analogue anti-aliasing filters** remove frequency components above half the sampling rate to prevent aliasing. **(0.5 marks)**
3. **16-bit ADCs** sample the CT secondary signals at a typical rate of 16 or 32 samples per cycle (e.g., 800 Hz or 1600 Hz for a 50 Hz system). **(0.5 marks)**
4. A **Full-cycle Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** engine extracts the **fundamental phasor**, the **2nd harmonic magnitude**, and the **5th harmonic magnitude** from each CT branch. **(1 mark)**
5. The **Differential & Bias Calculator** computes the fundamental differential current $I_{d,1}$ and the through (bias) current $I_{bias}$. **(0.5 marks)**
6. The **Harmonic Restraint Builder** forms the composite restraint quantity $I_{rest} = K_1 I_{bias} + K_2 I_{d,2} + K_3 I_{d,5}$. **(0.5 marks)**
7. The **Trip Comparator** checks the condition $I_{d,1} > I_{rest} + I_{min}$. If true *and* the harmonic ratios are below thresholds, a trip command is issued. **(0.5 marks)**
8. The **CT Saturation Detector** provides an additional adaptive restraint for external faults with saturated CTs. **(0.5 marks)**
9. The **Trip Output** drives the breaker trip coil through a lockout relay. **(0.5 marks)**

> **Mark distribution:** 1 + 0.5×7 = 4.5 for block-level + 2.5 for explanation = 7

#### Model Solution — (b) Composite Trip Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Basic biased differential equation:** (1 mark)

$$I_{d,1} > K_1 \cdot I_{bias} + I_{min}$$

**Step 2 — Add harmonic restraint terms:** (1 mark)

$$I_{d,1} > K_1 \cdot I_{bias} + K_2 \cdot I_{d,2} + K_3 \cdot I_{d,5} + I_{min}$$

**Step 3 — Define composite restraint quantity:** (0.5 marks)

$$I_{rest} = K_1 \cdot I_{bias} + K_2 \cdot I_{d,2} + K_3 \cdot I_{d,5}$$

**Step 4 — Final trip criterion:** (0.5 marks)

$$\boxed{I_{d,1} > I_{rest} + I_{min}}$$

**Discrimination logic — typical values:** (4 marks)

| Phenomenon | $I_{d,1}$ (pu) | $I_{bias}$ (pu) | $I_{d,2}$ (pu) | $I_{d,5}$ (pu) | $I_{rest}$ (pu) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Magnetising Inrush | 3.0 | 0.2 | 1.2 | 0.15 | $0.4(0.2)+0.15(1.2)+0.08(0.15)=0.272$ | $I_{d,2}/I_{d,1}=40\% \gg 15\%$ → **Restrained** |
| (ii) Over-excitation | 1.5 | 0.1 | 0.2 | 0.25 | $0.4(0.1)+0.15(0.2)+0.08(0.25)=0.090$ | $I_{d,5}/I_{d,1}=16.7\% \gg 7\%$ → **Restrained** |
| (iii) Internal Fault | 8.0 | 0.3 | 0.4 | 0.2 | $0.4(0.3)+0.15(0.4)+0.08(0.2)=0.196$ | $I_{d,2}/I_{d,1}=5\% < 15\%$; $I_{d,5}/I_{d,1}=2.5\% < 7\%$ → **TRIPS** |

**Conclusion:** The relay uses **K₂·I_{d,2}** to suppress inrush trips and **K₃·I_{d,5}** to suppress over-excitation trips, while remaining highly sensitive to genuine internal faults. **(0 marks extra, included in table values above)**

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q2(a)]

**(a)** With the help of a flowchart, explain the trip/restraining logic of a numerical differential relay with **dual harmonic restraint (2nd + 5th)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 MVA, 33 kV / 11 kV transformer is protected by a harmonic-restraint differential relay with $K_1 = 0.40$, $K_2 = 0.18$, $K_3 = 0.08$, and $I_{min} = 0.25$ pu. During an event, the relay measures: $I_{d,1} = 4.5$ pu, $I_{bias} = 0.35$ pu, $I_{d,2} = 0.30$ pu, $I_{d,5} = 0.10$ pu. Will the relay trip? Justify your answer with full calculations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Flowchart Logic (7 marks)

```
START → Acquire I_P and I_S samples → 
Compute I_d1 (fundamental differential) →
Compute I_bias (through current) →
Compute I_d2 (2nd harmonic magnitude) →
Compute I_d5 (5th harmonic magnitude) →
Form I_rest = K1·I_bias + K2·I_d2 + K3·I_d5 →
Test 1: I_d1 > I_rest + I_min ? 
        → NO → RESTRAIN (block)
        → YES → proceed
Test 2: (I_d2 / I_d1) > 0.15 ?
        → YES → RESTRAIN (inrush detected)
        → NO → proceed
Test 3: (I_d5 / I_d1) > 0.07 ?
        → YES → RESTRAIN (over-excitation detected)
        → NO → TRIP
END (reset and wait for next cycle)
```

**Mark distribution:** 3 marks for the flowchart / pseudo-code, 2 marks for explanation of each test, 2 marks for cross-blocking strategy and the *why* of thresholds.

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** (1 mark for listing all data)

$$I_{d,1} = 4.5 \text{ pu}, \;\; I_{bias} = 0.35 \text{ pu}, \;\; I_{d,2} = 0.30 \text{ pu}, \;\; I_{d,5} = 0.10 \text{ pu}$$

$$K_1 = 0.40, \;\; K_2 = 0.18, \;\; K_3 = 0.08, \;\; I_{min} = 0.25 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 1 — Compute Restraint Quantity:** (2 marks)

$$I_{rest} = (0.40)(0.35) + (0.18)(0.30) + (0.08)(0.10)$$

$$I_{rest} = 0.140 + 0.054 + 0.008 = 0.202 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Trip Threshold:** (1 mark)

$$I_{trip} = I_{rest} + I_{min} = 0.202 + 0.25 = 0.452 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Operating Quantity:** (1 mark)

$$I_{d,1} = 4.5 \text{ pu} \;\; \gg \;\; 0.452 \text{ pu} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Passes Test 1}$$

**Step 4 — Harmonic Ratio Cross-check:** (2 marks)

$$\frac{I_{d,2}}{I_{d,1}} = \frac{0.30}{4.5} = 0.0667 = 6.67\% \;\; < \;\; 15\% \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Passes Test 2}$$

$$\frac{I_{d,5}}{I_{d,1}} = \frac{0.10}{4.5} = 0.0222 = 2.22\% \;\; < \;\; 7\% \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Passes Test 3}$$

**Conclusion:** All three tests are passed → the relay **TRIPS**. **(Bonus 0 marks — content already in steps)** The event is interpreted as a **genuine internal fault** with negligible harmonic content. **(Wrap-up, included in step marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Harmonic Restraint questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the trip and restraint quantities** — A common error is writing $I_{op} > I_{bias}$ (forgetting harmonic terms). You will lose 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Always write `pu` or `A` next to numerical values.
> 3. **Skipping the harmonic-ratio cross-check** — In KTU valuation, the examiner expects to see the *boolean* output of each test, not just the composite equation.
> 4. **Mixing up $I_2$ (CT secondary current of winding 2) with $I_{d,2}$ (2nd harmonic of differential current)** — Use distinct notation: $I_{2\omega}$, $I_{2h}$, or $I_{d,2}$ for the harmonic, and $I_{S,1}$, $I_{P,1}$ for CT primaries.
> 5. **Not stating the typical K₂ and K₃ values** — Even if the question doesn't ask, mentioning the standard ranges (15–20 % and 7–10 %) earns you "extra-information" marks.
> 6. **Drawing a waveform sketch of inrush current** — In 7-mark questions, a small half-wave inrush waveform sketch showing asymmetry earns easy 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** Harmonic restraint relay = numerical differential relay that uses 2nd & 5th harmonic content of differential current to *block* false trips.
- ✅ **2nd Harmonic (100 Hz) → blocks magnetising inrush** (asymmetric half-wave saturation).
- ✅ **5th Harmonic (250 Hz) → blocks over-excitation** (symmetric peak clipping).
- ✅ **Composite trip equation:** $I_{d,1} > K_1 I_{bias} + K_2 I_{d,2} + K_3 I_{d,5} + I_{min}$
- ✅ **Typical K₁ = 0.30–0.50, K₂ = 0.15–0.20, K₃ = 0.07–0.10, I_min = 0.20–0.30 pu**.
- ✅ **2nd harmonic threshold ratio: 15–20 % of fundamental; 5th harmonic threshold: 7–10 %**.
- ✅ **Three-step Boolean decision:** (1) Operating vs. restraint+min check, (2) 2nd harmonic ratio check, (3) 5th harmonic ratio check.
- ✅ **DFT (Discrete Fourier Transform)** is the mathematical engine of harmonic restraint in numerical relays.
- ✅ **CT Saturation Detector** is an *additional* feature that uses per-branch 2nd harmonic to block external faults with saturated CTs.
- ✅ **Application domains:** power transformers (primary), generators, shunt reactors, HVDC converter transformers.
- ✅ **In numerical exam answers, ALWAYS show the full computation** — composite restraint + harmonic ratios + final boolean.
- ✅ **Distinguish notations carefully:** $I_{d,n}$ = differential n-th harmonic; $I_{S,1}$ / $I_{P,1}$ = CT fundamental currents.
- ✅ **Modern relays use "cross-blocking"**: if 2nd harmonic is detected in *any one* phase, all three phases are blocked — improves security but may slightly delay tripping for evolving faults.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Restricted Earth Fault Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Restricted Earth Fault Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Restricted Earth Fault (REF) Protection** is a *unit-type protection scheme* that operates exclusively for earth (ground) faults occurring **within a pre-defined, restricted zone** of a power system element — typically the star-connected winding of a transformer, an alternator stator winding, or a motor winding. The scheme is based on the **Merz-Price circulating-current principle**, in which current transformers (CTs) are installed on **all terminals** supplying the protected winding (line CTs on the three phases **plus** a neutral CT on the earthed neutral).

The adjective *"restricted"* is the key descriptor: unlike Overall Differential Protection which guards the *entire* apparatus between two sets of line CTs, REF Protection deliberately *restricts* its zone of coverage to the **winding between the line CTs and the neutral point**, providing extremely **high sensitivity to internal earth faults** while remaining **completely stable for external faults and phase-to-phase faults** outside the restricted zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEEET631 / Module 2:**
> REF protection is treated as a **specialised, high-impedance differential scheme** derived from the Merz-Price principle. Students must be able to: (a) draw the CT topology for both star- and delta-side applications, (b) explain why a neutral CT is mandatory on the earthed side, and (c) justify why REF cannot operate for a fault *outside* its restricted zone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Guarded Room" Intuition

Imagine a **high-security bank vault** (the restricted zone) protected by:
- **Four motion sensors**: three on the doors leading *out* of the vault (the three phase line CTs), and one on the back emergency exit connected to a hidden tunnel (the neutral CT).

Now consider the security rule:

> *"If the total current flowing INTO the room through all four sensors equals the total current flowing OUT — i.e., a perfect balance — then no crime is happening inside, even if a huge crowd is rushing past the doors."*

In REF protection, **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) is enforced at the neutral/earth point**. Under normal operation or any fault *outside* the protected winding, the sum of all CT secondary currents is **zero** (they merely circulate in the relay loop), so the relay stays inert. But if an earth fault occurs *inside* the protected winding, the neutral CT no longer balances the line CTs — a **net residual current** flows through the relay operating coil and trips the circuit breaker.

This is *why the protection is called "restricted"*: it is **bounded** by the physical position of the CTs. A fault on the transmission line beyond the line CTs is "outside the guarded room" — REF will deliberately ignore it, leaving that job to other protection stages (e.g., distance protection or overcurrent relays).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Stabilising Resistor (Rst)**: typically **10 Ω to 1000 Ω** for high-impedance REF schemes; selected based on through-fault stability calculations.
- **Metrosil / Non-Linear Resistor**: voltage-dependent resistance used across the relay coil to limit peak voltage during high internal faults; characteristic law: **V = K · I^β**, where K ≈ 450 and β ≈ 0.25 for standard Metrosil discs.
- **CT Knee-Point Voltage (Vk)**: critical parameter; must satisfy **Vk ≥ 2 × IFS × (Rct + Rl + Rr)** for stability (where IFS is the maximum symmetrical secondary through-fault current).
- **Relay Operating Voltage (Vop)**: typically **1.5 V to 15 V** for electromechanical REF relays; modern numerical relays use settings in **per-unit of CT rated secondary current (1 A or 5 A)**.
- **Reference Standard**: IEEE C37.91-2008 (Guide for Protective Relay Applications to Power Transformers) and IEC 60255-118 (Numerical Busbar Protection — closely related architecture).

> [!NOTE]
> The **CT polarity convention** is critical in REF schemes. KTU examiners frequently test whether the student has marked the dotted ends of the CTs correctly — a polarity reversal silently disables the scheme.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Merz-Price Circulating-Current Logic

The REF scheme is a **direct application of Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the neutral node** of the protected winding. The CT topology is engineered so that under *any* condition external to the restricted zone, the secondary currents form a closed circulating loop and the **differential (operating) current remains zero**.

#### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

**Step 1 — CT Placement**
Three **line CTs** (say CT-A, CT-B, CT-C) are mounted on the three phase terminals of the protected winding. A fourth CT — the **Neutral CT (CT-N)** — is mounted on the connection between the winding star-point and the earthing electrode.

**Step 2 — Secondary Interconnection**
All four CT secondaries are paralleled. The line CT secondaries are connected with a defined polarity (dot convention), and the neutral CT is connected with **opposite polarity** relative to the line CTs. This is the hallmark of circulating-current differential protection.

**Step 3 — Balanced / Through-Fault Condition (Stability)**
Under normal load, or for an external phase-to-phase fault, equal currents flow in the line CTs and an equal-and-opposite current flows in the neutral CT (because whatever enters the protected zone through the phases must leave through the neutral). Their secondary phasors cancel exactly at the relay junction.

$$\bar{I}_{\text{diff}} = \bar{I}_{a} + \bar{I}_{b} + \bar{I}_{c} - \bar{I}_{n} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Internal Earth Fault Condition (Operation)**
When an earth fault occurs on the protected winding, fault current flows from the system through **two of the three line CTs** (and possibly the third) **to the fault point**, and returns through the **earthing path** — which is NOT through the neutral CT for *all* fault positions. Therefore, the neutral CT no longer balances the line CTs, and a **non-zero spill current** energises the relay.

$$\bar{I}_{\text{diff}} = \bar{I}_{a} + \bar{I}_{b} + \bar{I}_{c} - \bar{I}_{n} \neq 0$$

**Step 5 — Trip Decision**
Once $I_{\text{diff}}$ exceeds the relay setting (and any biased threshold is met), the trip command is issued to the circuit breaker on the protected side.

### 2.2 Why the Zone is "Restricted" — The CT Topology Argument

| Protection Type | Zone Covered | CTs Required | Sensitivity to Internal Earth Faults |
|-----------------|--------------|--------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Overall Differential** | Whole transformer (HV + LV windings) | 3 HV + 3 LV CTs | Moderate — limited by magnetising inrush and CT saturation |
| **REF (HV side)** | HV winding between line CTs and neutral | 3 line + 1 neutral CT | **Very High** — operates for faults as low as ~5–10 % of winding |
| **REF (LV side)** | LV winding (delta side requires special connection) | 3 line + auxiliary CTs | High on delta with zig-zag or auxiliary CTs |

### 2.3 High-Impedance REF Scheme — Stability Theory

The **high-impedance** variant is the *de facto* industry standard because it **forces the CTs into saturation during external faults**, thereby preventing maloperation due to CT ratio errors. A stabilising resistor **Rst** is connected in series with the relay operating coil.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|--------|----------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| $I_{\text{diff}}$ | Differential (spill) current | $I_{\text{diff}} = \left\vert \bar{I}_{A} + \bar{I}_{B} + \bar{I}_{C} - \bar{I}_{N} \right\vert$ | A (secondary) | Must equal zero for stability |
| $V_{\text{relay}}$ | Voltage across relay coil | $V_{\text{relay}} = I_{\text{diff}} \cdot (R_{\text{relay}} + R_{\text{st}})$ | V | For high-impedance REF |
| $V_{\text{knee}}$ | Required CT knee-point voltage | $V_{\text{knee}} \geq 2 \cdot I_{\text{F,sym}} \cdot (R_{\text{ct}} + R_{\text{l}} + R_{\text{r}})$ | V | Stability criterion |
| $I_{\text{F,sym}}$ | Max. symmetrical through-fault current (secondary) | $I_{\text{F,sym}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{F,primary}}}{N_{\text{CT}}}$ | A | Compute from primary fault level |
| $R_{\text{st,min}}$ | Minimum stabilising resistance | $R_{\text{st,min}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{knee}}}{2 \cdot I_{\text{F,sym}}} - (R_{\text{ct}} + R_{\text{l}})$ | Ω | Conservative estimate |
| $V_{\text{pk}}$ | Peak voltage across CT secondary during internal fault | $V_{\text{pk}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{\text{F,sym}} \cdot (R_{\text{ct}} + R_{\text{l}} + R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}})$ | V | Must be < CT insulation rating |
| $V_{\text{metrosil}}$ | Metrosil non-linear voltage | $V = K \cdot I^{\beta}$ | V | $K \approx 450$, $\beta \approx 0.25$ |
| $I_{\text{op,min}}$ | Minimum operating current | $I_{\text{op,min}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{op}}}{R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}}}$ | A | Sensitivity setting |
| $\% \text{Winding protected}$ | Percentage of winding covered | Depends on fault position; 100 % if neutral CT present | % | REF covers 100 % of winding on earthed side |
| $I_{0}$ | Zero-sequence (residual) current | $I_{0} = \dfrac{I_{A} + I_{B} + I_{C}}{3}$ | A | Equivalent phasor quantity |
| $I_{\text{CT,ratio}}$ | CT transformation ratio | $N = \dfrac{I_{\text{primary}}}{I_{\text{secondary}}}$ | — | Typically 100/1, 200/1, 2000/1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall Avoidance:** Never write the absolute value expression with the literal `|` character inside a markdown table — it breaks the table renderer. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ (LaTeX math delimiters) instead.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Transformers**: REF is universally fitted on the **HV star winding** of generator step-up transformers (GSUTs) and grid transformers, providing **100 % winding protection** for earth faults — far superior to Buchholz relay (which detects only severe, slow-developing faults).
- **Hydro & Thermal Generators**: REF on the stator winding is more sensitive than overall differential for earth faults near the neutral end.
- **Large Industrial Motors (>1 MW)**: REF protects stator windings against earth faults, preventing catastrophic iron damage from prolonged arcing.
- **Unit-Connected Generator Systems**: REF + Overall Differential + 100 % Stator Earth Fault Protection using a low-frequency injection source form the triple-redundant earth-fault protection chain mandated by IEEE C37.101.

---

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stability Criterion for High-Impedance REF

**Objective:** Derive the minimum CT knee-point voltage required to ensure stability of a high-impedance REF scheme during the worst-case external (through-zone) fault.

**Given Data:**
- Maximum primary through-fault current (external fault): $I_{\text{F,prim}}$
- CT ratio: $N_{\text{CT}}$
- CT secondary winding resistance: $R_{\text{ct}}$
- Lead-loop resistance (CT to relay): $R_{\text{l}}$
- Relay coil resistance: $R_{\text{r}}$
- Stabilising resistance: $R_{\text{st}}$

**Step 1 — Compute the secondary through-fault current**

Because the external fault is *outside* the protected zone, all the fault current passes through the line CTs but is fully balanced by the neutral CT (since the protected winding itself is healthy and intact). The secondary current in each CT is:

$$
I_{\text{F,sym}} = \frac{I_{\text{F,prim}}}{N_{\text{CT}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the worst-case spill current**

In practice, CTs are *not perfectly identical*. Manufacturing tolerances, magnetising current differences, and ratio errors cause a small mismatch current to flow in the relay even during external faults. The conservative assumption is that one CT is fully saturated while the other is unsaturated, leading to a spill current of:

$$
I_{\text{spill}} \approx I_{\text{F,sym}}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage developed across the relay branch**

The relay operating coil is in series with $R_{\text{st}}$. The voltage that appears across the relay branch (assuming worst-case CT mismatch) is:

$$
V_{\text{relay}} = I_{\text{F,sym}} \cdot (R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}})
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the CT stability rule**

To keep the CTs from saturating severely (which would defeat stability), the voltage that the CT secondary can support without saturating — the **knee-point voltage** $V_{\text{knee}}$ — must exceed twice the maximum voltage that can appear across the CT secondary loop during an external fault:

$$
V_{\text{knee}} \geq 2 \cdot V_{\text{relay,external}} = 2 \cdot I_{\text{F,sym}} \cdot (R_{\text{ct}} + R_{\text{l}} + R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}})
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R_{\text{st,min}}$**

Rearranging for the minimum stabilising resistance:

$$
R_{\text{st,min}} = \frac{V_{\text{knee}}}{2 \cdot I_{\text{F,sym}}} - (R_{\text{ct}} + R_{\text{l}} + R_{\text{r}})
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A larger $R_{\text{st}}$ improves external-fault stability but reduces sensitivity to internal faults. The Metrosil is then used to *limit* the voltage developed across $R_{\text{st}}$ during heavy internal faults, preventing insulation breakdown of the CT secondary wiring.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensitivity Setting (Minimum Internal Fault Current Detected)

**Objective:** Find the smallest internal earth-fault current (as a percentage of rated winding current) that the REF scheme can detect.

**Given Data:**
- Relay operating voltage: $V_{\text{op}}$
- Stabilising resistance: $R_{\text{st}}$
- Relay coil resistance: $R_{\text{r}}$
- CT ratio: $N_{\text{CT}}$
- Rated primary winding current: $I_{\text{rated}}$

**Step 1 — Compute the relay branch resistance**

$$
R_{\text{branch}} = R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the secondary operating current**

The relay will operate when the spill current produces a voltage equal to or greater than $V_{\text{op}}$:

$$
I_{\text{op,sec}} = \frac{V_{\text{op}}}{R_{\text{branch}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Refer to primary side**

$$
I_{\text{op,prim}} = I_{\text{op,sec}} \cdot N_{\text{CT}} = \frac{V_{\text{op}} \cdot N_{\text{CT}}}{R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage of rated winding current**

$$
\% \text{ sensitivity} = \left( \frac{I_{\text{op,prim}}}{I_{\text{rated}}} \right) \times 100
$$

> [!TIP]
> Typical REF schemes achieve **sensitivity of 5 % to 15 %** of rated winding current — this is why REF is so valued for detecting **incipient faults** (incipient = beginning/developing) that other protections miss.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical Problem)

**Problem Statement:**

A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 11 kV / 132 kV, 30 MVA power transformer is star-connected on the HV side with the neutral solidly earthed. An REF protection scheme is provided on the HV winding. Given:

- CT ratio on HV side: $400 / 1$ A
- CT secondary winding resistance: $R_{\text{ct}} = 0.5 \, \Omega$
- Lead-loop resistance: $R_{\text{l}} = 0.3 \, \Omega$
- Stabilising resistance: $R_{\text{st}} = 50 \, \Omega$
- Relay coil resistance: $R_{\text{r}} = 1.0 \, \Omega$
- Relay operating voltage: $V_{\text{op}} = 1.5 \, \text{V}$
- Maximum external through-fault current (primary): $I_{\text{F,prim}} = 4000 \, \text{A}$
- CT knee-point voltage: $V_{\text{knee}} = 200 \, \text{V}$

**Calculate:**
1. The secondary through-fault current.
2. The maximum voltage that can appear across the relay branch during an external fault.
3. The minimum knee-point voltage required for stability. Comment on adequacy.
4. The minimum primary operating current (sensitivity).

---

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Secondary through-fault current**

$$
I_{\text{F,sym}} = \frac{I_{\text{F,prim}}}{N_{\text{CT}}} = \frac{4000}{400} = 10 \, \text{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage across relay branch during external fault**

$$
V_{\text{relay,ext}} = I_{\text{F,sym}} \cdot (R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}}) = 10 \times (50 + 1.0) = 10 \times 51 = 510 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Required minimum knee-point voltage**

$$
V_{\text{knee,min}} = 2 \cdot I_{\text{F,sym}} \cdot (R_{\text{ct}} + R_{\text{l}} + R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}})
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_{\text{knee,min}} = 2 \times 10 \times (0.5 + 0.3 + 50 + 1.0) = 20 \times 51.8 = 1036 \, \text{V}
$$

**Comment:** The available knee-point voltage is only **200 V**, which is **far less than the required 1036 V**. This indicates the chosen CTs are **inadequate** for this scheme. Either:
- Use CTs with $V_{\text{knee}} \geq 1100 \, \text{V}$ (preferred), OR
- Reduce $R_{\text{st}}$ (compromises stability), OR
- Use a lower-ratio CT (but then sensitivity is reduced).

**Step 4 — Minimum primary operating current**

First, secondary operating current:

$$
I_{\text{op,sec}} = \frac{V_{\text{op}}}{R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}}} = \frac{1.5}{51} \approx 0.0294 \, \text{A}
$$

Then, refer to primary:

$$
I_{\text{op,prim}} = 0.0294 \times 400 = 11.76 \, \text{A}
$$

The rated HV current of the 30 MVA / 132 kV transformer is:

$$
I_{\text{rated}} = \frac{30 \times 10^{6}}{\sqrt{3} \times 132 \times 10^{3}} \approx 131.2 \, \text{A}
$$

Sensitivity percentage:

$$
\% \text{ sensitivity} = \left( \frac{11.76}{131.2} \right) \times 100 \approx 8.96 \%
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The REF scheme can detect an earth fault as low as **~9 % of rated winding current**, equivalent to roughly **1 kA of primary fault current** at the HV terminals — exceptionally sensitive.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — REF Scheme Behaviour for Different Fault Types

| Fault Type | Location | $I_{\text{line CTs}}$ | $I_{\text{neutral CT}}$ | Spill Current $I_{\text{diff}}$ | REF Operates? |
|-----------|----------|-----------------------|-------------------------|---------------------------------|---------------|
| Normal Load | — | $I_{A}, I_{B}, I_{C}$ | $-(I_{A}+I_{B}+I_{C})$ | $\approx 0$ | No |
| External 3-φ fault | Outside zone | High | Equal & opposite | $\approx 0$ (limited by CT errors) | No (stable) |
| External Phase-Phase fault | Outside zone | High (2 phases) | Equal & opposite | $\approx 0$ | No |
| Internal Earth Fault (HV winding, near line) | Inside zone | High (fault path via 2 line CTs) | Partial | High | **YES** |
| Internal Earth Fault (HV winding, near neutral) | Inside zone | Low (limited by winding impedance to neutral) | Low (or zero, if fault is right at neutral) | Low but distinguishable | **YES** |
| Phase-to-Phase Fault (HV winding, no earth) | Inside zone | High in 2 phases | Same as load | $\approx 0$ | **No** (correctly does not operate) |
| Magnetising Inrush | Switching event | High but 2nd-harmonic rich | Equal & opposite | $\approx 0$ | No |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Power System Energised]) --> MEASURE[Sample CT Secondary Currents IA, IB, IC, IN]
    MEASURE --> KCL{Check Kirchhoff Sum: IA + IB + IC - IN}
    KCL -->|Sum approx 0| STABLE[State: STABLE / Healthy<br>No Trip]
    KCL -->|Sum exceeds setting| BIAS{Apply Bias / Restraint Check}

    BIAS -->|Outside bias curve| FAULT[Declare: Internal Earth Fault]

Missing any of these is the single biggest reason students lose 1–2 marks on REF diagram questions.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**  
Define *Restricted Earth Fault (REF) Protection*. Why is it called "restricted," and what is the role of the neutral CT in the scheme?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** REF protection is a unit-type differential scheme that detects earth faults within a **restricted zone** — typically the winding between the line CTs and the earthed neutral of a transformer or generator.

**"Restricted" — Why (1 Mark):** The zone of coverage is deliberately *bounded* by the physical location of the CTs. The scheme does NOT cover the entire apparatus (unlike overall differential protection); it covers only the winding between the line CTs and the neutral point.

**Role of Neutral CT (1 Mark):** The neutral CT is mounted on the connection between the star-point and the earthing electrode. During normal operation or external faults, it carries the vector sum of the three phase currents and balances the line CTs (so the relay sees zero spill current). For an internal earth fault, the neutral CT current no longer matches the line CT phasors, producing a net spill current that operates the relay.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**  
List **three advantages** and **two limitations** of high-impedance REF protection compared to simple overcurrent earth-fault protection.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **100 % winding protection** — detects earth faults anywhere along the protected winding, including those very close to the neutral point (which would be invisible to overcurrent relays).
2. **Extremely fast operation** (typically 30–50 ms) due to the high operating voltage developed across the stabilising resistor.
3. **Inherently stable for external faults** because the high impedance forces CT saturation to limit the spill current.

**Limitations:**
1. Requires **CTs with high knee-point voltage** — increasing cost.
2. **Cannot detect phase-to-phase faults** (only earth faults) — needs to be supplemented with overall differential protection.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat single-line diagram, explain the **working principle of Restricted Earth Fault Protection** for a star-connected, solidly-earthed HV winding of a power transformer. Clearly indicate the polarity of all CTs and explain the relay behaviour for:
   - (i) Normal load condition
   - (ii) External (through-zone) fault
   - (iii) Internal earth fault near the line end
   - (iv) Internal earth fault near the neutral end

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a 25 MVA, 11 kV / 66 kV star-delta transformer with a solidly-earthed 66 kV star winding, the following REF scheme parameters are given:

- HV-side CT ratio: **300 / 1 A**
- CT secondary resistance: $R_{\text{ct}} = 0.4 \, \Omega$
- Lead resistance: $R_{\text{l}} = 0.2 \, \Omega$
- Stabilising resistance: $R_{\text{st}} = 100 \, \Omega$
- Relay coil resistance: $R_{\text{r}} = 0.8 \, \Omega$
- Relay operating voltage: $V_{\text{op}} = 2.0 \, \text{V}$
- Maximum external through-fault current: $I_{\text{F,prim}} = 3000 \, \text{A}$
- CT knee-point voltage: $V_{\text{knee}} = 300 \, \text{V}$

Determine:
   1. The secondary through-fault current.
   2. The required minimum knee-point voltage for stability. Is the available CT adequate?
   3. The minimum primary operating current (sensitivity) in amperes and as a percentage of rated winding current.

---

### Model Answer — Question A

**Part (a) — Working Principle [7 Marks]**

**Diagram (3 Marks):** Draw the single-line schematic showing:

- Source connected to HV bus
- Three line CTs (CT-A, CT-B, CT-C) on phase terminals entering the HV winding
- Star-point of winding solidly earthed through a Neutral CT (CT-N)
- CT secondaries paralleled with proper polarities (line CTs dotted towards the winding; neutral CT dotted away from the winding, i.e., towards the earth)
- Stabilising resistor Rst and REF operating relay coil in series across the paralleled secondaries
- Metrosil across the relay coil for over-voltage protection

**Explanation (4 Marks):**

**(i) Normal Load (1 Mark):** The three phase currents enter the line CTs and sum to zero (in balanced conditions) — their secondary currents $I_{A}, I_{B}, I_{C}$ flow in one direction. The same phasor sum returns through the neutral CT in the *opposite* direction. The spill current $I_{A} + I_{B} + I_{C} - I_{N} \approx 0$, so the relay remains stable.

**(ii) External Through-Fault (1 Mark):** During a fault outside the restricted zone (e.g., on the 66 kV bus or external line), large currents flow through the line CTs but are returned via the neutral CT — the protected winding itself is intact. The high-impedance stabilising resistor keeps the spill current below the relay operating threshold, and any residual mismatch (due to CT errors) is absorbed by Rst. The relay remains stable.

**(iii) Internal Earth Fault Near Line End (1 Mark):** Significant fault current flows from the source through TWO (or all three) line CTs into the fault. The neutral CT sees *no part* of this current because the fault current returns via earth *before* reaching the neutral point. The spill current is large and exceeds the relay setting — **trip command issued within 30–40 ms**.

**(iv) Internal Earth Fault Near Neutral End (1 Mark):** Even here, the fault current must flow through the line CTs to reach the fault (since the line CTs are at the line terminals). The neutral CT may see only a small fraction (or none, if the fault is right at the neutral), so the spill current is non-zero and trips the relay. **This is the key advantage** of REF — it covers faults near the neutral that other protections miss.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Secondary Through-Fault Current:**

$$
I_{\text{F,sym}} = \frac{I_{\text{F,prim}}}{N_{\text{CT}}} = \frac{3000}{300} = 10 \, \text{A}
$$

**Step 2 (3 Marks) — Required Knee-Point Voltage:**

$$
V_{\text{knee,min}} = 2 \cdot I_{\text{F,sym}} \cdot (R_{\text{ct}} + R_{\text{l}} + R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}})
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_{\text{knee,min}} = 2 \times 10 \times (0.4 + 0.2 + 100 + 0.8) = 20 \times 101.4 = 2028 \, \text{V}
$$

**Comment (1 Mark):** The available knee-point voltage of **300 V is grossly inadequate**. The CTs will saturate heavily during external faults and the scheme will **maloperate**. The CT must be replaced with one having $V_{\text{knee}} \geq 2100 \, \text{V}$, or $R_{\text{st}}$ must be reduced.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Sensitivity:**

$$
I_{\text{op,sec}} = \frac{V_{\text{op}}}{R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}}} = \frac{2.0}{100.8} = 0.01984 \, \text{A}
$$

$$
I_{\text{op,prim}} = 0.01984 \times 300 = 5.95 \, \text{A}
$$

Rated HV current:

$$
I_{\text{rated}} = \frac{25 \times 10^{6}}{\sqrt{3} \times 66 \times 10^{3}} \approx 218.7 \, \text{A}
$$

Sensitivity:

$$
\% \text{ sensitivity} = \left( \frac{5.95}{218.7} \right) \times 100 \approx 2.72 \%
$$

**Interpretation:** Excellent sensitivity — the scheme can detect an earth fault current as low as **~2.7 % of rated current**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **Overall Differential Protection** with **Restricted Earth Fault Protection**. Under what circumstances is REF preferred over overall differential? List three practical situations where REF is mandatory.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **role of the stabilising resistor and the Metrosil** in a high-impedance REF scheme. Why is the Metrosil's voltage-current characteristic **non-linear ($V = K I^\beta$)**, and what would happen if it were replaced by a linear resistor of equivalent resistance?

---

### Model Answer — Question B

**Part (a) — Comparison [7 Marks]**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Feature | Overall Differential | REF |
|---------|---------------------|-----|
| CTs required | 6 (3 HV + 3 LV) | 4 (3 line + 1 neutral) |
| Zone covered | Entire transformer | Restricted winding only |
| Sensitivity to in-zone earth faults | Moderate | Very high |
| Detects phase-to-phase faults | Yes | No (earth faults only) |
| Stability against magnetising inrush | Requires 2nd-harmonic restraint | Naturally stable |
| Cost | Higher (6 CTs) | Lower (4 CTs) |

**When REF is Preferred (2 Marks):**
- When high sensitivity to low-magnitude earth faults is needed
- When 100 % winding coverage on the earthed side is mandatory
- When cost of CTs is a constraint

**Three Mandatory Applications (1 Mark):**
1. HV winding of generator step-up transformers (GSUTs)
2. Stator windings of large generators (>100 MVA)
3. HV windings of grid transformers with solidly-earthed neutrals

---

**Part (b) — Role of Rst and Metrosil [7 Marks]**

**Stabilising Resistor Rst (2 Marks):** Placed in series with the relay coil, Rst limits the spill current during external faults caused by CT mismatch. By making the relay branch highly resistive, it forces CT saturation to dominate the loop voltage, preventing false trips.

**Metrosil (Varistor) Role (2 Marks):** During heavy internal faults, the voltage across the CT secondary loop can reach **kV levels** (potentially damaging CT insulation and relay). The Metrosil is a **non-linear resistor** connected in parallel with the relay coil; it conducts only when the voltage exceeds a threshold, thereby *clamping* the voltage to a safe value.

**Why Non-Linear (V = K I^β) (2 Marks):** The non-linear characteristic means the Metrosil has *very high resistance at low voltages* (does not interfere with normal relay operation) and *very low resistance at high voltages* (absorbs excess energy during internal faults). This dual behaviour is impossible to achieve with a single linear resistor.

**Consequence of Replacing Metrosil with Linear Resistor (1 Mark):** A linear resistor would either (a) be too low in value, diverting operating current and reducing sensitivity, or (b) be too high in value, failing to clamp the voltage during faults. The scheme would either become **insensitive** or **unsafe** to insulation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in REF Protection Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong CT Polarity (–2 marks):** The neutral CT must be connected with **opposite polarity** to the line CTs. Students frequently draw all four CTs with the same dot orientation, which makes the scheme inactive. Always remember: *line CTs dotted towards the protected winding; neutral CT dotted away from it*.
>
> 2. **Confusing REF with Standby Earth Fault (–1 to 2 marks):** REF is a **unit protection** (operates only for faults inside its zone). Standby Earth Fault (SBEF) is a **non-unit backup** that operates for any earth fault on the protected side. Many students mix the two.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the KCL Sign Convention (–1 mark):** The differential current is $I_{A} + I_{B} + I_{C} - I_{N}$, *not* $I_{A} + I_{B} + I_{C} + I_{N}$. The negative sign for $I_N$ is essential.
>
> 4. **Not Computing the Knee-Point Check (–1 mark):** KTU examiners specifically test whether students verify that $V_{\text{knee}}$ is adequate. Skipping this stability check loses at least 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Missing Units in Final Answer (–0.5 mark):** Always quote the secondary current in **A**, the primary current in **A or kA**, and the sensitivity as a **percentage**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **REF = Restricted Earth Fault Protection** — a *unit-type*, *circulating-current*, *Merz-Price*-based differential scheme.
- **Zone of operation** is bounded by the line CTs on one side and the neutral CT on the other side.
- **CT topology**: 3 line CTs on phases + 1 neutral CT on the earthed neutral connection (4 CTs total).
- **Polarity**: Line CTs dotted *towards* the winding; Neutral CT dotted *away* from the winding (opposite to line CTs in the circulating loop).
- **Neutral CT is mandatory** for the KCL balance — its absence makes the scheme unstable.
- **High-impedance variant** uses a stabilising resistor $R_{\text{st}}$ (typically **10 Ω to 1000 Ω**) in series with the relay coil.
- **Stability criterion**: $V_{\text{knee}} \geq 2 \cdot I_{\text{F,sym}} \cdot (R_{\text{ct}} + R_{\text{l}} + R_{\text{st}} + R_{\text{r}})$.
- **Metrosil (V = K·I^β)** clamps the CT secondary voltage during heavy internal faults; its **non-linear characteristic** is essential — do not replace it with a linear resistor.
- **Sensitivity**: REF schemes typically achieve **2 % to 15 % of rated winding current** — far superior to overall differential.
- **Operates for**: internal earth faults only.
- **Does NOT operate for**: external faults, phase-to-phase faults, or magnetising inrush.
- **100 % winding coverage** is achieved on the earthed side — this is REF's signature advantage.
- **Applications**: GSUT HV windings, large generator stators, large motors, grid transformers.
- **Reference standards**: IEEE C37.91-2008, IEC 60255-118.
- **Formula to remember**: $R_{\text{st,min}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{knee}}}{2 \cdot I_{\text{F,sym}}} - (R_{\text{ct}} + R_{\text{l}} + R_{\text{r}})$.
- **Modern numerical REF relays** use the same KCL logic but implement the stabilising resistor and Metrosil virtually in firmware, with settings entered in per-unit of rated CT secondary current.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frame Leakage Protection
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frame Leakage Protection

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Frame Leakage Protection** (also widely known as **Frame Earth Protection** or **Frame Ground Protection**) is a sensitive, high-speed earth-fault protection scheme used primarily to safeguard the **stator windings of large rotating AC machines** — particularly **turbo-alternators (generators)** and large **HV motors** — against incipient (developing) winding-to-frame insulation breakdowns.

The scheme operates on the principle of detecting **zero-sequence (residual) currents** flowing through the **single grounding conductor** that bonds the machine frame (casing) to the station earth mat.
