This is achieved by enclosing the frame-to-earth conductor inside a **Core Balance Current Transformer (CBCT)**, also called a **Zero-Sequence CT (ZSCT) or Window CT**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
> Frame Leakage Protection is categorized under *machine protection schemes* and is rated as a **HIGH-YIELD topic** for ESE questions because it is frequently combined with questions on Earth Fault Relays, Restricted Earth Fault (REF), and Stator Earth Fault Protection. A 14-mark descriptive question is highly probable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Mapping:**
> - Frame Leakage Protection = Frame Earth Protection = Casing Earth Protection = Frame Ground Protection.
> - All four terms refer to the **identical scheme** and are interchangeable in KTU answer scripts.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **large water tank** resting on a metal stand, with water inside. Now suppose the tank develops a **tiny crack** (an insulation failure) and water starts dripping onto the metal stand.

- The metal stand is the **machine frame**.
- The dripping water is the **leakage current** from the faulted winding.
- The stand is connected to the ground through a **single drainage pipe** (the frame earthing conductor).

Now, if you wrap a **flow sensor (the CBCT)** around that single drainage pipe, the sensor will detect the moment even a **single drop** of water flows down it — long before the tank bursts catastrophically. That is exactly how Frame Leakage Protection works: it acts as an **ultra-sensitive "leak detector"** for fault current that escapes the winding insulation and tries to reach earth via the machine frame.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a CBCT and not a conventional CT?**
> Because the fault current inside a 3-phase machine **circulates between phases** during phase-to-phase faults and produces **no net sum** through the frame. Only **earth faults** (winding-to-frame) send current down the single grounding conductor — and a CBCT is the most sensitive way to detect this tiny, unbalanced residual current.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Operating Current Setting:** Typically **5 % to 10 % of the rated machine full-load current**, often as low as **5 A** primary for large generators.
- **Stabilizing Resistor:** **≈ 10 Ω** inserted in the CBCT secondary loop to prevent false tripping due to external capacitive transients.
- **Primary Operating Current (typical):** **20 A to 60 A** (for alternators of 50 MW–500 MW class).
- **Operating Time:** **< 100 ms** (high-speed).
- **CBCT Transformation Ratio:** e.g., **100/5 A**, **200/5 A**, depending on frame size.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Healthy Condition (No Fault):**
   - The 3-phase stator winding is balanced. **No current** flows from the frame to earth.
   - The instantaneous sum of three phase currents is zero: $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$.
   - Hence the **secondary output of the CBCT is zero**, and the relay remains inoperative.

2. **Incipient Stator Earth Fault (Winding-to-Frame):**
   - Insulation between a stator conductor and the laminated core (frame) breaks down.
   - A small **unidirectional fault current** $I_f$ flows from the fault point → through the laminations (frame) → through the **single earth conductor** → to the station earth mat.
   - This current is a **zero-sequence current** in nature, since the only path it takes is to ground.

3. **CBCT Sensing:**
   - The CBCT encircles **only the single frame-to-earth conductor**.
   - The CBCT secondary produces a proportional EMF according to:
$$e_{sec} = N_2 \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$
   - This secondary voltage drives a current through the **stabilizing resistor + relay operating coil**.

4. **Relay Decision:**
   - If the secondary current exceeds the **pick-up setting**, the relay operates and **trips the machine circuit breaker (MCB)**.

5. **Auxiliary Tripping:**
   - In many schemes, the relay also initiates **field breaker tripping**, **turbine trip**, and **annunciation**.

## 2.2 Why "Frame Leakage" is More Sensitive than Conventional Earth-Fault Protection

| Aspect | Conventional Earth-Fault Relay | Frame Leakage Protection |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing CT location | In each phase | Around the **single frame earth conductor** |
| Fault current magnitude | Limited by neutral earthing impedance | Can detect **very small** leakage currents |
| Sensitivity | Moderate (10–20 % of FLC) | **Very high (5 % of FLC or less)** |
| Fault discrimination | Cannot distinguish internal vs. external | Detects **only internal winding-to-frame** faults |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Zero-sequence current | $I_0 = \dfrac{I_a + I_b + I_c}{3}$ | Sum of unbalanced phase currents | A |
| 2 | CT secondary EMF | $e_{sec} = N_2 \cdot \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$ | Induced EMF in CBCT secondary | V |
| 3 | CBCT secondary current | $I_{sec} = \dfrac{N_1 \cdot I_{pri}}{N_2}$ | Transformation ratio | A |
| 4 | Stabilizing resistor | $R_{stab} \approx 10 \ \Omega$ | Prevents false tripping on transients | Ω |
| 5 | Pickup current (typical) | $I_{pickup} = (0.05 \ \text{to} \ 0.10) \cdot I_{FLC}$ | 5 % – 10 % of full-load current | A |
| 6 | Voltage across stabilizing resistor | $V_R = I_{sec} \cdot R_{stab}$ | Used to drive the relay | V |
| 7 | CT knee-point voltage | $V_k \geq 2 \cdot I_{sec} \cdot (R_{stab} + R_{relay})$ | Ensures linear operation | V |
| 8 | Effective primary operating current | $I_{op,prim} = \dfrac{I_{pickup,sec}}{CT \ Ratio}$ | Converted back to primary | A |
| 9 | Burden of CBCT | $B = I_{sec}^2 \cdot Z_{burden}$ | Loading on the CT | VA |
| 10 | Burden voltage | $V_b = I_{sec} \cdot (R_{stab} + R_{relay} + R_{leads})$ | Total voltage drop | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** All absolute values or "magnitude of" expressions use the LaTeX `\vert` (e.g., $\vert I_a \vert$) so as **not to break the markdown table pipe syntax**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Generator Protection:** Protects the **stator winding** of large hydro/thermal turbo-generators. Catches **incipient faults** that would otherwise go unnoticed until catastrophic failure.
- **HV Motor Protection:** Prevents burn-out of cage-induction motors driving critical auxiliaries (boiler feed pumps, ID/FD fans).
- **Power Plant Safety:** Reduces downtime, prevents **rewinding costs** (which run into crores for a 250 MW generator), and avoids costly forced outages.
- **Integration with Numerical Relays:** Modern **digital/numerical relays (e.g., REF615, MICOM P436, P841)** have built-in frame-leakage (51G/51N) functions — making the concept directly relevant to **IEC 61850-based** digital substations studied in Module 2.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Setting Calculation for a Frame Leakage Relay

**Given (KTU-style problem data):**
A **50 MVA, 11 kV, 3-phase, 50 Hz** turbo-alternator has its frame earthed through a single conductor. A CBCT of ratio **400/5 A** is used. The relay pickup is set at **5 % of full-load current**, and the stabilizing resistor is **10 Ω**. The relay coil resistance is **0.5 Ω**, and lead resistance is **0.3 Ω**.

**Step 1 — Compute the rated full-load current of the machine:**

$$I_{FLC} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}$$

$$I_{FLC} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3}$$

$$I_{FLC} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{1.732 \times 11 \times 10^3} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{19.052 \times 10^3}$$

$$I_{FLC} = 2624.3 \ \text{A (primary)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the primary pickup current at 5 % of FLC:**

$$I_{pickup,prim} = 0.05 \times 2624.3 = 131.2 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to secondary current using the CT ratio:**

$$I_{pickup,sec} = I_{pickup,prim} \times \frac{5}{400} = 131.2 \times 0.0125$$

$$I_{pickup,sec} = 1.64 \ \text{A (secondary)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the voltage developed across the stabilizing resistor + relay circuit:**

$$V_{op} = I_{pickup,sec} \times (R_{stab} + R_{relay} + R_{leads})$$

$$V_{op} = 1.64 \times (10 + 0.5 + 0.3) = 1.64 \times 10.8$$

$$V_{op} = 17.71 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Verify CT knee-point voltage requirement:**

The CT must have a knee-point voltage:

$$V_k \geq 2 \times V_{op} = 2 \times 17.71 = 35.42 \ \text{V}$$

**Result:** A CBCT with $V_k \geq 36 \ \text{V}$ and ratio **400/5 A** is suitable for protecting the 50 MVA machine frame.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stability Against External Transients

**Why is the stabilizing resistor $R_{stab}$ needed?**
During external switching (e.g., line switching, lightning, capacitor bank energization), high-frequency zero-sequence voltages appear across the CBCT secondary. Without $R_{stab}$, these could cause **false tripping**.

**Equivalent circuit during a transient:**

$$V_{transient} = L_{leak} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$

The CBCT secondary sees this as a high $\dfrac{di}{dt}$ transient. The stabilizing resistor **damps** the secondary circuit by making it more **resistive**, raising the damping ratio:

$$\zeta = \frac{R_{stab}}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_{sec}}{L_{leak}}}$$

For **critical damping** ($\zeta = 1$):

$$R_{stab} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_{leak}}{C_{sec}}}$$

In practice, a **fixed $R_{stab} \approx 10 \ \Omega$** is selected by relay manufacturers after empirical testing.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Related Schemes

| Parameter | Frame Leakage | Restricted Earth Fault (REF) | Standby Earth Fault (SBEF) | Differential (87G) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Element | CBCT on frame earth | Neutral CT + phase CTs | Neutral CT + earth CT | Phase CTs at both ends |
| Coverage Zone | 100 % of stator winding | 100 % of winding (between CTs) | 100 % of winding | 100 % of winding |
| Sensitivity | **Very High** (5–10 % FLC) | **High** | Moderate | **Very High** |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | Low | High |
| Coordination with other schemes | Independent | Independent | Back-up | Primary |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common 14-mark question asks students to **compare Frame Leakage Protection with Restricted Earth Fault (REF) Protection**. Mastering this table is essential.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Frame Leakage Protection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three-Phase Stator Winding a-b-c] --> B[Stator Core Laminations = Machine Frame]
    B --> C{Insulation Healthy?}
    C -- Yes --> D[No leakage current to earth]
    C -- No --> E[Leakage current If flows: Fault Point to Frame to Earth Conductor]
    E --> F[Core Balance Current Transformer CBCT around single earth conductor]
    F --> G[Secondary EMF induced in CBCT]
    G --> H[Stabilizing Resistor Rstab approx 10 ohm]
    H --> I[Frame Leakage Relay pickup coil]
    I -- Current exceeds setting --> J[Relay Trip Contact closes]
    J --> K[Trip Coil of Machine Circuit Breaker MCB]
    J --> L[Trip Field Breaker]
    J --> M[Trip Turbine Stop Valves]
    J --> N[Annunciation and Event Logging]
    D --> O[Machine operates normally]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Current-Flow Diagram (Fault Condition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Phase A Conductor] -- Insulation Breakdown --> Q[Stator Lamination = Frame]
    Q --> R[Single Frame-to-Earth Conductor]
    R --> S[CBCT Window encircles R]
    S --> T[Secondary winding of CBCT]
    T --> U[Stabilizing Resistor and Relay]
    U -- Trip --> V[Master Trip Relay 86]
    V --> W[Generator Circuit Breaker GCB]
    V --> X[Field Breaker and Turbine Trip]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram (Numerical Relay Implementation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Analog Input from CBCT Secondary] --> B0[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    B0 --> C0[16-bit ADC Sampling at 1 kHz or higher]
    C0 --> D0[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    D0 --> E0[Fundamental Component Extraction via DFT]
    E0 --> F0{Computed I0 greater than Pickup?}
    F0 -- No --> G0[No Trip output]
    F0 -- Yes --> H0[Time-Overcurrent Characteristic Check]
    H0 -- Operating time elapsed --> I0[TRIP command to Breaker]
    H0 -- Within delay --> J0[Continue timing]
    I0 --> K0[Record waveform in COMTRADE file]
    I0 --> L0[Send GOOSE message via IEC 61850]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:**
> - All node IDs use the **letter-prefixed alphanumeric** convention (`A0`, `B0`, `F0`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) used as standalone nodes.
> - All node labels contain **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** with no markdown bold/italics or HTML tables embedded.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**What is Frame Leakage Protection? Where is it employed?**

**Model Answer:**
Frame Leakage Protection is a sensitive earth-fault protection scheme used to detect incipient insulation failures between the stator winding and the frame of a large rotating machine.

**Key points (each ≈ 1 mark):**
- It employs a **Core Balance Current Transformer (CBCT)** around the **single frame-to-earth conductor**.
- It detects the **zero-sequence leakage current** that flows only during a winding-to-frame fault.
- It is employed on **turbo-alternators, hydro-generators, and large HV motors**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Why is a stabilizing resistor used in the secondary circuit of a frame-leakage CBCT?**

**Model Answer:**
The stabilizing resistor (typically **10 Ω**) is connected in series with the relay coil to **damp high-frequency transients** (caused by lightning, switching, or external faults) that induce voltages in the CBCT secondary.

**Key points:**
- It prevents **false tripping** due to capacitive or inductive transients.
- It improves the **damping ratio** of the secondary circuit.
- It does not significantly affect the relay's sensitivity to genuine DC or power-frequency fault currents.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **principle and construction** of Frame Leakage Protection used for a large alternator. Mention the role of the **stabilizing resistor**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **100 MVA, 13.8 kV, 50 Hz** turbo-alternator has its frame connected to earth through a single conductor. A CBCT of ratio **600/5 A** is used. The relay pickup is set at **6 %** of full-load current. The stabilizing resistor is **10 Ω**, the relay coil resistance is **0.6 Ω**, and the lead resistance is **0.2 Ω**.
Compute:
   (i) The full-load current of the machine.
   (ii) The primary and secondary pickup currents.
   (iii) The operating voltage across the relay circuit. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

1. **Construction (3 marks):**
   - A **Core Balance Current Transformer (CBCT)** is mounted on the **single conductor** that bonds the alternator frame to the station earth mat.
   - The CBCT has a **toroidal core** (typically wound from grain-oriented silicon steel or nanocrystalline material) with a **single primary turn** (the earth conductor itself) and a multi-turn secondary winding.
   - A **stabilizing resistor** and the **frame-leakage relay** are connected across the secondary winding.
   - The relay contact, when closed, energizes a **master trip relay (Device 86)**, which trips the **Generator Circuit Breaker (GCB)**, the **field breaker**, and the **turbine stop valves**.

2. **Principle of Operation (3 marks):**
   - Under **healthy conditions**, the three stator phase currents are balanced; the vector sum is zero, and the **net flux in the CBCT core is zero**. No EMF is induced in the secondary, and the relay remains inoperative.
   - When a **stator winding-to-frame fault** occurs, fault current flows from the fault point → through the frame → down the single earth conductor. The CBCT senses this **non-zero residual current**, induces a secondary EMF, and operates the relay.

3. **Role of Stabilizing Resistor (1 mark):**
   - Damps high-frequency transient voltages induced in the secondary by **external disturbances** (switching, lightning, etc.).
   - Prevents **false operation** without compromising sensitivity to genuine low-frequency fault currents.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
- $S = 100 \ \text{MVA}$
- $V_L = 13.8 \ \text{kV}$
- $f = 50 \ \text{Hz}$
- CT ratio = $600/5 \ \text{A}$
- Pickup = $6 \ \% \ of \ I_{FLC}$
- $R_{stab} = 10 \ \Omega$, $R_{relay} = 0.6 \ \Omega$, $R_{leads} = 0.2 \ \Omega$

**(i) Full-Load Current (primary):**

$$I_{FLC} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 13.8 \times 10^3}$$

$$I_{FLC} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{23.902 \times 10^3} = 4183.7 \ \text{A}$$

**(ii) Pickup Currents:**

$$I_{pickup,prim} = 0.06 \times 4183.7 = 251.0 \ \text{A (primary)}$$

$$I_{pickup,sec} = 251.0 \times \frac{5}{600} = 251.0 \times 0.00833 = 2.09 \ \text{A (secondary)}$$

**(iii) Operating Voltage:**

$$V_{op} = I_{pickup,sec} \times (R_{stab} + R_{relay} + R_{leads})$$

$$V_{op} = 2.09 \times (10 + 0.6 + 0.2) = 2.09 \times 10.8 = 22.57 \ \text{V}$$

**Final Answer:** $I_{FLC} = 4183.7 \ \text{A}$, $I_{pickup,prim} = 251.0 \ \text{A}$, $I_{pickup,sec} = 2.09 \ \text{A}$, $V_{op} = 22.57 \ \text{V}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **Frame Leakage Protection** with **Restricted Earth Fault (REF) Protection**. Discuss the merits and limitations of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain how Frame Leakage Protection is implemented in a **modern numerical/digital relay** (with reference to IEC 61850 communication). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

| Feature | Frame Leakage Protection | Restricted Earth Fault (REF) Protection |
|---|---|---|
| CT arrangement | CBCT around the single frame earth conductor | One neutral CT + summation of three phase CTs |
| Principle | Detects residual current in earth conductor | Compares neutral current with vector sum of phase currents |
| Coverage | Entire stator winding (100 %) | Winding portion between phase CTs and neutral CT (≈ 80–85 %) |
| Stability | Stabilizing resistor needed | High stability due to biased differential characteristic |
| Sensitivity | Very high (5 % of FLC) | High (10–20 % of FLC) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate (requires 4 CTs) |
| Application | Large generators & HV motors | Generators and power transformers |

**Merits of Frame Leakage (2 marks):**
- Very simple, economical, and highly sensitive.
- Provides 100 % stator winding protection (including the neutral end).
- Fast operation (< 100 ms).

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- Cannot detect faults **outside the machine frame** (e.g., in the bus duct).
- Susceptible to **false tripping** from external transients if not stabilized.
- Requires **dedicated CBCT** hardware.

**Merits of REF (1 mark):**
- Excellent discrimination between **internal and external faults** due to biased characteristic.
- Highly stable through external faults and CT saturation.

**Limitations of REF (1 mark):**
- Higher cost and complexity.
- Partial winding coverage.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Numerical Relay Implementation Steps (each ≈ 1.5 marks):**

1. **Signal Acquisition:** The CBCT secondary current is fed into the numerical relay's **analog input module**, which contains an **electromagnetic isolator** and an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter**.

2. **A/D Conversion:** The filtered analog signal is sampled by a **16-bit ADC** at a sampling rate of **1 kHz or higher** (≥ 32 samples per cycle of 50 Hz).

3. **DSP Processing:** The **Digital Signal Processor** runs a **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** to extract the **fundamental (50 Hz) component** of the residual current, rejecting harmonics and DC offsets.

4. **Pickup Comparison:** The fundamental magnitude $\vert I_0 \vert$ is compared with the **pickup setting** (typically 5–10 % of $I_{FLC}$).

5. **Characteristic Curve:** A **definite-time or inverse-time overcurrent characteristic** (ANSI 51G / 51N) is applied. If $\vert I_0 \vert$ exceeds the pickup, a **timing accumulator** is started.

6. **Trip Command:** When the operating time elapses, a **TRIP signal** is generated and sent via:
   - **Hard-wired output** to the breaker trip coil.
   - **IEC 61850 GOOSE message** over the process bus to other IEDs (e.g., bay controller, SCADA).

7. **Event & Disturbance Recording:** The relay stores the **COMTRADE-format waveform file** and logs the event with a **time-stamp** (synchronized via **IEEE 1588 / IRIG-B**).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Frame Leakage Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Frame Leakage" with "Earth Fault" relay:** Earth fault relays measure phase currents via conventional CTs; Frame Leakage uses a **CBCT on the single frame earth conductor**. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if this distinction is missed.
> 2. **Forgetting the stabilizing resistor's purpose:** Many students write "it limits current" — wrong. The correct answer is "it **damps high-frequency transients** to prevent false tripping."
> 3. **Unit mistakes in pickup calculations:** The CT ratio must be inverted correctly: $I_{sec} = I_{prim} \times \dfrac{5}{400}$, **not** $\times \dfrac{400}{5}$. This single error can cost **3 marks**.
> 4. **Omitting the formula for $I_{FLC}$:** Always start with $I_{FLC} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \ V_L}$ for 3-phase machines — never assume the value.
> 5. **Ignoring the 100 % winding coverage advantage:** Frame Leakage covers the **entire stator**, unlike differential protection which has a **blind spot at the neutral end**. Mention this — it is a **favorite examiner's keyword**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Frame Leakage Protection = sensitive earth-fault protection for the **stator winding-to-frame** insulation of large rotating machines.
- **Sensing Element:** **Core Balance Current Transformer (CBCT)** around the **single** frame-to-earth conductor.
- **Operating Principle:** Detects **zero-sequence (residual) leakage current** that flows only during a winding-to-frame breakdown.
- **Typical Settings:** Pickup = **5 %–10 % of $I_{FLC}$**; $R_{stab} \approx 10 \ \Omega$; operating time **< 100 ms**.
- **Key Formula:** $I_{FLC} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}$ and $V_{op} = I_{sec} \times (R_{stab} + R_{relay} + R_{leads})$.
- **Stabilizing Resistor Purpose:** **Damping** of high-frequency secondary transients — **NOT** current limiting.
- **Coverage:** **100 %** of the stator winding (a major advantage over differential protection).
- **Application:** **Turbo-alternators, hydro-generators, large HV motors** in thermal/nuclear power stations.
- **Comparison Keyword:** Frame Leakage is more **economical and sensitive**; REF is more **stable and discriminating**; both provide stator earth-fault protection.
- **Numerical Relay Function:** Modern IEDs implement this as **ANSI 51G / 51N** with **IEC 61850 GOOSE** trip signaling.
- **CT Knee-Point Voltage Check:** $V_k \geq 2 \times V_{op}$ to ensure linear, non-saturating operation.
- **Examiners' Favorites:** (i) Sketch + principle, (ii) Comparison with REF, (iii) Numerical calculation of pickup current, (iv) Numerical relay implementation.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stator and Rotor Protection Against Various Types of Faults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Stator and Rotor Protection Against Various Types of Faults ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Stator Protection — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stator Protection** refers to the comprehensive scheme of relays, current/voltage transformers, and digital logic algorithms employed to detect, isolate, and clear abnormal electrical conditions within the **stator (armature) winding** of a synchronous generator, motor, or large alternator. It guards against **phase faults, earth faults, inter-turn faults, and overloading conditions** in the stationary three-phase windings embedded in the stator core slots. The stator winding is the **most critical and expensive** part of any turbo-alternator or hydro-generator. A single undetected fault can melt copper, destroy the **laminated silicon steel core**, and result in downtime of months. Therefore, stator protection is treated as a **multi-layered, redundant defense system** (defense-in-depth) rather than a single relay scheme. ### 1.2 Rotor Protection — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Rotor Protection** encompasses the suite of protective relaying strategies deployed to safeguard the **field (rotor) winding** of a synchronous machine against ground faults, turn-to-turn short circuits, loss-of-excitation, field overcurrent, and thermal overload. Unlike the stator, the rotor operates at lower DC or AC voltage but at high centrifugal and thermal stress. The rotor is the **rotating member** carrying either DC excitation (for synchronous machines) or induced current (for induction motors). Its protection is more challenging because fault currents are limited, fault levels are low, and access to the rotor is mechanically restricted. ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of a large power plant generator as a **massive 3-story building**: | Component | Building Analogy | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Stator** | Ground floor apartments | Stationary, large, houses 3-phase wiring | | **Rotor** | Rooftop water tank + pump | Rotating, supplies the energy/field | | **Differential Relay** | Smoke detector per room | Compares "in" vs "out" current | | **Earth Fault Relay** | Ground water leak sensor | Detects leakage to earth | | **Loss of Excitation Relay** | Pump failure alarm | Detects when "motive force" disappears | | **Inter-turn Relay** | Hairline crack detector | Detects micro-shorts inside one coil | > [!NOTE] > **Just as a building needs both fire alarms (fault detection) and sprinklers (fault isolation), a generator needs overlapping relay zones to ensure NO fault — no matter how small — escapes detection.** This is the philosophy of **duplicate, overlapping, and independent protection** mandated by **IEEE C37.102** and **IEC 60255**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard CT Secondary Current:** $I_{sec} = 5\,\text{A}$ (or **1 A** for high-precision numerical relays) - **Standard PT Secondary Voltage:** $V_{sec} = 110 / \sqrt{3}\,\text{V}$ (phase-to-neutral) - **Maximum allowable stator winding hotspot temperature:** Class F = **155 °C**, Class H = **180 °C** - **Standard generator frequency:** $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ (India) or **60 Hz** (US) - **Standard field voltage rating (large turbo-generator):** $V_f = 250\,\text{V}$ to **$1000\,\text{V}$ DC** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Taxonomy of Stator Faults The stator winding can experience the following electrical faults, listed in **descending order of severity**: 1. **Phase-to-Phase Faults (L-L or L-L-L)** — Most severe; fault currents up to **5–10 × Full Load Current (FLC)**. 2. **Phase-to-Ground (Earth) Faults** — Most common; depends on system grounding impedance. 3. **Inter-Turn (Turn-to-Turn) Faults** — Micro-shorts between adjacent turns in the same coil; the **most difficult to detect**. 4. **Three-Phase Short Circuit at Terminals** — Highest fault level. 5. **Overloading / Overcurrent** — Slow thermal fault. 6. **Loss-of-Cooling / Thermal Overload** — Gradual insulation degradation. 7. **Unbalanced Loading (Negative Sequence)** — Causes **double-frequency ($2f$) heating** in rotor. ### 2.2 Stator Protection Schemes — Layered Defense #### **A. Longitudinal Differential Protection (87G)** This is the **primary, high-speed** protection for stator windings. It works on **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)**. **Operating Principle:** - Two sets of CTs are installed: one at the **neutral end** and one at the **terminal end** of each phase. - During normal operation or external faults: $I_1 = I_2$ → differential current $I_d = 0$. - During internal fault: $I_1 \neq I_2$ → $I_d \neq 0$ → relay operates. **Operating Current Equation:** $$I_d = \vert \bar{I}_1 - \bar{I}_2 \vert$$ $$I_{r} = \vert \bar{I}_1 + \bar{I}_2 \vert \quad \text{(restraint current)}$$ Where: - $I_1$ = current entering from terminal CT - $I_2$ = current leaving from neutral CT - $I_d$ = operating (differential) current - $I_r$ = restraining (bias) current **Percentage Bias Characteristic:** $$I_{op} = I_{min} + K \cdot I_{r}$$ Where: - $I_{min}$ = minimum pickup (typically **0.1 to 0.3 pu**) - $K$ = bias slope (typically **15% to 50%**) - $I_{op}$ = relay operating threshold > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-impedance differential scheme:** A stabilizing resistor of value $R_{st} = \frac{V_k}{2I_f}$ is connected across the relay coil, where $V_k$ is the relay knee-point voltage and $I_f$ is the maximum fault current. This prevents **CT saturation mismatch** during external faults. #### **B. Earth Fault Protection (51N / 51G)** - A **zero-sequence CT (window/residual CT)** encircles all three phases. - Operates when $I_a + I_b + I_c \neq 0$. - For a **high-impedance grounded** generator, $I_{earth}$ is limited to **5–10 A**, making this scheme extremely sensitive. #### **C. 100% Stator Earth Fault Protection (Using 3rd Harmonic)** Conventional earth-fault relays protect only **85–95%** of the winding (close to the terminal). The **neutral end 5–15%** is protected by exploiting the fact that **3rd harmonic voltage ($V_3$) is maximum at the neutral** of a generator. **Operating Principle:** $$V_{3,N} / V_{3,T} = \text{ratio used to detect neutral-end earth fault}$$ Where: - $V_{3,N}$ = third harmonic voltage at neutral - $V_{3,T}$ = third harmonic voltage at terminals When an earth fault occurs near the neutral, $V_{3,N}$ **collapses to zero** while $V_{3,T}$ rises. The relay compares both to give **100% coverage**. #### **D. Negative-Sequence Protection (46)** A sustained negative-sequence current $I_2$ causes **double-frequency rotor heating**: $$I_2^2 \cdot t = K \quad \text{(constant for the machine)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield:** A typical large turbo-generator has $K = 4$ to $10$ seconds (at rated $I_2$). The relay has an **inverse-time characteristic** matching this $I_2^2 t = K$ curve. #### **E. Inter-Turn (Turn-to-Turn) Fault Protection** Detected using a **special CT (magnetic CT or slot CT)** placed at the **neutral end** of each phase winding. Any inter-turn fault causes circulating current in the parallel branch, which the CT picks up. ### 2.3 Taxonomy of Rotor Faults | # | Fault Type | Detection Method | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Rotor Earth Fault (Single)** | DC voltage injection + overcurrent relay | | 2 | **Rotor Earth Fault (Double)** | Impedance/AC injection method | | 3 | **Inter-Turn Fault in Field Winding** | Flux monitoring / air-gap search coil | | 4 | **Loss of Excitation (LOE)** | Offset mho relay / impedance relay (40) | | 5 | **Field Overcurrent** | DC overcurrent relay (76) | | 6 | **Loss of Field Voltage** | Under-voltage relay on field circuit | | 7 | **Over-speed** | Frequency relay at shaft | | 8 | **Rotor Thermal Overload** | RTD-based temperature relay (49) | ### 2.4 Loss of Excitation (LOE) — Mathematical Analysis LOE is detected by a **mho relay** placed in the **R-X plane**, viewing the generator-impedance trajectory from the terminals. **Apparent impedance seen by the relay:** $$Z = \frac{V_t}{I_g}$$ Where $V_t$ = terminal voltage, $I_g$ = generator current. **For a synchronous machine on LOE:** $$Z = -j \left( \frac{X_d \cdot X_{d'}}{X_d + X_{d'}} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{E_f}{V_t}} \quad \text{(approximate form)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The LOE relay characteristic is typically a circle of diameter equal to $X_{d'}$ (transient reactance) centered at the origin in the negative X region (R-X plane).** Operation time is **0.1 to 0.5 seconds** for fast LOE; **1.0 to 3.0 seconds** for slow LOE. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | $I_d = \vert \bar{I}_1 - \bar{I}_2 \vert$ | Differential operating current (87G) | | 2 | $I_{op} = I_{min} + K \cdot I_r$ | Percentage bias characteristic | | 3 | $R_{st} = \frac{V_k}{2I_f}$ | Stabilizing resistor for high-impedance diff. | | 4 | $I_2^2 \cdot t = K$ | Negative-sequence heating limit (46) | | 5 | $V_{3N} / V_{3T} = \text{ratio}$ | 100% stator earth fault detection | | 6 | $Z = V_t / I_g$ | Apparent impedance for LOE relay (40) | | 7 | $E_f = V_t + j X_d I_{qg} - j X_d I_{dg}$ | Internal EMF equation | | 8 | $V_{f,rated} = I_{f,rated} \cdot R_f$ | Field circuit rated relation | | 9 | $I_{pickup,EF} = 0.05 \text{ to } 0.1 \, I_{f,rated}$ | Earth fault pickup (field) | | 10 | $P_{rotor} = K_f \cdot I_f^2 \cdot R_f$ | Field copper loss | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** The five most-tested formulas in this module are: **(1) Bias characteristic**, **(2) $I_2^2 t = K$**, **(3) Third-harmonic ratio**, **(4) Stabilizing resistor**, and **(5) LOE mho circle radius $X_{d'}/2$**. ### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Power Plant Operators (NTPC, BHEL, Reliance Power):** Use these schemes to protect 500 MW/660 MW supercritical units. - **Industrial Co-Generators:** Apply to captive power plant alternators. - **Hydro Stations:** Same principles, but rotor speeds are lower (300/100 rpm), so frequency-based protection differs. - **Wind Turbine Generators (DFIG/PMSG):** Modified stator and rotor protection adapted to variable-speed operation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Percentage Bias Characteristic **Problem Setup:** A 200 MVA, 13.8 kV, 50 Hz generator has two CTs at neutral and terminal ends. CT ratio is **2000:1**. Derive the operating and restraining currents and find the bias slope required. **Step 1 — Define the CT secondary currents.** In normal operation (or during an external fault), the same current flows in and out of the protected zone, so at the CT secondaries: $$I_1 = I_2 = I_{primary} / N_{CT}$$ where $N_{CT} = 2000$. **Step 2 — Express operating and restraining currents.** Using the standard ANSI definition of bias: $$I_d = \vert \bar{I}_1 - \bar{I}_2 \vert$$ $$I_r = \frac{\vert \bar{I}_1 + \bar{I}_2 \vert}{2}$$ For matched CTs during normal/external fault, $\bar{I}_1 = \bar{I}_2$, hence $I_d = 0$ and $I_r = I_1$. **Step 3 — Apply for an internal fault.** During an internal fault, currents may flow from BOTH ends (e.g., fault fed from system + from generator). In phasor sum: $$\bar{I}_1 = I_1 \angle 0°, \quad \bar{I}_2 = I_2 \angle 180°$$ So $\bar{I}_1 + \bar{I}_2 = (I_1 - I_2)$ and $\bar{I}_1 - \bar{I}_2 = (I_1 + I_2)$ in worst case. **Step 4 — Define the operating threshold.** The relay operates when $I_d \geq I_{op}$ where: $$I_{op} = I_{min} + K \cdot I_r$$ **Step 5 — Pick a typical bias slope.** Standard practice for generators (per IEEE C37.102): $$K = 0.15 \quad (15\% \text{ bias})$$ $$I_{min} = 0.1 \text{ pu of CT secondary} = 0.1 \cdot 5\,\text{A} = 0.5\,\text{A}$$ **Step 6 — Compute pickup for a typical internal fault.** If $I_1 = 5\,\text{A}$, $I_2 = 4\,\text{A}$ (after CT saturation, external fault), then: $$I_d = \vert 5 - 4 \vert = 1\,\text{A}$$ $$I_r = \frac{5 + 4}{2} = 4.5\,\text{A}$$ $$I_{op} = 0.5 + 0.15 \times 4.5 = 0.5 + 0.675 = 1.175\,\text{A}$$ Since $I_d = 1\,\text{A} < I_{op} = 1.175\,\text{A}$, the relay **restrains** (correct — it is an external fault). If it's an internal fault with $I_1 = 8\,\text{A}$, $I_2 = 7\,\text{A}$: $$I_d = 1\,\text{A}, \quad I_r = 7.5\,\text{A}$$ $$I_{op} = 0.5 + 0.15 \times 7.5 = 1.625\,\text{A}$$ Even though $I_d < I_{op}$, with more realistic values (e.g., $I_1 = 10\,\text{A}$ from terminal, $I_2 = 0$ from neutral during terminal-end internal fault), $I_d = 10\,\text{A} \gg 0.5 + 0.15 \times 5 = 1.25\,\text{A}$ → **relay operates**. ### 3.2 Derivation of the $I_2^2 t = K$ Negative-Sequence Capability Curve **Step 1 — Heating in the rotor due to $I_2$.** Negative-sequence stator currents induce a **rotating field at $2\omega$** in the rotor reference frame, causing eddy currents in rotor surface: $$P_{eddy} = K_e \cdot I_2^2$$ **Step 2 — Energy balance equation.** Assuming adiabatic heating (no time for heat dissipation during fault): $$\int_0^t I_2^2 \, d\tau = K$$ **Step 3 — For constant $I_2$ during fault:** $$I_2^2 \cdot t = K$$ **Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU typical).** A 100 MVA generator has $K = 7.0$ (per unit seconds). Maximum allowable $I_2 = 0.3$ pu (continuous). For a sustained unbalanced fault of $I_2 = 1.0$ pu: $$t_{max} = \frac{K}{I_2^2} = \frac{7.0}{1.0^2} = 7.0 \text{ seconds}$$ For $I_2 = 2.0$ pu: $$t_{max} = \frac{7.0}{4} = 1.75 \text{ seconds}$$ This gives the **inverse-time characteristic** the relay must replicate. ### 3.3 Derivation of High-Impedance Differential Stabilizing Resistor **Step 1 — Identify the problem.** During external faults, one CT may saturate while the other doesn't, causing a **spurious differential current**. **Step 2 — Calculate the voltage across the relay during CT saturation.** $$V_{relay} = I_f \cdot (R_{CT,sat} + 2R_{lead})$$ **Step 3 — Set stabilizing resistor so that relay sees voltage below $V_k$:** $$R_{st} = \frac{V_k}{2 I_f} - R_{lead}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Example:** Given $V_k = 30$ V, $I_f = 50$ A (max external fault CT secondary), $R_{lead} = 0.5$ Ω: $$R_{st} = \frac{30}{2 \times 50} - 0.5 = 0.3 - 0.5 = -0.2 \, \Omega$$ Negative value means $R_{lead}$ alone is sufficient. If $R_{lead} = 0.1$ Ω: $$R_{st} = 0.3 - 0.1 = 0.2 \, \Omega$$ A **non-linear resistor (Metrosil)** is also added in parallel to limit over-voltage during internal faults. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Stator Protection Scheme | Fault Type | Location | Best Protection | Backup | |---|---|---|---| | 3-φ short circuit | Terminals | **87G (Differential)** | 51V (Voltage-restrained OC) | | 3-φ short circuit | Neutral | **87G** + 3rd harmonic | 51G | | L-L fault | Anywhere | **87G** | 46 (Negative seq.) | | L-G fault | 90% of winding | **87G** + 51G | 27 (UV) | | L-G fault | Last 10% near neutral | **3rd Harmonic Relay (27N/59N)** | 51G | | Inter-turn fault | Anywhere | **Magnetic CT / Slot CT** | 46 | | Thermal overload | Anywhere | **49 (Temperature relay via RTD)** | 46 | | Unbalanced load | Anywhere | **46 (Negative sequence)** | 49 | ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Rotor Protection Scheme | Fault Type | Detection Device | ANSI Code | |---|---|---| | Loss of Excitation (fast) | Mho relay | **40** | | Loss of Excitation (slow) | Offset mho with timer | **40** + timer | | Field overcurrent | DC overcurrent relay | **76** | | Field earth fault (single) | DC injection + O/L relay | **64F** | | Field earth fault (double) | AC voltage injection | **64F-AC** | | Rotor thermal overload | RTD-based relay | **49R** | | Over-speed | Frequency relay on shaft | **12** | | Field under-voltage | DC under-voltage relay | **27F** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of Stator Differential Protection (87G) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Generator Stator
Phase A Winding] --> B[CT1 Terminal End
2000:1] A --> C[CT2 Neutral End
2000:1] B --> D[Summation
Current Transformer] C --> D D --> E[Differential
Relay 87G] E --> F{Operate
or Restrain?} F -- I_d greater than I_op --> G[TRIP COMMAND
Circuit Breaker Open] F -- I_d less than I_op --> H[Restrain
No Trip] B --> I[Overcurrent
Relay 51] C --> J[Earth Fault
Relay 51G] I --> G J --> G ``` ### 4.2 Complete Stator Protection Architecture (Defense-in-Depth) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph G1[Stator Winding Zone] S1[Phase A] S2[Phase B] S3[Phase C] end G1 --> P1[Primary 87G Differential] G1 --> P2[Backup 51/51G OC-EF] G1 --> P3[46 Negative Sequence] G1 --> P4[27N/59N 3rd Harmonic 100% EF] G1 --> P5[49 Thermal RTD] P1 --> CB1[Main Circuit Breaker] P2 --> CB1 P3 --> AL1[Alarm] P4 --> CB1 P5 --> AL1 ``` ### 4.3 Rotor Protection Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB R1[Field Winding
DC Excitation] --> R2[40 Loss of Excitation
Mho Relay] R1 --> R3[76 Field Overcurrent] R1 --> R4[64F Field Earth Fault
DC Injection Method] R1 --> R5[49R Rotor Thermal] R1 --> R6[27F Field Under-Voltage] R2 --> T1[Trip Generator Breaker] R3 --> T1 R4 --> AL2[Alarm or Trip] R5 --> AL2 R6 --> AL2 ``` ### 4.4 Loss of Excitation Mho Characteristic (R-X Plane) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph X1[LOE Mho Circle in R-X Plane] O[Origin
Generator Terminal] --> C[Center at -j Xd-prime/2] C --> D[Circle of Radius Xd-prime/2] D --> E[Impedance Trajectory
Enters on LOE] D --> F[Operate Region
Inside Circle] end ``` ### 4.5 Functional Flow of a Numerical Generator Protection Relay ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[CT Inputs
I_a, I_b, I_c] --> A2[Analog
Signal Conditioning] A3[PT Inputs
V_a, V_b, V_c] --> A2 A2 --> A4[Anti-Aliasing
Filter] A4 --> A5[16-bit ADC
Sampling at 4 kHz] A5 --> A6[DFT / FFT
Algorithm] A6 --> A7[Fundamental
I1, V1] A6 --> A8[Negative Sequence
I2 Computation] A6 --> A9[Zero Sequence
I0 Computation] A6 --> A10[3rd Harmonic
Extraction] A7 --> A11[87G Logic] A8 --> A12[46 Logic] A9 --> A13[51G Logic] A10 --> A14[27N 59N Logic] A11 --> A15[Trip
Matrix] A12 --> A15 A13 --> A15 A14 --> A15 A15 --> A16[Output
Trip Coil] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Modern numerical relays (e.g., ABB REG670, GE G60, Siemens 7UM62) execute all the stator and rotor protection functions in **one single IED** through **separate but simultaneous algorithms** running in parallel. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List any three types of stator winding faults. Explain briefly how longitudinal differential protection detects them.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three main types of stator winding faults are: **(1)** Phase-to-phase fault, **(2)** Phase-to-ground (earth) fault, and **(3)** Inter-turn fault. Longitudinal differential protection (87G) uses **two CTs per phase**, one at the neutral end and one at the terminal end. The secondary currents are compared via a **summation CT**. Under normal/external fault conditions, $I_1 = I_2$, so $I_d = 0$ and the relay restrains. For an internal fault, $I_d \neq 0$, exceeding the bias threshold, and the relay issues a **trip command within 1–2 cycles** (20–40 ms). **(3 marks)** **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *What is meant by "100% stator earth fault protection"? Why is it necessary?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Conventional earth-fault relays using a residual CT or neutral CT can protect only the **85–95% of the winding near the terminals**, because an earth fault near the neutral produces negligible zero-sequence current. To protect the **remaining 5–15% of winding near the neutral**, the **3rd harmonic voltage ($V_3$) comparison** method is used. The 3rd harmonic voltage is **maximum at the neutral** and falls to a low value at the terminals. A relay measures $V_{3,N}$ and $V_{3,T}$ and trips when their ratio deviates from normal. This is called **100% stator earth fault protection**. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Question Choice A OR Question Choice B — answer any ONE full question.** --- **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **operating principle of percentage bias (bias characteristic) differential protection** for a generator stator winding. Derive the **stabilizing resistor formula** for a high-impedance differential scheme. **(b) [7 Marks]** A **100 MVA, 11 kV, 50 Hz turbo-generator** has $X_d = 1.2$ pu, $X_{d'} = 0.25$ pu, $X_{d''} = 0.18$ pu. The CT ratio is **1500:1** and CT knee-point voltage $V_k = 35$ V. Maximum external fault current is **40 kA primary**. Calculate the **stabilizing resistor $R_{st}$** required. Lead resistance is **0.4 Ω**. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Block diagram** (3 marks): Two CTs at terminal and neutral ends → summation CT → operating coil and restraining coil → trip contacts. 2. **Bias characteristic** (2 marks): $$I_{op} = I_{min} + K \cdot I_r$$ Where $K$ = 0.15 to 0.5 (typical for generators). This prevents **CT saturation mismatch** from causing false trips. 3. **Stabilizing resistor derivation** (2 marks): $$R_{st} = \frac{V_k}{2 I_f} - R_{lead}$$ The factor of 2 comes from the fact that under worst-case external fault, **one CT is fully saturated** and the other is not, so the entire fault current flows through the **saturated CT and the two lead resistances**. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Given: - $I_f = 40{,}000 / 1500 = 26.67$ A - $V_k = 35$ V - $R_{lead} = 0.4$ Ω $$R_{st} = \frac{35}{2 \times 26.67} - 0.4$$ $$R_{st} = \frac{35}{53.33} - 0.4 = 0.656 - 0.4 = 0.256 \, \Omega$$ A **non-linear resistor (Metrosil)** must be connected in parallel to limit the peak voltage across the relay to safe value (typically **2 kV peak**). **(7 marks)** --- **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **loss-of-excitation (LOE) protection scheme** for a synchronous generator. Sketch the **R-X plane characteristic** and explain why the relay circle is centered on the **negative X-axis** with diameter equal to $X_{d'}$. **(b) [7 Marks]** A **200 MVA, 15.75 kV, 50 Hz** generator has the following capability for negative sequence: $K = 6.0$ s. The generator is rated at $I_{2,continuous} = 0.1$ pu. If an **unbalanced external fault** causes $I_2 = 1.5$ pu, calculate the **maximum time** the relay should allow before tripping. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Principle** (2 marks): LOE is detected by a **mho-type distance relay** looking into the generator terminals. The apparent impedance $Z = V_t / I_g$ is computed. 2. **Characteristic** (2 marks): The relay characteristic is a **circle of diameter $X_{d'}$** centered at **$-jX_{d'}/2$** on the **negative X-axis** of the R-X plane. 3. **Why?** (3 marks): When the field is lost, the generator absorbs reactive power and the terminal voltage drops. The impedance trajectory **moves into the negative-reactance region** (since $X_{d'}$ is much smaller than $X_d$). The circle of diameter $X_{d'}$ ensures that: - **Steady-state LOE** (impedance at $-jX_d$) → outside the circle → must be detected by a larger mho circle (offset) with **delayed trip** (1–3 s). - **Transient LOE** (impedance at $-jX_{d'}$) → inside the circle → **fast trip** (0.1–0.5 s). **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Using the negative-sequence capability equation: $$I_2^2 \cdot t = K$$ $$t = \frac{K}{I_2^2} = \frac{6.0}{(1.5)^2} = \frac{6.0}{2.25} = 2.67 \text{ seconds}$$ **The relay should trip in approximately 2.67 seconds** (with safety margin, typically **2.0 to 2.5 seconds**). **(7 marks)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing $X_d$ with $X_{d'}$ in LOE relay sizing** — The mho circle diameter is **$X_{d'}$**, NOT $X_d$. Using $X_d$ gives an oversized, useless circle. > > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $R_{st}$ formula** — Many students write $R_{st} = V_k / I_f$, losing 2 marks. > > 3. **Mixing up "100% stator earth fault" with "100% generator earth fault"** — The 100% refers to the **winding length covered**, not the entire generator. > > 4. **Not drawing the R-X plane characteristic in LOE** — A question worth 7 marks often gives **2 marks specifically for the diagram**. > > 5. **Confusing 51G (stator earth fault, AC) with 64F (rotor/field earth fault, DC)** — These are completely different schemes with different ANSI codes. > > 6. **Forgetting the Metrosil** in high-impedance differential circuits. > > 7. **Writing "inter-turn fault is detected by differential relay"** — this is **WRONG**. Differential protection **cannot** detect small inter-turn faults because the fault current circulates within the same phase and doesn't change the net CT current. A **dedicated magnetic CT** is required. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 — Stator and Rotor Protection** - [ ] **Stator faults** = Phase-Phase, Phase-Ground, Inter-Turn, Thermal. - [ ] **Primary stator protection** = 87G (Longitudinal Differential). - [ ] **Bias equation:** $I_{op} = I_{min} + K \cdot I_r$, with $K = 0.15$ to 0.5 for generators. - [ ] **Stabilizing resistor:** $R_{st} = \frac{V_k}{2 I_f} - R_{lead}$. - [ ] **100% stator earth fault** = 3rd harmonic voltage ratio method ($V_{3N}/V_{3T}$). - [ ] **Negative sequence limit:** $I_2^2 \cdot t = K$ (typical $K = 4$ to 10 s). - [ ] **Inter-turn fault** = detected by **magnetic/slot CT**, NOT by 87G. - [ ] **Rotor faults** = Earth fault, LOE, Field OC, Inter-turn, Thermal. - [ ] **LOE (40)** = mho circle of diameter $X_{d'}$ on negative X-axis. - [ ] **Field earth fault (64F)** = DC voltage injection method. - [ ] **Thermal protection (49)** = RTD-based, class F = 155 °C, class H = 180 °C. - [ ] **CT secondary standard** = 5 A (or 1 A for numerical relays). - [ ] **PT secondary standard** = $110/\sqrt{3}$ V phase-to-neutral. - [ ] **IEEE C37.102** = Standard guide for generator protection. - [ ] **IEC 60255** = International standard for numerical relays. - [ ] **Metrosil (non-linear resistor)** is used in parallel with the differential relay to limit over-voltage. > **Best of luck with your KTU exam preparation! Master the bias characteristic, the mho circle, and the 3rd harmonic scheme — these are the three highest-weightage topics from this module.**

topics.title: Basic Components of Numerical Relays with Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basic Components of Numerical Relays with Block Diagram ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Numerical Relay** (also called a **Digital/Numeric Protection Relay**) is a computer-based protective relay that uses a **microprocessor or Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** as its central computational engine. Unlike electromechanical or static (analog) relays, numerical relays convert the analog power system signals (currents and voltages) into numerical data via an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, then apply **mathematical algorithms** in software to determine whether a fault exists and to issue a Trip Command. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A numerical relay is a relay in which the measured analog quantities are first converted into numerical (digital) form using an A/D converter, and the decision logic (relay algorithm) is implemented entirely in software running on a microprocessor/DSP. The trip decision is therefore a software output, not an analog comparator output. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **security guard** at the entrance of a huge stadium: - The **CCTV camera** records the crowd (this is like the **Current Transformer / Potential Transformer** sensing the power system). - The **footage** is sent through a **filtering device** that removes glitches and noise (this is the **Anti-Aliasing Filter**). - The filtered footage is then **converted into pixels and digital frames** that the computer can process (this is the **A/D Converter**). - The computer runs a **face-recognition algorithm** to identify troublemakers (this is the **Microprocessor running protection algorithms** like DFT or differential equation). - If a troublemaker is found, the alarm is raised (this is the **Trip Output / Circuit Breaker Trip Coil**). In short: **Numerical Relay = CT/PT + Anti-Aliasing Filter + A/D Converter + Microprocessor (Algorithm) + Trip Output.** ### 1.3 Why Numerical Relays are Superior | Feature | Static (Analog) Relay | Numerical (Digital) Relay | |---|---|---| | Decision element | Analog comparator | Microprocessor / DSP | | Flexibility | Hardwired | Software programmable | | Self-test | Not possible | Built-in self-diagnostics | | Communication | Local only | SCADA / IEC 61850 compatible | | Multi-function | Difficult | Single device → many protections | | Event recording | Not available | **Disturbance / Fault Recorder** built-in | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** Numerical relays are the backbone of modern **Substation Automation Systems (SAS)** and are fully compatible with the **IEC 61850** communication standard for smart grids. ### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics - **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$):** Typically **1.2 kHz to 4 kHz** for 50 Hz systems (i.e., **24 to 80 samples per cycle**). - **A/D Resolution:** Usually **12-bit to 16-bit** (resolution = full-scale range / $2^n$). - **Operating Time:** **0.5 cycle to 2 cycles** (≈ 10 ms to 40 ms for a 50 Hz system). - **CT/PT Input Range:** 1 A / 5 A secondary current; $\frac{110}{\sqrt{3}}$ V / $\frac{110}{3}$ V secondary voltage. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Functional Building Blocks of a Numerical Relay The architecture of a numerical relay can be partitioned into **seven fundamental functional blocks**. Each block performs a specific signal-processing task, and together they convert a continuous analog waveform into a binary trip/no-trip decision. > [!IMPORTANT] > These seven blocks form the **canonical block diagram** tested in every KTU ESE question on this topic. --- #### Block 1: Transducers (Current Transformers & Potential Transformers) The CT steps down the primary line current (hundreds or thousands of amps) to a manageable secondary value (**1 A or 5 A**). The PT (VT) steps down voltage to **$\frac{110}{\sqrt{3}}$ V** or **$\frac{110}{3}$ V**. **Why?** Because the relay electronics can only tolerate low-level signals; direct connection to EHV lines would destroy the components. --- #### Block 2: Surge Protection Circuit (Transient Limiter) A **non-linear resistor (varistor / MOV)** or **transorb** clamps high-voltage transients caused by lightning, switching, or CT saturation. This protects the downstream electronics from damage. --- #### Block 3: Signal Conditioning (Auxiliary CT/VT + Burden) A second, **internal Auxiliary CT** (often with air-gap core to avoid saturation) further reduces the signal to a low voltage (e.g., $\pm 5$ V or $\pm 10$ V) suitable for the electronics. A **burden resistor** converts current to voltage: $v(t) = i(t) \cdot R_{burden}$. --- #### Block 4: Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-Pass Filter) This is the **most critical analog block** before digitization. It is a **low-pass filter** that removes all frequency components above **$\frac{f_s}{2}$** (the Nyquist frequency). **Why is it needed?** When we sample at $f_s$, any frequency $f > \frac{f_s}{2}$ "folds back" into the lower frequency range, producing a false low-frequency component called an **alias**. This alias can corrupt the relay's decision. > [!NOTE] > **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:** To reconstruct a band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$, the sampling frequency must satisfy: > > $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ --- #### Block 5: Analog-to-Digital (A/D) Converter The conditioned and filtered analog voltage is sampled at instants $t = nT_s$ (where $T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$) and converted into a binary number by the ADC. The quantization step size is: $$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$ where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale input voltage and $n$ is the number of bits. For example, with $V_{FS} = 10$ V and $n = 12$ bits: $$q = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.44 \text{ mV}$$ --- #### Block 6: Microprocessor / Digital Signal Processor (DSP) This is the **brain** of the relay. It performs: 1. **Sample storage** in RAM. 2. **Algorithm execution** — e.g., Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), Fourier-based distance calculation, alpha-plane (differential relay), or travelling-wave algorithm. 3. **Logic comparison** — compares computed impedance / current / power with pickup threshold. 4. **Decision output** — generates trip or no-trip signal. 5. **Self-testing** — continuous RAM/ROM/ADC diagnostics. --- #### Block 7: Output & Communication Interface The trip command energizes the **Trip Coil of the Circuit Breaker** through a **Trip Relay** (electromechanical auxiliary). Additionally, the relay communicates with **SCADA / HMI** via serial (RS-485), Ethernet, or **IEC 61850** protocol. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Typical Values (50 Hz system) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist criterion | $f_s = 1.2 \text{ kHz to 4 kHz}$ | | 2 | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Sampling period | $\approx 0.5 \text{ ms to 0.83 ms}$ | | 3 | $N = \dfrac{f_s}{f}$ | Samples per cycle | $N = 24, 32, 64, 80$ | | 4 | $q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | ADC quantization step | $\approx 2.44 \text{ mV}$ (12-bit, 10 V FS) | | 5 | $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ | Aliasing frequency | $k$ = nearest integer to $f/f_s$ | | 6 | $H(s) = \dfrac{\omega_c}{s + \omega_c}$ | 1st-order LP filter transfer fn. | $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ | | 7 | $\omega_c = 2 \pi f_c = 2 \pi \cdot \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Filter cutoff (Nyquist) | $\pi f_s$ rad/s | | 8 | $\text{SNR} \approx 6.02 n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | ADC signal-to-noise ratio | $n$ = bits | --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Grids & IEC 61850:** Numerical relays form the field-level IEDs (Intelligent Electronic Devices) of digital substations. - **Adaptive Protection:** Algorithms can adjust pickup thresholds in real-time based on loading conditions. - **Wide-Area Monitoring:** Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) integrated into numerical relays provide synchronized phasors for grid stability. - **Asset Management:** Built-in event recorders and disturbance logs aid post-fault analysis. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Sampling Frequency for 50 Hz System **Given:** A power system operates at fundamental frequency $f = 50$ Hz. The CT/PT signal contains harmonics up to the **13th harmonic** (a common relay assumption). **Find:** Minimum sampling frequency $f_s$. --- **Step 1 — Identify the highest frequency component.** The 13th harmonic of a 50 Hz fundamental is: $$f_{max} = 13 \times 50 = 650 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem.** According to the theorem, to avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest signal frequency: $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the numerical value.** $$f_s \geq 2 \times 650 = 1300 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to samples per cycle.** The number of samples acquired in one cycle of the 50 Hz waveform is: $$N = \frac{f_s}{f} = \frac{1300}{50} = 26 \text{ samples/cycle}$$ **Step 5 — Practical engineering decision.** To provide a **safety margin (typically 20–30%)**, KTU-recommended values use $f_s = 1600$ Hz or higher (i.e., **$N = 32$ samples per cycle**). $$\boxed{f_s \approx 1.6 \text{ kHz to 4 kHz (32 to 80 samples per cycle)}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Aliasing Frequency Calculation **Given:** A signal component of frequency $f = 850$ Hz exists in the line current. The relay samples at $f_s = 1200$ Hz. **Find:** The alias frequency as seen by the relay. --- **Step 1 — Compute $f / f_s$.** $$\frac{f}{f_s} = \frac{850}{1200} \approx 0.708$$ **Step 2 — Identify nearest integer $k$.** The nearest integer to 0.708 is $k = 1$. **Step 3 — Apply alias formula.** $$f_{alias} = \vert f - k \cdot f_s \vert$$ $$f_{alias} = \vert 850 - 1 \times 1200 \vert = \vert -350 \vert = 350 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 4 — Physical interpretation.** The relay will mistakenly interpret the 850 Hz harmonic as a 350 Hz signal. Since 350 Hz is close to the 7th harmonic (350 Hz), this can cause **false overcurrent trips** in distance relays or differential relays. > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The anti-aliasing filter **must** cut off all frequencies above $f_s / 2 = 600$ Hz **before** the ADC. Without the filter, this alias would corrupt the relay. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: ADC Quantization Error **Given:** A 12-bit ADC with full-scale input range $\pm 10$ V (i.e., $V_{FS} = 20$ V peak-to-peak). **Find:** Quantization step $q$ and maximum quantization error. --- **Step 1 — Apply the quantization formula.** $$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{20}{2^{12}} = \frac{20}{4096}$$ $$q = 4.8828 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 4.883 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum quantization error.** The maximum error is half of the quantization step (mid-rise quantizer): $$e_{max} = \pm \frac{q}{2} = \pm 2.44 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 3 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR).** $$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 \text{ dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 73.99 \text{ dB}$$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Component vs. Function | Component | Domain | Function | Failure Mode if Removed | |---|---|---|---| | CT / PT | Input transducer | Step-down HV/HV signals | Relay destroyed | | Surge protection | Input protection | Clamp transients | Electronics damaged | | Aux. CT + Burden | Signal conditioning | Convert to $\pm 5$ V | ADC saturation / damage | | Anti-aliasing LP filter | Signal conditioning | Reject $f > f_s/2$ | False trips due to aliasing | | A/D Converter | Digitization | Sample and quantize | Relay cannot process signal | | Microprocessor / DSP | Decision logic | Run algorithm | No trip decision possible | | Trip output relay + CB | Output | Isolate fault | Fault not cleared | | Communication port | Output | SCADA / IEC 61850 | No remote monitoring | --- ### 3.5 Derivation 4: First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter Cutoff The anti-aliasing filter is typically a **second-order Butterworth** LPF. For a first-order RC filter, the cutoff frequency is: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ **Example:** Choose $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and target $f_c = 600$ Hz. **Step 1 — Solve for C.** $$C = \frac{1}{2\pi R f_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 600}$$ $$C = \frac{1}{3.7699 \times 10^7} \approx 26.5 \text{ nF}$$ **Step 2 — Verify the cutoff in rad/s.** $$\omega_c = 2\pi f_c = 2\pi \times 600 = 3770 \text{ rad/s}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Canonical Block Diagram of a Numerical Relay The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end signal flow** from the power system bus to the circuit breaker trip coil. This is the figure most commonly asked for in KTU ESE Module 3 questions. ```mermaid flowchart LR BUS[Power System Bus
HV Transmission Line] CT[Current Transformer
CT 1A or 5A] PT[Potential Transformer
PT 110/sqrt3 V] SP[Surge Protection
Varistor MOV] ACT[Auxiliary CT
Air Gap Core] B[B burden Resistor
I to V Conversion] AAF[Anti Aliasing Filter
Low Pass RC LPF] ADC[Analog to Digital Converter
12 to 16 bit ADC] MUX[Multiplexer
3 Phase Sampling] DSP[Microprocessor DSP
Protection Algorithm] MEM[Memory RAM ROM EEPROM] LOGIC[Trip Logic Comparator
Pickup vs Measured] TR[Trip Output Relay] CB[Circuit Breaker Trip Coil] SCADA[SCADA IEC 61850
Communication Port] HMI[Local HMI Display
LED LCD Keypad] BUS --> CT BUS --> PT CT --> SP PT --> SP SP --> ACT ACT --> B B --> AAF AAF --> ADC ADC --> MUX MUX --> DSP DSP <--> MEM DSP --> LOGIC LOGIC --> TR TR --> CB DSP --> SCADA DSP --> HMI classDef analog fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 classDef digital fill:#b4d8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef output fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000000 classDef source fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 class BUS,CT,PT,SP,ACT,B,AAF analog class ADC,MUX,DSP,MEM,LOGIC digital class TR,CB,SCADA,HMI output ``` ### 4.2 Functional Subsystem Architecture To emphasize the layered nature of the relay, the same system is decomposed into **three subsystems** below: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT_LAYER[Analog Input Subsystem] IA1[CT Secondary] IA2[PT Secondary] IA3[Surge Protection] IA4[Auxiliary CT and Burden] IA5[Anti Aliasing Filter] end subgraph DIGITAL_LAYER[Digital Processing Subsystem] ID1[Sample and Hold] ID2[Multiplexer MUX] ID3[A to D Converter] ID4[DSP Microprocessor] ID5[Algorithm Library] end subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER[Output and Communication Subsystem] IO1[Trip Logic Comparator] IO2[Trip Output Relay] IO3[Circuit Breaker] IO4[SCADA Communication] IO5[Local HMI Display] end IA1 --> IA3 --> IA4 --> IA5 --> ID1 --> ID2 --> ID3 --> ID4 --> IO1 --> IO2 --> IO3 IA2 --> IA3 ID4 --> ID5 ID4 --> IO4 ID4 --> IO5 ``` ### 4.3 Role of Each Block (Quick Reference Table) | Block | Block Name | Role | |---|---|---| | CT / PT | Transducer | Step down HV/LV signals | | MOV | Surge Suppressor | Clamp lightning transients | | Aux. CT | Conditioning | Reduce current to voltage | | LP Filter | Anti-Aliasing | Remove $f > f_s/2$ | | S/H | Sample-and-Hold | Freeze input during ADC conversion | | MUX | Multiplexer | Share one ADC across 3 phases + neutral | | ADC | Digitizer | Convert analog sample to binary | | DSP | Processor | Run protection algorithm | | RAM / ROM | Memory | Store samples, algorithm, settings | | Trip Relay | Output | Interface low-power logic to high-power CB | | CB | Final Actuator | Physically isolate fault | | SCADA port | Communication | Remote control & monitoring | --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any **six** functional blocks of a numerical relay and state the function of any **three** of them.* **Model Answer:** The six functional blocks of a numerical relay are: 1. **CT / PT (Transducers):** Step-down high magnitude current and voltage to standardized secondary values (1 A / 5 A; 110 V / $\sqrt{3}$). 2. **Surge Protection Circuit (MOV / Transorb):** Clamps high-voltage transients caused by lightning or switching surges to protect downstream electronics. 3. **Signal Conditioning (Auxiliary CT + Burden):** Further reduces current to a low voltage (typically $\pm 5$ V or $\pm 10$ V) compatible with the ADC input range. 4. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A low-pass filter that removes all frequency components above $f_s/2$, preventing false low-frequency aliases after sampling. 5. **A/D Converter:** Samples the analog signal at instants $nT_s$ and converts it into a binary digital word of $n$-bit resolution. 6. **Microprocessor / DSP + Output Stage:** Executes the protection algorithm (DFT, alpha-plane, etc.), compares the result with the pickup threshold, and issues a trip signal to the circuit breaker. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**. What is the minimum sampling frequency required for a relay processing signals with harmonics up to the **13th harmonic** in a **50 Hz** system?* **Model Answer:** **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:** *A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples provided the sampling frequency satisfies:* $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ **Computation:** - Highest signal frequency: $f_{max} = 13 \times 50 = 650$ Hz. - Minimum sampling frequency: $f_s = 2 \times 650 = 1300$ Hz. - This corresponds to **26 samples per cycle**. In practice, KTU-recommended design uses $f_s = 1600$ Hz (32 samples/cycle) for a 20% safety margin. --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the **functional building blocks of a numerical relay**. Explain the role of the **anti-aliasing filter** and the **A/D converter** in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **12-bit ADC** is used in a numerical relay with a full-scale input voltage of $\pm 5$ V. Compute the **(i)** quantization step size, **(ii)** maximum quantization error, and **(iii)** Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) in dB. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):** The numerical relay consists of the following sequential blocks: ``` CT/PT → Surge Protection → Aux CT + Burden → Anti-Aliasing Filter → Sample-and-Hold → Multiplexer → A/D Converter → Microprocessor/DSP → Memory (RAM/ROM) → Trip Logic → Trip Output Relay → Circuit Breaker ``` **Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter:** When a signal containing frequency $f > f_s/2$ is sampled, it folds back into the lower frequency range. This produces an **alias frequency** $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ that the relay mistakes for a legitimate power-system frequency component. This can cause **false trips** or **failure to trip**. To prevent this, an **analog low-pass filter** (typically a 2nd-order Butterworth with cutoff $f_c = f_s / 2$) is placed *before* the ADC. It rejects all frequency components above the Nyquist frequency, ensuring that the sampled signal is **band-limited** to $f_{max} < f_s/2$. **Role of A/D Converter:** The ADC performs two operations: 1. **Sampling** — captures the instantaneous amplitude of the filtered signal at discrete time instants $t = nT_s$, where $T_s = 1/f_s$. 2. **Quantization** — maps each sample amplitude to the nearest level among $2^n$ discrete levels, producing an $n$-bit binary word. The digital output is then fed to the microprocessor for algorithm execution. The **resolution** of the ADC determines the smallest detectable change in input — a higher $n$ means finer resolution and lower quantization error. --- ### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):** **Given:** - $n = 12$ bits - Full-scale input: $\pm 5$ V → $V_{FS} = 10$ V (peak-to-peak span) **(i) Quantization step size $q$:** $$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096}$$ $$\boxed{q \approx 2.441 \text{ mV}}$$ **(ii) Maximum quantization error $e_{max}$:** $$e_{max} = \pm \frac{q}{2} = \pm \frac{2.441}{2}$$ $$\boxed{e_{max} = \pm 1.221 \text{ mV}}$$ **(iii) Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR):** $$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$ $$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76$$ $$\boxed{\text{SQNR} \approx 74.0 \text{ dB}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**. Why is the **anti-aliasing filter** placed **before** the A/D converter and **not after**? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A numerical relay samples a current signal at **$f_s = 2$ kHz**. The signal contains a harmonic component of **850 Hz**. Determine the **alias frequency** as observed by the relay. If the relay uses a **first-order RC low-pass filter** with $R = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, calculate the **capacitor value** required for the cutoff frequency to equal the Nyquist frequency. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):** **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:** *To reconstruct a band-limited analog signal whose maximum frequency is $f_{max}$, the sampling frequency must satisfy:* $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ Equivalently, the **Nyquist frequency** is $f_N = f_s / 2$, and any input frequency above $f_N$ will alias. **Why the filter is placed BEFORE the ADC:** The anti-aliasing filter must be **analog** and placed before the ADC because: 1. **Once a signal is aliased**, the alias becomes indistinguishable from a legitimate low-frequency component. **No digital filter can remove an alias** that has already been folded into the baseband during sampling. 2. Digital filters operate on **already-sampled data**; if the input spectrum contained energy above $f_s/2$, the damage is already done at the sampling instant. 3. An **analog low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$** ensures that the input to the ADC is strictly band-limited, satisfying the Nyquist condition. Placing the filter after the ADC is therefore **useless** for anti-aliasing purposes (it can only be used for additional digital smoothing, not alias prevention). --- ### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):** **Given:** - $f_s = 2 \text{ kHz} = 2000 \text{ Hz}$ - $f = 850 \text{ Hz}$ **Step 1 — Compute $f / f_s$:** $$\frac{f}{f_s} = \frac{850}{2000} = 0.425$$ **Step 2 — Identify nearest integer $k$.** Nearest integer to 0.425 is $k = 0$. **Step 3 — Compute alias frequency:** $$f_{alias} = \vert f - k \cdot f_s \vert = \vert 850 - 0 \vert = 850 \text{ Hz}$$ Since 850 Hz > Nyquist (1000 Hz? No — $f_N = f_s/2 = 1000$ Hz, so 850 Hz is below Nyquist). Let us recheck. > **Correction:** $f_N = 1000$ Hz, and the signal at 850 Hz is **below** the Nyquist limit, so it will not alias in this particular case. However, if the same harmonic appeared at, say, $f = 1850$ Hz, then $f_N = 1000$ Hz and aliasing would occur. **For $f = 1850$ Hz (recomputed for alias example):** $$f_{alias} = \vert 1850 - 1 \times 2000 \vert = 150 \text{ Hz}$$ The 1850 Hz harmonic appears as a 150 Hz component — within the protective band — and could cause false operation. **Capacitor calculation for $f_c = f_N = 1000$ Hz with $R = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$:** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} \implies C = \frac{1}{2\pi R f_c}$$ $$C = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4700 \times 1000} = \frac{1}{2.9531 \times 10^7}$$ $$\boxed{C \approx 33.86 \text{ nF}}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where Marks are Lost):** > > 1. **Confusing "samples per second" with "samples per cycle":** Always remember $f_s = N \cdot f$. For 50 Hz and 32 samples/cycle, $f_s = 1600$ Hz, NOT 32 Hz. > > 2. **Wrong alias formula:** Students often write $f_{alias} = f - f_s$ without absolute value or without the integer $k$. Always use $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ with $k$ = nearest integer to $f/f_s$. > > 3. **Quantization formula sign error:** Some students write $q = V_{FS}/2^{n-1}$ instead of $q = V_{FS}/2^n$. The correct exponent is exactly $n$. > > 4. **Misplaced anti-aliasing filter:** Many students describe the filter being inside or after the microprocessor. **The filter MUST be analog and BEFORE the ADC.** This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction if missed. > > 5. **Forgetting the multiplexer:** When asked to "draw the block diagram," students often omit the **MUX** that allows a single ADC to serve three phases. Adding the MUX is a 1-mark differentiator. > > 6. **Not stating the function of each block:** A block diagram without brief functional labels loses at least 3 marks in a 7-mark sub-question. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ Numerical relay = **CT/PT → Anti-Aliasing Filter → ADC → Microprocessor → Trip Output**. - ✅ **Seven canonical blocks:** Transducer, Surge Protection, Aux CT + Burden, Anti-Aliasing Filter, ADC, MPU/DSP, Trip Output. - ✅ **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. For 50 Hz + 13th harmonic → $f_s \geq 1300$ Hz; practical: **$f_s = 1600$ Hz (32 samples/cycle)**. - ✅ **Alias frequency:** $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$, where $k$ = nearest integer to $f/f_s$. - ✅ **ADC quantization step:** $q = V_{FS}/2^n$. - ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter placement:** **Analog, BEFORE the ADC.** Cutoff frequency $f_c = f_s/2$. - ✅ **Microprocessor functions:** Sampling, algorithm execution (DFT / alpha-plane / travelling wave), logic comparison, trip decision, self-test, communication. - ✅ **Typical ADC resolution:** 12–16 bits; **SQNR** = $6.02n + 1.76$ dB. - ✅ **Output stage:** Trip Relay + Circuit Breaker Trip Coil. - ✅ **Communication:** IEC 61850 / SCADA / local HMI. - ✅ **Advantages:** Multi-function, self-test, event recording, adaptive, remote communication, IEC 61850 compatible. - ✅ **Operating time:** 0.5 to 2 cycles (10–40 ms for 50 Hz). - ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter type:** 2nd-order Butterworth LPF (most common KTU answer). ---

topics.title: Processing Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processing Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Processing Unit** in a digital protection system is the intelligent computational core that receives conditioned analog signals from the transducer secondary, converts them into a discrete-time numerical form, and applies deterministic mathematical algorithms to extract the power system's phasor information (magnitude, phase angle, frequency, impedance, and symmetrical components) for the purpose of executing protection logic and issuing trip/control commands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The Processing Unit comprises the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuitry**, **Anti-Aliasing Filters**, and a **Digital Signal Processor (DSP) / Microprocessor** that executes phasor estimation algorithms (DFT, FFT, Kalman filtering, etc.) on the sampled power system signals to derive the relaying quantities (impedance, current differential, power swing detection, etc.) required for decision-making.

### 1.2 Constituent Functional Blocks

A canonical digital relay processing unit consists of **five sequential sub-systems**:

| # | Block | Primary Function |
|---|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Surge Protection / Surge Capacitor** | Divert high-frequency transients and lightning-induced surges |
| 2 | **Auxiliary CT / PT (Isolation)** | Provide galvanic isolation and scale the secondary quantities |
| 3 | **Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-Pass)** | Band-limit the analog input to satisfy the Nyquist criterion |
| 4 | **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit** | Freeze the instantaneous analog value during the ADC conversion time |
| 5 | **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** | Quantize the held analog value into a binary code (typically 12–16 bit) |
| 6 | **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** | Execute DFT/FFT, filtering, phasor extraction, and protection logic |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Universal Translator Analogy:**
> Imagine you are a customs officer at an international airport. Foreign travellers (analog power-system signals like 50 Hz current and voltage) speak many "languages" — some speak fast (high frequency), some are jittery (noise), some are loud (high magnitude). The **Processing Unit** is your translation desk:
> 1. The **anti-aliasing filter** is the *language interpreter* that filters out irrelevant fast chatter (frequencies above the Nyquist limit), retaining only meaningful conversation.
> 2. The **Sample-and-Hold** is the *notary* who takes a snapshot of the speaker's words at uniform intervals.
> 3. The **ADC** is the *stenographer* who converts spoken words (analog amplitude) into written text (binary numbers).
> 4. The **DSP** is the *judge* who reads the written transcript and decides — based on protocol (protection algorithm) — whether the traveller is genuine or a threat (fault vs. normal load).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Examinable)

- **Sampling Rate (f_s)**: For 50 Hz systems, KTU-protective relays typically sample at **f_s = 600 Hz to 4800 Hz** (12 to 96 samples/cycle).
- **ADC Resolution**: **12 bits to 16 bits** (commercial) / **18 to 24 bits** (precision).
- **Anti-Aliasing Cut-off Frequency**: Typically **f_c ≈ 0.4 × f_s** (Butterworth or elliptic low-pass).
- **Quantization Step (q)**: **q = V_range / 2^n** where n = number of bits.
- **Standard**: IEC 60255-118-1 for numerical protection; IEEE C37.118 for synchrophasors.
