- **Operating Temperature**: Industrial relays operate at **−25°C to +70°C**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently anchor questions around the **Nyquist rate**, **aliasing phenomenon**, and the **recursive DFT** algorithm. Master these three concepts and you secure ~70% of the marks in any question on the Processing Unit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Signal Conditioning Chain — Step-by-Step Logic

The flow of an analog signal from the power system CT/PT secondary to the relay's decision algorithm follows a **deterministic, lossless (in ideal sense) sequence**:

**Step 1 — Surge Attenuation:**
- A passive R-C or MOV (Metal-Oxide Varistor) network shunts high-voltage transients (lightning, switching) to ground.
- Time constant typically **τ = 1 ms to 10 ms**.

**Step 2 — Auxiliary CT/VT Scaling:**
- Reduces 5 A / 110 V secondary to a millivolt level (e.g., **±5 V** or **±10 V**) suitable for the relay's analog front-end.

**Step 3 — Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filtering:**
- The most critical analog block. A **Butterworth 2nd-order** or **elliptic 4th-order** active filter is preferred for its sharp roll-off.
- Cut-off frequency $f_c$ is selected such that $f_c \le \dfrac{f_s}{2.56}$ (provides ~8% guard band against the Nyquist frequency).

**Step 4 — Sample-and-Hold (S/H):**
- A MOSFET switch followed by a hold capacitor $C_h$ and a buffer amplifier.
- **Aperture time** (switching transient): 10–50 ns.
- **Acquisition time**: 1–10 μs.
- **Droop rate** (droop in held voltage due to capacitor leakage): typically < 1 mV/ms.

**Step 5 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):**
- Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC is the workhorse for protection relays (12–16 bits, 1–10 μs conversion).
- Delta-Sigma (Σ-Δ) ADC is used where ultra-high resolution (≥18 bits) and DC accuracy are required (e.g., metering, capacitor bank protection).

**Step 6 — Digital Signal Processing (DSP):**
- Performs the phasor extraction via **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)**, **Full Cycle / Half Cycle DFT**, **Wavelet Transform**, or **Kalman Filter**.
- Decision logic compares the extracted phasor (impedance, current, voltage) against the **pickup characteristic** (Mho, Quadrilateral, Impedance circle).

### 2.2 The Sampling Theorem (Nyquist-Shannon Criterion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling Theorem (Board-Examiner Favourite):**
> A band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be reconstructed exactly from its samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ if and only if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$. The minimum sampling rate $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$ is the **Nyquist rate**.

For a 50 Hz power system, the highest fault-generated frequency component of interest is typically the **3rd to 5th harmonic** (for transformer inrush and harmonic restraint), giving $f_{max} \approx 250$ Hz. Hence the Nyquist rate is **500 Hz**, and protection relays use a practical $f_s = 600$ Hz to 4800 Hz.

### 2.3 Aliasing — The Catastrophic Error

If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, the higher frequency components "fold back" into the baseband and appear as spurious low-frequency signals. This phenomenon is called **aliasing**, and it is irreversible — the original information is permanently lost.

$$f_{apparent} = \vert f_{actual} - k \cdot f_s \vert, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**Example:** Sampling a **440 Hz** signal at $f_s = 600$ Hz produces an alias at $\vert 440 - 600 \vert = 160$ Hz — which a 50 Hz relay would interpret as a slow, weird signal of dubious origin. Hence the **anti-aliasing filter is non-negotiable**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU numerical/proof questions on the Processing Unit. Memorize every entry.

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Nyquist rate | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$ | 500 Hz for 250 Hz signal |
| 2 | Sampling period | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | 0.2 ms for $f_s = 5$ kHz |
| 3 | Samples per cycle | $N = \dfrac{f_s}{f_{system}}$ | 96 samples/cycle (typical) |
| 4 | Quantization step | $q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | 2.44 mV for 5 V FS, 12-bit |
| 5 | Quantization SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 6.02 n + 1.76$ | 74 dB for n=12 |
| 6 | DFT (single bin) | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | — |
| 7 | Recursive DFT (Full Cycle) | $X_k^{new} = X_k^{old} + \dfrac{x_{k+N} - x_k}{N}$ | One multiplication per sample |
| 8 | Phasor magnitude | $\vert X \vert = \sqrt{X_R^2 + X_I^2}$ | pu or A, V |
| 9 | Phasor phase | $\angle X = \arctan\left(\dfrac{X_I}{X_R}\right)$ | radians or degrees |
| 10 | Apparent impedance | $Z_{apparent} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{I_{ph}}$ | ohms per phase |
| 11 | Alias frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f_{actual} - m \cdot f_s \vert$ | Hz |
| 12 | Filter order effect | $H(s) = \dfrac{\omega_c^n}{\prod_{k}(s - s_k)}$ | Butterworth pole pattern |

### 2.5 Why the Processing Unit Matters — Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why study this for KTU?**
> 1. The Processing Unit is the **brain** of every modern numerical relay (Schweitzer SEL-487B, ABB REL670, GE UR-series, Siemens 7SA86).
> 2. Phasor extraction quality directly determines **relay operating time** (typically 0.5 cycle to 3 cycles, i.e., 10 ms to 60 ms for 50 Hz).
> 3. **Wide-Area Monitoring Systems (WAMS)** and **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** depend on high-fidelity processing units for sub-second situational awareness.
> 4. In **IEC 61850**-based substations, the processing unit outputs are mapped to **GOOSE messages** for substation-wide protection coordination.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) for Phasor Extraction

A pure sinusoidal current waveform from a CT secondary is of the form:

$$i(t) = I_{m} \cos(\omega t + \phi) = I_{m} \left[ \cos(\omega t) \cos\phi - \sin(\omega t) \sin\phi \right]$$

This can be expressed in phasor form as:

$$I = \dfrac{I_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} \angle \phi \quad \text{(RMS phasor)}$$

**Step 1 — Continuous Fourier Series:**
The current $i(t)$ is periodic with period $T = 2\pi / \omega$. Its Fourier series representation is:

$$i(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega t) + b_n \sin(n\omega t) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Sampling:**
We sample $i(t)$ at uniform intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$, yielding the discrete sequence $i[n] = i(nT_s)$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$ over one fundamental period (i.e., N samples per cycle).

**Step 3 — Discrete Trigonometric Coefficients:**
Using the orthogonality of the sampled sinusoids, the discrete Fourier coefficients are:

$$a_1 = \dfrac{2}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} i[n] \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N}\right) \quad \text{(in-phase component)}$$

$$b_1 = \dfrac{2}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} i[n] \sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N}\right) \quad \text{(quadrature component)}$$

**Step 4 — Relating Coefficients to Phasor:**
Comparing with the continuous form, we identify $I_{m} \cos\phi = a_1$ and $I_{m} \sin\phi = b_1$. Therefore:

$$I_{m} = \sqrt{a_1^2 + b_1^2}, \qquad \phi = \arctan\left(\dfrac{b_1}{a_1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — DFT Form (Complex Exponentials):**
By Euler's identity, the complex phasor is:

$$X[1] = a_1 - j b_1 = \dfrac{2}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} i[n] e^{-j 2\pi n / N}$$

More generally, for any harmonic $k$:

$$X[k] = \dfrac{2}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} i[n] e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "2/N" scaling yields the **peak amplitude**. For RMS phasor, use $X_{RMS}[k] = X[k] / \sqrt{2}$. The choice of scaling is a frequent KTU 2-mark trick question.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Recursive Full-Cycle DFT (Running Sum)

The full-cycle DFT at sample $k$ is:

$$X_k = \dfrac{2}{N} \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} i_{k-N+r} e^{-j 2\pi r / N}$$

When a new sample $i_{k+1}$ arrives, the new DFT becomes:

$$X_{k+1} = \dfrac{2}{N} \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} i_{k+1-N+r} e^{-j 2\pi r / N}$$

Subtracting and adding, and shifting the index, we obtain the **elegant recursion**:

$$X_{k+1} = X_k + \dfrac{2}{N} \left( i_{k+1} - i_{k+1-N} \right) e^{-j 0} = X_k + \dfrac{2}{N}\left(i_{new} - i_{old}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **operating principle of the modern numerical relay**: at every new sample, only ONE complex multiplication and ONE complex addition are needed. This is why a relay can execute a 96-sample/cycle DFT at 5 kHz on a modest 32-bit DSP.

### 3.3 Derivation of Quantization Error Bound

A unipolar ADC with full-scale voltage $V_{FS}$ and $n$ bits has quantization step:

$$q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

The maximum quantization error (worst case) is:

$$e_{max} = \pm \dfrac{q}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

Assuming the quantization noise is uniformly distributed over $\pm q/2$, its RMS value is:

$$e_{rms} = \dfrac{q}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n \sqrt{12}}$$

The **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** for a full-scale sinusoidal input is:

$$SQNR = 6.02 n + 1.76 \quad \text{dB}$$

**Numerical Example (12-bit ADC, 5 V FS):**

$$q = \dfrac{5}{4096} = 1.22 \text{ mV}$$

$$e_{rms} = \dfrac{1.22 \text{ mV}}{\sqrt{12}} = 0.353 \text{ mV}$$

$$SQNR = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

This justifies why **12-bit ADC is the industry standard for 50 Hz numerical protection** — the 74 dB dynamic range is sufficient to resolve both high-magnitude fault currents and low-magnitude load currents with adequate margin.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Data Window | Harmonic Rejection | Computational Cost | Speed | Use Case |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------|----------|
| Full-Cycle DFT | 1 cycle (20 ms) | Excellent (zeros at harmonic frequencies) | N complex mults | 1 cycle delay | Distance, overcurrent |
| Half-Cycle DFT | 0.5 cycle (10 ms) | Good (rejects even harmonics) | N/2 mults | 0.5 cycle delay | Fast differential |
| Recursive DFT | 1 cycle sliding | Excellent | 1 mult + 1 add / sample | Real-time | All digital relays |
| FFT (Cooley-Tukey) | N log N | Excellent | N log₂N | Block processing | Metering, COMTRADE |
| Kalman Filter | Adaptive | Excellent | High (matrix) | Sub-cycle | Frequency, P-class PMU |
| Wavelet Transform | Multi-resolution | Excellent (transients) | Moderate | Sub-cycle | DC offset, travelling waves |
| Cosine Filter (Mimic) | 1 cycle | Moderate | Very low | < 1 cycle | Legacy relays |
| Walsh Function | 1 cycle | Poor | Very low | Fast | Early digital relays |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"Compare DFT and FFT for protection applications."* The table above gives you the answer. **DFT = real-time, recursive, one-bin extraction**; **FFT = batch processing, all harmonics, off-line / metering**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Processing Unit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU[Power System Primary: 400 kV / 11 kV] --> CT[CT and PT Secondary: 5A or 110V]
    CT --> SP[Surge Protection MOV plus RC]
    SP --> AUX[Auxiliary CT and VT Scaling to plus minus 5V]
    AUX --> AAF[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter fc less than fs by 2.56]
    AAF --> SH[Sample and Hold Circuit Aperture less than 50 ns]
    SH --> MUX[Analog Multiplexer 4 to 8 Channels]
    MUX --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter 12 to 16 bit SAR]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor 32 bit Floating Point]
    DSP --> PHA[Phasor Extraction DFT and Kalman]
    PHA --> LOG[Protection Logic Impedance and Differential]
    LOG --> TRIP[Trip Coil and GOOSE Publisher]
    DSP --> CLK[PLL and Oscillator 5 to 10 MHz]
    CLK --> SH
    CLK --> ADC
    DSP --> LCD[Human Machine Interface HMI]
    DSP --> COM[Communication Ethernet IEC 61850]
```

**Explanation of the flow:**

- The **Power System Primary** delivers high voltage / current.
- **CT/PT** steps this down to a safe secondary level.
- **Surge Protection** absorbs transients (e.g., 10 kA lightning pulse).
- **Auxiliary CT/VT** further scales to the relay's analog input range.
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter** restricts bandwidth to satisfy Nyquist.
- **Sample and Hold** captures instantaneous values.
- **Multiplexer** routes multiple channels (3-phase V + 3-phase I + neutral) sequentially to a single ADC.
- **ADC** digitizes each sample to a 12-/16-bit word.
- **DSP** executes the DFT/phasor extraction and protection algorithm.
- **Trip Coil** is energized on a trip decision; **IEC 61850 GOOSE** publishes the event to peers.

### 4.2 Anti-Aliasing Filter — Frequency Response Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Input Signal 0 to 10 kHz] --> LPF[2nd Order Butterworth Low Pass Filter fc = 1.5 kHz]
    LPF --> ADCX[ADC Sampling at fs = 4 kHz]
    ADCX --> DFTX[DFT Bin Extraction at Fundamental 50 Hz]
    DFTX --> OUT[Clean 50 Hz Phasor Magnitude and Phase]
    LPF -. attenuates .- NOISE[Above 2 kHz: -40 dB per decade attenuation]
    ADCX -. Nyquist limit .-> NYQ[Nyquist = 2 kHz]
```

### 4.3 Recursive DFT Update — Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OLD[Old Sample Buffer: x k to x k-N+1] --> REC[Recursive DFT Recomputation: Xk new = Xk old plus 2 over N times x new minus x old]
    NEW[New Sample xk+1 Arrives] --> REC
    REC --> OUTX[Updated Phasor: Magnitude Mk+1 and Phase Phik+1]
    OUTX --> PROT[Protection Characteristic Mho or Quadrilateral]
    PROT --> DEC[Decision: Trip or Restrain]
```

### 4.4 Sampling Phasor Extraction — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Sample Window: 20 ms full cycle N = 96 samples] --> S2[Multiply by Cosine Reference: 2 cos 2 pi n by N]
    S2 --> S3[Sum and Scale: Real part X_R]
    S1 --> S4[Multiply by Sine Reference: 2 sin 2 pi n by N]
    S4 --> S5[Sum and Scale: Imaginary part X_I]
    S3 --> S6[Compute Magnitude: sqrt X_R squared plus X_I squared]
    S5 --> S6
    S3 --> S7[Compute Phase: arctan X_I over X_R]
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S8[Send to Impedance Calculator: Z = V by I]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Compare with Pickup Characteristic]
    S9 --> S10{Inside Trip Zone?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Issue Trip Command to Breaker]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Reset and Wait for Next Window]
```

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU board exam, when asked to "draw the block diagram of a digital relay's processing unit", always include the **PLL/clock generation** block — it shows the examiner that you understand the synchronization between sampling and the system frequency. Many students lose 2 marks by omitting it.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Explain the function of an anti-aliasing filter in a digital protection relay. Why is it placed before the analog-to-digital converter?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **anti-aliasing filter** is a low-pass analog filter placed immediately before the ADC. Its function is to **band-limit the input signal to below half the sampling frequency** ($f_{max} \le f_s / 2$) in accordance with the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**.

If the input contains frequency components above $f_s/2$, they undergo **aliasing** — they fold back into the 0-to-$f_s/2$ baseband and appear as spurious low-frequency content. Once aliased, the original information is **irreversibly corrupted**, and the relay may mis-operate (false trip or failure to trip).

Hence, the anti-aliasing filter is placed **before the S/H and ADC** to ensure only legitimate, band-limited signals are quantized. A **Butterworth 2nd-order** filter with cut-off $f_c = f_s / 2.56$ is the industry standard, providing an ~8% guard band.

**[1 mark: function, 1 mark: Nyquist context, 1 mark: filter choice & location]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define quantization error. For a 16-bit ADC with a full-scale input of ±10 V, calculate the quantization step size and the maximum quantization error.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Quantization error is the difference between the actual continuous analog input voltage and the nearest discrete digital code produced by the ADC.

**Quantization step:**

$$q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{20 \text{ V}}{2^{16}} = \dfrac{20}{65536} = 305.18 \, \mu V$$

**Maximum quantization error (worst-case):**

$$e_{max} = \pm \dfrac{q}{2} = \pm 152.59 \, \mu V$$

**SQNR for verification:**

$$SQNR = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$

**[1 mark: definition, 1 mark: q calculation, 1 mark: e_max calculation]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of the processing unit of a numerical relay**. Clearly state the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **recursive full-cycle Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** algorithm used for phasor extraction in digital protection. Show that the algorithm requires only **one complex multiplication per sample**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(a) — Block Diagram & Block Roles (7 marks)

The processing unit of a numerical relay processes the CT/PT secondary signals to extract the power system phasor for protection decision-making. The block-level architecture is shown below:

| # | Block | Function |
|---|-------|----------|
| 1 | **Surge Protection (MOV/RC)** | Diverts HV transients; protects the relay input |
| 2 | **Auxiliary CT/VT** | Scales 5 A / 110 V to ±5 V / ±10 V for the analog front-end |
| 3 | **Anti-Aliasing Filter** | Low-pass filter with $f_c \le f_s/2.56$; prevents aliasing |
| 4 | **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** | Freezes the analog value during ADC conversion |
| 5 | **Multiplexer (MUX)** | Routes 6–8 input channels (3V + 3I + neutral + check) to one ADC |
| 6 | **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** | 12–16 bit SAR; quantizes analog sample to binary code |
| 7 | **PLL / Sampling Clock** | Synchronizes sampling to the power system frequency |
| 8 | **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** | Executes DFT/phasor extraction and protection logic |
| 9 | **Protection Algorithm** | Computes impedance, current diff, or power swing |
| 10 | **Decision Logic / Trip Output** | Issues trip/close command to breaker via trip coil |
| 11 | **Communication Port** | IEC 61850 / DNP3 / Modbus for SCADA integration |

**Key features:**
- All processing **post-ADC is digital** — immune to drift, noise, component aging.
- The **DSP is typically a 32-bit floating-point processor** (e.g., TMS320C6xxx) executing the relaying algorithm every sample.
- Total **operating time** of a numerical relay: **0.5 to 3 cycles** (10–60 ms at 50 Hz).

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(b) — Recursive DFT Derivation (7 marks)

The fundamental-frequency DFT of a discrete signal $x[n]$ over a full cycle of N samples is:

$$X_R = \dfrac{2}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k] \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

$$X_I = \dfrac{2}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k] \sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

The complex phasor is $X = X_R - j X_I$.

**Step 1 — Define DFT at sample index $r$:**

$$X_r = \dfrac{2}{N} \sum_{k=r-N+1}^{r} x[k] e^{-j 2\pi k / N}$$

**Step 2 — Define DFT at sample index $r+1$:**

$$X_{r+1} = \dfrac{2}{N} \sum_{k=r-N+2}^{r+1} x[k] e^{-j 2\pi k / N}$$

**Step 3 — Take the difference $X_{r+1} - X_r$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{r+1} - X_r &= \dfrac{2}{N} \left[ x[r+1] e^{-j 2\pi (r+1) / N} - x[r-N+1] e^{-j 2\pi (r-N+1) / N} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the periodicity of the complex exponential** $e^{-j 2\pi (r-N+1) / N} = e^{-j 2\pi r / N} \cdot e^{j 2\pi} = e^{-j 2\pi r / N}$ (because $e^{j 2\pi} = 1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{r+1} - X_r &= \dfrac{2}{N} \left[ x[r+1] - x[r-N+1] \right] e^{-j 2\pi r / N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — The recursive update formula:**

$$\boxed{X_{r+1} = X_r + \dfrac{2}{N} \left( x_{new} - x_{old} \right) e^{-j 2\pi r / N}}$$

**Step 6 — Computational cost analysis:**

At each new sample, the relay must:
- Compute the difference $(x_{new} - x_{old})$: **1 subtraction**.
- Multiply by the rotating phasor $e^{-j 2\pi r / N}$: **1 complex multiplication**.
- Add to the previous phasor $X_r$: **1 complex addition**.

**Total: 1 complex multiplication + 1 complex addition per sample.** ✓

This is why **modern numerical relays** can perform continuous phasor tracking at sampling rates of 1–5 kHz using modest computational hardware — the recursive DFT is the enabling algorithm.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** State and prove the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**. Explain the phenomenon of **aliasing** with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power system signal contains a **fundamental 50 Hz** and a **7th harmonic (350 Hz)**. Determine the **minimum sampling frequency** to avoid aliasing. If the actual sampling frequency is **600 Hz**, calculate the **apparent (aliased) frequency** of the 7th harmonic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(a) — Sampling Theorem & Aliasing (7 marks)

**Statement:**
A band-limited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz can be reconstructed exactly from its samples $x(nT_s)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ Hz.

**Proof Sketch:**

Consider a signal $x(t)$ band-limited to $f_{max}$. Its ideal sampled form is:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s)$$

Taking the Fourier transform and using the impulse-train modulation property:

$$X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

This is a **periodic replication** of the spectrum $X(f)$ at intervals of $f_s$.

**Case 1 — $f_s > 2 f_{max}$:** The spectral replicas do not overlap; a low-pass filter with cut-off $f_c$ where $f_{max} < f_c < (f_s - f_{max})$ can recover $X(f)$ uniquely. The original signal is reconstructed by inverse Fourier transform.

**Case 2 — $f_s < 2 f_{max}$:** The spectral replicas **overlap** (aliasing), corrupting the baseband copy of $X(f)$. Recovery is impossible.

**Hence, the minimum sampling rate is $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$.**

**Aliasing — Numerical Example:**
A signal $x(t) = \cos(2\pi \cdot 440 \, t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 600$ Hz. The sample sequence is $x[n] = \cos(2\pi \cdot 440 n / 600) = \cos(2\pi \cdot (440 - 600) n / 600) = \cos(2\pi \cdot 160 n / 600)$ after applying the identity $\cos(\theta) = \cos(-\theta)$.

Thus the **reconstructed signal appears to have a frequency of 160 Hz** — a complete misrepresentation of the original 440 Hz tone. This is aliasing.

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Fundamental frequency: $f_1 = 50$ Hz
- 7th harmonic: $f_7 = 7 \times 50 = 350$ Hz
- Highest frequency of interest: $f_{max} = 350$ Hz

**Part 1 — Minimum Sampling Frequency (Nyquist Rate):**

$$f_{s,min} = 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 350 = 700 \text{ Hz}$$

**Part 2 — Apparent (Aliased) Frequency at $f_s = 600$ Hz:**

Since $f_s = 600$ Hz $< 2 \times 350 = 700$ Hz, aliasing **will** occur.

The aliased frequency is the difference between the actual frequency and the nearest multiple of $f_s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{alias} &= \vert f_7 - 1 \cdot f_s \vert \\
&= \vert 350 - 600 \vert \\
&= 250 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The 350 Hz harmonic, when sampled at 600 Hz, will appear as a **250 Hz** signal — which the DFT bin of the 5th harmonic (250 Hz) will incorrectly pick up. This is a **classic KTU "trick" scenario**: a fault-generated 7th harmonic gets interpreted as a 5th harmonic, potentially defeating harmonic restraint logic in transformer differential protection.

**Remedies:**
- Increase $f_s$ to $\ge 700$ Hz (practical choice: 1200 Hz or 2400 Hz).
- Use a **steeper anti-aliasing filter** to attenuate the 7th harmonic before sampling.
- Use **digital filtering post-ADC** with high-Q notch filters at harmonic frequencies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing RMS and Peak scaling in DFT** (1 mark lost): If the question asks for RMS phasor, you MUST divide by $\sqrt{2}$. If it asks for peak, you do NOT.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2/N scaling factor** (2 marks lost): The DFT defined as $\frac{2}{N}\sum x[n]e^{-j...}$ gives the **peak amplitude**. The unity-scaled sum $\sum x[n]e^{-j...}$ gives the peak times N/2. Always state your normalization explicitly.
> 3. **Missing the rotating phasor $e^{-j 2\pi r / N}$ in the recursive DFT** (2 marks lost): Students often write the recursion as $X_{r+1} = X_r + (x_{new} - x_{old})/N$, which is WRONG. The rotating phasor is essential because the oldest sample's phase reference must be updated.
> 4. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter's cut-off formula** (1 mark lost): Use $f_c = f_s / 2.56$ for 8% guard band, NOT $f_s / 2$.
> 5. **Units mismatch in quantization error** (1 mark lost): $q$ and $e_{max}$ are in **Volts**, NOT bits. Always include units.
> 6. **Aliasing arithmetic errors** (1 mark lost): Use the absolute value carefully. $f_{alias} = |f - k \cdot f_s|$ where $k$ is the integer that minimizes the difference.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Processing Unit**

- [x] **Processing Unit** = Anti-aliasing filter + S/H + MUX + ADC + DSP + Protection Logic.
- [x] **Nyquist Rate** $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$; for a 50 Hz system with 5th harmonic, $f_s \ge 500$ Hz.
- [x] **Anti-aliasing filter** is a low-pass filter with $f_c = f_s / 2.56$, placed **before** the S/H and ADC.
- [x] **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** freezes the analog value; aperture time < 50 ns; droop rate < 1 mV/ms.
- [x] **ADC resolution** for protection: **12 to 16 bits** (SAR type); $q = V_{FS} / 2^n$.
- [x] **Quantization SNR**: $SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB (n = number of bits).
- [x] **DFT phasor extraction** uses the $X_R = \frac{2}{N}\sum x[n]\cos(\frac{2\pi n}{N})$ and $X_I$ forms.
- [x] **Phasor magnitude**: $|X| = \sqrt{X_R^2 + X_I^2}$; **Phase**: $\angle X = \arctan(X_I / X_R)$.
- [x] **Recursive DFT** requires only **1 complex multiplication + 1 addition per sample** — this is the key innovation enabling real-time numerical protection.
- [x] **Aliasing formula**: $f_{alias} = |f_{actual} - k \cdot f_s|$; once aliased, original info is **lost forever**.
- [x] **Half-cycle DFT** is faster (10 ms delay) but sensitive to even harmonics and DC offset.
- [x] **Full-cycle DFT** is the industry standard (20 ms delay) — completely rejects all DC offset and integer harmonics.
- [x] **Wavelet transform** is preferred for **transient / travelling-wave** applications (e.g., HVDC, UHV line protection).
- [x] **Kalman filter** is used in **PMU / P-class phasor estimation** where sub-cycle accuracy and frequency tracking are required.
- [x] **IEC 60255-118-1** and **IEEE C37.118** are the governing standards for numerical protection and synchrophasors.
- [x] **Operating time** of a modern numerical relay: **0.5 to 3 cycles** (10–60 ms for 50 Hz).
- [x] **Total vector error (TVE)** for PMU compliance must be < 1% (P-class) or < 3% (M-class) per IEEE C37.118.
- [x] **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** synchronizes the sampling clock to the system frequency — critical for accurate DFT phase angles.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked a 14-mark design question on the Processing Unit, structure your answer as: **(1) Block diagram with labels → (2) Explanation of each block → (3) Justification of choices (e.g., 12-bit ADC, 2nd-order Butterworth) → (4) Worked numerical example**. This four-part structure mirrors the KTU model answer key and almost always secures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Machine Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Human Machine Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Machine Interface (HMI)** in the context of **Digital Protection of Power Systems** is defined as the hardware and software architecture that enables two-way communication, supervision, command, and control between the human operator (substation/control center engineer) and the digital protection, automation, and measurement equipment (Numerical Relays, IEDs, RTUs, BCUs, and SCADA masters).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the HMI forms the **uppermost layer of the Substation Automation Architecture (SAA)**, sitting above the **Station Level (Station Bus)**, **Bay Level**, and **Process Level** of the **IEC 61850 standard**. It is the single point of interaction through which a human operator perceives the dynamic state of the power network and issues control directives.

The HMI in a modern digital substation typically comprises:

- **Mimic Displays** (Single-Line Diagrams — SLD)
- **Annunciator Panels** (Alarm Annunciation Windows)
- **Status Indicators** (Breaker ON/OFF, Isolator positions, Earth-switch positions)
- **Measurement Displays** (Voltage, Current, Frequency, MW, MVAR)
- **Event and Alarm Logs**
- **Trending and Disturbance Records (DR)**
- **Control Push-Buttons / Soft Keys / Touch Screens**
- **Data Acquisition Front-End (SCADA / EMS / DMS Gateway)**

> [!NOTE]
> **HMI vs SCADA vs EMS — Critical Distinction**
> - **HMI** = The *visual and tactile front-end* the operator sees.
> - **SCADA** = The *Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition* system that gathers field data.
> - **EMS** = *Energy Management System* — adds analytical & optimization layers (State Estimation, OPF, ALFC) on top of SCADA.
> The HMI is the **window**, SCADA is the **nervous system**, and EMS is the **brain**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cockpit Analogy

Imagine a Boeing 747 cockpit. The pilot does not have direct mechanical linkages to the engines or flaps anymore — everything is mediated through a **Glass Cockpit** with multiple LCD screens, annunciator lights, soft-keys, and a side-stick controller. The pilot pushes a button → a digital signal travels through avionics buses → an actuator moves → telemetry returns → the display updates.

> A **digital substation HMI is the "Glass Cockpit" of the power grid.**

- The **pilot** = the **Substation Engineer / Load Dispatcher**
- The **LCD screens** = the **HMI Mimic Display**
- The **avionics bus (ARINC 429 / AFDX)** = the **IEC 61850 Station Bus (Ethernet)**
- The **actuators** = the **Circuit Breakers, Tap Changers, Isolators**
- The **red/yellow annunciator lights in the cockpit** = the **Substation Annunciator Panel**
- The **black-box flight recorder** = the **Disturbance Recorder (DR) / COMTRADE file**

Just as a pilot trusts the cockpit to translate thousands of parameters into intuitive symbols and colors, the power system operator trusts the HMI to translate millions of sampled data points (currents, voltages, status bits) into a clear, unambiguous, **single-line picture** with one-click control.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Color Codes

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Standard Substation Color Convention (per IEC 60073 / IS 5)**
> - **Red** = Breaker **CLOSED** (Live circuit / Energized) — also used for **TRIP / DANGER**
> - **Green** = Breaker **OPEN** (Dead circuit / Safe)
> - **Amber / Yellow** = **CAUTION / Intermediate state** (Auto/Manual, Local/Remote, Spring charging)
> - **Blue** = **DC Power ON**, healthy auxiliary supply
> - **White** = **Normal / Healthy status indication**
> - **Flashing** = **Acknowledged but not yet cleared alarm**

| Element | KTU/Industry Standard |
|---|---|
| **Operator Action Time (Latency)** | **< 1 second** for status, **< 2 seconds** for commands |
| **Display Refresh Rate** | **1–2 seconds** for measurements, **< 100 ms** for trip alarms |
| **Alarm Acceptance Time** | **< 5 seconds** (operator must acknowledge) |
| **MTBF of HMI Workstation** | **> 50,000 hours** |
| **Resolution (Modern HMIs)** | **1920 × 1080** minimum, often dual-monitor |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Why HMI Matters in Digital Protection**
> The most sophisticated **Numerical Relay** is useless if, during a fault, the operator cannot identify the faulty feeder, acknowledge the alarm, or initiate backup control. The HMI is therefore a **critical reliability component** of the protection chain — its failure is treated as a **station-level outage** under modern grid codes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of HMI in a Digital Substation

The KTU 2024 syllabus and the **IEC 61850 standard** recognize a layered architecture. The HMI occupies the **highest layer**:

> **Process Level → Bay Level → Station Level → HMI / SCADA Level**

### 2.1.1 The Four Functional Levels (Bottom-Up)

**Level 0 — Process Level (Primary Plant)**
- Current Transformers (CTs), Voltage Transformers (CVTs/PTs), Circuit Breakers, Isolators, Power Transformers, Sensors.

**Level 1 — Bay Level (Protection & Control IEDs)**
- Numerical Relays, Bay Control Units (BCUs), Metering Units, PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units). Each bay has its own IED running protection logic, metering, and event recording.

**Level 2 — Station Level (Station Bus & Communication)**
- Station Controller / Station HMI, Gateway / RTU, GPS clock (IRIG-B / PTP — IEEE 1588), Ethernet switches. All Level-1 IEDs communicate here via **IEC 61850 (MMS, GOOSE, SV)** over a redundant **Station Bus (100 Mbps / 1 Gbps Ethernet)**.

**Level 3 — Network Control Level (SCADA / DMS / EMS)**
- The Load Dispatch Center (LDC) / State Load Despatch Centre (SLDC) in India. SCADA master, historian, EMS applications, operator workstations.

> [!NOTE]
> **The HMI is physically realized at Level 2 (Station HMI) and at Level 3 (Control Center HMI).** They are often *logically identical* but communicate over a **WAN / ICCP (Inter-Control Center Protocol)** link.

## 2.2 The Four Core Functions of an HMI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The HMI performs **Four Cardinal Functions** in digital protection (memorize for KTU):
> 1. **Supervision (Monitoring / Telemetry)**
> 2. **Control (Command / Telecontrol)**
> 3. **Annunciation (Alarms & Events)**
> 4. **Archival (Data Logging / Trending / DR retrieval)**

### Function 1 — Supervision (The "Eyes")

The HMI polls each IED periodically using **MMS Reports** (Unbuffered / Buffered) over **TCP/IP**. Typical polling rates are:

| Data Class | Polling Rate | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Measurements (V, I, P, Q, f)** | **1–5 seconds** | Real-time mimic display |
| **Status (Breaker, Isolator)** | **On-change (event-driven)** | Mimic + Status panel |
| **Alarms (Pickup, Trip, Protection Operated)** | **< 100 ms push (Buffered Report)** | Annunciator window |
| **Metering (Energy kWh)** | **15 minutes** | Billing / Energy accounting |

### Function 2 — Control (The "Hands")

The HMI issues control commands — *Close*, *Open*, *Raise Tap*, *Lower Tap* — using the **IEC 61850 SBO (Select-Before-Operate)** two-step handshake:

**Step 1 — SelectWithValue (SBOw):** The HMI sends a *Select* command to the IED with the desired control attribute and **tags** the control point.

**Step 2 — Operate:** The HMI sends *Operate* within a predefined **SBO Timeout (typically 5–30 seconds)**. The IED verifies the *Operate* attribute matches the *Select* attribute, then drives the binary output to the breaker coil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two-Step Control?** It prevents **accidental operation** due to double-clicks, network echoes, or stale data. A "ghost" select that is never operated simply times out.

### Function 3 — Annunciation (The "Voice")

The **Annunciator System** in a substation is a hard-wired or software-implemented sequence that alerts the operator to abnormal conditions. Modern digital HMIs use **software annunciators** built on the **ISA 18.1 alarm sequence standard**.

The four classic ISA sequences are:

| ISA Sequence | Logic | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **ISA-A (Manual Reset)** | Alarm stays ON until operator acknowledges AND condition clears. | Most common in substation HMIs. |
| **ISA-M (Manual Acknowledge)** | Acknowledged by operator; auto-clears when condition normal. | Used for momentary faults. |
| **ISA-F (First-Out)** | Identifies the FIRST alarm in a sequence of related alarms. | Used for transformer protection (Buchholz + Oil Temp + Winding Temp — which fired first?). |
| **ISA-A-4 / Custom** | Lock-in until master reset. | Fire / Security alarms. |

**KTU 2024 — Annunciator Flash Rate (per ISA 18.1):**
- **Fast Flash (~ 4 Hz):** **NEW** alarm, unacknowledged.
- **Slow Flash (~ 0.5 Hz):** **ACKNOWLEDGED** but fault still present.
- **Steady ON:** **ACKNOWLEDGED and Cleared** (memory of event).
- **OFF:** Normal.

### Function 4 — Archival (The "Memory")

The HMI logs three classes of data:

1. **Event Log** — Time-stamped status changes (Breaker OPEN at 14:22:33.456). Stored for **≥ 1 year**.
2. **Alarm Log** — All annunciator activations and acknowledgements.
3. **Disturbance Records (DR / COMTRADE)** — Triggered on protection pickup/trip. Per **IEEE C37.111 (COMTRADE)** standard with `.cfg` and `.dat` files. Stored for **≥ 6 months**, often permanently in modern digital relays.

## 2.3 Communication Protocols Behind the HMI

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — The Protocol Stack You Must Know**
> The HMI never "talks analog" to a modern IED. It uses one of these digital protocols:

| Protocol | Layer | Speed | Use in HMI |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IEC 61850 MMS** | Application (TCP/IP) | 100 Mbps | HMI ↔ IED (client-server) |
| **IEC 61850 GOOSE** | Link Layer (Direct Ethernet) | < 4 ms | IED ↔ IED (peer-to-peer trip) |
| **IEC 61850 SV (Sampled Values)** | Link Layer | < 1 ms | Process Bus — MU → IED |
| **DNP 3.0** | Application | Serial/IP | Legacy RTU ↔ SCADA |
| **Modbus TCP / RTU** | Application | 10–100 Mbps | Simple metering |
| **ICCP (IEC 60870-6 / TASE.2)** | Application | IP | Control Center ↔ Control Center |
| **IEEE C37.118 (PMU → PDC)** | Application | UDP | Synchrophasor streaming |
| **NTP / PTP (IEEE 1588)** | — | — | Time synchronisation (±1 µs for PTP) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although HMI is a *systems* topic (not heavily formula-based), the following equations and relations are **high-yield for KTU derivations and numerical problems**:

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau_{d} = t_{op} - t_{ack}$ | **Alarm Display Latency** — time between IED event and HMI display. KTU acceptable: **< 500 ms**. | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $f_{refresh} = \frac{1}{T_{poll}}$ | **HMI Display Refresh Rate** = inverse of polling period. KTU typical: **0.5 Hz to 2 Hz** for mimic. | Hz |
| 3 | $C_{HMI} = N_{IEDs} \times (D_{meas} + D_{status} + D_{alarms})$ | **HMI Channel Capacity** — total data points the HMI must handle. | data points |
| 4 | $T_{SBO,timeout} = T_{select,max} - T_{operate,min}$ | **Select-Before-Operate Window** — must exceed network round-trip and operator reaction time. Typical **5–30 s**. | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{tsync} = \pm 1 \mu s$ (PTP) | **Time Sync Accuracy** required for event correlation across bays. | microseconds (µs) |
| 6 | $S_{DR} = N_{channels} \times f_{s} \times N_{samples} \times 2 \text{ bytes}$ | **Disturbance Record Storage Size** — for 8 ch, 4 kHz, 1 s record ≈ **64 kB**. | bytes (B) |
| 7 | $P_{event} = \frac{N_{alarms}}{T_{window}}$ | **Alarm Rate** — if **> 10/min**, alarm-flood condition → operator desensitization. | alarms/min |
| 8 | $\text{MTBF}_{HMI} > 50{,}000 \text{ h}$ | **Reliability metric** — Mean Time Between Failures. | hours (h) |
| 9 | $R_{availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | **HMI Availability** — KTU/Grid code typical: **> 99.95%** (≈ "four nines"). | dimensionless |
| 10 | $B_{bus} \geq N_{IEDs} \times (D_{GOOSE} + D_{SV} + D_{MMS})$ | **Station Bus Bandwidth** — for 30 IEDs, GOOSE 4 ms, SV 4800 frames/s, MMS 100 Mbps. | Mbps |

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

The HMI is the **single most scrutinised human-factor component** in any substation commissioning. Under **CEA (Central Electricity Authority) Regulations 2010 (Technical Standards for Connectivity)** and the **CBIP Manual on Substation Automation**, the HMI must undergo **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)** and **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)** covering:

- **Latency tests** (event-to-display < 500 ms).
- **Command validation tests** (SBO handshake).
- **Alarm flood tests** (≥ 50 alarms in 10 s).
- **Cyber-security tests** (per **IEC 62351**, role-based access).
- **Redundancy tests** (primary HMI failure → secondary HMI takeover in < 5 s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world HMI Failures That Caused Grid Blackouts**
> 1. **Northeast Blackout, USA 2003** — Alarm flooding at the control center (~ 300 alarms in 1 hour) desensitized operators; they failed to identify the tripping line in time.
> 2. **Indian Grid Disturbance 2012** — Inconsistent HMI displays between the SLDC and the substation led to a 5-hour blackout affecting 620 million people.
> 3. **European Grid 2006** — A poorly configured HMI timeout caused a back-to-back trip propagation across three countries.
> **Take-away:** A *good* HMI is one that **reduces, not increases, operator cognitive load**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: HMI Latency Budget

The **total end-to-end latency** from a field event (e.g., breaker tripping) to the operator's screen showing the red status is critical. KTU exams often ask you to break this down and verify it against a budget.

Let us derive the total latency $\tau_{total}$ from process-level event to HMI pixel update.

**Step 1 — Identify the latency components:**

$$
\tau_{total} = \tau_{sample} + \tau_{proc} + \tau_{encode} + \tau_{net} + \tau_{decode} + \tau_{render}
$$

Where each term represents:
- $\tau_{sample}$ — time for the IED to sample the new status (one protection cycle).
- $\tau_{proc}$ — time for the relay logic to detect a change in the binary input.
- $\tau_{encode}$ — time to encapsulate the data into a **MMS Report PDU** at the IED.
- $\tau_{net}$ — network propagation + switch queuing delay over the **Station Bus**.
- $\tau_{decode}$ — time for the HMI to parse the MMS Report.
- $\tau_{render}$ — time to redraw the affected icon on the screen.

**Step 2 — Assign typical KTU/Industry values:**

For a typical numerical relay with **sampling rate** $f_s = 4 \text{ kHz}$ (protection) and a **station bus** of length $\le 100$ m:

$$
\tau_{sample} = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{4000} = 0.25 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\tau_{proc} = 0.50 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(relay decision cycle)}
$$

$$
\tau_{encode} = 0.10 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(MMS PDU construction)}
$$

$$
\tau_{net} = \frac{L_{cable}}{v_{prop}} + t_{switch} = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^{8}} + 0.05 = 0.0005 + 0.05 = 0.0505 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\tau_{decode} = 0.20 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(HMI client-side parsing)}
$$

$$
\tau_{render} = 16.7 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(one 60 Hz frame — worst case)}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{total} &= 0.25 + 0.50 + 0.10 + 0.0505 + 0.20 + 16.70 \\[4pt]
&= 17.80 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with KTU acceptance criteria:**

For **trip events**, the **IEC 61850 Type 1A performance class** requires **$\tau_{total} < 3$ ms for GOOSE trips** (peer-to-peer, no HMI in the loop) and the HMI annunciator display must be **< 500 ms** per KTU/CEA grid code.

$$
\tau_{total} = 17.80 \text{ ms} \;\; \ll \;\; 500 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** A modern HMI meets the latency budget comfortably; the **bottleneck is the human eye's perception (~ 100 ms)** and the **display refresh (~ 16.7 ms)**, not the communication stack.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: SBO Timeout Calculation

The **Select-Before-Operate (SBO)** protocol has a strict timeout window. Let us derive the minimum allowable timeout.

**Step 1 — Define the SBO sequence timing:**

- $T_{select,ack}$ = Time from HMI sends *Select* → IED acknowledges.
- $T_{operator,react}$ = Time operator takes to press *Operate* after *Select* is acknowledged.
- $T_{network,RTT}$ = Network round-trip time for the *Operate* command.

**Step 2 — The SBO timeout must satisfy:**

$$
T_{SBO,timeout} \geq T_{select,ack} + T_{operator,react} + T_{network,RTT} + T_{safety,margin}
$$

**Step 3 — Assign values:**

$$
T_{select,ack} = 50 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{MMS report RTT})
$$

$$
T_{operator,react} = 2.0 \text{ s} \quad (\text{human reaction — KTU standard})
$$

$$
T_{network,RTT} = 5 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{fast Ethernet, 2 switches})
$$

$$
T_{safety,margin} = 1.0 \text{ s} \quad (\text{KTU/Grid code buffer})
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the minimum timeout:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{SBO,timeout,min} &= 0.050 + 2.000 + 0.005 + 1.000 \\[4pt]
&= 3.055 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Standard practice (round up for safety):**

$$
T_{SBO,timeout} = 5 \text{ s to } 30 \text{ s} \quad \text{(typical KTU setting)}
$$

This is the timer you will see configured inside the IED as the **`SBO Timeout`** parameter in IEC 61850 datasets.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of HMI Display Technologies

| Parameter | **LED Mimic Panel** (Legacy) | **LCD HMI** (Modern) | **Touch-Screen HMI** (Current) | **Video Wall** (Control Center) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Display Type** | Discrete LED tiles | 17–24" LCD monitor | 22–42" projected-capacitive | 4–16 large 55" panels |
| **Resolution** | N/A (segmented) | 1920 × 1080 | 1920 × 1080 / 4K | 4K / 8K per panel |
| **Color Depth** | 3 (R/Y/G) typical | 16.7 M colors | 16.7 M colors | 1.07 B colors |
| **Refresh Rate** | 100 ms (hardwired) | 60 Hz | 60 Hz | 60 Hz |
| **Latency** | < 100 ms | < 200 ms | < 200 ms | < 500 ms |
| **Interactivity** | Push-buttons | Mouse + keyboard | Multi-touch + gestures | Mouse + keyboard |
| **Failure Mode** | Single LED burnt | Backlight fail (1 mttr) | Touch overlay fail | Single panel swap |
| **MTBF** | > 100,000 h | ~ 50,000 h | ~ 40,000 h | ~ 60,000 h per panel |
| **Cost per workstation** | ₹ 5–10 lakh | ₹ 8–15 lakh | ₹ 12–20 lakh | ₹ 1–5 crore (control center) |
| **Use in Substation** | Old 220 kV substations | New 110/220 kV | New 400/765 kV | SLDC / RLDC |
| **Cyber-Security Risk** | None (no IP) | Medium | Medium-High | High (Internet-facing) |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Alarm Priority Classification (KTU Standard)

| Priority | Color | Audible | Auto-Print | Operator Response Time | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P1 — Critical** | Red | Continuous horn | Yes | < 5 s | **Breaker Trip, Bus Bar Protection Operated, Transformer Differential, Fire** |
| **P2 — High** | Amber | Intermittent buzzer | Yes | < 30 s | **Overload, Earth Fault, Battery Charger Fail, DC Under-Voltage** |
| **P3 — Medium** | Yellow | Single chime | No | < 5 min | **Tap Changer in transition, Communication Fail (single IED), Annunciation Supply Fail** |
| **P4 — Low / Informational** | White/Green | None | No | Next shift | **Energy meter reset, Time sync correction, Config change** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **P1 alarm color** with the *breaker color*. **Red = TRIP ALARM** in the annunciator window, but **Red = Breaker CLOSED** on the mimic diagram. These are **independent conventions** within the same HMI.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — HMI Architecture in a Digital Substation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Process Level: CT, CVT, Breaker, Isolator, Transformer] --> B[Bay Level: Numerical Relay, BCU, PMU, Metering IED]
    B --> C[Station Bus: Redundant IEC 61850 Ethernet, PRP or HSR]
    C --> D[Station Level: Station Controller, GPS Clock, RTU, Gateway]
    D --> E[Station HMI Workstation 1: Operator Console]
    D --> F[Station HMI Workstation 2: Engineer Console]
    D --> G[Station Historian: DR, Event, Alarm Archive]
    D --> H[WAN Gateway: ICCP to SLDC]
    H --> I[SLDC SCADA Master: Control Center HMI]
    I --> J[SLDC EMS: State Estimation, OPF, ALFC]
    I --> K[SLDC Video Wall: Operator Display]
    E --> L[Annunciator Window: ISA-A Sequence]
    F --> M[Engineering Workstation: Configuration, DR Retrieval]
    E --> N[Mimic Display: SLD with Live Status]
    F --> O[Trending and Measurement View]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Select-Before-Operate (SBO) Handshake

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator at HMI
    participant HMI as HMI Workstation
    participant IED as Bay Control IED
    participant BR as Circuit Breaker Coil
    OP->>HMI: Right-click Breaker Icon, select Close
    HMI->>IED: SelectWithValue [sBOw: CLOSE, tag=A1B2]
    IED-->>HMI: SelectAck [tag=A1B2, OK]
    HMI->>OP: Show "Selected - press EXECUTE to confirm"
    OP->>HMI: Press EXECUTE Soft Key
    HMI->>IED: Operate [tag=A1B2, value=CLOSE]
    IED->>BR: Drive Close Coil (DC Pulse)
    BR-->>IED: Aux Contact: 52a closes
    IED-->>HMI: Status Report: Breaker = CLOSED
    HMI-->>OP: Mimic Icon turns RED, event logged
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Annunciator Sequence (ISA-A)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> NewAlarm : Field Event Detected by IED
    NewAlarm : FAST FLASH Red + Horn ON
    NewAlarm --> Acknowledged : Operator presses ACK
    Acknowledged : SLOW FLASH Red + Horn OFF
    Acknowledged --> ReturnedNormal : IED reports condition cleared
    ReturnedNormal : STEADY ON Red (latched memory)
    ReturnedNormal --> Normal : Operator presses RESET
    Acknowledged --> ReturnedNormal : Auto if condition clears first
    NewAlarm --> ReturnedNormal : Auto-clear if ISA-M sequence
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — HMI Functional Decomposition (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Field Hardware: CT, CVT, Breaker] --> B[Signal Conditioning: Anti-Aliasing, Isolation]
    B --> C[IED Acquisition: Sampling at 4 kHz]
    C --> D[Protection Logic: Trip Decision]
    D --> E[Communication Stack: IEC 61850 MMS GOOSE SV]
    E --> F[Station Bus: Managed Ethernet Switch PRP HSR]
    F --> G[HMI Server: Data Concentrator and Historian]
    G --> H[HMI Client: Display Rendering Engine]
    H --> I[Operator Visual Interface: Mimic Annunciator Trending]
    I --> J[Operator Cognition and Decision]
    J --> K[Operator Command: SBO Sequence]
    K --> F
    F --> E
    E --> D
    D --> L[Breaker Trip Coil or Close Coil]
    L --> A
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Architecture — Hierarchical Levels of HMI in Power System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Level 0: Process - Primary Plant, CT, CVT, Breaker, Isolator]
    L1[Level 1: Bay Level - Numerical Relays, BCU, PMU, Merging Units]
    L2[Level 2: Station Level - Station HMI, RTU, Station Controller, Historian]
    L3[Level 3: Control Center - SCADA Master, EMS, DMS, Operator HMI]
    L4[Level 4: Corporate / Market - Energy Market, Billing, Asset Management]
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU 2024:** When asked *"Draw the HMI architecture"*, a clean **3-level** version (Process → Station HMI → Control Center) with the **IEC 61850 Station Bus** explicitly marked between Bay Level and Station Level is sufficient for full marks. Always label **redundant buses** (PRP / HSR) — the examiner checks for redundancy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]**

### Q1. Define Human Machine Interface in the context of digital protection of power systems. Mention any two functions performed by the HMI. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HMI in Digital Protection** is the hardware and software system that provides a two-way communication link between the human operator (substation/control center engineer) and the digital protection, control, and measurement equipment (Numerical Relays, IEDs, RTUs, SCADA).

**Two functions performed by the HMI (any two of the following):**

1. **Supervision (Monitoring):** The HMI continuously polls IEDs over the station bus (IEC 61850 MMS) and displays real-time measurements (V, I, P, Q, f) and status (Breaker ON/OFF, Isolator positions) on a mimic Single-Line Diagram.

2. **Control (Command):** The HMI issues remote commands to IEDs to Open/Close breakers, Raise/Lower tap changers, using the two-step **Select-Before-Operate (SBO)** protocol to prevent accidental operation.

3. **Annunciation:** The HMI displays alarms using a defined color and audible code (per ISA 18.1) — fast-flash red with horn for new unacknowledged critical alarms, slow-flash after acknowledgement, steady after return-to-normal.

4. **Data Archival:** The HMI stores event logs, alarm logs, and disturbance records (COMTRADE) for post-event analysis and grid code compliance.

**(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for any two functions × 1/2 each = 1 mark, total 3 marks)**

---

### Q2. List the standard color convention used in substation mimic diagrams for (a) a closed circuit breaker, (b) an open circuit breaker, and (c) an auto/manual selector switch. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Element | Color Convention | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Closed Circuit Breaker** | **RED** | Red indicates the circuit is **LIVE / ENERGISED** — current is flowing, voltage is present. The breaker icon is filled red. |
| **(b) Open Circuit Breaker** | **GREEN** | Green indicates the circuit is **DEAD / ISOLATED / SAFE**. The breaker icon is filled green. |
| **(c) Auto / Manual Selector** | **AMBER / YELLOW** | Amber indicates an **INTERMEDIATE / CAUTIONARY** state — control authority is in transition (Local/Remote, Auto/Manual, or test mode). |

(1 mark per correct item, total **3 marks**)

> Refer **IEC 60073 / IS 5** for the full standard. Memorize: **Red = Live, Green = Safe, Amber = Caution.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 3 — Internal Choice Pattern (Per KTU 2024 Scheme)**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a digital substation** showing the role of HMI at each level. Discuss the **four cardinal functions** of the HMI in detail. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Explain the **IEC 61850 Select-Before-Operate (SBO) protocol** used for issuing control commands from the HMI to the IED. Why is a two-step handshake necessary? `[7 Marks]`

---

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Digital Substation Architecture and HMI Functions `[7 Marks]`

**Block Diagram (draw on answer sheet — 3 marks):**

A typical digital substation per **IEC 61850** has the following layered architecture (bottom to top):

1. **Process Level (Level 0):** Contains the primary plant — Current Transformers, Voltage Transformers (CVTs), Circuit Breakers, Isolators, Power Transformers, and Sensors. These are the physical assets that actually carry, switch, and transform power.

2. **Bay Level (Level 1):** Each bay (line, transformer, bus coupler) has its own set of **Numerical Relays / Bay Control Units (BCUs)** that perform protection (overcurrent, distance, differential), metering, and bay-level control. The IED samples CT/CVT outputs, executes protection algorithms, and drives the breaker trip coil.

3. **Station Level (Level 2):** All Bay-Level IEDs communicate over a **Redundant Station Bus (IEC 61850 Ethernet, PRP or HSR)** to the **Station Controller**, **Station HMI Workstations**, **RTU/Gateway**, **GPS Clock**, and **Station Historian**. The HMI lives here physically.

4. **Control Center Level (Level 3):** The **SLDC** (State Load Despatch Centre) receives data from the station via the WAN gateway and **ICCP (TASE.2)**, presenting it on its own HMI as part of the SCADA/EMS. The control center HMI is logically similar to the station HMI.

**The Four Cardinal Functions of the HMI `[2 marks, 0.5 each]`:**

1. **Supervision (Monitoring):** Polls IEDs over MMS Reports (TCP/IP) at 1–5 second intervals for measurements (V, I, MW, MVAR, Hz) and on-change for binary status (Breaker, Isolator). The HMI paints the mimic Single-Line Diagram (SLD) with live colors.

2. **Control (Command):** Issues Open/Close commands to breakers and Raise/Lower to tap changers using the **SBO** two-step handshake. The HMI must pass **role-based authentication** (Operator vs. Engineer) and **interlocking** checks before issuing the command.

3. **Annunciation:** Generates visual and audible alarms per **ISA 18.1** sequence — fast-flash for new alarms, slow-flash for acknowledged, steady for cleared-but-unreset. Critical alarms (P1) have horns; routine alarms (P4) are silent.

4. **Archival:** Stores event logs (timestamped status changes), alarm logs, and disturbance records (COMTRADE — `.cfg` + `.dat`) on the station historian. These are critical for **post-fault analysis**, **regulatory compliance**, and **grid code audit**.

---

#### (b) IEC 61850 Select-Before-Operate (SBO) Protocol `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — What is SBO? `[2 marks]`**

The **Select-Before-Operate (SBO)** is a control model defined in **IEC 61850-7-2 (ACSI — Abstract Communication Service Interface)** that requires a **two-step handshake** between the HMI (client) and the IED (server) before any physical switching action is performed. It is a **safety mechanism** to prevent inadvertent or duplicate operation of high-energy primary equipment.

**Step 2 — The Two Steps `[3 marks]`:**

- **Step 1 — `SelectWithValue (SBOw)`:** The HMI sends a *Select* command to the IED. This command includes:
  - The **Data Object** being controlled (e.g., `CB1.POS.CtlVal`).
  - The **desired state** (e.g., `01` for Open, `10` for Close).
  - A unique **tag / check attribute** (e.g., `0xA1B2`).
  - The IED acknowledges the select and **latches** the control point — meaning no other client can issue a select to that point until either the *Operate* arrives, or the **SBO timeout** expires.

- **Step 2 — `Operate`:** The HMI sends an *Operate* command within the SBO timeout window. This command must:
  - Carry the **same tag** as the original *Select*.
  - Carry the **same desired state**.
  - The IED validates both, then drives the **binary output** to the breaker close/open coil.

**Step 3 — Why is the Two-Step Handshake Necessary? `[2 marks]`:**

1. **Accidental Operation Prevention:** A single-click network glitch, mouse double-click, or screen artefact cannot cause a trip. The *Operate* must be a **deliberate, second confirmation** by the operator.

2. **Interlocking Validation:** The IED performs **interlocking checks** (e.g., *"Can the breaker close while the earth switch is closed?"*) during the *Select* phase. If interlocking fails, the select is **NACKed** and the HMI shows an alarm before any command is issued.

3. **Tag-based Anti-Echo:** The unique tag prevents a **stale or duplicate** *Operate* from firing twice (e.g., due to a network retransmission).

4. **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):** Some SBO implementations require the *Select* to come from one user (e.g., the Operator) and the *Operate* from the same user within a session — preventing a malicious external actor from completing a stolen *Select*.

**Conclusion `[bonus 0.5 mark]`:** SBO is a **fundamental safety protocol** in IEC 61850 — it is the digital equivalent of a *Permit-to-Work* card. KTU students must remember: **Select = reservation, Operate = execution; both must match.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **different types of annunciator sequences** used in substation HMIs as per **ISA 18.1**. Describe the **flash rate and color code** for each state. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** What are the **major communication protocols** used between the HMI and the IED in a digital substation? Compare **IEC 61850**, **DNP 3.0**, and **Modbus** in terms of architecture, speed, and suitability for digital protection. `[7 Marks]`

---

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) ISA 18.1 Annunciator Sequences `[7 Marks]`

**Introduction `[1 mark]`:**

The **Instrument Society of America (ISA) Standard 18.1 — 1979 (R2004)** "Annunciator Sequences and Specifications" defines a uniform methodology for alarm annunciation. In digital substations, the annunciator logic is implemented in **software** within the HMI/Station Controller, but it follows the same ISA sequence definitions. The standard defines four basic sequences: **A, M, F, and A-4**.

**Description of Each Sequence `[4 marks, 1 each]`:**

1. **ISA-A (Manual Acknowledge, Automatic Reset, Non-Interlock):**
   - The alarm appears, the operator acknowledges it, and the alarm **automatically returns to normal** when the field condition clears. **Most common in substations.**
   - Use case: Overcurrent alarm, Earth fault alarm, DC supply fail.

2. **ISA-M (Manual Acknowledge, Automatic Return-to-Normal):**
   - The alarm is acknowledged by the operator, but the **annunciator window automatically clears** the moment the field condition returns to normal, without requiring a separate reset.
   - Use case: Momentary alarms (e.g., a brief communication loss to one IED).

3. **ISA-F (First-Out Annunciation):**
   - Used when **multiple alarms can fire in rapid succession** (e.g., transformer protection: Buchholz, Oil Temp, Winding Temp, PRD, Oil Level — all could fire together during a transformer internal fault). The ISA-F sequence identifies and **highlights the FIRST** alarm to fire, helping the operator diagnose the root cause.
   - Implementation: A "first-out" flag is set on the first alarm; subsequent alarms are dimmed or shown as "second-out".

4. **ISA-A-4 (Manual Acknowledge, Lock-In, Field Return + Reset Required):**
   - The alarm **latches** and cannot be reset until the field condition has cleared AND the operator has pressed a separate **RESET** pushbutton.
   - Use case: **Fire alarms, Security breach, Battery explosion** — alarms that must be physically inspected before being cleared.

**Flash Rate and Color Code `[2 marks, see table below]`:**

| Annunciator State | Color | Flash Rate | Audible |
|---|---|---|---|
| **New Unacknowledged** | **RED** | **Fast Flash (~ 4 Hz)** | Continuous horn |
| **Acknowledged, Fault Still Present** | **RED** | **Slow Flash (~ 0.5 Hz)** | Silent |
| **Acknowledged, Fault Cleared (Memory)** | **RED** | **Steady ON** | Silent |
| **Normal** | — | **OFF** | Silent |

> The flash rate of **~ 4 Hz fast / ~ 0.5 Hz slow** is the KTU/industry standard per ISA 18.1.

---

#### (b) Communication Protocols: IEC 61850 vs DNP 3.0 vs Modbus `[7 Marks]`

**Introduction `[1 mark]`:**

Modern digital substations use a **protocol stack** to communicate between the HMI (control level) and the IEDs (bay level). The three most relevant protocols for the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **IEC 61850 (International Standard for Substation Communication)**
2. **DNP 3.0 (Distributed Network Protocol, version 3)**
3. **Modbus (originally Modicon / Schneider proprietary, now open)**

**Detailed Comparison `[5 marks, see table]`:**

| Parameter | **IEC 61850** | **DNP 3.0** | **Modbus (TCP / RTU)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard Body** | IEC TC 57 | DNP Users Group | Modbus Organization (originally Modicon) |
| **Architecture** | Object-oriented, with **Logical Nodes (LN)**, **Data Objects (DO)**, **Datasets** | Master-Slave with **Binary Inputs, Analog Inputs, Binary Outputs, Counters** | Pure Master-Slave, register-based |
| **Physical Layer** | Ethernet (100 Mbps / 1 Gbps) | Serial (RS-232/485) or IP | RS-232/485 (RTU) or TCP/IP |
| **Data Model** | **Self-describing** (SCL files — `.icd`, `.iid`, `.scd`, `.ssd`) | Pre-defined point lists | Pure register map |
| **Transfer Speed** | < 4 ms (GOOSE), < 1 ms (SV), 100 Mbps (MMS) | ~ 100 ms typical | ~ 10 ms (TCP), ~ 100 ms (RTU) |
| **Time Sync** | Native (IEC 61850-7-2, PTP IEEE 1588) | DNP3 supports time-stamping | None native |
| **Redundancy** | Native (PRP, HSR) | Manual (dual masters) | None |
| **Cyber-Security** | Native per **IEC 62351** | Add-on (DNP3-SA) | None native |
| **Configuration Tool** | **SCL (Substation Configuration Language)** XML files | Point list spreadsheets | Manual register map |
| **Suitability for HMI** | **Excellent** — current KTU/CEA choice for new substations | **Good** for legacy retrofit | **Limited** — small RTUs / metering only |
| **Cost of IED** | High (silicon + license) | Medium | Low |

**Conclusion `[1 mark]`:**

For **KTU 2024 digital protection context**, **IEC 61850 is the mandated standard** for new digital substations (per CEA 2010 connectivity regulations). **DNP 3.0** is still widely deployed in legacy Indian substations and serves as the **SCADA-master to RTU** link. **Modbus** is relegated to **simple metering and small PLC integration**, and is **not recommended** for trip-class protection signaling due to its lack of time-sync, security, and speed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on HMI Questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing ISA sequence letters with Flash rates** — A student who writes *"ISA-A means slow flash"* loses 1 mark instantly. **ISA-A is Manual Acknowledge, not a flash rate.** The flash rate is a **property of the annunciator state**, not the sequence.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "SBO" in control questions** — Many answers describe a *"click and the breaker opens"* protocol. The examiner expects the **two-step SBO** with `SelectWithValue` and `Operate`, plus a discussion of *why* the second step is needed. Skipping the SBO = 3 marks lost.
>
> 3. **Writing "HMI = SCADA"** — This is the most common conceptual error. **HMI is the front-end display; SCADA is the data acquisition system that feeds the HMI.** Drawing a block diagram that boxes them together costs a mark for "lack of clear understanding".
>
> 4. **No color code mentioned in mimic diagram** — Any mimic-diagram question **without** explicit color annotations (Red=Closed, Green=Open, Amber=Auto/Manual) is marked down by 1–2 marks. Always annotate.
>
> 5. **Mixing up "trip" red and "closed" red** — Students write *"Red = Trip"*, but trip is the **annunciator**; on the **mimic**, red means the breaker is **closed (live)**. Examiner notes this as a -0.5 mark confusion.
>
> 6. **No redundancy shown in architecture diagram** — Modern digital substations are **always** drawn with redundant station buses (PRP / HSR) and dual HMI workstations. Omission = -1 mark.
>
> 7. **Skipping numerical units in formula-based questions** — Always write units: **ms, Mbps, Hz, kB, alarms/min**. The unit is sometimes the discriminator between full and partial marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HMI in Digital Protection (Module 3, KTU 2024)**

- **Definition:** HMI = hardware + software for two-way operator ↔ IED/SCADA communication.
- **Architecture:** 4 levels — Process (L0) → Bay (L1) → Station (L2, **HMI lives here**) → Control Center (L3).
- **Bus:** Redundant **IEC 61850 Station Bus** (PRP/HSR Ethernet) connects L1 to L2.
- **Four Functions:** **Supervision, Control, Annunciation, Archival**.
- **Supervision** uses **MMS Reports** (TCP/IP), 1–5 s polling, on-change for status.
- **Control** uses **SBO (Select-Before-Operate)** two-step handshake with a **unique tag** to prevent accidental operation. Typical SBO timeout = **5–30 seconds**.
- **Annunciation** uses **ISA 18.1** sequences: **A (manual ack), M (auto clear), F (first-out), A-4 (lock-in)**.
- **Flash rates:** Fast **~ 4 Hz** = new; Slow **~ 0.5 Hz** = acked; Steady = cleared-but-unreset; Off = normal.
- **Color Code (Mimic):** **Red = Breaker CLOSED (Live)**, **Green = Breaker OPEN (Safe)**, **Amber = Auto/Manual / Caution**, **Blue = DC Healthy**, **White = Normal Info**.
- **Color Code (Annunciator):** **Red = P1 Critical (Trip)**, **Amber = P2 High**, **Yellow = P3 Medium**, **White = P4 Info**.
- **Protocols:** **IEC 61850 (MMS, GOOSE, SV) — KTU/CEA mandated for new substations;** **DNP 3.0 — legacy SCADA;** **Modbus — small metering only.**
- **GOOSE trip latency:** **< 4 ms** (Type 1A performance class) — peer-to-peer, **NOT via HMI**.
- **HMI display latency:** **< 500 ms** for trip events, **< 1 s** for measurements.
- **Time Sync:** **PTP (IEEE 1588)** → **± 1 µs accuracy** for event correlation across bays.
- **Archival:** Event log **≥ 1 year**, DR/COMTRADE **≥ 6 months**, historian must be **redundant (RAID-1 or RAID-6)**.
- **Reliability:** **MTBF > 50,000 h**, **Availability > 99.95%** ("four nines").
- **Cyber-Security:** **IEC 62351** mandates authentication, encryption, role-based access on the HMI. Always log the operator ID, timestamp, and action for every command.
- **Pitfall:** A **good HMI reduces operator cognitive load** — alarm flooding (> 10/min) is a *design failure*, not an operator failure. The 2003 US/2003 Italy/2012 India blackouts all had alarm-flood roots.
- **Formula to remember:** $\tau_{total} = \tau_{sample} + \tau_{proc} + \tau_{encode} + \tau_{net} + \tau_{decode} + \tau_{render} \approx 17.8$ ms ≪ 500 ms budget.
- **Formula to remember:** $T_{SBO,timeout,min} = T_{select,ack} + T_{operator,react} + T_{RTT} + T_{margin} \approx 3$ s; **set to 5–30 s in practice.**
- **Formula to remember:** $R_{availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \geq 0.9995$.

> **Closing Note:** The HMI is the **only** component in a digital substation that a human **sees, hears, and touches** — every other subsystem is invisible inside cubicles. Mastering the HMI means mastering the *human* side of digital protection, which is exactly what the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework emphasizes under the **CO3 (Design of Digital Protection Schemes)** and **CO4 (Substation Automation)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Principle of Operation** of a digital protection relay refers to the systematic, sequential, and mathematically rigorous method by which a numerical relay acquires analog voltage and current signals from the power system, conditions them, converts them to discrete digital samples, processes these samples through computational algorithms running on a microprocessor or Digital Signal Processor (DSP), compares the resulting relaying quantity against a predefined operating characteristic, and finally issues a trip command to the circuit breaker when a fault is detected.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, the principle of operation of a digital relay is founded upon four universally accepted pillars:

1. **Signal Acquisition** — deriving proportional low-level analog signals via Current Transformers (CTs) and Potential Transformers (PTs).
2. **Signal Conditioning & Conversion** — anti-aliasing filtering, multiplexing, sample-and-hold, and Analog-to-Digital (A/D) conversion.
3. **Numerical Computation** — application of relaying algorithms (Fourier, Walsh, Least-Squares, etc.) to extract the fundamental phasor.
4. **Decision Logic & Tripping** — comparison of computed relaying quantity with the relay setting and issuing a trip pulse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A digital relay is *not* an electromechanical device with moving parts; it is essentially a **dedicated, real-time, fault-grade industrial computer**. The "principle of operation" therefore must be understood in terms of *signal flow*, *sampling mathematics*, and *algorithmic decision-making*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **medical doctor** performing an **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** on a patient in real time:

- The **electrodes** (sensors) attached to the body are like **CTs and PTs** in the power system — they sense the raw signals (voltage/current waves).
- The **low-pass filter** in the ECG machine removes high-frequency noise — this is the **Anti-Aliasing Filter** of the digital relay.
- The **A/D converter** turns the smooth analog heartbeat waveform into a series of digital numbers — this is the **Sampling and A/D Conversion** block.
- The **computer's screen** displays the waveform, calculates the heart rate, and compares it to safe limits — this is the **DSP/Microprocessor** running relaying algorithms.
- The moment an abnormal heartbeat is detected, the **alarm rings and the doctor intervenes** — this is the **Trip Output** to the circuit breaker.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Picture in One Line:**
> *"Sense the wave → Clean the noise → Convert to numbers → Compute the phasor → Compare to setting → Trip the breaker."*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Digital Protection

The following **standard metrics** govern the principle of operation of any numerical relay:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **System Frequency (f)** | **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz** (USA) | Fundamental relaying frequency |
| **Sampling Frequency (fₛ)** | Typically **4 f, 8 f, 12 f, 16 f, 32 f** samples per cycle | Governs algorithm accuracy |
| **Nyquist Frequency (f_N)** | **≥ 2 × f_max** | Anti-aliasing design constraint |
| **A/D Converter Resolution** | **12-bit, 14-bit, or 16-bit** | Determines measurement precision |
| **Operating Time** | **< 1 cycle (20 ms at 50 Hz)** for high-speed protection | Modern numerical relay benchmark |
| **CT Secondary** | **1 A or 5 A** | Analog input range |
| **PT Secondary** | **110 / √3 V** (line-to-neutral) | Analog input range |
