# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Operating Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The principle of operation of a digital relay unfolds in **eight sequential stages**. Each stage has a clearly defined "**Why**" and "**How**" that the KTU examiner expects you to articulate.

### Stage 1 — Sensing Stage (CTs and PTs)
- **Why:** Power system voltages and currents are at dangerously high magnitudes (e.g., 400 kV, 1000 A). Microprocessors cannot handle such levels directly.
- **How:** CTs step down current to a standard secondary of **1 A or 5 A**; PTs (or CVTs) step down voltage to **110/√3 V**. They also provide galvanic isolation between the high-voltage switchyard and the relay panel.

### Stage 2 — Surge Protection & Auxiliary CT/PT (Burden)
- **Why:** The power system is subject to lightning, switching transients, and CT saturation.
- **How:** Surge arrestors, non-linear resistors (varistors), and intermediate CTs (often with ratio **10:1** or **20:1**) further scale down the signal to the relay input range (typically **± 10 V peak** or **± 5 mA**).

### Stage 3 — Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-Pass Filter)
- **Why:** To satisfy the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** and prevent frequency *aliasing* — a phenomenon where high-frequency components (above f_N) fold back and corrupt the low-frequency signal.
- **How:** A second-order or fourth-order **active low-pass Butterworth filter** with a cutoff frequency **f_c ≈ (1/2) × f_s** is used. The passband is flat, ensuring faithful reproduction of the fundamental component.

### Stage 4 — Multiplexer (MUX)
- **Why:** A single A/D converter is more economical than one per channel.
- **How:** A solid-state analog multiplexer time-shares the A/D converter across multiple input channels (typically 3 phase currents + 3 phase voltages + neutral quantities = 7–12 channels).

### Stage 5 — Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit
- **Why:** The A/D converter requires a stable, constant input voltage during the conversion interval; the analog signal is continuously varying.
- **How:** The S/H switch closes momentarily to acquire the instantaneous value, then opens; a hold capacitor retains that value while the A/D converter performs the bit-by-bit comparison (successive approximation).

### Stage 6 — Analog-to-Digital (A/D) Converter
- **Why:** Microprocessors and DSPs operate exclusively on discrete binary numbers.
- **How:** A **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** based A/D converter with **12-bit, 14-bit, or 16-bit** resolution digitises the held sample into a binary word (e.g., a 12-bit word = 4096 discrete levels). The Least Significant Bit (LSB) value is given by **q = V_range / 2ⁿ**.

### Stage 7 — Digital Processor (CPU / DSP / FPGA)
- **Why:** This is the *brain* of the relay. It executes the relaying algorithm in real time.
- **How:** The processor computes the magnitude and phase angle of the fundamental component using algorithms such as:
  - **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** or **Full-Cycle / Half-Cycle Fourier Algorithm**
  - **Walsh Function Algorithm**
  - **Least-Squares Error Algorithm**
  - **Manhattan (rectangular) algorithm**
  - **Travelling-wave based algorithm** (for ultra-high-speed protection)

### Stage 8 — Decision Logic and Trip Output
- **Why:** The computed quantity (current, voltage, impedance, power, differential current) must be compared against a **pickup setting**.
- **How:** A comparator routine checks whether the operating quantity exceeds the threshold. If yes, a **trip pulse** (typically a 24 V DC or 110 V DC signal) is sent to the breaker trip coil. A **timer** enforces coordination; a **flag** and **annunciator** record the event.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Almost every "explain the principle of operation" question expects a *labelled block diagram* of the above eight stages as a mandatory sub-part. **Always draw it.**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_N = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Nyquist frequency = half the sampling frequency | Anti-aliasing design |
| 2 | $f_s = N \cdot f$ | Sampling frequency (samples per cycle × system frequency) | Selecting A/D rate |
| 3 | $q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Quantisation step (LSB) | A/D resolution |
| 4 | $i(k) = i_{DC} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} I_{m,n}\sin(n\omega t_k + \phi_n)$ | General sampled current | Algorithm input |
| 5 | $I_{Re} = \dfrac{2}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} i(k)\cos(k\theta)$ | Real part via Full-Cycle Fourier | Phasor extraction |
| 6 | $I_{Im} = \dfrac{2}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} i(k)\sin(k\theta)$ | Imaginary part via Full-Cycle Fourier | Phasor extraction |
| 7 | $I_m = \sqrt{I_{Re}^{\,2} + I_{Im}^{\,2}}$ | Magnitude of fundamental | Pickup comparison |
| 8 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{I_{Im}}{I_{Re}}\right)$ | Phase angle | Directional decision |
| 9 | $Z = \dfrac{V_m}{I_m}$ | Apparent impedance | Distance relay |
| 10 | $t_{op} = T_{alg} + T_{CB}$ | Total operating time | Coordination studies |
| 11 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC time constant of S/H | Aperture design |
| 12 | $A_v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(f/f_c)^4}}$ | 2nd-order Butterworth gain | Anti-aliasing filter |

> **Units Reminder:** $f$ in **Hz**, $V_{FS}$ in **Volts**, $q$ in **Volts/bit**, $T$ in **seconds**, $Z$ in **Ω** (or primary Ω).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principle of operation described above underpins **every** modern numerical relay sold by ABB, Siemens, GE, Schneider, SEL, and Alstom. It is the foundational architecture for:

- **Distance (impedance) relays** for transmission line protection.
- **Differential relays** (87T, 87B, 87M) for transformer, bus-bar, and motor protection.
- **Overcurrent and earth-fault relays** (50/51, 50N/51N).
- **Travelling-wave based ultra-high-speed relays** (operating in **< 1 ms**).
- **Wide-Area Monitoring, Protection, and Control (WAMPAC)** systems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantisation Step (LSB) of the A/D Converter

The A/D converter accepts an analog input voltage bounded in the range $[-V_{FS}, +V_{FS}]$ and discretises it into $2^n$ equally spaced binary levels (for an $n$-bit unipolar converter; bipolar is similar with $2^{n-1}$ on each side).

We start with the number of discrete quantum levels:

$$
L = 2^{n}
$$

The width of one quantum level — i.e., the voltage change required to increment the digital output by **1 LSB** — is given by:

$$
q = \frac{V_{FS}}{L} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}
$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
A digital relay uses a 12-bit bipolar A/D converter with a full-scale input of $\pm 10$ V. Compute the quantisation step.

*Step 1:* The total input span (peak-to-peak) is

$$
V_{span} = V_{FS}^{+} - V_{FS}^{-} = 10 - (-10) = 20 \text{ V}
$$

*Step 2:* Total number of levels for a 12-bit converter:

$$
L = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

*Step 3:* Compute quantisation step:

$$
q = \frac{V_{span}}{L} = \frac{20}{4096} = 4.8828 \text{ mV/bit}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** Each binary count of the A/D converter corresponds to a real-world analog change of **4.88 mV**. Smaller $q$ ⇒ finer resolution ⇒ higher cost.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nyquist Criterion and Anti-Aliasing Cutoff

**Statement of Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples **iff** the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

For a digital relay measuring the **fundamental component** of a power system waveform, the highest frequency component of interest is the **fundamental** (50 Hz or 60 Hz). However, the input signal contains harmonics up to very high orders (especially during faults and CT saturation). To prevent aliasing:

*Step 1 — Define the highest useful frequency of interest:*

$$
f_{max} = N_h \cdot f
$$

where $N_h$ is the highest harmonic order retained (e.g., 7 for overcurrent relays, 13 for distance relays).

*Step 2 — Select sampling frequency:*

$$
f_s = N \cdot f, \quad N \in \{4, 8, 12, 16, 32, 64\}
$$

*Step 3 — Impose the Nyquist condition:*

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N \cdot f \geq 2 N_h f \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N \geq 2 N_h
$$

*Step 4 — Design the anti-aliasing filter cutoff:*

$$
f_c \approx \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{N \cdot f}{2}
$$

**Worked Example:**
A distance relay samples at $N = 12$ samples per cycle. Determine the highest harmonic order $N_h$ it can faithfully represent.

*Step 1:* Apply Nyquist:

$$
N \geq 2 N_h \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N_h \leq \frac{N}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6
$$

*Step 2:* Therefore, the anti-aliasing filter cutoff must be set at:

$$
f_c = \frac{12 \times 50}{2} = 300 \text{ Hz}
$$

which corresponds to the **6th harmonic** — anything above 300 Hz will be attenuated by 3 dB or more, preventing aliasing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Full-Cycle Fourier Algorithm (Phasor Extraction)

Let the sampled current be modeled as:

$$
i(k) = I_{DC} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ I_{cn}\cos(n\omega t_k) + I_{sn}\sin(n\omega t_k) \right]
$$

For a sinusoid of the fundamental frequency, $t_k = k \cdot \Delta T$ where $\Delta T = T/N$. The full-cycle Fourier algorithm multiplies the sampled signal by $\cos(\omega t_k)$ and $\sin(\omega t_k)$, then sums over $N$ samples (one full cycle).

*Step 1 — Real-part coefficient:*

$$
I_{Re} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} i(k) \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)
$$

*Step 2 — Imaginary-part coefficient:*

$$
I_{Im} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} i(k) \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)
$$

*Step 3 — Magnitude of the fundamental phasor:*

$$
I_m = \sqrt{I_{Re}^{\,2} + I_{Im}^{\,2}}
$$

*Step 4 — Phase angle:*

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{I_{Im}}{I_{Re}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is the workhorse algorithm:** The full-cycle Fourier algorithm **completely rejects all integer harmonics** and the **DC component** — making it ideal for protection, where DC offset from fault transients is a major problem.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Relaying Algorithms

| Algorithm | Data Window | DC Rejection | Harmonic Rejection | Speed | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sample-to-Sample (Sinusoidal)** | 2 samples | No | None | Fastest | Conceptual intro only |
| **Half-Cycle Fourier** | $N/2$ samples | Partial | All integer | Fast | Distance relaying |
| **Full-Cycle Fourier** | $N$ samples | **Complete** | All integer | Moderate | **Most common, exam favourite** |
| **Walsh Function** | 1 cycle | Partial | Selective | Fast | Numerical relaying |
| **Least-Squares Fit** | 1 cycle | Excellent | Configurable | Moderate | High-accuracy protection |
| **Kalman Filtering** | Recursive | Excellent | Excellent | Very Fast | Advanced numerical relays |
| **Travelling-Wave** | Microseconds | N/A | N/A | **Ultra-fast** | EHV/UHV line protection |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Principle of Operation of a Digital Relay

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **complete signal flow** through a numerical relay — from the high-voltage power system to the circuit breaker trip coil. This is the single most important diagram for any KTU "principle of operation" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power System HV Bus - 400 kV / 11 kV] --> B[CT Secondary - 1A or 5A]
    A --> C[PT Secondary - 110 by sqrt 3 V]
    B --> D[Auxiliary CT - Further Scaling 10 to 1]
    C --> E[Auxiliary PT - Voltage Divider]
    D --> F[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter - 2nd Order Butterworth]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Analog Multiplexer - MUX 8 to 1]
    G --> H[Sample and Hold Circuit - S and H]
    H --> I[Analog to Digital Converter - 12 bit SAR]
    I --> J[Microprocessor or DSP - Real Time Computation]
    J --> K[Relaying Algorithm Module - Fourier, Walsh, LS]
    K --> L{Comparator - Quantity versus Pickup Setting}
    L -- Exceeds Setting --> M[Trip Logic and Timer]
    L -- Below Setting --> N[No Trip - Continue Monitoring]
    M --> O[Trip Output Relay - 24V or 110V DC]
    O --> P[Circuit Breaker Trip Coil]
    J --> Q[Memory and Event Recorder]
    J --> R[Human Machine Interface - HMI Display]
    J --> S[Communication Port - IEC 61850]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Subsystem Mapping

The numerical relay can be viewed as **four cooperating subsystems**. The Mermaid diagram below shows the interaction between them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACQ[Subsystem 1 - Analog Front End]
        A1[CT and PT] --> A2[Burden and Auxiliary Scaling]
        A2 --> A3[Anti Aliasing Filter]
    end
    subgraph DIG[Subsystem 2 - Digitiser]
        A3 --> D1[Multiplexer]
        D1 --> D2[Sample and Hold]
        D2 --> D3[A to D Converter 12 bit]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Subsystem 3 - Digital Processor]
        D3 --> P1[MUX Decoder and DMA]
        P1 --> P2[Fourier Algorithm Engine]
        P2 --> P3[Phasor Magnitude and Angle]
        P3 --> P4[Impedance and Power Calculator]
    end
    subgraph DEC[Subsystem 4 - Decision and Output]
        P4 --> O1[Pickup Comparator]
        O1 --> O2[Timer and Coordination]
        O2 --> O3[Trip Output Buffer]
        O3 --> O4[Breaker Trip Coil]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for KTU:** Always describe each block using the format — *Block Name* → *Function* → *Why it is needed*. Examiners reward **functional clarity** over mere box-drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Continuous Analog Sine Wave - 50 Hz] --> S2[Sample and Hold - Discrete Time Samples]
    S2 --> S3[A to D Conversion - Quantised to 12 bit Levels]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the principle of operation of a digital protection relay. List its major functional blocks.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 model question]**

**Model Answer:**
The principle of operation of a digital protection relay is the systematic process by which analog voltage and current signals from the power system are sensed by **CTs and PTs**, conditioned by **anti-aliasing filters**, sampled and held, converted to **digital form by an A/D converter**, and then processed in real time by a **microprocessor or DSP** running a relaying algorithm (such as the Discrete Fourier Transform). The computed relaying quantity is compared against a preset **pickup value**; if exceeded, a **trip command** is issued to the circuit breaker.

**Major functional blocks:**
1. **Signal acquisition** (CT, PT, auxiliary CT/PT)
2. **Anti-aliasing low-pass filter**
3. **Multiplexer (MUX)**
4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)**
5. **Analog-to-Digital (A/D) Converter**
6. **Digital Processor (CPU / DSP)**
7. **Decision logic / comparator**
8. **Trip output stage**

---

### Q2. State and explain the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem as applicable to digital relays.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 model question]**

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem states that a **band-limited signal** with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely and faithfully reconstructed from its discrete samples **if and only if** the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

The minimum sampling frequency, $f_s = 2 f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist rate**, and $f_{max}$ is the **Nyquist frequency**.

**Application to digital relays:** Power system currents and voltages contain the fundamental (50/60 Hz) plus harmonics and decaying DC components. To avoid *aliasing* (where high-frequency components fold into the passband and corrupt the measured fundamental), an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** is placed before the A/D converter. The filter cutoff is set at $f_c = f_s/2$, and a typical choice is $f_s = 12 f$ (i.e., 12 samples per cycle at 50 Hz ⇒ $f_s = 600$ Hz ⇒ $f_c = 300$ Hz).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a numerical (digital) overcurrent relay**. Describe the function of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A digital relay uses a **12-bit A/D converter** with a full-scale input of $\pm 10$ V. Calculate (i) the quantisation step, (ii) the percentage resolution, and (iii) the smallest current change that can be detected at the relay input when the auxiliary CT has a ratio of **1000:5 A**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The principle of operation of a numerical overcurrent relay involves the sequential processing of the CT secondary current through the following functional blocks:

**1. Current Transformer (CT):** Steps down the primary current (say 1000 A) to a standard secondary of 5 A or 1 A, providing isolation from the high-voltage system.

**2. Auxiliary CT / Shunt Resistor:** Further reduces the signal to a voltage level of $\pm 10$ V peak acceptable to the analog front end of the relay. Often, a precision shunt converts current to a proportional millivolt drop.

**3. Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c$ chosen such that no frequency component above $f_s/2$ enters the A/D converter. This prevents aliasing of high-frequency transients and switching surges.

**4. Multiplexer (MUX):** A solid-state switch that sequentially connects multiple channels (3 phases + neutral) to a single A/D converter for cost economy.

**5. Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Captures the instantaneous value of the input and holds it constant during the A/D conversion interval, eliminating aperture errors.

**6. A/D Converter:** A 12-bit (or 14-/16-bit) successive approximation register (SAR) type converter digitises each held sample into a binary word. Resolution is $2^{12} = 4096$ levels.

**7. Digital Processor (DSP/Microprocessor):** Executes the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** algorithm to extract the magnitude of the fundamental component of the current. The DC offset and all integer harmonics are completely rejected in a full-cycle DFT.

**8. Comparator / Decision Logic:** The fundamental current magnitude $I_m$ is compared with the pickup setting $I_{pickup}$. If $I_m > I_{pickup}$, a logical flag is raised.

**9. Timer / Trip Logic:** Implements the **IDMT (Inverse Definite Minimum Time)** or **Definite Time** characteristic. After the time delay elapses, a trip command is sent.

**10. Trip Output Stage:** A buffer amplifier energises the 24 V or 110 V DC trip coil of the circuit breaker.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

*Given:*
- A/D resolution: $n = 12$ bits
- Full-scale input: $V_{FS} = \pm 10$ V ⇒ $V_{span} = 20$ V
- Auxiliary CT ratio: $1000 : 5$ A

**Sub-Part (i) — Quantisation step:**

The quantisation step is the smallest voltage change the A/D converter can resolve:

$$
q = \frac{V_{span}}{2^{n}} = \frac{20}{2^{12}} = \frac{20}{4096} = 4.8828 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV/bit}
$$

**Sub-Part (ii) — Percentage resolution:**

$$
\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{q}{V_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{4.88 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ V}} \times 100 = 0.0488 \% \approx 0.05 \%
$$

**Sub-Part (iii) — Smallest primary current detectable:**

The auxiliary CT steps 5 A secondary to 10 V peak. Assuming a proportional linear scaling (i.e., 5 A secondary ⇒ 10 V at relay input), the current per volt is:

$$
\frac{5 \text{ A}}{10 \text{ V}} = 0.5 \text{ A/V}
$$

The smallest current change detectable on the secondary side is:

$$
\Delta I_{sec} = q \times 0.5 = 4.8828 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 = 2.4414 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 2.44 \text{ mA}
$$

Referred to the primary side via the CT ratio (1000:5 ⇒ ratio = 200):

$$
\Delta I_{pri} = 2.44 \text{ mA} \times 200 = 0.488 \text{ A}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Quantisation step = **4.88 mV**, percentage resolution = **0.0488 %**, smallest primary current detectable = **0.488 A**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam — Alternative Model 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**
**(a)** Explain the **role of the anti-aliasing filter** in a digital relay. With a neat sketch, describe the **second-order Butterworth filter** response and derive its transfer function. Why is a Butterworth response preferred over a Chebyshev response? **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A 50 Hz power system signal is sampled at a rate of **2400 Hz** by a digital relay. Determine (i) the **number of samples per cycle**, (ii) the **Nyquist frequency**, and (iii) the **maximum harmonic order** that can be reconstructed without aliasing. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Role of the anti-aliasing filter:**
The anti-aliasing filter is a **low-pass filter** placed immediately before the A/D converter. Its purpose is to **band-limit** the input signal so that no frequency component above $f_s/2$ reaches the sampler. If high-frequency components (e.g., from switching transients, arcing faults, or CT saturation) are not attenuated, they will fold back into the 0–$f_s/2$ passband as spurious low-frequency components — a phenomenon called **aliasing** — and corrupt the measured fundamental.

**Second-order Butterworth filter — Transfer function:**
The standard normalised second-order Butterworth low-pass transfer function is:

$$
H(s) = \frac{1}{s^{2} + \sqrt{2}\,s + 1}
$$

De-normalising by substituting $s \to s/\omega_c$ (where $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ is the cutoff frequency):

$$
H(s) = \frac{\omega_c^{2}}{s^{2} + \sqrt{2}\,\omega_c s + \omega_c^{2}}
$$

The magnitude response is:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^{4}}}
$$

This is a **maximally flat** (i.e., no ripple) response in the passband, with a $-3$ dB attenuation at the cutoff frequency $f_c$ and a roll-off of $-40$ dB/decade in the stopband.

**Why Butterworth is preferred over Chebyshev:**
- Butterworth has a **monotonically flat** passband with **no ripple**, ensuring faithful reproduction of the fundamental.
- Chebyshev filters achieve a **steeper roll-off** but introduce **passband ripple** (Type I) or **stopband ripple** (Type II), which would distort the measured magnitude and cause errors in pickup decisions.
- For protective relaying, **measurement fidelity** in the passband is more critical than a marginally faster roll-off.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

*Given:*
- $f = 50$ Hz
- $f_s = 2400$ Hz

**Sub-Part (i) — Number of samples per cycle:**

$$
N = \frac{f_s}{f} = \frac{2400}{50} = 48 \text{ samples/cycle}
$$

**Sub-Part (ii) — Nyquist frequency:**

$$
f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{2400}{2} = 1200 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Sub-Part (iii) — Maximum harmonic order:**

The highest harmonic that can be reconstructed without aliasing is:

$$
N_{h,max} = \frac{f_N}{f} = \frac{1200}{50} = 24^{\text{th}} \text{ harmonic}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $N = 48$, $f_N = 1200$ Hz, $N_{h,max} = 24^{\text{th}}$ harmonic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bipolar A/D span:** Many students write $V_{span} = 10$ V instead of **20 V** for a $\pm 10$ V A/D. This **doubles the error** in the quantisation step.
> 2. **Confusing the ratio direction in CTs:** A CT labelled *1000:5* has a **primary-to-secondary** ratio of 200, *not* 0.005. Wrong ratio ⇒ wrong primary current answer.
> 3. **Skipping the anti-aliasing filter in the block diagram:** Examiners allot at least **1 mark** specifically for the AAF. Omitting it costs marks.
> 4. **Writing the Fourier formula without defining $N$ and $k$:** Always state the data window size $N$ and the sample index $k$ explicitly.
> 5. **Mixing up Nyquist frequency vs Nyquist rate:** $f_N = f_s/2$ is the Nyquist *frequency*; $2 f_{max}$ is the *minimum* sampling rate.
> 6. **Forgetting the DC offset rejection property of the full-cycle Fourier algorithm:** It is a one-line statement worth a mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Digital relay = real-time, fault-grade computer** — operation is *signal-flow* based, not *electromechanical*.
- [x] The **eight functional blocks** are: **CT/PT → Auxiliary CT/PT → Anti-Aliasing Filter → MUX → S/H → A/D → DSP/CPU → Comparator → Trip Output**.
- [x] **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The anti-aliasing filter cutoff is $f_c = f_s/2$.
- [x] **Quantisation step:** $q = V_{span} / 2^n$. For $\pm 10$ V, 12-bit, $q = 4.88$ mV.
- [x] **Common sampling rates:** $N = 4, 8, 12, 16, 32$ samples/cycle. $N = 12$ at 50 Hz is a textbook default.
- [x] **Full-Cycle Fourier Algorithm:** completely rejects **all integer harmonics** and **DC offset** — the most important algorithm for KTU.
- [x] **Phasor extraction:** $I_m = \sqrt{I_{Re}^{2} + I_{Im}^{2}}$; $\phi = \tan^{-1}(I_{Im}/I_{Re})$.
- [x] **Butterworth anti-aliasing filter:** maximally flat passband, $-40$ dB/decade roll-off for 2nd order; preferred over Chebyshev for relaying because of zero passband ripple.
- [x] **Operating time** of a digital relay: $t_{op} = T_{alg} + T_{CB}$ (typically **< 1 cycle** = 20 ms at 50 Hz for the algorithm alone).
- [x] **Always draw the labelled block diagram** in any 7-mark or 14-mark "principle of operation" question — it is the universal key to scoring.
- [x] **A/D resolution matters:** 12-bit is industry standard; 16-bit is for high-precision phasor measurement units (PMUs).
- [x] **Directional / impedance protection** is implemented *algorithmically* — the same hardware can perform overcurrent, distance, or differential functions simply by changing the software — this is the **multifunction** advantage of numerical relays.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Numerical Relays with Electromechanical and Static Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Numerical Relays with Electromechanical and Static Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Relays** are protective devices that detect abnormal conditions in a power system and initiate tripping commands to isolate faulty sections. The evolution of relays spans three major generations: **Electromechanical**, **Static**, and **Numerical**.

### 1.1.1 Electromechanical Relays (EMR)

An **electromechanical relay** is a protective device whose operating principle relies on the **mechanical displacement of a moving element** under the influence of electromagnetic forces produced by the operating quantities (current/voltage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operating Principle:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction — A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force, causing physical movement of a rotor or armature.

**Common EMR constructions:**
- **Attracted armature type** (solenoid, plunger)
- **Induction disc type** (shaded pole, wattmetric)
- **Induction cup type** (high-speed distance relays)
- **Balanced beam type** (differential relays)

### 1.1.2 Static Relays

A **static relay** is a relay in which the measurement and decision-making functions are performed by **solid-state analog electronic components** (transistors, diodes, op-amps, Zener diodes, comparators) **without any moving parts**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operating Principle:** Analog signal conditioning using rectifier → filter → level detector → comparator → output decision. The relay characteristic (e.g., IDMT, instantaneous) is implemented via hardware RC networks and analog comparators.

### 1.1.3 Numerical Relays

A **numerical relay** (also called a **digital relay** or **microprocessor-based relay**) is a relay that uses one or more **microprocessors, microcontrollers, or Digital Signal Processors (DSPs)** to sample the analog inputs (currents and voltages) and execute **protection algorithms entirely in software**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operating Principle:** Analog inputs → Anti-aliasing filter → **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** → Microprocessor/DSP executes protection algorithms (e.g., Fourier analysis, impedance calculation, differential logic) → Trip decision via Digital-to-Analog Converter or direct digital output.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Traffic Police Evolution"**

Imagine three generations of traffic police controlling a busy intersection (the **power system**) where vehicles are **currents** and a crash represents a **fault**:

| Generation | Analogy | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Electromechanical** | A **human officer with a hand-cranked signal lever** | Physically moves a heavy lever (armature/disc) to stop traffic. **Slow, mechanical, needs regular greasing and adjustment**, can only do one job at a time. |
| **Static** | An **officer using a transistorized walkie-talkie and a fixed logic circuit** | No moving parts, reacts faster, but the logic circuit is **hardwired** — changing rules requires a new circuit board. |
| **Numerical** | A **modern smart officer with a tablet and AI software** | Samples traffic continuously, runs multiple algorithms in parallel (overspeed, wrong-way, collision avoidance), **self-diagnoses issues**, logs events, communicates with a central control room, and can be **reprogrammed remotely** for new rules. |

This analogy captures the three pillars of advancement: **Speed → Intelligence → Connectivity**.

---

## 1.3 Historical Evolution & Generational Timeline

| Era | Technology | Active Components | Signal Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1900s–1960s** | Electromechanical | Coils, magnets, springs, induction disc/cup | Purely analog, mechanical |
| **1960s–1980s** | Static | Transistors, op-amps, diodes, comparators | Analog electronic |
| **1980s–Present** | Numerical/Digital | Microprocessors, DSPs, FPGAs, ADC/DAC | Digital (sampled data) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly categorizes relays into these three classes. The comparison table is a **frequently tested 14-mark question** and also appears as a **short 3-mark Part A question** asking to list any 6–8 differences.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — A Comparative Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Electromechanical Relay — Operating Logic Steps

The electromechanical relay operates on the **electromagnetic force principle**:

1. **Sensing:** CT/VT secondary current/voltage energizes the relay coil.
2. **Force Generation:** A magnetic field is produced, exerting force $F_m$ on the moving element (armature or disc).
3. **Mechanical Motion:** The moving element rotates/tilts against a restraining torque $T_r$ provided by a spring or counter-weight.
4. **Decision:** When operating torque $T_o > T_r$ (threshold pickup), the contacts close.
5. **Trip Output:** Mechanical contacts send the trip command to the circuit breaker.

**The electromagnetic force equation** (for a magnetic actuator):

$$F_m = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I^2 \cdot \frac{dL}{dx}$$

where $L$ is the inductance and $x$ is the air-gap displacement.

> [!NOTE]
> The relay characteristic (time-current) is **intrinsic to the mechanical design** — changing it requires **physical redesign** of the disc, magnet, or damping coil.

---

### 2.1.2 Static Relay — Operating Logic Steps

1. **Sensing:** CT/VT secondary signal is fed to an **input transducer/rectifier**.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** AC → DC conversion via full-wave rectifier and smoothing capacitor.
3. **Level Detection:** A **Schmitt trigger** or **op-amp comparator** detects when the rectified signal exceeds a preset reference (pickup level).
4. **Time Delay Generation:** An **RC integrator** or **monostable multivibrator** produces the prescribed operating time (IDMT, definite time).
5. **Amplification & Trip:** A power amplifier drives the trip coil of the breaker.

> [!NOTE]
> The characteristic shape is **determined by analog circuit components** — modifying it requires **changing hardware components** (resistors, capacitors).

---

### 2.1.3 Numerical Relay — Operating Logic Steps

1. **Sensing:** CT/VT secondaries feed **input isolation transformers** (for galvanic isolation and scaling).
2. **Anti-Aliasing Filtering:** A low-pass **anti-aliasing filter** band-limits the input to $\le f_s/2$ per the Nyquist criterion.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Multiplexed analog channels are sampled at a defined sampling rate $f_s$ (typically **12–64 samples per cycle**, i.e., 600 Hz to 3.2 kHz for a 50 Hz system).
4. **Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** The microprocessor executes:
   - **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** or **Full-Cycle / Half-Cycle Fourier Algorithm** to extract fundamental phasors.
   - Protection algorithms (impedance, differential, overcurrent, etc.).
5. **Decision Logic:** Software evaluates if the measured quantity violates the pickup characteristic.
6. **Trip Output:** A digital output bit drives the **trip relay (auxiliary electromechanical relay)** to command the breaker.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are routinely required for numerical relay design and comparison questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing (Nyquist criterion) | Hz |
| 2 | $f_s = N \cdot f_0$ | Sampling rate where $N$ = samples/cycle, $f_0$ = system frequency | Hz |
| 3 | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k^2}$ | RMS computation from $N$ digital samples | Volts |
| 4 | $V_1 = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k / N}$ | Fundamental phasor via DFT | Volts |
| 5 | $Z = \frac{V_{phasor}}{I_{phasor}}$ | Apparent impedance seen by distance relay | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $I_{diff} = I_1 + I_2$ | Differential current (for transformer/unit protection) | A |
| 7 | $I_{bias} = \frac{\vert I_1 - I_2 \vert}{2}$ | Bias / restraining current | A |
| 8 | $t_{op} = \frac{k \cdot \beta}{(I/I_s)^\alpha - 1}$ | **IDMT characteristic** (IEC 60255-151), $\alpha=0.02$, $\beta=0.14$ (standard inverse) | seconds |
| 9 | $F_m = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{dx}$ | Electromagnetic force on armature | N |
| 10 | $t_{op,EM} = k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I^2}{I^2 - I_{pickup}^2}\right)$ | Operating time of induction disc relay | seconds |
| 11 | $t_{trip,total} = t_{relay} + t_{breaker}$ | Total fault clearance time (relay + breaker) | seconds |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Numerical Relays

> [!NOTE]
> Numerical relays are the **backbone of modern digital substations**, mandated by standards such as **IEC 61850** (communication), **IEC 60255-118** (PMU/synchrophasor), and **IEEE C37.2** (device numbering). They enable:
> - **Adaptive protection** — relay settings adapt in real-time to changing network topology.
> - **Wide-Area Monitoring (WAMS)** via PMU data streams.
> - **Cyber-secure substation automation** with GOOSE messaging.
> - **Asset management** through continuous self-monitoring.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Generational Step

| Evolution | The "Why" — Engineering Need |
|---|---|
| EMR → Static | Eliminate moving parts → **higher reliability, faster operation, less maintenance**. |
| Static → Numerical | Enable **multifunction, communication, self-test, and adaptability** via software. |
| Numerical → Smart Grid | **Integration with IoT, AI-based fault classification, IEC 61850 interoperability**. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Detailed Comparative Derivation

## 3.1 Derivation: Operating Time Comparison Between Relay Generations

This section demonstrates the **quantitative superiority** of numerical relays through rigorous mathematical derivation.

### 3.1.1 Electromechanical Induction Disc Relay Operating Time

For an induction disc relay, the operating time is derived from the disc equation of motion:

$$J \cdot \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + D \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = T_{op} - T_{r}$$

where:
- $J$ = moment of inertia of the disc (kg·m²)
- $D$ = damping coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- $\theta$ = angular displacement (rad)
- $T_{op}$ = operating torque $\propto (I/I_s)^2$
- $T_r$ = restraining torque (constant)

**Step 1:** In steady-state operation (constant acceleration negligible), the angular velocity stabilizes:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} \approx 0 \implies \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{T_{op} - T_r}{D}$$

**Step 2:** The time to traverse a fixed contact gap $\theta_{total}$:

$$t_{op} = \frac{\theta_{total} \cdot D}{T_{op} - T_r}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $T_{op} = K (I/I_s)^2$ and $T_r = K$ (pickup at $I = I_s$):

$$t_{op} = \frac{\theta_{total} \cdot D \cdot K}{K(I/I_s)^2 - K} = \frac{\theta_{total} \cdot D}{(I/I_s)^2 - 1}$$

$$\boxed{t_{op}^{EMR} = \frac{K_1}{(I/I_s)^2 - 1}}$$

**Step 4:** Typical values: $t_{op} \approx \mathbf{10\,ms}$ to **several seconds** depending on fault current multiple.

---

### 3.1.2 Static Relay Operating Time

For a static relay using an RC integrator for time delay:

$$v_{cap}(t) = V_{in}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

**Step 5:** The comparator triggers when $v_{cap}(t) = V_{ref}$:

$$V_{ref} = V_{in}\left(1 - e^{-t_{op}/RC}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Solving for the operating time:

$$e^{-t_{op}/RC} = 1 - \frac{V_{ref}}{V_{in}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{op}^{Static} = -RC \cdot \ln\left(1 - \frac{V_{ref}}{V_{in}}\right)}$$

**Step 7:** Typical values: $t_{op} \approx \mathbf{5–20\,ms}$.

---

### 3.1.3 Numerical Relay Operating Time

The numerical relay's operating time consists of:

$$t_{op}^{Numerical} = t_{sampling} + t_{computation} + t_{output}$$

**Step 8:** Sampling window time (one full cycle for FCF algorithm):

$$t_{sampling} = \frac{1}{f_0} = 20\,\text{ms (for 50 Hz system)}$$

**Step 9:** Computation time (DSP algorithm execution, DFT on $N$ samples):

$$t_{computation} = N \cdot T_{cycle,DSP}$$

For a 32-sample/cycle DFT on a 100 MIPS DSP: $t_{comp} \approx \mathbf{0.5–2\,ms}$.

**Step 10:** Output relay actuation time (solid-state or fast electromagnetic):

$$t_{output} \approx \mathbf{1–3\,ms}$$

**Step 11:** Total operating time:

$$\boxed{t_{op}^{Numerical} \approx 20 + 2 + 2 = 24\,\text{ms (typical, half-cycle algorithm reduces to 12 ms)}}$$

---

### 3.1.4 Consolidated Speed Comparison

| Relay Type | Typical Operating Time | Speed Category |
|---|---|---|
| Electromechanical | 10 ms – 3000 ms | **Slow** (mechanical inertia dominated) |
| Static | 5 ms – 100 ms | **Fast** (electronic, but no parallel processing) |
| Numerical | **8 ms – 25 ms** | **Very Fast** (single-cycle DSP) |

---

## 3.2 Burden on CT/VT — Comparative Analysis

**Step 12:** Define CT burden as the total impedance seen by the CT secondary:

$$Z_{burden} = Z_{relay} + Z_{lead\,wires}$$

**Step 13:** For an electromechanical relay, the operating coil draws substantial current — typical burden:

$$Z_{burden}^{EMR} \approx 1\text{–}10\,\Omega \text{ at CT secondary}$$

**Step 14:** For a static relay, high-impedance op-amp inputs reduce burden:

$$Z_{burden}^{Static} \approx 0.1\text{–}1\,\Omega$$

**Step 15:** For a numerical relay, the input is fed through a **burden resistor** (typically 0.1 Ω for current input) and an **isolation transformer**, presenting very low burden:

$$Z_{burden}^{Numerical} \approx 0.01\text{–}0.1\,\Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Lower CT burden → **less CT saturation**, **higher accuracy**, and ability to share CTs between multiple relays (modern multi-function numerical IEDs).

---

## 3.3 CT Saturation Immunity — Mathematical Justification

Numerical relays can correct for CT saturation in software:

**Step 16:** Phasor extraction with **Half-Cycle Fourier (HCF) Algorithm**:

$$V_1^{HCF} = \frac{4}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N/2 - 1} v_k \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N} + \frac{\pi}{N}\right) \cdot e^{j(\pi/2 - \pi/N)}$$

**Step 17:** Saturation detection logic checks if the second harmonic exceeds a threshold:

$$THD_{ratio} = \frac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} > 0.15 \implies \text{Saturation Flag} = 1$$

**Step 18:** When saturation is flagged, the relay may **block or restrain** the tripping decision — a feature unavailable in EMR or static relays.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architectural Block Diagrams — All Three Generations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EMRBlock["ELECTROMECHANICAL RELAY ARCHITECTURE"]
        A1[CT/VT Secondary] --> A2[Operating Coil]
        A2 --> A3{Electromagnetic Force}

        A4 -->|Mechanical motion| A5[Contact Closure]
        A5 --> A6[Trip Coil of Breaker]
        A4 -->|Spring restoring| A7[Restraining Spring]
    end

    subgraph StaticBlock["STATIC RELAY ARCHITECTURE"]

        B2 --> B3[Smoothing Filter]

        B5 --> B6[Schmitt Trigger]
        B6 --> B7[Power Amplifier]
        B7 --> B8[Trip Coil of Breaker]
    end

    subgraph NumBlock["NUMERICAL RELAY ARCHITECTURE"]
        C1[CT/VT Secondary] --> C2[Input Isolation Transformer]
        C2 --> C3[Anti-Aliasing LPF]

        C4 --> C5[Analog Multiplexer]
        C5 --> C6[ADC - 12 to 16 bit]
        C6 --> C7[Microprocessor / DSP]
        C7 -->|Software Algorithms| C8[Protection Logic]
        C8 --> C9[Trip Decision Register]
        C9 --> C10[Output Relay - Solid State]
        C10 --> C11[Trip Coil of Breaker]
        C7 <--> C12[Memory RAM/ROM]
        C7 <--> C13[Communication Port - IEC 61850]
        C7 <--> C14[Display / HMI]
        C7 <--> C15[Event / Fault Recorder]
    end

    classDef emrStyle fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

---

## 4.2 Evolution Pathway Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Generation 1\nElectromechanical\n1900 to 1960] -->|Move Parts Removed| G2[Generation 2\nStatic - Analog\n1960 to 1980]
    G2 -->|Microprocessor Added| G3[Generation 3\nNumerical - Digital\n1980 to Present]
    G3 -->|Smart Grid Integration| G4[Smart Grid IED\nIEC 61850 + AI\nPresent to Future]

    G1 -->|"Slow, Mechanical\nSingle Function"| F1[Limitations]
    G2 -->|"Rigid Hardware\nNo Self-Test"| F2[Limitations]
    G3 -->|"Software Dependent\nCyber Concerns"| F3[Limitations]

    classDef gen1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gen2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gen3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gen4 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef limit fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px

    class G1 gen1
    class G2 gen2
    class G3 gen3
    class G4 gen4
    class F1,F2,F3 limit
```

---

## 4.3 Comprehensive Master Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Electromechanical (EMR) | Static | Numerical |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating Principle** | Electromagnetic force & mechanical motion | Analog solid-state electronics (no moving parts) | Digital signal processing via microprocessor/DSP |
| **Active Components** | Coils, magnets, springs, induction disc/cup | Transistors, op-amps, diodes, Zener, comparators | Microprocessor, DSP, ADC, memory, FPGA |
| **Signal Domain** | Purely analog (mechanical) | Analog (electronic) | Digital (sampled data) |
| **Operating Time** | 10 ms – 3000 ms (slowest) | 5 ms – 100 ms | **8 ms – 25 ms** (fastest) |
| **Accuracy** | ± 5% to ± 10% | ± 2% to ± 5% | **± 1% or better** |
| **CT/VT Burden** | High (1–10 Ω) | Moderate (0.1–1 Ω) | **Very Low (0.01–0.1 Ω)** |
| **Characteristic Flexibility** | **Fixed** (requires hardware change) | Limited (hardware redesign needed) | **Fully programmable via software** |
| **Multifunction Capability** | **Single function per relay** | Limited multifunction | **Up to 20+ functions in one IED** |
| **Self-Diagnostics** | **Not available** | Minimal | **Comprehensive (continuous self-test)** |
| **Communication** | **None** | Limited (analog outputs) | **Full digital (IEC 61850, DNP3, Modbus, GOOSE)** |
| **Event/Fault Recording** | **Not available** | Limited (analog fault recorder) | **COMTRADE / IEEE C37.111 digital fault records** |
| **Data Logging** | None | Minimal | **Comprehensive (sequential events recorder)** |
| **Remote Configuration** | **Impossible** | Difficult | **Full remote access via SCADA/engineering tool** |
| **Power Consumption** | High (coil energization) | Moderate | **Low** |
| **Size & Weight** | Large, heavy (kg scale) | Compact, lighter | **Very compact (cm scale)** |
| **Reliability** | Good but mechanical wear | Moderate (component aging, drift) | **High (no moving parts, MTBF > 100 years)** |
| **Maintenance** | **Frequent** (lubrication, calibration, contact cleaning) | Moderate (periodic calibration) | **Minimal** (self-diagnostic driven) |
| **Sensitivity to Environment** | High (vibration, dust, humidity, temperature) | High (temperature, transients, voltage spikes) | **Low** (temperature-compensated, shielded) |
| **Initial Cost** | Low | Moderate | **High** |
| **Lifecycle Cost (TCO)** | **High** (frequent maintenance, replacement) | Moderate | **Low** (despite high initial cost) |
| **Settings Range** | Narrow | Moderate | **Very wide (fine resolution)** |
| **Adaptive Protection** | **Not possible** | **Not possible** | **Yes** (real-time setting changes) |
| **Cybersecurity** | N/A (no network) | Limited | **Critical concern (networked device)** |
| **Typical Applications** | Legacy systems, backup protection | Intermediate generation substations | **Modern digital substations, smart grids** |
| **Standards Compliance** | IEC 60255 series (legacy) | IEC 60255 series | IEC 60255, IEC 61850, IEEE C37.90, C37.2 |
| **Failure Mode** | Predictable mechanical wear | Drift, component aging | Software bugs, hardware failure (rare) |

---

## 4.4 IED Functional Architecture (Numerical Relay Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        I1[Ia CT Input] --> I2[Burden Resistor 0.1 ohm]
        I2 --> I3[Isolation Transformer]

        I4 --> I5[Anti-Aliasing LPF cutoff 1.5 kHz]
        I5 --> I6[Sample and Hold MUX]
    end

    subgraph DigitalStage["Digital Processing Stage"]
        I6 --> D1[12/16 bit ADC]
        D1 --> D2[DSP - 32 bit Floating Point]
        D2 --> D3[Fourier Algorithm DFT]
        D3 --> D4[Protection Functions Library]
        D4 --> D5{Logic Decision}
        D5 -->|Trip| D6[Trip Register]
        D5 -->|Alarm| D7[Alarm Register]
        D5 -->|No Action| D8[Reset]
    end

    subgraph CommStage["Communication and HMI Stage"]
        D2 --> H1[Ethernet IEC 61850]
        D2 --> H2[Serial RS485 DNP3]
        D2 --> H3[USB Engineering Port]
        D2 --> H4[Front Panel LCD HMI]
        D2 --> H5[Fault Recorder COMTRADE]
        D2 --> H6[Event Log SOE]
    end

    classDef input fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef digital fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef comm fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px

    class I1,I2,I3,I4,I5,I6 input

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six advantages of numerical relays over electromechanical relays.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher Accuracy:** Numerical relays achieve accuracy of ±1% or better through software-based phasor extraction (DFT), compared to ±5–10% for electromechanical relays.

2. **Multifunction Capability:** A single numerical IED (Intelligent Electronic Device) can perform overcurrent, distance, differential, breaker failure, and auto-reclosing functions simultaneously — replacing 5–10 separate electromechanical relays.

3. **Self-Diagnostics & Reliability:** Continuous self-testing identifies failures before protection is needed, achieving MTBF > 100 years, whereas EMRs require scheduled manual inspection.

4. **Communication Capability:** Direct IEC 61850 / DNP3 / Modbus Ethernet interface for SCADA integration, GOOSE messaging, and remote configuration — completely absent in EMRs.

5. **Event/Fault Recording:** COMTRADE-format digital fault records (waveforms, phasors, timestamps) enable **post-event analysis**, which is impossible with electromechanical relays.

6. **Programmable Characteristics:** IDMT curves (standard inverse, very inverse, extremely inverse), quadrilateral impedance characteristics, and bias differentials can all be **selected via software settings** without any hardware change.

*(Any 6 of the above, well-explained, fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Compare static relays and electromechanical relays based on: (i) moving parts, (ii) operating speed, and (iii) CT burden.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Electromechanical Relay | Static Relay |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Moving Parts** | **Yes** — armature, disc, contacts are all physically moving | **No** — purely electronic, no mechanical motion |
| **(ii) Operating Speed** | **Slow** (10 ms – 3 sec) due to inertia of disc/armature | **Fast** (5 – 100 ms) — only electronic delay |
| **(iii) CT Burden** | **High** (1–10 Ω) — heavy current required to drive the operating coil | **Low** (0.1–1 Ω) — high-impedance op-amp/comparator inputs |

> [!NOTE]
> A neat tabular comparison with brief explanations fetches **full 3 marks** in KTU Part A.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 - Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**With neat block diagrams, explain the working principle of electromechanical, static, and numerical relays. Compare their salient features in a tabular form.**

#### **Part (a) — Working Principles with Block Diagrams [7 Marks]**

**Electromechanical Relay (3 marks):**
- **Construction:** Operating coil + magnetic circuit + moving armature/disc + restraining spring + contacts.
- **Working:** Current from CT flows through the operating coil, producing magnetic flux. Flux attracts the armature or rotates the induction disc against spring restraint. When operating torque exceeds restraining torque beyond a pickup value, the disc/armature moves and closes the trip contacts.
- **Reference:** Block diagram from Section 4.1 (EMRBlock subgraph).

**Static Relay (2 marks):**
- **Construction:** Rectifier + filter + comparator + RC time delay + amplifier.
- **Working:** AC signal from CT is rectified and filtered to DC. The DC level is compared with a reference. If it exceeds pickup, a timing circuit (RC network) introduces the desired IDMT delay. The output amplifier drives the trip coil. **No moving parts.**

**Numerical Relay (2 marks):**
- **Construction:** Isolation transformer + anti-aliasing filter + ADC + microprocessor/DSP + memory + I/O.
- **Working:** Analog CT/VT signals are sampled by ADC, processed by DSP using **DFT-based phasor extraction**. Software algorithms (overcurrent, distance, differential) run cyclically. The trip decision is issued via a digital output bit. The same hardware performs multiple protection functions.

#### **Part (b) — Comprehensive Tabular Comparison [7 Marks]**

Students must present the **comparison matrix from Section 4.3** of these notes, covering **at least 12 parameters** including:
1. Operating principle
2. Speed (with values)
3. Accuracy
4. CT/VT burden
5. Multifunction capability
6. Self-diagnostics
7. Communication
8. Event recording
9. Maintenance
10. Initial cost
11. Lifecycle cost
12. Reliability

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Writing only 5–6 differences** → KTU expects **minimum 10–12 parameters** for full 7 marks. Aim for ≥12.
> 2. **No numerical values** → Vague answers like "numerical is faster" without specifying time ranges fetch only 50% credit. Always quantify: *"8–25 ms vs 10–3000 ms"*.
> 3. **Confusing static with numerical** → Static relays are **analog electronic** (no microprocessor). Numerical relays **must have a microprocessor/DSP**. This distinction is worth 1 mark minimum.
> 4. **Missing block diagram** → A 7-mark question on "working principles" **mandates a diagram**. Without it, max 5/7.
> 5. **No mention of standards** (IEC 61850, IEC 60255-151, COMTRADE) → Examiners reward awareness of relevant standards with 0.5–1 bonus mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023 - Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Discuss in detail the evolution of protective relays from electromechanical to numerical type. Highlight the advantages, limitations, and modern application areas of numerical relays.**

#### **Part (a) — Evolution & Generational Shift [7 Marks]**

**Generation 1 — Electromechanical (1900–1960):** *(2 marks)*
- Operating principle: Electromagnetic induction and force.
- Examples: Induction disc overcurrent relay, induction cup distance relay, attracted armature instantaneous relay.
- **Limitations:** Mechanical wear, fixed characteristics, no communication, requires regular maintenance, slow response, high CT burden.

**Generation 2 — Static (1960–1980):** *(2 marks)*
- Operating principle: Analog solid-state electronics (transistors, op-amps).
- Examples: Static overcurrent relay, static distance relay, static differential relay.
- **Advantages over EMR:** No moving parts, faster (5–100 ms), lower burden, more compact.
- **Limitations:** Characteristics still hardware-defined, limited self-test, sensitive to transients and temperature, drift in component values.

**Generation 3 — Numerical (1980–Present):** *(3 marks)*
- Operating principle: Digital sampling + microprocessor/DSP + software algorithms.
- Examples: SEL 487B, ABB REL670, GE Multilin 845, Siemens SIPROTEC 7SA86.
- **Advantages:** Multifunction, programmable, self-diagnostic, communication (IEC 61850), fault recording, adaptive protection, compact.
- **Modern applications:** Smart substations, microgrid protection, HVDC converter station protection, phasor measurement units (PMUs), wide-area monitoring systems (WAMS), IEC 61850 process bus.

#### **Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations & Modern Applications [7 Marks]**

**Key Advantages of Numerical Relays (3 marks):**
1. **Multifunctionality** — One IED replaces 10–20 EMRs.
2. **Self-Diagnostics** — Continuous self-test, MTBF > 100 years.
3. **Communication** — IEC 61850, DNP3, Modbus, IEC 60870-5-104 for full SCADA integration.
4. **Programmability** — Settings, logic, and characteristics all in software.
5. **Event Recording** — COMTRADE fault waveforms, sequence-of-events log.
6. **Remote Access** — Configuration and monitoring from control center.
7. **Adaptive Protection** — Real-time setting adjustment based on network conditions.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Higher Initial Cost** — 5–10× more expensive than equivalent static relays.
2. **Software Dependency** — Bugs, version mismatches, update management.
3. **Cybersecurity Risk** — Networked device, vulnerable to cyberattacks (NERC CIP compliance required).
4. **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** — Sensitive to high-level EMI in HV switchyards (requires IEC 60255-26 compliance testing).
5. **Skilled Personnel** — Requires engineers trained in software tools and IED configuration.

**Modern Application Areas (2 marks):**
- **Digital Substations** (IEC 61850-based process bus, station bus architecture).
- **Smart Grid** integration with DER (Distributed Energy Resources).
- **Microgrid Protection** — bidirectional power flow handling.
- **HVDC & FACTS** device protection.
- **Wide-Area Protection** using PMU data streams.
- **Cyber-Physical Substation Automation.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B Specific:**
> 1. **Skipping Generation 2 (Static)** → Many students jump directly from EMR to Numerical. **Static relays form the critical bridge** — omitting them loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Not mentioning specific IED models or standards** → Vague answers lose 1 mark. Name **at least 2 standards** (IEC 61850, COMTRADE/IEEE C37.111, IEC 60255-151) and **1–2 real IED families** (SEL, ABB, GE, Siemens).
> 3. **Forgetting cybersecurity** → A current 2024-scheme examiner expects **cybersecurity as a modern concern**. Missing it = -1 mark.
> 4. **No mention of adaptive/WAMS** → For 2024 syllabus, **adaptive protection** and **PMU-based wide-area protection** are explicitly listed topics. Include them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Use before exam**

- [ ] **Three Generations:** EMR → Static → Numerical. Each generation solved limitations of the previous.
- [ ] **EMR Principle:** Electromagnetic force $\rightarrow$ mechanical motion $\rightarrow$ contact closure. Slow (10 ms – 3 s).
- [ ] **Static Principle:** AC $\rightarrow$ rectifier $\rightarrow$ comparator $\rightarrow$ RC timer $\rightarrow$ trip. No moving parts. Medium speed (5–100 ms).
- [ ] **Numerical Principle:** ADC samples signals $\rightarrow$ DSP runs software algorithms $\rightarrow$ digital trip output. Fastest (8–25 ms).
- [ ] **Key DFT Formula:** $V_1 = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum v_k e^{-j 2\pi k / N}$ — fundamental phasor extraction.
- [ ] **Nyquist Criterion:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ — sample rate must exceed twice the highest signal frequency.
- [ ] **Burden Hierarchy:** EMR (highest) > Static > Numerical (lowest).
- [ ] **CT Saturation:** Numerical relays detect it via **2nd harmonic ratio** and can restrain/block.
- [ ] **IDMT Equation:** $t_{op} = \frac{0.14}{(I/I_s)^{0.02} - 1}$ (IEC standard inverse) — implementable purely in software on numerical relays.
- [ ] **Multifunction:** One numerical IED = 10–20 electromechanical relays (overcurrent + distance + differential + breaker failure + auto-reclose + metering + fault recording).
- [ ] **Standards to Memorize:** IEC 60255 (relays), IEC 61850 (substation communication), IEEE C37.111 (COMTRADE), IEC 60870-5-104 (SCADA protocol).
- [ ] **Cybersecurity:** Numerical relays are **networked devices** — NERC CIP / IEC 62351 standards apply.
- [ ] **Lifecycle Cost (TCO):** Numerical > Static > EMR initial cost, but **reverse for lifecycle cost**.
- [ ] **MTBF:** Electromechanical (~50 years), Static (~75 years), **Numerical (>100 years)**.
- [ ] **Exam Tip:** For 14-mark comparison questions, **always include a block diagram** + **table with ≥12 parameters** + **quantitative values** (numbers, units).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of Numerical Relays - Communication in Protective Relays (IEC 61850)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advantages of Numerical Relays - Communication in Protective Relays (IEC 61850)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Numerical Relays

A **Numerical Relay** is a microprocessor-based (or Digital Signal Processor-based) protective relay in which the analog current and voltage signals from the power system are converted into digital data using Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs). The digitized signals are then processed using sophisticated mathematical algorithms implemented in software to make accurate, reliable, and fast protection decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> A numerical relay is a computer-based relay where the protection algorithms (such as Fourier analysis, traveling wave detection, impedance calculation, differential protection logic) are implemented entirely through software running on a microprocessor/DSP/FPGA platform. It represents the third generation of protective relays, succeeding electromechanical and static (analog electronic) relays.

## 1.2 Definition of Communication in Protective Relays & IEC 61850

**Communication in Protective Relays** refers to the exchange of digital data (status, measurements, commands, configuration, fault records, and time synchronization signals) between protective Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs), bay controllers, SCADA systems, and control centers through standardized communication networks.

**IEC 61850** is the international communication standard titled *"Communication Networks and Systems for Power Utility Automation"*, published by the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC). It is the **first globally unified, object-oriented, and Ethernet-based standard** for substation communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 61850 Core Identity:**
> - Standard ID: **IEC 61850 (2010 onwards, with 2014/2020 amendments)**
> - Replaces: **IEC 60870-5-103** and proprietary vendor protocols (UCA 2.0, DNP 3.0 partially)
> - Default Physical Layer: **100 Mbit/s Ethernet (Optical/Copper)**
> - Key Innovation: **Abstract Data Modeling + ACSI (Abstract Communication Service Interface) + SCL (Substation Configuration Language)**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Smart Substation Hospital" Analogy)

Imagine a modern **multi-specialty hospital** that replaces an old rural clinic:

- **Old Electromechanical Relays** = A **stethoscope** that only detects one thing (heartbeat), needs manual tuning, and cannot store or share information.
- **Static (Analog) Relays** = A **basic ECG machine** that gives a graph but cannot be networked with other machines.
- **Numerical Relays** = A **modern Patient Monitor** that digitizes vital signs, runs multiple algorithms, displays waveforms, stores 24-hour history, and sends alerts over Wi-Fi to a central nursing station.
- **IEC 61850** = The **Hospital Information System (HIS)** — a common language that allows the heart monitor, ventilator, and infusion pump (IEDs from different vendors) to talk to the central station (SCADA), share standardized reports (COMTRADE files), and trigger coordinated alarms (GOOSE messages).

> [!TIP]
> **Why Numerical Relays + IEC 61850 Are Inseparable in KTU 2024:**
> Numerical relays are inherently **digital computers with Ethernet ports**. Without a unified standard like IEC 61850, each vendor's relay would speak a different "language." IEC 61850 ensures **interoperability**, meaning an ABB relay, a Siemens relay, and a Schneider relay can all work in the **same substation** and exchange data seamlessly.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Sampling Rate:** 80 samples/cycle (50 Hz system → **4000 Hz**, 60 Hz system → **4800 Hz**) per IEC 61850-9-2 (Sampled Values)
- **GOOSE Latency Requirement:** **< 4 ms** (Type 1A) for tripping, **< 10 ms** (Type 1B)
- **Standard Reference Voltage:** **110 V** (phasor magnitude scaling)
- **Standard Reference Current:** **1 A or 5 A** (CT secondary)
- **Ethernet Speed:** **100 Mbit/s** (Fast Ethernet, IEC 61850 default) up to **1 Gbit/s** (Process Bus)
- **Time Sync Precision:** **±1 µs** via **IEEE 1588 (PTP)** for Sampled Values

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Advantages of Numerical Relays Over Electromagnetic and Static Relays

Numerical relays dominate modern substations because they integrate **measurement, protection, communication, recording, and self-testing** into a single device. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups these into functional categories:

### 2.1.1 Functional and Operational Advantages

1. **Multi-Function Capability (One Relay, Many Protections)**
   A single numerical relay can implement **distance, overcurrent, differential, and breaker failure** protections simultaneously, replacing 4–5 discrete relays used in older schemes.

2. **Adaptive Characteristics**
   The relay can **change its settings in real time** based on system conditions (e.g., load encroachment, power swing detection, adaptive impedance zone modification).

3. **Self-Test and Self-Monitoring**
   Continuous **watchdog checks, memory tests, ADC calibration**, and **CT/VT supervision** detect internal failures. This shifts maintenance from *time-based* to *condition-based*.

4. **High Accuracy and Repeatability**
   Digital processing eliminates drift caused by **temperature, aging of analog components, and component tolerance** in static relays.

5. **Wide Operating Range**
   A single numerical relay works accurately for currents from **0.1 A to 50 kA** (after appropriate CT selection) by changing gain in software.

6. **Event Recording and Disturbance Storage**
   Stores **COMTRADE files** (IEEE C37.111 standard format: `.cfg` configuration + `.dat` data) capturing pre-fault and post-fault waveforms for post-event analysis.

7. **Remote Configuration and Diagnostics**
   Engineers can **change settings, retrieve fault records, and run diagnostics** from a remote control center via IEC 61850, avoiding site visits.

8. **Metering and Power Quality**
   Built-in **DFT/FFT-based** measurement of voltage, current, frequency, active/reactive power, harmonics (up to 63rd harmonic in modern relays), and flicker.

9. **Integration with SCADA/EMS**
   Direct mapping to **IEC 61850 MMS, DNP 3.0, Modbus, IEC 60870-5-104** for SCADA integration.

10. **Reduced Wiring and Panel Space**
    One numerical relay replaces multiple hardware relays, reducing **copper wiring, panel size, and CT/VT burden**.

### 2.1.2 Communication-Specific Advantages (Tied to IEC 61850)

- **Peer-to-Peer GOOSE Messaging:** Trip commands transmitted in **< 4 ms** over Ethernet, replacing hardwired inter-trip cables.
- **Sampled Values (SV):** Streaming of digitized CT/VT outputs over Ethernet, enabling **non-conventional instrument transformers (NCITs)** like optical CTs and Rogowski coils.
- **Plug-and-Play Interoperability:** SCL (Substation Configuration Language, an **XML-based** file, `.scd`/`.icd`/`.iid`) allows IEDs from different vendors to be configured uniformly.
- **High Reliability via Redundancy:** Protocols like **HSR (High-Availability Seamless Redundancy)** and **PRP (Parallel Redundancy Protocol)** defined in IEC 61850-8-1 provide **zero-loss redundancy**.

## 2.2 IEC 61850 Architecture — Layered Model

IEC 61850 uses a **three-layer substation model**:

| Layer | Function | Typical IEDs | Communication Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Station Level** | SCADA, Engineering, Time Sync | Operator PC, HMI, Gateway, GPS Clock | **MMS** (ISO 9506) over TCP/IP |
| **Bay Level** | Protection, Control, Measurement | Numerical Relays, Bay Controllers, Metering IEDs | **MMS + GOOSE** |
| **Process Level** | Primary Equipment Interface | Merging Units (MU), Breaker IEDs, Sensors | **GOOSE + Sampled Values (SV)** |

## 2.3 IEC 61850 Data Model Hierarchy (Highly Important for KTU)

IEC 61850 organizes data in a strict hierarchy:

**Logical Device (LD)** → **Logical Node (LN)** → **Data Object (DO)** → **Data Attribute (DA)**

Example path:
```
IED1 / Protection / PDIS1 / Str / general / d (distance protection start, "general" data object, "d" attribute)
```

### 2.3.1 Important Logical Node Prefixes (KTU High-Yield)

| Prefix | Logical Node Class | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | Protection Functions | **PDIS** (Distance), **PIOC** (Instantaneous Overcurrent), **PDIF** (Differential), **PTOC** (Time Overcurrent) |
| **C** | Control | **CILO** (Interlocking), **CSWI** (Switch Controller) |
| **M** | Metering & Measurement | **MMXU** (3-phase measurement), **MHAI** (Harmonics) |
| **A** | Automatic Control | **AVCO** (Voltage Control), **ATCC** (Tap Changer) |
| **I** | Interface/Archives | **IHMI** (Human-Machine Interface), **ITCI** (Telecontrol) |
| **L** | System Logical Nodes | **LPHD** (Physical Device), **LLN0** (Logical Node Zero) |
| **S** | Sensors & Supervision | **SVBR** (Vibration), **SCBR** (Circuit Breaker) |
| **T** | Instrument Transformers | **TCTR** (Current Transformer), **TVTR** (Voltage Transformer) |
| **X** | Switchgear | **XCBR** (Circuit Breaker), **XSWI** (Switch) |
| **Z** | Power System Equipment | **ZBAT** (Battery), **ZGEN** (Generator) |

## 2.4 IEC 61850 Communication Profiles (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Profile | Standard Clause | Function | Latency Class | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification)** | IEC 61850-8-1 | Client-Server reporting, configuration, control | **500–1000 ms** | SCADA telemetry, settings, fault records |
| **GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event)** | IEC 61850-8-1 | Fast peer-to-peer event messaging (Trip, Interlock) | **< 4 ms (Type 1A)**, < 10 ms (Type 1B) | Inter-trip, breaker failure, busbar protection |
| **Sampled Values (SV)** | IEC 61850-9-2 | Streaming of digitized current/voltage samples | **< 3 ms** (Process Bus) | Digital input to protection IEDs from Merging Units |
| **PTP (IEEE 1588v2 Precision Time Protocol)** | IEC 61850-9-3 | Time synchronization | **±1 µs** | Sync for SV streams across substation |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S. No. | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s = N \cdot f_{sys}$ | $N$ = samples per cycle (typically 80), $f_{sys}$ = 50 Hz | Hz |
| 2 | Nyquist Criterion | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling to avoid aliasing | Hz |
| 3 | GOOSE Latency Budget (Type 1A) | $t_{GOOSE} \leq 4$ ms | From event detection to receive | ms |
| 4 | GOOSE Retransmission (Backoff) | $T_n = 2^{(n-1)} \cdot t_0$ | Exponential backoff, $n$ retransmission, $t_0$ initial | ms |
| 5 | SV Message Rate (IEC 61850-9-2 LE) | $R_{SV} = 4000$ or $4800$ frames/s | At 50 Hz or 60 Hz, 80 samples/cycle | frames/s |
| 6 | Bandwidth (SV per IED) | $B_{SV} = R_{SV} \cdot L_{frame}$ | $L_{frame} \approx 130$ bytes per ASDU | Mbit/s |
| 7 | CT Secondary Standard | $I_{sec} = 1$ A or $5$ A | IEC standard secondary currents | A |
| 8 | VT Secondary Standard | $V_{sec} = 100 / \sqrt{3}$ or $110 / \sqrt{3}$ | Phase-to-neutral | V |
| 9 | Bandwidth Estimate (Full Process Bus) | $B_{total} = N_{MU} \cdot B_{SV} + N_{GOOSE} \cdot B_{GOOSE}$ | Total Ethernet bandwidth required | Mbit/s |
| 10 | Protection Operating Time | $t_{op} = t_{detect} + t_{process} + t_{GOOSE}$ | End-to-end trip latency | ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick for Communication Protocols:**
> - **MMS** = "**M**ail **S**ervice" (slow, reliable, client-server) → 500 ms class
> - **GOOSE** = "**G**roup **O**peration **O**n **S**ubstation **E**vent" (fast, multicast, peer-to-peer) → 4 ms class
> - **SV** = "**S**ample **V**alues" (continuous, streaming, multicast) → Real-time

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Numerical relays with IEC 61850 are deployed in:
- **EHV/HV Substations** (400 kV, 220 kV, 110 kV) — Almost 100% in India since 2010
- **Smart Grids and Microgrids** — Distributed Energy Resource (DER) integration
- **Digital Substations** (Process Bus architecture) — MUs replace copper wiring
- **HVDC Converter Stations** — Protection of DC cables and converter transformers
- **Railway Traction Substations** — 25 kV OHE protection
- **Wind and Solar Farms** — IEC 61850-7-420 for DER
- **Industrial Process Plants** — Substation automation for petrochemical, steel plants

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Step-by-Step: GOOSE Message Transmission and Latency Calculation

The KTU 2024 syllabus frequently tests how GOOSE messages achieve their **4 ms latency target** despite running over standard Ethernet. Below is the exhaustive analytical breakdown.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Event Detection in IED

A numerical relay (say, **PDIS1 — Distance Protection Zone 1**) detects a fault. Internal processing time is dominated by the protection algorithm (e.g., **Full-Cycle Fourier Transform** or **Traveling Wave** based).

$$
t_{detect} = t_{alg} + t_{judge}
$$

For a **Fourier-based** numerical distance relay at 50 Hz with **1-cycle (20 ms) DFT**:
- $t_{alg} = 20$ ms (one full cycle of data window)
- $t_{judge} \approx 0.5$ ms
- Therefore $t_{detect} \approx 20.5$ ms

For a **half-cycle (10 ms) accelerated algorithm**:
- $t_{alg} = 10$ ms, $t_{judge} \approx 0.5$ ms
- $t_{detect} \approx 10.5$ ms

### 3.1.2 Step 2: GOOSE Frame Construction

The IED constructs a **Layer 2 Ethernet frame** (not IP-based, to bypass TCP/IP stack delays). The frame is a multicast frame on a reserved **EtherType 0x88B8** (per IEC 61850-8-1).

$$
L_{frame} = L_{header} + L_{PDU} + L_{FCS}
$$

Where:
- $L_{header} = 14$ bytes (MAC + EtherType)
- $L_{PDU} = $ GOOSE PDU (typically **100–200 bytes**)
- $L_{FCS} = 4$ bytes (Frame Check Sequence)
- Typical $L_{frame} \approx 130$–$200$ bytes

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Transmission Delay on 100 Mbit/s Ethernet

$$
t_{tx} = \frac{L_{frame} \cdot 8}{R_{link}}
$$

For a 150-byte frame on 100 Mbit/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tx} &= \frac{150 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}} \\
t_{tx} &= \frac{1200}{10^{8}} \text{ s} \\
t_{tx} &= 12 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **negligible** compared to the 4 ms budget.

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Network Propagation and Switching Delay

Ethernet switches introduce forwarding delay, typically:

$$
t_{switch} = 5 \text{ to } 10 \, \mu s \text{ per switch (cut-through)}
$$

For a substation with **3 switches** in the path:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{sw,total} &= 3 \times 7 \, \mu s \\
t_{sw,total} &= 21 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Total GOOSE Latency

$$
t_{GOOSE,total} = t_{tx} + t_{sw,total} + t_{rx,proc}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{GOOSE,total} &= 12 \, \mu s + 21 \, \mu s + 30 \, \mu s \text{ (receiver processing)} \\
t_{GOOSE,total} &= 63 \, \mu s \\
t_{GOOSE,total} &\approx 0.063 \, ms
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **far below 4 ms**, confirming the real-time performance of GOOSE.

### 3.1.6 Step 6: End-to-End Trip Time (Operating Time of Protection)

$$
t_{op} = t_{detect} + t_{GOOSE,total} + t_{CB}
$$

Where $t_{CB}$ is the circuit breaker operating time ($\approx$ 2–3 cycles = 40–60 ms for 50 Hz).

For a 1-cycle algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{op} &= 20.5 \text{ ms} + 0.063 \text{ ms} + 50 \text{ ms} \\
t_{op} &\approx 70.6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a half-cycle algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{op} &= 10.5 \text{ ms} + 0.063 \text{ ms} + 50 \text{ ms} \\
t_{op} &\approx 60.6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **comparable to or better than** static/electromechanical relay trip times, demonstrating that numerical relays meet **KTU-required operating times of 60–100 ms** for HV/EHV lines.

## 3.2 Analytical Step-by-Step: Sampled Values Bandwidth Calculation (For Process Bus)

### 3.2.1 Step 1: Define Sampling Parameters

Per IEC 61850-9-2 LE (Light Edition), the standard sampling rate is:
- $N = 80$ samples/cycle
- $f_{sys} = 50$ Hz
- Frames per second: $f_s = 80 \times 50 = 4000$ frames/s

### 3.2.2 Step 2: Frame Structure (ASDU)

Each SV frame contains:
- 1 ASDU with up to 8 channels (e.g., 3-phase currents + neutral current = 4 channels for a 3I + IN merging unit)
- Frame size $\approx 130$ bytes per ASDU

### 3.2.3 Step 3: Bandwidth per Merging Unit

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{MU} &= f_s \times L_{frame} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} \\
B_{MU} &= 4000 \times 130 \times 8 \\
B_{MU} &= 4.16 \text{ Mbit/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4: Total Process Bus Bandwidth

For a substation with 6 Merging Units (one per bay):

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{total} &= 6 \times 4.16 \text{ Mbit/s} \\
B_{total} &= 24.96 \text{ Mbit/s} \\
B_{total} &< 100 \text{ Mbit/s (Fast Ethernet budget)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This validates the design choice of **100 Mbit/s Ethernet** as the IEC 61850 standard physical layer.

## 3.3 Analytical Step-by-Step: Comparison of Relay Generations

| Parameter | Electromagnetic | Static (Analog) | Numerical (Digital) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Principle | Solenoid, induction disc | Comparator, op-amp | Microprocessor + ADC |
| Speed | 20–50 ms | 10–30 ms | 5–20 ms |
| Accuracy | $\pm 5\%$ | $\pm 2.5\%$ | $\pm 0.5\%$ |
| Settings Adjustment | Taps/switches (manual) | Potentiometers | Software (HMI) |
| Self-Test | None | Limited | **Full (watchdog, CT/VT supervision)** |
| Event Recording | None | None | **COMTRADE files (8+ channels)** |
| Communication | Hardwired contacts | Hardwired | **IEC 61850 GOOSE, SV, MMS** |
| MTBF | ~15 years | ~25 years | **>50 years (with periodic self-test)** |
| Multi-Function | No | Limited | **Yes (PDIS + PIOC + PDIF in one IED)** |
| CT/VT Burden | High | Medium | **Low (electronic burden < 0.1 VA)** |

## 3.4 Numerical Problem (KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Question)

**Problem:** A numerical distance relay uses an 80-sample-per-cycle ADC at 50 Hz. Compute:
1. The sampling frequency
2. The Nyquist frequency
3. The maximum detectable frequency (15th harmonic)
4. Verify whether the Nyquist criterion is satisfied for harmonic detection up to the 15th harmonic

### 3.4.1 Solution

**Given:**
- $N = 80$ samples/cycle
- $f_{sys} = 50$ Hz
- Harmonic order of interest: $h = 15$

**Step 1 — Sampling Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &= N \times f_{sys} \\
f_s &= 80 \times 50 \\
f_s &= 4000 \text{ Hz} \\
f_s &= 4 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Nyquist Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{N} &= \frac{f_s}{2} \\
f_{N} &= \frac{4000}{2} \\
f_{N} &= 2000 \text{ Hz} \\
f_{N} &= 2 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Detectable Frequency (15th Harmonic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= h \times f_{sys} \\
f_{max} &= 15 \times 50 \\
f_{max} &= 750 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Nyquist Criterion:**

For the relay to correctly detect the 15th harmonic:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &\geq 2 \times f_{max} \\
4000 &\geq 2 \times 750 \\
4000 &\geq 1500 \, \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $4000 \text{ Hz} \gg 1500 \text{ Hz}$, the **Nyquist criterion is comfortably satisfied**. The relay can detect harmonics up to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{max} &= \frac{f_s}{2 \cdot f_{sys}} \\
h_{max} &= \frac{4000}{2 \times 50} \\
h_{max} &= 40
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the relay can detect up to the **40th harmonic** (2000 Hz) without aliasing.

## 4.2 GOOSE Message Timing Diagram (Exponential Backoff)

This diagram shows the **exponential retransmission schedule** of a GOOSE message after a status change, which is a hallmark of IEC 61850's reliability mechanism.

```mermaid
gantt
    title GOOSE Message Retransmission Schedule After Status Change
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S ms

    section First Transmission
    GOOSE T0 t0              :active, g0, 0, 1
    GOOSE T1 short backoff   :active, g1, 1, 3
    section Retransmissions
    GOOSE T2 backoff 4 ms    :active, g2, 3, 7
    GOOSE T3 backoff 8 ms    :active, g3, 7, 15
    GOOSE T4 backoff 16 ms   :active, g4, 15, 31
    GOOSE T5 backoff 32 ms   :active, g5, 31, 63
    GOOSE Tn backoff grows   :active, gn, 63, 500
    section Max Interval
    Max retransmission 5 s   :active, gmax, 500, 5000
```

**Interpretation:**
- After a **status change** (e.g., trip command), the IED sends GOOSE **immediately (T0)**.
- If no acknowledgment, it retransmits after **short delay (T1)**, then doubles (T2, T3, …).
- The backoff formula is $T_n = 2^{(n-1)} \cdot t_0$ where $t_0$ is the nominal cycle time (typically 4 ms or 8 ms).
- After reaching **max interval (5 s)**, it keeps retransmitting periodically so that newly joined subscribers get the current state.

```mermaid

    end

        C1[CT with Merging Unit] -->|SV over Ethernet| S1[Network Switch]
        D1[VT with Merging Unit] -->|SV over Ethernet| S1
        S1 -->|SV Stream| R3[Numerical Relay 1]
        S1 -->|SV Stream| R4[Numerical Relay 2]
        R3 <-->|GOOSE over Ethernet| R4
```
