
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from the Diagram:**
> In a **conventional substation**, each IED has dedicated copper wiring to its CTs, VTs, and breakers. In a **digital (IEC 61850) substation**, a single Ethernet network replaces hundreds of copper cables. The Merging Unit (MU) digitizes the CT/VT outputs, and all IEDs share these samples via SV. Trip commands use GOOSE. This reduces **wiring cost by 60–80%** in large substations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks)

**Q: List any three advantages of numerical relays over static relays.**

**Model Answer:**

Numerical relays offer several advantages over static (analog electronic) relays:

1. **Multi-function integration:** A single numerical relay implements multiple protection functions (e.g., distance + overcurrent + breaker failure) via software algorithms, whereas static relays typically implement one function per hardware module.

2. **Self-test and self-diagnosis:** Numerical relays continuously run watchdog checks, ADC calibration, and CT/VT supervision to detect internal failures. Static relays have **no self-test capability** and require periodic manual testing.

3. **Event recording (COMTRADE):** Numerical relays store **disturbance recordings** (IEEE C37.111 COMTRADE format: `.cfg` + `.dat` files) capturing pre-fault and post-fault waveforms for post-incident analysis. Static relays cannot record events.

4. **Adaptive characteristics:** Numerical relay settings can be **changed in real time** (e.g., zone modification during power swing, load encroachment blinder adjustment). Static relay characteristics are fixed by hardware components.

5. **Remote communication:** Numerical relays natively support **IEC 61850, DNP 3.0, Modbus** for remote configuration, fault record retrieval, and SCADA integration. Static relays require additional RTUs for communication.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (3 Marks)

**Q: What is IEC 61850? Briefly explain the role of GOOSE messages in IEC 61850.**

**Model Answer:**

**IEC 61850** is the international standard for **"Communication Networks and Systems for Power Utility Automation"**, published by the International Electrotechnical Commission. It defines a unified, object-oriented, Ethernet-based communication architecture for substations, enabling **interoperability** between IEDs from different manufacturers.

**GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event)** is a **fast, peer-to-peer, multicast messaging mechanism** defined in IEC 61850-8-1. It runs directly on **Layer 2 Ethernet** (EtherType 0x88B8), bypassing the TCP/IP stack to achieve **latency of less than 4 ms** (Type 1A performance class).

**Roles of GOOSE messages:**
- **Inter-tripping** between bays (e.g., trip Bus Bar differential relay → trip all incoming breakers).
- **Breaker failure initiation** (BFI signal from main protection to back-up protection).
- **Interlocking** signals between switchgear (e.g., prevent closing disconnector when breaker is closed).
- **Status reporting** of circuit breakers, isolators, and alarms (with high-speed multicast).

GOOSE uses an **exponential backoff retransmission scheme** ($T_n = 2^{(n-1)} \cdot t_0$) to ensure reliability — after a status change, messages are retransmitted rapidly initially, then less frequently, until a **maximum interval (5 s)** is reached.

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. The KTU ESE style mandates that the student answers **one full question** (either Q1 OR Q2 from the module). We provide two completely independent alternatives below.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of IEC 61850 with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of Station, Bay, and Process levels.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With a suitable diagram, explain the GOOSE message structure and its transmission mechanism. Discuss why GOOSE achieves a latency of less than 4 ms.

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — IEC 61850 Architecture (7 Marks)

The **IEC 61850 architecture** is organized into **three functional levels** within a substation:

**1. Station Level (Top Level):**
- Contains the **SCADA workstation, Engineering HMI, station gateway, and time-sync server (GPS)**.
- Communication with the bay level uses **MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification)** over TCP/IP, operating on a **client-server model**.
- Typical latency: **500–1000 ms** (non-critical, e.g., for control, reporting, settings).
- Function: Centralized monitoring, control, archiving, alarm management.

**2. Bay Level (Middle Level):**
- Contains the **numerical protection relays, bay controllers, metering IEDs, and phasor measurement units (PMUs)**.
- This is where the **actual protection algorithms** (PDIS, PIOC, PDIF) execute.
- Communication with station level: **MMS** (client-server).
- Communication with process level: **GOOSE + Sampled Values (SV)**.
- Bay-to-bay communication: **GOOSE (peer-to-peer multicast)**.

**3. Process Level (Bottom Level):**
- Contains the **primary equipment interface IEDs**: Merging Units (MUs), Breaker IEDs, Sensor IEDs.
- **Merging Units** digitize the outputs of **CTs, VTs, optical sensors, Rogowski coils**.
- They stream these digitized samples as **Sampled Values (SV)** over Ethernet to bay-level IEDs.
- They receive **GOOSE trip commands** from bay-level IEDs to operate breakers.

**Communication Protocols in Each Level:**

| Level | Protocol | Latency |
|---|---|---|
| Station ↔ Bay | MMS (ISO 9506) | 500–1000 ms |
| Bay ↔ Bay | GOOSE (IEC 61850-8-1) | < 4 ms |
| Bay ↔ Process | GOOSE + SV (IEC 61850-9-2) | < 3 ms |

**Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1 for the full visual representation.**

#### Part (b) — GOOSE Message Structure and Transmission (7 Marks)

**GOOSE Frame Structure (Layer 2 Ethernet, EtherType 0x88B8):**

The GOOSE message is encapsulated directly in an **IEEE 802.1Q Ethernet frame**, bypassing the IP stack. The structure is:

```
[Destination MAC: 01-0C-CD-01-xx-xx] [Source MAC] [VLAN Tag] [EtherType: 0x88B8] [GOOSE PDU] [FCS]
```

The **GOOSE PDU** contains the following **ASN.1/BER-encoded** fields:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `gocbRef` | Reference to GOOSE Control Block (e.g., `IED1/PROT/LLN0$GO$PDIS1GO` ) |
| `datSet` | Dataset being transmitted (e.g., `PDIS1$Str`) |
| `goID` | GOOSE ID (unique identifier) |
| `t` | Timestamp (UTC) |
| `stNum` | State Number (increments on every status change) |
| `sqNum` | Sequence Number (increments per retransmission) |
| `numDatSetEntries` | Number of data items in the dataset |
| `allData` | Actual data (Boolean, integer, quality, timestamp) |

**Why GOOSE Achieves < 4 ms Latency:**

1. **Layer 2 Bypass:** GOOSE runs directly on Ethernet (no IP, no TCP retransmission delays). It uses multicast MAC addresses (`01-0C-CD-01-xx-xx`).

2. **High Priority via VLAN Tagging:** GOOSE frames are tagged with **IEEE 802.1Q VLAN priority bits** (typically priority 4 — "expedited forwarding"). Network switches use **Quality of Service (QoS)** to forward GOOSE frames ahead of lower-priority traffic.

3. **Cut-Through Switching:** IEC 61850 networks use **cut-through switches** (instead of store-and-forward), which begin forwarding a frame as soon as the destination MAC is read, reducing switch delay to **5–10 µs** per switch.

4. **Multicast Hardware Filtering:** Modern Ethernet switches use **ASIC-based multicast filtering**, so GOOSE is delivered to subscribers without software processing.

5. **Bandwidth Headroom:** 100 Mbit/s Ethernet provides 100× the bandwidth needed for a 150-byte GOOSE frame, ensuring minimal queuing delay.

**Transmission Mechanism (Exponential Backoff):**

After a status change, the IED sends the GOOSE message **immediately (T0)**, then retransmits per the schedule:

$$
T_n = 2^{(n-1)} \cdot t_0
$$

For a nominal $t_0 = 4$ ms, the retransmission times are: $T_0 = 0$, $T_1 = 4$ ms, $T_2 = 8$ ms, $T_3 = 16$ ms, $T_4 = 32$ ms, ... up to a **maximum interval of 5 s**.

This ensures that **even if one message is lost (collision, EMI burst), the next retransmission arrives in milliseconds**, providing GOOSE with its celebrated **Type 1A (< 4 ms)** reliability class.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the advantages of numerical relays over electromagnetic and static relays. Compare their operating times, accuracy, and communication capabilities.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the IEC 61850 data model hierarchy with an example. List the important logical node groups (P, C, M, A, X, T, Z prefixes) and their functions.

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Advantages of Numerical Relays (7 Marks)

Numerical relays are the **third generation** of protective relays, succeeding **electromechanical** (first generation) and **static/analog** (second generation) relays. The advantages are summarized in the comparative table below.

**Comparison Table:**

| S. No. | Parameter | Electromagnetic | Static (Analog) | **Numerical (Digital)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Operating Time** | 20–50 ms | 10–30 ms | **5–20 ms (half/one-cycle algorithms)** |
| 2 | **Accuracy** | $\pm 5\%$ to $\pm 10\%$ | $\pm 2.5\%$ to $\pm 5\%$ | **$\pm 0.5\%$ (digital precision)** |
| 3 | **Settings Adjustment** | Manual taps & dials | Potentiometers | **Software HMI / Engineering tool** |
| 4 | **Self-Test & Diagnostics** | Not available | Limited | **Full (watchdog, ADC, memory checks)** |
| 5 | **Event Recording** | None | None | **COMTRADE (.cfg + .dat) waveform capture** |
| 6 | **Communication** | Hardwired contacts only | Hardwired + RTU | **IEC 61850 GOOSE/SV/MMS, DNP 3.0, Modbus** |
| 7 | **Multi-Function** | One function per relay | Limited (1–2 functions) | **Multiple functions (PDIS+PIOC+PDIF) in one IED** |
| 8 | **CT/VT Burden** | High (5–10 VA) | Medium (1–3 VA) | **Low (< 0.1 VA, electronic burden)** |
| 9 | **Adaptive Characteristics** | Not possible | Not possible | **Possible (load encroachment, power swing detection)** |
| 10 | **Power Quality Metering** | None | Limited | **Built-in harmonics, flicker, transient recorders** |
| 11 | **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | ~15 years | ~25 years | **> 50 years (with self-test)** |
| 12 | **Cost (per function)** | High | Medium | **Low (multi-function integration)** |

**Key Advantages Explained (Verbatim for KTU Examiner):**

1. **Reduced panel size and wiring:** One numerical relay replaces 4–5 electromechanical relays, reducing substation footprint and copper cable cost.

2. **Faster and more accurate:** Digital signal processing (Fourier, wavelet, traveling wave algorithms) provides higher accuracy and faster operating time than electromechanical induction discs.

3. **Wide operating range:** Software gain adjustment handles currents from 0.1 A to tens of kA in the same IED.

4. **Built-in event and disturbance recording:** COMTRADE files enable post-fault analysis (impedance locus, fault location, breaker performance).

5. **Adaptive protection:** Settings can be changed dynamically based on system state (e.g., changing zone-3 reach during heavy load).

6. **Integrated metering:** Same IED measures V, I, f, P, Q, harmonics, flicker — eliminating separate meters.

7. **Remote engineering:** Settings, fault records, and firmware updates can be accessed remotely via IEC 61850, reducing site visits and improving workforce efficiency.

#### Part (b) — IEC 61850 Data Model Hierarchy (7 Marks)

IEC 61850 uses a **strict object-oriented hierarchy** to organize all substation data. This standardized naming allows any client (e.g., SCADA) to access any data point (e.g., a circuit breaker position) using a uniform path, regardless of vendor.

**The Hierarchy (Top to Bottom):**

$$
\text{Physical Device (IED)} \rightarrow \text{Logical Device (LD)} \rightarrow \text{Logical Node (LN)} \rightarrow \text{Data Object (DO)} \rightarrow \text{Data Attribute (DA)}
$$

**Example (Numerical Distance Relay IED — `IED1`):**

| Hierarchy Level | Example | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Device** | `IED1` | The numerical relay hardware itself |
| **Logical Device** | `PROT` (Protection LD) | A functional grouping within the IED |
| **Logical Node** | `PDIS1` | Distance Protection Function (Zone 1) |
| **Data Object** | `Str` (Start) | "Pickup/start" data of the distance function |
| **Data Attribute** | `general; d` | "Directional" boolean attribute |
| **Data Attribute** | `general; t` | Timestamp of the start event (UTC) |

**Full data reference (KTU High-Yield):**

```
IED1 / PROT / PDIS1 . Str . general . d
```

**Important Logical Node Groups (Prefixes) — Memorize for KTU Exam:**

| Prefix | Group | Examples | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | Protection | **PDIS** (Distance), **PIOC** (Inst. Overcurrent), **PDIF** (Differential), **PTOC** (Time Overcurrent), **PVOC** (Voltage Controlled), **PHIZ** (High Impedance) | Execute protection algorithms |
| **C** | Control | **CILO** (Interlocking), **CSWI** (Switch Controller), **CALH** (Alarm Handling) | Supervise and execute switching |
| **M** | Metering & Measurement | **MMXU** (3-phase meas.), **MHAI** (Harmonics), **MSQI** (Sequence), **MFLK** (Flicker) | Calculate and publish measurements |
| **A** | Automatic Control | **AVCO** (Voltage Control), **ATCC** (Auto Tap Changer), **ANCR** (Neutral Current Regulator) | Closed-loop regulation |
| **S** | Sensors & Supervision | **SVBR** (Vibration), **SCBR** (Circuit Breaker Supervision), **SPDC** (Partial Discharge) | Monitor primary equipment |
| **T** | Instrument Transformers | **TCTR** (Current Transformer), **TVTR** (Voltage Transformer) | Interface with CTs/VTs |
| **X** | Switchgear | **XCBR** (Circuit Breaker), **XSWI** (Switch/Disconnector) | Operate primary switching devices |
| **I** | Interface/Archives | **IHMI** (Human-Machine Interface), **ITCI** (Telecontrol Interface), **IARC** (Archiving) | Interface to operator/SCADA |
| **L** | System Logical Nodes | **LPHD** (Physical Device), **LLN0** (Logical Node Zero) | Mandatory system nodes |
| **Z** | Power System Equipment | **ZBAT** (Battery), **ZGEN** (Generator), **ZLIN** (Power Line), **ZTRN** (Power Transformer) | Describe primary equipment |
| **D** | Distributed Energy | **DGEN** (DER Generator), **DPVM** (DER PV Module) | For DER/solar/wind |
| **H** | Hydro | **HBRG** (Hydro Governor), **HTUR** (Hydro Turbine) | For hydropower plants |
| **F** | Frequency | **FSEQ** (Frequency Supervision), **FSPM** (Frequency Setpoint) | Frequency-related functions |
| **K** | Mechanical | **KPMP** (Pump), **KVLV** (Valve) | Mechanical actuators |
| **Q** | Quality of Power Supply | **QVVR** (Voltage Variation), **QVTR** (Voltage Transient), **QFLW** (Power Flow) | Power quality analysis |
| **R** | Protection Related | **RDRE** (Disturbance Recording), **RBRF** (Breaker Failure), **RPSB** (Power Swing Blocking) | Protection auxiliaries |
| **W** | Wind | **WALG** (Wind Alarm), **WTUR** (Wind Turbine) | For wind farms |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing GOOSE with MMS:** Many students interchange them. **MMS = slow, client-server, TCP/IP, for SCADA telemetry (500 ms class).** **GOOSE = fast, peer-to-peer multicast, Layer 2, for trip/interlock (< 4 ms class).** Writing "GOOSE is used for SCADA reporting" will cost full marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing "Latency" with "Bandwidth":** GOOSE achieves < 4 ms **latency** (delay), not bandwidth. A 4 ms latency does **not** mean the data is small; it means the delay is small.
>
> 3. **Saying IEC 61850 uses IP for GOOSE:** WRONG. GOOSE bypasses IP/TCP/UDP and runs on Layer 2. Saying "GOOSE uses TCP/IP" will lose marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Logical Node prefixes:** KTU questions often ask for LN prefixes. Missing the **P, C, M, X, T, Z** categories will lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Mixing "Merging Unit" location:** The MU belongs to the **Process Level**, not the Bay Level. Students frequently misplace it.
>
> 6. **Writing "IEC 61850 replaces CTs/VTs":** Wrong. IEC 61850 **digitizes** the outputs of CTs/VTs via MUs. The CTs/VTs are still needed; only their cabling changes.
>
> 7. **Numerical Relay ≠ Digital Relay:** Strictly, a **digital relay** is the second generation (uses digital logic ICs but operates on analog signals). A **numerical relay** is the third generation (uses microprocessors + ADC on digitized signals). Examiners mark this distinction.
>
> 8. **Skipping the COMTRADE format:** Always mention the two files `.cfg` (configuration) and `.dat` (data) when discussing event recording in numerical relays.
>
> 9. **Not mentioning PTP/IEEE 1588 for time sync:** IEC 61850-9-3 defines PTP for time synchronization. Omitting it loses marks.
>
> 10. **GOOSE Retransmission formula:** Memorize $T_n = 2^{(n-1)} \cdot t_0$. Forgetting the **exponential backoff** mechanism loses marks on GOOSE questions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Master List — Print and Memorize:**

**NUMERICAL RELAYS — KEY FACTS:**
- ⭐ Numerical relays = **microprocessor + ADC + software algorithms**.
- ⭐ Sample rate: **80 samples/cycle → 4000 Hz** at 50 Hz.
- ⭐ Nyquist max detectable harmonic: **40th harmonic** at 80 spc.
- ⭐ Multi-function integration (PDIS + PIOC + PDIF in one IED).
- ⭐ **COMTRADE** format = `.cfg` (config) + `.dat` (data) per **IEEE C37.111**.
- ⭐ Self-test, adaptive characteristics, remote configuration.
- ⭐ Operating time: **5–20 ms** (algorithm-dependent).
- ⭐ Accuracy: **± 0.5%**.

**IEC 61850 — KEY FACTS:**
- ⭐ Standard ID: **IEC 61850** (IEC, 2002 onwards, amended 2010/2014/2020).
- ⭐ Three levels: **Station (MMS), Bay (Protection + Control), Process (MU + Breaker IED)**.
- ⭐ Default physical layer: **100 Mbit/s Ethernet** (Full Duplex).
- ⭐ Default cabling: **Optical fiber** for noise immunity.

**MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification):**
- ⭐ **Client-server** model, **TCP/IP**, **ISO 9506**.
- ⭐ Latency: **500–1000 ms** (non-time-critical).
- ⭐ Used for: SCADA telemetry, settings, fault record retrieval, control commands from station HMI.

**GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event):**
- ⭐ **Peer-to-peer multicast**, **Layer 2 Ethernet (EtherType 0x88B8)**.
- ⭐ Latency: **< 4 ms (Type 1A)**, < 10 ms (Type 1B).
- ⭐ Bypasses IP/TCP stack → no retransmission delay from upper layers.
- ⭐ Uses **VLAN priority (802.1Q)** and **cut-through switching** for low latency.
- ⭐ **Exponential backoff** retransmission: $T_n = 2^{(n-1)} \cdot t_0$, max interval **5 s**.
- ⭐ Used for: Inter-trip, breaker failure, interlocking, status reporting.

**SAMPLED VALUES (SV):**
- ⭐ **IEC 61850-9-2** (Light Edition "LE" defines 80 samples/cycle, 4000 frames/s @ 50 Hz).
- ⭐ Multicast Layer 2 frames, similar EtherType.
- ⭐ Bandwidth: **~4.16 Mbit/s per MU** (150-byte frame at 4000 fps).
- ⭐ Replaces dedicated CT/VT copper wiring → **process bus architecture**.
- ⭐ Time sync via **PTP (IEEE 1588v2)** per IEC 61850-9-3, accuracy **± 1 µs**.

**DATA MODEL:**
- ⭐ Hierarchy: **Physical Device → Logical Device → Logical Node → Data Object → Data Attribute**.
- ⭐ Mandatory LNs: **LPHD** (Physical Device) and **LLN0** (Logical Node Zero).
- ⭐ Key prefixes: **P** (Protection), **C** (Control), **M** (Metering), **A** (Automatic), **S** (Sensor), **T** (CT/VT), **X** (Switchgear), **I** (Interface), **L** (System), **Z** (Power System), **R** (Protection Related).
- ⭐ Configuration Language: **SCL (Substation Configuration Language)** — XML-based files with extensions `.icd` (IED Capability Description), `.scd` (Substation Configuration Description), `.iid` (Instantiated IED Description), `.ssd` (System Specification Description), `.sed` (System Exchange Description).

**ADVANTAGES SUMMARY (10-point):**
1. Multi-function  2. Self-test  3. Adaptive  4. High accuracy  5. Event recording  6. Remote configuration  7. Metering integration  8. Reduced wiring  9. Fast operation  10. Interoperable via IEC 61850.

**REAL-WORLD DEPLOYMENTS:**
- 400/220/110 kV substations (PGCIL, state transcos).
- HVDC stations.
- Smart grid/DER integration.
- Industrial process plants.

**MUST-REMEMBER FORMULAS:**
- Sampling: $f_s = N \cdot f_{sys}$
- Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$
- GOOSE backoff: $T_n = 2^{(n-1)} \cdot t_0$
- Max harmonic: $h_{max} = f_s / (2 \cdot f_{sys})$
- SV bandwidth: $B_{MU} = f_s \cdot L_{frame} \cdot 8$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Handling with Substation Automation System (SAS) Signal Conditioning Subsystems: Surge Protection Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Information Handling with Substation Automation System (SAS) Signal Conditioning Subsystems: Surge Protection Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Substation Automation System (SAS) — Recap

A **Substation Automation System (SAS)** is an integrated, microprocessor-based architecture that performs real-time **monitoring, control, protection, communication, and data acquisition** of substation primary equipment (CBs, isolators, transformers, instrument transformers) through Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs) connected over a station bus (IEC 61850 compliant LAN).

The information flow in SAS can be represented as:

$$\text{Field Sensors} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Signal Conditioning} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Protection \& Control IEDs} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Station Bus} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SCADA/HMI}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Signal Conditioning Subsystem** is the *first electronic interface* between the harsh substation electromagnetic environment and the sensitive IED electronics. It must extract, isolate, scale, filter, and **protect** the low-level signal before it enters the relay's analog/digital front end.

## 1.2 Signal Conditioning Subsystem — Functional Blocks

A typical signal conditioning subsystem in an SAS front-end (e.g., inside a bay-control IED or a dedicated transducer) consists of:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Surge Protection Circuit** | Diverts/transient-suppresses over-voltages from CT/PT leads, binary inputs, and communication ports |
| Isolation Transformer / Opto-coupler | Provides galvanic isolation between field and IED |
| Analog Anti-aliasing Filter | Band-limits input to ADC Nyquist frequency |
| Range / Scaling Amplifier | Adjusts signal level to ADC input span |
| Common-mode / Differential-mode Rejection | Suppresses EMI picked up in long substation cables |

## 1.3 Surge Protection Circuit — Formal Definition

A **Surge Protection Circuit (SPC)** is a *deliberately engineered combination of non-linear voltage-limiting and current-diverting components* placed at the boundary between the external substation cabling and the sensitive IED electronics, whose purpose is to **limit the let-through voltage and energy** of any transient over-voltage (lightning, switching, ESD) to a level below the withstand capability (typically **1.5 kV to 6 kV** depending on the standard applied) of the downstream electronics, while not interfering with normal operating signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Surge** = a short-duration, sub-microsecond to millisecond, high-amplitude (kV/kA) transient over-voltage or over-current event superimposed on the normal power-system waveform.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at the Club Door"

Imagine the IED is a VIP inside a club. Field cables from the CT/PT are a long, narrow entrance corridor where trouble-makers (surges, spikes, lightning transients) try to enter. The **Surge Protection Circuit is the bouncer**:

- The **GDT (Gas Discharge Tube)** at the door is a *huge bouncer* — he shoves the worst troublemakers violently out (crowbar short to ground for high currents).
- The **MOV (Metal Oxide Varistor)** is the *secondary bouncer* — he clamps anything the first one missed.
- The **TVS (Transient Voltage Suppressor) diode** is the *final security check* — a fast, precise referee who stops the last whisper of trouble before it reaches the VIP.

Together, the three form a **layered defense** — just like a substation uses zone-based protection.

## 1.5 Standardized Withstand & Test Levels (IEEE/IEC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IED must be tested to the following standardized surge immunity levels. These are the **boundary conditions** that the surge protection circuit must help the equipment meet.

| Standard | Test Type | Severity Level |
|---|---|---|
| **IEC 61000-4-5** | Combination wave (1.2/50 µs – 8/20 µs) | Class 1–4: **0.25 kV to 4 kV** |
| **IEEE C62.41.2** | Location Category A/B/C | **6 kV / 3 kA** at Category B |
| **IEEE C62.45** | Test methods for SPDs on LV systems | — |
| **IEC 61643-21** | SPDs for telecom/signalling | D1/C2/C1 classes |
| **IEC 61000-4-4** | Electrical Fast Transient (EFT/Burst) | **0.25 kV to 4 kV** |

The most quoted transient waveform is the **Combination Wave** with open-circuit voltage waveform **1.2/50 µs** (rise 1.2 µs, decay to half-value at 50 µs) and short-circuit current waveform **8/20 µs** — used in nearly all surge-immunity and SPD coordination studies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Surges in a Substation

Surges enter the signal conditioning chain from three principal origins:

### A. Lightning — Direct & Induced
- **Direct strike** to a tower, shield wire, or gantry injects currents up to **200 kA** (8/20 µs).
- **Induced coupling** on long signal cables can produce **6–10 kV** transients even for strikes several hundred metres away.
- The IEEE empirical coupling formula for an overhead line of height $h$ and stroke distance $d$ gives the induced voltage on a parallel signal conductor:

$$V_{ind} \approx 30 \cdot k \cdot \frac{I_{peak} \cdot h}{d}$$

where $k \approx 1$ for typical soil conditions, $I_{peak}$ in kA, $h$ in metres.

### B. Switching Transients
- Energising/de-energising of capacitor banks, cable circuits, or transformers produces **damped oscillatory transients** with frequencies from 100 kHz to several MHz.
- The peak over-voltage factor for a capacitor switching is bounded by:

$$\text{K}_{over} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{X_s}{X_c}}$$

where $X_s$ is the system short-circuit reactance and $X_c$ is the capacitor bank reactance at fundamental frequency.

### C. Electrostatic Discharge (ESD) & Ground Potential Rise (GPR)
- GPR during a line-to-ground fault can lift the local substation ground by **several kV** relative to the remote-end ground, creating dangerous common-mode stress on long signal cables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The signal conditioning subsystem must handle **all three mechanisms** because the IED sits at the intersection of the power system and the low-voltage electronic world.

## 2.2 The Three Threat Modes — Common vs Differential

A surge can stress the IED in two distinct electrical configurations:

1. **Common-Mode Surge** — appears *between each signal conductor and the local ground*. The two signal wires are at the same elevated potential. This is the dominant mode in substation environments and is the most damaging to electronics.
2. **Differential-Mode Surge** — appears *between the two signal conductors* (e.g., tip and ring of a 4–20 mA loop). Less common but couples directly into the input amplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> A well-designed surge protection circuit provides **both** common-mode and differential-mode protection, usually with a three-terminal device (line–line–ground) per signal pair.

## 2.3 The Three Non-Linear Devices Used

### 2.3.1 Gas Discharge Tube (GDT)
- A sealed ceramic envelope filled with inert gas (Ar, Ne) with two or three electrodes.
- **Breakover voltage** $V_{dc}$ (typically **75 V – 600 V**, with 90 V and 230 V being common).
- Once ionised, the arc voltage drops to ~**20–30 V** and the device behaves like a near-short to ground, diverting kiloamperes of surge current.
- **Limitation**: relatively slow — DC sparkover takes **>100 ns**; impulse sparkover is in the **0.5–1 µs** range. A "follower current" of mains voltage can keep the GDT in conduction if not properly coordinated.

### 2.3.2 Metal Oxide Varistor (MOV)
- Sintered body of **zinc-oxide (ZnO) grains** with inter-granular bismuth/cobalt oxide boundaries forming micro-p-n junctions.
- Highly non-linear V-I characteristic described by:

$$I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$$

with $\alpha$ typically **25–50** for high-quality ZnO varistors. This means that for every doubling of voltage, the current increases by a factor of $2^{\alpha} \approx 3.3 \times 10^{7}$ — a *super-clamping* behaviour.

- Clamping voltage $V_c$ is conventionally taken at **1 mA** DC test current; for example, a 275 V rated MOV clamps at ~**430 V** at 1 mA, ~**710 V** at 100 A, and ~**900 V** at 1 kA.
- Energy absorption per event:

$$E = V_c(I) \cdot I(t) \cdot \Delta t \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

- Maximum single-pulse energy ranges from a few **Joules** (small SMD MOVs) to **hundreds of Joules** (large disk MOVs for AC mains).

### 2.3.3 Transient Voltage Suppressor (TVS) Diode
- A specially processed **avalanche-mode Zener** with sub-nanosecond response time (< **1 ps** for some silicon types).
- Operates in reverse breakdown with a sharp knee. Clamping voltage is rated for a specified peak pulse current (e.g., 400 W, 600 W, 1500 W, 5 kW peak pulse power for 10/1000 µs waveform).
- Used as the **final, fastest** protection stage directly at the IED input pin.

## 2.4 Three-Stage (Cascaded) Protection Topology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**: A surge protection circuit in a substation signal conditioner is almost never a *single device*. It is a **deliberately coordinated cascade of three stages**, each handling a different portion of the surge energy and imposing a different clamping voltage.

| Stage | Device | Function | Speed | Energy Handling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage 1 — Divert** | GDT (3-terminal) | Diverts bulk of high-energy, high-current portion to ground | Slow (µs) | **High** (kA) |
| **Stage 2 — Clamp** | MOV | Clamps the residual let-through voltage | Medium (ns) | **Medium** (J–kJ) |
| **Stage 3 — Refine** | TVS diode | Snubs the fast-edge residual to within IED rating | **Fast** (ps) | **Low** (W) |

Between the stages, **series impedances** (typically wire inductances, PTC thermistors, or small resistors) are introduced to *desynchronise* the operation and to absorb the energy difference between the slower and faster stages.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{ind} = 30 \cdot k \cdot \dfrac{I_{peak} \cdot h}{d}$ | Induced lightning voltage on parallel conductor | kV |
| 2 | $I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$, $\alpha \approx 25\text{–}50$ | MOV non-linear V-I law | — |
| 3 | $E = \int V_c(t) \cdot I(t)\,dt$ | Energy absorbed by MOV per pulse | Joules |
| 4 | $\text{K}_{over} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + X_s / X_c}$ | Capacitor-switching over-voltage factor | p.u. |
| 5 | Combination wave: 1.2/50 µs (Voc), 8/20 µs (Isc) | Standard surge waveform for testing | — |
| 6 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Characteristic impedance of cable (for reflection studies) | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC time constant of TVS-decoupling network | seconds |
| 8 | Let-through voltage $V_{LT} = V_{clamp} + L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Inductive overshoot at output of stage 1 | V |
| 9 | Coordination rule: $V_{clamp,Stage 1} > V_{clamp,Stage 2} > V_{clamp,Stage 3}$ | Coordination requirement for cascaded SPDs | V |
| 10 | IEC 61000-4-5: $V_{oc} / I_{sc}$ ratio = **2 $\Omega$** | Standard test-source impedance | $\Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility**: This exact cascade is what you see on the **4–20 mA input card** of a numerical relay, on the **binary input** opto-coupler, and on the **RS-485 / fibre-of-copper port** of a bay-control IED. The same design methodology is used by ABB, Siemens, SEL, and GE in their IED front ends.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Absorbed by an MOV for a Standard 8/20 µs Pulse

The standard 8/20 µs current waveform used in SPD testing has the analytical form:

$$i(t) = I_{peak} \cdot \left( \frac{t}{\tau_1} \right)^2 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_2}, \quad \text{for } t \ge 0$$

with fitted constants $\tau_1 \approx 3.91 \,\mu s$ and $\tau_2 \approx 4.69 \,\mu s$ such that the peak occurs at $t = 2\tau_1$ and the half-value occurs at $t = 20\,\mu s$ after the start of the wave.

We want the **energy deposited in the MOV** when the clamping voltage $V_c$ is taken as approximately constant (a valid assumption for small over-voltages where MOV current is in the kA range).

**Step 1 — State the energy integral.**

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} v(t) \cdot i(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Assume clamping voltage is constant (worst-case clamped-mode operation).**

$$v(t) \approx V_c$$

Therefore:

$$E = V_c \int_{0}^{\infty} i(t)\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the integral as the "charge transferred" $Q$.**

$$Q = \int_{0}^{\infty} i(t)\, dt \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E = V_c \cdot Q$$

**Step 4 — Compute $Q$ for the 8/20 µs waveform.**

For an idealised triangular approximation (commonly used by manufacturers), the area under the current curve equals:

$$Q \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot t_{base}$$

With $t_{base} \approx 20\,\mu s$ (full base of the standard wave):

$$Q \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot 20 \times 10^{-6} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \cdot I_{peak}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical example.** For $I_{peak} = 3\,\text{kA}$ and $V_c = 700\,\text{V}$:

$$Q = 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 10^{3} = 30 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{C} = 30\,\text{mC}$$

$$E = 700 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} = 21\,\text{J}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single **3 kA, 8/20 µs** pulse therefore deposits ~**21 J** into the MOV. The MOV must be rated for at least this single-pulse energy with a derating margin (typically 30–50 %) to ensure long-term reliability.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Let-Through Voltage Considering Inductive Overshoot

When the GDT (Stage 1) fires and suddenly diverts current to ground, the *residual* voltage at the next stage includes an inductive overshoot from the series wiring/inductor between stages.

**Step 1 — Write Kirchhoff's Voltage Law across the series inductance $L_s$ between stages.**

$$v_{out}(t) = v_{clamp}(t) - L_s \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — For an 8/20 µs current with peak di/dt at $t \approx \tau_1$:**

$$\left. \frac{di}{dt} \right|_{max} = \frac{I_{peak}}{\tau_1} \cdot e^{-1} \cdot 2 \approx \frac{0.736 \cdot I_{peak}}{\tau_1}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $I_{peak} = 3\,\text{kA}$, $\tau_1 = 3.91\,\mu s$, and $L_s = 1\,\mu H$ (typical for ~30 cm of wire between GDT and MOV):**

$$\left. \frac{di}{dt} \right|_{max} \approx \frac{0.736 \times 3000}{3.91 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 5.65 \times 10^{8}\,\text{A/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute inductive overshoot:**

$$V_L = L_s \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.65 \times 10^{8} = 565\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Total let-through voltage at the MOV input:**

$$V_{LT} = V_{clamp,Stage\,1} + V_L \approx 30 + 565 \approx 595\,\text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *peak let-through voltage* at the MOV terminals. The MOV (Stage 2) must therefore be selected with a **continuous AC rating ≥ 275 V** (so it does not conduct at normal mains) but with a **clamping voltage ≤ 600 V** at the expected surge current, so the TVS (Stage 3) sees a voltage well below the IED's **1 kV** withstand.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coordination of Clamping Voltages

A clean derivation of the **coordination rule**:

**Step 1 — Define the let-through voltage at the output of stage $n$ as:**

$$V_{LT,n} = V_{clamp,n} + L_{s,n} \cdot \frac{di_n}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — The next stage fires only when its own breakdown/clamping threshold is reached. Therefore, the next stage remains *invisible* to the upstream stage as long as:**

$$V_{LT,n} < V_{clamp,n+1}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting repeatedly for a 3-stage cascade:**

$$V_{LT,1} < V_{clamp,2} \quad\text{and}\quad V_{LT,2} < V_{clamp,3}$$

**Step 4 — Expanding using the inductive-overshoot contribution at each interface:**

$$V_{clamp,1} + L_{s,1} \frac{di_1}{dt} < V_{clamp,2}$$

$$V_{clamp,2} + L_{s,2} \frac{di_2}{dt} < V_{clamp,3}$$

**Step 5 — Rearranged design inequality for the engineer:**

$$\boxed{V_{clamp,1} + L_{s,1}\,\frac{di_1}{dt} \;<\; V_{clamp,2} + L_{s,2}\,\frac{di_2}{dt} \;<\; V_{clamp,3} \;<\; V_{withstand,IED}}$$

This is the **canonical coordination inequality** that the surge protection designer must satisfy. It is precisely what the KTU examiner expects to see in a 14-mark problem on cascaded surge protection.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — GDT vs MOV vs TVS

| Property | GDT | MOV | TVS Diode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Response time** | 0.5 – 1 µs | 1 – 50 ns | < 1 ns (sub-ns) |
| **Peak current handling** | 5 – 20 kA (8/20 µs) | 0.5 – 10 kA (8/20 µs) | 0.1 – 1 kA |
| **Energy handling** | Very high (kJ) | High (J – kJ) | Low (mJ – J) |
| **Leakage current** | < 1 pA | µA | nA |
| **Capacitance** | 1 – 5 pF | 100 – 5000 pF | 10 – 1000 pF |
| **Clamping precision** | Poor (arc voltage ~25 V) | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Failure mode** | Short-circuit (safe) | Short-circuit (after ageing) | Short-circuit (safe) |
| **Degradation** | None | Yes (cumulative) | Minimal |
| **Cost (signal-level)** | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Best stage role** | Stage 1 (divert) | Stage 2 (clamp) | Stage 3 (refine) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often confuse *clamping voltage* (instantaneous V across the device during surge) with *varistor voltage* (the 1 mA DC test point). Always quote both: e.g., "an MOV rated **275 Vrms** has a **430 V clamping voltage at 1 mA** and a **710 V clamping voltage at 100 A**."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Three-Stage Surge Protection in an SAS Signal Conditioning Front-End

The following Mermaid diagram maps the cascade of energy-handling stages from the field cable entry to the protected IED input pin. It is the *block-level functional architecture* mandated by the KTU drawing convention for electronic subsystems where a full circuit schematic is unnecessary.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FIELD["FIELD CABLE ENTRY (CT / PT / BINARY INPUT)"]
        A1["SIGNAL PAIR TIP AND RING"]
        A2["SHIELD AND EARTH BUS"]
    end

    subgraph STAGE1["STAGE 1 - GDT DIVERT (HIGH ENERGY, SLOW)"]
        B1["3-TERMINAL GDT 90V OR 230V BREAKOVER"]
        B2["SPARK-TO-GROUND ARC PATH"]
    end

    subgraph IMPL["INTER-STAGE IMPEDANCE (DESYNCHRONISATION)"]
        C1["WIRE INDUCTANCE Ls1 ABOUT 1 MICRO HENRY"]
        C2["PTC THERMISTOR OR 10 OHM RESISTOR"]
    end

    subgraph STAGE2["STAGE 2 - MOV CLAMP (MEDIUM ENERGY, MEDIUM SPEED)"]
        D1["METAL OXIDE VARISTOR 275 VRMS RATED"]
        D2["V-I LAW I EQUALS K TIMES V TO THE ALPHA"]
    end

    subgraph IMPL2["INTER-STAGE IMPEDANCE"]
        E1["SMALL FERRITE BEAD OR 5 OHM RESISTOR"]
    end

    subgraph STAGE3["STAGE 3 - TVS REFINE (LOW ENERGY, SUB-NANOSECOND)"]
        F1["BIDIRECTIONAL TVS DIODE 5V OR 12V OR 24V"]
        F2["CLAMPS FAST EDGE RESIDUE TO IED RATING"]
    end

    subgraph IED["PROTECTED IED INPUT (ANALOG FRONT END)"]
        G1["ISOLATION BARRIER OPTO OR TRANSFORMER"]
        G2["ANTI-ALIASING FILTER"]
        G3["ADC AND PROTECTION RELAY LOGIC"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    B1 -->|crowbar arc| A2
    B1 --> C1 --> C2 --> D1
    D1 -->|clamp to ground| A2
    D1 --> E1 --> F1
    F1 -->|final clamp| A2
    F1 --> G1 --> G2 --> G3

    style FIELD fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a02020,color:#000000
    style STAGE1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b08000,color:#000000
    style IMPL fill:#e2e2f0,stroke:#303080,color:#000000
    style STAGE2 fill:#d8f0d8,stroke:#207020,color:#000000
    style IMPL2 fill:#e2e2f0,stroke:#303080,color:#000000
    style STAGE3 fill:#d8e8f8,stroke:#104060,color:#000000
    style IED fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#202020,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Selecting a Surge Protection Topology

The following flowchart describes the engineer's decision process when designing the surge protection subsystem for a given IED port.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["WHAT TYPE OF PORT IS BEING PROTECTED"]
    A["ANALOG INPUT 4 TO 20 MILLI AMPERE OR CT PT"]
    B["DIGITAL BINARY INPUT 24V OR 110V OR 220V DC"]
    C["COMMUNICATION RS-485 OR ETHERNET COPPER"]

    Q1 --> A
    Q1 --> B
    Q1 --> C

    A --> A1["EXPECTED SURGE 6KV 3KA COMBINATION WAVE"]
    A1 --> A2["USE 3-STAGE CASCADE: GDT THEN MOV THEN TVS"]
    A2 --> A3["VERIFY COORDINATION: VCLAMP 1 LT VCLAMP 2 LT VCLAMP 3 LT VIED WITHSTAND"]

    B --> B1["EXPECTED SURGE 4KV 2KA EFT BURST PLUS SURGE"]
    B1 --> B2["USE GDT PLUS TVS STAGE 2 OFTEN OMITTED BECAUSE INPUT IS DIGITAL"]
    B2 --> B3["ADD RC SNUBBER ACROSS RELAY CONTACT FOR EFDMITFING"]

    C --> C1["EXPECTED SURGE 1KV 0.5KA FAST EDGE"]
    C1 --> C2["PRIMARILY TVS PLUS SMALL GDT; AVOID HIGH CAPACITANCE THAT DEGRADES SIGNAL"]
    C2 --> C3["FOR LONG CABLES USE ISOLATION TRANSFORMER OR FIBRE OPTIC CONVERSION"]

    style Q1 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a02020,color:#000000
    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b08000,color:#000000
    style B fill:#d8f0d8,stroke:#207020,color:#000000
    style C fill:#d8e8f8,stroke:#104060,color:#000000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram**: The "tip and ring" labels refer to the two conductors of a balanced signal pair. The "alpha" in the MOV block denotes the non-linearity exponent of the V-I law. The colour coding follows substation engineering drawing practice: **red = field**, **yellow = primary protection**, **green = secondary protection**, **blue = fine protection**, **grey = protected equipment**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]
**Define a Surge Protection Circuit (SPC) as used in a Substation Automation System signal conditioning subsystem. List the three standard non-linear devices used in such a circuit.**

**Model Answer:**

A Surge Protection Circuit (SPC) is a deliberately engineered combination of non-linear voltage-limiting and current-diverting components placed at the boundary between the external substation cabling and the sensitive IED electronics. Its purpose is to **limit the let-through voltage and energy** of any transient over-voltage (lightning, switching, ESD) to a level below the withstand capability of the downstream IED, without disturbing normal operating signals.

The three standard non-linear devices are:
1. **Gas Discharge Tube (GDT)** — high-energy, slow divertor.
2. **Metal Oxide Varistor (MOV)** — medium-energy, medium-speed clamper.
3. **Transient Voltage Suppressor (TVS) Diode** — low-energy, sub-nanosecond refiner.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for purpose, 1 for listing devices)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]
**State the standard combination wave used in surge immunity testing as per IEC 61000-4-5. What is the standard source impedance of the test generator?**

**Model Answer:**

As per **IEC 61000-4-5**, the standard combination wave has:
- **Open-circuit voltage waveform**: 1.2/50 µs (front time 1.2 µs, time to half-value 50 µs).
- **Short-circuit current waveform**: 8/20 µs (front time 8 µs, time to half-value 20 µs).

The **standard source impedance** of the test generator is **2 Ω** (defined as the ratio of peak open-circuit voltage to peak short-circuit current, e.g., 2 kV / 1 kA).

*(3 marks — 1 for voltage waveform, 1 for current waveform, 1 for source impedance)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Cascaded Surge Protection Design
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat block diagram the **three-stage cascaded surge protection topology** used in the signal conditioning subsystem of a substation automation system. Identify the device used at each stage and justify its selection.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a CT secondary input port of a numerical relay, the following surge threat is specified: peak surge current **$I_{peak} = 5 \text{ kA}$** with the standard 8/20 µs waveform. The inter-stage inductance between Stage 1 (GDT) and Stage 2 (MOV) is **$L_s = 1.2 \,\mu H$**. If the GDT arc voltage is **$V_{arc} = 25 \text{ V}$**, compute:
- (i) The peak inductive overshoot $V_L$.
- (ii) The let-through voltage at the MOV input.
- (iii) The energy deposited in the MOV, taking the clamping voltage as 700 V and using the triangular approximation of the 8/20 µs waveform.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Three-Stage Cascaded Surge Protection

A three-stage cascaded topology is the de-facto standard for protecting sensitive IED inputs in a substation. Each stage has a deliberately chosen role:

**Stage 1 — GDT (Gas Discharge Tube):**
- **Function**: Bulk energy diversion. When the surge voltage exceeds the GDT's DC sparkover voltage (typically 90 V, 230 V, or 600 V), the gas inside the tube ionises and forms a low-impedance arc (≈ 25 V) to ground.
- **Why GDT**: It can divert **kiloamperes** of surge current with negligible let-through voltage, and it does not age.
- **Trade-off**: Slow response (~1 µs impulse sparkover), so a fast-edge overshoot gets through to Stage 2.

**Stage 2 — MOV (Metal Oxide Varistor):**
- **Function**: Clamp the residual let-through voltage. The MOV's non-linear V-I law ($I = k V^{\alpha}$, $\alpha \approx 35$) provides clamping within a few hundred nanoseconds.
- **Why MOV**: Best balance of energy handling (hundreds of joules per pulse) and clamping performance. Also available in small SMD packages for compact IED front ends.
- **Trade-off**: Capacitive (100s of pF), so not suitable for high-frequency signals; ages cumulatively with repeated surges.

**Stage 3 — TVS Diode:**
- **Function**: Snub the *fast-edge* residual that escapes Stages 1 and 2, ensuring the final voltage at the IED pin is below its rated withstand.
- **Why TVS**: Sub-nanosecond response, sharp clamping, low leakage, and predictable behaviour.

**Inter-stage impedances** (wire inductance, ferrite beads, PTC thermistors, or 5–10 Ω resistors) are inserted between stages to **desynchronise** the stages and to absorb the energy difference.

**Block diagram** (as in Section 4.1):
- Field signal pair → GDT (Stage 1) → $L_s$ → MOV (Stage 2) → ferrite → TVS (Stage 3) → IED input.
- The shield/ground of the field cable is bonded to the substation earth at the cabinet entry.

*(7 marks — 2 for stage identification, 3 for justification of each device, 2 for inter-stage impedance role)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computations

**Given:** $I_{peak} = 5\,\text{kA}$, $L_s = 1.2\,\mu\text{H}$, $V_{arc} = 25\,\text{V}$, $V_{clamp,MOV} = 700\,\text{V}$, 8/20 µs waveform.

**(i) Peak inductive overshoot $V_L$:**

$$\left.\frac{di}{dt}\right|_{max} = \frac{0.736 \cdot I_{peak}}{\tau_1} = \frac{0.736 \times 5 \times 10^3}{3.91 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\left.\frac{di}{dt}\right|_{max} = \frac{3680}{3.91 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 9.41 \times 10^{8}\,\text{A/s}$$

$$V_L = L_s \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.41 \times 10^{8} = 1129\,\text{V}$$

**(ii) Let-through voltage at MOV input:**

$$V_{LT} = V_{arc} + V_L = 25 + 1129 = 1154\,\text{V}$$

> The MOV (rated clamping 700 V at higher currents) will fire at this voltage, dropping the actual voltage to its own clamping curve.

**(iii) Energy deposited in MOV (triangular approximation):**

For a triangular current pulse with base 20 µs:

$$Q \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot t_{base} = \frac{1}{2} \times 5000 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 50\,\text{mC}$$

$$E = V_{clamp} \cdot Q = 700 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} = 35\,\text{J}$$

The MOV must therefore be rated for a single-pulse energy of at least **35 J** with a safety factor of 1.5, i.e., **≥ 52.5 J**, which corresponds to a 20 mm or larger disk-type MOV commonly used in substation signal conditioners.

*(7 marks — 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii))*

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the three non-linear protection devices — GDT, MOV, and TVS diode — based on the following parameters: (i) response time, (ii) peak current handling, (iii) energy handling, (iv) capacitance, and (v) typical role in a cascaded protection scheme. Use a comparative table.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The signal conditioning subsystem of a numerical relay is exposed to a 4 kV / 2 kA combination wave surge as per IEC 61000-4-5. Design a coordinate surge protection circuit by:
- (i) Selecting a 3-terminal GDT with appropriate DC sparkover voltage.
- (ii) Selecting a MOV with continuous AC voltage rating.
- (iii) Selecting a TVS diode for the IED input rated at 5 V.
- (iv) Stating the canonical coordination inequality and verifying it numerically for your design choices.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparative Table

| Parameter | GDT | MOV | TVS Diode |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Response time | 0.5 – 1 µs | 1 – 50 ns | < 1 ns |
| (ii) Peak current (8/20 µs) | 5 – 20 kA | 0.5 – 10 kA | 0.1 – 1 kA |
| (iii) Energy per pulse | Up to kJ | J – kJ | mJ – J |
| (iv) Capacitance | 1 – 5 pF | 100 – 5000 pF | 10 – 1000 pF |
| (v) Stage role | Stage 1 (divert) | Stage 2 (clamp) | Stage 3 (refine) |

*(7 marks — 5 × 1 mark per row, 2 marks for the stage role summary)*

#### Part (b) — Design

**(i) GDT selection:** A 3-terminal GDT with **DC sparkover voltage = 90 V** (commonly used for 24 V / 48 V signal lines). This ensures the GDT does not fire under normal operation but fires quickly when the surge exceeds 90 V.

**(ii) MOV selection:** A MOV with **continuous AC voltage rating = 275 Vrms**. This is the standard "275 V MOV" used in substation signal conditioners, allowing direct connection across the signal line and ground without conduction at normal mains.

**(iii) TVS selection:** A **bidirectional TVS diode with standoff voltage 5 V** and clamping voltage of **6.5 V at 10 A**. This is sized for the 5 V ADC reference of the IED.

**(iv) Coordination inequality:**

$$V_{clamp,1} + L_{s,1}\,\frac{di_1}{dt} \;<\; V_{clamp,2} + L_{s,2}\,\frac{di_2}{dt} \;<\; V_{clamp,3} \;<\; V_{withstand,IED}$$

Substituting design values:
- $V_{clamp,1} = 25\,\text{V (GDT arc)} + L_{s,1} \cdot di_1/dt \approx 25 + 1130 = 1155\,\text{V}$ (from a similar calc as in Q-A).
- $V_{clamp,2}$ at 100 A is ~**710 V** (from MOV datasheet).
- $V_{clamp,3} = 6.5\,\text{V}$.
- $V_{withstand,IED} = 1000\,\text{V}$ (typical for a 5 V ADC pin per manufacturer datasheet).

The inequality $1155 > 710$ **fails** — meaning at the first instant, the GDT lets through more voltage than the MOV would clamp. The MOV **must** fire and absorb the overshoot. This is the normal operating regime: the MOV's *high-current* clamping curve (e.g., 800 V at 1 kA) becomes the operative $V_{clamp,2}$, and the inequality is satisfied for the steady-state portion of the surge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects the student to **recognise that the coordination inequality is a *design target***, not a strict inequality at every instant — the MOV's non-linear behaviour is precisely what *enforces* coordination by clamping the let-through.

*(7 marks — 1 for (i), 1 for (ii), 1 for (iii), 4 for (iv) including the inequality statement, the numerical substitution, and the design interpretation)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing V-I relation of MOV**: Students often write $I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$ as $I \propto V^{\alpha}$ *without* stating the value of $\alpha$ or the units. Always write $\alpha \approx 25$–$50$.
> 2. **Wrong waveform pairing**: The 1.2/50 µs is the *open-circuit voltage* waveform; 8/20 µs is the *short-circuit current* waveform. They are part of the **same** combination wave.
> 3. **Treating MOV and TVS as interchangeable**: MOVs and TVS diodes are *both* clamping devices but with completely different speed/energy trade-offs. Marking is harsh on this.
> 4. **Forgetting the inter-stage impedance**: A cascade without a series impedance is *not* a coordinated cascade — it is two clamps fighting each other.
> 5. **Ignoring the standard source impedance of 2 Ω** in IEC 61000-4-5 — the *same* generator with a different load impedance delivers different currents, and the SPD rating must be matched to the *application* impedance, not just the open-circuit voltage.
> 6. **No mention of failure modes**: MOVs *age* and eventually short-circuit; GDTs can latch into DC follow-current. The KTU examiner rewards awareness of these failure modes.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Surge Protection Circuits in SAS Signal Conditioning**

- ☐ **SAS Signal Conditioning** = Surge protection → Isolation → Filtering → Scaling → ADC.
- ☐ **Surge** = short-duration, high-amplitude transient (µs to ms, kV/kA).
- ☐ **Sources**: Lightning (direct + induced), switching transients, ESD, ground potential rise.
- ☐ **Modes**: Common-mode (line-to-ground, dominant) and Differential-mode (line-to-line).
- ☐ **Three stages**:
  - Stage 1 = **GDT** (90 V, 230 V, 600 V) — high energy, slow (µs).
  - Stage 2 = **MOV** (275 Vrms typical) — medium energy, medium speed (ns).
  - Stage 3 = **TVS** (5 V, 12 V, 24 V) — low energy, very fast (sub-ns).
- ☐ **MOV law**: $I = k V^{\alpha}$, $\alpha \approx 25$–$50$.
- ☐ **Standard test waveform**: 1.2/50 µs (Voc) + 8/20 µs (Isc), 2 Ω source impedance (IEC 61000-4-5).
- ☐ **Coordination inequality**:
  $$V_{clamp,1} + L_{s,1} \frac{di_1}{dt} < V_{clamp,2} + L_{s,2} \frac{di_2}{dt} < V_{clamp,3} < V_{withstand,IED}$$
- ☐ **Energy per 8/20 µs pulse** (triangular approx): $E = V_c \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot t_{base}$.
- ☐ **Inductive overshoot** at inter-stage: $V_L = L_s \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$, dominant contributor to let-through.
- ☐ **Standards to know**: IEEE C62.41, IEEE C62.45, IEC 61000-4-5, IEC 61643-21, IEC 61000-4-4.
- ☐ **Failure modes**: GDT — DC follow-current latch; MOV — cumulative ageing → short; TVS — fail-short (safe).
- ☐ **Application to**: 4–20 mA inputs, binary inputs, RS-485 / copper Ethernet ports, CT/PT secondaries.
- ☐ **Common pitfall**: Treating MOV and TVS as interchangeable — they are *complementary* in a cascade.
- ☐ **Engineering mantra**: *Divert first, clamp second, refine last.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anti-Aliasing Filter
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anti-Aliasing Filter

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** is a low-pass analog filter placed immediately *upstream* of the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) in a digital protection relay. Its singular, non-negotiable purpose is to band-limit the incoming continuous-time analog signal (from CTs / PTs) to a frequency range strictly less than half of the sampling frequency, thereby satisfying the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** and eliminating the catastrophic phenomenon of **aliasing**.

In the context of KTU Module 3 (Digital Signal Processing for Relaying), the AAF is treated as a **preconditioning analog stage** whose design directly determines the fidelity of all subsequent digital computations — Fourier coefficients, impedance loci, travelling-wave detections, and differential protections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEEET631, Module 3):**
> The anti-aliasing filter is identified as a *fundamental* preprocessing block. Without it, the entire digital protection algorithm (distance, overcurrent, differential) becomes mathematically invalid because the discrete samples no longer represent the true primary waveform.

## 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value in Power-System Protection | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Power Frequency | $f_0$ | **50 Hz** (India) / **60 Hz** (USA) | Hz |
| Highest Harmonic of Interest | $f_{max}$ | Typically up to the **31st harmonic** (≈ 1550 Hz for 50 Hz) or higher for travelling-wave schemes | Hz |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | **720 Hz, 1440 Hz, 2880 Hz** (common in numerical relays) | Hz |
| Nyquist Frequency | $f_N$ | $f_s / 2$ | Hz |
| Stopband Attenuation | $A_s$ | **≥ 40 dB** (industrial standard) | dB |
| Passband Ripple | $A_p$ | **≤ 0.5 dB** (typical) | dB |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stroboscopic" Intuition

Imagine filming a spinning fan wheel with a video camera:
- If the camera takes **more than 2 snapshots per revolution** (Nyquist rule), you can reconstruct the true speed of rotation perfectly.
- If the camera takes **fewer snapshots**, the wheel appears to rotate **backwards** or **stand still** — even though it is actually spinning forward at high speed. This phantom motion is exactly what *aliasing* does to a current/voltage waveform.

The **Anti-Aliasing Filter** is the equivalent of a *mechanical governor* on the fan: it physically prevents the fan blades from rotating faster than what the camera can resolve. In the electrical domain, it physically *removes* all frequency components above $f_s/2$ so the ADC can never be fooled into recording a false "phantom" harmonic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why can't we just rely on the ADC's natural behaviour?**
> An ADC *samples* the input — it does **not** filter it. If a 2 kHz signal enters a system sampling at 1 kHz, the ADC will faithfully record a 0 Hz (DC) value. The damage is already done *before* any digital code is produced. The AAF must act on the **continuous-time signal** itself.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The 5-Step Decision Chain

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Highest Frequency of Interest ($f_{max}$).**
   The protection engineer decides which harmonics are diagnostically meaningful. For a simple overcurrent relay, $f_{max}$ may be just $f_0$ (50 Hz). For a distance relay using Fourier algorithms, $f_{max}$ typically extends to the **5th–13th harmonic** (250–650 Hz). For travelling-wave / phasor-extraction units, it may exceed **5 kHz**.

2. **Step 2 — Choose the Sampling Frequency ($f_s$).**
   The 2024 Scheme module emphasizes $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$. Practically, a *guard band* of 20–30% is added, giving $f_s \approx 2.5\,f_{max}$ to $3\,f_{max}$. Common relay sampling rates: **720, 960, 1440, 2880 Hz**.

3. **Step 3 — Define the Filter Specifications.**
   - **Cutoff frequency $f_c$**: chosen as $f_c \le f_s/2 - \Delta f$ (with $\Delta f$ being the transition band). Often $f_c$ is set close to $f_{max}$.
   - **Stopband edge $f_{stop}$**: $\le f_s/2$.
   - **Passband ripple $A_p$** and **stopband attenuation $A_s$**: determined by measurement accuracy required (e.g., ±5% current magnitude).

4. **Step 4 — Select Filter Type and Order.**
   Trade-off between:
   - **Butterworth** — maximally flat passband, gentle roll-off (needs higher order).
   - **Chebyshev Type-I** — sharper roll-off but with passband ripple.
   - **Bessel** — best phase linearity (preserves wave shape), poor amplitude roll-off.
   - **Elliptic (Cauer)** — sharpest roll-off, ripple in both bands.

5. **Step 5 — Hardware Realization.**
   Implemented using **active op-amp topologies** (Sallen-Key, Multiple Feedback) since discrete LC filters are bulky at sub-kHz cutoffs. Modern numerical relays integrate the AAF as a **Surface-Mount analog front-end IC** (e.g., AFE031, AD7606 family).

## 2.2 Why the "Why" Matters

- **Why low-pass and not band-pass?** — Because the lowest relevant frequency is DC (for offset decaying components in transient currents) and the highest is $f_{max}$. Anything between is passed uniformly.
- **Why place it *before* the Sample-and-Hold?** — The S/H is a *sampling* device, not a *filtering* device. Filtering after sampling is mathematically impossible without violating the sampling theorem.
- **Why not use a digital filter inside the DSP?** — A digital filter can suppress aliasing *only* if the analog input was already band-limited before sampling. Otherwise, the alias has *already corrupted* the data.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why AAF is Critical |
|---|---|
| **Distance Relay (Impedance Measurement)** | False impedance locus if 3rd/5th harmonic aliases into fundamental band |
| **Differential Protection (Transformer/Busbar)** | CT saturation produces high-frequency noise that aliases → false tripping |
| **Travelling-Wave Fault Location** | Requires bandwidth up to 100 kHz; AAF must match the high $f_s$ |
| **Phasor Measurement Units (PMU)** | Synchrophasor accuracy class M-class demands < 1% TVE → strict AAF tuning |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Specification | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** | Hz |
| 2 | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | **Nyquist Frequency** (folding frequency) | Hz |
| 3 | $f_c = f_{max}$ (typical) | **Filter Cutoff Frequency** | Hz |
| 4 | $A_s = 20 \log_{10} \!\left(\dfrac{1}{\delta_{alias}}\right)$ | **Stopband Attenuation** ($\delta_{alias}$ = max alias magnitude) | dB |
| 5 | $n \ge \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{10^{0.1A_s}-1}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(f_{stop}/f_c\right)}$ | **Butterworth Filter Order** | — |
| 6 | $\vert H(j\omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1+(\omega/\omega_c)^{2n}}$ | **Butterworth Magnitude Response** | — |
| 7 | $\tau_g = \dfrac{d\phi(\omega)}{d\omega}$ | **Group Delay** (must be flat for transient fidelity) | s |
| 8 | $f_{stop} \le f_s - f_c$ | **Transition Band** constraint | Hz |
| 9 | $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | **Ideal ADC SNR** ($N$ = bit resolution) | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> In the markdown table above, the absolute-value notation in formula 6 is rendered using `\vert` (not the pipe character) to preserve the integrity of the markdown table structure.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sampling Frequency from Harmonic Content

A numerical relay must extract the fundamental (50 Hz) and detect at least the 5th harmonic (250 Hz) for inrush discrimination in transformer protection.

**Given:**
- Fundamental frequency $f_0 = 50$ Hz
- Highest harmonic of interest $n_h = 5$, so $f_{max} = 5 \times 50 = 250$ Hz

**Step 1 — Apply Nyquist Theorem:**

$$f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

$$f_s \ge 2 \times 250$$

$$f_s \ge 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Add Engineering Guard Band (30%):**

$$f_s = 1.3 \times 500 = 650 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Select Standardized Sampling Rate (from common relay tables):**

$$f_s = 720 \text{ Hz} \quad (\text{commonly used, gives } 14.4 \text{ samples/cycle})$$

**Step 4 — Compute Nyquist Frequency:**

$$f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{720}{2} = 360 \text{ Hz}$$

This means **any frequency component above 360 Hz will be aliased back into the 0–360 Hz band** unless suppressed by the AAF.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Filter Order (Butterworth)

**Problem Statement:**
Design a 2nd-stage Butterworth low-pass anti-aliasing filter for a relay with:
- Cutoff (passband edge): $f_c = 250$ Hz
- Stopband edge: $f_{stop} = 360$ Hz (just below $f_N$)
- Minimum stopband attenuation: $A_s = 40$ dB
- Passband ripple: $A_p = 0.5$ dB

**Step 1 — Convert attenuation to linear scale:**

$$A_{s,\text{lin}} = 10^{A_s/20} = 10^{40/20} = 10^2 = 100$$

So the magnitude must drop by a factor of 100 at $f_{stop}$.

**Step 2 — Butterworth Attenuation Equation:**

For $f > f_c$, the attenuation of an $n$-th order Butterworth filter is:

$$A(f) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^{2n}\right] \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Set $A(f_{stop}) = A_s$ and solve for $n$:**

$$40 = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + \left(\frac{360}{250}\right)^{2n}\right]$$

$$4 = \log_{10}\!\left[1 + (1.44)^{2n}\right]$$

$$10^4 = 1 + (1.44)^{2n}$$

$$(1.44)^{2n} = 10^4 - 1 \approx 9999$$

**Step 4 — Take logarithms:**

$$2n \cdot \log_{10}(1.44) = \log_{10}(9999)$$

$$2n \cdot 0.1584 = 3.9999$$

$$2n = 25.25$$

$$n = 12.6$$

**Step 5 — Round Up to Integer Order:**

$$n = 13 \quad (\text{always round up to guarantee attenuation})$$

A **13th-order Butterworth** filter is required. (In practice, this is often split as a 6th-order active stage + 7th-order active stage, or a single high-order Bessel for transient fidelity.)

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Alias Frequency Calculation

If a 2 kHz noise component leaks through a poorly-designed AAF in a system sampling at 1 kHz:

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{noise} - k \cdot f_s \vert$$

where $k$ is the integer that brings $f_{alias}$ into the range $[0, f_s/2]$.

$$f_{alias} = \vert 2000 - 2 \times 1000 \vert = 0 \text{ Hz (DC offset!)}$$

**Implication:** A 2 kHz noise tone aliases to a **DC offset**, which the relay will interpret as a real decaying DC component of a fault current — leading to **false differential operation** or **incorrect impedance reach**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Combined Pre-Filter + Digital Filter Strategy

Modern relays cascade an analog 2nd-order AAF (cutoff ≈ 1.5 kHz) with a digital FIR filter inside the DSP. The digital filter handles:
- DC offset removal
- Anti-aliasing for *decimation* (down-sampling)

The combined response must satisfy:

$$A_{total}(f) = A_{AAF}(f) + A_{digital}(f) \ge A_s \;\; \forall f \ge f_s/2$$

This **decimation filter** concept is central to Sigma-Delta ADC architectures used in modern numerical relays.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Signal Flow in a Numerical Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CT PT Secondary] --> B[Surge Arrester and Overvoltage Clamp]
    B --> C[Burden Resistor and Scaling]
    C --> D[Anti Aliasing Filter Low Pass Analog]
    D --> E[Sample and Hold S H]
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    F --> G[Digital Anti Alias Decimation Filter]
    G --> H[Digital Signal Processor DSP Core]
    H --> I[Protection Algorithm Distance Differential Overcurrent]
    I --> J[Trip Coil Output Relay Contacts]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style F fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#28A745,color:#000
    style H fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#DC3545,color:#000
    style J fill:#C3E6CB,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of an Active Butterworth AAF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Vin from CT PT] --> B[Buffer Op Amp U1]
    B --> C[2nd Order Sallen Key Stage U2]
    C --> D[2nd Order Multiple Feedback Stage U3]
    D --> E[Output Vout to S H]
    E -.->|Sample Clock| F[Sample and Hold IC]

    subgraph FILTER_CHAIN [Active Filter Cascade]
        B
        C
        D
    end

    style A fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style FILTER_CHAIN fill:#E2E3E5,stroke:#383D41,color:#000
    style F fill:#D1ECF1,stroke:#0C5460,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Response Schematic — AAF in Action

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_SPECTRUM [Input Spectrum from CT]
        A1[DC Component]
        A2[Fundamental 50 Hz]
        A3[3rd Harmonic 150 Hz]
        A4[5th Harmonic 250 Hz]
        A5[Noise 2 kHz]
        A6[RF Interference 50 kHz]
    end

    A1 & A2 & A3 & A4 & A5 & A6 --> B[Anti Aliasing Filter]

    subgraph OUTPUT_SPECTRUM [Output Spectrum to ADC]
        B --> C1[DC Preserved]
        B --> C2[Fundamental Preserved]
        B --> C3[3rd Harmonic Preserved]
        B --> C4[5th Harmonic Preserved]
        B --> C5[Noise 2 kHz Attenuated by 40 dB]
        B --> C6[RF 50 kHz Fully Blocked]
    end
```
