# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "aliasing" in the context of digital protection of power systems. State the Nyquist criterion that must be satisfied to avoid it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Aliasing is the phenomenon in which a high-frequency component of a continuous-time signal, when sampled at a rate below twice its frequency, appears in the reconstructed digital signal as a false lower-frequency component. To avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy the Nyquist criterion:

$$f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$$

where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component present in the input signal. In power-system protection, since true band-limiting is non-ideal, an **anti-aliasing filter** is inserted before the ADC to enforce this condition physically.

*(1 mark — definition; 2 marks — Nyquist statement + role of AAF)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Why is a Butterworth filter preferred over a Chebyshev filter as an anti-aliasing filter in most numerical relays?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Butterworth filter exhibits a **maximally flat magnitude response** in the passband (no ripple), ensuring that the harmonics of interest (fundamental, 3rd, 5th) are attenuated equally and the phasor magnitude computed by the relay is accurate. A Chebyshev filter, although having a sharper roll-off, introduces **passband ripple** which can distort the amplitude of individual harmonic components, leading to errors in impedance calculation and harmonic-based inrush detection. The slight extra order required for Butterworth is justified by its superior fidelity in the diagnostic frequency band.

*(2 marks — Butterworth advantage; 1 mark — Chebyshev drawback)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Full Module 3]*

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the role and placement of the anti-aliasing filter in a numerical relay. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A numerical distance relay samples the secondary current waveform at $f_s = 1440$ Hz. The protection algorithm requires accurate extraction of harmonic components up to the 7th harmonic of a 50 Hz system. Design a suitable 2nd-order Butterworth anti-aliasing filter and justify the choice of cutoff frequency and the resulting order. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) [7 marks]**

The anti-aliasing filter is the **first analog processing block** immediately after the CT/PT secondary burden resistors and before the Sample-and-Hold (S/H) and ADC stages. Refer to the block diagram below:

- **Placement:** CT/PT → Overvoltage Protection → Burden → **AAF** → S/H → ADC → DSP
- **Function:** Restricts the analog bandwidth to $f \le f_s/2$ (Nyquist limit).
- **Why before S/H:** The S/H is a sampling switch — it does not filter. If aliasing occurs, the S/H and ADC faithfully digitize the *wrong* signal.
- **Why before ADC:** The ADC is a quantizer; it does not remove frequency components. Aliasing must be prevented in the continuous-time domain.
- **Design criteria:** Passband (0 to $f_{max}$) flat, stopband ($>f_s/2$) attenuates by ≥ 40 dB.
- **Typical realization:** 2nd–6th order active Butterworth op-amp cascade (Sallen-Key or MFB topology).

*(2 marks block diagram; 2 marks placement reasoning; 2 marks function; 1 mark typical realization)*

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify $f_{max}$:**

$$f_{max} = 7 \times 50 = 350 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Check Nyquist:**

$$f_s/2 = 1440/2 = 720 \text{ Hz} \quad > \quad 350 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Choose Cutoff Frequency:**

For minimal distortion of the 7th harmonic, set:

$$f_c = 350 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Set Stopband Edge:**

$$f_{stop} = 720 - 350 = 370 \text{ Hz (theoretical minimum transition band)}$$

**Step 5 — Choose Attenuation Spec:** $A_s = 40$ dB, $A_p = 0.5$ dB.

**Step 6 — Compute Order:**

$$n \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{10^{0.1 \times 40}-1}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(f_{stop}/f_c\right)} = \frac{\log_{10}(99.995)}{\log_{10}(370/350)} = \frac{2.0}{0.0241} \approx 83$$

This absurdly high order reveals that the transition band (370 – 350 = 20 Hz) is too narrow. A practical design **widens the cutoff** to $f_c = 250$ Hz (3rd–5th harmonic band) and accepts the 7th harmonic with reduced fidelity.

**Revised Step 6:** With $f_c = 250$ Hz, $f_{stop} = 500$ Hz:

$$n \ge \frac{2.0}{\log_{10}(500/250)} = \frac{2.0}{0.301} = 6.64 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = 7$$

**Step 7 — Justification:** A 7th-order Butterworth gives:
- < 0.5 dB loss at 250 Hz ✓
- ≥ 40 dB attenuation at 500 Hz ✓
- Realizable as 3 cascaded Sallen-Key 2nd-order stages + 1 first-order stage.

*(1 mark each for Steps 1–6, 2 marks for Step 7 justification)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the minimum order $n$ of a Butterworth anti-aliasing filter given passband edge $f_c$, stopband edge $f_{stop}$, and stopband attenuation $A_s$ dB. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a numerical relay in a 50 Hz system, the AAF is a 2nd-order Butterworth with cutoff at 500 Hz. Compute the alias frequency if a 1.2 kHz noise component is present at the input. If the sampling rate is 1 kHz, what is the impact on the protection algorithm? Suggest two remedial measures. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) [7 marks]**

The squared magnitude response of an $n$-th order Butterworth filter is:

$$\vert H(j\omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2n}}$$

At the stopband edge $\omega_{stop}$:

$$\vert H(j\omega_{stop})\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega_{stop}/\omega_c)^{2n}} = 10^{-A_s/10}$$

Rearranging:

$$1 + (f_{stop}/f_c)^{2n} = 10^{A_s/10}$$

$$(f_{stop}/f_c)^{2n} = 10^{A_s/10} - 1$$

Taking $\log_{10}$ on both sides:

$$2n \log_{10}(f_{stop}/f_c) = \log_{10}(10^{A_s/10} - 1)$$

$$\boxed{\,n \ge \frac{\log_{10}(10^{A_s/10} - 1)}{2 \log_{10}(f_{stop}/f_c)}\,}$$

*(3 marks for magnitude response setup, 2 marks for the algebraic manipulation, 2 marks for the final boxed formula)*

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify Sampling Parameters:**

$$f_s = 1 \text{ kHz}, \quad f_N = 500 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_c = 500 \text{ Hz (AAF cutoff)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute AAF Attenuation at 1.2 kHz (2nd-order Butterworth):**

$$A(1200) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + (1200/500)^4\right] = 10 \log_{10}[1 + 33.18] \approx 15.4 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Alias Frequency:**

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{noise} - k f_s \vert$$

For $k = 1$:

$$f_{alias} = \vert 1200 - 1000 \vert = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Impact on Protection Algorithm:**

200 Hz is the **4th harmonic** of 50 Hz. The relay will misinterpret the noise as a true 4th harmonic content, leading to:
- False harmonic restraint in differential protection → failure to trip on internal fault.
- Spurious impedance locus shift in distance relay → over-reach or under-reach.

**Step 5 — Remedial Measures (any two):**
1. Increase AAF order from 2 to ≥ 4 (e.g., cascade 2 Sallen-Key stages) to push attenuation at 1.2 kHz to > 40 dB.
2. Increase $f_s$ to ≥ 3 kHz so that 1.2 kHz falls within the passband and the noise is sampled faithfully (then removed by digital filtering).
3. Add a **notch filter** at 1.2 kHz if the noise source is known (e.g., switching converter clock).
4. Use Sigma-Delta ADC with inherent oversampling and digital decimation filter.

*(2 marks Step 1–2, 2 marks Step 3, 2 marks Step 4, 1 mark Step 5)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on Anti-Aliasing Filter Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "sampling rate" with "Nyquist rate"** — The Nyquist *rate* is $2 f_{max}$, NOT the sampling frequency. Stating "$f_s = 2 f_{max}$" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the guard band** — A 30% margin is industry practice; a design using exactly $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is technically correct but practically rejected.
> 3. **Not specifying the filter type** — "Low-pass filter" alone is incomplete. Always state *order*, *topology* (Butterworth, Bessel, etc.), and *cutoff frequency*.
> 4. **Skipping the AAF in block diagrams** — A numerical relay block diagram drawn *without* the AAF is incomplete and will lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Inverting the aliasing formula** — The correct form is $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$, NOT $f_{alias} = f_s - f$.
> 6. **Forgetting practical impairments** — CT saturation, CVT transients, and capacitor switching produce real high-frequency components; a design that ignores these is unrealistic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Anti-Aliasing Filter**

- **AAF is an analog low-pass filter** placed *before* the S/H and ADC — never after.
- **Purpose:** Enforce $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ physically by removing all frequency components above $f_s/2$.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ ; Nyquist Frequency $f_N = f_s/2$.
- **Practical Sampling Rates in Relays:** 720, 1440, 2880 Hz (50 Hz base).
- **Common Filter Type:** Butterworth (maximally flat passband) — typical order 2–8.
- **Key Spec:** $A_s \ge 40$ dB at $f \ge f_N$ to ensure alias magnitude < 1% of true signal.
- **Butterworth Order Formula:**
  $$n \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(10^{0.1A_s}-1\right)}{2\log_{10}\!\left(f_{stop}/f_c\right)}$$
- **Alias Frequency:**
  $$f_{alias} = \vert f_{noise} - k f_s \vert \quad \text{where } k \in \mathbb{Z}$$
- **Bessel preferred for:** travelling-wave / high-frequency transient analysis (linear phase).
- **Chebyshev preferred for:** sharp roll-off when minor passband ripple is acceptable.
- **Implementation:** Sallen-Key or Multiple-Feedback active op-amp topologies; never use LC at sub-kHz cutoffs.
- **Modern trend:** Sigma-Delta ADC + digital decimation filter reducing analog AAF order to 1 or 2.
- **Block Diagram Rule:** CT/PT → AAF → S/H → ADC → DSP → Trip Logic (AAF *must* appear).
- **Pitfall:** Anti-aliasing is impossible *after* sampling — the analog front-end is non-negotiable.
- **Industry ICs:** AFE031, AD7606, MAX125 — all integrate AAF + S/H + ADC in single package.

> **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024):**
> *"Aliasing cannot be digitally undone — only analog-prevented. The AAF is the unsung hero of every numerical relay."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion Subsystem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conversion Subsystem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Conversion Subsystem:**
> The **Conversion Subsystem** is the *front-end hardware interface* of a numerical (digital) relay. It performs the critical task of **transducing, conditioning, and digitizing** the high-magnitude power-system signals (currents and voltages) into low-level, computer-compatible binary data that the relay's CPU/DSP can process in real time.

It essentially acts as the **"sensory organ"** of the digital relay, bridging the gap between the *analog power system world* and the *digital processor world*.

## 1.2 Block Identity within the Numerical Relay

The conversion subsystem forms **Block-1** of the standard numerical relay architecture and is universally followed by the **Digital Signal Processing (DSP) subsystem** and the **Decision/Output subsystem**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Stethoscope"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Imagine a doctor using a **digital stethoscope** to listen to your heartbeat. The stethoscope does not store the raw sound wave; instead, it:
> 1. **Picks up** the weak heartbeat sound (analog signal).
> 2. **Amplifies and filters** it (signal conditioning).
> 3. **Converts** it into digital numbers a chip can understand (A/D conversion).
>
> The **Conversion Subsystem** of a numerical relay does *exactly the same thing*, but for the *currents and voltages* flowing in a 400 kV transmission line.
> - **Power system signal** = Heartbeat (high voltage/current, dangerous to touch).
> - **CT/PT** = Stethoscope's pickup sensor (steps the signal down safely).
> - **Anti-aliasing filter** = Removes background noise (room chatter).
> - **A/D Converter** = Translates sound into numbers for the chip.
> - **Relay CPU** = The doctor's brain interpreting the numbers.

## 1.4 Major Functional Blocks

The Conversion Subsystem comprises **six sequential functional stages**:

| Stage | Functional Block | Primary Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Instrument Transformers (CTs & PTs)** | Step down HV/EHV currents & voltages to safe, measurable levels |
| 2 | **Surge Protection / Spike Arresters** | Clamp transient over-voltages from switching/lightning |
| 3 | **Auxiliary CTs / Scaling Resistors** | Further scale the signal to match the analog front-end (AFE) input range |
| 4 | **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | Band-limit the signal to satisfy the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** |
| 5 | **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit** | Freeze the instantaneous analog value during the A/D conversion window |
| 6 | **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** | Quantize the analog sample into a finite-bit binary word |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 focus:**
> The detailed internal architecture, the *why* behind each stage, the relevant mathematical transfer functions, and the **A/D converter topologies** (Successive Approximation, Flash, Sigma-Delta) are the *core examinable material* in this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Operational Breakdown

### Stage 1 — Instrument Transformers (CTs & PTs)

**Why needed:** A 400 kV line carries currents up to **4000 A** and voltages up to **√2 × 400 kV/√3 ≈ 326 kV (phase)**. A relay's analog front-end typically accepts **±5 V** or **±10 V**. Direct connection is impossible.

- **Current Transformer (CT):** Steps current proportionally. Operates in **near-short-circuit** secondary condition. Standard secondary: **1 A** or **5 A**.
- **Voltage Transformer (VT/PT):** Steps voltage proportionally. Standard secondary: **110 V / √3** (phase) or **110 V** (line-to-line).
- **CVT (Capacitor Voltage Transformer):** Used at EHV (≥ 220 kV) since wound PTs become uneconomical.

### Stage 2 — Surge Protection & Transient Limiting

- **Purpose:** Protect the sensitive AFE from very-fast **transients (VFTs)**, **chopped wave surges**, and **secondary switching transients**.
- **Components:** **Metal-Oxide Varistors (MOVs)**, **Transient Voltage Suppressors (TVS)**, and **spark gaps**.
- **Clamping Voltage:** Typically set to **two times** the rated secondary voltage.

### Stage 3 — Auxiliary CTs & Burden Resistors

- **Auxiliary CT:** A small intermediate CT (e.g., 5 A → 0.1 A) with high linearity, used for *isolation* and *fine scaling*.
- **Burden Resistor (R_b):** Converts the CT secondary current into a proportional voltage: $V = I_s \times R_b$.
- **CT Knee-Point Voltage (V_knee):** The voltage at which a 10% increase in secondary voltage causes a 50% increase in exciting current. Must exceed the maximum peak fault voltage to **avoid saturation**.

### Stage 4 — Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter (CRITICAL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The #1 most-examined concept in this topic.**

**Why needed — Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:**
A signal must be sampled at **at least twice** the highest frequency component present in it, **else frequency aliasing** (false low-frequency folding) will corrupt the data.

> If a 50 Hz power signal contains harmonics up to the *k*-th order, then:
> $$f_{max} = k \cdot 50 \text{ Hz}$$

If the sampling rate is fixed at $f_s$, the anti-aliasing filter must band-limit the input to:

$$f_c = \frac{f_s}{2}$$

**Topology:** A **second-order Butterworth active low-pass filter** is the industry standard because it provides a *maximally flat passband* — critical since protective relays must not distort the magnitude of the fundamental or the dominant harmonics used for decision-making.

**Transfer Function (2nd-order Butterworth):**

$$H(s) = \frac{\omega_c^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,\omega_c\, s + \omega_c^2}$$

where $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ is the cutoff angular frequency.

### Stage 5 — Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit

- **Purpose:** The ADC takes a finite time (the **aperture time** + **conversion time**) to produce its digital output. If the input changes during this window, the conversion is erroneous.
- **Working:** A **MOSFET switch** connects the input to a **hold capacitor (C_h)**. When the switch is closed (track mode), the output follows the input. When it opens (hold mode), the capacitor *freezes* the instantaneous value.
- **Aperture Uncertainty ($\Delta t_A$):** A small jitter in the sampling instant. The maximum input slew rate must satisfy:
$$\left|\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right|_{max} \cdot \Delta t_A \leq \frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}$$

### Stage 6 — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)

This is the **heart of the digitisation process**. Three properties are paramount for protection:

1. **Resolution (n bits):** Number of distinct digital output codes. Typical: **12-bit, 14-bit, or 16-bit**.
2. **Sampling Rate (f_s):** Samples per second. Typical: **1.2 kHz to 4 kHz** per channel for 50/60 Hz systems.
3. **Conversion Time (t_conv):** Time to complete one quantization. Must be < sampling interval.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize These for the Board Exam**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Units / Typical Value |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | CT turns ratio | $I_p / I_s = N_2 / N_1$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | CT secondary voltage from burden | $V_s = I_s \cdot (R_{burden} + Z_{CT,sec})$ | Volts |
| 3 | CT accuracy limit factor | $K_{ALF} = V_{knee} / (I_{sn} \cdot R_{burden})$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Anti-aliasing cutoff | $f_c = f_s / 2$ | Hz |
| 6 | Quantization step (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Volts |
| 7 | Quantization error (peak) | $e_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Volts |
| 8 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $SQNR_{dB} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 9 | Aperture error constraint | $\left|\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right| \cdot \Delta t_A \leq \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$ | V/s · s |
| 10 | 2nd-order Butterworth $\omega_c$ | $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | rad/s |
| 11 | Butterworth damping factor | $\zeta = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Aliasing folded frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - n f_s \vert,\ n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Hz |
| 13 | Maximum harmonic order sampled | $k_{max} = \lfloor f_s / (2 \cdot f_1) \rfloor$ | Order |

> [!WARNING]
> **In exam tables, write `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical bar `|`.** The bar symbol breaks markdown table rendering.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Protection:** Every numerical distance relay, differential relay, and overcurrent relay in substations worldwide uses this exact signal chain. A poorly designed anti-aliasing filter can cause a relay to **misoperate** (false trip or failure to trip), which can lead to cascading blackouts.
- **Smart Grid & Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs):** High-fidelity ADCs (often 16-bit, 5–10 kSa/s) are at the heart of **WAMS (Wide-Area Monitoring Systems)**, providing GPS-time-stamped synchrophasors.
- **Power Quality Monitoring:** The same architecture (with higher sampling rates, e.g., 100 kSa/s) is used to capture **transients, harmonics, and flicker** in compliance with **IEC 61000-4-30**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sampling Rate from the Nyquist–Shannon Theorem

We require a sampling rate that **completely captures** the information content of a band-limited analog signal.

**Step 1 — Definition.** A signal $x(t)$ is **band-limited** to $f_{max}$ Hz if its Fourier transform $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_{max}$.

**Step 2 — Sampling operation.** The sampled signal is mathematically expressed as the product of the signal with a Dirac comb:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Frequency domain.** Multiplication in time becomes *convolution in frequency* with the Fourier series of the impulse train:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

This shows that the spectrum of $x(t)$ is **replicated** at every integer multiple of $f_s$.

**Step 4 — Non-overlapping condition.** To recover $x(t)$ uniquely by low-pass filtering, the replicas must not overlap. This gives:

$$f_s - f_{max} \geq f_{max} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{f_s \geq 2 f_{max}}$$

**Step 5 — Practical protection setting.** For a 50 Hz system with the 13th harmonic of interest ($f_{max} = 650$ Hz), the *theoretical* Nyquist rate is **1300 Hz**. In practice, protection engineers choose $f_s = 4$ to $8 \times f_{max}$ (i.e., **oversampling**) to relax the anti-aliasing filter's roll-off requirements.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantization Error and SQNR

**Step 1 — LSB (Least Significant Bit) size.** A unipolar ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS}$ and resolution $n$ divides the range into $2^n$ equal steps:

$$\Delta V = \text{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization error model.** Assuming the error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed over one LSB centred at zero, the **peak quantization error** is:

$$e_{q,peak} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 3 — RMS noise power.** For a uniform distribution of width $\Delta V$:

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta V} \int_{-\Delta V/2}^{\Delta V/2} e^2 \, de = \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{12} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 4^n}$$

**Step 4 — RMS signal power** (full-scale sinusoid, amplitude $V_{FS}/2$):

$$\overline{x^2} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{V_{FS}}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

**Step 5 — SQNR.**

$$SQNR = \frac{\overline{x^2}}{\overline{e_q^2}} = \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{V_{FS}^2 / (12 \cdot 4^n)} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8} = 1.5 \cdot 4^n$$

**Step 6 — Convert to decibels.** Using $4^n = 2^{2n}$:

$$SQNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(4^n) = 1.76 + 20 n \log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02\,n$$

$$\boxed{SQNR_{dB} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** Each additional bit of ADC resolution improves the SQNR by **6.02 dB**, equivalent to roughly **doubling** the signal-to-noise power ratio.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aperture Uncertainty Constraint for the S/H Circuit

**Step 1 — Set-up.** The S/H aperture uncertainty is $\Delta t_A$ seconds. During this interval the input may change by:

$$\Delta V_{in} = \left|\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right|_{max} \cdot \Delta t_A$$

**Step 2 — Acceptable error.** This change must be smaller than **half of one LSB** to avoid code errors:

$$\Delta V_{in} \leq \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 3 — Final constraint.**

$$\boxed{\left|\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right|_{max} \cdot \Delta t_A \leq \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** A 14-bit ADC ($n = 14$) with $V_{FS} = 10$ V and $\Delta t_A = 1$ ns, fed by a 50 Hz, peak ±5 V sinusoid:

$$\left|\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 5 = 1570.8 \text{ V/s}$$

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{10}{2^{15}} = 3.05 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$$

$$\text{Required aperture uncertainty} \leq \frac{3.05 \times 10^{-4}}{1570.8} \approx 1.94 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 194 \text{ ns}$$

The S/H must be designed with aperture jitter $\leq 194$ ns, which is readily achievable with modern CMOS S/H circuits.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff for 50 Hz / 13th Harmonic Protection

**Step 1 — Identify signal bandwidth.** Up to the 13th harmonic:

$$f_{max} = 13 \times 50 = 650 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Choose sampling rate.** Use $f_s = 4 \times f_{max} = 2600$ Hz (oversampling factor = 4).

**Step 3 — Anti-aliasing cutoff:**

$$f_c = \frac{f_s}{2} = 1300 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Verify aliasing immunity.** A frequency component at 1400 Hz (i.e., $f_{in} - f_s = 1400 - 2600 = -1200$ Hz) would alias to 1200 Hz, which is **above** $f_c$ and thus attenuated by the filter. ✅

**Step 5 — Butterworth -3 dB point:** By definition, the 2nd-order Butterworth gives -3 dB at 1300 Hz and rolls off at **-40 dB/decade**, providing ~40 dB of rejection at 2600 Hz — sufficient for protection-grade aliasing suppression.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Conversion Subsystem

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end signal flow** of a numerical relay's conversion subsystem, from the power system bus down to the digital processor bus. It is engineered as a *block-level functional architecture* (per protocol) rather than a physical schematic, ensuring clean rendering.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HV Bus Current & Voltage"] --> B["Stage 1 Instrument Transformers CT & VT"]
    B --> C["Stage 2 Surge Protection MOV & TVS Clamps"]
    C --> D["Stage 3 Auxiliary CT & Burden Resistor Network"]
    D --> E["Stage 4 Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter 2nd Order Butterworth"]
    E --> F["Stage 5 Sample and Hold Circuit With Hold Capacitor"]
    F --> G["Stage 6 Analog to Digital Converter 14 bit Successive Approximation"]
    G --> H["Digital Output Bus to DSP Subsystem"]

    A -. "Primary 4000 A and 400 kV" .- B
    B -. "Secondary 1 A or 5 A and 110 V" .- C
    D -. "Scaled to plus or minus 5 V or 0 to 10 V" .- E
    E -. "Band limited below fs over 2" .- F
    F -. "Frozen analog value" .- G
    G -. "12 14 or 16 bit binary word" .- H

    style A fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style B fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style C fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style D fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    style G fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style H fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Internal ADC Sub-system (S/H + MUX + ADC)

Modern numerical relays use a **multiplexed ADC architecture** where a single high-speed ADC is shared across multiple input channels (Phase-A current, Phase-B current, Phase-C current, Neutral current, Phase-A voltage, …). The Mermaid block diagram below shows the internal arrangement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MUX["Analog Multiplexer 8 to 16 to 1"] --> SH["Sample and Hold S and H"]
    SH --> ADC["Analog to Digital Converter 14 bit"]
    ADC --> DRV["Output Driver and Serializer"]
    DRV --> CPU["Relay CPU or DSP Bus"]

    CH1["Ia Phase A Current"] --> MUX
    CH2["Ib Phase B Current"] --> MUX
    CH3["Ic Phase C Current"] --> MUX
    CH4["In Neutral Current"] --> MUX
    CH5["Va Phase A Voltage"] --> MUX
    CH6["Vb Phase B Voltage"] --> MUX
    CH7["Vc Phase C Voltage"] --> MUX
    CH8["V0 Zero Sequence Voltage"] --> MUX

    SH -. "Track and Hold Control Signal from CPU" .- SH
    ADC -. "EOC End of Conversion Interrupt" .- CPU
```

## 4.3 Functional Decision Flow — Choosing ADC Resolution

This decision flow is the *engineering reasoning* a designer follows when sizing an ADC for a protection relay.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"What is the maximum primary fault current"} --> A1["Compute CT secondary current at fault"]
    A1 --> Q2{"What is the smallest differential current the relay must detect"}
    Q2 --> A2["Compute dynamic range D in dB"]
    A2 --> Q3{"What sampling rate is required for harmonic order k"}
    Q3 --> A3["Set fs equal to 4 to 8 times k times 50"]
    A3 --> Q4{"Required accuracy at minimum pickup"}
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1.** *Define the Conversion Subsystem of a numerical relay and list its six functional stages in the correct signal-flow order.*

**Model Answer:**
The conversion subsystem is the front-end hardware block of a numerical relay that transduces, conditions, and digitizes high-magnitude power system signals into computer-compatible binary words.
The six stages in signal-flow order are:
1. **Instrument Transformers (CT/VT)** — stepping down primary quantities.
2. **Surge Protection Circuits (MOV/TVS)** — clamping transients.
3. **Auxiliary CTs and Burden Resistors** — further scaling to AFE range.
4. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter** — band-limiting per Nyquist criterion.
5. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit** — freezing the analog sample.
6. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — quantizing into binary words.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the six stages in order.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** *State the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem. What is the consequence of violating it?*

**Model Answer:**
**Statement:** A band-limited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling rate satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

**Consequence of violation:** If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, high-frequency components fold back into the baseband and appear as **false low-frequency signals** — a phenomenon called **aliasing**. In a relay, this can cause incorrect impedance estimation and false trip decisions.

*(3 marks: 1 for theorem, 1 for formula, 1 for consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### OR — Question Pattern: Part (a) 7 marks, Part (b) 7 marks

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of the conversion subsystem** of a numerical relay. Describe the role of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **SQNR (Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio)** expression for a uniform n-bit ADC. A relay uses a **14-bit ADC** with a full-scale range of $\pm 10$ V. Compute the **LSB size, peak quantization error, and the SQNR in dB**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram and explanation** *(refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes).*

| Block | Role (1 mark each — pick 6 to fill 6 of 7 marks) |
|:---|:---|
| CT/VT | Step down primary current/voltage to safe measurable levels (1 A/5 A, 110 V) |
| Surge protection | Clamp VFT and switching transients to protect downstream electronics |
| Auxiliary CT + burden | Convert CT current to AFE-compatible voltage (±5 V or ±10 V) |
| Anti-aliasing LPF | Band-limit signal to ≤ fs/2 to prevent frequency folding |
| S/H circuit | Hold input constant during ADC conversion to avoid aperture error |
| ADC | Quantize analog sample into a finite-bit binary word |
| Digital bus interface | Deliver the binary word to the DSP/CPU subsystem |

*(1 additional mark for block-diagram neatness & correct signal-flow arrows.)*

**(b) Derivation and numerical evaluation**

From **Derivation 2 in Section 3.2** of these notes:

$$\text{LSB} = \Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{20}{2^{14}} = \frac{20}{16384} = 1.2207 \text{ mV}$$

$$e_{q,peak} = \pm \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}} = \pm \frac{20}{2^{15}} = \pm 0.6104 \text{ mV}$$

$$SQNR_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02(14) + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = \mathbf{86.04 \text{ dB}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice — Alternative to Question A)*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** and its implications for the **design of the anti-aliasing filter** in a numerical relay. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 Hz numerical relay must faithfully capture signals up to the **15th harmonic**. The relay uses a **12-bit ADC** with full-scale range $\pm 5$ V and a sample-and-hold circuit with an aperture uncertainty of **2 ns**. Determine: **(i)** the minimum theoretical sampling rate, **(ii)** a practical sampling rate (4× oversampling), **(iii)** the anti-aliasing filter cutoff frequency, **(iv)** the LSB and peak quantization error, and **(v)** whether the S/H aperture of 2 ns is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation** *(refer to the theoretical analysis in Section 2.1 — Stage 4, and Derivation 1 in Section 3.1 of these notes).*

Key points to write:
1. Statement of the theorem: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (2 marks)
2. Replication of spectrum at multiples of $f_s$ (1 mark)
3. Definition of aliasing and why it's catastrophic for protective relays (1 mark)
4. Why a *Butterworth* LPF is used (maximally flat passband, monotonic roll-off) (1 mark)
5. Transfer function of 2nd-order Butterworth (1 mark)
6. Practical oversampling (4× to 8×) and its benefit (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical evaluation**

**(i) Maximum signal frequency:**
$$f_{max} = 15 \times 50 = 750 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_{s,\min} = 2 f_{max} = 1500 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Practical sampling rate (4× oversampling):**
$$f_s = 4 \times f_{s,\min} = 4 \times 1500 = 6000 \text{ Hz} = \mathbf{6 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(iii) Anti-aliasing cutoff:**
$$f_c = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{6000}{2} = \mathbf{3000 \text{ Hz}}$$

**(iv) LSB and peak quantization error** (with $V_{FS} = 10$ V total range, $n = 12$):
$$\Delta V = \text{LSB} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.441 \text{ mV}$$
$$e_{q,peak} = \pm \frac{10}{2^{13}} = \pm 1.221 \text{ mV}$$

**(v) Aperture adequacy check:**

For a 50 Hz sinusoid of peak 5 V:
$$\left|\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f \cdot V_{peak} = 2\pi (50)(5) = 1570.8 \text{ V/s}$$

Allowable aperture uncertainty (half LSB criterion):
$$\Delta t_A^{max} = \frac{V_{FS}/2^{n+1}}{\vert dV/dt \vert_{max}} = \frac{1.221 \times 10^{-3}}{1570.8} = 7.77 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 777 \text{ ns}$$

Since the actual aperture $\Delta t_A = 2$ ns $\ll 777$ ns, the **S/H is comfortably sufficient** (by a factor of ~388). ✅

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $f_s$ and $f_c$:** Writing $f_c = f_s$ (instead of $f_s/2$) is the *most common* one-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to include 1.76 dB in SQNR:** Many students write only the $6.02n$ term, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $V_{FS}$ as the peak instead of peak-to-peak:** Always state whether the full-scale is peak or peak-to-peak; the LSB formula uses the *total* range.
> 4. **Not justifying Butterworth choice:** Examiners award 1 mark specifically for naming *maximally flat passband* as the reason.
> 5. **Skipping the S/H block in the block diagram:** A common oversight. The S/H is *mandatory* between the LPF and the ADC.
> 6. **Wrong unit for the cutoff:** It should be Hz (or rad/s) — not "samples per second" — for the anti-aliasing filter.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Conversion Subsystem**

- **Six functional blocks** (in order): **CT/VT → Surge Protection → Auxiliary CT/Burden → Anti-Aliasing LPF → S/H → ADC → Digital Bus**.
- **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ is the *theoretical* minimum; **oversample 4×–8×** in practice.
- **Anti-aliasing filter:** Always a **2nd-order Butterworth LPF** with cutoff $f_c = f_s / 2$. Provides -40 dB/decade roll-off and maximally flat passband.
- **ADC resolution:** $n = 12, 14,$ or $16$ bits for protection-grade relays.
- **LSB:** $\Delta V = V_{FS} / 2^n$.
- **Peak quantization error:** $\pm V_{FS} / 2^{n+1}$ (half-LSB).
- **SQNR:** $\approx 6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB.
- **Aperture rule:** $\left| dV/dt \right|_{max} \cdot \Delta t_A \leq V_{FS} / 2^{n+1}$.
- **CT knee-point voltage** must exceed the **maximum secondary peak fault voltage** to prevent saturation-induced measurement errors.
- **Sample-and-Hold** sits **between** the LPF and the ADC — never omit it in the block diagram.
- **Butterworth damping factor** = $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ (this gives the maximally flat magnitude response).
- **Harmonic order of interest** in protection: up to the **13th** (some relays up to the 15th or 21st).
- **Industry standard secondary CT:** **1 A or 5 A**; **VT secondary:** **110 V / √3** (phase).
- **Anti-aliasing filter** is the **single most important block** — without it, all downstream digital processing is meaningless due to aliasing.
- **ADC architectures to remember:** **Successive Approximation (SAR)** — most common; **Flash** — fastest; **Sigma-Delta** — highest resolution.
- **Avoid** using the vertical bar `|` in markdown tables; use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Sampling Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Sampling Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem)**
> A bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ whose highest frequency component is $f_{max}$ Hz (i.e., its spectrum is zero for $\vert f \vert > f_{max}$) can be **uniquely and completely reconstructed** from its samples, provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ is at least **twice** the highest frequency present in the signal. Mathematically, $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The minimum allowable sampling rate, $f_s = 2 f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $\frac{f_s}{2}$ is the **Nyquist Frequency** (folding frequency).

In the context of **Digital Protection of Power Systems**, the sampling theorem is the foundational bridge that allows analog signals (currents and voltages from Current Transformers / Voltage Transformers) to be faithfully converted into a stream of discrete digital numbers that a numerical relay or digital fault recorder can process using microprocessors, DSPs, or FPGAs.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|-----------|
| Continuous Signal | $x(t)$ | Time-varying analog waveform from CT/VT secondary |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | Number of samples taken per second (Hz) |
| Sampling Period | $T_s$ | Time interval between two consecutive samples ($1/f_s$) |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_N$ | $2 \cdot f_{max}$, the minimum sampling rate required |
| Nyquist Frequency | $f_N/2$ | The folding frequency, equal to $f_{max}$ |
| Bandlimited Signal | — | Signal whose spectrum is zero beyond $f_{max}$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stroboscopic Camera" of Power Signals

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: Stroboscopic Snapshot of a Spinning Wheel**
> Imagine a wheel spinning at a constant speed (representing a 50 Hz power waveform). If you take a photograph of the wheel at evenly spaced instants using a strobe light, you can reconstruct its full motion **only if** the strobe flashes fast enough. If the strobe flashes too slowly, the wheel appears to spin **backward or at the wrong speed** — this distortion is called **aliasing**. The Sampling Theorem gives the exact rule: to faithfully capture a wheel rotating at $N$ revolutions per second, you need to take at least $2N$ snapshots per second. In digital relays, $f_{max}$ corresponds to the highest harmonic in the fault waveform (e.g., 1500 Hz for a 30th harmonic of 50 Hz), and $f_s$ is the relay's ADC clock rate.

## 1.4 Why the Sampling Theorem Matters in Power System Protection

> [!TIP]
> Numerical (digital) relays do **not** see continuous waveforms — they see a **discrete sequence of numbers**. Every protection algorithm (Fourier DFT, differential, impedance calculation, traveling-wave detection) operates on these samples. If the sampling theorem is violated:
> - High-frequency transients (from faults, lightning, switching) get **folded** into the low-frequency band.
> - The relay may **misjudge** the impedance, fail to operate, or operate for a fault on a healthy line (**misoperation**).
> - Protection security and dependability collapse.

Hence, choosing $f_s$ correctly is the **first design decision** in any numerical relay.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The theorem unfolds in three logical stages:

**Stage 1 — Multiplication by an Impulse Train (Sampling Operation)**
- The continuous signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by a periodic train of unit impulses $p(t)$ with period $T_s = 1/f_s$.
- The result is a discrete-time signal $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$, which physically is a sequence of impulses whose **amplitudes** equal the instantaneous values of $x(t)$ at $t = nT_s$.

**Stage 2 — Spectral Replication in the Frequency Domain**
- Multiplication in time $\Longleftrightarrow$ Convolution in frequency.
- The Fourier Transform of $x_s(t)$ is a **replicated** version of $X(f)$, centered at every integer multiple of $f_s$ (i.e., at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \dots$).
- If $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, the spectral replicas **do not overlap** — they remain separated by guard bands.

**Stage 3 — Reconstruction via Low-Pass Filtering**
- Passing $x_s(t)$ through an ideal low-pass filter with cutoff at $f_s/2$ recovers the original $X(f)$ exactly.
- This proves that the discrete samples carry **all** the information of $x(t)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, the spectral replicas **overlap** — a phenomenon called **aliasing** — and reconstruction becomes **mathematically impossible**. The original information is **irretrievably lost**.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist criterion | Hz; inequality is **strict** for practical systems |
| 2 | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Sampling period | seconds; smaller $T_s$ = more samples |
| 3 | $f_N = 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist rate | Hz; minimum legal sampling rate |
| 4 | $x_s(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$ | Sampled signal (impulse train form) | dimensionless amplitude |
| 5 | $X_s(f) = f_s \displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$ | Spectrum of sampled signal | Shows replication by factor $f_s$ |
| 6 | $N_{samples/cycle} = \dfrac{f_s}{f_{fundamental}}$ | Samples per power cycle | Integer; 16, 32, 64, 128 are common |
| 7 | $f_{max} = f_1 \cdot h_{max}$ | Highest harmonic of interest | $h_{max}$ = harmonic order, $f_1$ = fundamental |
| 8 | $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ | Folded (aliased) frequency | Hz; appears in baseband when undersampled |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Numerical Distance Relays:** Use 16–64 samples/cycle to compute impedance via DFT. A typical 50 Hz system with $h_{max} = 13$ requires $f_s \geq 1300$ Hz, so 32 samples/cycle ($f_s = 1600$ Hz) is common.
- **Traveling-Wave Based Protection:** Requires very high $f_s$ (1–10 MHz) to capture the nanosecond-scale wavefronts from faults.
- **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs):** Sample at 48–240 samples/cycle to support synchrophasor estimation compliant with IEEE C37.118.
- **Digital Fault Recorders (DFR):** Sample at 3.2–256 kHz depending on the bandwidth of interest.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sampling Theorem (Mathematical Proof)

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Impulse Train Modulation

The sampling operation is mathematically equivalent to multiplying the analog signal $x(t)$ by a periodic train of unit impulses $p(t)$:

$$
p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)
$$

This impulse train has Fourier series expansion:

$$
p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}
$$

where $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the sampling frequency and $k$ is an integer index.

### 3.1.2 The Sampled Signal

The sampled (discrete) signal is therefore:

$$
x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = x(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)
$$

Because the impulse $\delta(t - nT_s)$ "picks out" the value of $x(t)$ at $t = nT_s$:

$$
x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \, \delta(t - nT_s)
$$

> This is the **canonical discrete signal** used in every numerical relay.

### 3.1.3 Fourier Transform of the Sampled Signal

Taking the Fourier Transform of $x_s(t)$:

$$
X_s(f) = \mathcal{F}\!\left\{ x(t) \cdot p(t) \right\}
$$

Using the **multiplication-in-time / convolution-in-frequency** property:

$$
X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f)
$$

where $P(f)$ is the Fourier Transform of $p(t)$. From the Fourier series of $p(t)$ we know that $P(f)$ is itself an impulse train in frequency:

$$
P(f) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)
$$

### 3.1.4 Final Spectral Expression

Convolution with an impulse train **replicates** the spectrum $X(f)$ at every integer multiple of $f_s$:

$$
X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)
$$

This is the **central mathematical result** of the sampling theorem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The factor $f_s = 1/T_s$ simply scales the replicated spectrum; it does **not** change the shape. The replicas are centered at $f = 0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \pm 3f_s, \dots$

### 3.1.5 Recovery via Low-Pass Filtering

If the spectral replicas do **not overlap**, i.e., $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, then an ideal low-pass filter with:
- Passband gain $= T_s$
- Cutoff frequency $= f_s / 2$

will isolate the central replica $X(f)$ and reject all others. The output is exactly the original signal $x(t)$ — proving **lossless reconstruction**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Sampling for a 50 Hz Power System

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 50 Hz power system has significant harmonics up to the 15th order. Calculate the Nyquist rate, the minimum number of samples per cycle, and recommend a practical sampling frequency for a numerical distance relay.

**Step 1 — Identify the highest frequency**

$$
f_{max} = 15 \times 50 = 750 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Nyquist rate**

$$
f_N = 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 750 = 1500 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum samples per cycle**

$$
N_{min} = \frac{f_N}{f_1} = \frac{1500}{50} = 30 \text{ samples/cycle}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical choice (with 20% margin)**

Real systems add a safety margin. Choosing 32 samples/cycle:

$$
f_s = 32 \times 50 = 1600 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
f_s = 1600 \text{ Hz} > 1500 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Nyquist satisfied)}
$$

**Step 5 — Confirm sampling period**

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{1600} = 625 \,\mu s
$$

So the relay ADC takes a fresh sample every **625 microseconds**.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Aliasing Frequency Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A CT secondary has a high-frequency transient component at 1800 Hz. The relay samples at $f_s = 1000$ Hz (i.e., 20 samples/cycle on a 50 Hz system). Find the **apparent (aliased)** frequency that the relay will measure.

**Step 1 — Check Nyquist**

$$
f_s / 2 = 500 \text{ Hz} < 1800 \text{ Hz} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Aliasing will occur.}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the aliasing formula**

Find integer $k$ such that $\vert f - k f_s \vert < f_s/2$:

$$
k = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{1800}{1000}\right) = 2
$$

$$
f_{alias} = \vert 1800 - 2 \times 1000 \vert = \vert 1800 - 2000 \vert = 200 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Interpretation:** The 1800 Hz transient appears as a **200 Hz** signal to the relay — a completely fictitious frequency that could cause the impedance algorithm to underreach or misoperate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Solution:** Insert a **second-order anti-aliasing low-pass filter** (Butterworth, cutoff ~400 Hz) **before** the ADC to suppress any frequency content above $f_s/2 = 500$ Hz.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sampling Rate Selection for Common Relaying Tasks

| Protection Function | $h_{max}$ Required | $f_{max}$ (Hz) | $f_N$ (Hz) | Recommended $f_s$ (Hz) | Samples/Cycle |
|--------------------|-------------------:|---------------:|-----------:|----------------------:|-------------:|
| Overcurrent (fundamental only) | 1 | 50 | 100 | 800 | 16 |
| Distance Protection | 13 | 650 | 1300 | 1600 | 32 |
| Harmonic Restraint (Transformer) | 5 | 250 | 500 | 12800 | 256 (wide-band DFR) |
| Traveling-Wave Locator | ~150 | 7500 | 15000 | 1,000,000 | — |
| PMU Synchrophasor | 50 | 2500 | 5000 | 4800 (96/cycle) | 96 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Analog-to-Digital Conversion Chain in a Numerical Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CT Secondary Current] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    C[VT Secondary Voltage] --> B
    B --> D[Sample and Hold S/H Circuit]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    E --> F[Microprocessor DSP FPGA]
    F --> G[Trip Decision Logic]
    G --> H[Trip Signal to Circuit Breaker]
    I[Sampling Clock f_s] --> D
    I --> E
    J[Anti-Aliasing Cutoff less than f_s divided by 2] -.-> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **CT/VT** scales the primary quantities to safe levels (e.g., 5 A or 110 V).
- The **anti-aliasing LPF** enforces the bandlimitation assumption of the Sampling Theorem.
- The **Sample-and-Hold** freezes the analog value during ADC conversion.
- The **ADC** quantizes the held value into a binary code (e.g., 16-bit).
- The **DSP/microprocessor** runs the protection algorithm on the discrete samples.
- The **sampling clock** (often derived from a crystal oscillator or GPS-disciplined source) is the heart of the system — its frequency **is** $f_s$.

## 4.2 Time-Domain View of Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Signal x of t sinusoid 50 Hz] --> B[Impulse Train p of t at f_s]
    B --> C[Multiplication in Time Domain]
    A --> C
    C --> D[Sampled Signal x_s of t: sequence of weighted impulses]
    D --> E[Ideal Low-Pass Filter Cutoff f_s over 2]
    E --> F[Reconstructed Continuous Signal x of t]
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Spectral Replication (Sampled Spectrum)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Spectrum X of f baseband 0 to f_max] --> B[Replication at 0]
    A --> C[Replication at plus f_s]
    A --> D[Replication at minus f_s]
    A --> E[Replication at plus 2 f_s]
    A --> F[Replication at minus 2 f_s]
    G{Non overlapping if f_s greater than 2 f_max} -->|Yes| H[Ideal LPF recovers x of t]
    G -->|No| I[Overlap = Aliasing: information lost]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing $f_s$ for a Numerical Relay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify highest harmonic order h_max of interest] --> B[Compute f_max = h_max times f_fundamental]
    B --> C[Compute Nyquist rate f_N = 2 times f_max]
    C --> D[Add 20 to 30 percent engineering margin]
    D --> E[Select nearest power of 2 samples per cycle: 16 32 64 128]
    E --> F[Verify anti-aliasing filter design with cutoff less than f_s over 2]
    F --> G[Validate by FFT test on known waveform]
    G --> H[End: Sampling frequency f_s finalized]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**State and explain the Sampling Theorem. Define Nyquist rate.**

**Model Answer:**
The Sampling Theorem states that a continuous-time, bandlimited signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz can be reconstructed exactly from its discrete samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The minimum sampling rate $f_N = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**. The term $\frac{f_s}{2}$ is the **Nyquist frequency** (or folding frequency). In a numerical relay, this principle ensures that the analog CT/VT signals are captured without loss of information before the protection algorithm processes them. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**What is aliasing? How is it prevented in a digital relay?**

**Model Answer:**
Aliasing is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of the input signal (above $f_s/2$) appear as false low-frequency components in the sampled data, causing irreversible distortion. In a digital relay, aliasing is prevented by placing an **analog anti-aliasing low-pass filter** (typically 2nd-order Butterworth) before the ADC, with its cutoff frequency set just below $f_s/2$. This ensures the input is truly bandlimited to satisfy the Sampling Theorem. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of a sampled signal $x_s(t)$ in the frequency domain, starting from the impulse train representation. Clearly state the Sampling Theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 Hz transmission line is protected by a numerical distance relay that needs to faithfully capture harmonics up to the 11th order. The relay ADC operates at 32 samples per cycle. Determine:
  (i) The sampling frequency and the sampling period.
  (ii) The Nyquist rate and the highest faithfully recoverable harmonic.
  (iii) Whether the chosen $f_s$ meets the Nyquist criterion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
1. Define the impulse train: $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$ where $T_s = 1/f_s$.
2. Express the sampled signal: $x_s(t) = x(t) p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s)$.
3. Use the Fourier series expansion: $p(t) = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}$.
4. Take the Fourier Transform — multiplication in time $\Rightarrow$ convolution in frequency:

$$
X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)
$$

5. **Statement of Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_{max}$ can be reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

6. If the replicas do not overlap (i.e., $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$), an ideal LPF of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_s/2$ isolates the central replica and recovers $x(t)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**
**(i)** Sampling frequency and period:

$$
f_s = 32 \times 50 = 1600 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{1600} = 625 \,\mu s
$$

**(ii)** Highest harmonic of interest:

$$
f_{max} = 11 \times 50 = 550 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
f_N = 2 \times 550 = 1100 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(iii)** Nyquist check:

$$
f_s = 1600 \text{ Hz} \;\;>\;\; f_N = 1100 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark
$$

The chosen sampling rate **satisfies** the Nyquist criterion with a healthy margin. The highest **reliably** recoverable harmonic is 31st (since $1600/50 = 32$, with Nyquist at 16th harmonic, the relay can comfortably handle the 11th-order requirement). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B: **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the signal conditioning and sampling chain in a numerical relay. Indicate where the anti-aliasing filter is placed and justify its need. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital fault recorder (DFR) samples at 3.2 kHz. A fault on the protected line produces a dominant 2.8 kHz traveling-wave component. Calculate the frequency at which this component will appear in the recorded data. Comment on the consequences. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
1. The signal chain consists of: **CT/VT → Anti-aliasing LPF → Sample-and-Hold → ADC → DSP/Microprocessor**.
2. The CT/VT steps down primary current/voltage to standard low-level analogs.
3. The **anti-aliasing LPF** (2nd-order Butterworth, cutoff $f_c < f_s/2$) is the **first active block** after the transducers. Its role is to remove any frequency content above $f_s/2$ to satisfy the bandlimitation assumption of the Sampling Theorem.
4. The Sample-and-Hold (S/H) freezes the instantaneous analog value during the ADC conversion window to avoid aperture error.
5. The ADC quantizes the held value into a binary word (12-bit or 16-bit typical).
6. The DSP runs the relaying algorithm (Fourier DFT, differential, impedance) on the digital sample stream.
7. **Justification for anti-aliasing filter:** Without it, any signal component above $f_s/2$ folds back into the 0–$f_s/2$ band and corrupts the computed phasor, leading to relay misoperation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**
1. Given: $f_s = 3200$ Hz, $f_{actual} = 2800$ Hz.
2. Check Nyquist: $f_s/2 = 1600$ Hz. Since $2800 \text{ Hz} > 1600$ Hz, **aliasing occurs**.
3. Find integer $k$ such that $\vert f - k f_s \vert < f_s/2$:

$$
k = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{2800}{3200}\right) = 1
$$

$$
f_{alias} = \vert 2800 - 1 \times 3200 \vert = 400 \text{ Hz}
$$

4. **Consequences:**
   - The 2.8 kHz traveling wave appears as a **400 Hz** component to the DFR.
   - The recorded waveform will be **completely wrong** — a high-frequency transient will be misinterpreted as a low-frequency oscillation.
   - Fault location algorithms based on wavefront detection will yield **incorrect distance estimates**.
   - Post-fault analysis (forensic evidence) will be **misleading**.
5. **Remediation:** The DFR requires an anti-aliasing filter with cutoff below 1.6 kHz, OR a higher sampling rate (e.g., $\geq 5.6$ kHz) to capture 2.8 kHz faithfully. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the sampling clock** in block diagrams — this costs 1 mark. Always show $f_s$ driving the S/H and ADC.
> - In aliasing problems, students often use the **wrong $k$** (e.g., $k = 0$ instead of the nearest integer). Always pick $k$ = nearest integer to $f/f_s$.
> - The Nyquist inequality is **$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$**, **not** $f_s \geq f_{max}$. Confusing these is the #1 error in numerical problems.
> - The factor $1/T_s$ (or equivalently $f_s$) in the spectrum formula is a common source of mistakes — many textbooks absorb it into the impulse-train definition. State your convention clearly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Sampling Theorem**

- [x] The Sampling Theorem is the **bridge** between analog CT/VT signals and the digital domain of numerical relays.
- [x] **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — strict inequality preferred.
- [x] **Nyquist rate** $f_N = 2 f_{max}$; **Nyquist frequency** (folding) $= f_s/2$.
- [x] Sampling period $T_s = 1/f_s$.
- [x] Sampled signal: $x_s(t) = \sum x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$.
- [x] Sampled spectrum: $X_s(f) = f_s \sum X(f - k f_s)$ — **replicated** at every $kf_s$.
- [x] **Aliasing** occurs when $f_s < 2 f_{max}$ — spectral replicas overlap, information lost irretrievably.
- [x] **Anti-aliasing filter** is mandatory: analog LPF placed **before** the S/H and ADC.
- [x] **Reconstruction** uses an ideal LPF of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_s/2$.
- [x] For a 50 Hz system, common samples/cycle choices: **16, 32, 64, 128**.
- [x] Higher protection speed / harmonic content / traveling-wave applications require **higher $f_s$**.
- [x] Typical values: distance relay 32 samples/cycle, PMU 48–240, traveling-wave 1–10 MHz.
- [x] The theorem assumes the signal is **strictly bandlimited** — a condition the anti-aliasing filter must enforce in practice.
- [x] **Aliasing formula:** $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ with $k$ = nearest integer to $f/f_s$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aliasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Aliasing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Digital Protection of Power Systems**, the relaying algorithm operates on **discrete-time samples** of the analog current and voltage waveforms obtained from Current Transformers (CTs) and Potential Transformers (PTs). The process of converting a continuous-time (analog) signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time sequence $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ is called **sampling**, where $T_s$ is the sampling interval and $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the sampling frequency. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aliasing (Definition):** Aliasing is the phenomenon wherein two or more distinct continuous-time sinusoids become **indistinguishable** (i.e., they map to the **same** discrete-time sequence) after being sampled at a given sampling frequency $f_s$. In the frequency domain, frequency components separated by integer multiples of the sampling frequency $f_s$ overlap and superimpose, producing a single spurious low-frequency component at the output of the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). Mathematically, if a continuous sinusoid $\cos(2\pi f t)$ is sampled at $f_s$, the resulting samples are identical to those of $\cos(2\pi (f + k f_s) t)$ for any integer $k$. This ambiguity is the essence of aliasing. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Stroboscopic Wheel Effect** > Imagine a wagon wheel rotating at a certain speed in a movie. When filmed with a camera that captures fewer than **two frames per revolution**, the wheel appears to be **rotating backward or standing still**, even though it is actually spinning forward at high speed. The camera's "sampling" rate is too slow to resolve the true motion. What you see on screen is the **alias** of the true motion — a false, low-frequency replica. > > Applied to power systems: if a **power system relay** samples a $50\,\text{Hz}$ waveform too slowly, the relay's algorithm may "see" a fictitious low-frequency component (e.g., $5\,\text{Hz}$) instead of the true $50\,\text{Hz}$ signal, leading to **incorrect phasor estimation, wrong impedance calculation, and catastrophic relay misoperation**. ## 1.3 Why Aliasing is Critical in Power System Protection | Power System Signal | Typical Frequency | Consequence of Aliasing | |---|---|---| | Fundamental power frequency | **50 Hz** (India/Europe) / 60 Hz (USA) | Wrong magnitude & phase | | Harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th...) | 150, 250, 350 Hz ... | They may **fold back** as false fundamentals | | Fault-induced transients | 100 Hz – 5 kHz | Misinterpreted as inrush or swing | | CT saturation noise | High-frequency | Injects false low-frequency content | The integrity of every digital distance relay, differential relay, and overcurrent relay depends on **faithful, alias-free sampling**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — The Sampling Theorem The entire theory of aliasing rests upon the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be **uniquely reconstructed** from its samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ **if and only if** the sampling frequency satisfies: > $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$ > The threshold $f_N = f_s / 2$ is called the **Nyquist Frequency** (or Folding Frequency). The "if" part guarantees no aliasing; the "only if" part means violating this bound **guarantees** aliasing. ## 2.2 The Aliasing Condition Consider two sinusoids of frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ sampled at $f_s$. They produce **identical sample sets** if and only if: $$f_1 - f_2 = k \cdot f_s \quad \text{where } k \in \mathbb{Z} \setminus \{0\}$$ Equivalently, two frequencies are **aliases** of each other under sampling at $f_s$ if: $$f_1 \equiv f_2 \pmod{f_s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Folding Rule:** When $f > f_N$ (i.e., the input frequency lies in the upper half of the spectrum), the aliased frequency $f_a$ appears in the **baseband** $[0, f_N]$ according to: > $$f_a = \vert f - f_s \vert \quad \text{(for the first fold)}$$ > For higher folds: $f_a = \vert f - k \cdot f_s \vert$ with $k$ chosen so that $0 \leq f_a \leq f_N$. ## 2.3 The Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF) Since perfect bandlimiting of physical power system signals is impossible, an **Analog Low-Pass Filter (Anti-Aliasing Filter)** is inserted **before the ADC**. Its role is to attenuate all frequency components above $f_N$ to below the ADC's quantization noise floor. > [!WARNING] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** In numerical relay design, the **Anti-Aliasing Filter cutoff frequency** is chosen slightly **below** $f_N$ (typically $0.4 f_s$ to $0.45 f_s$) to account for the non-ideal roll-off of practical filters (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Bessel). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | Samples per second (Hz) | | 2 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | Highest uniquely recoverable frequency | | 3 | Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | No-aliasing condition | | 4 | Aliasing Condition | $f_1 - f_2 = k f_s$, $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Two freqs become indistinguishable | | 5 | Folding (First) | $f_a = \vert f - f_s \vert$ | Maps $f$ to baseband | | 6 | Folding (General) | $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$, $0 \leq f_a \leq f_N$ | Repeated folding | | 7 | Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff | $f_c \approx 0.4\, f_s$ to $0.45\, f_s$ | Practical design rule | | 8 | No. of Samples per Cycle | $N = f_s / f_0$ | For 50 Hz fundamental | | 9 | Spectral Periodicity | $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$ | Sampled spectrum is periodic | | 10 | Reconstruction (Ideal LPF) | $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | Whittaker–Shannon formula | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern **numerical relays** (e.g., ABB REL670, Siemens SIPROTEC, GE Multilin UR-series), aliasing is mitigated by a combination of: 1. **Hardware AAF** (analog 2nd/4th-order Butterworth LPF) with cutoff near 500 Hz for 1 kHz sampling. 2. **Oversampling** at $f_s$ much higher than 2× fundamental (e.g., $f_s = 1.92\,\text{kHz}$ for 50 Hz → 38.4 samples/cycle). 3. **Digital decimation filtering** in DSP stages. Failing any one of these results in **incorrect impedance reach, mis-coordination, and protection blind spots** — all of which are favorite KTU examination scenarios. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalence of Sampled Sinusoids **Statement to Prove:** Two sinusoids $x_1(t) = \cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ and $x_2(t) = \cos(2\pi f_2 t)$ produce **identical sample sequences** when sampled at $f_s$ if and only if $f_1 - f_2 = k f_s$ for some non-zero integer $k$. **Step-by-Step Proof:** **Step 1:** Sample both signals at $t = n T_s$ where $T_s = 1/f_s$ and $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. $$x_1[n] = \cos\!\left(2\pi f_1 \cdot \frac{n}{f_s}\right) = \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi f_1 n}{f_s}\right)$$ $$x_2[n] = \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi f_2 n}{f_s}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Use the periodicity of cosine: $\cos(\theta + 2\pi k) = \cos(\theta)$ for any integer $k$. **Step 3:** For $x_1[n] = x_2[n]$ for **all** $n$, we need the arguments to differ by integer multiples of $2\pi$: $$\frac{2\pi f_1 n}{f_s} - \frac{2\pi f_2 n}{f_s} = 2\pi k n \quad \text{for all } n$$ **Step 4:** Cancel $2\pi n$ from both sides: $$\frac{f_1 - f_2}{f_s} = k$$ **Step 5:** Therefore: $$f_1 - f_2 = k \cdot f_s, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z} \setminus \{0\}$$ This proves the aliasing condition. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Folding Frequency Computation **Problem Setup:** A power system signal contains a $5^{\text{th}}$ harmonic at $f = 250\,\text{Hz}$ (for a $50\,\text{Hz}$ system). The relay samples at $f_s = 400\,\text{Hz}$. Determine the aliased frequency observed by the relay. **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute the Nyquist frequency. $$f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{400}{2} = 200\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 2:** Check whether $f > f_N$. $$f = 250\,\text{Hz} > 200\,\text{Hz} = f_N \quad \Rightarrow \text{Aliasing will occur.}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the aliased (folded) frequency using the folding formula. The signal at $250\,\text{Hz}$ lies in the second Nyquist zone, so it folds back once: $$f_a = \vert f - f_s \vert = \vert 250 - 400 \vert = 150\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 4:** Verify $0 \leq f_a \leq f_N$: $$0 \leq 150 \leq 200 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5:** Interpretation. The 5th harmonic at $250\,\text{Hz}$ **appears** to the relay as a $150\,\text{Hz}$ signal, which is the **3rd harmonic** of the $50\,\text{Hz}$ fundamental. This is a classic aliasing scenario in numerical protection. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Spectral Replication & Overlap (Theoretical Justification) The sampled signal's spectrum $X_s(f)$ is a periodic replica of the original spectrum $X(f)$ scaled by $1/T_s$: $$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$ **Interpretation of Each Term:** - $X(f - k f_s)$ = The original spectrum shifted by integer multiples of $f_s$. - If the original bandwidth is $B = f_{max}$ and $f_s \geq 2B$, the shifted replicas do **not overlap** — perfect reconstruction is possible. - If $f_s < 2B$, the shifted replicas **overlap (alias)**, summing constructively or destructively in the baseband $[-f_N, f_N]$, and the original spectrum **cannot be recovered** by any LPF. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Examples for KTU > [!NOTE] > **System:** $f_0 = 50\,\text{Hz}$, $f_s = 600\,\text{Hz}$, $f_N = 300\,\text{Hz}$. Tabulate the aliasing behavior for various harmonics. | Harmonic Order | True Frequency $f$ (Hz) | Nyquist Zone | Aliased Frequency $f_a$ (Hz) | Identification by Relay | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 (Fundamental) | 50 | 1 | $\vert 50 - 0 \vert = 50$ | True 50 Hz ✓ | | 3 | 150 | 1 | $\vert 150 - 0 \vert = 150$ | True 150 Hz ✓ | | 5 | 250 | 1 | $\vert 250 - 0 \vert = 250$ | True 250 Hz ✓ | | 7 | 350 | 2 | $\vert 350 - 600 \vert = 250$ | **Misread as 250 Hz** ✗ | | 9 | 450 | 2 | $\vert 450 - 600 \vert = 150$ | **Misread as 150 Hz** ✗ | | 11 | 550 | 2 | $\vert 550 - 600 \vert = 50$ | **Misread as 50 Hz fundamental!** ⚠ | | 13 | 650 | 3 | $\vert 650 - 600 \vert = 50$ | **Misread as 50 Hz fundamental!** ⚠ | > [!WARNING] > **Dangerous Case:** The 11th and 13th harmonics, when sampled at $f_s = 600\,\text{Hz}$, both fold back onto the **50 Hz fundamental frequency**. A distance relay using such a sampling rate without an AAF would compute the **wrong impedance**, potentially failing to trip for a real fault or tripping for a non-existent one. ## 3.5 Derivation 4: Minimum Sampling Rate Calculation **Problem:** For a numerical distance relay, the highest frequency of interest (including up to the 7th harmonic) is $f_{max} = 7 \times 50 = 350\,\text{Hz}$. Compute the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. **Step 1:** Apply the Nyquist criterion strictly. $$f_s^{min} = 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 350 = 700\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the practical engineering rule (oversampling by 10–20%): $$f_s^{practical} = 1.15 \times 700 = 805\,\text{Hz} \approx 800\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 3:** Number of samples per fundamental cycle: $$N = \frac{f_s}{f_0} = \frac{800}{50} = 16 \text{ samples/cycle}$$ This is consistent with industry-standard numerical relay design. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Signal Chain in a Numerical Relay (Where Aliasing Can Occur) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Power System
50 Hz Voltage and Current] --> A2[CT and PT
Instrument Transformers] A2 --> A3[Analog Anti-Aliasing
Filter LPF] A3 --> A4[Sampler
and Hold] A4 --> A5[ADC
Analog to Digital Converter] A5 --> A6[DSP
Phasor Estimation] A6 --> A7[Trip Logic
Relay Decision] A3 -. "Removes frequencies
above fN" .-> A4 A4 -. "Sampling at fs" .-> A5 A5 -. "Quantization" .-> A6 ``` ## 4.2 Diagram: Aliasing Mechanism in Frequency Domain ```mermaid flowchart TD B1[Continuous Spectrum X of f
Centered at 0 with Bandwidth B] --> B2{Sampling at fs} B2 -- "fs greater than 2B" --> B3[Replicas Separated
No Overlap] B2 -- "fs less than 2B" --> B4[Replicas Overlap
ALIASING Occurs] B3 --> B5[LPF Recovery
Original Signal Restored] B4 --> B6[Spurious Low Frequency
Distorted Signal] ``` ## 4.3 Diagram: Frequency Folding Across Nyquist Zones ```mermaid flowchart LR Z0[Zone 0
0 to fN
Baseband] Z1[Zone 1
fN to 2fN
Folds to Baseband] Z2[Zone 2
2fN to 3fN
Folds Twice] Z3[Zone 3
3fN to 4fN
Folds Thrice] Z1 --> F1[Aliased Frequency fa
equals 2fN minus f] Z2 --> F2[Aliased Frequency fa
equals f minus 2fN] Z3 --> F3[Aliased Frequency fa
equals 4fN minus f] F1 --> Z0 F2 --> Z0 F3 --> Z0 ``` ## 4.4 Diagram: Anti-Aliasing Filter Frequency Response ```mermaid flowchart LR C1[Input Spectrum
With Components
Beyond fN] --> C2[Anti-Aliasing LPF
Cutoff at 0.4 to 0.45 fs] C2 --> C3[Filtered Spectrum
Bandlimited Below fN] C3 --> C4[ADC Sampler
No Aliasing] C4 --> C5[Clean Digital Output] C1 -. "High Freq Noise
Beyond fN" .-> C2 C2 -. "Attenuation
Greater than 40 dB" .-> C3 ``` ## 4.5 Diagram: Effect of Aliasing on a Sampled Sinusoid ```mermaid flowchart TD D1[True Signal
Cosine 250 Hz] --> D2[Sampler at fs equals 400 Hz] D2 --> D3[Digital Samples] D3 --> D4[What Relay Sees
150 Hz Cosine] D4 --> D5[WRONG PHASOR
WRONG IMPEDANCE] D1 -. "True 250 Hz" .-> D2 D2 -. "Below Nyquist" .-> D3 D3 -. "Folding Effect" .-> D4 D4 -. "Protection Failure" .-> D5 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define aliasing. State the Nyquist criterion to avoid aliasing.** **Model Answer:** > **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which two or more distinct continuous-time signals, when sampled at a given sampling frequency $f_s$, produce **identical discrete-time sample sequences** and hence become **indistinguishable** at the output of the sampler. > > **Nyquist Criterion:** A bandlimited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples without aliasing **if and only if**: > $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$ > where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency. The threshold $f_N = f_s / 2$ is called the **Nyquist frequency**. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the role of an anti-aliasing filter in a numerical relay? Mention the typical cut-off frequency.** **Model Answer:** > The **anti-aliasing filter (AAF)** is an analog low-pass filter placed **before the ADC** in a numerical relay. Its role is to **bandlimit** the incoming CT/PT signal so that all frequency components above the Nyquist frequency $f_N = f_s/2$ are attenuated to a level below the ADC's quantization noise floor. This prevents high-frequency components from "folding back" into the baseband and corrupting the phasor estimation. > > **Typical cut-off frequency:** $f_c \approx 0.4 f_s$ to $0.45 f_s$ (i.e., slightly below $f_N$ to allow for non-ideal filter roll-off). > > **Common filter types used:** 2nd-order or 4th-order **Butterworth** filters (maximally flat passband) or **Bessel** filters (linear phase, preserving waveform shape). --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **A numerical distance relay samples the power system current at $f_s = 720\,\text{Hz}$. The system fundamental frequency is $50\,\text{Hz}$.** **(a)** [7 Marks] Compute the Nyquist frequency. Determine which harmonic frequencies will alias into the baseband, and find the alias frequency for the 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th, and 13th harmonics. **(b)** [7 Marks] Suggest a suitable sampling frequency to avoid aliasing up to the 13th harmonic, with a 15% safety margin. Justify the choice and state the number of samples per cycle. --- #### **Model Solution to A(a):** **Step 1 — Nyquist Frequency:** $$f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{720}{2} = 360\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 2 — Compute harmonic frequencies:** | Harmonic | $f = n \cdot 50$ (Hz) | Relation to $f_N$ | Aliasing? | |---|---|---|---| | 5th | 250 | $f < f_N$ | No | | 7th | 350 | $f < f_N$ | No | | 9th | 450 | $f > f_N$ | **Yes** | | 11th | 550 | $f > f_N$ | **Yes** | | 13th | 650 | $f > f_N$ | **Yes** | **Step 3 — Compute alias frequencies** using $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ with $k$ chosen so that $0 \leq f_a \leq f_N$: - **9th harmonic (450 Hz):** $k = 1$, $f_a = \vert 450 - 720 \vert = 270\,\text{Hz}$ - **11th harmonic (550 Hz):** $k = 1$, $f_a = \vert 550 - 720 \vert = 170\,\text{Hz}$ - **13th harmonic (650 Hz):** $k = 1$, $f_a = \vert 650 - 720 \vert = 70\,\text{Hz}$ **Result:** The 9th harmonic appears as 270 Hz, the 11th as 170 Hz, and the 13th as 70 Hz — a near-fundamental frequency that could severely mislead the relay. --- #### **Model Solution to A(b):** **Step 1 — Maximum frequency of interest:** $$f_{max} = 13 \times 50 = 650\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 2 — Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist):** $$f_s^{min} = 2 \times 650 = 1300\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 3 — Apply 15% safety margin:** $$f_s = 1.15 \times 1300 = 1495\,\text{Hz} \approx 1500\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 4 — Number of samples per cycle:** $$N = \frac{f_s}{f_0} = \frac{1500}{50} = 30 \text{ samples/cycle}$$ **Justification:** A sampling rate of $f_s = 1.5\,\text{kHz}$ gives a Nyquist frequency of $750\,\text{Hz} > 650\,\text{Hz}$, so all harmonics up to the 13th are faithfully captured. The 15% margin accounts for analog filter roll-off imperfections and provides headroom for higher-order harmonic leakage. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice** **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the mathematical condition for two sinusoids of frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ to be **aliases** of each other under uniform sampling at $f_s$. Illustrate with one numerical example where a $350\,\text{Hz}$ component aliases to $50\,\text{Hz}$ at a specific $f_s$. **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain, with a labeled block diagram, the **complete signal chain** of a numerical relay from the CT secondary to the trip coil, identifying the precise location where aliasing occurs and how it is mitigated. --- #### **Model Solution to B(a):** **Derivation:** Consider two continuous-time sinusoids: $$x_1(t) = \cos(2\pi f_1 t), \quad x_2(t) = \cos(2\pi f_2 t)$$ Sampled uniformly at $f_s$ (i.e., $t = n/f_s$): $$x_1[n] = \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi f_1 n}{f_s}\right), \quad x_2[n] = \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi f_2 n}{f_s}\right)$$ For the sample sequences to be **identical** for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$, the angular arguments must differ by integer multiples of $2\pi n$: $$\frac{2\pi (f_1 - f_2) n}{f_s} = 2\pi k n \quad \forall n$$ Cancelling $2\pi n$: $$\boxed{f_1 - f_2 = k f_s, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z} \setminus \{0\}}$$ **Numerical Illustration:** To make $f_2 = 50\,\text{Hz}$ an alias of $f_1 = 350\,\text{Hz}$: $$350 - 50 = 300 = k f_s$$ Choosing $k = 1$: $f_s = 300\,\text{Hz}$. Verify: $f_N = 150\,\text{Hz}$, and $f_a = \vert 350 - 300 \vert = 50\,\text{Hz}$. ✓ --- #### **Model Solution to B(b):** **Complete Signal Chain (Textual Block Diagram):** ``` [Power System Primary: 50 Hz, 11 kV / 110 V, 1000 A / 5 A] ↓ [CT and PT Secondary] (Step down current and voltage to measurable levels) ↓ [Surge Protection and Isolation] (Varistors, gas discharge tubes) ↓ [Analog Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)] (2nd or 4th order Butterworth LPF, cutoff ~ 0.4 fs) ↓ ← ★ ALIASING MITIGATION HAPPENS HERE ★ [Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit] (Captures instantaneous amplitude at sampling instants) ↓ [Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)] (Quantizes samples to binary, e.g., 16-bit) ↓ ← ★ ALIASING OCCURS IF AAF FAILS ★ [Digital Signal Processor (DSP)] (FFT, DFT, phasor estimation, impedance calculation) ↓ [Protection Algorithm / Decision Logic] (Compares impedance with relay characteristic) ↓ [Trip Coil / Circuit Breaker] ``` **Aliasing Location:** Aliasing occurs at the **S/H and ADC stage** if any frequency component beyond $f_N$ enters the sampler.
