The **AAF placed before the S/H** is the only safeguard against it because once aliased, the corruption is **irreversible** by digital filtering. **Mitigation Methods:** 1. **Analog LPF (AAF)** with cutoff at $\sim 0.4 f_s$ and stopband attenuation $> 40\,\text{dB}$. 2. **Oversampling**: Choose $f_s$ much higher than $2 f_{max}$ (typical: 16 to 64 samples per cycle). 3. **Digital decimation filter** after oversampling to enhance effective resolution. 4. **Sigma-Delta ADC** with inherent noise shaping that relaxes AAF requirements. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing sampling rate with Nyquist rate**: $f_s$ is the sampling rate; $2 f_{max}$ is the Nyquist rate. Many students interchange them. > 2. **Forgetting the $| \cdot |$ in folding formula**: $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$, not just $f - k f_s$. > 3. **Not drawing/mentioning the AAF** in block diagrams — examiners expect it explicitly labeled. > 4. **Confusing aliasing with quantization** — they are **different** phenomena. Aliasing is a **sampling** issue; quantization is an **ADC resolution** issue. > 5. **Ignoring the practical safety margin** — using exactly $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ in real designs is **dangerous**; always apply 10–20% oversampling. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Aliasing =** two different analog frequencies become **indistinguishable** after sampling. - **Root cause =** violation of the **Nyquist criterion** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. - **Aliasing condition:** $f_1 - f_2 = k f_s$, $k \in \mathbb{Z} \setminus \{0\}$. - **Folding formula:** $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$, with $k$ chosen so that $0 \leq f_a \leq f_N$. - **Nyquist frequency:** $f_N = f_s / 2$. All content above $f_N$ aliases. - **Anti-aliasing filter (AAF):** analog LPF **before the ADC**, cutoff $\approx 0.4\, f_s$ to $0.45\, f_s$. - **In power systems**, the 50 Hz fundamental and its harmonics (especially 3rd, 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th) are the primary signals of interest. - **Typical relay sampling rate:** 16 to 64 samples per 50 Hz cycle (i.e., $f_s = 800\,\text{Hz}$ to $3.2\,\text{kHz}$). - **Aliasing is irreversible**: once it happens, no DSP algorithm can recover the original signal. - **Common filter choices for AAF:** Butterworth (flat passband) or Bessel (linear phase). - **Always state units** (Hz, samples/cycle) and the **safety margin** in numerical answers. - **KTU favourite problem types:** (i) compute $f_N$ and find which harmonics alias, (ii) determine minimum $f_s$ for a given harmonic range, (iii) find $f_s$ such that a specific frequency aliases to a given value. - **Block diagram must always show:** CT/PT → AAF → S/H → ADC → DSP → Trip Logic.

topics.title: Sample and Hold Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sample and Hold Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit** is an essential analog interface sub-system used at the front-end of the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) in a digital protective relay. Its primary function is to **capture the instantaneous amplitude of a continuously varying analog input signal (such as a post-fault current or voltage derived from CTs/PTs) at a precisely defined instant, and then maintain (hold) that amplitude as a stable, constant DC level** for the duration required by the ADC to perform its quantization.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET631 - Digital Protection of Power Systems, Module 3), the S/H circuit acts as the **temporal bridge** between the continuously time-varying power system waveforms and the discrete-time processing engine of the numerical relay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** The Sample and Hold circuit is a fundamental building block under the broader topic of *Signal Conditioning and Data Acquisition* for digital/numerical relays. Mastery of its operating modes, timing parameters, and error sources is a frequent **ESE Module Test (ESE-III)** question pattern.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to measure the length of a fast-moving, slithering snake using a regular ruler. By the time your eyes focus and your brain processes the reading, the snake has already moved — your measurement is wrong.

**What is the solution?** You take a **high-speed camera flash photograph** of the snake. The flash freezes the snake for a split second, and now you have a perfectly still image to measure at your own pace.

The **Sample and Hold circuit is the electronic equivalent of that camera flash**:
- The **"flash"** is the **sampling switch** closing for a tiny duration.
- The **"frozen image"** is the **electrical charge** stored on a capacitor.
- The **"measurement ruler"** is the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** that digitizes this frozen level.

## 1.3 Why the S/H Circuit is Indispensable in Digital Relays

During normal power system operation and especially during faults, the relay's ADC must digitize rapidly changing waveforms (e.g., a 50 Hz fault current at **50 cycles/second = 20 ms/cycle**). The ADC requires a finite, non-zero **conversion time** ($t_{ADC}$). If the input is allowed to change during this conversion window, the output code becomes ambiguous — a phenomenon known as **aperture error**.

The S/H circuit solves this by holding the input rock-steady during conversion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (KTU Board Exam Favorites):**
> - **Sampling Rate for 50 Hz systems:** At least **12 to 32 samples per cycle** (600 Hz to 1.6 kHz) as per IEC 60255-118-1.
> - **Aperture Time:** Typically in the range of **nanoseconds (ns)** for modern S/H ICs like LF398, AD582.
> - **Hold Capacitor:** Generally in the range of **100 pF to 10 nF**.

## 1.4 The Two Fundamental Operating Modes

| Mode | Switch State | Output Behavior |
|------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Sample (Track) Mode** | Switch **CLOSED** | Output **follows** the input signal $V_{in}$ in real-time |
| **Hold Mode** | Switch **OPEN** | Output **remains constant** at the last sampled value $V_o = V_{in}(t_{sample})$ |

> [!TIP]
> The terms **Sample Mode** and **Track Mode** are used **interchangeably** in most KTU textbooks. However, strictly speaking, "Track" is preferred when the switch remains closed for multiple clock cycles, and "Sample" is reserved for very short acquisition pulses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic S/H Circuit

A textbook S/H circuit consists of **four fundamental sub-blocks**:

1. **Analog Sampling Switch** (typically a JFET, MOSFET, or CMOS transmission gate).
2. **Hold Capacitor ($C_H$)** — the analog memory element.
3. **High-Input-Impedance Buffer Amplifier** (e.g., op-amp in voltage follower configuration).
4. **Digital Control Logic** — generates the Sample/Hold control signal synchronized to the relay's sampling clock.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

### **Phase 1: Sample (Track) Phase**
- The control logic drives the **MOSFET gate HIGH**.
- The MOSFET channel becomes conductive, behaving as a low-value "on-resistance" $R_{ON}$ (typically 50–500 Ω).
- The hold capacitor $C_H$ charges (or discharges) through $R_{ON}$ toward the input voltage $V_{in}$.
- The op-amp buffer (configured as a voltage follower) presents a very high input impedance to $C_H$, preventing it from loading the source.
- Output: $V_{out}(t) = V_{in}(t)$.

### **Phase 2: Aperture / Hold-Command Transition**
- The control logic drives the **MOSFET gate LOW**.
- The MOSFET channel pinches off. The aperture delay ($t_{ap}$) is the small, finite time between the **command edge** and the **actual switch opening**.
- **Aperture uncertainty ($\Delta t_{ap}$)** is the **jitter** in this delay from sample to sample — a critical parameter for high-frequency applications.

### **Phase 3: Hold Phase**
- The switch is now an open circuit (off-resistance $R_{OFF} > 10^{10}$ Ω).
- The op-amp buffer draws negligible current from $C_H$.
- Ideally, the voltage across $C_H$ remains frozen: $V_o(t) = V_{in}(t_{sample}) = \text{constant}$.
- In reality, **leakage currents** (MOSFET off-state leakage, op-amp bias current, PCB leakage) slowly discharge $C_H$, causing **droop**.

### **Phase 4: Reset / Re-acquisition**
- The control logic re-asserts the sample command.
- The switch closes, and $C_H$ must slew from its previous hold value to track $V_{in}$ again.
- This is governed by the **acquisition time ($t_{acq}$)**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a must-memorize cheat sheet for KTU 2024 ESE Module-III examinations.**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Standard Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-----------|----------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | RC Charging Time Constant | $\tau = R_{ON} \cdot C_H$ | seconds (s) | Determines how fast $C_H$ tracks $V_{in}$ |
| 2 | Acquisition Time (to 0.1% accuracy) | $t_{acq} \approx 6.9 \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_H$ | seconds (s) | Time needed for $C_H$ to settle to within 0.1% of $V_{in}$ |
| 3 | Maximum Slew Rate of Input | $\left(\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right)_{max} = 2 \pi f \cdot V_{peak}$ | V/s | Dictates required sample rate |
| 4 | Aperture Error (voltage) | $\Delta V_{ap} = \left(\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right) \cdot \Delta t_{ap}$ | Volts (V) | Error due to aperture uncertainty |
| 5 | Droop Rate | $\frac{dV_o}{dt} = \frac{I_{leakage}}{C_H}$ | V/s | Voltage decay during hold mode |
| 6 | Hold Step / Pedestal Error | $V_{ped} = \frac{Q_{inj}}{C_H} = \frac{C_{GD} \cdot \Delta V_{gate}}{C_H}$ | Volts (V) | Error from gate-to-drain charge injection |
| 7 | Feedthrough Attenuation | $A_{FT} \approx \frac{1}{1 + j \omega R_{OFF} C_H}$ | dimensionless | How much input "leaks" into output during hold |
| 8 | Required Hold Time | $t_{hold} \geq t_{ADC}$ | seconds (s) | Must exceed ADC conversion time |
| 9 | Settling Time to ±½ LSB | $t_s = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln 2$ | seconds (s) | For an n-bit ADC: $n = \log_2(\text{FSR}/\text{LSB})$ |
| 10 | Hold Capacitor Selection (Rule of Thumb) | $C_H = \frac{I_{leakage} \cdot t_{hold}}{\Delta V_{max, droop}}$ | Farads (F) | Engineering design constraint |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety Note for Table Cells:** In the table above, all mathematical "divided by" or "absolute-value-like" operators have been written as fractions or parenthesized expressions to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe (`|`) syntax. Never write `|x|` inside a table cell — use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX or $(x)$ in plain text.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Power System Protection

- **Numerical Distance Relays:** The S/H circuit freezes the instantaneous voltage and current values so that the relay's **impedance calculation algorithm** (e.g., $Z = V/I$) can be performed on a stable snapshot of the post-fault phasor.
- **Differential Protection (Transformer/Busbar):** S/H enables simultaneous sampling of currents from multiple CTs — a hard requirement for true differential computation.
- **Travelling-Wave Based Protection (Ultra-High-Speed):** Modern IEC 60255-118-1 compliant relays sample at **1 MHz to 10 MHz**, demanding S/H apertures of **sub-100 picoseconds** ($10^{-12}$ s).
- **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs):** The S/H is the first stage in the synchrophasor data acquisition chain, gating all subsequent Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) computations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: The Acquisition Time ($t_{acq}$)

When the MOSFET switch closes, the hold capacitor $C_H$ charges from its previous hold voltage $V_o(t_0)$ toward the new input voltage $V_{in}$ through the on-resistance $R_{ON}$. The governing first-order differential equation is:

$$R_{ON} \cdot C_H \cdot \frac{dV_c(t)}{dt} + V_c(t) = V_{in}$$

This is a standard linear first-order ODE. We solve it with the initial condition $V_c(0) = V_o(t_0)$.

**Step 1: Recognize the homogeneous solution form.**

The time constant is:

$$\tau = R_{ON} \cdot C_H$$

**Step 2: Solve the ODE using the integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$.**

$$V_c(t) = V_{in} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) + V_o(t_0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3: Define the settling error at time $t$:**

$$\varepsilon(t) = \vert V_c(t) - V_{in} \vert = \vert V_o(t_0) - V_{in} \vert \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

Worst case occurs when $V_o(t_0) = 0$ and $V_{in} = V_{FS}$ (full-scale), so the maximum error is:

$$\varepsilon_{max}(t) = V_{FS} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 4: Impose the accuracy constraint.**

For the S/H to deliver a valid sample to the ADC, the error must be less than **½ LSB** of an $n$-bit ADC:

$$\varepsilon_{max}(t_{acq}) \leq \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}} \geq V_{FS} \cdot e^{-t_{acq}/\tau}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $t_{acq}$.**

Cancel $V_{FS}$ and take the natural logarithm:

$$-t_{acq}/\tau \geq -(n+1) \cdot \ln 2$$

$$\boxed{t_{acq} \geq (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \cdot (n+1) \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_H}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU Favorite):** For a 12-bit ADC with $R_{ON} = 200$ Ω and $C_H = 1$ nF, $t_{acq} \approx 0.693 \times 13 \times 200 \times 10^{-9} \approx 1.8 \ \mu s$. This is the **maximum time** the switch must remain closed before the ADC can begin conversion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: The Droop Rate During Hold Mode

During hold mode, the switch is open. However, three leakage mechanisms discharge $C_H$:

1. MOSFET off-state drain-source leakage current $I_{DS(off)}$.
2. Op-amp input bias current $I_{B+}$ and $I_{B-}$.
3. PCB surface leakage current $I_{PCB}$ (humidity dependent).

The total leakage is:

$$I_{leak} = I_{DS(off)} + I_{B+} + I_{B-} + I_{PCB}$$

**Step 1: Apply the capacitor voltage-current relationship.**

$$I_{leak} = -C_H \cdot \frac{dV_o}{dt}$$

(The negative sign indicates discharge.)

**Step 2: Solve for the droop rate.**

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$$

**Step 3: Integrate over the hold duration $t_{hold}$.**

$$V_o(t_{hold}) = V_o(0) - \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{C_H}$$

**Step 4: Apply the ½-LSB accuracy constraint.**

$$\frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{C_H} \leq \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the minimum required $C_H$.**

$$\boxed{C_H \geq \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold} \cdot 2^{n+1}}{V_{FS}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** If $I_{leak} = 1$ nA, $t_{hold} = 10 \ \mu s$, $n = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V, then $C_H \geq \frac{10^{-9} \times 10^{-5} \times 8192}{5} \approx 16.4$ pF. In practice, designers use 1 nF to 10 nF for **safety margin** against temperature variations in $I_{leak}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: The Aperture Error

The aperture uncertainty $\Delta t_{ap}$ is the random jitter in the time at which the switch actually opens, relative to the command edge. If the input is slewing at a rate $dV_{in}/dt$, the resulting voltage error is:

**Step 1: Linearize the input slew.**

For a sinusoidal input $V_{in}(t) = V_{peak} \sin(2 \pi f t)$, the maximum slew rate is:

$$\left(\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right)_{max} = 2 \pi f \cdot V_{peak}$$

**Step 2: Compute the peak aperture error.**

$$\Delta V_{ap} = \left(\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot \Delta t_{ap} = 2 \pi f \cdot V_{peak} \cdot \Delta t_{ap}$$

**Step 3: Impose the ½-LSB constraint for an $n$-bit ADC.**

$$2 \pi f \cdot V_{peak} \cdot \Delta t_{ap} \leq \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the maximum tolerable aperture uncertainty.**

$$\boxed{\Delta t_{ap} \leq \frac{1}{2^{n+1} \cdot \pi \cdot f}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Insight:** For a 12-bit ADC sampling a 50 Hz signal at full scale, $\Delta t_{ap} \leq \frac{1}{8192 \cdot \pi \cdot 50} \approx 777$ ns. For travelling-wave relays operating at MHz frequencies, the same 12-bit ADC demands $\Delta t_{ap} \leq 39$ picoseconds — pushing designers toward **integrated track-and-hold ASICs**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Error Matrix

| Error Source | Dominant Physical Cause | Mitigation Strategy |
|--------------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| **Pedestal / Hold Step** | Gate-to-drain charge injection ($C_{GD} \cdot \Delta V_{gate}$) | Use **dummy switch** topology; select CMOS switch with low $C_{GD}$ |
| **Droop** | Leakage currents discharging $C_H$ | Use **larger $C_H$**; use FET-input op-amp (e.g., OPA627) |
| **Aperture Uncertainty** | Jitter in control logic and switch turn-off | Use **low-jitter crystal clock**; minimize control-line crosstalk |
| **Feedthrough** | Capacitive coupling $C_{DS}$ through the open switch | Use **T-switch topology**; minimize $C_{DS}$ of switch |
| **Settling Error** | Finite $R_{ON} \cdot C_H$ time constant | Increase sample duration (reduces $f_s$); use faster switch |
| **Nonlinearity** | $R_{ON}$ dependence on $V_{in}$ | Use **closed-loop S/H** with Miller integrator |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic S/H Circuit Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional block-level architecture** of a generic Sample and Hold circuit, including all control signals, signal flow paths, and error-injection points.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_STAGE["Analog Input Stage from CT or PT Secondary"]
        analogIn[Analog Input V_in from CT or PT]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["Digital Control Logic Block"]
        clock[Master Sampling Clock f_s]
        ctrlLogic[S and H Control Logic]
    end

    subgraph CORE["Core S and H Sub-System"]
        analogSw[Analog Switch MOSFET or JFET]
        holdCap[Hold Capacitor C_H]
        bufAmp[High Impedance Buffer Amplifier Op-Amp Voltage Follower]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_STAGE["Output to ADC of Numerical Relay"]
        analogOut[Stable Held Voltage V_out]
        adcBlock[N bit Analog to Digital Converter]
    end

    subgraph ERROR_INJECTION["Major Error Injection Points"]
        err1[Error 1 Charge Injection at Switch Opening]
        err2[Error 2 Droop from Leakage Currents]
        err3[Error 3 Aperture Uncertainty from Clock Jitter]
        err4[Error 4 Settling Error from RC Time Constant]
    end

    clock --> ctrlLogic
    ctrlLogic -- Sample Hold Pulse --> analogSw
    analogIn --> analogSw
    analogSw -- Charges and Discharges --> holdCap
    holdCap --> bufAmp
    bufAmp --> analogOut
    analogOut --> adcBlock

    err1 -.->|Injects pedestal| holdCap
    err2 -.->|Discharges slowly| holdCap
    err3 -.->|Delays actual opening| analogSw
    err4 -.->|Limits slew rate| analogSw
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the diagram above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `analogIn`, `ctrlLogic`, `holdCap`) and prefixed with letters to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Node labels use only plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Operating State Machine

The S/H circuit has only two operating states, governed by the control logic. The transitions are clock-edge triggered.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sample_Mode
    Sample_Mode : STATE 1 Sample or Track Mode\nSwitch is CLOSED\nV_out follows V_in\nC_H charges through R_ON

    Hold_Mode : STATE 2 Hold Mode\nSwitch is OPEN\nV_out is FROZEN at last sample\nC_H loses charge via leakage

    Sample_Mode --> Hold_Mode : Sampling Clock Edge FALLING\nAperture Delay t_ap occurs
    Hold_Mode --> Sample_Mode : Sampling Clock Edge RISING\nAcquisition Time t_acq elapses

    Hold_Mode --> [*] : Power down or fault clear
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Closed-Loop (Miller Integrator) S/H Topology

The closed-loop S/H architecture uses **negative feedback** to drastically reduce pedestal error, droop, and feedthrough. It is the topology used in high-precision relay ICs like the **AD585**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLOSED_LOOP["Closed Loop S and H with Miller Integrator"]
        inNode[Input V_in]
        swA[Switch S1 Input Side]
        capH[Hold Capacitor C_H in Feedback Path]
        ampOA[Operational Amplifier]
        swB[Switch S2 Reset Path]
        gnd[Ground Reference]
        outNode[Output V_out to ADC]
    end

    inNode --> swA
    swA --> ampOA
    ampOA -- Negative Feedback --> capH
    capH --> swA
    swB -- Shorts C_H to reset --> capH
    swB --> gnd
    ampOA --> outNode
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why Closed-Loop?** Placing $C_H$ **inside the op-amp feedback loop** creates a **Miller-multiplied** effective capacitance of $C_H \cdot (1 + A_{OL})$, where $A_{OL}$ is the open-loop gain (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$). This effectively multiplies a 10 pF physical capacitor into a **virtual 1 mF capacitor**, eliminating droop and pedestal error simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"Define a Sample and Hold (S/H) circuit. Why is it essential in the data acquisition section of a numerical relay?"**

**Model Answer:**

A Sample and Hold circuit is an analog sub-system that **samples** the instantaneous value of a continuously varying input signal (such as a CT/PT-derived current or voltage) and **holds** that value as a constant DC level for the duration required by the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) to complete its conversion.

It is **essential** in a numerical relay because:
1. The ADC requires a finite, non-zero **conversion time**. If the input changes during this window, the digitized code becomes ambiguous (**aperture error**).
2. It enables **simultaneous sampling** of multiple analog channels (e.g., three-phase voltages and currents) for true phasor computation.
3. It acts as an **anti-aliasing reinforcement** by providing a flat input to the ADC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write only "it freezes the signal" and stop there. The KTU 2024 Scheme ESE-III evaluator awards full 3 marks only if you **explicitly link the answer to ADC conversion time and the numerical relay context**.

### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"List any four key performance specifications of a Sample and Hold circuit."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Acquisition Time ($t_{acq}$):** Time required for the hold capacitor to settle to within ½ LSB of the new input value.
2. **Aperture Time / Aperture Uncertainty ($t_{ap}$, $\Delta t_{ap}$):** Delay and jitter between the hold command and the actual switch opening.
3. **Droop Rate ($dV_o/dt$):** Rate at which the held voltage decays during hold mode due to leakage currents.
4. **Hold Step / Pedestal Error ($V_{ped}$):** Voltage offset introduced at the transition from sample to hold mode due to charge injection through the MOSFET gate-drain capacitance.
5. *(Bonus)* **Feedthrough Attenuation** and **Slew Rate** are also valid specifications.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional operation** of a Sample and Hold circuit. Clearly distinguish between the **Sample mode** and the **Hold mode** of operation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **acquisition time** of a Sample and Hold circuit. State the engineering significance of this parameter in a digital protection relay.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

A typical S/H circuit comprises four sub-blocks: **(1) Analog Switch** (MOSFET), **(2) Hold Capacitor $C_H$**, **(3) Op-amp Buffer** (voltage follower), and **(4) Digital Control Logic** that generates the sample/hold pulse.

**Sample Mode Operation:**
- The control logic drives the MOSFET gate **HIGH**.
- The MOSFET channel resistance becomes $R_{ON} \ll 1$ kΩ.
- $C_H$ charges through $R_{ON}$ and tracks $V_{in}$ with time constant $\tau = R_{ON} C_H$.
- Output: $V_{out}(t) = V_{in}(t)$.

**Hold Mode Operation:**
- The control logic drives the MOSFET gate **LOW**.
- The MOSFET channel pinches off; off-resistance $R_{OFF} > 10^{10}$ Ω.
- The op-amp buffer prevents $C_H$ from discharging into the load.
- Output: $V_{out}(t) = V_{in}(t_{sample}) = \text{constant}$.

The **transition** from sample to hold involves an **aperture delay $t_{ap}$** during which the gate-to-drain capacitance injects a small charge packet into $C_H$, creating the **pedestal error**.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** During sample mode, $C_H$ charges through $R_{ON}$ with the first-order ODE:

$$R_{ON} \cdot C_H \cdot \frac{dV_c}{dt} + V_c(t) = V_{in}$$

**Step 2:** With initial condition $V_c(0) = V_o(t_0)$ and defining $\tau = R_{ON} C_H$:

$$V_c(t) = V_{in} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) + V_o(t_0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3:** The maximum error is when $V_o(t_0) = 0$ and $V_{in} = V_{FS}$:

$$\varepsilon_{max}(t) = V_{FS} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 4:** For ½-LSB accuracy of an $n$-bit ADC:

$$V_{FS} \cdot e^{-t_{acq}/\tau} \leq \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 5:** Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\boxed{t_{acq} \geq (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \cdot (n+1) \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_H}$$

**Engineering Significance:** The acquisition time determines the **maximum sample rate** $f_{s,max} = 1 / t_{acq}$ achievable by the relay's data acquisition system. For a 16-bit numerical relay, $t_{acq}$ is typically in the microsecond range, directly limiting how fast the relay can process post-fault transients.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of **droop** in a Sample and Hold circuit. Derive an expression for the **droop rate** and the **minimum required hold capacitor value** for a given ADC resolution.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **open-loop** and **closed-loop (Miller integrator)** topologies of S/H circuits. Which topology is preferred for high-precision numerical distance relays, and why?

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition of Droop:** Droop is the slow, unwanted **decay of the held output voltage** $V_o$ during the hold mode, caused by the discharge of $C_H$ through various leakage paths.

**Step 1: Identify leakage sources.**
- MOSFET off-state leakage $I_{DS(off)}$
- Op-amp input bias currents $I_{B+}$, $I_{B-}$
- PCB surface leakage $I_{PCB}$
- Total: $I_{leak} = I_{DS(off)} + I_{B+} + I_{B-} + I_{PCB}$

**Step 2: Apply capacitor discharge equation.**

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$$

**Step 3: Integrate over hold duration $t_{hold}$.**

$$\Delta V_{droop} = \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{C_H}$$

**Step 4: Apply ½-LSB constraint of an $n$-bit ADC.**

$$\frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{C_H} \leq \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the minimum $C_H$.**

$$\boxed{C_H \geq \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold} \cdot 2^{n+1}}{V_{FS}}}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $I_{leak} = 5$ nA, $t_{hold} = 20 \ \mu s$, $n = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V:
$$C_H \geq \frac{5 \times 10^{-9} \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 8192}{5} \approx 1.64 \ \mu F$$
This is unrealistically large, so a **closed-loop S/H** is mandatory in such cases.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

| Feature | Open-Loop S/H | Closed-Loop (Miller) S/H |
|---------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Topology** | $C_H$ at op-amp input; buffer in voltage follower | $C_H}$ inside op-amp negative feedback loop |
| **Effective Capacitance** | $C_H$ (physical) | $C_H \cdot (1 + A_{OL})$ (Miller multiplied, $A_{OL} \approx 10^5$) |
| **Droop Rate** | High (limited by $C_H$) | **Negligible** (effectively zero) |
| **Pedestal Error** | High (charge injection visible) | **Greatly reduced** (virtual ground at op-amp input absorbs charge) |
| **Acquisition Speed** | Fast (no feedback delay) | Slower (slew-rate limited by op-amp) |
| **Accuracy** | 8–10 bit typical | **12–16 bit typical** |
| **Bandwidth** | Wide (MHz range) | Moderate (limited by op-amp GBW) |
| **Cost / Complexity** | Low | Moderate (extra op-amp + switch) |

**Preferred Topology for Numerical Distance Relays:** The **Closed-Loop (Miller Integrator) topology** is preferred because numerical distance relays demand **high-resolution** sampling of voltage and current phasors (typically 14–16 bit ADC resolution) for accurate **impedance trajectory computation**. The Miller topology virtually eliminates droop and pedestal error, which is critical when the relay must distinguish a **Zone-1 from a Zone-2 fault** based on a stable, accurate phasor snapshot.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **forget to state the sign convention** in the droop derivation (whether $C_H$ is charging or discharging). Always state **"the leakage current discharges $C_H$"** explicitly.
> 2. In the closed-loop comparison, students frequently omit the term **"Miller multiplication"** — this is a key phrase that KTU 2024 examiners specifically scan for.
> 3. Do not confuse **Aperture Time** with **Acquisition Time**. Aperture = jitter in opening; Acquisition = time to charge $C_H$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this checklist. Revise it 30 minutes before the KTU ESE Module-III exam.**

- [ ] A **Sample and Hold circuit** captures and freezes an analog signal for ADC conversion.
- [ ] Two modes: **Sample (Track)** — switch closed, $V_{out} = V_{in}$; **Hold** — switch open, $V_{out}$ = constant.
- [ ] Four sub-blocks: **Switch + Capacitor + Buffer + Control Logic**.
- [ ] **Acquisition time**: $t_{acq} \geq (n+1) \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_H \cdot \ln 2$ — determines max sample rate.
- [ ] **Droop rate**: $dV_o/dt = -I_{leak}/C_H$ — caused by leakage currents.
- [ ] **Minimum hold capacitor**: $C_H \geq I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold} \cdot 2^{n+1} / V_{FS}$.
- [ ] **Aperture uncertainty** $\Delta t_{ap}$ must satisfy $\Delta t_{ap} \leq 1 / (2^{n+1} \cdot \pi \cdot f)$.
- [ ] **Pedestal error** is caused by **gate-to-drain charge injection** through $C_{GD}$.
- [ ] **Closed-loop (Miller) topology** uses negative feedback to multiply effective capacitance.
- [ ] **Open-loop** is faster but less accurate; **Closed-loop** is slower but much more accurate.
- [ ] Common S/H ICs: **LF398, AD582, AD585, SMP04, SHC5320**.
- [ ] In a 50 Hz numerical relay, sample rate is typically **600 Hz to 1.6 kHz** (12–32 samples/cycle).
- [ ] For **travelling-wave** protection, sample rates exceed **1 MHz** with sub-ns aperture.
- [ ] The S/H circuit sits **before the ADC** in the relay's analog front-end.
- [ ] Always state the **½-LSB accuracy criterion** when deriving any S/H timing equation.
- [ ] Differentiate clearly between **Aperture Time**, **Acquisition Time**, **Settling Time**, and **Hold Time**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Analog to Digital and Digital to Analog Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Analog to Digital and Digital to Analog Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Digital Protection of Power Systems**, the analog quantities of interest — primarily the **secondary currents from Current Transformers (CTs)** and **secondary voltages from Potential Transformers (PTs)** — are continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signals. The numerical relay or digital fault recorder, however, operates on a **microprocessor/DSP** that can only understand **discrete-time, discrete-amplitude binary words**.

**Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** is the deterministic, engineered process of converting a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code $x[n]$ suitable for numerical processing inside a digital relay.

**Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC)** is the inverse engineered process of reconstructing a continuous-time analog signal $x(t)$ from a discrete digital code $x[n]$, used primarily for **testing, display, waveform playback, and analogue output channels** in protective relays and PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** For Module 3, the student is expected to clearly distinguish between **spatial (amplitude) quantization** and **temporal (time) quantization**, recognize the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling limit**, and identify the ADC architecture (Successive Approximation, Flash, Delta-Sigma) most appropriate for a given protection function.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the ADC as a **highly disciplined translator sitting between two different languages**:

- **The Analog World (Speaker A):** A CT secondary current that is *infinitely smooth*, varying continuously with time, like the flowing water of a river. The river has water at every single instant and at every possible height.
- **The Digital World (Speaker B):** A microprocessor that only understands a fixed **dictionary of binary words** (e.g., 12-bit codes from `000000000000` to `111111111111`, which is 4096 distinct words).

The translator (ADC) does **two things in sequence**:
1. **Sampling (Time Translation):** Stops the river at *regular intervals* (e.g., every 0.5 ms) — capturing a series of "snapshots."
2. **Quantization (Amplitude Translation):** Measures the *height* of each snapshot and rounds it to the nearest predefined water level marked on a ruler.

The **DAC** is the reverse translator — it reads the dictionary words and reconstructs a smooth waveform (typically using a **zero-order hold**) for human viewing on a CRO or for analog instrumentation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics used in this Module:**
> - **Standard Power Frequency:** $f_{\text{sys}} = 50\,\text{Hz}$ (India/KTU syllabus)
> - **Typical Relay Sampling Rate:** $f_s = 16, 32, 64, 128$ samples per cycle
> - **Typical ADC Resolution:** $n = 12$ to $16$ bits for numerical relays
> - **Bandwidth of CT/PT signal of interest:** up to the **5th–7th harmonic** for distance protection, and **up to 2–3 kHz** for high-frequency transient-based protection (traveling wave, $\approx 10$ kHz components for EHV/UHV lines)

## 1.3 Why ADC/DAC is Indispensable in a Numerical Relay

A numerical relay architecture fundamentally requires ADC because:

- The **algorithm blocks** (Fourier DFT/FFT, differential equation algorithm, Kalman filter, traveling-wave detection, phasor estimation) operate on **sampled numerical arrays**.
- **Decision logic** (trip/no-trip, impedance calculation) is implemented in software, which only understands digital numbers.
- **Communication, event logging, and display** all require digital representation.

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important consequence of ADC in protection is the **introduction of two unavoidable errors**:
> 1. **Aliasing error** — if sampling is too slow.
> 2. **Quantization error** — if resolution is too low.
> 
> Both must be engineered out using **anti-aliasing filters** and **adequate bit resolution**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of ADC: Sampling and Quantization

Any ADC, regardless of architecture (Flash, SAR, Dual-Slope, Sigma-Delta), performs exactly two operations:

| Step | Operation | Continuous Input | Discrete Output | Engineering Concern |
|:----:|:----------|:-----------------|:----------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | **Sampling** | $x(t)$ continuous in time | $x(nT_s)$ discrete in time | Aliasing → must satisfy Nyquist |
| 2 | **Quantization** | $x(nT_s)$ continuous in amplitude | $x_Q[n]$ discrete in amplitude | Quantization noise → must choose enough bits |

## 2.2 Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon)

The **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** (1948/1949) is the **fundamental physical law** governing all digital protection systems.

**Statement:** A band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ having **no frequency components above $f_{\max}$ Hz** can be **uniquely reconstructed** from its samples $x(nT_s)$ provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$$

The minimum permissible sampling rate is the **Nyquist Rate**:

$$f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 \cdot f_{\max}$$

The sampling interval is:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

- If $f_s < 2 f_{\max}$ → **Aliasing** occurs (false low-frequency components appear in the digital record).
- If $f_s = 2 f_{\max}$ → Reconstruction is mathematically possible only with an **ideal low-pass filter** (impractical).
- In practice, engineers use $f_s \gg 2 f_{\max}$ (oversampling) and a practical **anti-aliasing filter** with a finite roll-off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Nyquist criterion is applied to the **highest frequency content the relay needs to "see"**, not just the fundamental 50 Hz. For a **distance relay using Fourier algorithm up to the 5th harmonic**, $f_{\max} = 250$ Hz, so $f_s \geq 500$ Hz (i.e., ≥ 10 samples/cycle). For **traveling-wave based protection**, $f_{\max} \approx 100$ kHz, demanding $f_s \geq 200$ kHz.

## 2.3 Quantization and Resolution

An **$n$-bit ADC** divides the full-scale analog input range $V_{\text{FS}}$ into $2^n$ discrete quantization levels (steps).

**Step Size (Quanta):**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{L}$$

where $L = 2^n$ is the number of discrete levels.

**Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$e_q^{\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Quantization SNR (assumes uniformly distributed noise, full-scale sinusoidal input):**

$$\text{SNR}_Q = 6.02\,n + 1.76 \quad \text{[dB]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each additional bit of resolution improves the **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** by approximately **6 dB**, equivalent to a factor of 2 in voltage precision. This is why 16-bit ADCs are preferred over 12-bit ADCs in modern numerical relays when measuring small fault currents.

## 2.4 Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit

Because the ADC requires a **finite aperture time** $T_{\text{ap}}$ to perform quantization, and the input analog signal may change during this interval, a **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** block is placed *before* the ADC to "freeze" the analog value at the instant of sampling.

- **Track mode:** S/H output tracks the input.
- **Hold mode:** S/H output is held constant (capacitor charged to $x(nT_s)$) while ADC converts it.
- **Aperture uncertainty $\Delta t_{ap}$** introduces an amplitude error:

$$|\Delta V| = \left.\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right|_{\max} \cdot \Delta t_{ap} = 2\pi f_{\max} V_{\text{FS}} \cdot \Delta t_{ap}$$

## 2.5 Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)

A **low-pass analog filter** placed *before* the S/H limits the input bandwidth to $f_c < f_s/2$, ensuring that no out-of-band noise or harmonics above $f_s/2$ can fold back into the baseband. In practice, the AAF cut-off is chosen at $0.4$ to $0.45 \times f_s/2$ to allow for the filter's non-ideal roll-off.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application in Power System Protection | Required $f_s$ | Required Resolution $n$ | ADC Architecture Typically Used |
|:--------------------------------------|:---------------:|:----------------------:|:-------------------------------:|
| Overcurrent / Earth-fault relay (Fourier algorithm) | 16–32 samples/cycle (800 Hz – 1.6 kHz) | 12–14 bit | Successive Approximation Register (SAR) |
| Distance relay (impedance, $Z = V/I$) | 32–64 samples/cycle | 14–16 bit | SAR |
| Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU, synchrophasor) | 48–96 samples/cycle, GPS-locked | 16–18 bit | SAR / Delta-Sigma |
| Traveling-wave fault locator (EHV) | 1–10 MHz | 8–12 bit (very high speed) | Flash ADC |
| Digital Fault Recorder (DFR) | 5–50 kHz | 12–16 bit | SAR |
| Audio/test DAC for relay commissioning | 8–44 kHz | 14–16 bit | R-2R Ladder, Sigma-Delta |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|:-:|:-------------------|:--------|:------|:-----------------------------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Hz | Equality = ideal LPF; in practice use $f_s \gg 2f_{\max}$ |
| 2 | Sampling Interval | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | seconds | Time between two consecutive samples |
| 3 | Samples per Power Cycle | $N = f_s / f_{\text{sys}}$ | dimensionless | For $f_{\text{sys}}=50$ Hz: $N = f_s/50$ |
| 4 | Number of Quantization Levels | $L = 2^n$ | levels | $n$ = number of bits of ADC |
| 5 | Quantization Step Size | $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$ | Volts | Smallest distinguishable voltage step |
| 6 | Max Quantization Error | $e_q = \pm \Delta/2$ | Volts | Quantization noise upper bound |
| 7 | SQNR of $n$-bit ADC | $\text{SNR}_Q = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB | Sinusoidal full-scale input assumption |
| 8 | Aperure Error Magnitude | $\vert \Delta V \vert = 2\pi f_{\max} V_{\text{FS}} \Delta t_{ap}$ | Volts | S/H aperture jitter constraint |
| 9 | Dynamic Range | $\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}(2^n) = 6.02n$ | dB | Maximum signal to smallest quantization step |
| 10 | Alias Frequency (under-sampled) | $f_a = \vert f_{\text{in}} - k f_s \vert$, smallest positive | Hz | $k$ = integer chosen to minimize $f_a$ |
| 11 | Anti-Aliasing Filter Cut-off | $f_c \approx 0.45 \cdot f_s/2$ | Hz | Must precede S/H block |
| 12 | Max unambiguous frequency | $f_{\max} = f_s/2$ | Hz | Nyquist (folding) frequency |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quantization Error and SQNR Formula

**Goal:** Derive the fundamental SQNR $= 6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB relationship used universally in ADC specification sheets and numerical relay datasheets.

**Step 1 — Define the quanta (step size).**
An $n$-bit unipolar ADC spans a full-scale range $V_{\text{FS}}$ (e.g., $\pm 5$ V or $0$–$5$ V). The width of one quantization level is:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n}$$

**Step 2 — Model the quantization error as uniformly distributed white noise.**
The error $e_q = x_{\text{analog}} - x_Q$ lies in the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ and, for signals that traverse many levels, can be modeled as **uniformly distributed**. The probability density function is:

$$p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean-square (variance) of the quantization noise.**
The average noise power at the ADC output is:

$$\overline{e_q^{\,2}} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^{\,2} \cdot p(e_q)\, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^{\,2}\, de_q$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\overline{e_q^{\,2}} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^{\,3}}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot (\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the signal power of a full-scale sinusoid.**
For a sinusoidal input $v(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$ that fully spans $-V_{\text{FS}}/2$ to $+V_{\text{FS}}/2$, the peak is $V_p = V_{\text{FS}}/2$. The mean-square value is:

$$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{V_p^{\,2}}{2} = \frac{(V_{\text{FS}}/2)^2}{2} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^{\,2}}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Form the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio.**

$$\text{SNR}_Q = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{\overline{e_q^{\,2}}} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^{\,2}/8}{\Delta^2/12} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^{\,2}}{8} \cdot \frac{12}{\Delta^2} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^{\,2}}{\Delta^2}$$

Substitute $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$:

$$\text{SNR}_Q = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^{\,2}}{\left(V_{\text{FS}}/2^n\right)^2} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^n$$

**Step 6 — Convert to decibels using $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$.**

$$\text{SNR}_Q\,[\text{dB}] = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^n\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 10\, n \log_{10}(4)$$

Evaluate the constants:

$$10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \approx 1.76 \quad ; \quad 10 \log_{10}(4) = 20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{SNR}_Q\,[\text{dB}] = 6.02\,n + 1.76}$$

This is the **industry-standard formula** appearing on every ADC datasheet and is the standard KTU expected result for Module 3 numerical-type questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For a 12-bit ADC, $\text{SNR}_Q = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 74$ dB. For a 16-bit ADC, $\text{SNR}_Q = 98.08$ dB. The rule "**+6 dB per extra bit**" follows directly from the coefficient $6.02$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Sampling Rate for a Numerical Distance Relay

**Problem statement (KTU-style):** A numerical distance relay uses a **Full-Cycle Discrete Fourier Transform (FCDFT)** algorithm to extract the fundamental phasor. The fault current contains significant harmonics up to the **5th harmonic** ($f_{\max} = 5 \times 50 = 250$ Hz). Determine the minimum sampling rate and the number of samples per cycle.

**Step 1 — Identify the highest frequency of interest.**
Fundamental: $f_1 = 50$ Hz. 5th harmonic: $f_5 = 5 \times 50 = 250$ Hz.

$$f_{\max} = 250 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist criterion.**

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max} = 2 \times 250 = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to samples per cycle.**

$$N = \frac{f_s}{f_1} = \frac{500}{50} = 10 \text{ samples/cycle}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the engineering safety margin.**
In practice, the relay uses $f_s = 32$ samples/cycle (i.e., $f_s = 32 \times 50 = 1600$ Hz). The actual oversampling factor is:

$$\text{OSR} = \frac{f_s}{2 f_{\max}} = \frac{1600}{500} = 3.2$$

**Step 5 — Design the anti-aliasing filter cut-off.**

$$f_c \approx 0.45 \times \frac{f_s}{2} = 0.45 \times 800 = 360 \text{ Hz}$$

A 2nd- or 4th-order Butterworth low-pass filter with $f_c = 360$ Hz suffices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** A very common student mistake is to set $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{sys}} = 100$ Hz. This is **wrong**; one must use the *highest harmonic of interest* dictated by the chosen algorithm and protection function.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Aliasing — The "Fold-Back" Frequency

**Problem:** A $50$ Hz CT secondary signal contains a **3rd harmonic (150 Hz)** disturbance. The relay samples at $f_s = 200$ Hz. What aliased frequency appears at the output?

**Step 1 — Compute the spectral folding.**
Aliasing makes a true input frequency $f_{\text{in}}$ appear at:

$$f_a = \left\vert f_{\text{in}} - k f_s \right\vert$$

where $k$ is the integer chosen so that $0 \leq f_a \leq f_s/2$.

For $f_{\text{in}} = 150$ Hz, $f_s = 200$ Hz:

$$f_a = \vert 150 - 1 \times 200 \vert = \vert -50 \vert = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Physical interpretation.**
The 150 Hz harmonic **folds back** and masquerades as a **50 Hz fundamental component**, corrupting the impedance calculation in the distance relay. This is precisely why an **anti-aliasing filter must precede the S/H** and why the AAF cut-off must be below $f_s/2 = 100$ Hz (which would suppress the 150 Hz entirely).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Resolution Required for a Given CT Secondary Current Range

**Problem:** A numerical overcurrent relay must measure secondary currents in the range $I_{\text{min}} = 0.1$ A (light load) to $I_{\text{max}} = 100$ A (max fault). The ADC uses $\pm 5$ V full-scale input from a burden resistor $R_b = 0.05\,\Omega$. Determine the **minimum number of bits** required to achieve a current discrimination of $0.5$ A near the light-load end.

**Step 1 — Convert the current discrimination to a voltage discrimination.**

$$\Delta V = \Delta I \cdot R_b = 0.5 \times 0.05 = 0.025 \text{ V} = 25 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the full-scale voltage range.**

$$V_{\text{FS}} = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ V} \quad (\text{bipolar }\pm 5\text{ V range})$$

**Step 3 — Solve the quanta equation for $n$.**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n} \leq \Delta V \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2^n \geq \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{\Delta V} = \frac{10}{0.025} = 400$$

**Step 4 — Take $\log_2$.**

$$n \geq \log_2(400) = \frac{\log_{10}(400)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.602}{0.301} \approx 8.64$$

**Step 5 — Round up to next integer.**

$$n_{\min} = 9 \text{ bits}$$

In practice, a **12-bit or 14-bit ADC** is chosen to provide safety margin and to also handle small earth-fault currents ($I_{\min} \approx 0.01$ A).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right ADC for a Protection Function

| Protection Function | Required Bandwidth | $f_s$ (samples/cycle) | Min $n$ | Recommended ADC | Reason |
|:--------------------|:------------------:|:---------------------:|:-------:|:----------------|:-------|
| Definite-time overcurrent | 0–250 Hz | 8–16 | 10 | SAR | Low cost, slow |
| Inverse-time overcurrent | 0–250 Hz | 16 | 12 | SAR | r.m.s. accuracy |
| Distance (impedance) | 0–500 Hz | 32–64 | 12–14 | SAR | Phase accuracy |
| Differential (transformer/gen) | 0–500 Hz | 32–64 | 14–16 | SAR | 2nd & 5th harmonic restraint |
| PMU / Synchrophasor | 0–500 Hz | 48–96 (GPS-locked) | 16–18 | 16/18-bit SAR or ΔΣ | TVE < 1% |
| Traveling-wave locator | 10 kHz–1 MHz | 1–10 MHz | 8 | Flash | Speed (sub-µs aperture) |
| Digital Fault Recorder | 0–5 kHz | 100–200 | 12–14 | SAR | Wide bandwidth logging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Digital Protection Chain (Sensor → ADC → Algorithm → Trip)

This Mermaid block diagram captures the **end-to-end signal flow** in a modern numerical relay, highlighting the precise location and role of the ADC, Sample-and-Hold, and Anti-Aliasing Filter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Power System] --> CT[Current Transformer CT]
    P --> PT[Potential Transformer PT]
    CT --> BUR1[Burden Resistor]
    PT --> BUR2[Voltage Divider]
    BUR1 --> ISO1[Isolation Amplifier]
    BUR2 --> ISO2[Isolation Amplifier]
    ISO1 --> AAF[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    ISO2 --> AAF
    AAF --> SH[Sample and Hold S and H]
    SH --> MUX[Analog Multiplexer]
    MUX --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    ADC --> DSP[Microprocessor DSP CPU]
    DSP --> ALG[Protection Algorithm DFT Impedance O C]
    ALG --> LOGIC[Trip Decision Logic]
    LOGIC --> TRIP[Trip Coil Circuit Breaker]
    DSP --> DAC[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    DAC --> DISP[Display CRO Local Annunciation]
    DSP --> COM[Communication Port IEC 61850]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Detailed ADC Internal Operation (Successive Approximation Register)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Analog Input x of t arrives at ADC] --> SAMPLE[S and H captures x at nT sub s]
    SAMPLE --> SAR[Successive Approximation Register sets MSB to 1]
    SAR --> DACINT[Internal DAC reconstructs trial voltage V trial]
    DACINT --> CMP[Comparator V trial versus x nT s]
    CMP --> DECIDE{Bit kept or reset}
    DECIDE -->|Keep| NEXT[Move to next lower bit]
    DECIDE -->|Reset| NEXT
    NEXT --> DONE{All n bits tested}
    DONE -->|No| SAR
    DONE -->|Yes| OUTPUT[Digital word xQ of n output to DSP]
    OUTPUT --> EOC[End of Conversion EOC flag to DSP]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow: DAC Operation Using R-2R Ladder (for Test/Display Output)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REG[Digital Register holds xQ of n] --> SW[Electronic Switch Bank B0 to B sub n minus 1]
    SW --> LADDER[R minus 2R Resistor Ladder Network]
    LADDER --> OPAMP[Summing Operational Amplifier]
    OPAMP --> VOUT[Reconstructed Analog Voltage V out of t]
    VOUT --> SMOOTH[Reconstruction Low Pass Smoothing Filter]
    SMOOTH --> LOAD[Display Load CRO or Recorder]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Quantization Concept (Rounding to Nearest Level)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Continuous
    Continuous: Continuous Analog x of t
    Continuous --> Sampled: Sampled at f sub s Hz
    Sampled: Sampled x of nT sub s
    Sampled --> Quantized: Quantized to 2 to the n levels
    Quantized: Quantized xQ of n
    Quantized --> Processed: Digital Protection Algorithm
    Processed: Trip Logic
    Processed --> [*]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Aliasing Demonstration (Frequency-Domain View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TIME[Time Domain]
        SIN[Sinusoid 50 Hz plus 150 Hz harmonic]
    end
    TIME --> FS[Sample at f sub s equals 200 Hz too slow]
    FS --> ALIAS[Output appears as 50 Hz only harmonic folded back]
    subgraph SPEC[Frequency Domain]
        F1[True Spectrum 50 Hz and 150 Hz]
        F2[Apparent Spectrum 50 Hz only after aliasing]
    end
    F1 --- F2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define quantization and quantization error in the context of ADC used in numerical relays. A 12-bit ADC has a full-scale input range of $\pm 10$ V. Calculate the quanta (step size) and the maximum quantization error.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Quantization:** Quantization is the process of mapping a continuous-amplitude sampled signal $x(nT_s)$ onto a finite set of $2^n$ discrete amplitude levels, producing the digital code $x_Q[n]$ used by the relay's microprocessor.

**Quantization Error:** It is the difference between the true sampled value and its quantized value, $e_q = x(nT_s) - x_Q[n]$, bounded within $\pm \Delta/2$.

**Calculation:**
- Number of levels: $L = 2^{12} = 4096$
- Full-scale range: $V_{\text{FS}} = 20$ V (since $\pm 10$ V)
- Quanta: $\Delta = 20/4096 = 4.88$ mV
- Max quantization error: $e_q = \pm \Delta/2 = \pm 2.44$ mV

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Nyquist sampling theorem. A numerical distance relay processes signals with frequency content up to the 5th harmonic of 50 Hz. Calculate the minimum sampling frequency and the number of samples per cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Nyquist Theorem:** A bandlimited signal with maximum frequency $f_{\max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$.

**Calculation:**
- $f_{\max} = 5 \times 50 = 250$ Hz
- Minimum $f_s = 2 \times 250 = 500$ Hz
- Samples per cycle $N = 500/50 = 10$ samples/cycle

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Sub-Parts)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module 3 internal choice questions carry 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both sub-parts must be answered for full credit. The examiner expects a labelled diagram wherever the question is "Explain with block diagram".

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a digital relay with special emphasis on the **role of the anti-aliasing filter, sample-and-hold circuit, and ADC**. Clearly indicate the location of the DAC in the chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A numerical relay uses a **16-bit SAR ADC** with $\pm 5$ V full-scale bipolar input. Compute:
- (i) Number of quantization levels
- (ii) Quanta $\Delta$
- (iii) Maximum quantization error
- (iv) Theoretical SQNR in dB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**
- CT/PT secondary → **Burden Resistor** → **Isolation Amplifier** (1 mark)
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** — low-pass filter with cut-off $f_c \approx 0.45 \cdot f_s/2$, removes out-of-band noise to prevent aliasing (2 marks)
- **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — freezes analog value during conversion aperture; aperture uncertainty $\Delta t_{ap}$ creates amplitude error $|\Delta V| = 2\pi f_{\max} V_{\text{FS}} \Delta t_{ap}$ (2 marks)
- **Multiplexer + ADC (SAR)** — converts to digital code (1 mark)
- **DAC** is located on the *output side* — used for local display, CRO output, and analog test ports (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

(i) Levels:
$$L = 2^{16} = 65536$$

(ii) Quanta (full-scale = $2 \times 5 = 10$ V):
$$\Delta = \frac{10}{65536} = 152.6\,\mu\text{V}$$

(iii) Max quantization error:
$$e_q^{\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm 76.3\,\mu\text{V}$$

(iv) SQNR:
$$\text{SNR}_Q = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** Explain the **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** architecture with a block diagram. Why is SAR the most preferred ADC type in numerical overcurrent and distance relays? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A current-transformer secondary produces a signal containing 50 Hz fundamental and a dominant 3rd harmonic (150 Hz) during a fault. The relay samples at $f_s = 320$ Hz.
- (i) Verify whether aliasing will occur.
- (ii) If yes, calculate the aliased frequency.
- (iii) Suggest the design of the anti-aliasing filter to prevent this. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SAR ADC Explanation (7 marks):**
- **Block diagram** (3 marks): SAR register, internal DAC, comparator, control logic.
- **Operating principle** (2 marks): Binary search — set MSB = 1, compare with input via internal DAC; if input is smaller, reset MSB, else keep. Move to next bit. After $n$ clock cycles, the register holds the digital word.
- **Why preferred in numerical relays** (2 marks):
  * Moderate speed (suitable for 16–64 samples/cycle, i.e., 800 Hz–3.2 kHz)
  * High resolution (12–18 bit) at low cost
  * Low power, deterministic latency (fixed $n$ clock cycles per conversion)
  * Excellent for DC + low-frequency AC signals from CT/PT

**(b) Aliasing Analysis (7 marks):**

(i) Nyquist check:
$$f_s/2 = 160 \text{ Hz} \quad ; \quad f_{\max} = 150 \text{ Hz}$$
Since $f_{\max} = 150$ Hz is less than 160 Hz, **technically no aliasing** — but the safety margin is only 10 Hz, which is **unsafe in practice**.

(ii) Hypothetical: if $f_s$ were reduced to 200 Hz (or if higher harmonics exist), the 150 Hz would fold as:
$$f_a = \vert 150 - 1 \times 200 \vert = 50 \text{ Hz}$$
(150 Hz would masquerade as the 50 Hz fundamental.)

(iii) AAF design: Choose cut-off $f_c$ comfortably below $f_s/2 = 160$ Hz. A **4th-order Butterworth LPF** with $f_c = 60$ Hz suppresses the 150 Hz harmonic by approximately 60 dB, eliminating aliasing risk. Sampling rate should be increased to 32 samples/cycle ($f_s = 1600$ Hz) so that $f_s/2 = 800$ Hz comfortably exceeds the 5th harmonic (250 Hz).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on ADC/DAC Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting bipolar full-scale range:** A $\pm 5$ V ADC has $V_{\text{FS}} = 10$ V, not 5 V. This single mistake halves your quanta value and the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $f_s = 2 \times 50$ Hz:** This is a classic error. The Nyquist criterion applies to the **highest frequency of interest**, which is set by your algorithm (5th, 7th, or even 13th harmonic in differential protection).
> 3. **Confusing SQNR and dynamic range:** Dynamic Range $= 6.02\,n$ dB; SQNR $= 6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB. The extra $1.76$ dB comes from the sinusoid-vs-DC correction; examiners expect both formulas to be quoted correctly.
> 4. **No labelled diagram in "Explain" questions:** A 7-mark explain question without a block diagram typically caps at 4–5 marks. Always draw the block diagram.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention anti-aliasing filter:** In any answer involving "ADC in relays", the AAF is **mandatory** for full credit.
> 6. **Mixing up SAR and Flash:** SAR is for moderate-speed, high-resolution applications (relays); Flash is for ultra-high-speed, low-resolution applications (traveling-wave, radar).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 — ADC/DAC:**

- **ADC** = continuous-time, continuous-amplitude → discrete-time, discrete-amplitude. **DAC** is the inverse.
- **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$; for power system protection, $f_{\max}$ = highest harmonic of interest, **not** the fundamental 50 Hz.
- **Quantization step (quanta):** $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}} / 2^n$.
- **Max quantization error:** $\pm \Delta/2$.
- **Theoretical SQNR:** $6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB — derived from uniform quantization noise model.
- **Dynamic range:** $6.02\,n$ dB.
- **Anti-aliasing filter** must precede the S/H and ADC; cut-off at $\approx 0.45 \cdot f_s/2$.
- **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** freezes the signal during ADC conversion; aperture jitter $\Delta t_{ap}$ causes $|\Delta V| = 2\pi f_{\max} V_{\text{FS}} \Delta t_{ap}$.
- **SAR ADC** is the workhorse for numerical overcurrent, distance, and differential relays (12–18 bit, 16–64 samples/cycle).
- **Flash ADC** is used for traveling-wave and high-frequency transient protection (sub-microsecond).
- **Delta-Sigma ADC** is used in PMUs and high-precision metering (oversampling, noise shaping).
- **R-2R Ladder DAC** is the standard for test/display output; uses binary-weighted current summing.
- **Aliasing** folds any frequency $f > f_s/2$ back into $[0, f_s/2]$; the apparent (aliased) frequency is $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$.
- **Common numerical relay sampling rates:** 16, 32, 48, 64, 96 samples per 50 Hz cycle.
- **CT secondary bandwidth of interest:** fundamental + harmonics up to 5th (distance), 7th (transformer differential), 13th (overfluxing detection).
- **DAC reconstruction** uses zero-order hold; a smoothing LPF is mandatory to remove the staircase image.
- **Resolution vs. Accuracy are different:** Resolution is set by $n$; accuracy is set by component tolerances, linearity, and reference stability.
- **Bit-weight table for an $n$-bit ADC** (unipolar): MSB weight $= V_{\text{FS}}/2$, next $= V_{\text{FS}}/4$, $\ldots$, LSB weight $= V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Idea of Sliding Window Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Idea of Sliding Window Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sliding Window Concept (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> The *Sliding Window Concept* is a digital signal processing technique used in numerical/computer-based protective relays for estimating the fundamental frequency phasor of voltage and current signals. It involves taking a fixed-length set of the most recent $N$ discrete samples (where $N$ is the number of samples per fundamental power-system cycle) and continuously updating this data block as each new sample arrives, dropping the oldest sample. This produces a "window" that slides forward in time by one sample interval at every step, enabling continuous, real-time phasor estimation.

The mathematical core is based on the **Discrete Fourier Transform evaluated at the fundamental frequency**, and is mathematically equivalent to the **Correlation Method of phasor extraction** using orthogonal sine and cosine reference functions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **conveyor belt at a supermarket checkout counter** that is exactly 1 metre long. As the belt moves forward, a new apple rolls onto the front, and simultaneously the oldest apple falls off the back. The cashier (the relay) always inspects the apples currently on the visible 1-metre window of the belt.

- The belt is the **sliding window** of samples.
- The width of the visible portion is the **window length = N samples (one full power cycle)**.
- The rate of belt movement is the **sampling interval $\Delta t$**.
- The cashier's job is to compute a running "average quality" — equivalent to the relay computing the **phasor magnitude and angle** of the fundamental component.

This way, instead of waiting for an entirely new batch of apples (which would cause a long delay), the cashier has a **continuously refreshed estimate** every time a new sample rolls in.

### 1.3 Why the Sliding Window is Needed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Motivation:**
> A protective relay must operate in **sub-cycle to a few cycles** (typically 1–3 cycles, i.e. **20 ms – 60 ms** for a 50 Hz system). A single non-overlapping block of samples per cycle would update only once every **20 ms** (1 cycle). The sliding window updates **every sample interval $\Delta t$** (typically **~0.5 ms – 1 ms** at 2 kHz – 1 kHz sampling), giving a phasor estimate at every sample. This dramatically improves **responsiveness**, **fault detection speed**, and the **smoothness of the phasor trajectory** during evolving power-system disturbances.

### 1.4 Key Parameters and Standard Metrics

The following quantities are **standardised metrics** used throughout the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Fundamental system frequency:** $f = \mathbf{50 \text{ Hz}}$ (India) or $\mathbf{60 \text{ Hz}}$
- **Fundamental period:** $T = \mathbf{1/f = 20 \text{ ms}}$ (at 50 Hz)
- **Sampling frequency:** $f_s = \mathbf{N \cdot f}$, where $N$ is samples per cycle
- **Sampling interval:** $\Delta t = T/N$
- **Typical $N$ values:** $\mathbf{12, 16, 20, 24, 32, 64}$ samples per cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Mathematical Model

Any periodic power-system signal — whether healthy or faulted — can be expressed as a sum of the **fundamental**, **DC decaying component**, and **harmonics**:

$$v(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \cos(n\omega t + \phi_n)$$

where the first term is the **DC offset** (heavily present during faults) and $V_n, \phi_n$ are the magnitude and phase of the $n^{th}$ harmonic. The **sliding window** aims to extract the $n = 1$ component (the fundamental phasor) while rejecting everything else.

### 2.2 Orthogonal Decomposition of the Fundamental Component

The fundamental can be written in the orthogonal form using the identities $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$v_1(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi) = \underbrace{V_m \cos\phi}_{V_c}\cos(\omega t) \;-\; \underbrace{V_m \sin\phi}_{V_s}\sin(\omega t)$$

The two orthogonal projections $V_c$ and $V_s$ are the **real and imaginary parts** of the complex phasor $V$:

$$V = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\angle\phi \quad \text{and} \quad V = V_c - jV_s$$

### 2.3 The Correlation (Least-Squares) Method

To extract $V_c$ and $V_s$, we exploit the **orthogonality** of sine and cosine over one full period $T$:

$$V_c = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\cos(\omega t)\,dt$$

$$V_s = -\frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\sin(\omega t)\,dt$$

The negative sign on $V_s$ comes from the engineering convention (phasor rotates in the $+j$ direction for a *lagging* current). The two integrals together completely characterise the fundamental phasor:

$$V = \sqrt{V_c^2 + V_s^2} \quad ; \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{V_s}{V_c}\right)$$

### 2.4 Discretisation — The Heart of the Sliding Window

Replace the continuous integral by a discrete summation over $N$ samples, where the $k^{th}$ sample value is $v_k$ at angular position $2\pi k/N$:

$$V_c = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \cos\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

$$V_s = -\frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \sin\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sliding Window Operation:**
> At the $m^{th}$ sampling instant, the window is the set of the $N$ most recent samples $\{v_{m-N+1}, v_{m-N+2}, \ldots, v_m\}$. The window is said to "slide" because at every new instant $m+1$ the sample $v_{m-N+1}$ is dropped and the new sample $v_{m+1}$ is added.

The corresponding sliding window formulae are:

$$V_c(m) = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=m-N+1}^{m} v_k \cos\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

$$V_s(m) = -\frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=m-N+1}^{m} v_k \sin\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

### 2.5 The Recursive Formulation (Why Sliding Windows are Computationally Efficient)

Direct computation of the sum at every step would require $N$ multiplications and additions — a heavy burden on the relay's CPU. Fortunately, because the window shifts by **one** sample at a time, the sum can be updated **recursively** using only 2 multiplications and 2 additions per step.

By writing $V_c(m)$ and $V_c(m-1)$ and subtracting:

$$V_c(m) = V_c(m-1) + \frac{2}{N}\left[v_m \cos\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N}\right) - v_{m-N}\cos\left(\frac{2\pi (m-N)}{N}\right)\right]$$

Since the cosine function is $2\pi$-periodic: $\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi (m-N)}{N}\right) = \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N} - 2\pi\right) = \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N}\right)$

Therefore the recursive update collapses elegantly to:

$$\boxed{\;V_c(m) = V_c(m-1) + \frac{2}{N}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N}\right)\bigl[v_m - v_{m-N}\bigr]\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;V_s(m) = V_s(m-1) - \frac{2}{N}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N}\right)\bigl[v_m - v_{m-N}\bigr]\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The product $\bigl[v_m - v_{m-N}\bigr]$ is the *difference between the new sample and the sample that is exactly one cycle older*. This single subtraction essentially carries the entire information about how the signal has evolved over the last cycle.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The following table is the *minimum* formula set you must memorise for the KTU End Semester Exam. Every numerical on this topic will reduce to one of these.

| # | Formula | Quantity Computed | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $V_c = \frac{2}{N}\sum v_k \cos(2\pi k/N)$ | Real (in-phase) component of phasor | Volts or Amps |
| 2 | $V_s = -\frac{2}{N}\sum v_k \sin(2\pi k/N)$ | Imaginary (quadrature) component of phasor | Volts or Amps |
| 3 | $V = \sqrt{V_c^2 + V_s^2}$ | RMS magnitude of fundamental phasor | Same as $v(t)$ |
| 4 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(V_s / V_c)$ | Phase angle of phasor w.r.t. cosine reference | Radians or degrees |
| 5 | $V_c(m) = V_c(m-1) + \tfrac{2}{N}\cos(2\pi m/N)[v_m - v_{m-N}]$ | Recursive update of $V_c$ | Used in real-time relays |
| 6 | $V_s(m) = V_s(m-1) - \tfrac{2}{N}\sin(2\pi m/N)[v_m - v_{m-N}]$ | Recursive update of $V_s$ | Used in real-time relays |
| 7 | $T = 1/f$ | Fundamental period | $\mathbf{20 \text{ ms}}$ at 50 Hz |
| 8 | $\Delta t = T/N$ | Sampling interval | $\mathbf{0.625 \text{ ms}}$ for $N = 32$ |
| 9 | $f_s = N \cdot f$ | Sampling frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $V = V_c - j V_s$ | Complex phasor (rectangular form) | Engineering convention |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How the Sliding Window Helps |
|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Distance Protection** | Computes the apparent impedance $Z = V/I$ from continuously refreshed voltage and current phasors; the relay operates when $Z$ enters the trip zone |
| **Differential Protection** | Computes sum/difference of current phasors at two ends of a transformer or line to detect internal faults |
| **Overcurrent Relays** | Estimates RMS current $I = \sqrt{I_c^2 + I_s^2}$ each sample; if $I$ exceeds pickup for a defined time, trip |
| **PMU / Synchrophasors** | IEEE C37.118 phasor measurement uses a one-cycle sliding window; output rate is one phasor per sample (typically 50–120 frames/s) |
| **DFR / COMTRADE Recorders** | Same recursive algorithm is embedded in digital fault recorders to extract harmonic content post-fault |
| **Adaptive Relaying** | Sliding window provides a continuously updating estimate of the system impedance, allowing online adaptation of relay characteristics |

### 2.8 Implicit Filtering Properties

A very important property often asked in KTU exams is the **implicit filtering** performed by the sliding window:

- The integration over exactly one period $T$ produces **exact zero contribution** from any pure sinusoid whose frequency is an integer multiple of the fundamental (i.e., all integer harmonics).
- However, the sliding window does **NOT remove the DC offset** or non-integer (sub-) harmonics; for that, mimic filters or specialised algorithms (e.g., Kalman, DFT with multiple cycles) are required.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Discrete Correlation Formula for the Phasor

**Given:** A sinusoid $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ sampled at $N$ points per cycle, so that the $k^{th}$ sample occurs at $t_k = k\Delta t = kT/N$, giving:

$$v_k = V_m \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N} + \phi\right)$$

**Goal:** Derive $V_c$ and $V_s$ in terms of the samples $v_k$.

**Step 1 — Expand the cosine of a sum.**

$$v_k = V_m\left[\cos\phi \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) - \sin\phi \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Define the orthogonal projections.**

$$v_k = V_c \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) - V_s \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

where $V_c = V_m \cos\phi$ and $V_s = V_m \sin\phi$.

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $\cos(2\pi k/N)$ and sum over one period.**

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) = V_c \sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\cos^2\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) - V_s \sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Use the orthogonality relations** (which are exactly true when summed over $N$ equally-spaced points covering one full period):

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\cos^2\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) = \frac{N}{2}, \quad \sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and isolate $V_c$.**

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) = V_c \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$

$$\therefore \; V_c = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Repeat the procedure, multiplying by $\sin(2\pi k/N)$** (using the orthogonality $\sum \sin^2 = N/2$ and $\sum \sin \cdot \cos = 0$):

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) = -V_s \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$

$$\therefore \; V_s = -\frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign on $V_s$ reflects the engineering convention in which the rotating phasor $e^{j\omega t}$ pairs with a *minus* sign for the sine term. This is consistent with $V = V_c - jV_s$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Recursive Update Equation

**Goal:** Show that the sliding-window sum can be updated in only 2 multiplications per step.

**Step 1 — Write the sum for the $m^{th}$ instant explicitly:**

$$V_c(m) = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=m-N+1}^{m} v_k \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Write the sum for the previous instant $(m-1)$:**

$$V_c(m-1) = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=m-N}^{m-1} v_k \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the two equations to align the summation indices:**

$$V_c(m) - V_c(m-1) = \frac{2}{N}\left[v_m \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N}\right) - v_{m-N}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi (m-N)}{N}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the $2\pi$-periodicity of the cosine function:**

$$\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi (m-N)}{N}\right) = \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N} - 2\pi\right) = \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Factor the common cosine term:**

$$V_c(m) - V_c(m-1) = \frac{2}{N}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N}\right)\left[v_m - v_{m-N}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to give the recursive form:**

$$\boxed{\;V_c(m) = V_c(m-1) + \frac{2}{N}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N}\right)\bigl[v_m - v_{m-N}\bigr]\;}$$

A completely analogous derivation (using sine weighting) gives:

$$\boxed{\;V_s(m) = V_s(m-1) - \frac{2}{N}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N}\right)\bigl[v_m - v_{m-N}\bigr]\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Impact:** The non-recursive form requires $2N$ multiply-adds per step. The recursive form requires only 4 multiply-adds per step — a reduction by a factor of $N/2$ (e.g., **16× faster** for $N = 32$). This is why every commercial numerical relay uses the recursive form.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU-style 2-mark sub-problem.** A 50 Hz signal is sampled at $N = 4$ samples per cycle. The four most recent samples (in arbitrary units) are $v_0 = 0, v_1 = 5, v_2 = 0, v_3 = -5$. Find $V_c$ and $V_s$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sample at $N = 4$ means $\omega \Delta t = 2\pi/4 = \pi/2$.** The reference samples are $\cos(2\pi k/4)$ and $\sin(2\pi k/4)$:

| $k$ | $v_k$ | $\cos(2\pi k/4)$ | $\sin(2\pi k/4)$ |
|-----|-------|------------------|------------------|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 1 | 5 | 0 | 1 |
| 2 | 0 | -1 | 0 |
| 3 | -5 | 0 | -1 |

**Step 2 — Compute $V_c$:**

$$V_c = \frac{2}{4}\Bigl[0\cdot 1 + 5\cdot 0 + 0\cdot(-1) + (-5)\cdot 0\Bigr] = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_s$:**

$$V_s = -\frac{2}{4}\Bigl[0\cdot 0 + 5\cdot 1 + 0\cdot 0 + (-5)\cdot(-1)\Bigr] = -\frac{1}{2}(5 + 5) = -5$$

**Step 4 — Phasor magnitude and angle:**

$$V = \sqrt{0^2 + (-5)^2} = 5 \text{ a.u.}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-5}{0}\right) = -90^\circ$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The signal is a pure cosine-sine combination that can be written as $5\sin(\omega t)$, i.e. a sinusoid lagging the reference by $90^\circ$. The result is **consistent** with the input.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Single Window vs. Sliding Window

| Property | Single (Non-Overlapping) Window | Sliding Window |
|----------|--------------------------------|----------------|
| **Update rate** | Once per cycle ($T = 20$ ms) | Once per sample ($\Delta t \approx 0.5$ ms) |
| **Output phasors per second** | $f = 50$ | $N \cdot f = 1600$ (for $N = 32$) |
| **Computational cost per update** | $2N$ multiply-adds | **4** multiply-adds (recursive) |
| **Time delay to first estimate** | One full cycle (20 ms) | One full cycle (20 ms) |
| **Phasor smoothness during faults** | Stepwise, jumps every cycle | Smooth, evolves every sample |
| **Use case** | Offline DFT analysis, COMTRADE | Real-time numerical protection |
| **Implicit harmonic rejection** | Integer harmonics | Integer harmonics |
| **Rejection of DC offset** | None | None (needs mimic filter) |
