From $\sin^{2} + \cos^{2} = 1$ and the relation $\cos(\cdot) = v_1'/\omega V_m$:

$$V_m^{\,2} = v_1^{\,2} + \left(\frac{v_1'}{\omega}\right)^{2} = v_1^{\,2} + \left(\frac{v_1''}{\omega^{2} \cdot \omega}\right)^{2}\cdot \omega^{2}\quad\text{(after manipulation)}$$

A more direct result — recognised at board level — is:

$$\boxed{\;V_m^{\,2} = v_1^{\,2} + \left(\frac{v_1''}{\omega^{2}}\right)^{2}\;}$$

A particularly elegant result is obtained if you are evaluating at the **positive peak** ($\theta + \omega t = \pi/2$), where $v_1' = 0$ and $v_1'' = -\omega^{2} v_1$ → $V_m = v_1$ directly.

The numerical second-derivative uses a **three-point stencil**:

$$v_1'' = \frac{v_{n+1} - 2 v_n + v_{n-1}}{\Delta T^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this elegant?** The second-derivative is just $-\omega^{2}$ times the *original* sample, hence it can be used to **detect the peak** of the sinusoid: the peak occurs exactly where $v_1''$ changes sign!

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Three-Sample Algorithm (Most Important for KTU)

**Step 0 — Sample at $t_1, t_2, t_3$ with $t_2 - t_1 = t_3 - t_2 = \Delta T$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= V_m \sin(\omega t_1 + \theta) = V_m \sin(\phi - \omega\Delta T) \\
v_2 &= V_m \sin(\omega t_1 + \theta + \omega\Delta T) = V_m \sin(\phi) \\
v_3 &= V_m \sin(\omega t_1 + \theta + 2\omega\Delta T) = V_m \sin(\phi + \omega\Delta T)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\phi = \omega t_2 + \theta$ is the **angle at the middle sample**.

**Step 1 — Sum $v_1$ and $v_3$ using sum-to-product**

Using the identity $\sin A + \sin B = 2 \sin\!\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\dfrac{B-A}{2}\right)$:

$$v_1 + v_3 = 2 V_m \sin(\phi) \cos(\omega\Delta T) = 2 v_2 \cos(\omega\Delta T)$$

**Step 2 — Difference $v_3 - v_1$**

Using $\sin B - \sin A = 2 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\dfrac{B-A}{2}\right)$:

$$v_3 - v_1 = 2 V_m \cos(\phi) \sin(\omega\Delta T)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the orthogonal components $V_m\sin\phi$ and $V_m\cos\phi$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m \sin\phi &= v_2 \\
V_m \cos\phi &= \frac{v_3 - v_1}{2 \sin(\omega\Delta T)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Use Pythagoras to obtain $V_m$**

$$
V_m^{\,2} = (V_m \sin\phi)^{2} + (V_m \cos\phi)^{2}
$$

$$\boxed{\;V_m = \sqrt{\,v_2^{\,2} + \left(\frac{v_3 - v_1}{2 \sin(\omega\Delta T)}\right)^{2}\,}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Phase angle**

$$\tan\phi = \frac{V_m \sin\phi}{V_m \cos\phi} = \frac{2 v_2 \sin(\omega\Delta T)}{v_3 - v_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This algorithm **does not require numerical differentiation** — hence it is inherently more accurate and less sensitive to high-frequency noise than the Mann–Morrison method.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Full-Cycle Cosine & Sine Filter (DFT at $f_0$)

**Step 1 — Define sampled signal** ($N$ samples per cycle)

$$v_k = V_m \sin(k \omega\Delta T + \theta), \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

**Step 2 — Expand the sine and match orthogonal basis**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_k &= V_m [\sin(k\omega\Delta T)\cos\theta + \cos(k\omega\Delta T)\sin\theta] \\
    &= V_I \sin(k\omega\Delta T) + V_R \cos(k\omega\Delta T)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V_R = V_m \cos\theta$ and $V_I = V_m \sin\theta$ are the **real and imaginary parts of the phasor**.

**Step 3 — Multiply by $\cos(k\omega\Delta T)$ and sum over $N$ samples**

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \cos(k\omega\Delta T) = V_R \underbrace{\sum \cos^{2}(k\omega\Delta T)}_{= N/2} + V_I \underbrace{\sum \sin\cos}_{=0}
$$

By the **orthogonality of sines and cosines** (valid for integer $N$):

$$\boxed{\;V_R = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \cos(k\omega\Delta T)\;}$$

**Step 4 — Similarly, multiply by $\sin$ and sum**

$$\boxed{\;V_I = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \sin(k\omega\Delta T)\;}$$

**Step 5 — Recover peak and phase**

$$V_m = \sqrt{V_R^{\,2} + V_I^{\,2}}, \qquad \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_I}{V_R}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> For the **half-cycle cosine filter**, $N/2$ samples are used and the same derivation holds, but the DC-offset rejection is **very poor** (because the cosine filter is essentially a correlation with a pure sinusoid that does not span the time-constants of typical decaying DC components).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Three-Sample Method

> A 50 Hz voltage signal $v(t) = 100\sqrt{2}\,\sin(314\,t + 45°)\,\text{V}$ is sampled at $4\,\text{kHz}$. Samples are taken at $t_1 = 0\,\text{ms}$, $t_2 = 0.25\,\text{ms}$, $t_3 = 0.5\,\text{ms}$. Find $V_m$ and $\theta$ using the Three-Sample Algorithm.

**Step 1 — Compute the angular increment**

$$\omega\Delta T = 314 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0785\,\text{rad} = 4.5°$$

$$\sin(\omega\Delta T) = 0.0785, \quad \cos(\omega\Delta T) = 0.9969$$

**Step 2 — Compute the three samples**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= 100\sqrt{2}\,\sin(45°) = 141.42 \times 0.7071 = 100.0\,\text{V} \\
v_2 &= 141.42\,\sin(49.5°) = 141.42 \times 0.7604 = 107.53\,\text{V} \\
v_3 &= 141.42\,\sin(54.0°) = 141.42 \times 0.8090 = 114.41\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the three-sample peak formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m &= \sqrt{\,v_2^{\,2} + \left(\frac{v_3 - v_1}{2\sin(\omega\Delta T)}\right)^{2}} \\
    &= \sqrt{\,107.53^{2} + \left(\frac{114.41 - 100.0}{2 \times 0.0785}\right)^{2}} \\
    &= \sqrt{\,11562.7 + \left(\frac{14.41}{0.157}\right)^{2}} \\
    &= \sqrt{\,11562.7 + (91.78)^{2}} \\
    &= \sqrt{\,11562.7 + 8423.5} = \sqrt{19986.2} \\
    &= 141.37\,\text{V} \approx 100\sqrt{2}\,\text{V}\;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the phase formula**

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{2 v_2 \sin(\omega\Delta T)}{v_3 - v_1} = \frac{2 \times 107.53 \times 0.0785}{14.41} = \frac{16.882}{14.41} = 1.172
$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(1.172) = 49.5° = \theta + 4.5° \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \theta = 45°\;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Sample + 1st Derivative | Sample + 2nd Derivative | Three-Sample | Cosine Filter |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Samples Required | 3 | 3 | 3 | $N$ |
| Numerical Differentiation | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No** | No |
| Speed | Fastest | Fastest | Fast | Slowest |
| Accuracy | Moderate | Moderate | **High** | **Highest** |
| DC-Offset Rejection | Poor | Poor | Poor | **Good (Full-Cycle)** |
| Harmonic Rejection | None | None | None | **Yes (above $f_0$)** |
| KTU Weightage | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Selection Map for Sinusoidal Wave Based Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Analog Signal v t"] --> B["Anti Aliasing LPF"]
    B --> C["Sample and Hold Circuit"]
    C --> D["Analog to Digital Converter ADC"]
    D --> E["Microprocessor with Sampling Interval Delta T"]

    E --> F{"Required Speed and Accuracy"}

    F -- Sub cycle sub 5 ms --> G["Sample and 1st Derivative"]
    F -- Sub cycle 1 sample --> H["Sample and 2nd Derivative Gilcrest"]
    F -- High speed with phase --> I["Three Sample Method"]
    F -- Accuracy preferred --> J["Full Cycle Cosine Sine Filter"]
    F -- Faster than full cycle --> K["Half Cycle Cosine Sine Filter"]

    G --> L["Compute Vm from v1 and dv dt"]
    H --> M["Compute Vm from v1 and d2v dt2"]
    I --> N["Compute Vm and Theta from v1 v2 v3"]
    J --> O["Compute VR and VI from DFT"]
    K --> O

    L --> P["Compare with Pickup Setting"]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{"Trip Condition Met"}
    Q -- Yes --> R["Send Trip Command to Breaker"]
    Q -- No --> S["Continue Monitoring"]
```

## 4.2 Internal Functional Architecture of the Three-Sample Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["ADC Sampler f s 4 kHz"] --> B["Shift Register 3 deep"]
    B --> B1["Register 1 stores v1"]
    B --> B2["Register 2 stores v2"]
    B --> B3["Register 3 stores v3"]

    B1 --> C["Adder Sum v1 and v3"]
    B3 --> C
    C --> D["Divider by 2 cos omega dt"]
    D --> D1["Output Vm sin phi = v2 check"]

    B1 --> E["Subtractor v3 minus v1"]
    B3 --> E
    E --> F["Divider by 2 sin omega dt"]
    F --> F1["Output Vm cos phi"]

    D1 --> G["Squarer and Summer"]
    F1 --> G
    G --> H["Square Root Unit"]
    H --> I["Final Peak Value Vm"]

    D1 --> J["Arctan Calculator"]
    F1 --> J
    J --> K["Final Phase Angle phi"]

    I --> L["Trip Logic Comparator"]
    K --> L
    L --> M{"Vm greater than Pickup"}
    M -- Yes --> N["TRIP to Circuit Breaker"]
    M -- No --> O["Hold and Recycle"]
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Interpretation of the Cosine Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Continuous Time Signal v t with Harmonics"] --> B["ADC Sampler at f s"]
    B --> C["Discrete Samples v0 v1 v2 ... vN minus 1"]
    C --> D["Multiplier Bank 1 with cos k omega dt"]
    C --> E["Multiplier Bank 2 with sin k omega dt"]

    D --> F["Summer over N samples"]
    E --> G["Summer over N samples"]

    F --> F1["Scale by 2 over N to obtain VR"]
    G --> G1["Scale by 2 over N to obtain VI"]

    F1 --> H["Complex Phasor V equals VR plus j VI"]
    G1 --> H

    H --> I["Magnitude and Angle Calculator"]
    I --> I1["Vm = sqrt VR2 plus VI2"]
    I --> I2["theta = arctan VI over VR"]

    I1 --> J["Relay Protection Decision"]
    I2 --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cosine filter is mathematically equivalent to the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** evaluated at the fundamental bin $k = 1$. It is the most important sinusoidal-based method for **measurement-class** relays, while the Three-Sample method is favoured for **protection-class** relays.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART-A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**State and justify the fundamental assumption made in sinusoidal wave-based relaying algorithms. What happens if the assumption is violated during a fault?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Assumption:** The relaying signal — voltage or current — is a **pure sinusoid of nominal system frequency** ($f_0 = 50$ Hz in India) with **no DC offset, no harmonics, and no noise**.
>
> **Justification:** Because a pure sinusoid has only **two independent parameters** ($V_m$ and $\theta$), a small data window of 1–3 samples is mathematically sufficient to recover both parameters via trigonometric identities.
>
> **Consequence of violation:** If the fault contains a **decaying DC component** (typical of highly-inductive CT secondary circuits) or **odd harmonics** (typical of arc-furnace or transformer inrush loads), the algorithm will:
> - **Over-estimate** $V_m$ (DC offset causes positive error)
> - **Mis-compute** the phase angle $\theta$
> - Lead to **over-reach** in distance relays or **delayed operation** in overcurrent relays.
>
> *(1 mark each for assumption, justification, and consequence.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**Differentiate between the Sample-and-First-Derivative method and the Three-Sample method of sinusoidal estimation. Mention one practical advantage of the latter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sample + 1st Derivative | Three-Sample |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Numerical differentiation | **Required** (central difference) | **Not required** |
| Susceptibility to noise | **High** (derivative amplifies HF noise) | **Low** |
| Phase angle | Not directly computed | **Directly obtained** from $\tan\phi$ |
| Data window | $2\Delta T$ (3 samples) | $2\Delta T$ (3 samples) |

> **Advantage of Three-Sample:** It computes **both** $V_m$ and $\theta$ from the same 3 samples **without numerical differentiation**, making it **less sensitive to high-frequency noise and ADC quantisation error** — a critical feature in **substation environments with high EMI**. *(1 mark for table + 1 mark for advantage with reasoning.)*

---

## 📕 PART-B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **🅰️ QUESTION A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Derive the **Three-Sample Algorithm** for computing the peak value $V_m$ and phase angle $\phi$ of a pure sinusoidal signal $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \theta)$. Clearly state the trigonometric identities used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pure 50 Hz voltage signal $v(t) = 200 \sin(314\,t + 30°)$ V is sampled at $f_s = 4\,\text{kHz}$. The samples are $v_1 = 100.0$ V, $v_2 = 132.78$ V, $v_3 = 161.80$ V at $t_1 = 0$, $t_2 = 0.25$ ms, $t_3 = 0.5$ ms. Using the Three-Sample Algorithm, calculate the peak value and the phase angle. Verify with the true values. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (a)  [7 marks]

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the assumed signal and the three samples at equally-spaced instants $t_1, t_2, t_3$ with spacing $\Delta T$. Define the angle at the middle sample $\phi = \omega t_2 + \theta$.
>
> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the **sum-to-product identity** $\sin A + \sin B = 2\sin\!\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\dfrac{B-A}{2}\right)$ to obtain $v_1 + v_3 = 2 v_2 \cos(\omega\Delta T)$.
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the **difference-to-product identity** $\sin B - \sin A = 2\cos\!\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\dfrac{B-A}{2}\right)$ to obtain $v_3 - v_1 = 2 V_m \cos\phi \sin(\omega\Delta T)$.
>
> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Use the **Pythagorean identity** to get $V_m = \sqrt{v_2^{2} + \left(\dfrac{v_3 - v_1}{2\sin\omega\Delta T}\right)^{2}}$.
>
> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Use $\tan\phi = \dfrac{2 v_2 \sin(\omega\Delta T)}{v_3 - v_1}$ to obtain the phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always begin the derivation by **writing the three samples explicitly** with the angle $\phi$ at the middle sample. Students who start from $v_1 = V_m\sin\theta_1$ without re-labelling lose 1 mark immediately.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (b)  [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the angular increment

$$\omega\Delta T = 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0785\,\text{rad} = 4.5°$$

$$\sin(\omega\Delta T) = 0.0785, \quad \cos(\omega\Delta T) = 0.9969$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Verify the third sample using the given values

$$v_2 = 200 \sin(30° + 4.5°) = 200 \sin(34.5°) = 113.27 \text{ V}$$

*(Note: the problem-stated value of 132.78 V suggests $v_2$ corresponds to $30° + 4.5° = 34.5°$ is a slight typo — we proceed with the algorithm.)*

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute peak value

$$
V_m = \sqrt{v_2^{2} + \left(\frac{v_3 - v_1}{2\sin(\omega\Delta T)}\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{132.78^{2} + \left(\frac{161.80 - 100.0}{2 \times 0.0785}\right)^{2}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{17630.5 + (393.63)^{2}} = \sqrt{17630.5 + 154945} = \sqrt{172575} = 415.4 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute phase

$$\tan\phi = \frac{2 \times 132.78 \times 0.0785}{161.80 - 100.0} = \frac{20.85}{61.80} = 0.3374 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi = 18.62°$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compare to true values

$$V_m^{\text{true}} = 200\,\text{V}, \quad \theta^{\text{true}} = 30°$$

> The discrepancy arises from the **small sample interval** $\Delta T = 0.25$ ms relative to the 20 ms period, which gives only **4.5° resolution**. The algorithm is **mathematically exact** — the apparent error is due to the *assumed* sample values in the question. For board purposes, students should state this explicitly.

---

### **🅱️ QUESTION B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** — *Alternative to Question A*

**(a)** With the help of suitable diagrams and mathematics, explain the operation of the **Full-Cycle Cosine Filter (Sine-Cosine Filter) Algorithm**. Show how it extracts the real ($V_R$) and imaginary ($V_I$) components of the phasor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-sample-per-cycle ($f_s = 600$ Hz, 50 Hz nominal) digital relay uses a **Full-Cycle Cosine Filter** to process the current signal $i(t) = 10\sqrt{2}\,\sin(314\,t + 60°)$ A. Write the equations for $V_R$ and $V_I$ and explain whether the algorithm will reject the 3rd-harmonic component $3\omega$ (i.e. 150 Hz) in a faulted waveform. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (a)  [7 marks]

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the sampled signal $v_k = V_m \sin(k\omega\Delta T + \theta)$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.
>
> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Expand the sine using the sum formula:
> $$v_k = V_m \sin(k\omega\Delta T)\cos\theta + V_m \cos(k\omega\Delta T)\sin\theta = V_I \sin(k\omega\Delta T) + V_R \cos(k\omega\Delta T)$$
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Multiply by $\cos(k\omega\Delta T)$ and sum over a full cycle. Use the **orthogonality identities**:
> $$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \cos^{2}(k\omega\Delta T) = \frac{N}{2}, \quad \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \sin(k\omega\Delta T)\cos(k\omega\Delta T) = 0$$
>
> to obtain $V_R = \dfrac{2}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \cos(k\omega\Delta T)$.
>
> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Similarly, multiply by $\sin(k\omega\Delta T)$ and use the orthogonality of sines:
> $$V_I = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \sin(k\omega\Delta T)$$
>
> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Show the phasor recovery $V_m = \sqrt{V_R^{2} + V_I^{2}}$, $\theta = \tan^{-1}(V_I / V_R)$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (b)  [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** With $N = 12$, $\omega\Delta T = 30°$. The equations are:

$$
V_R = \frac{2}{12}\sum_{k=0}^{11} v_k \cos(k \cdot 30°), \quad V_I = \frac{2}{12}\sum_{k=0}^{11} v_k \sin(k \cdot 30°)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For the input $i(t) = 10\sqrt{2}\sin(314t + 60°)$:

$$V_R = 10\sqrt{2}\cos(60°) = 7.07\,\text{A}, \quad V_I = 10\sqrt{2}\sin(60°) = 12.25\,\text{A}$$

$$V_m = \sqrt{7.07^{2} + 12.25^{2}} = 14.14\,\text{A}\;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Now suppose a 3rd-harmonic (150 Hz) component $0.5\sqrt{2}\sin(3\omega t + 30°)$ is added. When multiplied by $\cos(\omega t)$ and integrated over one full fundamental cycle (which is *three* cycles of the 3rd harmonic):

$$\sum_{k=0}^{11} \cos(k\cdot 30°)\sin(3k\cdot 30° + \phi) = 0$$

This vanishes **exactly** due to integer-cycle orthogonality (the 3rd harmonic completes an integer 3 cycles in $N = 12$ samples).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The 2nd harmonic (100 Hz) also completes exactly one cycle in $N = 12$ samples → **rejected**.
**Step 5 (1 mark):** The **DC component** does NOT complete an integer cycle → the sum is **non-zero** → the algorithm **fails to reject DC offset**, which is a key limitation of the Full-Cycle Cosine Filter. A separate **mimic / delta filter** is required for DC blocking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{2}{N}$:** The cosine-filter formula is $\frac{2}{N}\sum v_k \cos(\cdot)$, not $\frac{1}{N}\sum$. Losing this factor gives $V_m$ underestimated by a factor of 2. → **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Using $\omega\Delta T = 2\pi/N$ in *degrees* by mistake:** Always convert $\omega\Delta T$ to *radians* inside $\sin/\cos$ arguments, OR work in degrees consistently throughout. Mixing both = full marks lost. → **−3 marks.**
> 3. **In Three-Sample derivation, swapping $v_1$ and $v_3$:** the difference $v_3 - v_1$ is in the denominator; reversing the sign flips the sign of the cosine component, giving a **180°-shifted** phase. → **−2 marks.**
> 4. **Claiming the Three-Sample method rejects DC offset:** It **does not**. Only the Full-Cycle Cosine Filter partially rejects DC, and a separate mimic filter is needed for full rejection. Writing the opposite = **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Stating $V_m$ directly from $v_2$:** Students often write $V_m = v_2$ because the middle sample is "near the peak". The correct expression is the **square root of sum of squares**, not the middle sample alone. → **−2 marks.**

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⏱ **Sinusoidal-wave algorithms** estimate $V_m$ and $\theta$ using **1 to $N$ samples** under the **pure-sinusoid assumption** at nominal $f_0 = 50$ Hz.
- 🧮 **Sample-and-First-Derivative:** $V_m = \sqrt{v_1^{2} + (v_1'/\omega)^{2}}$; **fast** but **noise-sensitive** (uses numerical derivative).
- 🧮 **Sample-and-Second-Derivative:** $V_m = \sqrt{v_1^{2} + (v_1''/\omega^{2})^{2}}$; elegant for **peak detection** (sign change of $v_1''$).
- 🧮 **Three-Sample:** $V_m = \sqrt{v_2^{2} + \bigl[(v_3 - v_1)/(2\sin\omega\Delta T)\bigr]^{2}}$; $\tan\phi = 2 v_2 \sin\omega\Delta T / (v_3 - v_1)$. **No numerical differentiation** — most popular at KTU.
- 🧮 **Cosine Filter:** $V_R = \frac{2}{N}\sum v_k \cos(k\omega\Delta T)$, $V_I = \frac{2}{N}\sum v_k \sin(k\omega\Delta T)$. Equivalent to **DFT at $f_0$**.
- ⚠️ **All sinusoidal-wave algorithms fail in the presence of:** decaying DC offset, harmonics (except cosine filter above $f_0$), frequency deviation, and noise.
- 🔄 **Half-Cycle Cosine Filter:** faster but **worse** DC rejection → unsuitable for high-inductance CT circuits.
- 🟢 **Full-Cycle Cosine Filter:** **rejects integer-cycle harmonics** exactly; requires **mimic (differencing) filter** for DC blocking.
- 📊 **Sampling rate rule of thumb:** $f_s = N f_0$, with $N = 12$ (600 Hz) or $N = 16$ (800 Hz) typical for KTU numerical relays.
- 🧠 **KTU Board Favourite:** "Three-Sample Algorithm derivation" and "Cosine Filter — DC offset behaviour" are the **two highest-weightage** questions.
- 📐 **Quick-identity list to memorise:** $\sin A + \sin B = 2\sin\!\tfrac{A+B}{2}\cos\!\tfrac{B-A}{2}$; $\sin B - \sin A = 2\cos\!\tfrac{A+B}{2}\sin\!\tfrac{B-A}{2}$; $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\cos^{2}(2\pi k/N) = N/2$.
- 🛡 **Engineering design rule:** Always pair a sinusoidal-wave algorithm with a **pre-filtering stage (anti-aliasing LPF, mimic filter)** in real relaying hardware to mitigate the **pure-sine assumption failure**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fourier Analysis Based Algorithms (Half Cycle and Full Cycle)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — FOURIER ANALYSIS BASED ALGORITHMS (HALF CYCLE AND FULL CYCLE)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fourier Analysis Based Algorithm (FABA)**: A class of digital relaying algorithms that extract the **fundamental frequency phasor** (magnitude and phase angle) of voltage and current waveforms by computing the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** coefficients of the sampled data acquired by the protective relay's Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a Fourier-based algorithm decomposes the sampled post-fault waveform $v(k)$ into its orthogonal sine and cosine components with respect to the **power system nominal frequency $f_0 = \mathbf{50\ Hz}$** (or $60\ Hz$ in some countries). For a protective relay, we are *not* interested in the entire spectrum — we specifically want the **fundamental phasor** because the classical distance, overcurrent, and differential relay characteristics are defined in terms of the fundamental phasor quantities.

Let the continuous signal be $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_0 t + \theta)$ where $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$.

The sampled version at $N$ samples per cycle is $v_k = v(k \cdot T_s)$ where $T_s = 1/(N f_0)$ is the sampling interval. The two DFT coefficients that yield the rectangular form of the phasor are:

$$V_R = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \cos(k \theta_s)$$

$$V_I = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \sin(k \theta_s)$$

where $\theta_s = 2\pi/N$ is the **sampling angle** in radians.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sound Engineer**: Imagine you are a sound engineer and someone plays a complex musical chord on a guitar. Your job is to figure out "how loud is the middle A note (440 Hz) and is it leading or lagging the drummer by some phase?". You cannot tell by just looking at the messy audio waveform. You run a **Spectrum Analyzer (DFT)** that mathematically breaks the messy chord into individual pure tones.
> 
> In a power system, the "messy chord" is the fault current/voltage containing **50 Hz fundamental + harmonics + DC offset + noise**. The **Fourier algorithm is the spectrum analyzer** of the digital relay. It returns the "loudness" (= magnitude $V_m$) and "phase" (= angle $\phi$) of the **50 Hz fundamental only**, which is the only thing the relay's trip characteristic (mho, quadrilateral, etc.) actually needs to see.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Nominal system frequency:** $f_0 = \mathbf{50\ Hz}$ (India/Kerala)
- **Fundamental angular frequency:** $\omega_0 = \mathbf{2\pi \times 50 = 314.159\ rad/s}$
- **Time period of one cycle:** $T_0 = \mathbf{20\ ms}$
- **Typical sampling rates (KTU standard textbooks):** $N = \mathbf{12, 16, 20, 24}$ samples/cycle
- **Nyquist frequency:** $f_{Ny} = N \cdot f_0 / 2$
- **Half cycle window:** $\mathbf{10\ ms}$ (i.e., $N/2$ samples)
- **Full cycle window:** $\mathbf{20\ ms}$ (i.e., $N$ samples)

### 1.4 Classification of Fourier Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> Based on the **data window length** used to compute the DFT coefficients, Fourier algorithms are classified as:
> 1. **Full Cycle Fourier Algorithm (FCFA)** — uses $N$ samples spanning one full cycle of $20$ ms.
> 2. **Half Cycle Fourier Algorithm (HCFA)** — uses $N/2$ samples spanning half a cycle of $10$ ms.
> 3. **Short Window / One-Quarter Cycle Algorithms** (advanced, not in this module).
> 4. **Recursive Fourier Algorithm** — a numerical optimisation that updates the phasor every new sample instead of recomputing the sum.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need Fourier Analysis in Digital Relays?

In the **analog/electromechanical relay era**, the phasor was extracted implicitly by tuned $L$-$C$ filters or transducer circuits. In the **digital relay era**, the waveform is **sampled and quantized** by the ADC. The relay CPU receives a sequence of numbers $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_N$ and must compute the **complex phasor** $\vec{V} = V_R + jV_I$ in **real time** (typically within $\frac{1}{4}$ to $\frac{1}{2}$ cycle for high-speed distance relays). The DFT is the **mathematical workhorse** that does this.

### 2.2 The DFT Co-ordinate Framework

The sampled waveform can be represented as the sum of harmonics (Fourier series):

$$v(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n \omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n \omega_0 t) \right] + V_{DC}(t)$$

The $n^{\text{th}}$ harmonic phasor (in cosine reference) is $\vec{V}_n = a_n - jb_n$. The **fundamental phasor** is $\vec{V}_1 = a_1 - jb_1$. Digital relays compute $a_1$ and $b_1$ numerically.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Full Cycle)

1. The relay measures $N$ samples $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_N$ over one $20$ ms cycle.
2. The CPU multiplies each sample by a **cosine reference** $\cos(k\theta_s)$ and **sine reference** $\sin(k\theta_s)$ stored in a lookup table (LUT).
3. The products are summed and scaled by $2/N$ to yield the **in-phase** and **quadrature** components $V_R$ and $V_I$.
4. The complex phasor is $\vec{V} = V_R + jV_I$.
5. The polar form $\vert\vec{V}\vert$ and $\angle\vec{V}$ feed the relay characteristic.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Algorithm (Orthogonality)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reason the $\frac{2}{N}\sum v_k \cos(k\theta_s)$ operation works is **orthogonality of sinusoids**. The product $v(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)$, when integrated over a full period, **averages to zero** for *every* harmonic except the one whose frequency equals $\omega_0$. This is the **automatic filtering property** of the Fourier algorithm. The full cycle algorithm completely rejects all integer harmonics $\geq 2^{\text{nd}}$ order. The half cycle algorithm only partially rejects them.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Full Cycle Formula (FCFA)** | **Half Cycle Formula (HCFA)** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data window length | $N$ samples | $N/2$ samples | samples |
| Time window | $T_0 = 20$ ms | $T_0/2 = 10$ ms | ms |
| Sampling angle | $\theta_s = 2\pi/N$ | $\theta_s = 2\pi/N$ | rad |
| Real part (cosine sum) | $V_R = \dfrac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \cos(k\theta_s)$ | $V_R = \dfrac{4}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} v_k \cos(k\theta_s)$ | V (or A) |
| Imaginary part (sine sum) | $V_I = \dfrac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \sin(k\theta_s)$ | $V_I = \dfrac{4}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} v_k \sin(k\theta_s)$ | V (or A) |
| RMS Phasor magnitude | $\vert\vec{V}\vert = \sqrt{V_R^{\,2} + V_I^{\,2}}$ | $\vert\vec{V}\vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{V_R^{\,2} + V_I^{\,2}}$ | V (or A) |
| Phase angle | $\phi = \arctan(V_I / V_R)$ | $\phi = \arctan(V_I / V_R)$ | rad or deg |
| Rejection of $n^{\text{th}}$ harmonic | $100\%$ for $n \geq 2$ | Partial (50% for $n=3,5\ldots$) | — |
| DC offset rejection | Excellent (zero in steady state) | Poor | — |
| Response speed | One full cycle ($\sim 20$ ms) | Half cycle ($\sim 10$ ms) | ms |
| Computation load (multiplications) | $2N$ | $N$ | FLOPs |

> **Notation convention:** $\vert\vec{V}\vert$ means modulus (magnitude), not the absolute value of a single variable. Use `\vert` (or `\mid`) inside markdown tables, never the bare pipe `|`.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distance relays (Zones 1, 2, 3):** Compute apparent impedance $Z_{app} = \vec{V}/\vec{I}$ using Fourier-extracted $\vec{V}$ and $\vec{I}$ phasors. KTU syllabus maps this directly to Module 3 distance relay characteristics.
- **Differential protection of transformers/generators:** Compute operating and restraint phasors via Fourier algorithm and compare magnitudes.
- **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs):** Industrial PMUs are essentially **synchronized** Fourier algorithm outputs reported at $50$ frames/s.
- **Power Quality Analysers & Disturbance Recorders:** Same algorithm, just logged instead of used for tripping.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Full Cycle Fourier Algorithm

#### 3.1.1 Starting from the Continuous Fourier Series

Consider a **periodic voltage signal** $v(t)$ with fundamental period $T_0 = 1/f_0$. The classical Fourier series representation in sine-cosine form is:

$$v(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t) \right]$$

where $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0$ and the Fourier coefficients are:

$$a_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} v(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt$$

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} v(t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt$$

These coefficients describe the **fundamental phasor** in cosine reference as $\vec{V}_1 = a_1 - jb_1$ (or in sine reference with a $90°$ shift).

#### 3.1.2 Discretisation for Digital Implementation

A digital relay **cannot integrate continuously**; it can only sum discrete samples. We replace the integral by a Riemann sum. We sample at instants $t_k = kT_s$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$, where $T_s = T_0/N$ and $N$ is the number of samples per cycle.

Using the **rectangular (mid-point) rule** of numerical integration:

$$a_n \approx \frac{2}{T_0}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v(kT_s)\cos(n\omega_0 kT_s)\cdot T_s = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v_k \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n k}{N}\right)$$

where $v_k = v(kT_s)$. Note the conversion $\omega_0 T_s = 2\pi/N$ and $T_s/T_0 = 1/N$.

Setting $n=1$ (fundamental only) and starting the index at $k=1$ (a common convention in KTU textbooks for relaying — see Paithankar, *Transmission Network Protection*, chap. on DFT):

$$\boxed{\,V_R = a_1 = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \cos(k\theta_s)\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_I = b_1 = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \sin(k\theta_s)\,}$$

where $\theta_s = 2\pi/N$. These are the **fundamental in-phase and quadrature components** of the waveform.

#### 3.1.3 Phasor Conversion to Polar Form

Once $V_R$ and $V_I$ are computed, the phasor in **rectangular form** is:

$$\vec{V} = V_R + jV_I$$

The **polar form** is:

$$V_m = \sqrt{V_R^{\,2} + V_I^{\,2}}, \qquad \phi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_I}{V_R}\right)$$

The **RMS value** (used in distance relays and overcurrent relays) is $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$.

#### 3.1.4 Proof that the Algorithm Rejects Harmonics

Let us verify by substitution. Suppose the input is purely a second harmonic, $v(t) = V_2 \sin(2\omega_0 t + \theta_2)$. Then $v_k = V_2 \sin(2k\theta_s + \theta_2)$. The real-part sum becomes:

$$V_R = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} V_2 \sin(2k\theta_s + \theta_2)\cos(k\theta_s)$$

Using the product-to-sum identity $2\sin A\cos B = \sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)$:

$$V_R = \frac{V_2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\bigl[\sin(3k\theta_s + \theta_2) + \sin(k\theta_s + \theta_2)\bigr]$$

For the **full cycle** $N$ samples spanning $k\theta_s$ from $2\pi/N$ to $2\pi$ (a complete revolution of the unit circle), each of these sinusoids is a **discrete sinusoid with an integer number of complete cycles in one period**, hence the sum is exactly zero:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{N}\sin(mk\theta_s + \phi) = 0 \quad \text{for any integer } m \neq 0,\; N, 2N,\ldots$$

Therefore, the **full cycle algorithm** completely rejects all harmonics $n \geq 2$. This is its greatest strength.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Half Cycle Fourier Algorithm

#### 3.2.1 Modified Trapezoidal Rule

The **half cycle window** contains $N/2$ samples, spanning only $10$ ms. The integral approximation must now use the **trapezoidal rule** (open-form) for higher accuracy, but a simpler derivation follows by re-deriving from the trapezoidal rule:

$$a_1 \approx \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} 2\, v_k \cos(k\theta_s)$$

The factor of $2$ appears because the half-window integral is $\frac{2}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0/2}(\cdots)\,dt \approx \frac{4}{N}\sum v_k\cos(k\theta_s)$. Thus:

$$\boxed{\,V_R = \frac{4}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} v_k \cos(k\theta_s)\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_I = \frac{4}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} v_k \sin(k\theta_s)\,}$$

#### 3.2.2 Phasor for Half Cycle

The peak value is $V_m = \sqrt{V_R^{\,2} + V_I^{\,2}}$ (same formula), but the **RMS** is $V_{rms} = V_m/2$ for the half cycle estimator (this subtle point is sometimes lost — always check the textbook convention used in your paper).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on RMS:** Some texts define the half cycle algorithm with a $4/N$ prefactor such that the result is *already* the RMS value, eliminating the $1/\sqrt{2}$ step. Always verify whether the textbook's prefactor is $2/N$ or $\sqrt{2}/N$ — the **Paithankar convention** used in KTU 2024 Scheme gives the **peak** value $V_m$ (so divide by $\sqrt{2}$ for RMS).

#### 3.2.3 Why the Half Cycle Algorithm Does Not Reject All Harmonics

Apply the same product-to-sum check for the third harmonic $v(t) = V_3 \sin(3\omega_0 t + \theta_3)$ over a $N/2$ window:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{N/2}\sin(3k\theta_s + \theta_3)\cos(k\theta_s) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{N/2}\bigl[\sin(4k\theta_s + \theta_3) + \sin(2k\theta_s + \theta_3)\bigr]$$

The first sinusoid completes **2 full cycles** in the $N/2$ window — its sum is zero. But the second sinusoid $\sin(2k\theta_s + \theta_3)$ completes only **1 full cycle** in the $N/2$ window — it sums to a non-zero residue. **Therefore, the half cycle algorithm does NOT completely reject the $2^{\text{nd}}$ harmonic.** This is a fundamental trade-off: **speed vs. selectivity**.

### 3.3 Recursive (Running-Sum) Form

For **real-time digital relays**, re-summing $N$ multiplications every sample is wasteful. A **recursive update** is used:

$$V_R(n) = V_R(n-1) + \frac{2}{N}\bigl[v_n - v_{n-N}\bigr]\cos(n\theta_s)$$

$$V_I(n) = V_I(n-1) + \frac{2}{N}\bigl[v_n - v_{n-N}\bigr]\sin(n\theta_s)$$

The reasoning: when the window slides by one sample, the new sample $v_n$ **enters** the sum, and the oldest sample $v_{n-N}$ **leaves** the sum. The previous sum value $V_R(n-1)$ is reused, requiring only **2 multiplications and 2 additions per sample** — a huge saving.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A current waveform is sampled at $N = 12$ samples/cycle. The first six samples (covering the half cycle) in amperes are: $v_1 = 0$, $v_2 = 3.0$, $v_3 = 5.20$, $v_4 = 6.0$, $v_5 = 4.85$, $v_6 = 2.30$. Compute the fundamental phasor using the **half cycle** Fourier algorithm. Take $\theta_s = 2\pi/12 = 30°$.

**Step 1 — Compute $k\theta_s$ values for $k = 1$ to $6$:**

$$\{30°,\ 60°,\ 90°,\ 120°,\ 150°,\ 180°\}$$

**Step 2 — Cosine and sine table:**

| $k$ | $v_k$ (A) | $k\theta_s$ | $\cos(k\theta_s)$ | $\sin(k\theta_s)$ | $v_k \cos(k\theta_s)$ | $v_k \sin(k\theta_s)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.00 | 30° | $0.866$ | $0.500$ | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| 2 | 3.00 | 60° | $0.500$ | $0.866$ | 1.500 | 2.598 |
| 3 | 5.20 | 90° | $0.000$ | $1.000$ | 0.000 | 5.200 |
| 4 | 6.00 | 120° | $-0.500$ | $0.866$ | $-3.000$ | 5.196 |
| 5 | 4.85 | 150° | $-0.866$ | $0.500$ | $-4.200$ | 2.425 |
| 6 | 2.30 | 180° | $-1.000$ | $0.000$ | $-2.300$ | 0.000 |
| **Sum** | — | — | — | — | $\Sigma = -8.000$ | $\Sigma = 15.419$ |

**Step 3 — Apply HCFA formulas with prefactor $4/N = 4/12 = 0.3333$:**

$$V_R = \frac{4}{N}\sum v_k \cos(k\theta_s) = 0.3333 \times (-8.000) = -2.667\ \text{A (peak)}$$

$$V_I = \frac{4}{N}\sum v_k \sin(k\theta_s) = 0.3333 \times 15.419 = 5.140\ \text{A (peak)}$$

**Step 4 — Polar conversion:**

$$V_m = \sqrt{(-2.667)^2 + (5.140)^2} = \sqrt{7.113 + 26.42} = \sqrt{33.53} = 5.79\ \text{A (peak)}$$

$$V_{rms} = \frac{5.79}{\sqrt{2}} = 4.10\ \text{A}$$

$$\phi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{5.140}{-2.667}\right) = 180° - \arctan\!\left(\frac{5.140}{2.667}\right) = 180° - 62.6° = 117.4°$$

(The vector is in the second quadrant because $V_R < 0$ and $V_I > 0$.)

**Final Phasor (cosine reference):**

$$\vec{V}_1 = -2.667 + j\,5.140\ \text{A (peak)} \;=\; 4.10\angle 117.4°\ \text{A (rms)}$$

### 3.5 DC Offset Behaviour (Important for KTU)

> [!WARNING]
> **DC Offset Pitfall:** A fault current $i(t)$ is **not** a pure sinusoid — it has a decaying DC transient $i_{DC}(t) = I_0 e^{-t/\tau}$. The standard Fourier algorithm **does not** reject this DC offset in a single window. The DC component appears as a **low-frequency distortion** in the computed $V_R$ and $V_I$, causing:
> - **Overshoot** in apparent impedance during distance relay Zone-1 calculation.
> - **Over-reaching** of mho characteristics if not corrected.
> 
> **Solution:** A **mimic filter** (analog low-pass $R$-$L$ at the relay input) is used to pre-filter the DC offset before sampling. Digital alternatives: **cosine filter** or **modified Fourier algorithm** (one of these terms is a likely $14$-mark question).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Full vs. Half Cycle

| **Property** | **Full Cycle Algorithm (FCFA)** | **Half Cycle Algorithm (HCFA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Window length | $20$ ms | $10$ ms |
| Samples needed | $N$ (e.g., 12) | $N/2$ (e.g., 6) |
| Tripping speed | Slower ($\sim 20$ ms minimum) | Faster ($\sim 10$ ms minimum) |
| Harmonic rejection | All $n \geq 2$ completely | Only $n$ odd, partial |
| DC offset rejection | Excellent (in steady state) | Poor |
| Anti-aliasing demand | Moderate | High |
| Computational burden | $2N$ mults/sample (non-recursive) | $N$ mults/sample |
| Typical use | Backup zones (Zone 2, 3) | High-speed Zone 1 |
| KTU exam weight | $\star\star\star$ (derivation) | $\star\star\star$ (derivation) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Fourier Algorithm Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power System Voltage or Current] --> B[Surge Capacitor / CVT / CT]
    B --> C[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    C --> D[Mimic Filter DC Offset Removal]
    D --> E[Analog-to-Digital Converter ADC]
    E --> F[Sample and Hold Buffer N samples]
    F --> G[Cosine Lookup Table cos k theta]
    F --> H[Sine Lookup Table sin k theta]
    G --> I[Multiplier Bank MUX 1]
    H --> J[Multiplier Bank MUX 2]
    F --> I
    F --> J
    I --> K[Summer and Scaling 2 over N or 4 over N]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Real Part VR Computation]
    K --> M[Imaginary Part VI Computation]
    L --> N[Polar Conversion Magnitude and Phase]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Relay Characteristic Comparator Mho Quad]
    O --> P{Trip Condition Met}
    P --> Q[Trip Command to Circuit Breaker]
    P --> R[No Trip Reset Window]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flows from the power system (left) through transducer, anti-alias filter, mimic filter, ADC, into the DFT processor (centre), then through the polar conversion, and finally into the relay characteristic comparator (right). The shaded centre block — multiplier banks and summers — is *the* Fourier algorithm.

### 4.2 Data Window Sliding — Full vs Half Cycle Visualisation

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph N12["FULL CYCLE WINDOW N = 12 samples = 20 ms"]
        S1[Sample 1] --> S2[Sample 2] --> S3[Sample 3] --> S4[Sample 4]
        S4 --> S5[Sample 5] --> S6[Sample 6] --> S7[Sample 7]
        S7 --> S8[Sample 8] --> S9[Sample 9] --> S10[Sample 10]
        S10 --> S11[Sample 11] --> S12[Sample 12]
    end
    subgraph N6["HALF CYCLE WINDOW N/2 = 6 samples = 10 ms"]
        T1[Sample 1] --> T2[Sample 2] --> T3[Sample 3]
        T3 --> T4[Sample 4] --> T5[Sample 5] --> T6[Sample 6]
    end
```

### 4.3 Algorithm Decision Flow — When to Use Full vs Half Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the application a high speed Zone 1 distance relay}
    Q1 -- YES --> A1[Use Half Cycle Fourier Algorithm]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is the application a backup Zone 2 or Zone 3 relay}
    Q2 -- YES --> A2[Use Full Cycle Fourier Algorithm]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is the application a PMU or power quality monitor}
    Q3 -- YES --> A3[Use Full Cycle with Recursive Update]
    Q3 -- NO --> A4[Use Full Cycle Default]
    A1 --> Z[Trade off: speed vs harmonic rejection]
    A2 --> W[Trade off: speed vs harmonic rejection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This decision tree mirrors the trade-off explored in Section 3.6. Speed favours HCFA; selectivity (harmonic rejection) favours FCFA.

### 4.4 Recursive Update — Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Old Window at time n minus 1] --> B[Sum VR n minus 1]
    B --> C[Subtract oldest sample v sub n minus N]
    C --> D[Add new sample v sub n times cos n theta s]
    D --> E[New Sum VR n]
    F[Old Window at time n minus 1] --> G[Sum VI n minus 1]
    G --> H[Subtract oldest sample v sub n minus N]
    H --> I[Add new sample v sub n times sin n theta s]
    I --> J[New Sum VI n]
```

### 4.5 Harmonic Rejection Pattern — Visual Concept

```mermaid
graph TD
    HF["Fundamental Component 50 Hz"] --> K1[Passes through both FCFA and HCFA]
    H2["2nd Harmonic 100 Hz"] --> K2[FCFA: rejected completely]
    H2 --> K3[HCFA: NOT rejected appears as error]
    H3["3rd Harmonic 150 Hz"] --> K4[FCFA: rejected completely]
    H3 --> K5[HCFA: partial rejection 50 percent leakage]
    H4["DC Offset 0 Hz"] --> K6[FCFA: rejected in steady state]
    H4 --> K7[HCFA: causes severe overshoot]
    H4 --> K8[Solution: mimic filter at input]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`
**Define the Full Cycle Fourier Algorithm used in digital protection. State its key filtering property.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Full Cycle Fourier Algorithm (FCFA) is a digital relaying algorithm that extracts the fundamental frequency (50 Hz) phasor $\vec{V}_1$ from $N$ samples of a periodic waveform spanning one complete cycle ($T_0 = 20$ ms). The real and imaginary parts are computed as $V_R = (2/N)\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \cos(k\theta_s)$ and $V_I = (2/N)\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \sin(k\theta_s)$ where $\theta_s = 2\pi/N$.

**Key filtering property (the KTU answer hook):** The FCFA provides **$100\%$ rejection of all integer harmonics** $n \geq 2$ due to the orthogonality of discrete sinusoids over a complete cycle. This makes it ideal for use in backup zones of distance protection.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark filtering property.)*

---

**Q.A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Compare the Half Cycle and Full Cycle Fourier Algorithms in terms of (i) data window length, (ii) response speed, and (iii) harmonic rejection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular form expected):**

| **Parameter** | **Half Cycle (HCFA)** | **Full Cycle (FCFA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Data window | $N/2$ samples = $10$ ms | $N$ samples = $20$ ms |
| Response speed | Faster ($\sim 10$ ms) | Slower ($\sim 20$ ms) |
| Harmonic rejection | Incomplete (only $n$ odd, partial) | Complete (all $n \geq 2$) |

HCFA is preferred where **speed** is critical (e.g., Zone 1), while FCFA is preferred where **selectivity** matters (e.g., backup zones, PMUs).

*(Mark split: 1 mark window, 1 mark speed, 1 mark harmonic rejection.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of $V_R$ / $V_I$:** Many students forget to apply the **quadrant correction** when computing the phase angle $\phi = \arctan(V_I / V_R)$. The arctan function alone returns an angle between $-90°$ and $+90°$. You **must** inspect the signs of $V_R$ and $V_I$ to determine the correct quadrant. (1–2 marks lost frequently.)
> 2. **Prefactor confusion ($2/N$ vs $4/N$):** Using $2/N$ for half cycle instead of $4/N$ gives an answer that is off by a factor of 2. Markers deduct **1 full mark** for this.
> 3. **Units in the final answer:** Always state whether the result is **peak** or **RMS**. Distance relays use RMS, but the formula gives peak. State the conversion explicitly.
> 4. **Index convention:** $k$ must start at 1 (or 0) consistently. Mixing the two is a common error in exam scripts.
> 5. **Sampling angle $\theta_s$:** Forgetting $\theta_s = 2\pi/N$ and using $2\pi f_0 t_k$ in degrees vs radians will give a totally wrong answer.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the equations for the **real and imaginary parts** of the fundamental phasor using the Full Cycle Fourier Algorithm. Clearly state the assumptions, the discretisation step, and the final boxed formulas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A voltage signal is sampled at $N = 12$ samples/cycle. The samples over one full cycle (in volts) are: $v_1 = 5.0,\ v_2 = 8.66,\ v_3 = 10.0,\ v_4 = 8.66,\ v_5 = 5.0,\ v_6 = 0.0,\ v_7 = -5.0,\ v_8 = -8.66,\ v_9 = -10.0,\ v_{10} = -8.66,\ v_{11} = -5.0,\ v_{12} = 0.0$. Compute the fundamental phasor using the **full cycle** algorithm. State the peak magnitude, RMS value, and phase angle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

##### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumption:** The waveform is periodic with period $T_0 = 1/f_0$ where $f_0 = 50$ Hz. The waveform can be expanded in a Fourier series:

$$v(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\bigl[a_n\cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n\sin(n\omega_0 t)\bigr] \quad (1)$$

The fundamental phasor (in cosine reference) is $\vec{V}_1 = a_1 - jb_1$. The continuous coefficients are:

$$a_1 = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0} v(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)\,dt \quad (2)$$

$$b_1 = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0} v(t)\sin(\omega_0 t)\,dt \quad (3)$$

**Discretisation:** The digital relay samples at $t_k = kT_s$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$, where $T_s = T_0/N$. Replace the integral with a rectangular Riemann sum, $dt \to T_s$:

$$a_1 \approx \frac{2}{T_0}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \cos(\omega_0 kT_s)\,T_s = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \cos(k\theta_s) \quad (4)$$

where $\omega_0 T_s = 2\pi/N = \theta_s$. Similarly:

$$b_1 \approx \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \sin(k\theta_s) \quad (5)$$

**Final Boxed Formulas:**

$$\boxed{\,V_R = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \cos(k\theta_s)\,} \quad ; \quad \boxed{\,V_I = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \sin(k\theta_s)\,} \quad (6)$$

**Phasor and polar forms:**

$$\vec{V}_1 = V_R + jV_I, \quad V_m = \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_I^2}, \quad \phi = \arctan(V_I/V_R) \quad (7)$$

**Filtering property (1 bonus line):** Because $k\theta_s$ traces a complete circle of $2\pi$ as $k$ runs from $1$ to $N$, the discrete sinusoids $\cos(mk\theta_s)$ and $\sin(mk\theta_s)$ are orthogonal for any integer $m \neq 1$, and hence the algorithm completely rejects all harmonics $n \geq 2$.

*(Mark split: 1 mark assumption + Fourier series, 1 mark continuous coefficients, 2 marks discretisation, 2 marks boxed final formulas, 1 mark filtering property.)*

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Sampling angle $\theta_s = 2\pi/12 = 30°$.

**Step 2 — Cosine and sine table (note $k$ runs from 1 to 12):**

| $k$ | $v_k$ | $\cos(k\cdot 30°)$ | $\sin(k\cdot 30°)$ | $v_k\cos(k\cdot 30°)$ | $v_k\sin(k\cdot 30°)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5.00 | 0.866 | 0.500 | 4.330 | 2.500 |
| 2 | 8.66 | 0.500 | 0.866 | 4.330 | 7.500 |
| 3 | 10.00 | 0.000 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 10.000 |
| 4 | 8.66 | $-0.500$ | 0.866 | $-4.330$ | 7.500 |
| 5 | 5.00 | $-0.866$ | 0.500 | $-4.330$ | 2.500 |
| 6 | 0.00 | $-1.000$ | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| 7 | $-5.00$ | $-0.866$ | $-0.500$ | 4.330 | 2.500 |
| 8 | $-8.66$ | $-0.500$ | $-0.866$ | 4.330 | 7.500 |
| 9 | $-10.00$ | 0.000 | $-1.000$ | 0.000 | 10.000 |
| 10 | $-8.66$ | 0.500 | $-0.866$ | $-4.330$ | 7.500 |
| 11 | $-5.00$ | 0.866 | $-0.500$ | $-4.330$ | 2.500 |
| 12 | 0.00 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| **$\Sigma$** | — | — | — | **0.000** | **60.000** |

**Step 3 — Apply FCFA formulas with prefactor $2/N = 2/12 = 0.1667$:**

$$V_R = 0.1667 \times 0.000 = 0.000\ \text{V (peak)}$$

$$V_I = 0.1667 \times 60.000 = 10.000\ \text{V (peak)}$$

**Step 4 — Polar form:**

$$V_m = \sqrt{0^2 + 10^2} = 10.000\ \text{V (peak)}$$

$$V_{rms} = \frac{10.000}{\sqrt{2}} = 7.071\ \text{V}$$

$$\phi = \arctan(10/0) = 90°\ \text{(positive imaginary axis)}$$

**Final Phasor (cosine reference):**

$$\vec{V}_1 = 0 + j\,10.0\ \text{V (peak)} = 7.07\angle 90°\ \text{V (rms)}$$

**Verification (Examiner's perspective):** The given samples are exactly $v_k = 10\sin(k \cdot 30°)$, which is a pure sinusoid with peak $10$ V lagging cosine reference by $90°$. The algorithm correctly returns $V_R = 0$ and $V_I = 10$ V, confirming the implementation.

*(Mark split: 1 mark setup + $\theta_s$, 2 marks table, 1 mark $V_R V_I$, 1 mark peak magnitude, 1 mark RMS, 1 mark phase angle with quadrant check.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Derive the **Half Cycle Fourier Algorithm** formulas for $V_R$ and $V_I$ starting from the continuous Fourier integral. Explain why the prefactor is $\frac{4}{N}$ instead of $\frac{2}{N}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A current signal is sampled at $N = 12$ samples/cycle. The first six samples (half cycle) in amperes are: $i_1 = 0.00,\ i_2 = 4.50,\ i_3 = 7.79,\ i_4 = 9.00,\ i_5 = 7.79,\ i_6 = 4.50$. Use the **half cycle** algorithm to compute the fundamental phasor, its RMS value, and the phase angle. Also compute the **apparent impedance** $Z_{app} = \vec{V}/\vec{I}$ if the voltage phasor at the relay location is $\vec{V} = 10000\angle 0°\ \text{V (rms)}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

##### **Part (a) — Derivation of HCFA (7 marks):**

**Continuous form:** The fundamental in-phase coefficient is:

$$a_1 = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0} v(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)\,dt \quad (1)$$

**Half window integration:** For the half cycle algorithm, we use only half the period. By the symmetry of sinusoids:

$$\int_0^{T_0}\cos(\omega_0 t)\sin(\omega_0 t)\,dt = 0 \quad (\text{cross terms vanish})$$

For the **fundamental cosine** component, the integral over a half period is **twice** the integral over the full period scaled by the same area, giving:

$$a_1 = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0/2} 2\,v(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)\,dt \quad (2)$$

**Discretisation:** With $N/2$ samples in the half window, $T_s = T_0/N$, and replacing $dt \to T_s$:

$$a_1 = \frac{2}{T_0}\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} 2\,v_k\cos(k\theta_s)\,T_s = \frac{4}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} v_k\cos(k\theta_s) \quad (3)$$

The factor of $2$ in (2) becomes a factor of $2 \times 2/N = 4/N$ in (3). **This is the origin of the prefactor $4/N$.**

**Final Boxed Formulas (HCFA):**

$$\boxed{\,V_R = \frac{4}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} v_k \cos(k\theta_s)\,} \quad ; \quad \boxed{\,V_I = \frac{4}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} v_k \sin(k\theta_s)\,} \quad (4)$$

**Phasor and polar forms (same as FCFA):**

$$\vec{V}_1 = V_R + jV_I, \quad V_m = \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_I^2} \quad (5)$$

**Why the prefactor changes (KTU answer hook):** Because the half window has only half the samples, but we are approximating the **same full integral**, we need to **scale up** by an extra factor of $2$ to compensate for the missing half. Hence $4/N$ instead of $2/N$.

*(Mark split: 1 mark starting integral, 1 mark half-window logic, 2 marks discretisation showing the factor of 2, 1 mark boxed formulas, 1 mark scaling explanation, 1 mark consequence for harmonic rejection.)*

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** $\theta_s = 30°$, prefactor $4/N = 4/12 = 0.3333$.

**Step 2 — Cosine and sine table for $k = 1$ to $6$:**

| $k$ | $i_k$ (A) | $\cos(k\cdot 30°)$ | $\sin(k\cdot 30°)$ | $i_k\cos(k\cdot 30°)$ | $i_k\sin(k\cdot 30°)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.00 | 0.866 | 0.500 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| 2 | 4.50 | 0.500 | 0.866 | 2.250 | 3.897 |
| 3 | 7.79 | 0.000 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 7.790 |
| 4 | 9.00 | $-0.500$ | 0.866 | $-4.500$ | 7.794 |
| 5 | 7.79 | $-0.866$ | 0.500 | $-6.746$ | 3.895 |
| 6 | 4.50 | $-1.000$ | 0.000 | $-4.500$ | 0.000 |
| **$\Sigma$** | — | — | — | **$-13.496$** | **$23.376$** |

**Step 3 — Apply HCFA formulas:**

$$I_R = 0.3333 \times (-13.496) = -4.499\ \text{A (peak)}$$

$$I_I = 0.3333 \times 23.376 = 7.792\ \text{A (peak)}$$

**Step 4 — Polar form:**

$$I_m = \sqrt{(-4.499)^2 + (7.792)^2} = \sqrt{20.24 + 60.71} = \sqrt{80.95} = 8.997\ \text{A (peak)}$$

$$I_{rms} = \frac{8.997}{\sqrt{2}} = 6.36\ \text{A}$$

$$\phi_I = \arctan\!\left(\frac{7.792}{-4.499}\right) = 180° - \arctan\!\left(\frac{7.792}{4.499}\right) = 180° - 60.0° = 120°$$

**Final Current Phasor (cosine reference, peak):**

$$\vec{I}_1 = -4.50 + j\,7.79\ \text{A (peak)} = 6.36\angle 120°\ \text{A (rms)}$$

**Step 5 — Apparent Impedance Computation:**

The voltage phasor is $\vec{V} = 10000\angle 0°$ V (rms). The current phasor is $\vec{I} = 6.36\angle 120°$ A (rms). Therefore:

$$\vec{Z}_{app} = \frac{\vec{V}}{\vec{I}} = \frac{10000\angle 0°}{6.36\angle 120°} = \frac{10000}{6.36}\angle(0° - 120°) = 1572\angle -120°\ \Omega$$

In rectangular form:

$$\vec{Z}_{app} = 1572\cos(-120°) + j\,1572\sin(-120°) = -786 + j\,(-1362)\ \Omega$$

This is a negative-resistance result, which in practice indicates a **fault close to the relay** with reverse power flow (typical of an internal fault on a series-compensated line). For exam purposes, report $\vert Z_{app}\vert = 1572\ \Omega$ and $\angle Z_{app} = -120°$.

*(Mark split: 1 mark setup, 2 marks table, 1 mark $I_R$ and $I_I$, 1 mark polar current, 1 mark RMS conversion and phase, 1 mark impedance division.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Fourier Algorithms):**
> 
> - **Definition (1 line):** Fourier algorithm = digital method to extract 50 Hz phasor via DFT of sampled waveform.
> - **Full Cycle (FCFA):** $V_R = (2/N)\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \cos(k\theta_s)$, $V_I = (2/N)\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \sin(k\theta_s)$, prefactor $\mathbf{2/N}$.
> - **Half Cycle (HCFA):** $V_R = (4/N)\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} v_k \cos(k\theta_s)$, $V_I = (4/N)\sum_{k=1}^{N/2} v_k \sin(k\theta_s)$, prefactor $\mathbf{4/N}$.
> - **Sampling angle:** $\theta_s = 2\pi/N$ (always in **radians** inside calculator).
> - **Phasor magnitude:** $V_m = \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_I^2}$ (peak). **RMS** = peak / $\sqrt{2}$.
> - **Phase angle:** $\phi = \arctan(V_I / V_R)$ with **quadrant check** (signs of $V_R$ and $V_I$).
> - **FCFA filters:** $100\%$ rejection of harmonics $n \geq 2$ — high selectivity.
> - **HCFA filters:** Only partial harmonic rejection — faster ($10$ ms) but more noise.
> - **DC offset problem:** Neither algorithm rejects DC offset perfectly. Solution: **mimic filter** at input.
> - **Recursive form:** $V_R(n) = V_R(n-1) + (2/N)(v_n - v_{n-N})\cos(n\theta_s)$ — saves CPU cycles.
> - **KTU favourite traps:** Forget quadrant correction (-1 to -2 marks), use $2/N$ for HCFA (-1 mark), omit $1/\sqrt{2}$ for RMS (-1 mark), radians vs degrees (-1 mark), inconsistent sample indexing (-1 mark).
> - **Real-world uses:** Distance relays, PMUs, differential protection, fault recorders.
> - **Anti-aliasing:** Sampling rate must obey Nyquist ($f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$). For 50 Hz with $N = 12$, $f_s = 600$ Hz, $f_{Ny} = 300$ Hz, so harmonics up to the 5th are captured.
> - **Symbolic mnemonic:** **F**ourier **C**atches **A**ll **H**armonics = **FCAH**; HCFA catches **Half**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Least Squares Based Algorithm. Digital Filters - Fundamentals of Infinite Impulse Response Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Least Squares Based Algorithm. Digital Filters - Fundamentals of Infinite Impulse Response Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4 — LEAST SQUARES ALGORITHM & IIR FILTER FUNDAMENTALS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Official Terminology)**
> **Least Squares Algorithm (LSA):** A parametric estimation technique that fits a mathematically assumed signal model (e.g., a pure sinusoid of known frequency) to a finite set of sampled data points by minimizing the sum of the squared errors between the assumed model and the actual samples. It is the workhorse algorithm for **real-time phasor, amplitude, and frequency estimation** in modern numerical relays.
>
> **Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) Filter:** A discrete-time linear shift-invariant (LTI) filter whose output depends on both the current and past input samples **AND** on the past output samples, producing an impulse response that theoretically extends to infinity. Mathematically, it is realized via a linear constant-coefficient difference equation with feedback.

### 1.1 Intuitive Overview — Plain English Analogy

#### 🧠 Analogy for Least Squares Algorithm
Imagine you are a tailor asked to stitch a straight-line "trend" through 20 wavy, noisy data points recorded on a graph. You cannot connect every point individually (that would over-fit the noise). Instead, you place a straight ruler such that the **sum of the squared vertical distances from every point to the ruler is as small as possible**. That optimal ruler is the "Least Squares Fit." In a digital relay, the noisy data points are voltage/current samples contaminated with harmonics, noise, and DC offset; the assumed "ruler" is a pure 50/60 Hz sinusoid of unknown magnitude and phase. LSA finds the **best-fit sinusoid**, giving you a clean estimate of the fundamental phasor even in heavy noise.

#### 🧠 Analogy for IIR Filters
Think of an IIR filter as an **echo chamber**. You shout a single sharp clap (an impulse) into a room with reflective walls, and the sound bounces back repeatedly, decaying gradually over time. The resulting audio waveform is the **impulse response**, and it is *infinite* in duration (in theory). Mathematically, this "echo" is created by feeding the output back into the input — a concept called **feedback**. In contrast, a Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filter is like a one-way corridor: the impulse enters, bounces off absorbers, and exits forever after a finite time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In KTU examinations, students are frequently asked the difference between "recursive" (IIR) and "non-recursive" (FIR) filters. The presence of past output terms in the difference equation is the **defining feature** of an IIR filter.

### 1.2 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

- **Power System Nominal Frequency:** $f_0 = 50\,\text{Hz}$ (India) → Angular frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 314.159\,\text{rad/s}$.
- **Sampling Frequency:** $f_s$ typically chosen as $N \cdot f_0$ where $N$ is samples-per-cycle (commonly **12, 16, 24, 32, or 64**).
- **Sampling Interval:** $T_s = 1/f_s$ seconds.
- **Data Window Length:** $D$ samples (commonly one full cycle, $D = N$).
- **Phasor Magnitude:** $|V|$, $|I|$ in **per-unit (pu)** or **kV / kA**.
- **Phasor Angle:** $\angle V$, $\angle I$ in **radians** or **degrees**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why IIR Filters Matter in Protection:** IIR filters (especially low-pass and band-pass realizations like Butterworth) are extensively used in numerical relays for **anti-aliasing**, **DC offset removal**, and **harmonic extraction** because they achieve a much sharper roll-off (steeper transition band) than FIR filters of the same order, requiring far fewer coefficients and less computation — critical for sub-cycle relay operating times (≤ 4 ms for distance relays).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Signal Model Used in Protection Relays

The standard model for the faulted waveform in a power system (as per KTU and IEEE tutorials) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= \sum_{h=1}^{H} X_h \cos(h\omega_0 t + \phi_h) + A_0 e^{-t/\tau_{dc}} + \epsilon(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $X_h$ and $\phi_h$ are the magnitude and phase of the $h$-th harmonic.
- $A_0 e^{-t/\tau_{dc}}$ is the exponentially decaying **DC offset component** (extremely important during faults).
- $\epsilon(t)$ is random measurement noise.
- $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ is the **fundamental angular frequency**.

For the simplest Least Squares problem, we assume the signal is a **pure fundamental sinusoid** with unknown magnitude and phase, and we aim to estimate these.

### 2.2 Trigonometric Reformulation (Linear in Parameters)

Using the identity $A\cos(\omega t + \phi) = X_c \cos(\omega t) + X_s \sin(\omega t)$, where $X_c = A\cos\phi$ and $X_s = -A\sin\phi$, the assumed model becomes **linear in the unknown parameters** $X_c$ and $X_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t_k) &= X_c \cos(\omega_0 t_k) + X_s \sin(\omega_0 t_k) + e(t_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $t_k = kT_s$ is the discrete sampling instant, and $e(t_k)$ is the error between the model and the actual sample.

### 2.3 The Least Squares Optimization Criterion

The Least Squares Algorithm seeks parameters $X_c$ and $X_s$ that **minimize** the sum of squared errors over a data window of $D$ samples:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(X_c, X_s) &= \sum_{k=0}^{D-1} \left[\, x(t_k) - X_c \cos(\omega_0 t_k) - X_s \sin(\omega_0 t_k) \,\right]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a standard **linear least squares** problem because the model is linear in the unknowns.

### 2.4 Matrix Formulation — The KTU Board Favorite

Define the column vectors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x} &= \begin{bmatrix} x(t_0) \\ x(t_1) \\ \vdots \\ x(t_{D-1}) \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\omega_0 t_0) & \sin(\omega_0 t_0) \\ \cos(\omega_0 t_1) & \sin(\omega_0 t_1) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ \cos(\omega_0 t_{D-1}) & \sin(\omega_0 t_{D-1}) \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ X_s \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then the optimization yields the **normal equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{\theta} &= (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The matrix $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}$ is a $2 \times 2$ matrix for the fundamental-only model. This is why LSA is computationally efficient and is the **foundation of all modern numerical distance relays**.

### 2.5 Phasor Recovery from LSA Estimates

Once $X_c$ and $X_s$ are obtained, the magnitude and phase of the fundamental phasor are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|X| &= \sqrt{X_c^2 + X_s^2} \\[4pt]
\angle X &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{-X_s}{X_c}\right) \quad \text{(with quadrant correction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.6 Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) Filter — Theoretical Foundation

#### 2.6.1 General Difference Equation

The IIR filter is described by the following linear constant-coefficient difference equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k] \;-\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k]
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $x[n]$ is the input sample at discrete time $n$.
- $y[n]$ is the output sample.
- $b_k$ are the **feed-forward (FIR part)** coefficients (numerator).
- $a_k$ are the **feedback (IIR part)** coefficients (denominator), with $a_0 = 1$ by convention.
- $M$ is the order of the feed-forward section, $N$ is the order of the feedback section. The filter is of order $\max(M, N)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:** When all $a_k = 0$ for $k \geq 1$, the filter has no feedback and becomes a **Finite Impulse Response (FIR)** filter with finite memory of $M+1$ samples. The presence of any non-zero $a_k$ gives an infinite impulse response.

#### 2.6.2 Transfer Function (Z-Domain)

Taking the z-transform of the difference equation (with zero initial conditions):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) &= \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Zeros** of $H(z)$ are roots of $B(z) = 0$.
- **Poles** of $H(z)$ are roots of $A(z) = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion (Cauchy's Argument):** A causal IIR filter is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the z-plane, i.e., $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ for every pole $p_i$.

#### 2.6.3 Impulse Response — Why "Infinite"?

When the input is $\delta[n]$ (unit impulse), the output is the impulse response $h[n]$. For an IIR filter, $h[n]$ is a recursive sequence that does not terminate — it decays or oscillates indefinitely (in theory, for infinite time, in practice, until numerical precision is lost). This is the **infinite impulse response**.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Key Condition |
|---|---------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Continuous sinusoid model | $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ | $A$: magnitude (V/A), $\phi$: phase (rad) | Single-frequency signal |
| 2 | Linearized form | $x(t) = X_c\cos(\omega_0 t) + X_s\sin(\omega_0 t)$ | $X_c = A\cos\phi,\; X_s = -A\sin\phi$ | Unknowns become linear |
| 3 | LSE objective | $J = \sum_{k=0}^{D-1} e(t_k)^2$ | $e(t_k)$: residual error (V/A) | Minimize w.r.t. $\mathbf{\theta}$ |
| 4 | Normal equation | $\mathbf{\theta} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^2$ | Requires $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}$ invertible |
| 5 | Phasor magnitude | $\vert X \vert = \sqrt{X_c^2 + X_s^2}$ | Magnitude in V or A | Always positive |
| 6 | Phasor angle | $\angle X = \arctan(-X_s / X_c)$ | Angle in radians | Quadrant correction needed |
| 7 | IIR difference equation | $y[n] = \sum b_k x[n-k] - \sum a_k y[n-k]$ | $a_k, b_k$: real coefficients | At least one $a_k \neq 0$ |
| 8 | IIR transfer function | $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ | $z = e^{j\omega T_s}$ | Rational in $z^{-1}$ |
| 9 | Frequency response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z) \big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega T_s}}$ | $\omega \in [0, \pi f_s]$ | Periodic in $\omega$ |
| 10 | BIBO Stability | All poles satisfy $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ | Unit circle in z-plane | Causal IIR required |
| 11 | Impulse response (IIR) | $h[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k h[n-k] + b_n$ | Recursive definition | Decays to zero for stable filter |
| 12 | Impulse response (FIR) | $h[n] = b_n$ for $0 \le n \le M$, else 0 | Non-recursive | Strictly finite length |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Role of LSA | Role of IIR Filter |
|------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Distance Protection (Zones 1, 2, 3)** | Estimates fundamental voltage & current phasors to compute apparent impedance $Z = V/I$ | Pre-filters samples to remove noise & DC offset |
| **Differential Protection of Transformers** | Compares estimated phasors at both ends | Band-pass filter isolates fundamental from inrush harmonics |
| **Frequency Estimation Relays (81)** | LSA extended with $\omega$ as unknown tracks frequency drift | Low-pass filter rejects measurement noise |
| **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** | Synchrophasor estimation at reporting rates of 25–50 frames/s | Anti-aliasing pre-filter before sampling |
| **Fault Locators** | Estimates pre-fault and during-fault phasors | IIR low-pass filter for traveling-wave detection post-processing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Least Squares Solution (Normal Equation)

**Starting point:** The residual at sample $k$ is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
e_k = x_k - X_c \cos(\omega_0 t_k) - X_s \sin(\omega_0 t_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Form the cost function $J$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
J(X_c, X_s) = \sum_{k=0}^{D-1} \left[\, x_k - X_c \cos(\omega_0 t_k) - X_s \sin(\omega_0 t_k) \,\right]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to $X_c$ and set to zero** (necessary condition for the minimum):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J}{\partial X_c} = -2 \sum_{k=0}^{D-1} \cos(\omega_0 t_k)\left[\, x_k - X_c \cos(\omega_0 t_k) - X_s \sin(\omega_0 t_k) \,\right] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\sum_{k=0}^{D-1} \cos^2(\omega_0 t_k)\right] X_c + \left[\sum_{k=0}^{D-1} \cos(\omega_0 t_k)\sin(\omega_0 t_k)\right] X_s = \sum_{k=0}^{D-1} x_k \cos(\omega_0 t_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the partial derivative with respect to $X_s$ and set to zero:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J}{\partial X_s} = -2 \sum_{k=0}^{D-1} \sin(\omega_0 t_k)\left[\, x_k - X_c \cos(\omega_0 t_k) - X_s \sin(\omega_0 t_k) \,\right] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\sum_{k=0}^{D-1} \cos(\omega_0 t_k)\sin(\omega_0 t_k)\right] X_c + \left[\sum_{k=0}^{D-1} \sin^2(\omega_0 t_k)\right] X_s = \sum_{k=0}^{D-1} x_k \sin(\omega_0 t_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Write the normal equation in matrix form** (this is the KTU board answer):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix}
\sum \cos^2(\omega_0 t_k) & \sum \cos(\omega_0 t_k)\sin(\omega_0 t_k) \\
\sum \cos(\omega_0 t_k)\sin(\omega_0 t_k) & \sum \sin^2(\omega_0 t_k)
\end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H}}
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ X_s \end{bmatrix}
=
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \sum x_k \cos(\omega_0 t_k) \\ \sum x_k \sin(\omega_0 t_k) \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{x}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve via matrix inversion:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ X_s \end{bmatrix} = \left(\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H}\right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Recovery of the fundamental phasor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
|X| &= \sqrt{X_c^2 + X_s^2}, \quad \angle X = \arctan\!\left(\frac{-X_s}{X_c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why we minimize $e^2$ and not $\vert e \vert$:** The squared-error criterion yields a *closed-form linear* solution, is analytically tractable, and has the beautiful statistical property that, under zero-mean Gaussian noise, the LSA estimate is the **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: IIR Transfer Function from the Difference Equation

**Starting point — the difference equation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] + a_1 y[n-1] + a_2 y[n-2] + \cdots + a_N y[n-N] = b_0 x[n] + b_1 x[n-1] + \cdots + b_M x[n-M]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the z-transform** (using time-shift property: $y[n-k] \leftrightarrow z^{-k}Y(z)$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) + a_1 z^{-1} Y(z) + \cdots + a_N z^{-N} Y(z) = b_0 X(z) + b_1 z^{-1} X(z) + \cdots + b_M z^{-M} X(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Factor $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z)\left[\, 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N} \,\right] = X(z)\left[\, b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M} \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^N$** to express in standard polynomial form:
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = \frac{b_0 z^N + b_1 z^{N-1} + \cdots + b_M z^{N-M}}{z^N + a_1 z^{N-1} + \cdots + a_N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Factor to find poles and zeros:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = b_0 \, \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M}(z - z_k)}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(z - p_k)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $z_k$ are the **zeros** and $p_k$ are the **poles** of the filter.

**Step 6 — Stability condition:** All poles must satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for BIBO stability.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Worked Example: First-Order IIR Low-Pass Filter**
> 
> Given a first-order IIR filter with the difference equation:
> $$y[n] = 0.6\, y[n-1] + 0.4\, x[n]$$
> 
> **Q1: Find the transfer function $H(z)$ and impulse response $h[n]$.**
> 
> **Q2: Is the filter stable? Justify.**
> 
> **Q3: Compute the steady-state output when $x[n] = \cos(\pi n / 4)$.**

### Solution:

**Q1:**
Taking the z-transform of both sides:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) &= 0.6\, z^{-1} Y(z) + 0.4\, X(z) \\[4pt]
Y(z)\left[1 - 0.6 z^{-1}\right] &= 0.4 X(z) \\[4pt]
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} &= \frac{0.4}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The impulse response (using geometric series expansion of $H(z) = 0.4 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.6)^n z^{-n}$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
h[n] = 0.4 \cdot (0.6)^n, \quad n \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$
This is an **infinite** sequence decaying exponentially — confirming the "infinite" nature of the impulse response.

**Q2:** The pole is at $z = 0.6$. Since $\vert 0.6 \vert = 0.6 < 1$, the pole lies inside the unit circle. **Therefore, the filter is BIBO stable.**

**Q3:** The frequency response is $H(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{0.4}{1 - 0.6 e^{-j\omega}}$. At $\omega = \pi/4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(e^{j\pi/4}) &= \frac{0.4}{1 - 0.6(\cos(\pi/4) - j\sin(\pi/4))} = \frac{0.4}{1 - 0.6(0.7071 - j\,0.7071)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.4}{1 - 0.4243 + j\,0.4243} = \frac{0.4}{0.5757 + j\,0.4243}
\end{aligned}
$$

The magnitude and phase:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert H(e^{j\pi/4}) \vert &= \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{0.5757^2 + 0.4243^2}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{0.3314 + 0.1800}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{0.5114}} = \frac{0.4}{0.7151} \approx 0.559
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\angle H(e^{j\pi/4}) &= -\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.4243}{0.5757}\right) = -\arctan(0.7370) \approx -36.4^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

The steady-state output is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{ss}[n] &= 0.559 \cos(\pi n / 4 - 36.4^\circ)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — LSA vs. IIR Filter Comparison

| Criterion | Least Squares Algorithm | IIR Filter |
|-----------|-------------------------|------------|
| **Mathematical Nature** | Parametric optimization (statistical) | Deterministic signal processing |
| **Goal** | Estimate unknown parameters of assumed model | Modify amplitude/phase spectrum of input |
| **Computational Core** | $\mathbf{\theta} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{x}$ | $y[n] = \sum b_k x[n-k] - \sum a_k y[n-k]$ |
| **Memory Required** | Stores $D$ past samples (data window) | Stores $M + N$ past input/output samples |
| **Output** | Phasor $\vert X \vert \angle X$ | Filtered time-domain sample $y[n]$ |
| **Use in Protection** | Direct phasor estimation | Pre-conditioning of raw samples |
| **Typical Order** | Data window $D = 12$ to $64$ samples | Filter order $N = 1$ to $8$ |
| **Stability Concern** | $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}$ must be invertible | All poles inside unit circle |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LSA in a Numerical Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog CT/VT\nSecondary Signal] --> B[Anti-Aliasing\nIIR Low-Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Sample & Hold\nADC]
    C --> D[Digital Data\nBuffer of D samples]
    D --> E[Construct H Matrix\ncos and sin basis]
    E --> F[Compute H^T H]
    F --> G[Compute H^T x]
    G --> H[Matrix Inversion\nTheta equals HTH inv times HTx]
    H --> I[Extract Xc and Xs]
    I --> J[Phasor Magnitude\nand Angle]
    J --> K[Relay Trip Logic\nImpedance Distance]
    styleA fill:#FFE4B5
    styleB fill:#B0E0E6
    styleC fill:#B0E0E6
    styleD fill:#98FB98
    styleE fill:#98FB98
    styleF fill:#DDA0DD
    styleG fill:#DDA0DD
    styleH fill:#FFB6C1
    styleI fill:#FFB6C1
    styleJ fill:#F0E68C
    styleK fill:#FF6347
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** CT/VT outputs are first cleaned by an analog anti-aliasing filter, then sampled. The samples populate the data window vector $\mathbf{x}$, while the $\mathbf{H}$ matrix is pre-computed from the known fundamental frequency. The matrix operations yield the phasor estimate, which is consumed by the relay's decision algorithm.

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Direct-Form I IIR Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input x n] --> N0[b0 multiply]
    IN --> D1[Delay z^-1]
    D1 --> N1[b1 multiply]
    D1 --> D2[Delay z^-1]
    D2 --> N2[b2 multiply]
    D2 --> D3[Delay z^-1]
    D3 --> N3[b3 multiply]
    N0 --> SUM1[Sum Node +]
    N1 --> SUM1
    N2 --> SUM1
    N3 --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> MID[Middle Node w n]
    MID --> OUT[Output y n]
    MID --> FB1[Delay z^-1]
    FB1 --> A1[a1 multiply]
    A1 --> SUM2[Subtract from input]
    FB1 --> FB2[Delay z^-1]
    FB2 --> A2[a2 multiply]
    A2 --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> IN
    styleIN fill:#87CEEB
    styleOUT fill:#90EE90
    styleSUM1 fill:#FFD700
    styleSUM2 fill:#FFA07A
    styleMID fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot for a Stable Second-Order IIR Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Z_PLANE["Z-PLANE — Unit Circle Boundary"]
        P1[Pole at z = 0.5 + j 0.3]:::pole
        P2[Pole at z = 0.5 - j 0.3]:::pole
        Z1[Zero at z = -1 + j 0]:::zero
        Z2[Zero at z = 0 + j 0]:::zero
        UC[(Unit Circle radius = 1)]:::circle
    end
    P1 -.- P2
    Z1 -.- Z2
    classDef pole fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef zero fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef circle fill:none,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:3px,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the pole-zero plot:** Both poles (red X marks) lie **inside the unit circle** — the filter is **stable**. The zeros (cyan circles) at $z=0$ and $z=-1$ indicate notch behaviour at DC and at the Nyquist frequency respectively.

## 4.4 Signal Flow Comparison: LSA Window vs. IIR Recursion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LSA["LEAST SQUARES ALGORITHM (NON-RECURSIVE ESTIMATION)"]
        L1[New sample x n arrives] --> L2[Push into data window]
        L2 --> L3[Shift older samples]
        L3 --> L4[Recompute H^T H and H^T x]
        L4 --> L5[Invert 2x2 matrix]
        L5 --> L6[Output phasor estimate]
    end
    subgraph IIRF["IIR FILTER (RECURSIVE COMPUTATION)"]
        I1[New sample x n arrives] --> I2[Compute b0 x n plus b1 x n-1 plus ...]
        I2 --> I3[Subtract a1 y n-1 plus a2 y n-2 plus ...]
        I3 --> I4[Store y n into shift register]
        I4 --> I5[Output y n and recycle]
    end
    styleL1 fill:#FFE4B5
    styleL6 fill:#98FB98
    styleI1 fill:#B0E0E6
    styleI5 fill:#FFB6C1
```
