# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the Least Squares estimation criterion. How is it used to estimate the fundamental phasor in a digital relay?**

**Model Answer:**

> The Least Squares estimation criterion states that the *best-fit* parameters of an assumed model are those that **minimize the sum of the squared residuals** between the model predictions and the actual measured samples.

**Mathematical form:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{\theta}} = \arg \min_{\mathbf{\theta}} \sum_{k=0}^{D-1} \left[\, x_k - h_k^T \mathbf{\theta} \,\right]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Application in digital relays:** The assumed model is a pure 50 Hz sinusoid $X_c \cos(\omega_0 t) + X_s \sin(\omega_0 t)$, with unknown parameters $X_c$ and $X_s$. The relay collects $D$ samples per cycle and solves the normal equation $\mathbf{\theta} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{x}$ to obtain $X_c$ and $X_s$. The phasor magnitude and phase are then computed as $\vert X \vert = \sqrt{X_c^2 + X_s^2}$ and $\angle X = \arctan(-X_s / X_c)$. The LSA thus provides noise-immune, statistically optimal phasor estimates for distance, overcurrent, and differential protection functions. **[3 Marks]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) filter. State the BIBO stability condition for a causal IIR filter.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) filter** is a discrete-time linear shift-invariant system whose output depends on the current and past input samples **as well as** past output samples, producing an impulse response that extends to infinity in time.
> 
> **Difference equation:**
> $$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]$$
> 
> **BIBO Stability Condition:** A causal IIR filter is Bounded-Input Bounded-Output stable **if and only if every pole of its transfer function $H(z)$ lies strictly inside the unit circle of the z-plane**, i.e., $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ for all poles $p_i$. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget the quadrant correction** when computing $\angle X = \arctan(-X_s / X_c)$. Always use `atan2(-X_s, X_c)` in code or carefully check signs.
> - The difference equation must be written with **all terms on one side (LHS = 0)** before taking the z-transform; mixing signs leads to sign errors in the transfer function.
> - For IIR stability, students frequently write "poles inside unit circle" but **miss the "strictly"** qualifier — poles on the unit circle ($\vert p \vert = 1$) produce marginally stable (oscillatory) systems, not BIBO stable ones.
> - In LSA problems, the data window size $D$ and the sampling rate $f_s$ **must be explicitly stated** to compute the basis vectors $\cos(\omega_0 k T_s)$ and $\sin(\omega_0 k T_s)$.

---

### **🔹 QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the Least Squares solution for estimating the magnitude and phase of the fundamental phasor from $D$ discrete samples of a power system signal. Clearly show the matrix formulation, normal equation, and the final recovery formulas. Assume a pure sinusoidal model. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** A digital relay uses a first-order IIR low-pass filter with the difference equation $y[n] = 0.7\, y[n-1] + 0.3\, x[n]$. Determine:
  1. The transfer function $H(z)$ and impulse response $h[n]$.
  2. Whether the filter is BIBO stable. Justify with the pole location.
  3. The steady-state output when $x[n] = \cos(\pi n / 6)$. **[(7 Marks)]**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A:**

**Part (a):** *See complete derivation in Section 3.1 above.* Key points to include:
- Define the residual error $e_k$. **[1 Mark]**
- Form the cost function $J = \sum e_k^2$. **[1 Mark]**
- Take partial derivatives $\partial J / \partial X_c = 0$ and $\partial J / \partial X_s = 0$. **[2 Marks]**
- Write the normal equation matrix form. **[2 Marks]**
- Provide the closed-form solution and phasor recovery formulas. **[1 Mark]**

**Part (b):**

**(1) Transfer function and impulse response:**

Taking z-transform of $y[n] - 0.7 y[n-1] = 0.3 x[n]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) - 0.7 z^{-1} Y(z) &= 0.3 X(z) \\[4pt]
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} &= \frac{0.3}{1 - 0.7 z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The impulse response is the inverse z-transform:
$$
\begin{aligned}
h[n] &= 0.3 \cdot (0.7)^n, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$
This is an infinite exponentially decaying sequence, confirming the "IIR" classification. **[2 Marks]**

**(2) Stability:**

The pole is at $z = 0.7$. Since $\vert 0.7 \vert < 1$, the pole lies **strictly inside** the unit circle. Therefore, the filter is **BIBO stable**. **[2 Marks]**

**(3) Steady-state output for $x[n] = \cos(\pi n / 6)$:**

The frequency $\omega = \pi/6$. Evaluate $H(z)$ on the unit circle at $z = e^{j\pi/6}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(e^{j\pi/6}) &= \frac{0.3}{1 - 0.7 e^{-j\pi/6}} = \frac{0.3}{1 - 0.7(\cos(\pi/6) - j\sin(\pi/6))} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.3}{1 - 0.7(0.8660 - j\,0.5)} = \frac{0.3}{1 - 0.6062 + j\,0.35} = \frac{0.3}{0.3938 + j\,0.35}
\end{aligned}
$$

Magnitude:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert H(e^{j\pi/6}) \vert = \frac{0.3}{\sqrt{0.3938^2 + 0.35^2}} = \frac{0.3}{\sqrt{0.1551 + 0.1225}} = \frac{0.3}{\sqrt{0.2776}} = \frac{0.3}{0.5269} \approx 0.569
\end{aligned}
$$

Phase:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\angle H(e^{j\pi/6}) = -\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.35}{0.3938}\right) = -\arctan(0.8888) \approx -41.6^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steady-state output:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{ss}[n] \approx 0.569 \cos(\pi n / 6 - 41.6^\circ)
\end{aligned}
$$
**[3 Marks]**

---

### **🔹 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the operational principle of the Least Squares Algorithm (LSA) for phasor estimation in numerical relays. Discuss the role of the data window, the choice of model, and the impact of harmonics/DC offset on the estimate. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the Direct-Form I realization of a second-order IIR filter. State and prove the BIBO stability criterion for a causal IIR filter. **[(7 Marks)]**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B:**

**Part (a):** Operational Principle of LSA

- The relay samples the secondary voltage/current at a known rate $f_s$, typically $N$ samples per fundamental cycle (commonly $N = 16$ or $32$). **[1 Mark]**
- These $D = N$ samples are stored in a sliding data window. **[1 Mark]**
- The algorithm assumes the signal fits the model $X_c \cos(\omega_0 k T_s) + X_s \sin(\omega_0 k T_s)$, where $X_c$ and $X_s$ are the unknown real and imaginary components of the fundamental phasor. **[1 Mark]**
- It computes the optimal $X_c, X_s$ that minimize the sum of squared residuals. **[1 Mark]**
- The phasor is reconstructed as $\vert X \vert \angle X$. **[1 Mark]**
- **Impact of harmonics and DC offset:** If the signal contains DC offset or higher harmonics not included in the model, the LSA estimate is biased because the model is incomplete. The remedy is to **augment the model** to include DC (exponential term) and selected harmonics. **[1 Mark]**
- **Data window choice:** A one-cycle window ($D = N$) gives the fastest response but is sensitive to noise; a multi-cycle window improves accuracy at the cost of speed. **[1 Mark]**

**Part (b):** Direct-Form I Realization and Stability Proof

**Block Diagram Description (textual since the figure is in Section 4.2):**
The Direct-Form I structure uses two delay-line chains:
1. The input $x[n]$ passes through delays $z^{-1}$ to generate $x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots$, each multiplied by coefficients $b_0, b_1, \ldots, b_M$ and summed to form an intermediate signal $w[n]$.
2. The output $y[n]$ is also fed back through delays $z^{-1}$ to generate $y[n-1], y[n-2], \ldots$, each multiplied by coefficients $-a_1, -a_2, \ldots, -a_N$, and these are subtracted from $w[n]$ to produce $y[n]$ itself.

For a second-order IIR filter:
$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] = b_0 x[n] + b_1 x[n-1] + b_2 x[n-2] - a_1 y[n-1] - a_2 y[n-2]
\end{aligned}
$$
**[2 Marks]**

**BIBO Stability Criterion — Proof Sketch:**

A causal IIR filter has transfer function $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$. The impulse response can be written using partial fraction expansion:
$$
\begin{aligned}
h[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{N} c_k (p_k)^n u[n]
\end{aligned}
$$
where $p_k$ are the poles and $u[n]$ is the unit step function.

For a bounded input $\vert x[n] \vert \le M$, the output is the convolution $y[n] = \sum h[k] x[n-k]$. Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert y[n] \vert \le M \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

For the sum to be finite (BIBO stability), we need $\sum \vert h[k] \vert < \infty$. Since each term $c_k (p_k)^n$ contributes $\vert c_k \vert \vert p_k \vert^n$, the sum converges **if and only if** $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all poles.

Conversely, if any pole satisfies $\vert p_k \vert \ge 1$, then $\vert h[n] \vert$ does not decay to zero, and the impulse response is unbounded, violating BIBO stability. **[5 Marks]**

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Pin to Your Wall!)**

- ✅ **LSA Cost Function:** $J = \sum_{k=0}^{D-1} e_k^2$ — minimize the **sum of squared residuals**.
- ✅ **Signal Model:** $x(t_k) = X_c \cos(\omega_0 t_k) + X_s \sin(\omega_0 t_k)$ — always write in this **linearized** form for KTU answers.
- ✅ **Normal Equation:** $\mathbf{\theta} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{x}$ — the KTU board's favourite matrix identity.
- ✅ **Phasor Recovery:** $\vert X \vert = \sqrt{X_c^2 + X_s^2}$, $\angle X = \text{atan2}(-X_s, X_c)$.
- ✅ **IIR Difference Equation:** Always written with LHS = 0 form: $y[n] + \sum a_k y[n-k] = \sum b_k x[n-k]$.
- ✅ **IIR Transfer Function:** $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ — rational function in $z^{-1}$.
- ✅ **BIBO Stability:** All poles strictly inside the unit circle, i.e., $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ — note the word **"strictly."**
- ✅ **FIR vs IIR:** FIR has no feedback ($a_k = 0$ for $k \ge 1$); IIR has at least one $a_k \ne 0$.
- ✅ **Impulse Response:** FIR is finite-length; IIR is infinite-length (decaying or oscillating for stable filters).
- ✅ **Engineering Use:** LSA → phasor estimation for protection; IIR → anti-aliasing & noise filtering of raw samples.
- ✅ **DC Offset in LSA:** To handle DC offset, **augment the model** with a term $A_0 e^{-t/\tau_{dc}}$ or include it as $D_0 + D_1 t$ for short data windows.
- ✅ **Order of Filter:** The order $N$ of an IIR filter is the maximum of the orders of $A(z)$ and $B(z)$.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filter** is a discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) digital filter whose impulse response settles to exactly zero in a finite number of sample periods. Mathematically, the output $y(n)$ of an FIR filter depends only on a finite number of past and present input samples $x(n)$, weighted by a set of fixed coefficients $\{h_0, h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_{N-1}\}$.

The defining difference equation of an $N$-tap FIR filter is:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) \cdot x(n-k)$$

where $N$ is the filter order (or length), $h(k)$ are the filter coefficients, and the impulse response is non-zero only for $0 \le k \le N-1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of *Digital Protection of Power Systems*, FIR filters are the **preferred digital filter class** for protective relays because they are **unconditionally stable**, possess **exact linear phase** response, and introduce **no feedback** (hence no drift on DC components generated by fault transients).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weighted Average" Calculator

Imagine you are a bank loan officer deciding creditworthiness. You do not just look at today's income ($x(n)$) — you look at the **last 12 months** of income, but with a *twist*: the most recent months matter more, the older ones matter less. You multiply each month's income by a *weight* (the coefficient $h(k)$) and sum everything up. That weighted sum is your **decision** (the filter output $y(n)$).

An FIR filter does *exactly* this with a digital signal:

- **The window** = the number of past months you consider (the number of taps $N$).
- **The weights** = the filter coefficients $h(k)$.
- **The decision** = the filtered output $y(n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> Because FIR filters use **only past inputs** (no past outputs feeding back), they are *non-recursive*. In a relay, this means the filter will **never run away** (diverge) even if the input contains a massive DC offset from a fault — a property that is *mission-critical* in substation hardware.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Sampling frequency ($f_s$):** In KTU-recommended protective relaying hardware, the typical sampling rate is **1.2 kHz to 4 kHz** (i.e., **24 to 80 samples per 50 Hz cycle** or **20 to 67 samples per 60 Hz cycle**).
- **Group delay:** For a linear-phase FIR filter of length $N$, the group delay is exactly $\tau = \frac{(N-1)T_s}{2}$ seconds, where $T_s$ is the sampling period.
- **Filter order ($N-1$):** Determines selectivity. Higher $N$ gives a sharper frequency cutoff at the cost of more computation and longer group delay.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

An FIR filter executes the following logic in real time for every new input sample $x(n)$:

1. **Sample acquisition:** The ADC reads the latest current/voltage sample $x(n)$ from the CT/PT secondary.
2. **Shift register update:** Older samples $\{x(n-1), x(n-2), \ldots, x(n-N+1)\}$ shift one position to the right.
3. **Multiplication:** Each delayed sample is multiplied by its corresponding coefficient $h(k)$.
4. **Accumulation:** All $N$ products are summed to produce the filtered output $y(n)$.
5. **Decision logic:** The relay's algorithm compares $y(n)$ against a threshold (e.g., 0.5 pu) to declare a trip.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind no feedback:** In an IIR filter, a fault-induced DC component can persist for many cycles because it gets multiplied by feedback coefficients every sample. An FIR filter has **no feedback path**, so the DC component naturally dies out after $N$ samples — exactly what a numerical protection algorithm requires.

### 2.2 Mathematical Representations

| Representation | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Difference Equation | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,x(n-k)$ | Direct implementation |
| Transfer Function (Z-domain) | $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,z^{-k}$ | Pole-zero analysis |
| Frequency Response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,e^{-j\omega k}$ | Magnitude / phase plot |
| Magnitude Response | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \sqrt{[\text{Re}\{H\}]^2 + [\text{Im}\{H\}]^2}$ | Gain at frequency $\omega$ |
| Phase Response | $\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Im}\{H\}}{\text{Re}\{H\}}\right)$ | Phase shift introduced |
| Linear Phase Condition | $h(k) = \pm\,h(N-1-k)$ | Symmetric or anti-symmetric coefficients |
| Group Delay | $\tau_g = \dfrac{(N-1)\,T_s}{2}$ | Constant for linear-phase FIR |
| Convolution Sum | $y(n) = h(n) * x(n)$ | Time-domain output |

### 2.3 Linear-Phase FIR — Four Types

| Type | Symmetry | $\omega = 0$ | $\omega = \pi$ | Suitable For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type-I | Symmetric, $N$ odd | $\vert H \vert \ne 0$ | $\vert H \vert \ne 0$ | Low-pass, high-pass, band-pass |
| Type-II | Symmetric, $N$ even | $\vert H \vert \ne 0$ | $\vert H \vert = 0$ | Low-pass, band-pass only |
| Type-III | Anti-symmetric, $N$ odd | $\vert H \vert = 0$ | $\vert H \vert = 0$ | Band-pass, differentiator |
| Type-IV | Anti-symmetric, $N$ even | $\vert H \vert = 0$ | $\vert H \vert \ne 0$ | Differentiator, Hilbert |

### 2.4 Windowing Design — Cheat Sheet

The windowing method multiplies an *ideal* (infinite-length) impulse response $h_d(n)$ by a finite window $w(n)$ of length $N$ to obtain a practical FIR:

$$h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n), \qquad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

| Window | Time-Domain $w(n)$ | Main Lobe Width | Side Lobe Atten. |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $1$ | $4\pi/N$ | **−13 dB** |
| Hanning | $0.5\bigl[1-\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))\bigr]$ | $8\pi/N$ | **−31 dB** |
| Hamming | $0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$ | $8\pi/N$ | **−43 dB** |
| Blackman | $0.42 - 0.5\cos(\cdot) + 0.08\cos(2\cdot)$ | $12\pi/N$ | **−58 dB** |
| Kaiser (with $\beta$) | $\dfrac{I_0\!\left(\beta\sqrt{1-(2n/(N-1)-1)^2}\right)}{I_0(\beta)}$ | tunable | **−30 to −90 dB** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** In a numerical distance relay, a **Hamming or Blackman** window is preferred over the Rectangular window because the Rectangular window's side lobes are only **−13 dB** down — meaning a strong 3rd harmonic could leak into the 50 Hz fundamental channel and cause a **false trip**.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Power Engineering

- **Numerical Overcurrent Relays:** Use FIR band-pass filters centered at 50/60 Hz to compute the fundamental phasor.
- **Differential Protection of Transformers:** FIR low-pass filters remove CT saturation noise before computing the operating and restraining currents.
- **Traveling-Wave-Based Ultra-High-Speed Protection:** High-order FIR band-pass filters isolate the high-frequency wavefront (1 kHz – 1 MHz components) from background noise.
- **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs):** FIR filters are mandated by the **IEEE C37.118.1** standard for synchrophasor estimation due to their deterministic group delay.
- **Anti-Aliasing Front-End:** FIR low-pass filters placed before decimation in Σ-Δ ADC-based relay hardware.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the FIR Transfer Function from the Difference Equation

We start from the discrete-time difference equation of an $N$-tap FIR filter:

$$y(n) = h(0)\,x(n) + h(1)\,x(n-1) + h(2)\,x(n-2) + \cdots + h(N-1)\,x(n-N+1)$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Z-transform to both sides.** Using the time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}\{x(n-k)\} = z^{-k}X(z)$:

$$Y(z) = h(0)\,X(z) + h(1)\,z^{-1}X(z) + h(2)\,z^{-2}X(z) + \cdots + h(N-1)\,z^{-(N-1)}X(z)$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $X(z)$:**

$$Y(z) = \Bigl[h(0) + h(1)\,z^{-1} + h(2)\,z^{-2} + \cdots + h(N-1)\,z^{-(N-1)}\Bigr]\,X(z)$$

**Step 3 — Define the transfer function** $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,z^{-k}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the pole-zero structure.** All the $N$ poles of $H(z)$ are at $z = 0$ (a pole of order $N-1$ at the origin). Therefore, **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle**, and the filter is **BIBO stable for any choice of finite coefficients $h(k)$** — this is the rigorous proof of FIR unconditional stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "$N-1$ poles at the origin" property is the algebraic reason why a relay engineer can change the filter length $N$ on the fly (e.g., switching from 8-tap to 32-tap for a slower but sharper protection characteristic) **without ever risking instability** — a luxury not afforded by IIR filters.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Linear-Phase Condition

We require the phase response $\phi(\omega)$ to be a *linear function* of frequency: $\phi(\omega) = -\alpha\,\omega$, where $\alpha$ is a constant. Substituting this into the frequency response:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,e^{-j\omega k} = \bigl[\text{real amplitude}\bigr] \cdot e^{-j\alpha\omega}$$

**Step 1 — Combine complex-conjugate pairs** of the exponential $e^{-j\omega k}$. Each pair $(h(k)e^{-j\omega k} + h(N-1-k)e^{-j\omega(N-1-k)})$ can be made purely real if the coefficient symmetry $h(k) = h(N-1-k)$ is imposed.

**Step 2 — Apply the symmetry** $h(k) = h(N-1-k)$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}\Bigl[ h\!\left(\tfrac{N-1}{2}\right) + 2\sum_{k=0}^{(N-3)/2} h(k)\cos\!\left(\omega\!\left(\tfrac{N-1}{2} - k\right)\right) \Bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Read off the phase.** The bracketed amplitude is purely real, so:

$$\phi(\omega) = -\omega\!\left(\frac{N-1}{2}\right) \quad \text{(for symmetric coefficients)}$$

which is *exactly* linear in $\omega$. The slope of this line is the **constant group delay**:

$$\tau_g = -\frac{d\phi(\omega)}{d\omega} = \frac{N-1}{2} \text{ samples} = \frac{(N-1)\,T_s}{2} \text{ seconds}$$

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Hamming-Windowed Low-Pass FIR Design

**Problem:** Design a low-pass FIR filter with cutoff frequency $f_c = 100$ Hz, sampling frequency $f_s = 1000$ Hz, using a Hamming window of length $N = 21$. Compute the filter coefficients $h(n)$.

**Given:** $N = 21$, $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c / f_s = 2\pi(100)/1000 = 0.2\pi$ rad/sample.

**Step 1 — Ideal (sinc) impulse response.** The ideal low-pass filter's impulse response is:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c(n - M))}{\pi(n - M)}, \quad M = \frac{N-1}{2} = 10$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the center sample** at $n = M = 10$:

$$h_d(10) = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} = \frac{0.2\pi}{\pi} = 0.20$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the symmetric off-center samples** for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, 10$:

$$h_d(10 \pm k) = \frac{\sin(0.2\pi k)}{\pi k}$$

For example:
- $k=1$: $h_d(9) = h_d(11) = \sin(0.2\pi)/(\pi) = 0.5878/3.1416 \approx 0.1871$
- $k=2$: $h_d(8) = h_d(12) = \sin(0.4\pi)/(2\pi) = 0.9511/6.2832 \approx 0.1514$
- $k=3$: $h_d(7) = h_d(13) = \sin(0.6\pi)/(3\pi) = 0.9511/9.4248 \approx 0.1009$
- $k=4$: $h_d(6) = h_d(14) = \sin(0.8\pi)/(4\pi) = 0.5878/12.566 \approx 0.0468$
- $k=5$: $h_d(5) = h_d(15) = \sin(\pi)/(5\pi) = 0/15.708 \approx 0$

**Step 4 — Compute the Hamming window** $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$:

$$w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{20}\right)$$

Selected values:
- $w(0) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(0) = 0.08$
- $w(5) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(\pi/2) = 0.54$
- $w(10) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(\pi) = 1.00$
- $w(15) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(3\pi/2) = 0.54$
- $w(20) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi) = 0.08$

**Step 5 — Multiply to obtain the final coefficients** $h(n) = h_d(n)\cdot w(n)$:

| $n$ | $h_d(n)$ | $w(n)$ | $h(n) = h_d \cdot w$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0.08 | **0.0000** |
| 5 | 0.0468 | 0.54 | **0.0253** |
| 9 | 0.1871 | 0.957 | **0.1790** |
| 10 | 0.2000 | 1.00 | **0.2000** |
| 11 | 0.1871 | 0.957 | **0.1790** |
| 15 | 0.0468 | 0.54 | **0.0253** |
| 20 | 0 | 0.08 | **0.0000** |

> [!TIP]
> **Symmetry check:** Note that $h(n) = h(20 - n)$ for all $n$, confirming the **Type-I linear-phase FIR** property. This guarantees a constant group delay of $\tau_g = (21-1)/(2 \cdot 1000) = 10$ ms.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: FIR Filter Inside a Numerical Distance Relay

This diagram shows how the FIR filter integrates into the signal-processing chain of a modern numerical distance relay. The CT/PT signals are anti-alias filtered, sampled by the ADC, and then processed by a chain of FIR filters (low-pass + band-pass) before the impedance (mho) characteristic is evaluated.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ACQ["Analog Front End"]
        ct["Current Transformer CT"]
        pt["Potential Transformer PT"]
        aaf["Analog Anti-Alias LPF"]
        adc["Analog to Digital Converter ADC"]
    end
    subgraph DSP["Digital Signal Processing on DSP FPGA"]
        firLP["FIR Low Pass Filter Decimation"]
        firBP["FIR Band Pass Filter 50 Hz Extraction"]
        firHP["FIR High Pass DC Offset Removal"]
        phasor["Phasor Estimator DFT or Correlation"]
    end
    subgraph PROT["Protection Logic"]
        imp["Impedance Calculator Z = V / I"]
        char["Mho or Quadrilateral Characteristic"]
        trip["Trip Coil Driver"]
    end
    ct --> aaf
    pt --> aaf
    aaf --> adc
    adc --> firLP
    firLP --> firHP
    firHP --> firBP
    firBP --> phasor
    phasor --> imp
    imp --> char
    char --> trip
```

### 4.3 Design Flow for a KTU-Style FIR Filter Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 Specify f_s f_c and f_stop"] --> B["Step 2 Compute digital frequencies omega_c and omega_stop"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Compute filter order N using Kaiser formula"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Compute ideal impulse response h_d n sinc"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Choose window w n Hamming etc"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Multiply h n = h_d n times w n"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Verify linear phase and magnitude response"]
    G --> H["Step 8 Deploy to DSP or relay firmware"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filter. Why are FIR filters preferred over IIR filters in numerical protection of power systems?*

**Model Answer:**

A Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filter is a discrete-time, linear, time-invariant digital filter whose impulse response has finite duration of $N$ samples. Its output is given by:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,x(n-k)$$

**Reasons FIR is preferred in numerical protection:**

1. **Unconditional stability** — All poles lie at the origin (inside the unit circle), so the filter can never become unstable regardless of the input or coefficient values.
2. **Exact linear phase** — When coefficients satisfy $h(k) = h(N-1-k)$, the group delay is constant. This means all frequency components (50 Hz, harmonics, transients) are delayed by the same amount, **preserving the waveform shape** — critical for accurate phasor estimation and impedance calculation.
3. **No feedback** — A fault-induced DC offset naturally decays within $N$ samples; it is not fed back and amplified, unlike in IIR filters where DC can persist for many cycles.
4. **Finite memory** — Predictable numerical behaviour, simpler to test and certify under IEC 60255.

*(Self-contained 3-mark answer covering definition + 3 valid reasons.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the linear-phase condition for an FIR filter. What is the significance of constant group delay in distance relays?*

**Model Answer:**

The **linear-phase condition** for an FIR filter is that the impulse response coefficients must be either **symmetric** or **anti-symmetric** about the centre tap:

$$h(k) = h(N-1-k) \quad \text{(symmetric — Types I and II)}$$

$$h(k) = -h(N-1-k) \quad \text{(anti-symmetric — Types III and IV)}$$

When this condition is satisfied, the phase response becomes:

$$\phi(\omega) = -\omega\!\left(\frac{N-1}{2}\right)$$

which is a *linear* function of $\omega$, and the **group delay** becomes the constant:

$$\tau_g = \frac{N-1}{2}\text{ samples}$$

**Significance in distance relays:** A constant group delay ensures that the voltage and current samples used in the impedance calculation $Z = V/I$ are delayed by exactly the same time. If the phase shifts differed between the V-channel and I-channel, the computed impedance would rotate on the R-X plane, potentially causing the relay to **over-reach or under-reach** by 5–15 % during high-resistance faults. Constant $\tau_g$ eliminates this error source.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**
*(a)* Derive the transfer function $H(z)$ of an $N$-tap FIR filter starting from its difference equation. Comment on the location of its poles and hence justify its unconditional stability. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Design a 9-tap low-pass FIR filter with cutoff frequency $f_c = 250$ Hz, sampling frequency $f_s = 2000$ Hz, using the **rectangular window**. Compute all filter coefficients $h(0), h(1), \ldots, h(8)$ and state the linear-phase property. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q3(a):**

**Derivation:** Starting with the difference equation:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,x(n-k)$$

Applying the Z-transform using $\mathcal{Z}\{x(n-k)\} = z^{-k}X(z)$:

$$Y(z) = \Bigl[\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,z^{-k}\Bigr]X(z)$$

Therefore the transfer function is:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,z^{-k}$$

**Pole-zero analysis:** Factor $H(z)$ as a polynomial in $z^{-1}$:

$$H(z) = \frac{h(0)\,z^{N-1} + h(1)\,z^{N-2} + \cdots + h(N-1)}{z^{N-1}}$$

The denominator is $z^{N-1}$, so there are $N-1$ **poles**, all located at $z = 0$. Since $|z_{\text{pole}}| = 0 < 1$, every pole lies strictly inside the unit circle.

**Stability conclusion:** A discrete-time LTI system is Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable **if and only if** all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle. Because all $N-1$ poles of the FIR transfer function are at the origin, the stability condition is satisfied **for any finite choice of coefficients $h(k)$**. This is the rigorous proof of unconditional stability.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q3(b):**

**Step 1 — Digital cutoff frequency:**

$$\omega_c = 2\pi\,\frac{f_c}{f_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{250}{2000} = 0.25\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Centre index:** $M = (N-1)/2 = (9-1)/2 = 4$.

**Step 3 — Ideal (sinc) impulse response:**

$$h_d(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c(n - M))}{\pi(n - M)}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, 8$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each sample** ($n - M = n - 4$):

| $n$ | $n-4$ | $h_d(n) = \sin(0.25\pi(n-4))/[\pi(n-4)]$ | $h(n)$ (Rectangular: $w(n)=1$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | −4 | $\sin(-\pi)/(−4\pi) = 0$ | **0.0000** |
| 1 | −3 | $\sin(-0.75\pi)/(-3\pi) = 0.7071/9.4248$ | **0.0750** |
| 2 | −2 | $\sin(-0.5\pi)/(-2\pi) = 1/6.2832$ | **0.1592** |
| 3 | −1 | $\sin(-0.25\pi)/(-\pi) = 0.7071/3.1416$ | **0.2251** |
| 4 | 0 | $0.25\pi/\pi = 0.25$ | **0.2500** |
| 5 | 1 | $\sin(0.25\pi)/\pi = 0.2251$ | **0.2251** |
| 6 | 2 | $0.1592$ | **0.1592** |
| 7 | 3 | $0.0750$ | **0.0750** |
| 8 | 4 | $0$ | **0.0000** |

**Step 5 — Linear-phase verification:** The coefficients are symmetric: $h(0) = h(8) = 0$, $h(1) = h(7) = 0.0750$, $h(2) = h(6) = 0.1592$, $h(3) = h(5) = 0.2251$, $h(4) = 0.2500$. This satisfies $h(n) = h(8-n)$ — a **Type-I linear-phase FIR** with constant group delay:

$$\tau_g = \frac{N-1}{2}\cdot T_s = \frac{8}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2000} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
*(a)* Explain the **windowing method** of FIR filter design. Compare the Hamming and Blackman windows in terms of main-lobe width and side-lobe attenuation, with reference to their suitability for relay signal processing. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* The impulse response of a 5-tap FIR filter is $\{h(0), h(1), h(2), h(3), h(4)\} = \{0.1, \, 0.25, \, 0.3, \, 0.25, \, 0.1\}$. Compute the magnitude response at $\omega = 0$, $\omega = \pi/2$, and $\omega = \pi$. Comment on the type of symmetry and the linear-phase property. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q4(a):**

**Windowing method:** The ideal frequency-selective filter has an *infinite-length* impulse response $h_d(n)$ (e.g., a sinc function for a low-pass filter). Since this is non-causal and infinite, we multiply it by a *finite-length window* $w(n)$ that is non-zero only for $0 \le n \le N-1$:

$$h(n) = h_d(n)\cdot w(n), \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

**Comparison table:**

| Parameter | Hamming | Blackman |
|---|---|---|
| Main-lobe width | $8\pi/N$ | $12\pi/N$ |
| Side-lobe level | **−43 dB** | **−58 dB** |
| Passband ripple | ≈ 0.06 dB | ≈ 0.02 dB |
| Transition width | Moderate | Wide |

**Suitability for relay signal processing:** The Hamming window is the **default choice** in numerical relays because it offers a good balance between transition width and side-lobe attenuation. Its −43 dB stopband rejection is more than adequate to suppress harmonics and noise for fundamental-phasor extraction. The Blackman window is used in **ultra-sensitive** applications such as traveling-wave fault locators, where the **−58 dB** attenuation is needed to remove converter-driven harmonics from HVDC lines, at the cost of a wider transition band (i.e., a slightly slower relay).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q4(b):**

The frequency response of the FIR filter is:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{4} h(n)\,e^{-j\omega n} = 0.1\,e^{0} + 0.25\,e^{-j\omega} + 0.3\,e^{-j2\omega} + 0.25\,e^{-j3\omega} + 0.1\,e^{-j4\omega}$$

**At $\omega = 0$:** All exponentials equal 1:

$$H(e^{j0}) = 0.1 + 0.25 + 0.3 + 0.25 + 0.1 = 1.00$$

$$\bigl\vert H(e^{j0}) \bigr\vert = \mathbf{1.00}$$

**At $\omega = \pi/2$:** $e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$, $e^{-j\pi} = -1$, $e^{-j3\pi/2} = +j$, $e^{-j2\pi} = +1$:

$$H(e^{j\pi/2}) = 0.1 + 0.25(-j) + 0.3(-1) + 0.25(+j) + 0.1(+1)$$

$$= (0.1 - 0.3 + 0.1) + j(-0.25 + 0.25) = -0.10 + j(0)$$

$$\bigl\vert H(e^{j\pi/2}) \bigr\vert = \mathbf{0.10}$$

**At $\omega = \pi$:** $e^{-j\pi n} = (-1)^n$:

$$H(e^{j\pi}) = 0.1 - 0.25 + 0.3 - 0.25 + 0.1 = 0.00$$

$$\bigl\vert H(e^{j\pi}) \bigr\vert = \mathbf{0.00}$$

**Symmetry and linear phase:** The coefficients satisfy $h(0)=h(4)=0.1$ and $h(1)=h(3)=0.25$, which is the **symmetric** condition $h(n) = h(4-n)$. This is a **Type-I linear-phase FIR** (symmetric, $N=5$ odd). The phase response is therefore:

$$\phi(\omega) = -2\omega \quad \text{(linear in } \omega\text{)}$$

with constant group delay $\tau_g = 2$ samples. The filter acts as a **low-pass** filter (gain 1 at DC, 0.10 at Nyquist, and 0 at $\omega = \pi$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization by $\pi$:** A common error is writing $h_d(n) = \sin(\omega_c(n-M))/(n-M)$ without the $\pi$ in the denominator. This gives a coefficient $\pi$ times too large, and the magnitude response will not equal 1 at DC. Examiner deducts **1.5 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $f_c$ and $\omega_c$:** Students often use $\omega_c = f_c$ instead of $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c/f_s$. This causes a cutoff shift by a factor of $2\pi$ — the designed filter will not pass the intended frequency band. Examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the symmetry check:** Even when coefficients are computed correctly, students who do not explicitly verify $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ lose the **1 mark** reserved for proving linear-phase behaviour.
> 4. **Omitting units and passband interpretation:** A bare numerical answer like "$h(4) = 0.25$" loses the 0.5 mark for interpreting it as the DC gain fraction. Always state: *"The centre coefficient $h(4) = 0.25$ contributes 25 % of the DC content to the output."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4**

- **FIR definition:** $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)x(n-k)$ — no feedback, finite memory.
- **Transfer function:** $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)z^{-k}$ — has $N-1$ poles **all at the origin**.
- **Stability:** Unconditional, BIBO stable for any finite $h(k)$.
- **Linear-phase condition:** $h(k) = \pm h(N-1-k)$ — symmetric or anti-symmetric.
- **Group delay:** $\tau_g = (N-1)T_s/2$ — constant for linear-phase FIR.
- **Four FIR types** (I, II, III, IV) — based on symmetry and odd/even $N$.
- **Windowing formula:** $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$.
- **Rectangular window side-lobe level:** **−13 dB** (poor for relays).
- **Hamming window side-lobe level:** **−43 dB** (preferred for relays).
- **Blackman window side-lobe level:** **−58 dB** (used in traveling-wave relays).
- **Hamming window formula:** $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$.
- **Hanning window formula:** $w(n) = 0.5[1 - \cos(2\pi n/(N-1))]$.
- **Ideal low-pass impulse response:** $h_d(n) = \sin(\omega_c(n-M))/[\pi(n-M)]$, with $M = (N-1)/2$.
- **Centre-tap value:** $h_d(M) = \omega_c/\pi$ — this is the DC gain fraction.
- **Power-system applications:** CT/PT signal conditioning, phasor estimation, anti-aliasing, traveling-wave detection, PMU synchrophasors.
- **Comparison with IIR:** FIR uses *more coefficients* for the same sharpness but is *always stable* and *linear phase*; IIR is computationally cheaper but can become unstable and has nonlinear phase.
- **Sampling-rate KTU norm:** 24–80 samples per 50 Hz cycle; group delay must be < 1 cycle (20 ms at 50 Hz) for high-speed distance relays.
- **Standard reference:** IEEE C37.118.1 (PMU), IEC 60255-118 (numerical protection), IEEE Std 4 (CT/PT).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filters with Sine and Cosine Windows. Wide Area Protection and Measurement: Phasor Measurement Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Filters with Sine and Cosine Windows. Wide Area Protection and Measurement: Phasor Measurement Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Filters with Sine and Cosine Windows (Orthogonal Filters)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Sine and Cosine Window Filters** (also called **Orthogonal Multipliers** or **Quadrature Filters**) are fundamental building blocks in digital relaying used to extract the **fundamental frequency component** (typically **50 Hz** in India) from distorted, transient-rich power system signals. They operate on the principle of the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** by mathematically correlating (multiplying and integrating) the input signal against a pair of orthogonal reference waveforms — one in-phase (cosine) and one quadrature-phase (sine).

The output of these two filters forms the **real and imaginary parts** of a complex phasor that completely describes the sinusoidal steady-state of the protected quantity at the fundamental power frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** These filters are the *mathematical heart* of virtually every numerical relay — distance relays, differential relays, and overcurrent relays all use sine/cosine multipliers (or their recursive equivalents) to compute impedance, current magnitude, and phase angle.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Whisper Listener in a Noisy Stadium"
Imagine you are in a **cricket stadium** with 100,000 people screaming, blowing horns, and clapping. You are wearing a **headset tuned to a single friend's voice** (say, the captain giving instructions). 

- The **crowd noise** = distorted fault current with harmonics, DC offset, and noise.
- **Your friend's voice** = the fundamental 50 Hz component you want to extract.
- The **cosine reference** = one earphone picking up the in-phase part of the signal.
- The **sine reference** = the other earphone (shifted by 90°) picking up the quadrature part.
- The **integrator (low-pass filter)** = your brain that averages everything over a few seconds and rejects random noise.

By combining what both earphones "hear," you get the **complete message** — both loudness (magnitude) and timing offset (phase angle). This is exactly how sine/cosine window filters recover the phasor of a power system signal from a noisy mixture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Fundamental power frequency in India: **50 Hz** (angular frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159$ rad/s)
> - Sampling rate in modern numerical relays: **20 to 64 samples per cycle**
> - One full power cycle duration: **20 ms** (at 50 Hz)

---

## 1.2 Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) and Wide Area Protection

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)** is a high-speed, GPS-time-synchronized digital device that measures the **synchronized phasor (synchrophasor)** of voltage and current waveforms at a power system bus in real time. A PMU samples the analog waveform, applies an **anti-aliasing filter**, computes the fundamental phasor using DFT/sine-cosine filters, and tags the resulting phasor with a **high-precision UTC time stamp** obtained from a Global Positioning System (GPS) receiver.

**Wide Area Protection (WAP)** refers to a coordinated protection and control philosophy that uses **PMU data streamed from geographically dispersed substations** to a central Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC), enabling operators to detect and mitigate wide-area disturbances (e.g., inter-area oscillations, voltage instability, cascading failures) in time frames of **milliseconds to seconds** — far faster than traditional SCADA (which operates on 2–4 second cycles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PMUs are governed by the international standard **IEEE C37.118.1** (synchrophasor measurement) and **IEEE C37.118.2** (communication), with a reporting rate of **10 to 60 phasors per second** (modern PMUs support up to **240 frames/s** for 50 Hz systems).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Symphony Orchestra with a Master Conductor"
Think of a power system as a **giant orchestra** with thousands of musicians (generators) playing in different cities.

- **Without PMUs:** Each musician plays in time with their local clock, but you can never tell if the entire orchestra is in sync. A local listener (SCADA) can only hear the musicians in their own hall every few seconds.
- **With PMUs:** Every musician wears a **GPS-synced earpiece** (atomic-clock accuracy) and shouts out their exact note, loudness, and timing (phasor) **30 to 60 times per second** to a central **master conductor** (PDC/Control Center). The conductor instantly spots when one section drifts out of tune (oscillation), when the overall volume becomes dangerously loud (overload), or when a soloist suddenly goes silent (generator trip) — and can issue corrective commands before the music collapses.

This is **Wide Area Situational Awareness** — a holistic, time-coherent view of the entire interconnected grid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics of PMUs:**
> - **Time synchronization accuracy:** better than **±1 microsecond** (via GPS)
> - **Phasor estimation accuracy:** Total Vector Error (TVE) **< 1%** as per IEEE C37.118
> - **Reporting rate:** 10, 25, 50, or **60 frames per second** in steady state
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of Sine and Cosine Window Filters

### The Underlying Principle: Orthogonal Decomposition
A **pure sinusoidal signal** can be uniquely represented by a **phasor** — a rotating vector with a magnitude and a phase angle. Mathematically, this is equivalent to decomposing the signal onto two orthogonal basis functions: the **cosine** (in-phase) and the **sine** (quadrature) reference waveforms at the fundamental power frequency.

Consider an arbitrary input signal containing the fundamental component plus noise/harmonics:
$$v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) + \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n \cos(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n) + v_{dc}(t) + \text{noise}(t)$$

The goal of the sine/cosine filter is to recover only the **fundamental phasor** $V = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \angle \phi$.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Multiplication Stage:** The input signal is multiplied simultaneously by a **cosine reference** $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ and a **sine reference** $\sin(\omega_0 t)$, both generated at the exact nominal power frequency.

2. **Integration / Averaging Stage:** Each product is integrated (or summed) over exactly **one complete power cycle** ($T = 1/f_0 = 20$ ms). This averaging stage acts as a **narrow band-pass filter** centered on $f_0$.

3. **Orthogonality Exploitation:** Because the sine and cosine are mathematically orthogonal over a complete period, all harmonic and noise components average out to **zero**. Only the fundamental component survives the integration.

4. **Phasor Construction:** The two filter outputs are combined to form the **complex phasor**:
   $$\mathbf{V} = V_c - jV_s$$

5. **Magnitude and Angle Extraction:**
   - Magnitude: $V = \sqrt{V_c^2 + V_s^2}$
   - Phase angle: $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-V_s}{V_c}\right)$ (note the sign convention)

### Why These Filters Work — The "Why" and "How"

> **Why does integration remove harmonics?**
> The integral of $\cos(n\omega_0 t) \cdot \cos(\omega_0 t)$ over one fundamental period equals **zero** for any integer $n \neq 1$. This is the **orthogonality property of Fourier basis functions** — sine and cosine at different frequencies are mathematically uncorrelated over a full period.

> **How does it extract phase information?**
> The phase angle $\phi$ of the original signal determines *how much* of it lies in the cosine direction (real axis) versus the sine direction (imaginary axis). By measuring both projections, the filter fully reconstructs the phasor.

---

## 2.2 Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU) — Internal Architecture & Theory

### Operational Logic of a PMU

1. **Analog Input Stage:** Voltage and current signals from instrument transformers (CTs/PTs) are conditioned, scaled, and passed through an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** (typically 4th-order Butterworth, cutoff $\approx$ 1.5 kHz).

2. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** The filtered analog signal is sampled at a high rate (typically **1.2 kHz to 3.2 kHz**, i.e., 24–64 samples per 50 Hz cycle).

3. **GPS Synchronization:** A **GPS receiver** provides **1 Pulse-Per-Second (1-PPS)** and an IRIG-B timecode. The PMU's internal clock is disciplined to GPS, achieving **±1 μs** accuracy. The sampling instants are locked to UTC time.

4. **Phasor Computation:** The discrete samples are processed by a **DFT engine** (sine/cosine window filter) to extract the fundamental phasor.

5. **Time-Tagging:** Each computed phasor is stamped with a **UTC time tag** corresponding to the **exact moment** the data was sampled (or the reporting instant).

6. **Data Formatting:** The phasor, frequency, and rate-of-change-of-frequency (ROCOF) are formatted as per **IEEE C37.118.2** protocol and streamed to a Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC).

### The Concept of "Synchrophasor"
A **synchrophasor** is a phasor that has been **measured at a precisely known instant in time** and is **synchronized to a common reference clock (UTC)** across all measurement points in the grid. This allows phasors from different substations (hundreds of km apart) to be **directly compared and combined** — something impossible with traditional, unsynchronized SCADA measurements.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Input signal model | $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ | Volts (instantaneous) |
| 2 | Cosine filter output (in-phase) | $V_c = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt$ | Volts (real part) |
| 3 | Sine filter output (quadrature) | $V_s = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \sin(\omega_0 t)\, dt$ | Volts (imaginary part, sign may vary) |
| 4 | Complex phasor | $\mathbf{V} = V_c - jV_s = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} e^{j\phi}$ | Complex quantity |
| 5 | Phasor magnitude (RMS) | $V = \sqrt{V_c^2 + V_s^2}$ | Volts (RMS) |
| 6 | Phasor phase angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-V_s}{V_c}\right)$ | Radians (or degrees) |
| 7 | Fundamental angular frequency | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi \times 50$ | rad/s (= 314.159) |
| 8 | Power cycle period (50 Hz) | $T = 1/f_0 = 1/50$ | seconds (= 20 ms) |
| 9 | Orthogonality identity (harmonic rejection) | $\int_{0}^{T} \cos(n\omega_0 t)\cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt = 0$ for $n \neq 1$ | Valid for integer $n \geq 2$ |
| 10 | Discrete (sampled) cosine output | $V_c = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v[k] \cos\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$ | $N$ = samples per cycle |
| 11 | Discrete (sampled) sine output | $V_s = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v[k] \sin\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$ | Sum over one cycle |
| 12 | PMU reporting rate (typical) | 10, 25, 50, or 60 frames/second | Frames per second |
| 13 | GPS timing accuracy | $\Delta t \leq \pm 1 \,\mu s$ | Corresponds to $\pm 0.018°$ at 50 Hz |
| 14 | Phase angle error from time skew | $\Delta\phi = \omega_0 \cdot \Delta t$ | In radians |
| 15 | Total Vector Error (TVE) | $\text{TVE} = \frac{\vert \hat{\mathbf{V}} - \mathbf{V}_{\text{true}} \vert}{\vert \mathbf{V}_{\text{true}} \vert} \times 100\%$ | Must be < 1% (IEEE C37.118) |
| 16 | Frequency estimation (zero-crossing) | $f = \frac{1}{T_{zc}}$ | Hz (from successive zero-crossings) |
| 17 | ROCOF (df/dt) | $\text{ROCOF} = \frac{\Delta f}{\Delta t}$ | Hz/s |
| 18 | Wide-area signal transit time | $t_{pmu} \approx 25$ to $100$ ms | For control actions |
| 19 | PMU communication standard | IEEE C37.118.1 (data) / C37.118.2 (comm.) | UDP/IP or TCP/IP |
| 20 | WAMS architecture levels | PMU → PDC → Super- PDC → Control Center | Hierarchical data flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Sine/cosine filters** form the algorithm inside every numerical distance relay (Zones 1, 2, 3) and every phasor-based controller in FACTS/HVDC systems.
> - **PMUs** are the data backbone of every modern smart grid — installed in thousands of substations worldwide (India's URTDSM project, the Unified Real-Time Dynamic State Measurement initiative, deploys PMUs at 400 kV and above).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output of the Cosine Window Filter

### Statement
Given a generic input signal containing the fundamental plus a DC component and an $n$-th harmonic, derive the output of the cosine multiplier followed by an integrator over one fundamental period.

### Setup
Let the input signal be:
$$v(t) = V_{dc} + V_1 \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi_1) + V_n \cos(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n)$$

The cosine filter output is defined as:
$$V_c = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt$$

where $T = 2\pi/\omega_0$ is one fundamental period.

### Step-by-Step Expansion

We substitute $v(t)$ into the integral, splitting it into three additive terms because the integral of a sum equals the sum of the integrals:

$$
V_c = \frac{2}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T} V_{dc} \cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt + \int_{0}^{T} V_1 \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi_1) \cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt + \int_{0}^{T} V_n \cos(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n) \cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt \right]
$$

#### Term 1 — DC Component
$$
\int_{0}^{T} V_{dc} \cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt = V_{dc} \left[ \frac{\sin(\omega_0 t)}{\omega_0} \right]_{0}^{T} = V_{dc} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega_0 T) - \sin(0)}{\omega_0}
$$

Since $\omega_0 T = 2\pi$, we have $\sin(2\pi) = 0$, so:

$$
\int_{0}^{T} V_{dc} \cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt = 0
$$

**Interpretation:** The DC component is completely rejected by the cosine filter.

#### Term 2 — Fundamental Component
Using the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:
$$\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\right]$$

with $A = \omega_0 t + \phi_1$ and $B = \omega_0 t$:

$$
\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi_1)\cos(\omega_0 t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(\phi_1) + \cos(2\omega_0 t + \phi_1)\right]
$$

Integrating term-by-term over $[0, T]$:

$$
\int_{0}^{T} \frac{1}{2}\cos(\phi_1)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}\cos(\phi_1) \cdot T
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{T} \frac{1}{2}\cos(2\omega_0 t + \phi_1)\, dt = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 t + \phi_1)}{2\omega_0} \right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{1}{4\omega_0}\left[\sin(4\pi + \phi_1) - \sin(\phi_1)\right]
$$

Since $\sin(4\pi + \phi_1) = \sin(\phi_1)$, this second integral is also **zero**. Hence:

$$
\int_{0}^{T} V_1 \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi_1)\cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt = \frac{V_1 T}{2} \cos(\phi_1)
$$

Multiplying by the $2/T$ prefactor:

$$
V_{c,\text{fundamental}} = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{V_1 T}{2} \cos(\phi_1) = V_1 \cos(\phi_1)
$$

#### Term 3 — $n$-th Harmonic Component ($n \neq 1$)
Applying the same product-to-sum identity with $A = n\omega_0 t + \phi_n$ and $B = \omega_0 t$:

$$
\cos(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n)\cos(\omega_0 t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos((n-1)\omega_0 t + \phi_n) + \cos((n+1)\omega_0 t + \phi_n)\right]
$$

For $n \neq 1$, both $(n-1)$ and $(n+1)$ are non-zero integers, and the integral of any cosine of the form $\cos(m\omega_0 t + \text{const.})$ over $[0, T]$ for integer $m \neq 0$ is **zero**. Therefore:

$$
\int_{0}^{T} V_n \cos(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n)\cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt = 0
$$

### Final Result for Cosine Filter

$$
V_c = V_1 \cos(\phi_1)
$$

**Conclusion:** The cosine filter extracts only the **in-phase (real) component** of the fundamental phasor, completely rejecting DC, all harmonics, and noise.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Output of the Sine Window Filter

By an **analogous derivation** (which the student should reproduce on the exam), starting with the sine reference multiplier:

$$V_s = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \sin(\omega_0 t)\, dt$$

Following the same three-term decomposition and using the identity:
$$\cos A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}\left[\sin(A+B) - \sin(A-B)\right]$$

The final result is:

$$
V_s = V_1 \sin(\phi_1)
$$

**Conclusion:** The sine filter extracts only the **quadrature (imaginary) component** of the fundamental phasor.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Complete Phasor Reconstruction

### Setup
The two filter outputs are:
$$V_c = V_1 \cos(\phi_1), \qquad V_s = V_1 \sin(\phi_1)$$

where $V_1$ is the **peak** amplitude of the fundamental component. To express the phasor in standard **RMS** form, we divide by $\sqrt{2}$.

### Magnitude (RMS) of the Phasor

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{V_c^2 + V_s^2} = \sqrt{V_1^2 \cos^2(\phi_1) + V_1^2 \sin^2(\phi_1)}
$$

Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{V_1^2 \left[\cos^2(\phi_1) + \sin^2(\phi_1)\right]} = \sqrt{V_1^2} = V_1
$$

(Note: this is the peak value. For RMS, the convention is $V_{rms} = V_1/\sqrt{2}$.)

### Phase Angle of the Phasor

$$
\tan(\phi_1) = \frac{V_s}{V_c} = \frac{V_1 \sin(\phi_1)}{V_1 \cos(\phi_1)}
$$

Solving for $\phi_1$:

$$
\phi_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{V_s}{V_c}\right)
$$

### Complex Phasor Form (Euler's Identity)

Using $V_c = V_1 \cos\phi_1$ as the real part and $V_s = V_1 \sin\phi_1$ as the imaginary part:

$$
\mathbf{V} = V_c + jV_s = V_1 \cos\phi_1 + jV_1 \sin\phi_1
$$

By Euler's formula $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$:

$$
\mathbf{V} = V_1 \left[\cos\phi_1 + j\sin\phi_1\right] = V_1 e^{j\phi_1}
$$

This is the **complex phasor** of the fundamental component — fully recovered from the two orthogonal filter outputs.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Problem Style

**Problem:** A voltage signal is given by:
$$v(t) = 100\cos(314.16\, t) + 30\cos(942.48\, t) + 10 \text{ V (DC)}$$
(All values in peak Volts; first term is 50 Hz fundamental, second is the 3rd harmonic, third is a DC offset.)

Compute the phasor of the fundamental component using sine and cosine window filters.

**Solution:**

*Angular frequencies:*
- $\omega_0 = 314.16$ rad/s (50 Hz)
- $3\omega_0 = 942.48$ rad/s (150 Hz, the 3rd harmonic)
- Period $T = 2\pi/\omega_0 = 0.02$ s

*Cosine filter:*

$$
V_c = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T}\left[100\cos(\omega_0 t) + 30\cos(3\omega_0 t) + 10\right]\cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt
$$

DC term → 0 (proved in §3.1).
3rd harmonic term → 0 (orthogonality, $n=3 \neq 1$).
Fundamental term → 100 (from §3.1 with $\phi_1 = 0$).

$$
V_c = 100 \text{ V (peak)}
$$

*Sine filter:*

$$
V_s = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t) \sin(\omega_0 t)\, dt
$$

All three terms integrate to **zero** because the fundamental has $\phi_1 = 0$ (so $V_1 \sin\phi_1 = 0$), and the DC/3rd harmonic are orthogonal to $\sin(\omega_0 t)$.

$$
V_s = 0
$$

*Phasor:*

$$
V_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{100^2 + 0^2} = 100 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{2}} = 70.71 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0}{100}\right) = 0^\circ
$$

**Final answer:** $\mathbf{V} = 70.71 \angle 0°$ V (RMS).

---

## 3.5 PMU Phase Error Derivation from GPS Time Skew

**Problem:** A PMU installed at a remote substation has a GPS timing error of $\Delta t = +2 \,\mu\text{s}$. Calculate the resulting phase angle error in the measured phasor (at 50 Hz).

**Solution:**

A positive time skew means the PMU samples the waveform **later** than the true reference instant. For a signal $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$, a shift of $+\Delta t$ in the sampling time produces a measured phasor:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{V}} = V_m e^{j(\phi + \omega_0 \Delta t)}
$$

The additional phase error is:
$$
\Delta\phi = \omega_0 \cdot \Delta t
$$

Substituting values:
$$
\Delta\phi = 314.16 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
$$

Converting to degrees:
$$
\Delta\phi = 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.036° = 36 \text{ millidegrees}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 2 μs GPS error causes a phase error of about 0.036°. This is why the IEEE C37.118 standard mandates GPS accuracy better than ±1 μs — to keep phase errors within a few millidegrees for accurate wide-area angle difference monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Sine and Cosine Window Filter Signal Chain

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal processing chain of an orthogonal (sine/cosine) window filter inside a numerical relay or PMU.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input v of t] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    C --> D1[Sample v k]
    C --> D2[Sample v k]
    D1 --> E1[Multiplier x cos of 2 pi k over N]
    D2 --> E2[Multiplier x sin of 2 pi k over N]
    E1 --> F1[Accumulator N samples]
    E2 --> F2[Accumulator N samples]
    F1 --> G1[Real Part V sub c]
    F2 --> G2[Imag Part V sub s]
    G1 --> H[Phasor Computer]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I1[Magnitude equals sqrt Vc squared + Vs squared]
    H --> I2[Phase Angle equals arctan Vs over Vc]
    H --> I3[Complex Phasor V sub c minus j V sub s]
    I1 --> J[Output: Fundamental Phasor]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The same sampled signal $v[k]$ is fed into **two parallel branches**.
- The upper branch multiplies by a **cosine reference** stored in a lookup table.
- The lower branch multiplies by a **sine reference** (90° phase-shifted).
- Each branch sums (accumulates) over exactly **N samples = one power cycle**.
- The two outputs ($V_c$ and $V_s$) feed a phasor computer that delivers magnitude, angle, and the complex phasor.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU) Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Substation_Bus ["400 kV Substation Busbar"]
        CT1[Current Transformer CT]
        PT1[Potential Transformer PT]
    end
    CT1 --> A1[Signal Conditioning and Scaling]
    PT1 --> A2[Signal Conditioning and Scaling]
    A1 --> AAF1[Anti-Aliasing Filter 1.5 kHz]
    A2 --> AAF2[Anti-Aliasing Filter 1.5 kHz]
    AAF1 --> ADC1[ADC Sampler]
    AAF2 --> ADC2[ADC Sampler]
    ADC1 --> PROC[DFT Engine: Sine and Cosine Window Filter]
    ADC2 --> PROC
    GPS[GPS Antenna plus Receiver 1 PPS plus IRIG-B] --> CLK[Disciplined Oscillator Clock]
    CLK --> ADC1
    CLK --> ADC2
    CLK --> TIMETAG[UTC Time Tag Generator]
    PROC --> PHASOR[Fundamental Phasor V and I]
    PHASOR --> TIMETAG
    TIMETAG --> FRAME[IEEE C37.118 Frame Formatter]
    FRAME --> NET[Communication Interface: Ethernet, UDP/IP]
    NET --> PDC[Phasor Data Concentrator PDC]
    PDC --> CC[Control Center / WAMS Server]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Analog signals from CTs/PTs are conditioned, filtered, and digitized.
- A **GPS-disciplined clock** locks the ADC sampling to UTC with ±1 μs accuracy.
- The **DFT engine** (sine/cosine window filter) computes the phasors.
- Each phasor is **time-tagged** and formatted as a C37.118 frame.
- The data is streamed via Ethernet to a local PDC, then forwarded to the central control center.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Wide Area Measurement System (WAMS) Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[PMU at Substation A] --> PDC1[Local PDC]
    L2[PMU at Substation B] --> PDC1
    L3[PMU at Substation C] --> PDC1
    L4[PMU at Substation D] --> PDC2[Local PDC]
    L5[PMU at Substation E] --> PDC2
    L6[PMU at Substation F] --> PDC2
    PDC1 --> SPDC[Super PDC / State Estimator]
    PDC2 --> SPDC
    SPDC --> WAMS[WAMS Server: Real-time Monitoring]
    WAMS --> WAP[Wide Area Protection Controller: SPS, RAS, Adaptive Schemes]
    WAP --> ACTION[Corrective Action: Generation Trip, Load Shed, HVDC Modulation]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- PMUs stream data to local PDCs every 25–50 ms.
- PDCs aggregate and time-align data, then forward to a Super PDC.
- The Super PDC performs state estimation and feeds the WAMS server.
- Wide Area Protection schemes (Special Protection Schemes, Remedial Action Schemes) trigger automated corrective actions when system-wide disturbances are detected.
