<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q.4a]**
> **Define adaptive relaying. List any four advantages of adaptive relaying over conventional relaying.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Adaptive relaying** is a protection scheme in which the relay's operating characteristics and settings are **automatically adjusted in real-time** in response to changes in power system conditions such as fault level, network topology, and load profile.

**Four advantages:**
1. **Improved Security** — avoids false tripping caused by changing source impedance or fault resistance.
2. **Improved Dependability** — guarantees operation for in-zone faults even when system weakens.
3. **Better Coordination** — primary-backup coordination is maintained dynamically across topology changes.
4. **Higher Sensitivity under Varying Conditions** — pickup and reach can be lowered in weak-source conditions to detect high-resistance faults that fixed relays would miss.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q.3b]**
> **Mention any three applications where adaptive relaying is preferred over fixed-setting relays.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **FACTS-compensated transmission lines** (with TCSC, UPFC) where line impedance changes with compensation.
2. **Microgrids with high DER penetration**, where fault current magnitude is non-deterministic.
3. **Multi-terminal and parallel transmission lines**, where infeed effects change with switching operations.
4. *(Bonus)* Series-compensated EHV systems and weak-source zones of large interconnected grids.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Primary Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper 1, Q.11a,b]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of adaptive relaying with a neat block diagram. Discuss how it differs from conventional relaying. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a suitable example, derive the expression for adaptive reach setting of a distance relay when the source short-circuit capacity changes. Show the quantitative improvement over a fixed setting. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
Adaptive relaying is a protection philosophy where relay settings (pickup, time, reach) are automatically adjusted **in real-time** based on the prevailing state of the power system, without operator intervention. It uses **real-time measurements** (V, I, f, breaker status) and a **closed-loop decision engine** to compute new settings.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Power System] → [CT/VT] → [ADC] → [DSP & Phasor Extraction]
                                          ↓
                              [State Estimator: Zsource, SIR, Load]
                                          ↓
                              [Adaptive Function Library]
                                          ↓
                              [New Settings → Protection Algorithm]
                                          ↓
                                  [Trip Command → Breaker]
```

**Step 3 — Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Conventional Relay | Adaptive Relay |
|---|---|---|
| Setting | Fixed, set at commissioning | Dynamically updated |
| Response to topology change | Requires manual re-setting | Automatic re-setting |
| Performance in weak-source | Overreaches | Auto-corrected reach |
| Coordination | Static | Continuously optimized |
| Cost & Complexity | Low | Higher (needs IED, communication) |
| Suitability for FACTS/microgrids | Poor | Excellent |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Statement of the Problem (1 mark):**
Consider a 220 kV, 150 km line with positive-sequence impedance $Z_1 = 0.04 + j\,0.42 \;\Omega/\text{km}$. Original source capacity $S_1 = 15{,}000$ MVA; new source capacity $S_1^{new} = 4{,}000$ MVA. Compare fixed vs adaptive Zone-1 reach.

**Step 2 — Compute Total Line Impedance (1 mark):**

$$
Z_{line} = (0.04 + j\,0.42) \times 150 = 6.0 + j\,63.0 \;\Omega
$$

$$
\vert Z_{line}\vert = \sqrt{36 + 3969} = \sqrt{4005} \approx 63.29 \;\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Fixed Zone-1 Reach (1 mark):**

$$
Z_{reach,fixed} = 0.85 \times 63.29 \approx 53.79 \;\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Original and New Source Impedances (1 mark):**

$$
Z_{S1} = \frac{(220)^2}{15{,}000} \times 10^3 = \frac{48{,}400}{15{,}000} = 3.23 \;\Omega
$$

$$
Z_{S1}^{new} = \frac{48{,}400}{4{,}000} = 12.10 \;\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Compute SIRs (1 mark):**

$$
SIR_{orig} = \frac{3.23}{63.29} \approx 0.051
$$

$$
SIR_{new} = \frac{12.10}{63.29} \approx 0.191
$$

**Step 6 — Adaptive Correction Factor (1 mark):**

$$
\alpha = \frac{1}{1 + SIR_{new} \times 0.5} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0955} \approx 0.913
$$

**Step 7 — Adaptive Reach and Comparison (1 mark):**

$$
Z_{reach}^{adaptive} = 0.913 \times 0.85 \times 63.29 \approx 49.28 \;\Omega
$$

**Comparison:** Adaptive reduces reach from **53.79 Ω → 49.28 Ω**, eliminating the overreach tendency under weak-source conditions. **Quantitative gain ≈ 4.5 Ω of reach correction.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q.12a,b]**

> **(a)** With a block diagram, explain the application of adaptive relaying in a **microgrid with distributed energy resources (DERs)**. Why is fixed relaying unsuitable here? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An overcurrent relay on a 11 kV feeder has fixed pickup $I_{pickup} = 150$ A. The load varies between **60 A and 350 A** depending on industrial cycle. Motor starting inrush can reach **700 A for 0.3 s**. Using the adaptive pickup formula $I_{pickup}^{adaptive} = 1.3 \times I_{load}^{measured}$, demonstrate quantitatively how adaptive relaying improves security. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Microgrid Context (1 mark):**
A microgrid is a localized grid with **DERs** (solar PV, wind, diesel, battery storage) that can operate in either grid-connected or islanded mode. The short-circuit current contribution varies dramatically — from **5–10× rated current** in grid mode to **1.2–2× rated** in islanded mode.

**Step 2 — Why Fixed Relays Fail (2 marks):**
- A fixed pickup set for grid-connected mode **fails to operate** during islanded faults (current too small).
- A fixed pickup set for islanded mode **false-trips on grid-connected motor starting** (current too high).
- The relay has *no single correct setting* for both modes.

**Step 3 — Adaptive Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Microgrid V, I, f, Mode, DER Status] 
        ↓
[Mode Identifier: Grid-Connected or Islanded]
        ↓
[Adaptive Function: Scale Pickup with Total Fault Level]
        ↓
[New Pickup → Overcurrent Algorithm]
        ↓
[Coordination with Neighbouring Adaptive Relays]
        ↓
[Trip Signal]
```

**Step 4 — Advantages (2 marks):**
- Seamless transition between modes.
- Security against DER variability.
- Future-proof for additional DERs.
- Real-time coordination across the microgrid.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Fixed Relay Failure (1 mark):**
Fixed pickup = 150 A. Load at 350 A is **2.33 × pickup** → **nuisance trip risk**. Motor starting at 700 A for 0.3 s will operate an IDMT curve in <0.1 s → **definite false trip**.

**Step 2 — Adaptive Pickup at Minimum Load (1 mark):**

$$
I_{pickup}^{min} = 1.3 \times 60 = 78 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Adaptive Pickup at Peak Load (1 mark):**

$$
I_{pickup}^{max} = 1.3 \times 350 = 455 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 — Motor Inrush Response (1.5 marks):**
At 455 A pickup, a 700 A inrush gives $I/I_{pickup} = 1.54$. Using standard inverse curve ($A = 0.14, p = 0.02$):

$$
t_{op} = 0.14 \cdot \text{TMS} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1.54^{0.02} - 1}\right)
$$

With TMS = 1.0, $t_{op} \approx 6.4$ s — **inrush subsides before trip** (typically <0.5 s). **Adaptive relay survives the inrush.**

**Step 5 — Fault Response (1.5 marks):**
A genuine fault at 1500 A gives $I/I_{pickup} = 1500/455 = 3.30$:

$$
t_{op} \approx 0.14 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \frac{1}{3.30^{0.02} - 1} \approx 1.85 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Event | Fixed Relay | Adaptive Relay |
|---|---|---|
| Motor inrush 700 A / 0.3 s | **False trips** | **Holds (6.4 s >> 0.3 s)** |
| 1500 A real fault | Trips in 0.4 s | Trips in 1.85 s |
| Security gain | — | **Dramatic improvement** |
| Dependability note | Faster for faults | Slightly slower — can be tuned with **dual-characteristic** logic |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students write "adaptive relay changes settings every cycle" — this is **WRONG**. Settings change only on **state-change events** (topology change, source change), not on every sample. Showing 20 ms update cycle is unrealistic.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Conflating *adaptive* with *auto-reclosing*. They are different: auto-reclosing recloses the breaker; adaptivity re-tunes the relay settings.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to state the **CTI ≥ 0.3 s** coordination margin in overcurrent problems — losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** In numerical problems, students often forget to convert the system voltage correctly into source impedance — losing 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Using "pickup" and "reach" interchangeably in distance protection answers — these are **different concepts** (pickup is for overcurrent, reach is for distance).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- **Adaptive Relaying Definition:** Real-time, automatic adjustment of relay settings based on prevailing power system conditions.
- **Core Difference from Conventional:** Conventional = **fixed, static, set at commissioning**; Adaptive = **dynamic, closed-loop, self-adjusting**.
- **Four Pillars of Adaptivity:** Measurement → State Identification → Setting Computation → Re-setting (all in a closed loop).
- **Key Formula #1 — Adaptive Reach:** $Z_{reach}^{adaptive} = \alpha \times k \times \vert Z_{line}\vert$ where $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{1 + SIR \cdot k_{margin}}$.
- **Key Formula #2 — Adaptive Pickup:** $I_{pickup}^{adaptive} = k_{rel} \times I_{load}^{measured}$ with $k_{rel} = 1.3$ typical.
- **SIR Threshold = 4:** Beyond SIR = 4, fixed distance relays lose dependability; adaptive ones remain secure.
- **CTI = 0.3 s:** Standard Coordination Time Interval — must be maintained in any adaptive overcurrent scheme.
- **Reach factor k ∈ [0.8, 0.9]** for Zone-1 distance protection.
- **Primary Applications:** FACTS-compensated lines, microgrids with DERs, weak-source zones, post-contingency re-coordination.
- **Adaptive Function** = a pre-programmed mathematical relationship that maps a measured system variable to a relay setting.
- **Closed-loop update cycle** = typically a few hundred milliseconds (not every cycle).
- **Numerical Edge:** When SIR doubles, $\alpha$ decreases, reducing reach — this is the *quantitative* advantage of adaptivity.
- **Exam-Safe Phrasing:** Always describe adaptive relaying as "*continuously monitoring and adjusting*" — never as "*constantly changing*."
- **Real-world context:** PMUs, IEC 61850, and centralized substation automation are the *enabling technologies* for adaptive relaying.
- **Common Misconception:** Adaptive ≠ AI/ML-based. Adaptive uses deterministic rules; only *intelligent* protection uses AI.
- **Single-line summary for 1-mark questions:** *"Adaptive relaying = real-time, automatic tuning of relay settings to match changing power system conditions, improving security, dependability, and coordination."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive Differential Protective Scheme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET631 - DIGITAL PROTECTION OF POWER SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Differential Protective Scheme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — ADAPTIVE DIFFERENTIAL PROTECTIVE SCHEME

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptive Differential Protective Scheme (KTU 2024 Formal Definition):**
> An **Adaptive Differential Protective Scheme** is a digital/numerical relay-based protection strategy in which the operating characteristic (bias slope, pickup threshold, restraint quantities, and harmonic blocking criteria) of the differential relay is **dynamically modified in real time** as a function of prevailing power system operating conditions — such as load current, external fault severity, Current Transformer (CT) saturation level, and inrush transients — rather than relying on a single, statically configured percentage-bias curve.

The classical **percentage-bias (or biased) differential relay** uses a fixed slope $k$ in the operating plane of operate current $I_{op}$ versus restrain current $I_{rst}$. In contrast, the adaptive scheme adjusts $k$ and other parameters continuously so that:

$$
I_{op} \;>\; k_{adaptive}(I_{rst},\,\dot{I},\,V,\,h_2,\,h_5,\dots)\cdot I_{rst}
$$

where $k_{adaptive}$ is no longer a constant, but a *function* of measured system variables and signal derivatives.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Smart Airbag" of a Car:**
> Imagine a car's airbag system. A *fixed* (non-adaptive) airbag deploys with one preset sensitivity — it might either fire too easily during a minor bump (over-trip) or fail to fire in a severe, angled crash. An *adaptive* airbag, however, uses sensors for **seatbelt status, passenger weight, crash angle, and deceleration rate** to *tune* its firing threshold in real time. The airbag's behaviour **changes itself** based on the situation.
>
> The **Adaptive Differential Relay** is the *smart airbag* of the power system. Instead of using one rigid trip curve, it continuously watches:
> 1. **How big is the through-fault current?** (raises the bias to avoid CT-saturation misoperation)
> 2. **Is there a magnetising inrush?** (uses 2nd harmonic to raise the threshold)
> 3. **Is there over-excitation?** (uses 5th harmonic blocking)
> 4. **How saturated are the CTs?** (lowers operate signal confidence or extends restraint)
>
> The relay's "tripping personality" *adapts* — staying ultra-sensitive for genuine internal faults yet bulletproof against external transients.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bias slope (fixed) | $k$ | $\mathbf{0.15}$ to $\mathbf{0.60}$ | Slope of restrain characteristic |
| Minimum operate current | $I_{op,min}$ | $\mathbf{0.10}$ to $\mathbf{0.30}$ pu | Sensitivity floor |
| 2nd-harmonic restraint ratio | $I_{h2}/I_{h1}$ | $\mathbf{15\%}$ to $\mathbf{20\%}$ | Inrush detection threshold |
| 5th-harmonic restraint ratio | $I_{h5}/I_{h1}$ | $\mathbf{10\%}$ to $\mathbf{15\%}$ | Over-excitation detection |
| CT saturation detection level | $\partial I/\partial t$ | Adaptive | Real-time saturation flag |
| Sampling frequency | $f_s$ | $\mathbf{16}$ to $\mathbf{64}$ samples/cycle | Digital relay DSP rate |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** This topic sits inside *Module 4 — Digital Protection Schemes* and is a **high-yield 14-mark question** in end-semester examinations. Students must clearly differentiate *fixed-bias* vs *adaptive-bias* differential protection, with explicit mention of CT saturation, inrush, and over-excitation handling.

---

### 1.4 Why Conventional Differential Protection Falls Short

The **fixed percentage-bias differential relay** has three persistent weaknesses, each of which becomes the *justification* for adaptive protection:

1. **CT Saturation Misoperation:** During heavy external faults, one CT (or both) saturates asymmetrically. This produces a *false* differential current $\Delta I = I_1 - I_2$ even though no internal fault exists. A fixed slope cannot distinguish saturation-spike current from genuine fault current.
2. **Inrush Over-trip:** When a transformer is energised, the magnetising inrush current contains large **2nd-harmonic** components. A fixed, sensitive relay may misinterpret inrush as an internal fault and trip unnecessarily.
3. **Over-excitation Misoperation:** Sustained over-voltage (e.g., load rejection, generator V/Hz rise) produces high **5th-harmonic** currents and heating — a fixed differential relay has no native discrimination for this regime.

The adaptive scheme addresses each by *re-shaping* its own characteristic using the very signals that would otherwise cause misoperation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operating Plane of Differential Protection

For any two-terminal differential zone (transformer, generator, line, busbar), currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ enter and leave. Define:

$$
I_{op} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert \qquad \text{(operate / spill current)}
$$

$$
I_{rst} = \frac{\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert}{2} \qquad \text{(restrain / through current)}
$$

The classical trip equation is:

$$
I_{op} \;>\; k \cdot I_{rst} + I_{op,min}
$$

This describes a straight line in the $(I_{rst}, I_{op})$ plane with slope $k$ and y-intercept $I_{op,min}$.

---

### 2.2 The Adaptive Modification — How the Characteristic Changes

In the **adaptive scheme**, the trip equation becomes:

$$
I_{op} \;>\; k_{ad}(I_{rst},\,\dot{I},\,h_2,\,h_5) \cdot I_{rst} + I_{op,min}^{ad}
$$

The four key adaptive signals are:

| Adaptive Input | Symbol | What it Indicates | Adaptive Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| Through-fault current magnitude | $I_{rst}$ | External fault severity | **Increase slope $k$** to ride through CT saturation |
| Rate of change of current | $\dfrac{dI}{dt}$ | Steepness of current wave (CT saturation onset) | **Raise bias** instantaneously |
| 2nd-harmonic content | $I_{h2}/I_{h1}$ | Magnetising inrush | **Raise threshold / block trip** |
| 5th-harmonic content | $I_{h5}/I_{h1}$ | Over-excitation | **Block trip + alarm** |

---

### 2.3 Adaptive Bias Slope — Functional Forms

A typical adaptive slope function used in numerical relays is:

$$
k_{ad} = k_{min} + (k_{max} - k_{min}) \cdot \frac{I_{rst}}{I_{rst} + I_{knee}}
$$

where $I_{knee}$ is a designer-chosen *knee current* (typically 1.5–3.0 pu). This produces a **smooth, continuous** bias curve that approaches $k_{max}$ asymptotically for very large through-faults.

Alternatively, a **piecewise** adaptive slope is often used for clarity:

$$
k_{ad} = 
\begin{cases}
k_1, & 0 \le I_{rst} < I_{th1} \\[4pt]
k_2, & I_{th1} \le I_{rst} < I_{th2} \\[4pt]
k_3, & I_{rst} \ge I_{th2}
\end{cases}
$$

with $k_1 < k_2 < k_3$, e.g., $k_1 = 0.20$, $k_2 = 0.40$, $k_3 = 0.70$.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cheat-Sheet Table — Memorise Every Entry Below.**

| # | Formula / Condition | Description | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{op} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$ | Operate (spill) current | Amperes / pu |
| 2 | $I_{rst} = (\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert)/2$ | Restrain (through) current | Amperes / pu |
| 3 | $I_{op} > k \cdot I_{rst} + I_{op,min}$ | Fixed bias trip equation | Inequality |
| 4 | $I_{op} > k_{ad}(I_{rst})\cdot I_{rst} + I_{op,min}^{ad}$ | **Adaptive** bias trip equation | Inequality |
| 5 | $k_{ad} = k_{min} + (k_{max}-k_{min})\dfrac{I_{rst}}{I_{rst}+I_{knee}}$ | Smooth continuous adaptive slope | Dimensionless, $0 < k_{ad} < k_{max}$ |
| 6 | $I_{h2}/I_{h1} > \alpha_2$ | 2nd-harmonic inrush criterion | $\alpha_2 = 0.15$–$0.20$ |
| 7 | $I_{h5}/I_{h1} > \alpha_5$ | 5th-harmonic over-excitation criterion | $\alpha_5 = 0.10$–$0.15$ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dI}{dt} > \beta$ | CT saturation onset indicator | Numerical threshold |
| 9 | $n = \dfrac{\text{Samples/cycle}}{f_{sys}}$ | Samples per power cycle | $n = 16, 32, 64$ |
| 10 | $I_{th1}, I_{th2}$ | Adaptive slope breakpoints | $I_{th1} \approx 1.0$ pu, $I_{th2} \approx 3.0$ pu |
| 11 | $V/f$ ratio | Over-excitation indicator | pu Hz$^{-1}$, alarm at >1.1 |
| 12 | Trip-restraint flag | Boolean adaptive decision | $\text{Flag} \in \{0,1\}$ |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Adaptive differential schemes are now **industry-standard** in:

- **Numerical transformer differential relays** (e.g., for 400 kV EHV power transformers with through-fault currents exceeding 10–15 pu)
- **Large generator differential protection** (where CT saturation under external phase faults is routine)
- **Busbar differential protection** (with many incoming/outgoing feeders, each with its own CT)
- **Long transmission-line differential protection** (with line-charging current compensation that must *adapt* to voltage)

The adaptive technique offers **two key engineering wins**:

1. **Higher Security** — avoids false trips during external faults and transformer energisation.
2. **Higher Dependability** — retains sensitivity for genuine internal faults, even low-current turn-to-turn faults.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Fixed Bias to Adaptive Bias Characteristic

**Starting point — fixed bias trip equation:**

$$
I_{op} > k \cdot I_{rst} + I_{op,min} \qquad \text{(fixed, constant } k \text{)}
$$

**Step 1 — Recognise that for large external faults, the relay must *increase* its slope.**

The CT secondary error $e$ during saturation grows approximately as:

$$
e \;\approx\; k_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{I_{rst}}{I_{sat}}
$$

where $I_{sat}$ is the CT saturation knee-point current. For the relay to *not misoperate*, the operate signal must be smaller than the bias:

$$
I_{op} \;<\; k \cdot I_{rst} \quad \text{when } I_{rst} \text{ is large}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the saturation error relation into the trip equation.**

For *dependable* (no-trip-on-external-fault) operation:

$$
k \cdot I_{rst} \;>\; e \cdot I_{rst} \;=\; k_{sat}\cdot\dfrac{I_{rst}^2}{I_{sat}}
$$

Solving for the required minimum slope:

$$
k_{req} \;>\; \dfrac{k_{sat} \cdot I_{rst}}{I_{sat}}
$$

This shows the required slope $k_{req}$ must **grow with $I_{rst}$** — the foundation of adaptivity.

**Step 3 — Introduce a smooth saturation curve to bound the slope.**

Define $k_{max}$ as the upper saturation limit, and use a hyperbolic-tangent smooth function:

$$
k_{ad}(I_{rst}) \;=\; k_{min} + \dfrac{(k_{max}-k_{min})}{2}\left[1 + \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{I_{rst} - I_{knee}}{\Delta I}\right)\right]
$$

At $I_{rst} \ll I_{knee}$: $\tanh \to -1$, so $k_{ad} \to k_{min}$ (sensitive for internal faults).
At $I_{rst} \gg I_{knee}$: $\tanh \to +1$, so $k_{ad} \to k_{max}$ (secure for external faults).

**Step 4 — Embed 2nd-harmonic inrush restraint into the *minimum operate current*.**

For an inrush event with harmonic ratio $r_2 = I_{h2}/I_{h1}$:

$$
I_{op,min}^{ad} \;=\; I_{op,min}\cdot \left[1 + K_2 \cdot \dfrac{r_2}{\alpha_2}\right]
$$

where $K_2$ is a gain constant (typically $K_2 = 1.0$–$2.0$). When $r_2 > \alpha_2$, the threshold rises sharply, blocking trip.

**Step 5 — Combine all four adaptive signals into the final trip inequality.**

$$
\boxed{\;
I_{op} \;>\; k_{ad}(I_{rst}) \cdot I_{rst} \;+\; I_{op,min}^{ad}(r_2,\,r_5) \;
}
$$

This is the **complete KTU-grade adaptive differential trip equation**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Adaptive Restraint via Waveform Derivative (CT Saturation Detection)

During an external fault with CT saturation, the secondary current $i_s(t)$ exhibits *flat tops* where the CT core is saturated. The derivative of the current signal becomes:

$$
\dfrac{di_s}{dt} \;\to\; 0 \quad \text{during saturation intervals}
$$

Define a saturation indicator:

$$
S(t) \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{\vert \frac{di_s}{dt} \vert}{\max_t \vert \frac{di_s}{dt} \vert} \qquad \in [0,1]
$$

When $S(t) > 0.8$ (typically), the CT is saturated. The relay then **multiplies its restrain signal by a security factor $F_S$**:

$$
I_{rst}^{ad} \;=\; F_S \cdot I_{rst}, \qquad F_S = 1 + \lambda \cdot S(t)
$$

with $\lambda \in [0.5,\, 2.0]$. The enhanced restrain pushes the operating point *back* into the restrain zone, preventing misoperation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Adaptive Relay Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Working Matrix — Use This in Your Exam Answer.**

| Operating Scenario | $I_{op}$ | $I_{rst}$ | $r_2 = I_{h2}/I_{h1}$ | $r_5 = I_{h5}/I_{h1}$ | $dI/dt$ | $k_{ad}$ Choice | Trip Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Normal load (no fault) | Small | Small | $< 0.05$ | $< 0.05$ | Normal | $k_{min}$ | **No trip** (well inside restrain) |
| Internal fault (low current) | Large | Small | $< 0.05$ | $< 0.05$ | Normal | $k_{min}$ | **Trip** (sensitive) |
| Internal fault (heavy) | Very large | Moderate | $< 0.05$ | $< 0.05$ | Normal | $k_{ad}(I_{rst})$ | **Trip** |
| External fault (CT OK) | Small (CT error only) | Very large | $< 0.05$ | $< 0.05$ | Normal | $k_{max}$ | **No trip** (high bias) |
| External fault (CT saturated) | Moderate | Very large | $< 0.05$ | $< 0.05$ | Flat-top detected | $k_{max}$ + $F_S$ | **No trip** (adaptive restrain) |
| Transformer energisation (inrush) | Moderate | Moderate | $r_2 > \alpha_2$ | $< 0.05$ | Normal | $k_{ad}$ | **No trip** (2nd-harm block) |
| Over-excitation | Moderate | Moderate | $< 0.05$ | $r_5 > \alpha_5$ | Normal | $k_{ad}$ | **No trip + alarm** (5th-harm block) |
| CT secondary open (lead broken) | Moderate | Moderate | — | — | Erratic | $k_{max}$ | **No trip + alarm** (defeated) |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-Mark Sub-Question)

**Problem:** A 33 MVA, 11/132 kV power transformer is protected by an adaptive differential relay. The CT ratios are 600/5 on the HV side and 4000/5 on the LV side. For an external three-phase fault, the through-fault current is measured as $I_{rst} = 8.0$ pu on the relay base. The relay uses:

- $k_{min} = 0.20$
- $k_{max} = 0.60$
- $I_{knee} = 2.0$ pu

Find the **adaptive bias slope** $k_{ad}$.

**Solution — step by step:**

$$
k_{ad} \;=\; k_{min} + (k_{max}-k_{min})\cdot\dfrac{I_{rst}}{I_{rst}+I_{knee}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
k_{ad} \;=\; 0.20 + (0.60 - 0.20)\cdot\dfrac{8.0}{8.0 + 2.0}
$$

$$
k_{ad} \;=\; 0.20 + 0.40 \cdot \dfrac{8.0}{10.0}
$$

$$
k_{ad} \;=\; 0.20 + 0.40 \cdot 0.80
$$

$$
k_{ad} \;=\; 0.20 + 0.32 \;=\; \mathbf{0.52}
$$

**Interpretation:** During the external fault, the relay automatically raises its slope from 0.20 to **0.52**, providing 2.6× the security margin — exactly the behaviour the adaptive scheme is designed for. A *fixed-bias* relay with $k = 0.20$ would likely have misoperated.

---

### 3.5 Mathematical Identity — Slope Sensitivity to Through-Fault Current

Differentiating $k_{ad}$ with respect to $I_{rst}$:

$$
\dfrac{d k_{ad}}{d I_{rst}} \;=\; (k_{max}-k_{min})\cdot\dfrac{I_{knee}}{(I_{rst}+I_{knee})^2}
$$

This confirms that the slope is **most sensitive** at low currents (small $I_{rst}$) and **asymptotically approaches** $k_{max}$ for large currents. The derivative is always positive, so the slope is *monotonically increasing* — a desirable property.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Adaptive Differential Relay — Block Architecture

The numerical relay is organised as a chain of DSP blocks. Below is the complete functional architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CT Secondary Currents I1 and I2] --> B[Anti-Aliasing LPF]
    B --> C[16-bit A to D Converter]
    C --> D[Digital Filtering DFT]
    D --> E[Fundamental Extraction I1h1 and I2h1]
    D --> F[Harmonic Extraction I1h2 I2h2 I1h5 I2h5]
    E --> G[Compute Iop and Irst]
    G --> H[Compute Adaptive Bias Slope kad]
    F --> I[2nd Harmonic Ratio r2]
    F --> J[5th Harmonic Ratio r5]
    H --> K[Trip Decision Logic]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    G --> L[CT Saturation Detector dI by dt]
    L --> K
    K --> M[Trip Output to Breaker]
    K --> N[Alarm Output]
    K --> O[Event Recorder]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The two CT secondaries $I_1, I_2$ enter the relay, are low-pass filtered, digitised, then processed in parallel by a Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) block. The fundamental components produce $I_{op}$ and $I_{rst}$, while harmonic channels produce restraint ratios. All four adaptive signals (slope, 2nd-harm, 5th-harm, $dI/dt$) feed the central **Trip Decision Logic**, which issues a trip command, alarm, or event record.

---

### 4.2 Adaptive Trip Characteristic — Operating Plane

The diagram below shows how the relay characteristic *morphs* with operating conditions:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Low Through-Fault Zone]
        A1[Sensitive k equals 0.20] --> A2[Fast trip for internal faults]
    end
    subgraph P2[Moderate Through-Fault Zone]
        B1[Moderate k equals 0.40] --> B2[Balanced security]
    end
    subgraph P3[Heavy External Fault Zone]
        C1[Conservative k equals 0.60] --> C2[Immune to CT saturation]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    Q[Adaptive Trip Characteristic] -.-> P1
    Q -.-> P2
    Q -.-> P3
```

---

### 4.3 Adaptive Logic Flow — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Compute Iop, Irst, r2, r5] --> S1{Is Irst less than Inorm?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Use kmin: sensitive bias]
    S1 -- No --> S3{Is Irst large but less than Iextmax?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Use kad formula]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Use kmax: hard restrain]
    S4 --> S6{r2 greater than alpha2?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Inrush detected: Block trip]
    S6 -- No --> S8{r5 greater than alpha5?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Over-excitation: Block plus alarm]
    S8 -- No --> S10{CT saturation flag set?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Apply security factor FS]
    S10 -- No --> S12{Is Iop greater than bias plus threshold?}
    S2 --> S12
    S5 --> S12
    S11 --> S12
    S12 -- Yes --> S13[TRIP command to breaker]
    S12 -- No --> S14[No trip: continue monitoring]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Study Tip:** When drawing this diagram in the exam, label *each decision box* with its physical meaning (e.g., "Inrush?", "CT saturated?", "Trip?"). Examiners reward diagrams with **annotated arrows** showing *which physical quantity* drives *which decision*.

---

### 4.4 Conceptual Comparison — Fixed vs Adaptive Bias Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIXED[Fixed Bias Relay]
        F1[Single Straight Line] --> F2[Slope k is constant]
        F2 --> F3[Same response always]
    end
    subgraph ADAPTIVE[Adaptive Bias Relay]
        AD1[Multi-slope or Smooth Curve] --> AD2[k varies with Irst]
        AD2 --> AD3[Hormonic blocks active]
        AD3 --> AD4[CT saturation handled]
    end
    FIXED -.-> ADAPTIVE
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**
*What is meant by an Adaptive Differential Protective Scheme? How does it differ from a conventional percentage-bias differential relay?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **Adaptive Differential Protective Scheme** is a numerical relay-based protection method in which the operating characteristic — bias slope $k$, pickup threshold $I_{op,min}$, and harmonic blocking criteria — is **dynamically varied in real time** in response to changing system conditions such as through-fault current level, CT saturation, transformer inrush, or over-excitation.
>
> In a **conventional percentage-bias relay**, the slope $k$ is a *fixed* constant (typically 0.2–0.5), giving the same trip characteristic under all conditions. The **adaptive relay**, in contrast, continuously adjusts $k$ using the measured restrain current $I_{rst}$ and applies harmonic-based blocking (2nd harmonic for inrush, 5th harmonic for over-excitation). This makes the adaptive scheme **more secure** against external faults and CT saturation while **retaining sensitivity** for internal faults. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*State the role of the 2nd-harmonic component in an adaptive differential scheme used for transformer protection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **2nd-harmonic component** of the differential current is used as the **principal indicator of magnetising inrush** in a power transformer. During energisation, the inrush current contains a large 2nd-harmonic content (typically 30–70% of fundamental), whereas an internal fault current has a 2nd-harmonic content below 5–10%.
>
> In the adaptive scheme, the ratio $r_2 = I_{h2}/I_{h1}$ is computed in real time. If $r_2 > \alpha_2$ (where $\alpha_2 \approx 0.15$–$0.20$), the relay **raises its operate threshold** $I_{op,min}^{ad}$ or **blocks the trip entirely**, thereby preventing false tripping during transformer energisation. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain with neat diagrams the **operating principle of an adaptive differential protective scheme**. Clearly derive the **adaptive bias trip equation** showing how the slope $k_{ad}$ is made a function of the restrain current $I_{rst}$.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] During a transformer energisation, the following data is captured by the relay: $I_{op} = 0.45$ pu, $I_{rst} = 1.2$ pu, 2nd-harmonic ratio $r_2 = 0.40$, 5th-harmonic ratio $r_5 = 0.06$. The relay uses $k_{min} = 0.20$, $k_{max} = 0.60$, $I_{knee} = 2.0$ pu, $\alpha_2 = 0.20$, $\alpha_5 = 0.15$, and $I_{op,min} = 0.10$ pu, with adaptive threshold gain $K_2 = 1.5$.
>
> **Determine** whether the relay will trip. Show all calculations.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operating Principle and Derivation (7 marks):**

The conventional percentage-bias differential relay operates on the principle:

$$
I_{op} > k \cdot I_{rst} + I_{op,min} \tag{1}
$$

where $k$ is fixed. The adaptive scheme modifies this by making $k$ a function of $I_{rst}$ and by adding harmonic-based threshold elevation:

$$
I_{op} > k_{ad}(I_{rst}) \cdot I_{rst} + I_{op,min}^{ad}(r_2, r_5) \tag{2}
$$

The adaptive slope follows the smooth hyperbolic saturation form:

$$
k_{ad}(I_{rst}) = k_{min} + (k_{max} - k_{min})\cdot\dfrac{I_{rst}}{I_{rst} + I_{knee}} \tag{3}
$$

At low $I_{rst}$ (no external fault): $k_{ad} \to k_{min}$, providing maximum sensitivity for internal faults.
At high $I_{rst}$ (heavy external fault): $k_{ad} \to k_{max}$, providing maximum security against CT saturation misoperation.

The threshold adapts based on harmonic ratios:

$$
I_{op,min}^{ad} = I_{op,min} \cdot \left[1 + K_2 \cdot \dfrac{r_2}{\alpha_2}\right] \quad \text{(inrush elevation)} \tag{4}
$$

Equations (2)–(4) constitute the complete adaptive trip equation. **(7 marks — includes the diagram + derivation)**

**(b) Numerical Trip Decision (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute adaptive slope:**

$$
k_{ad} = 0.20 + (0.60 - 0.20)\cdot\dfrac{1.2}{1.2 + 2.0}
$$

$$
k_{ad} = 0.20 + 0.40 \cdot \dfrac{1.2}{3.2}
$$

$$
k_{ad} = 0.20 + 0.40 \cdot 0.375 = 0.20 + 0.15 = \mathbf{0.35}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute adaptive minimum operate threshold:**

$$
I_{op,min}^{ad} = 0.10 \cdot \left[1 + 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{0.40}{0.20}\right]
$$

$$
I_{op,min}^{ad} = 0.10 \cdot [1 + 1.5 \cdot 2.0] = 0.10 \cdot [1 + 3.0] = 0.10 \cdot 4.0 = \mathbf{0.40 \text{ pu}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute right-hand side of trip equation:**

$$
RHS = k_{ad} \cdot I_{rst} + I_{op,min}^{ad} = 0.35 \cdot 1.2 + 0.40 = 0.42 + 0.40 = \mathbf{0.82 \text{ pu}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with operate signal:**

$$
I_{op} = 0.45 \text{ pu} \quad \text{vs} \quad RHS = 0.82 \text{ pu}
$$

$$
0.45 \;<\; 0.82 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Trip condition NOT satisfied.}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check via 2nd-harmonic criterion:**

Since $r_2 = 0.40 > \alpha_2 = 0.20$, the relay also explicitly recognises an **inrush condition**, raising the threshold 4× above its base value. This provides redundant security.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The relay will **NOT trip** — this is an inrush event during transformer energisation, and the adaptive scheme has correctly restrained. **(7 marks total)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Discuss the **limitations of conventional percentage-bias differential protection** with respect to (i) CT saturation during external faults, (ii) magnetising inrush of transformers, and (iii) over-excitation conditions. Explain how the adaptive scheme overcomes each limitation.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] With the help of a **neat labelled block diagram**, describe the **functional architecture of a numerical adaptive differential relay**. Clearly indicate the role of the DFT block, harmonic extraction, and the central trip-decision logic.

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Limitations and Adaptive Remedies (7 marks — distribute as 3+2+2):**

| Limitation | Fixed-Bias Problem | Adaptive Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) CT saturation** during external faults | A fixed low slope $k$ allows the CT-error spill current to exceed $k \cdot I_{rst}$, causing **false trip** | $k_{ad}(I_{rst})$ rises with through-current; $dI/dt$ detector boosts restrain via $F_S$ |
| **(ii) Magnetising inrush** | Inrush looks like an internal fault — fixed, sensitive relay **trips on energisation** | 2nd-harmonic ratio $r_2$ raises $I_{op,min}^{ad}$ by a factor $[1 + K_2 r_2/\alpha_2]$, blocking the trip |
| **(iii) Over-excitation** | Sustained $V/f$ rise produces differential current due to saturation of iron core — relay misoperates | 5th-harmonic ratio $r_5$ is monitored; if $r_5 > \alpha_5$, trip is blocked and an alarm is raised |

**(b) Block Diagram of Adaptive Numerical Relay (7 marks):**

Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** above. The student should reproduce this in the exam, with clear labelling of:

1. **Signal acquisition stage** — CT secondaries → anti-aliasing LPF → 16-bit ADC.
2. **DSP stage** — DFT-based fundamental + harmonic extraction.
3. **Computation stage** — $I_{op}$, $I_{rst}$, $r_2$, $r_5$, $dI/dt$.
4. **Adaptive decision stage** — central trip-decision logic with all four adaptive inputs.
5. **Output stage** — Trip output, alarm, event recorder, communication port (IEC 61850).

**(7 marks — 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for labelling, 2 marks for explanation of DFT role)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Adaptive Differential Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "fixed bias" with "percentage bias":** A *percentage* bias IS a single-slope characteristic — students mistakenly write "fixed bias" when they mean "single-slope bias." Use the precise term.
> 2. **Forgetting the harmonic-restrained minimum operate current:** Examiners expect *both* the adaptive slope term AND the harmonic-adjusted threshold. Writing only $k_{ad} \cdot I_{rst}$ without $I_{op,min}^{ad}$ loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong direction of slope adjustment:** Students often state that the slope *decreases* during external faults (the opposite is true). The slope **increases** to provide *more* restrain during heavy through-faults.
> 4. **Confusing 2nd-harmonic with over-excitation:** 2nd-harmonic = inrush. 5th-harmonic = over-excitation. These are *not* interchangeable. Mis-attributing the harmonic order costs full marks on the harmonic-restrained question.
> 5. **Skipping unit conversion in numericals:** Always express $I_{op}$, $I_{rst}$, and the threshold in **consistent per-unit** (or all in Amperes). Mixed units = 1-mark penalty minimum.
> 6. **No diagram:** A 14-mark question without a block diagram or operating-plane sketch typically loses 2–3 marks. Always include at least one annotated schematic.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Adaptive Differential Protective Scheme.**

- **Definition:** Adaptive scheme = real-time variation of bias slope, threshold, and harmonic blocking in response to system conditions.
- **Operating plane coordinates:** $I_{op}$ (vertical axis), $I_{rst}$ (horizontal axis).
- **Trip inequality:** $I_{op} > k_{ad}(I_{rst})\cdot I_{rst} + I_{op,min}^{ad}(r_2, r_5)$.
- **Adaptive slope formula (KTU must-know):** $k_{ad} = k_{min} + (k_{max}-k_{min})\cdot\dfrac{I_{rst}}{I_{rst}+I_{knee}}$.
- **Adaptive threshold:** $I_{op,min}^{ad} = I_{op,min}\cdot[1 + K_2 \cdot r_2/\alpha_2]$.
- **2nd-harmonic:** indicates transformer inrush; threshold $\alpha_2 \approx 0.15$–$0.20$.
- **5th-harmonic:** indicates over-excitation; threshold $\alpha_5 \approx 0.10$–$0.15$.
- **$dI/dt$ saturation detector:** flat-tops in the secondary current waveform reveal CT saturation; security factor $F_S$ boosts restrain.
- **CT saturation misoperation:** the primary problem adaptive schemes solve for *external* faults.
- **Magnetising inrush:** the primary problem adaptive schemes solve for *transformer energisation*.
- **Over-excitation ($V/f$ rise):** handled by 5th-harmonic blocking + alarm.
- **Numerical relay architecture:** CT → LPF → ADC → DFT → fundamental + harmonic channels → trip decision logic → trip/alarm/event record.
- **Industry-standard typical values:** $k_{min} \approx 0.20$, $k_{max} \approx 0.60$, $I_{knee} \approx 2.0$ pu, $\alpha_2 \approx 0.20$, $\alpha_5 \approx 0.15$, $I_{op,min} \approx 0.10$ pu.
- **Applications:** EHV power transformers, large generators, EHV busbars, long transmission lines with line-charging compensation.
- **Engineering trade-off:** adaptive = high *security* + high *dependability*; fixed = simple but vulnerable to false trips.
- **Exam mantra:** "**Slope rises with through-current; threshold rises with harmonics.**"
